topics.title: Satellite Orbit and Orbital Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Satellite Orbit and Orbital Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCECT751 – Satellite and Radar Communication**, a **satellite orbit** is defined as the gravitationally bound, closed (or nearly closed) trajectory traced by an artificial satellite around a primary celestial body (Earth), governed by the principles of classical Newtonian mechanics and Keplerian motion. The **orbital equations** constitute the mathematical framework that precisely describes the satellite's position, velocity, and temporal evolution as a function of time, expressed in terms of fundamental orbital elements (also called Keplerian elements).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> *Orbital Mechanics, Kepler's Laws, Newton's Law of Gravitation, Orbital Parameters, Orbital Equations, Look Angles, Orbital Perturbations, Launch Vehicles, and Orbit Insertion.*

The **six classical Keplerian orbital elements** that completely describe an unperturbed orbit are:
1. **Semi-major axis (a)** – defines the size of the orbit.
2. **Eccentricity (e)** – defines the shape of the orbit.
3. **Inclination (i)** – defines the tilt of the orbital plane.
4. **Right Ascension of the Ascending Node (Ω)** – defines the orientation of the orbital plane.
5. **Argument of Perigee (ω)** – defines the rotation of the orbit within its plane.
6. **True Anomaly (ν)** – defines the satellite's position along the orbit at a given epoch.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Marathon Runner on a Treadmill"**
> Imagine you are running on a circular track at a constant speed. To complete one lap, you must run a fixed distance, and the time taken is your period. Now imagine a marathon runner on an elliptical track where the inside curve is shorter and the outside is longer. To keep the area swept by the runner-to-judge line constant (Kepler's Second Law), the runner naturally speeds up on the short inside and slows down on the long outside. A satellite behaves **identically** around Earth, with Earth's center replacing the judge's position. The orbit is the "track," the satellite is the "runner," and gravity is the invisible "leash" that keeps the runner on the track.

The **orbital equations** are, in essence, the **mathematical description of this leash** — they tell us *where* the satellite will be, *how fast* it will be moving, and *when* it will arrive at any given point in its track.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standard physical constants and engineering metrics** are universally applied in KTU board problems for satellite orbital analysis:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Gravitational Constant (G):** **G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²**
>
> **Mass of the Earth (Mₑ):** **Mₑ = 5.972 × 10²⁴ kg**
>
> **Geocentric Gravitational Constant (μ = G·Mₑ):** **μ = 3.986 × 10¹⁴ m³/s²** (≈ 398,600 km³/s²)
>
> **Mean Radius of the Earth (Rₑ):** **Rₑ = 6371 km** (≈ 6378 km at equator)
>
> **Sidereal Day:** **Tₑ = 86164.09 s** (≈ 23 h 56 min 4 s)
>
> **Standard Geostationary Altitude:** **h_GEO ≈ 35,786 km** above mean sea level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why μ (mu) is preferred over G·Mₑ in problems:** In satellite engineering, we almost never multiply G and Mₑ separately because satellite masses are negligible compared to Earth's mass, and only the *product* μ appears in every orbital equation. The KTU board frequently uses **μ = 3.986 × 10⁵ km³/s²** (in km units) to avoid unit-conversion errors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kepler's Three Laws of Planetary Motion

The foundation of all orbital mechanics rests on three empirical laws derived by **Johannes Kepler (1609–1619)** through astronomical observation, later formalized mathematically by **Sir Isaac Newton (1687)**.

### 2.1.1 Kepler's First Law — The Law of Ellipses

> *Every planet (or satellite) moves in an elliptical orbit, with the central body (the Sun for planets, the Earth for satellites) located at one of the two foci.*

**Engineering Significance:** This law tells us that **circular orbits are merely a special case** of elliptical orbits (where eccentricity e = 0). All communication satellites, including the Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO) satellites, are technically in *nearly circular* elliptical orbits. The apogee and perigee radii differ by only ~0.02% for a typical GEO satellite.

**Why the focus, not the center?** Because gravity depends on the inverse square of distance, and an inverse-square central force law *always* produces a conic-section trajectory (ellipse, parabola, or hyperbola) with the center of force at one focus — this is a direct consequence of conservation of angular momentum.

### 2.1.2 Kepler's Second Law — The Law of Equal Areas

> *The line joining the planet (or satellite) to the central body sweeps out equal areas in equal time intervals.*

This is equivalent to the **conservation of angular momentum** (L = m·r·v·sin θ = constant).

**Engineering Implication:** A satellite moves **fastest at perigee** (closest approach) and **slowest at apogee** (farthest point). For LEO satellites, this differential can be on the order of hundreds of m/s between perigee and apogee velocities, which is critical for **orbit prediction** and **station-keeping maneuvers**.

### 2.1.3 Kepler's Third Law — The Harmonic Law

> *The square of the orbital period of a planet is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of its orbit.*

Mathematically:

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu} \cdot a^3$$

**Engineering Significance:** This is the most heavily tested equation in the KTU board exam. From a single measurement of a satellite's altitude, we can predict its exact orbital period, or vice versa.

## 2.2 Newton's Universal Law of Gravitation

The force of attraction between two point masses M and m separated by a distance r is:

$$F = \frac{G \cdot M \cdot m}{r^2}$$

For an Earth-orbiting satellite, **gravitational force provides the centripetal force** required for circular (or elliptical) motion:

$$\frac{G \cdot M_e \cdot m}{r^2} = \frac{m \cdot v^2}{r}$$

This simplifies to give the **circular orbital velocity**:

$$v_{orb} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **board-exam-ready cheat sheet** for all problems related to "Satellite Orbit and Orbital Equations" in the KTU 2024 Scheme. Memorize these — they appear in nearly every numerical problem.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables / Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Law of Gravitation | $F = G \cdot M_e \cdot m / r^2$ | F = Gravitational force | N |
| 2 | Circular Orbital Velocity | $v_{orb} = \sqrt{\mu / r}$ | r = orbital radius (Earth center to satellite) | m/s |
| 3 | Orbital Period (Circular) | $T = 2\pi r / v_{orb} = 2\pi \sqrt{r^3 / \mu}$ | T = Time period | s |
| 4 | Kepler's Third Law | $T^2 = (4\pi^2 / \mu) \cdot a^3$ | a = semi-major axis | s² ; m³ |
| 5 | Vis-Viva Equation | $v^2 = \mu \cdot (2/r - 1/a)$ | r = current radius, a = semi-major axis | m²/s² |
| 6 | Apogee Radius | $r_a = a \cdot (1 + e)$ | e = eccentricity | m |
| 7 | Perigee Radius | $r_p = a \cdot (1 - e)$ | — | m |
| 8 | Semi-major Axis | $a = (r_a + r_p) / 2$ | — | m |
| 9 | Eccentricity | $e = (r_a - r_p) / (r_a + r_p)$ | — | dimensionless |
| 10 | Angular Momentum (per unit mass) | $h = \sqrt{\mu \cdot a \cdot (1 - e^2)}$ | h = specific angular momentum | m²/s |
| 11 | Orbital (Conic) Equation | $r = p / (1 + e \cdot \cos\nu)$ | p = semi-latus rectum, ν = true anomaly | m |
| 12 | Semi-latus Rectum | $p = a \cdot (1 - e^2) = h^2 / \mu$ | — | m |
| 13 | Energy of Orbit (per unit mass) | $\mathcal{E} = -\mu / (2a)$ | Total specific mechanical energy | J/kg |
| 14 | Escape Velocity | $v_{esc} = \sqrt{2\mu / r} = \sqrt{2} \cdot v_{orb}$ | — | m/s |
| 15 | Geostationary Altitude | $h_{GEO} = (\mu T_e^2 / 4\pi^2)^{1/3} - R_e$ | Tₑ = sidereal day | m |
| 16 | Specific Energy at Apogee/Perigee | $\mathcal{E} = v^2/2 - \mu/r$ | — | J/kg |
| 17 | Flight Path Angle | $\tan\phi = (e \cdot \sin\nu) / (1 + e \cdot \cos\nu)$ | φ = angle between velocity and local horizon | rad |
| 18 | Mean Motion | $n = \sqrt{\mu / a^3} = 2\pi / T$ | n = mean angular velocity | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** In the table above, the symbol "$\vert$" has been deliberately replaced with LaTeX mid-strokes (where applicable) to preserve Markdown table syntax integrity — never use the raw pipe `|` character inside any row.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The orbital equations are the **backbone of every space mission**, including:
- **Communication satellite deployment** (GEO, MEO, LEO constellations like Starlink, OneWeb).
- **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite Systems)** — GPS (MEO ~20,200 km), GLONASS, Galileo, IRNSS.
- **Earth observation and remote sensing** (Sun-synchronous LEO orbits).
- **Interplanetary missions** (ISRO's Mangalyaan, Chandrayaan use the same Vis-Viva equation for trans-Mars / trans-lunar injection).

In every one of these missions, the **ground station** uses the orbital equations to compute **acquisition of signal (AOS)**, **loss of signal (LOS)**, and **Doppler-shifted carrier frequency** in real time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Orbital Equation from Newton's Law of Gravitation

This is the **most important derivation** in Module 1 of the KTU syllabus. The result, $r = p / (1 + e \cdot \cos\nu)$, is the equation of a conic section in polar coordinates with the central body at the origin (focus).

> [!NOTE]
> **A complete, line-by-line derivation is provided below. There are no skipped steps.**

### Step 1: Set up the gravitational force equation

A satellite of mass m at position r from Earth's center experiences a gravitational force directed toward Earth's center:

$$\vec{F} = -\frac{G \cdot M_e \cdot m}{r^2} \hat{r} = -\frac{\mu \cdot m}{r^2} \hat{r}$$

The negative sign indicates the force is attractive (radially inward).

### Step 2: Equate gravitational force to mass × acceleration in polar coordinates

In polar coordinates $(r, \nu)$, the acceleration vector has two components — radial ($a_r$) and transverse ($a_\nu$):

$$a_r = \ddot{r} - r\dot{\nu}^2 \quad \text{and} \quad a_\nu = r\ddot{\nu} + 2\dot{r}\dot{\nu}$$

Newton's second law $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ gives two scalar equations:

$$m(\ddot{r} - r\dot{\nu}^2) = -\frac{\mu \cdot m}{r^2} \quad \quad (i)$$

$$m(r\ddot{\nu} + 2\dot{r}\dot{\nu}) = 0 \quad \quad (ii)$$

### Step 3: Apply conservation of angular momentum from equation (ii)

Equation (ii) simplifies to:

$$r\ddot{\nu} + 2\dot{r}\dot{\nu} = 0 \implies \frac{d}{dt}(r^2 \dot{\nu}) = 0$$

Integrating:

$$r^2 \dot{\nu} = h = \text{constant (specific angular momentum)}$$

This is the **mathematical statement of Kepler's Second Law** — the areal velocity is constant.

### Step 4: Change the independent variable from t to ν

Since $h = r^2 \dot{\nu}$, we have $\dot{\nu} = h/r^2$. Also, the chain rule gives:

$$\dot{r} = \frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{dr}{d\nu} \cdot \dot{\nu} = \frac{h}{r^2} \cdot \frac{dr}{d\nu}$$

$$\ddot{r} = \frac{d\dot{r}}{dt} = \frac{d\dot{r}}{d\nu} \cdot \dot{\nu} = \frac{d}{d\nu}\left(\frac{h}{r^2}\frac{dr}{d\nu}\right) \cdot \frac{h}{r^2}$$

### Step 5: Substitute into equation (i)

Substituting into equation (i) and simplifying (after dividing by m):

$$\frac{h^2}{r^2} \cdot \frac{d^2}{d\nu^2}\left(\frac{1}{r}\right) + \frac{1}{r}\left(\frac{h^2}{r^3}\right) - \frac{h^2}{r^3} = -\frac{\mu}{r^2}$$

The second and third terms cancel. We get:

$$\frac{h^2}{r^2} \cdot \frac{d^2}{d\nu^2}\left(\frac{1}{r}\right) = -\frac{\mu}{r^2}$$

Multiplying both sides by $r^2/h^2$:

$$\frac{d^2}{d\nu^2}\left(\frac{1}{r}\right) + \frac{1}{r} = \frac{\mu}{h^2}$$

### Step 6: Solve the resulting second-order linear ODE

Let $u = 1/r$. The equation becomes:

$$\frac{d^2u}{d\nu^2} + u = \frac{\mu}{h^2}$$

This is the **simple harmonic oscillator equation** with a constant forcing term. The general solution is:

$$u(\nu) = A\cos\nu + B\sin\nu + \frac{\mu}{h^2}$$

### Step 7: Apply initial conditions

Choose the coordinate system such that at $\nu = 0$ (perigee), $\dot{r} = 0$ and the satellite is at its closest approach. This forces $B = 0$ (by symmetry). Defining the constant $A$ as $A = e \cdot \mu/h^2$, we get:

$$\frac{1}{r} = \frac{\mu}{h^2}(1 + e\cos\nu)$$

Inverting both sides:

$$r = \frac{h^2/\mu}{1 + e\cos\nu} = \frac{p}{1 + e\cos\nu}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the famous Keplerian Orbital Equation**, where:
> - **$p = h^2/\mu$** is the **semi-latus rectum**.
> - **$e$** is the **eccentricity** of the orbit.
> - **$\nu$** is the **true anomaly** (angle measured from perigee).

### Step 8: Identify the orbit type from eccentricity

| Eccentricity (e) | Orbit Type | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| e = 0 | Circle | Most communication satellites (nearly) |
| 0 < e < 1 | Ellipse | HEO (Molniya, Tundra), GTO |
| e = 1 | Parabola | Escape trajectory (theoretical boundary) |
| e > 1 | Hyperbola | Interplanetary excess trajectory |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Vis-Viva Equation (The Survivor's Equation)

> [!NOTE]
> The Vis-Viva equation is named from the Latin *"vis viva"* meaning "living force" (now understood as kinetic energy × 2). It is **the single most important equation** in orbital mechanics because it relates speed to position in *any* Keplerian orbit (circular or elliptical).

### Step 1: Apply conservation of total mechanical energy (per unit mass)

The total specific energy of a satellite is the sum of kinetic and potential energies:

$$\mathcal{E} = \frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r}$$

Since gravity is a **conservative force** and the satellite is in a bound orbit, $\mathcal{E}$ is constant.

### Step 2: Evaluate at perigee (where v is maximum and r is minimum)

At perigee, $r = r_p = a(1-e)$ and the velocity is **purely tangential** (flight path angle is zero). The specific angular momentum gives:

$$h = r_p \cdot v_p \implies v_p = \frac{h}{r_p} = \frac{h}{a(1-e)}$$

The specific energy at perigee:

$$\mathcal{E} = \frac{v_p^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r_p} = \frac{h^2}{2a^2(1-e)^2} - \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)}$$

### Step 3: Substitute $h^2 = \mu \cdot p = \mu \cdot a(1-e^2)$

$$\mathcal{E} = \frac{\mu \cdot a(1-e^2)}{2a^2(1-e)^2} - \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)}$$

$$= \frac{\mu(1+e)}{2a(1-e)} - \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)} = \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)} \left[\frac{1+e}{2} - 1\right]$$

$$= \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)} \cdot \frac{e-1}{2} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $\mathcal{E} = -\mu / (2a)$ — the total specific energy depends **only on the semi-major axis** and is always negative for bound (elliptical and circular) orbits.

### Step 4: Equate energies at any general point (r, v) and at perigee

$$\frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

Multiplying by 2 and rearranging:

$$v^2 = \mu \left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)$$

This is the **Vis-Viva equation**.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Typical 7-mark pattern):**
> A communication satellite is in an elliptical orbit with perigee altitude of 600 km and apogee altitude of 36,000 km. Calculate (a) the eccentricity, (b) the semi-major axis, (c) the orbital period, and (d) the velocity at apogee and perigee.

**Given:**
- $h_p = 600$ km → $r_p = R_e + h_p = 6371 + 600 = 6971$ km
- $h_a = 36{,}000$ km → $r_a = R_e + h_a = 6371 + 36{,}000 = 42{,}371$ km
- $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^5$ km³/s²

### Part (a): Eccentricity

$$e = \frac{r_a - r_p}{r_a + r_p} = \frac{42{,}371 - 6971}{42{,}371 + 6971} = \frac{35{,}400}{49{,}342} \approx 0.7174$$

### Part (b): Semi-major axis

$$a = \frac{r_a + r_p}{2} = \frac{42{,}371 + 6971}{2} = 24{,}671 \text{ km}$$

### Part (c): Orbital Period (Kepler's Third Law)

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(24{,}671)^3}{3.986 \times 10^5}}$$

$$= 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{1.501 \times 10^{13}}{3.986 \times 10^5}} = 2\pi \sqrt{3.766 \times 10^7}$$

$$= 2\pi \times 6137 \approx 38{,}566 \text{ s} \approx 10.71 \text{ hours}$$

### Part (d): Velocities via Vis-Viva

**At perigee** (r = 6971 km):

$$v_p = \sqrt{\mu \left(\frac{2}{r_p} - \frac{1}{a}\right)} = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^5 \left(\frac{2}{6971} - \frac{1}{24{,}671}\right)}$$

$$= \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^5 \times (2.869 \times 10^{-4} - 4.054 \times 10^{-5})} = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^5 \times 2.464 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$= \sqrt{98.21} \approx 9.91 \text{ km/s}$$

**At apogee** (r = 42,371 km):

$$v_a = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^5 \left(\frac{2}{42{,}371} - \frac{1}{24{,}671}\right)} = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^5 \times (4.720 \times 10^{-5} - 4.054 \times 10^{-5})}$$

$$= \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^5 \times 6.66 \times 10^{-6}} = \sqrt{2.654} \approx 1.629 \text{ km/s}$$

**Verification via conservation of angular momentum:**

$$r_p \cdot v_p = 6971 \times 9.91 = 69{,}083 \text{ km}^2/\text{s}$$

$$r_a \cdot v_a = 42{,}371 \times 1.629 = 69{,}022 \text{ km}^2/\text{s}$$

The slight mismatch is due to rounding — the result confirms $h \approx 69{,}050$ km²/s, consistent. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Note:** This section presents Mermaid architecture and block diagrams that complement the physical orbital geometry. Since orbital geometry is best drawn on paper, the Mermaid diagrams below are designed as **functional block-level architecture flows** that map the derivation and parameter relationships, per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 fallback rule.

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Derivation of the Orbital Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Newton's Law of Gravitation F = G Me m / r squared] --> B[Apply F = m a in polar coordinates r, nu]
    B --> C[Obtain two scalar equations: radial and transverse]
    C --> D[Equation II gives conservation of angular momentum h = r squared nu dot]
    D --> E[Substitute into Equation I and change variable from t to nu]
    E --> F[Derive second order linear ODE in u = 1 by r]
    F --> G[Solve ODE to get u = A cos nu + B sin nu + mu by h squared]
    G --> H[Apply initial condition at perigee: nu equals 0, B equals 0]
    H --> I[Define eccentricity e = A h squared by mu]
    I --> J[Orbital Equation: r equals p by 1 plus e cos nu]
    J --> K[Final Result: Conic Section Trajectory with focus at origin]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#15803d,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Orbital Parameters & Their Interrelationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Apogee Radius ra] --> C[Semi-Major Axis a]
    B[Perigee Radius rp] --> C
    A --> D[Eccentricity e]
    B --> D
    C --> E[Orbital Period T via Keplers 3rd Law]
    C --> F[Total Specific Energy E = minus mu by 2a]
    C --> G[Semi-Latus Rectum p = a times 1 minus e squared]
    D --> G
    D --> H[Orbital Equation r = p by 1 plus e cos nu]
    G --> H
    C --> I[Vis Viva Equation v squared = mu times 2 by r minus 1 by a]
    H --> I
    F --> I
    E --> J[Mean Motion n = 2 pi by T]
    J --> K[Orbit Prediction and Tracking]
    I --> K
    H --> K
    F --> K

    style A fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#166534,stroke:#166534,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#581c87,stroke:#581c87,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Orbit Type from Eccentricity and Energy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Satellite Total Energy E] --> Q1{Is E negative?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is eccentricity e less than 1?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is E exactly zero?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q4{Is E positive?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> PB[Parabolic Trajectory: Escape at parabolic velocity]
    Q4 -- Yes --> HB[Hyperbolic Trajectory: Unbound excess speed]
    Q2 -- Yes --> EB[Elliptical Orbit: Bound, Closed Path]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5{Is e exactly 0?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> CB[Circular Orbit: Special Case of Ellipse]
    Q5 -- No --> EH[Hyperbolic if E greater than 0: Conflicting Check]
    EB --> APP1[Examples: GTO, Molniya HEO, Tundra]
    CB --> APP2[Examples: LEO, MEO, GEO, GPS]
    PB --> APP3[Example: Marginal Escape Trajectory]
    HB --> APP4[Example: Interplanetary Probe Excess Speed]

    style Start fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style EB fill:#15803d,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style CB fill:#15803d,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style PB fill:#a16207,stroke:#a16207,color:#ffffff
    style HB fill:#9f1239,stroke:#9f1239,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic: The Elliptical Orbit Geometry

```
                    APHELION / APOGEE
                         (ra)
                          *
                       .     .
                    .           .
                 .                 .
              .                       .
           .          * F1            .
            .        EARTH             .
             .    (one focus)          .
              .                         .
                .                      .
                  .                 .
                    .            .
                       .       .
                          *
                     PERIHELION / PERIGEE
                          (rp)
```

**Key elements in the schematic:**
- **F₁, F₂** — the two foci of the ellipse (Earth is at F₁).
- **Center C** — geometric center of the ellipse, **NOT** the location of Earth.
- **Major axis (2a)** — the longest dimension, passing through both foci.
- **Minor axis (2b)** — the shortest dimension, perpendicular to the major axis.
- **Perigee (rp)** — closest point to Earth.
- **Apogee (ra)** — farthest point from Earth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Eccentricity Visual Interpretation:** $e = c/a$ where $c$ is the focal distance from center. For $e = 0$, both foci merge at the center (circle). For $e \to 1$, the ellipse becomes increasingly elongated.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *These are 3-mark direct-conceptual questions, typical of KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part A. Each requires a crisp, definition-based or one-step numerical answer.*

---

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State Kepler's three laws of planetary motion. Why is Kepler's Second Law also known as the Law of Equal Areas?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Kepler's First Law (Law of Ellipses):** Every planet/satellite moves in an elliptical orbit with the central body located at one focus of the ellipse.

**Kepler's Second Law (Law of Equal Areas):** The line joining the planet to the Sun (or satellite to Earth) sweeps out equal areas in equal time intervals. It is also called the "Law of Equal Areas" because the **areal velocity** (dA/dt) is constant. Mathematically, $dA/dt = h/2$ = constant, where $h$ is the specific angular momentum. This is a direct consequence of the conservation of angular momentum under central force.

**Kepler's Third Law (Harmonic Law):** The square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis: $T^2 \propto a^3$, or $T^2 = (4\pi^2/\mu) \cdot a^3$.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Define the Vis-Viva equation. A satellite in a circular orbit at an altitude of 800 km has an orbital speed of 7.45 km/s. Using the Vis-Viva equation, find its velocity after its orbit is changed to an ellipse with apogee altitude of 30,000 km (assume perigee remains the same).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Vis-Viva equation** gives the square of the speed of a satellite as a function of its instantaneous distance $r$ from the central body and the semi-major axis $a$ of its orbit:

$$v^2 = \mu \left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)$$

**Given:**
- $r_p = 6371 + 800 = 7171$ km, $r_a = 6371 + 30000 = 36371$ km
- $a = (r_p + r_a)/2 = (7171 + 36371)/2 = 21{,}771$ km
- Velocity needed at apogee: $v_a = \sqrt{\mu(2/r_a - 1/a)}$

$$v_a = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^5 \left(\frac{2}{36{,}371} - \frac{1}{21{,}771}\right)} = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^5 \times (5.498 \times 10^{-5} - 4.594 \times 10^{-5})}$$

$$v_a = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^5 \times 9.04 \times 10^{-6}} = \sqrt{3.603} \approx 1.898 \text{ km/s}$$

**Answer:** $v_a \approx 1.90$ km/s.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> *Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B includes sub-questions of 7 marks each. Two full question choices are provided for maximum practice utility.*

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**(a) Derive the Keplerian Orbital Equation $r = p / (1 + e \cos\nu)$ starting from Newton's law of gravitation. State the physical meaning of each term in the final equation. (7 marks)**

**(b) A satellite is in an elliptical orbit with the perigee and apogee distances from Earth's center being 7000 km and 42,000 km, respectively. Compute the (i) eccentricity, (ii) semi-major axis, (iii) orbital period, and (iv) specific angular momentum of the orbit. (7 marks)**

### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Starting equation:** Newton's Universal Law of Gravitation between Earth (mass $M_e$) and satellite (mass $m$) at distance $r$:

$$F = \frac{G M_e m}{r^2} = \frac{\mu m}{r^2}$$

In polar coordinates, the acceleration components are:

$$a_r = \ddot{r} - r\dot{\nu}^2, \quad a_\nu = r\ddot{\nu} + 2\dot{r}\dot{\nu}$$

Setting $F = ma$ in polar form gives two scalar equations:

$$\ddot{r} - r\dot{\nu}^2 = -\frac{\mu}{r^2} \quad (1)$$

$$r\ddot{\nu} + 2\dot{r}\dot{\nu} = 0 \quad (2)$$

From (2), $d/dt(r^2 \dot{\nu}) = 0$, so $r^2 \dot{\nu} = h$ (constant). Substituting $\dot{\nu} = h/r^2$ and changing the independent variable from $t$ to $\nu$, we obtain the second-order ODE:

$$\frac{d^2u}{d\nu^2} + u = \frac{\mu}{h^2} \quad \text{where } u = \frac{1}{r}$$

The general solution is $u = A\cos\nu + B\sin\nu + \mu/h^2$. Choosing the coordinate system such that perigee corresponds to $\nu = 0$ (where $\dot{r} = 0$), we get $B = 0$. Defining $A = e \cdot \mu/h^2$, we obtain:

$$\frac{1}{r} = \frac{\mu}{h^2}(1 + e\cos\nu) \implies r = \frac{h^2/\mu}{1 + e\cos\nu} = \frac{p}{1 + e\cos\nu}$$

**Physical meaning:**
- $r$: instantaneous distance from Earth's center to the satellite.
- $p = h^2/\mu$: semi-latus rectum, a geometric property of the conic.
- $e$: eccentricity, defines the orbit shape ($e = 0$: circle, $0 < e < 1$: ellipse).
- $\nu$: true anomaly, the angle from perigee to the satellite's current position.

### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $r_p = 7000$ km, $r_a = 42{,}000$ km.

**(i) Eccentricity:**

$$e = \frac{r_a - r_p}{r_a + r_p} = \frac{42{,}000 - 7000}{42{,}000 + 7000} = \frac{35{,}000}{49{,}000} = 0.7143$$

**(ii) Semi-major axis:**

$$a = \frac{r_a + r_p}{2} = \frac{42{,}000 + 7000}{2} = 24{,}500 \text{ km}$$

**(iii) Orbital Period (Kepler's Third Law):**

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{(24{,}500)^3}{3.986 \times 10^5}}$$

$$= 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{1.4706 \times 10^{13}}{3.986 \times 10^5}} = 2\pi\sqrt{3.690 \times 10^7} = 2\pi \times 6074.5 = 38{,}161 \text{ s}$$

$$\approx 10.6 \text{ hours}$$

**(iv) Specific Angular Momentum:**

$$h = \sqrt{\mu \cdot a (1 - e^2)} = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^5 \times 24{,}500 \times (1 - 0.5102)}$$

$$= \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^5 \times 24{,}500 \times 0.4898} = \sqrt{4.783 \times 10^9} \approx 69{,}160 \text{ km}^2/\text{s}$$

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024] — 14 Marks

**(a) State and prove the Vis-Viva equation. Show that for a circular orbit, the Vis-Viva equation reduces to the standard circular orbital velocity formula. (7 marks)**

**(b) A geostationary satellite is at an altitude of 35,786 km above Earth's surface. Calculate (i) its orbital velocity, (ii) its orbital period (compare with sidereal day), and (iii) the escape velocity at that altitude. (7 marks)**

### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Statement:** For a satellite in any Keplerian orbit with semi-major axis $a$ at instantaneous distance $r$ from the central body, the square of its orbital speed is:

$$v^2 = \mu \left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)$$

**Proof:** The total specific mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic and gravitational potential energies:

$$\mathcal{E} = \frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} \quad \text{(constant along the orbit)}$$

For an elliptical orbit, evaluating $\mathcal{E}$ at perigee (where $r_p = a(1-e)$ and velocity is purely tangential with $v_p = h/r_p$) and using $h^2 = \mu a(1-e^2)$:

$$\mathcal{E} = \frac{h^2}{2a^2(1-e)^2} - \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)} = \frac{\mu a(1-e^2)}{2a^2(1-e)^2} - \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)}$$

$$= \frac{\mu(1+e)}{2a(1-e)} - \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)} = \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)} \cdot \frac{(1+e) - 2}{2} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

Setting this equal to the general expression for $\mathcal{E}$:

$$\frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = -\frac{\mu}{2a} \implies v^2 = \mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)$$

**Circular orbit reduction:** For a circular orbit, $a = r$ (constant radius), so:

$$v^2 = \mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{r}\right) = \frac{\mu}{r} \implies v = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}$$

This is the standard formula for circular orbital velocity. ✓

### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $h_{GEO} = 35{,}786$ km, so $r_{GEO} = R_e + h = 6371 + 35{,}786 = 42{,}157$ km.

**(i) Orbital Velocity:**

$$v_{GEO} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^5}{42{,}157}} = \sqrt{9.456} \approx 3.075 \text{ km/s}$$

**(ii) Orbital Period:**

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{r^3}{\mu}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{(42{,}157)^3}{3.986 \times 10^5}} = 2\pi\sqrt{1.879 \times 10^8} = 2\pi \times 13{,}707.6 \approx 86{,}164 \text{ s}$$

This is **23 h 56 min 4 s**, which equals one **sidereal day** — confirming that the satellite is geostationary (it rotates in sync with Earth's rotation, as viewed from the ground).

**(iii) Escape Velocity:**

$$v_{esc} = \sqrt{\frac{2\mu}{r}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot v_{GEO} = 1.4142 \times 3.075 \approx 4.348 \text{ km/s}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Module 1):**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in μ:** Most students use $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s² in formulas while computing radii in km. ALWAYS be consistent: if $r$ is in km, use $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^5$ km³/s². Mixing SI and CGS units is the **#1 reason** for numerical errors in board exams.
>
> 2. **Altitude vs. Orbital Radius confusion:** The orbit radius $r$ is measured from **Earth's center**, NOT the surface. Always add $R_e = 6371$ km to the altitude before plugging into the formula. Forgetting this loses 2–3 marks per question.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the "+1" in the orbital equation:** The equation is $r = p / (1 + e \cos\nu)$, **not** $r = p / (e \cos\nu)$. The "+1" is essential — it makes the equation reduce to $r = a$ at $\nu = 90°$, which is the geometric mean property of an ellipse.
>
> 4. **Sign error in energy:** Bound orbits have **negative** total energy ($\mathcal{E} = -\mu / 2a$). A positive sign is a fatal error and indicates confusion between bound and unbound (hyperbolic) trajectories.
>
> 5. **Eccentricity interpretation:** A common error is computing $e$ as $(r_a + r_p)/(r_a - r_p)$ — the correct formula is $e = (r_a - r_p)/(r_a + r_p)$. The numerator is the **difference**, the denominator is the **sum**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to square angular momentum:** The Vis-Viva equation is derived for *specific* (per-unit-mass) angular momentum. When using $h = r_p v_p$ for a satellite of mass $m$, divide by $m$ first. Most KTU problems are phrased per unit mass, so $h$ is directly $r v$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Satellite Orbit and Orbital Equations"**

- ☐ **Kepler's First Law** → Orbit is an ellipse with central body at one focus.
- ☐ **Kepler's Second Law** → Equal areas in equal times → Conservation of angular momentum ($h = r^2 \dot{\nu}$ = constant).
- ☐ **Kepler's Third Law** → $T^2 = (4\pi^2/\mu) a^3$ — tested in nearly every numerical.
- ☐ **Newton's Gravitation** → $F = G M_e m / r^2$ — provides centripetal force for the orbit.
- ☐ **Orbital Equation** → $r = p/(1 + e\cos\nu)$ — conic section with focus at origin.
- ☐ **Semi-latus rectum** → $p = a(1-e^2) = h^2/\mu$.
- ☐ **Apogee** → $r_a = a(1+e)$; **Perigee** → $r_p = a(1-e)$.
- ☐ **Semi-major axis** → $a = (r_a + r_p)/2$.
- ☐ **Eccentricity** → $e = (r_a - r_p)/(r_a + r_p)$.
- ☐ **Vis-Viva Equation** → $v^2 = \mu(2/r - 1/a)$ — works for **any** Keplerian orbit.
- ☐ **Total specific energy** → $\mathcal{E} = -\mu/(2a)$ — always **negative** for bound orbits.
- ☐ **Circular orbit simplification** → $e = 0$, $a = r$, $v = \sqrt{\mu/r}$.
- ☐ **Escape velocity** → $v_{esc} = \sqrt{2\mu/r} = \sqrt{2} \cdot v_{orb}$.
- ☐ **Geostationary altitude** → 35,786 km above mean sea level; $T = 86,164$ s (one sidereal day).
- ☐ **Orbit types by e:** Circle (0), Ellipse (0<e<1), Parabola (e=1), Hyperbola (e>1).
- ☐ **Flight path angle** → $\tan\phi = e\sin\nu / (1 + e\cos\nu)$; $\phi = 0$ at perigee and apogee.
- ☐ **Constants to memorize:** $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^5$ km³/s²; $R_e = 6371$ km; $T_e = 86,164$ s.
- ☐ **For GEO:** Always verify $T = $ sidereal day as a sanity check on your calculations.
- ☐ **For GTO (Geostationary Transfer Orbit):** Typically $r_p \approx 6578$ km (LEO perigee), $r_a \approx 42,164$ km, $e \approx 0.73$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always show units in every step of your numerical solution. KTU examiners award partial marks for correct methodology with minor arithmetic errors — but **never** for the final answer alone. Write the formula first, substitute the values with units, and then compute — this is the KTU valuation key's "three-step golden rule."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion** are three fundamental scientific laws describing the motion of planets around the Sun, formulated by the German astronomer **Johannes Kepler** (1571–1630) between 1609 and 1619. These laws, derived empirically from Tycho Brahe's astronomical observations, became the cornerstone of classical orbital mechanics and form the foundational mathematical basis for modern **satellite communication engineering**, orbital maneuvering, and **trajectory analysis** in astrodynamics.

In the context of **Satellite and Radar Communication (PCECT751)**, Kepler's laws are the governing kinematic principles that describe how artificial Earth satellites, the Moon, and interplanetary probes move through space. They are mathematically equivalent to **Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation** under the simplifying assumption of a *two-body problem* (i.e., a single dominant central body and a much smaller satellite).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCECT751 – Module 1):**
> Kepler's laws MUST be studied in conjunction with **Newton's law of gravitation**, **orbital parameters** (apogee, perigee, eccentricity, semi-major axis), and the **two-body problem** formulation. Examiner questions often interlink Kepler's laws with orbital perturbations and satellite trajectory problems.

### 1.2 The Three Laws – At a Glance

| Law # | Year | Official Name | Core Statement |
|:-----:|:----:|:--------------|:---------------|
| 1st Law | 1609 | **Law of Ellipses** | The orbit of a planet (or satellite) is an **ellipse** with the central body (Sun/Earth) located at **one focus**. |
| 2nd Law | 1609 | **Law of Equal Areas** | The line joining the planet and the Sun sweeps out **equal areas in equal intervals of time**. |
| 3rd Law | 1619 | **Harmonic Law** | The square of the orbital period ($T^2$) is **directly proportional** to the cube of the semi-major axis ($a^3$) of the orbit. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are spinning a ball tied to a string in a circle.**
> - If the string were elastic and you stretched/compressed it rhythmically, the ball would trace an **ellipse** with your hand at one focus → **Kepler's First Law**.
> - When the string is short (ball close to hand), it moves **fast**; when the string is long (ball far from hand), it moves **slow** — but the "area swept" per second is the same → **Kepler's Second Law**.
> - A longer string (larger orbit) means it takes much longer to complete one revolution, and this relationship is mathematically fixed: $T^2 \propto a^3$ → **Kepler's Third Law**.

In satellite communication, we simply replace "your hand" with the **Earth** and "the ball" with the **satellite**. The string represents **gravitational force** instead of mechanical tension.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical constants are **mandatory** for solving KTU numerical problems on Kepler's laws:

- **Universal Gravitational Constant:** $G = 6.674 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2$
- **Standard Gravitational Parameter of Earth:** $\mu = GM_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$
- **Mean Radius of Earth:** $R_E = 6{,}371 \, \text{km}$ (or $6.371 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}$)
- **Mass of Earth:** $M_E = 5.972 \times 10^{24} \, \text{kg}$
- **Mass of Sun:** $M_S = 1.989 \times 10^{30} \, \text{kg}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Kepler's First Law — The Law of Ellipses

#### 2.1.1 Operational Concept
Every planet (and by extension, every satellite) revolves around its primary body in an **elliptical orbit**. The primary body is positioned at **one of the two foci** of the ellipse, *not* at the geometric center. The empty focus has no physical significance.

#### 2.1.2 Geometric Construction of the Ellipse

An ellipse is defined as the locus of points such that the **sum of distances** to two fixed points (the foci) is **constant** and equal to $2a$, where $a$ is the **semi-major axis**.

Let the two foci be $F_1$ (occupied by Earth/Sun) and $F_2$ (empty). For any point $P$ on the ellipse:

$$r_1 + r_2 = 2a$$

where:
- $r_1$ = distance from $F_1$ to point $P$ (the satellite)
- $r_2$ = distance from $F_2$ to point $P$

#### 2.1.3 Orbital Points of Engineering Importance
- **Perigee (for Earth satellites) / Perihelion (for planets around Sun):** Closest point to the central body. Distance = $r_p = a(1-e)$.
- **Apogee (for Earth satellites) / Aphelion (for planets around Sun):** Farthest point from the central body. Distance = $r_a = a(1+e)$.
- **Semi-major axis:** $a = (r_a + r_p)/2$
- **Semi-minor axis:** $b = a\sqrt{1-e^2}$

where $e$ is the **orbital eccentricity**, with $0 \leq e < 1$ for bound (closed) orbits. A **circular orbit** is the special case where $e = 0$.

#### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility
- **Geostationary orbit selection:** Engineers use Kepler's 1st law to compute the precise elliptical transfer orbit (Hohmann transfer) when moving a satellite from a low Earth parking orbit to a geostationary orbit at 35,786 km.
- **GPS constellation design:** Each GPS satellite traces a near-circular ($e \approx 0$) orbit, but small residual ellipticity must be accounted for using Kepler's geometry.

---

### 2.2 Kepler's Second Law — The Law of Equal Areas

#### 2.2.1 Operational Concept
As a satellite moves along its elliptical orbit, the **radius vector** (imaginary line joining the satellite to the central body) sweeps out **equal areas in equal times**. This means:
- At **perigee** (closest approach) → satellite moves **fastest**.
- At **apogee** (farthest point) → satellite moves **slowest**.

#### 2.2.2 Mathematical Foundation — Conservation of Angular Momentum
Kepler's 2nd law is a direct consequence of the **conservation of angular momentum** ($L = mvr$) in a central force field. The gravitational force always points along the radius vector, producing **zero torque** about the central body.

$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F} = 0 \implies \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = 0 \implies L = \text{constant}$$

The **areal velocity** (rate of area sweep) is given by:

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{L}{2m} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\mu a(1-e^2)} = \text{constant}$$

#### 2.2.3 Real-World Utility
- **Doppler shift compensation in satellite links:** Because the satellite's velocity changes throughout its orbit, the received frequency drifts. Kepler's 2nd law provides the velocity profile required for ground-station tracking and Doppler pre-correction.
- **LEO constellation management:** Low Earth Orbit satellites (e.g., Starlink) spend most of their time at apogee where they are slower, which affects handoff timing between ground stations.

---

### 2.3 Kepler's Third Law — The Harmonic Law

#### 2.3.1 Operational Concept
The square of the orbital period $T$ is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis $a$:

$$T^2 \propto a^3$$

The constant of proportionality is determined by the mass of the central body and Newton's gravitational constant.

#### 2.3.2 The Generalized Newton's Form
For an orbit around a central body of mass $M$:

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{GM}a^3 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu}a^3$$

For an Earth-orbiting satellite, using $\mu = GM_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$:

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}}$$

#### 2.3.3 Real-World Utility
- **Orbital period prediction for Geostationary Satellites:** Substituting $a = 42{,}164 \, \text{km}$ (geostationary radius) gives $T = 23\text{h } 56\text{m } 4\text{s}$ (one sidereal day), which is precisely why GEO satellites appear stationary relative to Earth.
- **Mission planning for deep-space probes:** Used to compute transfer times to Mars, Jupiter, etc.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the following table. It covers ~90% of KTU numerical questions on Kepler's laws.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units (SI) |
|:-:|:-------------|:---------|:---------------|:-----------|
| 1 | Kepler's 3rd Law (Newton form) | $T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu}a^3$ | $T$=period, $a$=semi-major axis, $\mu=GM$ | $T$ in s, $a$ in m |
| 2 | Semi-major axis from apsides | $a = \frac{r_a + r_p}{2}$ | $r_a$=apogee, $r_p$=perigee distance | meters |
| 3 | Orbital eccentricity | $e = \frac{r_a - r_p}{r_a + r_p}$ | Dimensionless ratio | unitless |
| 4 | Perigee distance | $r_p = a(1-e)$ | Closest approach | meters |
| 5 | Apogee distance | $r_a = a(1+e)$ | Farthest distance | meters |
| 6 | Semi-minor axis | $b = a\sqrt{1-e^2}$ | Conjugate axis / 2 | meters |
| 7 | Specific angular momentum | $h = \sqrt{\mu a(1-e^2)}$ | $h = L/m$ | m²/s |
| 8 | Areal velocity (constant) | $\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{h}{2}$ | Rate of area swept | m²/s |
| 9 | Vis-viva equation | $v^2 = \mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)$ | $v$=orbital speed at radius $r$ | m/s |
| 10 | Orbital period (Earth sat) | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}}$ | Sidereal period | seconds |
| 11 | Circular orbit velocity | $v_c = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}$ | For $e=0$ | m/s |
| 12 | Escape velocity | $v_{esc} = \sqrt{\frac{2\mu}{r}}$ | Parabolic trajectory | m/s |
| 13 | Geostationary orbit radius | $a_{GEO} = \left(\mu T_{sid}^2 / 4\pi^2\right)^{1/3}$ | $\approx 42{,}164$ km | meters |

**Conversion reminders used in KTU problems:**
- $1 \, \text{AU} = 1.496 \times 10^{11} \, \text{m}$ (Earth–Sun distance)
- $1 \, \text{sidereal day} = 86{,}164 \, \text{s}$
- $R_E = 6{,}371 \, \text{km}$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Kepler's Third Law from Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation

This derivation is a **high-weightage KTU question** (often worth 5–7 marks) and must be memorized with full logical continuity.

#### 3.1.1 Starting Premise — Newton's Law of Gravitation
Consider a satellite of mass $m$ orbiting a central body of mass $M$ in a **circular orbit** of radius $r$ (we begin with circular for simplicity, then extend to elliptical using $a$).

The gravitational force acting on the satellite is:

$$F_g = \frac{GMm}{r^2}$$

#### 3.1.2 Equating to Centripetal Force
For a stable circular orbit, the gravitational force must provide exactly the centripetal force required for circular motion:

$$F_c = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

Equating the two:

$$\frac{GMm}{r^2} = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

#### 3.1.3 Solving for Orbital Velocity
Cancel $m$ from both sides and multiply by $r$:

$$\frac{GM}{r} = v^2$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{r}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}$$

This is the **circular orbital velocity** formula.

#### 3.1.4 Expressing Velocity in Terms of Period
The orbital period $T$ is the time taken to traverse the circumference $2\pi r$:

$$v = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{2\pi r}{T}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{2\pi r}{T} = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{r}}$$

#### 3.1.5 Isolating the Period
Square both sides:

$$\frac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2} = \frac{GM}{r}$$

Cross-multiply:

$$4\pi^2 r^2 \cdot r = GM \cdot T^2$$

$$4\pi^2 r^3 = GM \cdot T^2$$

#### 3.1.6 Final Form
Rearrange to obtain Kepler's Third Law:

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{GM}r^3$$

For an **elliptical orbit**, replace $r$ (constant radius) with the **semi-major axis** $a$ (the *average* orbital radius):

$$\boxed{T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu}a^3}$$

This confirms that **$T^2$ is proportional to $a^3$**, with proportionality constant $\frac{4\pi^2}{\mu}$ that depends only on the central body's mass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does $r$ become $a$ in the elliptical case?**
> Newton's derivation above was done for circular orbits. The generalization to ellipses comes from **Newton's proof** that the period of an elliptical orbit depends only on the semi-major axis — a result derivable via the **conservation of energy** and **Kepler's 2nd law**. For KTU exams, you may simply state: *"By Newton's generalization, $r \to a$ for elliptical orbits."*

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Vis-Viva Equation (High-Yield Analytical Tool)

The **vis-viva equation** gives the orbital speed of a satellite at any point in its elliptical orbit, given its current distance $r$ from the central body and the orbit's semi-major axis $a$.

#### 3.2.1 Energy Conservation Setup
The **total mechanical energy** $E$ of the satellite (per unit mass) is the sum of kinetic and potential energies:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}v^2 - \frac{\mu}{r}$$

By the conservation of energy, $E$ is constant throughout the orbit.

#### 3.2.2 Energy in Terms of Semi-Major Axis
For an elliptical orbit, the total specific energy is known to be:

$$E = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

This is a standard result from orbital mechanics (derivable via integration or by evaluating at apsides).

#### 3.2.3 Equating Both Expressions
Set the two expressions for $E$ equal:

$$\frac{1}{2}v^2 - \frac{\mu}{r} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

#### 3.2.4 Solving for $v^2$
Rearrange:

$$\frac{1}{2}v^2 = \frac{\mu}{r} - \frac{\mu}{2a}$$

$$\frac{1}{2}v^2 = \mu\left(\frac{1}{r} - \frac{1}{2a}\right)$$

$$v^2 = \mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)$$

$$\boxed{v = \sqrt{\mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)}}$$

This is the **vis-viva equation**.

#### 3.2.5 Verification at Special Points
- **At perigee** ($r = r_p = a(1-e)$):

$$v_p = \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}}$$

- **At apogee** ($r = r_a = a(1+e)$):

$$v_a = \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1-e)}{a(1+e)}}$$

- **For circular orbit** ($r = a$):

$$v_c = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{a}}$$ ✓ *(matches our earlier result)*

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Problem Scenario | Applicable Formula(s) | Key Given Quantities |
|:-----------------|:---------------------|:---------------------|
| Find orbital period from altitude | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{a^3/\mu}$ | Altitude $h$, $a = R_E + h$ |
| Find satellite speed at apogee/perigee | Vis-viva with $r = r_a$ or $r_p$ | $a$, $e$, $\mu$ |
| Find eccentricity from altitude extremes | $e = (r_a - r_p)/(r_a + r_p)$ | Apogee and perigee altitudes |
| Find areal velocity | $dA/dt = h/2 = \sqrt{\mu a(1-e^2)}/2$ | $a$, $e$, $\mu$ |
| Compare periods of two planets/satellites | $T_1^2/T_2^2 = a_1^3/a_2^3$ | Two semi-major axes |
| Circular orbit velocity | $v = \sqrt{\mu/r}$ | Radius $r$ |
| Verify bound orbit | $E < 0 \implies a > 0$ | Total energy sign |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Geometric Architecture of an Elliptical Orbit (Kepler's 1st Law)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams the spatial geometry of an elliptical orbit as required for KTU answer sheets. A hand-drawn version with the eccentricity and apsides labeled is also expected in exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["F1 EARTH OR SUN AT FOCUS 1"]
    F2["F2 EMPTY FOCUS 2"]
    P["P PERIGEE OR PERIHELION CLOSEST POINT"]
    A["A APOGEE OR APHELION FARTHEST POINT"]
    C["C GEOMETRIC CENTER OF ELLIPSE"]
    V1["V1 VERTEX OF MAJOR AXIS"]
    V2["V2 VERTEX OF MAJOR AXIS"]
    MINOR["SEMI MINOR AXIS B"]
    MAJOR["SEMI MAJOR AXIS A"]

    F1 --- C
    F2 --- C
    V1 --- V2
    P --- F1
    A --- F1
    F1 -.- MAJOR
    C -.- MAJOR
    MINOR -.- V1
    MINOR -.- V2
```

**Key geometric labels for exam diagrams:**
- Distance from center $C$ to either focus: $c = ae$
- Distance from center to vertex: $a$
- $\text{Foci separation} = 2c = 2ae$

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow of Kepler's Three Laws in Satellite Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT["GIVEN ORBITAL STATE R VECTOR AND V VECTOR"]
    K1["KEPLER 1 LAW ELLIPSE WITH PRIMARY AT ONE FOCUS"]
    ORBPARAMS["ORBITAL PARAMETERS A E I OMEGA W NU"]
    K2["KEPLER 2 LAW EQUAL AREAS IN EQUAL TIMES"]
    CONSANGMOM["CONSERVATION OF ANGULAR MOMENTUM H CONSTANT"]
    K3["KEPLER 3 LAW T SQUARED PROPORTIONAL TO A CUBED"]
    GRAV["NEWTON LAW OF GRAVITATION F G M m OVER R SQUARED"]

    INPUT --> K1
    K1 --> ORBPARAMS
    ORBPARAMS --> K2
    K2 --> CONSANGMOM
    ORBPARAMS --> K3
    K3 --> GRAV
    GRAV -.VALIDATES.-> K3
    GRAV -.VALIDATES.-> K1
    CONSANGMOM -.EXPLAINS.-> K2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **input** to any orbital analysis is the satellite's position $\vec{r}$ and velocity $\vec{v}$ vectors.
- **Kepler's 1st law** gives the *shape* of the orbit (ellipse → orbital parameters).
- **Kepler's 2nd law** governs *temporal evolution* of position (where the satellite is at time $t$) — physically grounded in angular momentum conservation.
- **Kepler's 3rd law** links *period* to *orbit size* — physically grounded in Newton's gravitation.
- Newton's law is the **unifying foundation** that mathematically validates all three empirical Kepler laws.

---

### 4.3 Areal Velocity Interpretation (Kepler's 2nd Law)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["TIME INTERVAL DELTA T AT PERIGEE"]
    T2["EQUAL TIME INTERVAL DELTA T AT APOGEE"]
    A1["SMALL SWEPT AREA D A1 SATELLITE MOVES FAST"]
    A2["LARGE SWEPT AREA D A2 SATELLITE MOVES SLOW"]
    EQ["D A1 = D A2 EQUAL AREAS IN EQUAL TIMES"]
    V1["HIGH SPEED V MAX AT PERIGEE"]
    V2["LOW SPEED V MIN AT APOGEE"]

    T1 --> A1
    T2 --> A2
    A1 --> EQ
    A2 --> EQ
    T1 --> V1
    T2 --> V2
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State and explain Kepler's three laws of planetary motion.**

**Model Answer:**

**Kepler's First Law (Law of Ellipses):** The orbit of every planet (or satellite) is an *ellipse* with the central body (Sun or Earth) located at one of the two foci. Mathematically, for any point on the ellipse, the sum of distances to the two foci equals $2a$, where $a$ is the semi-major axis.

**Kepler's Second Law (Law of Equal Areas):** The radius vector joining the planet and the Sun sweeps out *equal areas in equal intervals of time*. This implies areal velocity $dA/dt$ is constant, and is a direct consequence of the conservation of angular momentum in a central force field. The satellite moves fastest at perigee and slowest at apogee.

**Kepler's Third Law (Harmonic Law):** The square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis: $T^2 \propto a^3$. In Newton's form: $T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{GM}a^3$. This links the *size* of an orbit to its *time period*, and the proportionality constant depends only on the mass of the central body.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**An Earth satellite has a perigee altitude of 600 km and an apogee altitude of 1200 km. Calculate its orbital period.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given data:**
- Perigee altitude: $h_p = 600 \, \text{km}$ → $r_p = R_E + h_p = 6371 + 600 = 6971 \, \text{km} = 6.971 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}$
- Apogee altitude: $h_a = 1200 \, \text{km}$ → $r_a = R_E + h_a = 6371 + 1200 = 7571 \, \text{km} = 7.571 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}$
- Earth's standard gravitational parameter: $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute semi-major axis:**

$$a = \frac{r_a + r_p}{2} = \frac{7.571 \times 10^6 + 6.971 \times 10^6}{2} = 7.271 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Kepler's Third Law:**

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}}$$

$$a^3 = (7.271 \times 10^6)^3 = 3.8446 \times 10^{20} \, \text{m}^3$$

$$\frac{a^3}{\mu} = \frac{3.8446 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}} = 9.6462 \times 10^{5} \, \text{s}^2$$

$$\sqrt{9.6462 \times 10^5} = 982.15 \, \text{s}$$

$$T = 2\pi \times 982.15 = 6171.2 \, \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 6171 \, \text{s} \approx 102.85 \, \text{minutes}}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Pattern:** Part B questions on Kepler's laws are typically split into **(a) 7 marks — descriptive/derivation** and **(b) 7 marks — numerical application**. Both sub-parts must be answered with complete steps to secure full marks.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Derive Kepler's Third Law of planetary motion starting from Newton's law of universal gravitation. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b)** A geostationary satellite must have an orbital period equal to one sidereal day ($T = 86164$ s). Calculate the required orbital radius (semi-major axis) and verify whether the orbit is above or below the geostationary belt at 42164 km.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

**Assumptions:**
1. The central body (Sun/Earth) is spherically symmetric and much more massive than the satellite → it remains at one focus.
2. The orbit is approximately a two-body problem with no third-body perturbations.
3. Initially, we consider a circular orbit (radius $r$), then generalize to elliptical (replace $r$ by $a$).

**Derivation steps:**

**(Step 1)** Gravitational force on satellite of mass $m$ at distance $r$ from mass $M$:

$$F = \frac{GMm}{r^2}$$

**(Step 2)** For circular motion, equate to centripetal force:

$$\frac{GMm}{r^2} = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

**(Step 3)** Simplify and solve for orbital velocity $v$:

$$v^2 = \frac{GM}{r}$$

**(Step 4)** Express velocity in terms of period $T$ using $v = 2\pi r / T$:

$$\left(\frac{2\pi r}{T}\right)^2 = \frac{GM}{r}$$

**(Step 5)** Expand and rearrange:

$$\frac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2} = \frac{GM}{r} \implies 4\pi^2 r^3 = GM \cdot T^2$$

**(Step 6)** Final form of Kepler's Third Law (for circular orbit):

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{GM}r^3 \quad \text{or} \quad T^2 \propto r^3$$

**(Step 7)** For elliptical orbit, replace $r$ with semi-major axis $a$ (Newton's generalization):

$$\boxed{T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu}a^3, \quad \text{where } \mu = GM}$$

**Conclusion:** The period depends only on the semi-major axis and the mass of the central body, not on the satellite's mass or the orbit's eccentricity.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Orbital period: $T = 86164 \, \text{s}$ (one sidereal day)
- $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$

**Step 1 — Solve Kepler's 3rd Law for $a$:**

$$a^3 = \frac{\mu T^2}{4\pi^2}$$

$$T^2 = (86164)^2 = 7.4242 \times 10^9 \, \text{s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$a^3 = \frac{(3.986 \times 10^{14})(7.4242 \times 10^9)}{4\pi^2}$$

$$a^3 = \frac{2.9593 \times 10^{24}}{39.4784} = 7.4961 \times 10^{22} \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Take cube root:**

$$a = (7.4961 \times 10^{22})^{1/3} = 4.2164 \times 10^7 \, \text{m}$$

$$a = 42{,}164 \, \text{km}$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**
The computed semi-major axis $a = 42164 \, \text{km}$ matches the standard geostationary belt radius. Hence the orbit lies **exactly on the geostationary belt** (a circular GEO orbit requires $e = 0$, $a = 42164 \, \text{km}$).

$$\boxed{a_{GEO} = 42{,}164 \, \text{km}}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the geometric construction of an elliptical orbit as required by Kepler's First Law. Define perigee, apogee, semi-major axis, semi-minor axis, and eccentricity with proper mathematical relationships.

**(b)** An Earth-orbiting satellite has an eccentricity of 0.2 and a perigee altitude of 500 km. Determine: (i) the apogee altitude, (ii) the orbital period, and (iii) the velocity at perigee and apogee.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Geometric Description [7 Marks]

An **ellipse** is the locus of all points $P$ such that the sum of distances from two fixed points (foci $F_1$ and $F_2$) is constant and equals $2a$. For an orbiting satellite:

- **Foci:** Two fixed points. The central body (Earth/Sun) is at $F_1$; $F_2$ is empty.
- **Perigee ($P$):** Closest point to $F_1$. Distance $r_p = a(1-e)$.
- **Apogee ($A$):** Farthest point from $F_1$. Distance $r_a = a(1+e)$.
- **Semi-major axis ($a$):** Half the longest diameter. $a = (r_a + r_p)/2$.
- **Semi-minor axis ($b$):** Half the shortest diameter. $b = a\sqrt{1-e^2}$.
- **Eccentricity ($e$):** Measure of ellipse elongation. $e = c/a$, where $c$ is the focus–center distance.

**Key relationships:**

$$r_p = a(1-e), \quad r_a = a(1+e)$$

$$a = \frac{r_a + r_p}{2}, \quad e = \frac{r_a - r_p}{r_a + r_p}$$

For a **circular orbit**, $e = 0$, and $r_p = r_a = a$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Eccentricity: $e = 0.2$
- Perigee altitude: $h_p = 500 \, \text{km}$ → $r_p = 6371 + 500 = 6871 \, \text{km} = 6.871 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}$
- $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$

**(i) Apogee altitude:**

$$r_a = r_p \cdot \frac{1+e}{1-e} = 6.871 \times 10^6 \times \frac{1.2}{0.8} = 6.871 \times 10^6 \times 1.5 = 1.0307 \times 10^7 \, \text{m}$$

$$r_a = 10{,}307 \, \text{km} \implies h_a = 10307 - 6371 = 3936 \, \text{km}$$

**Semi-major axis:**

$$a = \frac{r_a + r_p}{2} = \frac{10307 + 6871}{2} = 8589 \, \text{km} = 8.589 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}$$

**(ii) Orbital period:**

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{(8.589 \times 10^6)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$$a^3 = 6.336 \times 10^{20} \, \text{m}^3$$

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{1.5897 \times 10^6} = 2\pi \times 1260.99 = 7923.3 \, \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 7923 \, \text{s} \approx 132.05 \, \text{minutes}}$$

**(iii) Velocity at perigee and apogee (Vis-Viva equation):**

$$v = \sqrt{\mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)}$$

**At perigee ($r = r_p = 6.871 \times 10^6$ m):**

$$v_p = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14}\left(\frac{2}{6.871 \times 10^6} - \frac{1}{8.589 \times 10^6}\right)}$$

$$v_p = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14}(2.9102 \times 10^{-7} - 1.1643 \times 10^{-7})}$$

$$v_p = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 1.7459 \times 10^{-7}} = \sqrt{6.959 \times 10^7}$$

$$\boxed{v_p \approx 8342 \, \text{m/s}}$$

**At apogee ($r = r_a = 1.0307 \times 10^7$ m):**

$$v_a = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14}\left(\frac{2}{1.0307 \times 10^7} - \frac{1}{8.589 \times 10^6}\right)}$$

$$v_a = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14}(1.9404 \times 10^{-7} - 1.1643 \times 10^{-7})}$$

$$v_a = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 7.761 \times 10^{-8}} = \sqrt{3.094 \times 10^7}$$

$$\boxed{v_a \approx 5562 \, \text{m/s}}$$

**Sanity check:** $v_p > v_a$ ✓ (consistent with Kepler's 2nd law — satellite moves faster at perigee).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in Kepler's law problems — KTU Examiner's Perspective:**
>
> 1. **Confusing altitude with orbital radius** *(–2 marks)*: KTU questions often give "altitude above Earth's surface" but the formula needs the **radius from Earth's center**. Always compute $r = R_E + h$ first.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to use $\mu$ instead of $GM$ separately** *(–1 mark)*: When using $T^2 = (4\pi^2/GM)a^3$, students often plug in $G = 6.674 \times 10^{-11}$ and $M = 5.972 \times 10^{24}$ separately. This works but is error-prone. **Use $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$** for Earth satellites.
>
> 3. **Mixing up $e$ in the apsides formulas** *(–2 marks)*: $r_p = a(1-e)$ and $r_a = a(1+e)$ — students frequently swap them. Remember: $r_p$ corresponds to $(-e)$ and $r_a$ to $(+e)$.
>
> 4. **Not converting km to m in numerical problems** *(–1 mark)*: $\mu$ is in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$. If $a$ is in km, the answer will be off by a factor of $\sim 10^9$.
>
> 5. **Skipping assumptions in derivation** *(–1 to –2 marks)*: Always state the assumptions before deriving Kepler's 3rd law (central force, two-body, circular → elliptical generalization).
>
> 6. **Not drawing the orbital diagram** *(–1 mark in 14-mark questions)*: For Kepler's 1st law, KTU examiners expect a labeled diagram with foci, perigee, apogee, and semi-major/minor axes.
>
> 7. **Ignoring unit consistency in the vis-viva equation** *(–1 mark)*: $r$ and $a$ MUST be in the same units (both meters or both km) — do not mix.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Kepler's Laws (PCECT751, Module 1)**

- **Kepler's 1st Law:** Orbits are ellipses; central body at one focus; **NOT** at the center.
- **Kepler's 2nd Law:** Equal areas in equal times → $dA/dt$ is constant → conservation of angular momentum ($L = mvr$).
- **Kepler's 3rd Law:** $T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu}a^3$ → period depends ONLY on $a$ and $\mu$, not on $m$ or $e$.
- **Eccentricity:** $e = 0$ (circle), $0 < e < 1$ (ellipse), $e = 1$ (parabola), $e > 1$ (hyperbola).
- **Apsides:** Perigee = closest, Apogee = farthest. $r_p = a(1-e)$, $r_a = a(1+e)$.
- **Semi-major axis:** $a = (r_a + r_p)/2$ — the **most important** orbital parameter.
- **Vis-viva equation:** $v^2 = \mu(2/r - 1/a)$ — gives speed at any orbital position.
- **Earth constants to memorize:** $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$, $R_E = 6371 \, \text{km}$.
- **GEO orbit radius:** $a_{GEO} \approx 42{,}164 \, \text{km}$, period = 1 sidereal day (86164 s).
- **LEO altitude range:** 160 km – 2000 km, period ~88 – 127 minutes.
- **Velocity order:** $v_p > v_a$ (perigee faster than apogee) — confirms 2nd law.
- **Newton's role:** Kepler's laws are kinematical; Newton's gravitation is the dynamical cause.
- **Diagram must include:** Two foci, center $C$, perigee $P$, apogee $A$, semi-major axis $a$, semi-minor axis $b$, focus–center distance $c = ae$.
- **Common exam traps:** Altitude vs. radius, km vs. m, swapping $r_p$ and $r_a$, using $G \cdot M$ instead of $\mu$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Locating Satellite in the Orbit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Locating Satellite in the Orbit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Locating a satellite in its orbit** is the geometric and kinematic process of determining the precise position and velocity vector of an Earth-orbiting satellite at any given instant of time $t$, expressed in an Earth-Centred Earth-Fixed (ECEF) or Earth-Centred Inertial (ECI) reference frame. This is achieved by combining the six classical **Keplerian Orbital Elements** with the satellite's ground-track geometry, and then converting the in-plane coordinates into a real-world **look-angle** (azimuth and elevation) as seen from a known Earth station.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> Locating a satellite in orbit involves defining its position using orbital parameters such as the semi-major axis, eccentricity, inclination, argument of perigee, right ascension of ascending node, and true anomaly — collectively called the **Keplerian elements** — together with the satellite's sub-point latitude/longitude and the look angles (azimuth and elevation) from a ground station.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in your college campus and a friend asks: *"Where exactly is the drone flying right now?"* To answer, you need three things:

1. **A reference map of the sky** → the orbital plane and the ECI frame.
2. **Where the drone started and how it's tilted** → orbital elements (shape, orientation).
3. **Where it is on its loop right now** → true anomaly (its angular position from perigee).

Just as a courier on a circular route needs only *angle from start* and *distance from centre*, a satellite in an elliptical orbit needs its *shape parameters* and *current angular position*. Once you know these, you can point a dish antenna (knowing the station's coordinates) toward the sky and "see" the satellite — that's the **look angle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - **Standard gravitational parameter of Earth:** $\mu = GM_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$
> - **Mean radius of Earth:** $R_E = 6378.137\ \text{km}$
> - **One sidereal day:** $86164.0905\ \text{s}$ (used for Geostationary orbit)
> - **Geostationary orbital radius:** $r_{GEO} = 42164.17\ \text{km}$

## 1.3 Why Is This Topic Critical for KTU?

A satellite cannot communicate with Earth if we don't know **where** to point the antenna. Hence, *locating the satellite* is the foundational step that bridges **orbital mechanics** (Module 1) with **link design** (Module 2 onwards) and **antenna tracking** (Module 4).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Keplerian Orbital Elements

A satellite's location and orbit are fully described by **six independent parameters** (since the orbit exists in 3-D space and time gives the 4th dimension for motion).

| # | Element | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|--------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | Semi-major axis | $a$ | Half the longest axis of the ellipse (size) | km |
| 2 | Eccentricity | $e$ | Shape of orbit ($0$ = circle, $0<e<1$ = ellipse) | — |
| 3 | Inclination | $i$ | Tilt of orbital plane w.r.t. Earth's equator | degrees |
| 4 | Right Ascension of Ascending Node (RAAN) | $\Omega$ | Where orbit crosses equator going north, measured from Vernal equinox | degrees |
| 5 | Argument of Perigee | $\omega$ | Angle from ascending node to perigee, measured in orbital plane | degrees |
| 6 | True Anomaly | $\nu$ | Current angular position of satellite from perigee | degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The first five elements *define the orbit itself* (constants for a Keplerian orbit). The **true anomaly $\nu$** is the *time-varying* element that tells us **where the satellite is right now**.

## 2.2 Relationship Between Anomalies

A satellite on an elliptical orbit moves faster at perigee and slower at apogee. To handle this, three anomaly angles are used:

- **True Anomaly $\nu$** — geometric, the actual angle from perigee to satellite, measured at the focus.
- **Eccentric Anomaly $E$** — auxiliary angle measured at the centre of the ellipse from the major axis.
- **Mean Anomaly $M$** — uniform-time reference: $M = n(t - t_p)$, where $n$ is the mean motion.

### Kepler's Equation (The Master Equation of Orbital Mechanics)

$$
M = E - e \sin E
$$

This transcendental equation is solved iteratively for $E$ given $M$ and $e$.

### Conversion: $E \leftrightarrow \nu$

$$
\tan\!\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right) = \sqrt{\frac{1+e}{1-e}}\ \tan\!\left(\frac{E}{2}\right)
$$

### Time-Dependent Position (Mean Motion)

$$
n = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{a^{3}}}\quad \Longrightarrow \quad M = n\,(t - t_{p})
$$

where $t_p$ = time of perigee passage.

## 2.3 Satellite Position in the Orbital Plane

Once $\nu$ is known, the satellite's position $\vec{r}$ and velocity $\vec{v}$ in the perifocal frame ($P\!-\!Q\!-\!W$) are:

$$
r = \frac{a\,(1-e^{2})}{1 + e\cos\nu}
$$

$$
\vec{r}_{PQW} = r\cos\nu\,\hat{P} \;+\; r\sin\nu\,\hat{Q}
$$

$$
\vec{v}_{PQW} = -\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{a(1-e^{2})}}\sin\nu\,\hat{P} \;+\; \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{a(1-e^{2})}}\left(e + \cos\nu\right)\hat{Q}
$$

## 2.4 Look Angle Computation (Azimuth & Elevation)

For a ground station at latitude $\phi_e$ and longitude $\theta_e$ observing a satellite whose subsatellite point is at latitude $\phi_s$ and longitude $\theta_s$:

### Central Angle (geocentric) between station and subsatellite point:

$$
\lambda = \arccos\!\bigl[\sin\phi_e\,\sin\phi_s + \cos\phi_e\,\cos\phi_s\,\cos(\theta_s - \theta_e)\bigr]
$$

### Elevation Angle:

$$
\tan\varepsilon = \frac{\cos\lambda - \dfrac{R_E}{r}}{\sin\lambda}
$$

(Valid when $\varepsilon > 0$; otherwise satellite is below horizon.)

### Azimuth Angle:

$$
\tan A = \frac{\sin(\theta_s - \theta_e)}{\cos\phi_e\,\tan\phi_s - \sin\phi_e\,\cos(\theta_s - \theta_e)}
$$

### Slant Range (station-to-satellite distance):

$$
d = \sqrt{R_E^{2} + r^{2} - 2 R_E r \cos\lambda}
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Mean motion | $n = \sqrt{\mu/a^{3}}$ | rad/s |
| Kepler's equation | $M = E - e\sin E$ | Solve iteratively for $E$ |
| $E \to \nu$ | $\tan(\nu/2) = \sqrt{(1+e)/(1-e)}\ \tan(E/2)$ | $\nu$ in same quadrant as $E$ |
| Radius from focus | $r = a(1-e^{2})/(1+e\cos\nu)$ | km |
| Orbital period | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{a^{3}/\mu}$ | seconds |
| Geostationary orbit | $a_{GEO} = \left(\mu T_{sid}^{2}/(4\pi^{2})\right)^{1/3} \approx 42164$ km | $T_{sid} = 86164.09$ s |
| Central angle | $\lambda = \arccos[\sin\phi_e\sin\phi_s + \cos\phi_e\cos\phi_s\cos\Delta\theta]$ | $0 \le \lambda \le \pi$ |
| Elevation | $\tan\varepsilon = \dfrac{\cos\lambda - R_E/r}{\sin\lambda}$ | $\varepsilon > 0$ for visibility |
| Slant range | $d = \sqrt{R_E^{2} + r^{2} - 2R_E r \cos\lambda}$ | km |
| Subsatellite latitude | $\phi_s = \arcsin(\sin i \,\sin\nu_{orbit})$ | varies with time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For numerical problems in KTU exams**, always retain the sign of $\sin\lambda$ in the elevation formula as positive (since the denominator $\sin\lambda$ is non-negative for $\lambda \in [0, \pi]$). Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation only when needed; tables above already use parentheses.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TT&C (Telemetry, Tracking & Command)**: Every ISRO/GSAT ground station computes look angles in real time to track satellites.
- **Antenna Pointing Systems**: Drive motors on parabolic dishes use $A$ and $\varepsilon$ directly.
- **Collision Avoidance**: Space agencies share orbital element sets (TLE — Two-Line Element) to predict conjunctions.
- **Launch & Insertion**: Determines the precise burnout velocity required to reach the desired orbit.
- **Coverage Analysis**: Used to determine footprint and which regions the satellite can serve.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Kepler's Equation $M = E - e\sin E$

### Starting Point: Conservation of Areal Velocity (Kepler's 2nd Law)

The area swept by the radius vector in time $dt$ is $\tfrac{1}{2}r^{2}d\nu$. The total area of the ellipse is $\pi a b = \pi a^{2}\sqrt{1-e^{2}}$. Setting areal velocity constant:

$$
\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\mu a(1-e^{2})}
$$

### Step 1: Area from Perigee to the Satellite

The area $A$ swept from perigee to a point at eccentric anomaly $E$ is the sum of:
- Area of triangle formed by the focus-to-centre geometry: $\tfrac{1}{2} b a \sin E$ (subtended area)
- The triangular correction to convert from centre to focus: $\tfrac{1}{2} a b e \sin E$

So,

$$
A = \frac{a b}{2}\,(E - e\sin E)
$$

### Step 2: Equate to Fractional Time

The fraction of the orbital period is $t/T$:

$$
\frac{A}{\pi a b} = \frac{t - t_{p}}{T}
$$

Substituting $A$:

$$
\frac{E - e\sin E}{2\pi} = \frac{t - t_{p}}{T}
$$

### Step 3: Introduce Mean Anomaly

Define $M = n(t - t_p)$ where $n = 2\pi/T$. Then:

$$
\boxed{M = E - e\sin E}
$$

This is **Kepler's Equation**, the cornerstone of satellite location.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — True Anomaly from Eccentric Anomaly

### Geometry of the Ellipse

In the orbital plane, the satellite's position $(x, y)$ in the perifocal frame is:

$$
x = a\cos E - ae \quad ; \quad y = a\sqrt{1-e^{2}}\ \sin E
$$

### Step 1: Express True Anomaly

The true anomaly is the polar angle at the focus:

$$
\tan\nu = \frac{y}{x} = \frac{a\sqrt{1-e^{2}}\sin E}{a(\cos E - e)}
$$

### Step 2: Half-Angle Trigonometric Identity

Using the identities $\sin\nu = \dfrac{2\sin(\nu/2)\cos(\nu/2)}{1}$, $\cos\nu = \cos^{2}(\nu/2) - \sin^{2}(\nu/2)$ and the standard half-angle reductions, we obtain:

$$
\tan\!\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right) = \sqrt{\frac{1+e}{1-e}}\ \tan\!\left(\frac{E}{2}\right)
$$

This is the **canonical conversion** from eccentric to true anomaly.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Radius Vector $r$ in Terms of True Anomaly

### Step 1: Distance from the Centre

The distance from the **centre** of the ellipse to the satellite is:

$$
\rho = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}} = a\sqrt{(\cos E - e)^{2} + (1-e^{2})\sin^{2}E}
$$

Simplify inside the square root:

$$
(\cos E - e)^{2} + (1-e^{2})\sin^{2}E = 1 - 2e\cos E + e^{2}\cos^{2}E
$$

(after expanding and using $\sin^{2}E + \cos^{2}E = 1$)

### Step 2: Use Half-Angle to Remove $\cos E$

Recall $\cos E = 1 - 2\sin^{2}(E/2)$, so:

$$
1 - 2e\cos E + e^{2}\cos^{2}E = (1 - e\cos E)^{2}
$$

Therefore $\rho = a(1 - e\cos E)$.

### Step 3: Shift Origin from Centre to Focus

The focus is offset from the centre by $ae$ along the major axis. Using the geometry of the focus and the half-angle conversion:

$$
r = a(1 - e\cos E) = \frac{a(1-e^{2})}{1 + e\cos\nu}
$$

This is the **focal polar equation** of an ellipse in celestial mechanics.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Elevation Angle Formula

Consider a ground station $G$ at distance $R_E$ from Earth's centre $O$, and a satellite $S$ at distance $r$. The satellite is at elevation $\varepsilon$ above the local horizon at $G$.

### Step 1: Apply Cosine Rule in Triangle $OGS$

The angle at $O$ (central angle) is $\lambda$. The side $GS$ (slant range) is $d$. Then:

$$
d^{2} = R_E^{2} + r^{2} - 2R_E r \cos\lambda
$$

### Step 2: Resolve the Triangle at the Station

At station $G$, the angle between $GO$ and $GS$ is $(90^{\circ} + \varepsilon)$ (since elevation is measured above the local horizontal, and the local vertical is along $GO$).

Apply cosine rule again in triangle $OGS$ to find $\cos(90^{\circ} + \varepsilon) = -\sin\varepsilon$:

$$
r^{2} = R_E^{2} + d^{2} - 2R_E d\cos(90^{\circ} + \varepsilon) = R_E^{2} + d^{2} + 2R_E d \sin\varepsilon
$$

### Step 3: Eliminate $d$ and Solve for $\varepsilon$

Substituting the expression for $d^{2}$ and simplifying, we get:

$$
\boxed{\tan\varepsilon = \frac{\cos\lambda - R_E/r}{\sin\lambda}}
$$

(For visibility, $\cos\lambda > R_E/r$, i.e., $\varepsilon > 0$.)

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Flow for Locating a Satellite

| Step | Input | Computation | Output |
|------|-------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | $t$ (current time) | $M = n(t - t_p)$ | Mean anomaly |
| 2 | $M, e$ | Solve $M = E - e\sin E$ iteratively | Eccentric anomaly $E$ |
| 3 | $E, e$ | $\tan(\nu/2) = \sqrt{(1+e)/(1-e)}\ \tan(E/2)$ | True anomaly $\nu$ |
| 4 | $a, e, \nu$ | $r = a(1-e^{2})/(1+e\cos\nu)$ | Orbital radius |
| 5 | $\Omega, \omega, i, \nu$ | Rotation matrix $R_{ECI \leftarrow PQW}$ | ECI coordinates |
| 6 | $\phi_e, \theta_e$ | Earth rotation matrix | ECEF coordinates |
| 7 | ECEF $\vec{r}_S$, station $\vec{r}_G$ | $\vec{d} = \vec{r}_S - \vec{r}_G$ | Slant range vector |
| 8 | $\vec{d}$ | Spherical decomposition | $A, \varepsilon, d$ |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Locating a Satellite

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Time t] --> B[Compute Mean Anomaly M = n times t minus tp]
    B --> C[Keplers Equation: M = E minus e sin E]
    C --> D[Solve Iteratively for E]
    D --> E[Convert E to True Anomaly nu]
    E --> F[Compute Orbital Radius r from nu and a and e]
    F --> G[Transform Perifocal PQW to ECI Frame]
    G --> H[Rotate ECI to ECEF using Earth Rotation]
    H --> I[Subtract Ground Station Vector to Get Slant Range d]
    I --> J[Decompose d into Azimuth A and Elevation epsilon]
    J --> K[Output: A, epsilon, d]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Look-Angle Determination Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Orbital Elements Database] --> B1[Orbit Propagator SGP4 Model]
    A2[Ground Station Coordinates] --> C1[Reference Frame Converter ECEF]
    B1 --> D1[ECI Position Vector of Satellite]
    C1 --> D2[ECEF Position Vector of Station]
    D1 --> E1[Vector Subtraction]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Slant Range d]
    E1 --> F2[Azimuth A]
    E1 --> F3[Elevation epsilon]
    F1 --> G1[Antenna Controller]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Tracking Dish Antenna]
```

## 4.3 Coordinate Frame Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Perifocal Frame PQW] -->|Rotation by nu| R1[Radial Transverse Normal]
    R1 -->|Rotation by i, Omega, omega| R2[ECI Frame X Y Z]
    R2 -->|Earth Rotation by GMST| R3[ECEF Frame Xp Yp Zp]
    R3 -->|Latitude and Longitude| R4[Geodetic Coordinates]
    R4 --> R5[Sub-satellite Point lat lon]
    R5 --> R6[Look Angles A and epsilon]
```

## 4.4 Satellite Visibility Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Earth Centre O] --> B2[Station G on surface]
    A2 --> C2[Satellite S at radius r]
    B2 -->|Line of sight| C2
    B2 --> D2[Local Horizon Plane]
    C2 --> E2[Elevation Angle epsilon above horizon]
    B2 --> F2[Azimuth A measured clockwise from North]
    A2 --> G2[Central Angle lambda between OG and OS]
```
