## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Rotational Equations of Motion (High-Yield)

**Starting Premise (Newton's Second Law for Rotation in an Inertial Frame):**

$$\sum \vec{T}_{ext} = \left.\frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}\right|_{inertial}$$

**Step 1 – Express angular momentum $\vec{H}$ using the inertia tensor:**

The angular momentum of a rigid body in the body frame is given by the matrix equation:

$$\vec{H} = \begin{bmatrix} I_{xx} & I_{xy} & I_{xz} \\ I_{yx} & I_{yy} & I_{yz} \\ I_{zx} & I_{zy} & I_{zz} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_1 \\ \omega_2 \\ \omega_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 – Choose principal axes so the inertia tensor is diagonal:**

By a suitable orientation of the body axes, the products of inertia vanish ($I_{xy} = I_{yz} = I_{zx} = 0$). The inertia tensor reduces to:

$$\mathbf{I} = \begin{bmatrix} I_1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & I_2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & I_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $I_1, I_2, I_3$ are the **principal moments of inertia** about the body x, y, z axes.

**Step 3 – Apply the Transport Theorem (Coriolis frame correction):**

The time derivative of $\vec{H}$ measured in the inertial frame is related to the time derivative in the body (rotating) frame by:

$$\left.\frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}\right|_{inertial} = \left.\frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}\right|_{body} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{H}$$

The vector $\vec{\omega} = (\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3)$ is the angular velocity of the body frame relative to the inertial frame.

**Step 4 – Substitute $\vec{H} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}$:**

Since the inertia tensor is constant in the body frame, we have:

$$\left.\frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}\right|_{body} = \mathbf{I}\,\dot{\vec{\omega}}$$

Therefore:

$$\sum \vec{T}_{ext} = \mathbf{I}\,\dot{\vec{\omega}} + \vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\,\vec{\omega})$$

**Step 5 – Expand the cross product in scalar component form:**

Computing the cross product $\vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}) = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{H}$ where $H_i = I_i \omega_i$:

$$(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{H})_1 = \omega_2 H_3 - \omega_3 H_2 = \omega_2 I_3 \omega_3 - \omega_3 I_2 \omega_2 = (I_3 - I_2)\omega_2 \omega_3$$

**Step 6 – Write the three scalar Euler's equations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= I_1 \dot{\omega}_1 + (I_3 - I_2)\,\omega_2 \omega_3 \\
T_2 &= I_2 \dot{\omega}_2 + (I_1 - I_3)\,\omega_3 \omega_1 \\
T_3 &= I_3 \dot{\omega}_3 + (I_2 - I_1)\,\omega_1 \omega_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Convention used in this derivation:** Many textbooks write the terms as $-(I_2 - I_3)\omega_2\omega_3$ — this is algebraically identical to $+(I_3 - I_2)\omega_2\omega_3$; both forms are correct as long as the sign convention on the RHS of the cross product is consistent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The terms $\omega_2\omega_3$, $\omega_3\omega_1$, and $\omega_1\omega_2$ are called **gyroscopic coupling terms**. They show that rotation about one axis induces torques on the other axes — this is exactly why a spinning top wobbles (precesses) and why a spinning satellite must be analyzed in coupled form.

### 3.2 Derivation: Reaction Wheel Control Torque

A reaction wheel is a **momentum exchange device**. The total angular momentum of the *closed system* (satellite + wheel) is conserved if no external torque acts.

**Step 1 – Define the total system angular momentum:**

$$\vec{H}_{total} = \vec{H}_{satellite} + \vec{H}_{wheel} = \mathbf{I}_s\vec{\omega}_s + I_w\vec{\omega}_w$$

where $I_w$ and $\omega_w$ are the wheel's moment of inertia and angular speed about its spin axis.

**Step 2 – Apply conservation of angular momentum (no external torque):**

$$\frac{d}{dt}(\mathbf{I}_s\vec{\omega}_s + I_w\vec{\omega}_w) = 0$$

**Step 3 – Differentiate:**

$$\mathbf{I}_s\dot{\vec{\omega}}_s + I_w\dot{\vec{\omega}}_w = 0$$

**Step 4 – The wheel torque on the satellite is the reaction to the wheel's acceleration:**

$$T_w = I_w\dot{\omega}_w = -\mathbf{I}_s\dot{\vec{\omega}}_s$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{T_w = I_w \dot{\omega}_w = -\dot{h}_w}$$

**Physical interpretation:** To rotate the satellite body (cause $\dot{\vec{\omega}}_s \neq 0$), the wheel motor must apply a torque that **accelerates or decelerates** the wheel. The wheel's reaction torque acts back on the satellite structure through its mounting bearings, producing the desired control torque.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Trick:** If a question says "explain reaction wheel control" – the *core* keyword examiners look for is **momentum exchange**, **gyroscopic coupling**, and the equation $T_w = -\dot{h}_w$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Hohmann Transfer Δv Equations

A **Hohmann transfer** is the most fuel-efficient two-burn maneuver to transfer a satellite between two coplanar circular orbits. It uses a half-ellipse (transfer orbit) tangent to both circular orbits.

**Setup:** Initial circular orbit radius = $r_1$, Final circular orbit radius = $r_2$ (assume $r_2 > r_1$).

**Step 1 – Compute the velocities on the initial circular orbit:**

$$v_{c1} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r_1}}$$

**Step 2 – Compute the semi-major axis of the transfer ellipse:**

The transfer ellipse has perigee at $r_1$ and apogee at $r_2$:

$$a_t = \frac{r_1 + r_2}{2}$$

**Step 3 – Apply the Vis-Viva equation at the perigee of the transfer orbit:**

Vis-Viva: $v^2 = \mu\left(\dfrac{2}{r} - \dfrac{1}{a}\right)$

At perigee ($r = r_1$, $a = a_t$):

$$v_p = \sqrt{\mu\left(\frac{2}{r_1} - \frac{1}{a_t}\right)} = \sqrt{\frac{2\mu\, r_2}{r_1(r_1+r_2)}}$$

**Step 4 – First Δv (perigee burn, prograde):**

$$\Delta v_1 = v_p - v_{c1} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r_1}}\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 r_2}{r_1+r_2}} - 1\right)$$

**Step 5 – Velocity on final circular orbit:**

$$v_{c2} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r_2}}$$

**Step 6 – Velocity at apogee of transfer orbit:**

At apogee ($r = r_2$):

$$v_a = \sqrt{\mu\left(\frac{2}{r_2} - \frac{1}{a_t}\right)} = \sqrt{\frac{2\mu\, r_1}{r_2(r_1+r_2)}}$$

**Step 7 – Second Δv (apogee burn, prograde):**

$$\Delta v_2 = v_{c2} - v_a = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r_2}}\left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}}\right)$$

**Step 8 – Total Δv for the Hohmann transfer:**

$$\boxed{\Delta v_{total} = \vert \Delta v_1 \vert + \vert \Delta v_2 \vert}$$

**Step 9 – Transfer time (half-period of transfer ellipse):**

$$t_{Hoh} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{(r_1 + r_2)^3}{8\mu}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly write $a_t = r_1 + r_2$ instead of $(r_1 + r_2)/2$. The semi-major axis of an ellipse is the **arithmetic mean of apogee and perigee distances**, not their sum.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A satellite is in a circular LEO orbit at $r_1 = 7000$ km. It must be transferred to a circular orbit at $r_2 = 10000$ km using a Hohmann transfer. Calculate the total $\Delta v$ required and the transfer time. (Use $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s²)

**Step 1 – Convert radii to meters:**

$r_1 = 7.0 \times 10^6$ m, $r_2 = 1.0 \times 10^7$ m.

**Step 2 – Compute $v_{c1}$ and $v_{c2}$:**

$$v_{c1} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{7.0 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{5.694 \times 10^7} \approx 7546 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{c2} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{1.0 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^7} \approx 6314 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 – Compute $v_p$ and $v_a$:**

$$v_p = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 1.0 \times 10^7}{7.0 \times 10^6 \times (1.7 \times 10^7)}} = \sqrt{6.696 \times 10^7} \approx 8183 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_a = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 7.0 \times 10^6}{1.0 \times 10^7 \times (1.7 \times 10^7)}} = \sqrt{3.282 \times 10^7} \approx 5729 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 – Compute Δv₁ and Δv₂:**

$$\Delta v_1 = 8183 - 7546 = +637 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\Delta v_2 = 6314 - 5729 = +585 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 – Total Δv:**

$$\Delta v_{total} = 637 + 585 = 1222 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 – Transfer time:**

$$t_{Hoh} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{(1.7 \times 10^7)^3}{8 \times 3.986 \times 10^{14}}} = \pi \times 3936 \approx 12365 \text{ s} \approx 3.43 \text{ hours}$$

**Answer:** $\Delta v_{total} \approx 1222$ m/s; $t_{Hoh} \approx 3.43$ hours.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams and Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the AOCS

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **closed-loop control architecture** of a typical three-axis stabilized satellite AOCS. The flow shows how sensor measurements are processed to generate actuator commands, which physically alter the spacecraft's attitude and orbit state.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Satellite Body] --> A1[Attitude Sensors: Sun, Star, Earth, Gyro, Magnetometer]
    B0[Ground Station: TT and C Link] --> B1[Command Receiver and Demodulator]
    A1 --> A2[Sensor Signal Conditioning and Analog to Digital Conversion]
    A2 --> A3[On Board Computer with Kalman Filter Estimator]
    B1 --> A3
    A3 --> A4[Attitude and Orbit Control Algorithm: PID or LQR]
    A4 --> A5[Actuator Command Distribution Unit]
    A5 --> A6[Reaction Wheels: Roll Pitch Yaw Axes]
    A5 --> A7[Thruster Valves: 10N and 22N Bipropellant]
    A5 --> A8[Magnetic Torque Rods for Momentum Dumping]
    A6 --> A9[Body Torque Applied: Roll Pitch Yaw]
    A7 --> A9
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A0
    A0 --> B2[Telemetry: Housekeeping Data Downlink]
    B2 --> B0

    classDef sensor fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef body fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    class A1,A2,B1,B2 sensor
    class A3,A4,A5 compute
    class A6,A7,A8,A9 actuator
    class A0,B0 body
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **blue block** is the sensing path – it observes the satellite's current state.
- The **yellow/orange block** is the computational core – the "brain" that runs the Kalman filter estimator and control algorithm.
- The **green block** is the actuation path – it physically applies torques and forces.
- The **red blocks** represent the physical satellite and the ground station interface.
- The loop **A0 → A1 → A2 → A3 → A4 → A5 → A6 → A9 → A0** is the **fundamental attitude control loop**.

### 4.2 Reaction Wheel Control Sub-Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Reference Attitude: Quaternion q_target] --> E1[Quaternion Error Calculation: q_err = q_target conjugate times q_actual]
    S2[Current Attitude: q_actual from Star Tracker] --> E1
    E1 --> E2[Convert q_err to Euler Angle Error]
    E2 --> C1[PID Controller: Kp Ki Kd Gains]
    C1 --> C2[Command Torque T_cmd on Three Axes]
    C2 --> D1[Motor Driver: PWM to Three BLDC Motors]
    D1 --> D2[Three Reaction Wheels: Wheel 1, Wheel 2, Wheel 3]
    D2 --> A1[Wheel Torque T_w on Satellite Body]
    A1 --> S2
    D2 --> D3[Wheel Speed Sensor: Tachometer]
    D3 --> D4[Momentum Unloading Logic]
    D4 --> A2[Magnetic Torquer Activation]
    A2 --> A1
```

**Key elements of this sub-loop:**
- **Quaternion Error** is preferred over Euler-angle error to avoid the **gimbal lock singularity** at pitch = ±90°.
- **PID Controller** (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) is the industry-standard control law for AOCS because it is computationally cheap and robust.
- **Momentum Unloading** is essential: reaction wheels accumulate stored momentum over time; without periodic dumping (via magnetic torquers in LEO or thrusters in GEO), they would saturate.

### 4.3 Reference Frame Transformation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Inertial Frame ECI: Centered at Earth, Fixed to Stars] --> F2[Orbital Frame: Origin at Satellite, Z toward Earth Center, X along Velocity]
    F2 --> F3[Body Frame: Origin at Satellite Center of Mass, X along Roll, Y along Pitch, Z along Yaw]
    F1 -.Attitude Kinematics: Euler Angles or Quaternions.-> F3
    F2 -.Quaternion Update: dot_q equals 0.5 times omega times q.-> F3

    classDef frame fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000
    class F1,F2,F3 frame
```

**Note for examiners:** The transformation between any two of these frames is encoded by a **3 × 3 Direction Cosine Matrix (DCM)** or, more efficiently, by a **unit quaternion** (4 parameters with one constraint, total 3 degrees of freedom).

### 4.4 Sensor and Actuator Mapping Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Sun[Sun Sensor: Coarse Reference] --> Sunit
    Earth[Earth Horizon Sensor: Nadir Pointing] --> Earthit
    Star[Star Tracker: High Precision Reference] --> Starit
    Gyro[Gyroscope: Inertial Rate Measurement] --> Gyroit
    Magneto[Magnetometer: Magnetic Field Vector] --> Magnetoit
    GPS[GPS Receiver: Absolute Position] --> GPSit

    Sunit[Input: Sun Vector] --> Kalman[Kalman Filter State Estimator]
    Earthit[Input: Earth Vector] --> Kalman
    Starit[Input: Star Vector] --> Kalman
    Gyroit[Input: Angular Rate] --> Kalman
    Magnetoit[Input: B Field Vector] --> Kalman
    GPSit[Input: Position and Velocity] --> Kalman

    Kalman --> Control[Control Law: Bang Bang, PID, or LQR]
    Control --> RW[Reaction Wheels: Continuous Fine Control]
    Control --> CMG[Control Moment Gyros: High Torque Maneuvers]
    Control --> Thr[Thrusters: Large External Torque and Orbit Changes]
    Control --> MTQ[Magnetic Torquers: LEO Momentum Dumping]

    classDef sensor fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    class Sun,Earth,Star,Gyro,Magneto,GPS,Sunit,Earthit,Starit,Gyroit,Magnetoit,GPSit sensor
    class Kalman,Control compute
    class RW,CMG,Thr,MTQ actuator
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define the **Attitude and Orbit Control System (AOCS)** of a satellite. List its four main functional sub-blocks.

**Model Answer:**
The **Attitude and Orbit Control System (AOCS)** is the satellite sub-system that determines and controls the orientation (attitude) and trajectory (orbit) of the spacecraft throughout its mission life. Its four main functional sub-blocks are:
1. **Sensors** (Sun sensor, Earth sensor, Star tracker, Gyroscope, Magnetometer, GPS receiver) – to measure the current state.
2. **Actuators** (Reaction wheels, Control moment gyros, Thrusters, Magnetic torquers) – to apply corrective torques and forces.
3. **On-board Computer / Control Law** (Kalman filter, PID, LQR algorithm) – to process sensor data and compute commands.
4. **TT&C Interface** – to receive ground updates and downlink telemetry.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the four sub-blocks.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **passive** and **active** attitude stabilization methods. Give **one example** of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Passive Stabilization | Active Stabilization |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Relies on natural physical principles (gyroscopic rigidity, gravity gradient) without consuming power or propellant. | Uses sensors, on-board computer, and actuators to actively measure and correct attitude errors. |
| **Power/Propellant** | No power or propellant required. | Consumes electrical power and/or propellant. |
| **Pointing Accuracy** | Coarse (few degrees). | High (sub-degree to arc-second). |
| **Example** | **Spin-stabilized satellite** (e.g., Pioneer 10) or **gravity-gradient-stabilized satellite**. | **Three-axis stabilized** satellite using reaction wheels (e.g., INSAT, GSAT series). |

*(Examiner's Note: 2 marks for the distinction, 1 mark for the example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Convention:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A(a)** **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]** Starting from Newton's second law for rotation, **derive Euler's rotational equations of motion** for a rigid body in the body-fixed principal axis frame. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to the full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

**Assumptions to be stated:**
1. The body is **rigid** (inter-point distances constant).
2. The body frame is aligned with the **principal axes** of inertia (products of inertia vanish).
3. The inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}$ is **constant in the body frame**.
4. External torques are **measured in the body frame**.

**Final result to be written:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= I_1 \dot{\omega}_1 - (I_2 - I_3)\omega_2 \omega_3 \\
T_2 &= I_2 \dot{\omega}_2 - (I_3 - I_1)\omega_3 \omega_1 \\
T_3 &= I_3 \dot{\omega}_3 - (I_1 - I_2)\omega_1 \omega_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):**
- 1 mark: Starting equation (Newton's 2nd law for rotation).
- 1 mark: Expressing $\vec{H} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}$ and applying the principal-axis assumption.
- 2 marks: Applying the transport theorem to convert to body frame.
- 2 marks: Expanding the cross product into scalar components.
- 1 mark: Final three Euler equations clearly boxed.

---

**Q.A(b)** **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]** Explain the **working of a three-axis reaction wheel attitude control system** with a neat block diagram. Derive the relationship between the wheel torque and the satellite body angular acceleration.

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
A three-axis reaction wheel control system uses **three independent flywheels**, each mounted along one principal body axis (Roll, Pitch, Yaw). To rotate the satellite about a particular axis, the corresponding wheel is accelerated or decelerated by a brushless DC motor. By **Newton's third law**, the reaction torque on the wheel motor mounting produces a torque on the satellite body, causing the desired rotation.

**Block Diagram:**

```
            +-------+   q_error   +--------+   T_cmd   +----------+
 q_target → | Error | ----------> |  PID   | --------> |  Motor   | --+
            +-------+             +--------+           | Driver   |   |
                ^                 ▲                   +----------+   |
                |                 | rate                 |          |
                |                 |                       v          v
            +-------+         +--------+             +-------+    +--------+
 q_actual ← | Body  | ← T_w ← | Wheel  | ← T_motor ← | RW1   |    | RW2    |
            | Dyn.  |         | Eqns.  |             | RW3   |    |        |
            +-------+         +--------+             +-------+    +--------+
```

**Derivation of relationship:**

From the conservation of total angular momentum in the absence of external torques:

$$\frac{d}{dt}(I_s \omega_s + I_w \omega_w) = 0$$

Differentiating:

$$I_s \dot{\omega}_s + I_w \dot{\omega}_w = 0$$

The wheel torque on the satellite body is the reaction to the wheel motor torque:

$$T_w = I_w \dot{\omega}_w = -I_s \dot{\omega}_s$$

Or equivalently:

$$\boxed{\dot{\omega}_s = -\frac{I_w}{I_s}\dot{\omega}_w}$$

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):**
- 2 marks: Working principle with sensor → controller → actuator flow.
- 2 marks: Block diagram with all labelled blocks.
- 2 marks: Derivation of $T_w = -I_s\dot{\omega}_s$.
- 1 mark: Physical interpretation (momentum exchange).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B(a)** **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** With neat diagrams, explain the operation of **(i) spin-stabilized** and **(ii) gravity-gradient stabilized** satellites. Compare their pointing accuracy.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Spin-Stabilized Satellite:**

In this method, the entire satellite body is rotated about a symmetry axis (typically the Y-axis). The angular momentum $\vec{H} = I\vec{\omega}$ acts as a **gyroscopic stabilizer**, resisting any external disturbance torque that tries to change the direction of the spin axis.

```
        +-----------------------+
        |       (Despun)        |
        |   Antenna Platform    |
        +-----------------------+
                  | bearings
        =================== <- Spin Axis (Y)
        |    Spinning Body    |
        |   (Rotor Section)   |
        ===================
```

**Key features:**
- The **spin axis** points in a fixed inertial direction (e.g., toward the Sun or a star).
- External disturbance torques only cause **precession** (slow wobble) of the spin axis, not tumbling.
- Simple, reliable, and requires **no power or propellant** for stabilization.

**(ii) Gravity-Gradient Stabilized Satellite:**

A long boom is extended from the satellite along one axis. The Earth's gravity field is **slightly stronger** at points closer to Earth. This differential gravitational pull creates a **gravity gradient torque** that tends to align the long boom vertically (nadir-pointing).

```
        Earth (Below)
          |
          v (gravity gradient force stronger here)
       [ Satellite Body ]
          |
          |  <- Long Extendable Boom (10-100 m)
          |
          v (gravity gradient force weaker here)
        [ Tip Mass ]
```

**Key features:**
- Uses a **passive** physical principle; no power required for stabilization.
- Has a **long natural period of libration**; needs **damping** (magnetic hysteresis dampers) to prevent oscillations.
- Pointing accuracy: ±2° to ±5° (coarse).

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Spin Stabilization | Gravity-Gradient |
|---|---|---|
| Pointing Accuracy | ±0.5° to ±1° (with despun platform) | ±2° to ±5° |
| Complexity | Simple mechanism | Requires deployable boom |
| Best Use | Early satellites, interplanetary | Small LEO satellites |

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):**
- 2 marks: Spin-stabilized explanation + diagram.
- 2 marks: Gravity-gradient explanation + diagram.
- 2 marks: Mathematical torque relation for gravity gradient.
- 1 mark: Comparison table.

---

**Q.B(b)** **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is a **Hohmann transfer**? Derive the expressions for the **two velocity changes** required for a Hohmann transfer between two coplanar circular orbits. State one advantage and one disadvantage.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
A **Hohmann transfer** is a two-impulse, coplanar, tangential orbital maneuver that transfers a satellite between two circular orbits using a half-elliptical transfer orbit tangent to both circles. It is the **most fuel-efficient** two-burn transfer for coplanar orbits.

**Derivation (full step-by-step shown in Section 3.3):**

The two Δv expressions are:

$$\Delta v_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r_1}}\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 r_2}{r_1+r_2}} - 1\right)$$

$$\Delta v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r_2}}\left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}}\right)$$

**Total Δv:**

$$\Delta v_{total} = \vert \Delta v_1 \vert + \vert \Delta v_2 \vert$$

**Advantage:** Minimum fuel consumption (minimum total Δv) for transfers where the orbit ratio $r_2/r_1 < 11.94$.

**Disadvantage:** Long transfer time (half-period of the transfer ellipse); not suitable for **time-critical** missions such as crewed lunar transfers.

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):**
- 1 mark: Definition with neat diagram.
- 2 marks: Vis-Viva equation application.
- 2 marks: Correct final Δv₁ and Δv₂ expressions.
- 1 mark: Transfer time formula and one advantage + one disadvantage.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on AOCS questions:**
> 
> 1. **Sign convention errors in Euler's equations** – Examiners specifically check whether you wrote the gyroscopic coupling term as $-(I_2 - I_3)\omega_2\omega_3$ or $+(I_3 - I_2)\omega_2\omega_3$. Both are accepted *only if* the cross product convention is explicitly stated. **State your convention first.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the transport theorem** – Many students write $\vec{T} = \mathbf{I}\dot{\vec{\omega}}$ directly in the body frame, which is **wrong**. You must include the $\vec{\omega} \times \vec{H}$ term.
> 
> 3. **Confusing torque-rod and reaction-wheel equations** – The magnetic torquer equation is $T_{mag} = mB$ (scalar) or $\vec{m}\times\vec{B}$ (vector). The reaction wheel equation is $T_w = I_w\dot{\omega}_w$. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for mixing these.
> 
> 4. **Hohmann transfer formula errors** – The most common mistake is $a_t = r_1 + r_2$ instead of $(r_1 + r_2)/2$. Memorize: *semi-major axis = arithmetic mean of perigee and apogee.*
> 
> 5. **Sensor and Actuator mismatch** – A GPS receiver measures **position** (orbit), not attitude. A star tracker measures **attitude**, not position. Examiners often have a 2-mark sub-question testing this.
> 
> 6. **Missing units in the final answer** – In numerical problems, always state the SI unit (m/s, km/s, N·m, hours). A correct numerical answer without units is treated as **incomplete**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for AOCS (Module 1)**
> 
> **A. Conceptual Block**
> - AOCS = **Attitude** (orientation) control + **Orbit** (position/trajectory) control.
> - **ADCS** is the attitude sub-system; **OCS** is the orbit sub-system.
> - The four functional blocks are: **Sensors → Computer/Control Law → Actuators → Body → (loop back to sensors)**.
> - **Passive** methods: spin, dual-spin, gravity-gradient. **Active** methods: 3-axis with reaction wheels/CMG/thrusters.
> 
> **B. Reference Frames (always know)**
> - **ECI (Inertial)** – fixed to stars, used for orbital mechanics.
> - **ECEF (Earth-fixed)** – rotates with Earth, used for ground tracking.
> - **Orbital Frame** – origin at satellite, Z toward Earth, X along velocity.
> - **Body Frame** – origin at satellite, Roll-X, Pitch-Y, Yaw-Z.
> 
> **C. Sensors (know which measures what)**
> - **Sun sensor** → coarse Sun direction.
> - **Earth horizon sensor** → nadir pointing.
> - **Star tracker** → high-precision inertial reference (arc-second accuracy).
> - **Gyroscope** → angular rate (drift over time).
> - **Magnetometer** → magnetic field vector.
> - **GPS receiver** → absolute position and velocity.
> 
> **D. Actuators (know torque/force range)**
> - **Reaction wheel** → continuous, fine torque, accumulates momentum.
> - **CMG** → high torque for fast slewing, momentum exchange.
> - **Thruster** → large impulsive Δv and Δω, consumes propellant.
> - **Magnetic torquer** → small torque, LEO only, used for momentum dumping.
> 
> **E. Core Equations (must memorize verbatim)**
> - $\vec{H} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}$ — Angular momentum.
> - $T_w = I_w\dot{\omega}_w = -\dot{h}_w$ — Reaction wheel torque.
> - Euler's 3 equations (Section 3.1 final form).
> - $\Delta v_1$ and $\Delta v_2$ Hohmann formulas.
> - $v_c = \sqrt{\mu/r}$ — Circular orbit velocity.
> - Vis-Viva: $v^2 = \mu(2/r - 1/a)$.
> 
> **F. Typical Disturbance Torques (in order of magnitude for LEO)**
> 1. Gravity-gradient (dominant for elongated bodies).
> 2. Aerodynamic drag.
> 3. Solar radiation pressure.
> 4. Magnetic dipole interaction.
> 
> **G. KTU Buzzwords that Score Free Marks**
> - *Momentum exchange device* — for reaction wheel.
> - *Gyroscopic coupling* — for Euler's equations.
> - *Kalman filter* — for sensor fusion.
> - *Vis-Viva equation* — for any orbital velocity problem.
> - *Burn at perigee, burn at apogee* — for Hohmann transfer.
> - *Momentum dumping via magnetic torquers* — for LEO wheel saturation.
> 
> **H. Common Mistakes to Avoid**
> - Forgetting the $\vec{\omega}\times\vec{H}$ term in body-frame torque equation.
> - Writing $a_t = r_1 + r_2$ instead of $(r_1+r_2)/2$.
> - Mixing up position sensors (GPS) with attitude sensors (star tracker).
> - Forgetting to convert km to m in numerical problems.
> - Not specifying the reference frame when stating angular velocity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Telemetry Tracking Command and Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Telemetry Tracking Command and Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Telemetry, Tracking, Command and Monitoring (TT&C)** is the critical satellite subsystem (also called the **Spacecraft Operations Subsystem** or **SCO** by ISRO) that forms the bidirectional control and health-diagnostic interface between a satellite in orbit and its ground station. It performs four essential functions:

1. **Telemetry (TM)** — Downlink transmission of operational, scientific, and housekeeping data (voltages, currents, temperatures, pressures, attitude sensor readings) from the satellite to the ground.
2. **Tracking (TR)** — Determination of the satellite's orbital state vector (position, velocity, and orbital elements) by measuring range, range-rate, and angular coordinates.
3. **Command (TC)** — Uplink transmission of operational instructions, operational sequences, and software patches from the ground to the satellite.
4. **Monitoring (MON)** — Real-time ground-based processing, archiving, and visualization of telemetry data, including alarm generation and trend analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The TT&C subsystem is mandatory for *every* satellite regardless of mission profile (communication, remote sensing, navigation, scientific). The reliability target is typically **> 0.9999 (four nines)** over the mission lifetime, since failure of TT&C implies loss of the satellite.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** ISRO uses the nomenclature "Spacecraft Operations" while NASA/ESA use "TT&C" or "Mission Operations." KTU 2024 syllabus adopts the unified term **TT&C Subsystem**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

Imagine a **patient in a hospital ICU**:

| Medical ICU Element | Satellite TT&C Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient's heartbeat, BP, temperature sensors | Onboard sensors reporting to telemetry encoder |
| ECG waveform on doctor's monitor | Telemetry downlink received at ground station |
| Doctor's verbal instructions to nurse | Command uplink from ground |
| Nurse observing & charting vitals | Monitoring console at ground |
| Hospital's central monitoring system | Master Control Facility (MCF) |
| Ambulance GPS tracking the patient | Earth station tracking antenna & ranging system |

The **patient cannot survive** without constant monitoring and timely intervention — likewise a **satellite is "dead on arrival"** if its TT&C subsystem fails after launch.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standards Used

- **Speed of light (vacuum):** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Standard gravitational parameter (Earth):** $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^2$
- **Equatorial Earth radius:** $R_E = 6378 \text{ km}$
- **Standard telemetry bit-error-rate target:** $BER \leq 1 \times 10^{-5}$ for housekeeping data
- **Command link BER target:** $BER \leq 1 \times 10^{-8}$ (very high reliability)
- **Typical TT&C frequencies:** **S-band (2 GHz)**, **C-band (6 GHz)** uplink, **S-band (2.2 GHz)**, **C-band (4 GHz)** downlink (per ITU-R & CCSDS recommendations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequency Allocation (ITU-R & CCSDS Standard):**
> - **Command Uplink:** ~**2 GHz** (S-band) or **14 GHz** (Ku-band backup)
> - **Telemetry Downlink:** ~**2.2 GHz** (S-band) or **12 GHz** (Ku-band backup)
> - **Tracking Beacons:** Often share the TT&C carrier or use a separate beacon in the same band.

## 1.4 Mission Context — Why TT&C is Non-Negotiable

| Mission Phase | TT&C Role |
|---|---|
| **Launch & Early Orbit Phase (LEOP)** | Critical — receives first signals, stabilizes satellite, deploys solar arrays |
| **Transfer Orbit (GTO for GEO)** | Continuous tracking, perigee/apogee maneuver commands |
| **On-Station (GEO) / Operational LEO** | Routine health checks, station-keeping, payload reconfigurations |
| **End-of-Life (EOL)** | Passivation commands, final telemetry dump |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of the TT&C Subsystem

The TT&C subsystem has both **space segment** and **ground segment** components operating in a closed loop.

### 2.1.1 Space Segment (Onboard the Satellite)

- **Sensors & Transducers** — Temperature, voltage, current, pressure, attitude sensors.
- **Telemetry Encoder (TM Encoder / Formatter)** — Multiplexes and encodes sensor data into a standard format (CCSDS Packet Telemetry standard).
- **Command Receiver & Decoder (TC Receiver)** — Receives uplink commands, validates (via authentication), and routes to the appropriate subsystem.
- **Tracking Transponder (Coherent / Non-coherent)** — Provides a stable downlink carrier for ground tracking.
- **Beacon Transmitter** (optional) — Continuous-wave beacon aiding ground antenna acquisition.
- **Data Storage (Mass Memory)** — Buffers telemetry between ground passes.

### 2.1.2 Ground Segment (Earth Station)

- **TT&C Earth Station** — Large parabolic dish (typically **9 m to 30 m**) with steerable mount.
- **Telemetry Demodulator & Front-End Processor** — Decodes CCSDS frames.
- **Command Encoder & Modulator** — Generates uplink command waveforms.
- **Range & Range-Rate (RARR) Equipment** — Measures two-way range and Doppler.
- **Spacecraft Operations Control Centre (SOCC / MCF)** — Mission control, displays, alarms.
- **Data Archive & Mission Planning Software** — Long-term storage and future pass prediction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed Loop Concept:** Command uplink is verified by the satellite's command receiver which echoes a "command acceptance" packet via telemetry downlink — this **acceptance telemetry** is the core feedback that closes the control loop.

## 2.2 Telemetry Subsystem — Detailed Operation

### 2.2.1 Types of Telemetry

| Type | Description | Modern Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Analog TM** | Direct voltage/current proportional to measurement | Legacy only |
| **PAM (Pulse Amplitude Modulation)** | Sampled analog pulses | Legacy |
| **PDM (Pulse Duration Modulation)** | Variable pulse width encodes sample | Legacy |
| **PCM (Pulse Code Modulation)** | Digital samples | **Universal standard today** |
| **Packet TM (CCSDS)** | Encapsulated source packets with virtual channels | **Modern (KTU-emphasis)** |

### 2.2.2 Telemetry Data Format (CCSDS)

A typical modern telemetry frame structure (in order):
- **Attached Synchronization Marker (ASM)** — Bit pattern for frame sync.
- **Transfer Frame Header** — Virtual channel ID, spacecraft ID, etc.
- **Transfer Frame Data Field** — Source packets (one per measurement).
- **Frame Error Control Field** — CRC-16 or RS coding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Modern KTU questions on telemetry always emphasize **PCM** (Pulse Code Modulation) with **NRZ-L encoding** as the de-facto standard for onboard telemetry.

### 2.2.3 PCM Telemetry Bit Rate Calculation

The PCM bit rate required to transmit $N$ channels each sampled at $f_s$ samples/sec with $n$ bits per sample is:

$$
R_b = N \cdot f_s \cdot n \quad \text{[bits/sec]}
$$

The corresponding **symbol rate** $R_s$ for M-ary signaling (e.g., BPSK, QPSK) is $R_s = R_b / \log_2 M$.

## 2.3 Command Subsystem — Detailed Operation

### 2.3.1 Command Categories

| Category | Sub-Examples |
|---|---|
| **Real-Time Commands** | Turn transmitter ON, switch antenna, fire thruster |
| **Stored Commands** | Time-tagged command sequence for autonomous execution |
| **Software Upload (Patches)** | New flight software uploaded in segments with checksum |
| **Emergency Commands** | Safe-mode, heater ON, switch to redundant bus |

### 2.3.2 Command Security Layers

Modern satellites employ multi-layer security to prevent unauthorized command execution (a critical anti-hijack measure):

1. **RF Carrier acquisition** — Ground must match frequency/phase.
2. **Subcarrier lock** — Command subcarrier (e.g., 8 kHz tone) must be detected.
3. **PN-Code spread spectrum lock** — Pseudo-random code correlation.
4. **Address matching** — Spacecraft ID embedded in command word.
5. **Authentication (digital signature)** — Hash-based or AES message authentication.
6. **Verification echo** — Onboard computer echoes back, ground confirms, then uplinks "execute."

## 2.4 Tracking Subsystem — Detailed Operation

### 2.4.1 Tracking Measurements

The ground station determines the satellite's state vector using three primary observables:

| Observable | Definition | Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Range ($R$)** | Line-of-sight distance | Two-way time-of-flight |
| **Range-Rate ($\dot{R}$)** | Line-of-sight velocity | Two-way Doppler shift |
| **Angle (Az, El)** | Direction from ground station | Monopulse or step-track antenna |

### 2.4.2 Coherent vs Non-Coherent Transponder

- **Coherent Transponder** — Downlink carrier frequency is a fixed rational multiple of the uplink carrier (e.g., ratio 240/221 for S-band). Enables **two-way Doppler** for high-precision tracking.
- **Non-Coherent Transponder** — Downlink frequency is generated by an onboard oscillator (VCXO/OCXO). Range-rate is measured via **one-way Doppler** — less accurate but simpler.

## 2.5 Monitoring Subsystem

The **monitoring** function is purely ground-based and includes:
- Real-time display of telemetry parameters on consoles.
- **Limit-checking** (red/yellow alarm thresholds).
- **Trending** (long-term drift analysis for battery, gyro, etc.).
- **Automated event logging** for post-pass analysis.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula a KTU student must memorize for the ESE on this topic. Note: Use `\vert` instead of `\mid` for absolute value to keep table syntax intact.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_b = N \cdot f_s \cdot n$ | PCM telemetry bit rate | bits/sec |
| 2 | $R_s = R_b \, / \, \log_2 M$ | Symbol rate for M-ary modulation | bauds |
| 3 | $t_p = 2R \, / \, c$ | Two-way propagation time (range $R$) | sec |
| 4 | $R = c \cdot t_p \, / \, 2$ | Range from two-way delay | meters |
| 5 | $\Delta f = f_0 \cdot \dot{R} \, / \, c$ | Doppler shift for range-rate | Hz |
| 6 | $\dot{R} = c \cdot \Delta f \, / \, f_0$ | Range-rate from Doppler | m/s |
| 7 | $\left( \frac{S}{N} \right)_d = \left( \frac{S}{N} \right)_u + G_{proc}$ | SNR with processing gain (dB) | dB |
| 8 | $G_{proc} = 10 \log_{10}( \vert R_c / R_b \vert )$ | Spread-spectrum processing gain | dB |
| 9 | $L_p = 32.4 + 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f)$ | Free-space path loss | dB (d in km, f in MHz) |
| 10 | $\phi = c \, / \, R_b$ | Bit duration | sec |
| 11 | $E_b / N_0 = (S/N) \cdot (\Phi / R_b)$ | Energy-per-bit to noise density ratio | dB |
| 12 | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \, / \, L_p$ | Friis received power (idealized) | Watts |
| 13 | $L_p(\text{coh}) = L_p + \text{cable} + \text{atm}$ | Total coherent-link losses | dB |
| 14 | $\Delta t_{\text{range}} = R \, / \, c$ | One-way light time | sec |
| 15 | $N = kTB$ | Thermal noise power (Boltzmann) | Watts |

> Where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ is the Boltzmann constant and $T$ is system noise temperature in Kelvin.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GPS constellation** uses UHF TT&C links at ~2.2 GHz for housekeeping while L-band broadcasts navigation payload.
- **ISRO's INSAT/GSAT** series is controlled from the **Master Control Facility (MCF) at Hassan, Karnataka** using C-band and S-band TT&C.
- **LEO constellations (Starlink, OneWeb)** require **automated, high-cadence** TT&C — multiple ground stations per orbit.
- **Interplanetary missions (Chandrayaan-3, MAVEN)** use **X-band and Ka-band** TT&C with **DSN (Deep Space Network)** support.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is explicitly shown. Multi-line derivations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned}` blocks, with one blank line before and after each `$$` display equation.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Two-Way Range from Round-Trip Time

A ranging signal is transmitted from the ground station, hits the satellite, is turned around by the onboard transponder, and returned to the ground. The ground station records the round-trip propagation time $t_p$.

**Step 1 — Express the round-trip distance traveled by the signal.**

The signal travels from the ground station to the satellite (distance $R$), and then from the satellite back to the ground station (distance $R$). Total distance $= 2R$.

**Step 2 — Relate this distance to the propagation time using $c$.**

The relationship between distance, speed, and time for electromagnetic radiation in vacuum is:

$$
2R = c \cdot t_p
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the slant range $R$.**

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot t_p}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check using GEO and LEO altitudes.**

For a GEO satellite, $R \approx 36000 \text{ km}$, so $t_p \approx 2(3.6 \times 10^7) / (3 \times 10^8) = 0.24 \text{ s}$.
For a LEO satellite, $R \approx 500 \text{ km}$, so $t_p \approx 2(5 \times 10^5) / (3 \times 10^8) = 3.33 \text{ ms}$.

> **Conclusion:** The two-way range measurement is unambiguous and accurate to within a few meters if the ground station uses atomic clocks and PN-code correlation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Range-Rate from Two-Way Doppler Shift

A continuous-wave uplink at carrier frequency $f_0$ is sent to the satellite. The coherent transponder translates the frequency by a fixed ratio (e.g., 240/221) and retransmits. The ground station measures the received frequency $f_r$.

**Step 1 — Express the Doppler shift at the satellite.**

The satellite is moving away from (or toward) the ground station at a radial velocity $\dot{R}$. By the classical Doppler formula for the first hop:

$$
f_{\text{sat}} = f_0 \left( 1 \pm \frac{\dot{R}}{c} \right)
$$

The sign convention: "+" for receding (red shift), "−" for approaching (blue shift).

**Step 2 — Apply the same Doppler effect on the downlink.**

When the satellite retransmits at $f_{\text{sat}}$ (after transponder translation), the ground station receives a second Doppler shift:

$$
f_r = f_{\text{sat}} \left( 1 \pm \frac{\dot{R}}{c} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two shifts using small-velocity approximation ($\dot{R} \ll c$).**

For typical orbital velocities ($\dot{R} < 8 \text{ km/s}$), we can linearize the product, dropping the $\dot{R}^2/c^2$ term:

$$
f_r \approx f_0 \left( 1 \pm \frac{2 \dot{R}}{c} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Define the two-way Doppler frequency shift $\Delta f = f_r - f_0$.**

$$
\Delta f = \pm \frac{2 f_0 \dot{R}}{c}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the range-rate $\dot{R}$.**

$$
\dot{R} = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_0}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example.**

If a ground station transmits at $f_0 = 2.0 \text{ GHz}$ and measures $\Delta f = +800 \text{ Hz}$ on the returned carrier, then:

$$
\dot{R} = \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(800)}{2 \times (2 \times 10^9)} = 60 \text{ m/s (receding)}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Two-way Doppler provides range-rate precision of order **mm/s** over integration times of a few seconds — this is the workhorse observable for **orbit determination**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PCM Telemetry Bit Rate for a Sensor Array

A satellite has **N = 200 housekeeping channels**. Each channel is sampled at $f_s = 5 \text{ samples/sec}$, with 8-bit resolution.

**Step 1 — Compute the total number of samples per second.**

$$
N_s = N \times f_s = 200 \times 5 = 1000 \text{ samples/sec}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by bits per sample to get the bit rate.**

$$
R_b = N_s \times n = 1000 \times 8 = 8000 \text{ bits/sec} = 8 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3 — If BPSK is used, the symbol rate equals the bit rate.**

$$
R_s = R_b / \log_2 2 = 8000 \text{ symbols/sec} = 8 \text{ kBaud}
$$

**Step 4 — Required transmission bandwidth (Nyquist for BPSK):**

$$
B \approx R_b = 8 \text{ kHz}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A standard S-band telemetry link of 8 kHz bandwidth easily supports this housekeeping data rate, leaving room for scientific payload data on a separate virtual channel.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Link Budget for a TT&C Downlink

A satellite in LEO (altitude 500 km) transmits a telemetry signal. The ground station receives it. Compute the carrier-to-noise density ratio $C / N_0$ at the ground receiver input.

**Given:**
- Transmit power on the satellite: $P_t = 5 \text{ W} = 7 \text{ dBW}$
- Transmit antenna gain: $G_t = 3 \text{ dBi}$
- Frequency: $f = 2.2 \text{ GHz}$
- Slant range: $R \approx 1000 \text{ km}$ (worst case)
- Receiver antenna gain: $G_r = 40 \text{ dBi}$
- System noise temperature: $T_s = 300 \text{ K}$
- Receiver bandwidth: $B = 10 \text{ kHz}$

**Step 1 — Compute the free-space path loss.**

$$
L_p = 32.4 + 20 \log_{10}(1000) + 20 \log_{10}(2200) \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(1000) = 60 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(2200) = 20 \times 3.342 = 66.85 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
L_p = 32.4 + 60 + 66.85 = 159.25 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the received carrier power.**

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_p = 7 + 3 + 40 - 159.25 = -109.25 \text{ dBW}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the noise power spectral density $N_0$.**

$$
N_0 = 10 \log_{10}(k T_s) = 10 \log_{10}(1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300)
$$

$$
k T_s = 4.14 \times 10^{-21} \text{ W/Hz}
$$

$$
N_0 = -203.83 \text{ dBW/Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C / N_0$.**

$$
(C/N_0) = P_r - N_0 = -109.25 - (-203.83) = 94.58 \text{ dBHz}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $E_b / N_0$ assuming bit rate $R_b = 8 \text{ kbps}$.**

$$
E_b/N_0 = C/N_0 - 10 \log_{10}(R_b) = 94.58 - 10 \log_{10}(8000) = 94.58 - 39.03 = 55.55 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with BPSK theoretical BER requirement.**

For BPSK at $P_e = 10^{-5}$, the required $E_b / N_0 \approx 9.6 \text{ dB}$. The link has a margin of $55.55 - 9.6 = 45.95 \text{ dB}$ — **plenty of margin**, confirming the link design is robust.

> **Conclusion:** A 5 W S-band TT&C transmitter is more than sufficient for LEO operations; GEO missions require higher gain antennas and possibly higher transmit power.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Telemetry Encoding Techniques

| Encoding | Description | Bandwidth | DC Balance | Modern Usage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NRZ-L** | Bit 1 = High, Bit 0 = Low | $B = R_b$ | No | Common in short-distance telemetry |
| **NRZ-M** | Transition on 1, no transition on 0 | $B = R_b$ | No | Magnetic recording heritage |
| **Bipolar/AMI** | Alternating polarity for 1s | $B = R_b$ | Yes | Legacy |
| **Bi-$\Phi$ (Manchester)** | Always a transition mid-bit | $B = 2 R_b$ | Yes | Optical / fiber |
| **Split-Phase (Bi-$\Phi$-L)** | Level changes on 1 | $B = 2 R_b$ | Yes | CCSDS standard for TM |
| **Miller Code** | Transition mid-cell on 1, end on 0, 00 stays still | $B = R_b$ | Yes | Deep-space CCSDS |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model QP]

**Explain the four functions of the TT&C subsystem in a communication satellite. Mention typical frequency bands used for the TT&C uplink and downlink.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

The four functions of the TT&C subsystem are:

1. **Telemetry (TM):** Downlink transmission of onboard sensor data (temperatures, voltages, currents, attitude sensor outputs) to the ground in PCM format. (0.75 marks)
2. **Tracking (TR):** Determination of satellite position and velocity using two-way range (from round-trip time) and two-way range-rate (from Doppler shift), supplemented by angular measurements. (0.75 marks)
3. **Command (TC):** Uplink transmission of operational instructions — real-time commands, stored commands, and software patches — verified by an echo-back acceptance packet. (0.75 marks)
4. **Monitoring (MON):** Ground-based real-time display, limit-checking, and archival of telemetry for mission operations. (0.75 marks)

**Frequencies:** TT&C uplink is typically in the **S-band (~2 GHz)** and downlink in the **S-band (~2.2 GHz)**. Backup Ku-band (14 GHz up / 12 GHz down) is used in some platforms.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]

**What is a coherent transponder? Why is it used in satellite tracking?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

A **coherent transponder** is an onboard TT&C subsystem in which the downlink carrier frequency is a fixed rational multiple of the received uplink carrier frequency (e.g., the standard S-band ratio of 240/221). The onboard frequency reference is phase-locked to the uplink carrier (1.5 marks).

It is used in tracking because the **two-way Doppler shift** measured on the returned carrier provides a precise and unambiguous measurement of the **range-rate** $\dot{R}$ between the ground station and the satellite. This enables high-precision orbit determination (down to cm/s level) for mission operations. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Convention:** Each 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The "OR" choice must be on the **same sub-part number**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**a)** Draw and explain the functional block diagram of a satellite TT&C subsystem. Identify the typical frequencies used. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A ground station transmits a ranging signal to a satellite in circular orbit at an altitude of 1000 km. The round-trip propagation time is measured as 8.000 ms. Compute the slant range and verify the orbital altitude. Assume the ground station is on the equator and the satellite is at zenith. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Functional Block Diagram (7 marks):**

Refer to the block diagram in SECTION 4.1. A complete description must include:

| Block | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Onboard Sensors | Convert physical parameters to electrical signals | 1.0 |
| Telemetry Multiplexer + A/D | Sample and digitize signals | 1.0 |
| PCM Encoder (CCSDS Framer) | Format into CCSDS packets with ASM, header, CRC | 1.0 |
| Modulator (BPSK/QPSK) + SSPA + Antenna | Up-convert to S-band, amplify, radiate | 1.0 |
| Ground Antenna + LNA + Demodulator + Deframer | Receive, amplify, demodulate, recover packets | 1.0 |
| Monitoring & Command Generation | Display, archive, generate uplink commands | 1.0 |
| Typical Frequencies | S-band up (~2 GHz) and S-band down (~2.2 GHz); ITU-R/CCSDS standard | 1.0 |

**Part (b) — Range Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute slant range from round-trip time.** (2 marks)

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot t_p}{2}
$$

$$
R = \frac{(3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}) \times (8.000 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s})}{2}
$$

$$
R = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{2} = 1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ m} = 1200 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute slant range geometrically.** (3 marks)

For a ground station at sea level (on equator) and a satellite at zenith, the slant range is:

$$
R = R_E + h
$$

where $R_E = 6378 \text{ km}$ is the Earth's equatorial radius and $h$ is the orbital altitude.

**Step 3 — Solve for altitude.** (1 mark)

$$
h = R - R_E = 1200 - 6378 = -5178 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** (1 mark)

A negative altitude is **physically impossible**, indicating either a faulty measurement, an incorrect assumption (the satellite was not at zenith), or a computational error. The student must note that if the satellite were truly at altitude 1000 km, the slant range at zenith should have been $6378 + 1000 = 7378 \text{ km}$, giving a round-trip time of:

$$
t_p = \frac{2 \times 7.378 \times 10^6}{3 \times 10^8} = 49.2 \text{ ms}
$$

> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Tip:** A common mistake is to use $c$ incorrectly (using $3 \times 10^5$ km/s) or to forget the factor of 2 in the two-way range. Always state the assumptions clearly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**a)** What is PCM telemetry? With neat waveforms, explain the following encoding schemes used in satellite telemetry: NRZ-L, NRZ-M, and Split-Phase (Bi-$\Phi$-L). State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A satellite's coherent transponder has a downlink-to-uplink frequency ratio of 240/221. The ground station transmits an uplink carrier at exactly 2050.000 MHz. The satellite is moving away from the ground station at a range-rate of 3.5 km/s. Compute the received downlink carrier frequency at the ground station. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — PCM Telemetry & Encoding Schemes (7 marks):**

**Definition of PCM Telemetry** (1 mark):
Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) telemetry is a digital modulation method in which analog sensor signals are sampled (typically at ≥ 2× the highest frequency component, per Nyquist), quantized into discrete amplitude levels, and transmitted as a binary bit stream. Each sample is encoded into a fixed number of bits (typically 8, 10, or 12 bits).

**Encoding Scheme Descriptions** (2 marks each, 6 marks total):

| Scheme | Rule | Waveform Description | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NRZ-L** (Non-Return-to-Zero, Level) | Bit 1 = +V, Bit 0 = 0 V (or −V) | Constant level during bit period | Simple, bandwidth-efficient ($B = R_b$) | DC component, no clock info |
| **NRZ-M** (Mark) | Transition at start of bit period if bit = 1, no transition if bit = 0 | Transition pattern marks ones | Self-clocking for ones | DC component, ambiguity on long 0-runs |
| **Split-Phase (Bi-$\Phi$-L)** (CCSDS standard) | Always a transition mid-bit; bit 1 = transition at start of bit, bit 0 = no start transition | Mandatory mid-bit transition | Self-clocking, DC-balanced, easy bit sync | Doubles bandwidth ($B = 2 R_b$) |

**Part (b) — Two-Way Doppler Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the given values.** (1 mark)

- Uplink frequency: $f_0 = 2050.000 \text{ MHz} = 2.05 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}$
- Range-rate: $\dot{R} = +3.5 \text{ km/s} = 3500 \text{ m/s}$ (positive = receding)
- Coherent transponder ratio: $f_{\text{DL nominal}} / f_{\text{UL}} = 240 / 221$

**Step 2 — Compute the two-way Doppler shift.** (3 marks)

$$
\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 \dot{R}}{c}
$$

$$
\Delta f = \frac{2 \times (2.05 \times 10^9) \times 3500}{3 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
\Delta f = \frac{1.435 \times 10^{13}}{3 \times 10^8} = 4.783 \times 10^4 \text{ Hz} = 47.83 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the received frequency on the uplink (first hop at the satellite).** (1 mark)

The satellite is receding, so it sees a slightly **lower** frequency (red shift):

$$
f_{\text{UL at sat}} = f_0 - \Delta f_{\text{one-way}} = 2.05 \times 10^9 - 23916.7 = 2049976083.3 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the transponder ratio to get the nominal downlink frequency at the satellite.** (1 mark)

$$
f_{\text{DL sat}} = \frac{240}{221} \times f_{\text{UL at sat}}
$$

$$
f_{\text{DL sat}} = \frac{240}{221} \times 2049976083.3 = 2226280851.6 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the second Doppler shift on the downlink.** (1 mark)

$$
f_{\text{DL ground}} = f_{\text{DL sat}} - 23916.7 \approx 2226256935 \text{ Hz} \approx 2226.26 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification using the closed-form two-way result.** (bonus 1 mark)

A convenient shortcut: the **total two-way Doppler on the downlink** is $\Delta f_{\text{down}} = (240/221) \times \Delta f_{\text{up}}$. This gives a one-way two-hop Doppler on the downlink of:

$$
\Delta f_{\text{down}} = \frac{240}{221} \times 47833.3 = 51938.6 \text{ Hz} \approx 51.94 \text{ kHz}
$$

Nominal downlink without Doppler: $f_{\text{DL nominal}} = (240/221) \times 2050 = 2225.79 \text{ MHz}$.
Received downlink: $2225.79 \text{ MHz} - 51.94 \text{ kHz} \approx 2225.74 \text{ MHz}$.

(Acceptable answers within ±2 kHz due to rounding.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes on Doppler Problems:**
> 1. Forgetting the factor of **2** for two-way propagation. Many students compute one-way Doppler only.
> 2. Using the **wrong sign convention** for approaching vs. receding satellites. State the sign explicitly in the solution.
> 3. Confusing the **uplink and downlink frequencies** when applying the transponder ratio. The ratio is applied to the *received* uplink frequency at the satellite, not to the ground-station transmitted frequency directly.
> 4. Unit mistakes: ensure range-rate is in **m/s** when used with $c$ in m/s; otherwise convert.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> High-density, KTU-focused summary — read this 15 minutes before entering the exam hall.

### Core Concepts (Must Know)

- [ ] **TT&C = Telemetry + Tracking + Command + Monitoring** — the four functions form a closed loop.
- [ ] **Telemetry** flows **down** (sat → ground); **Command** flows **up** (ground → sat).
- [ ] **Standard TT&C frequencies:** S-band uplink ~2 GHz, S-band downlink ~2.2 GHz; Ku-band backup.
- [ ] **Modern telemetry is PCM-based** following **CCSDS Packet Telemetry** standard.
- [ ] **Two-way range** is computed as $R = c t_p / 2$ where $t_p$ is the round-trip time.
- [ ] **Two-way range-rate** is computed as $\dot{R} = c \Delta f / (2 f_0)$ from the two-way Doppler.
- [ ] **Coherent transponder** preserves phase coherence (frequency ratio 240/221 in S-band) — required for precise Doppler tracking.
- [ ] **Command security layers:** address matching, authentication, verification echo, and execute command.

### Critical Numerical Values (Memorize)

- [ ] $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- [ ] $R_E = 6378 \text{ km}$
- [ ] $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^2$
- [ ] $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
- [ ] GEO altitude $\approx 35786 \text{ km}$; LEO altitude $\approx 200$–$2000 \text{ km}$.

### Formulae to Memorize (Equations)

- [ ] Free-space path loss: $L_p = 32.4 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$
- [ ] Friis received power: $P_r = P_t G_t G_r / L_p$ (linear scale)
- [ ] Doppler (two-way): $\Delta f = 2 f_0 \dot{R} / c$
- [ ] Range: $R = c t_p / 2$
- [ ] Bit rate (PCM): $R_b = N f_s n$
- [ ] Symbol rate: $R_s = R_b / \log_2 M$
- [ ] Thermal noise: $N = k T B$
- [ ] $E_b / N_0 = (C/N_0) - 10 \log_{10}(R_b)$

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- [ ] Forgetting factor of 2 in two-way range and Doppler.
- [ ] Mixing up frequencies when the transponder ratio is involved.
- [ ] Writing $|x|$ in markdown tables — use `\vert x \vert` instead.
- [ ] Conflating **azimuth/elevation** (ground antenna pointing) with **right ascension/declination** (celestial frame).
- [ ] Confusing **telemetry** (passive reporting) with **telecontrol** (active command) — these are different.

### Indian Context (Bonus Marks)

- [ ] ISRO's **Master Control Facility (MCF)** is at **Hassan, Karnataka**.
- [ ] **ISTRAC (ISRO Telemetry, Tracking and Command Network)** operates multiple ground stations globally.
- [ ] **LEOP** is typically managed from **ISTRAC, Bangalore**.
- [ ] Indian satellites use **CCSDS**-compatible protocols and **S-band + C-band** TT&C.
