<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Satellite Power System (SPS):** The on-board electrical power subsystem (EPS) of a spacecraft that generates, stores, conditions, regulates, and distributes electrical energy to every active payload and bus component throughout the satellite's operational life. In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, it is classified as one of the **five essential spacecraft subsystems**, along with Structure, Attitude Control, Telemetry-Tracking-Command (TT&C), and Thermal Control.

The Power System is fundamentally the **"lifeblood"** of a satellite. Without it, the transponders cannot amplify signals, the onboard computer cannot process commands, the thrusters cannot fire, and the sensors cannot acquire data. The power budget directly dictates the **mission lifetime, payload capacity, and orbital choice** of the satellite.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Satellite is a Camel Carrying a Smartphone in the Desert":**
> - **The Sun** = an infinite, free gas station (solar arrays tap this).
> - **The Batteries** = the water bottles the camel carries for the night journey (eclipse).
> - **The Power Conditioner (PCDU)** = a smart voltage regulator that protects your phone from overcharging or sudden blackouts.
> - **The Load** = the smartphone running multiple apps (transponders, heaters, computers).
> - **The Desert (Eclipse)** = when the Sun sets, the camel must rely entirely on stored energy. A poorly designed battery = a dead satellite.

Just as a smartphone user monitors battery percentage, satellite engineers monitor **State of Charge (SOC)** and **Depth of Discharge (DOD)** to ensure the satellite survives 15 years in orbit.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar Constant (AM0) | $G_{SC}$ | **1367 W/m²** (≈1.353 kW/m²) | Solar flux outside Earth's atmosphere at 1 AU |
| Earth Albedo Reflection | — | ~30% of $G_{SC}$ | Reflected sunlight contributing to array heating |
| Eclipse Duration (GEO) | $T_e$ | **72 min/day** (max, around equinox) | Battery must supply full load during this period |
| Eclipse Duration (LEO) | $T_e$ | ~35 min/orbit | Frequent charge-discharge cycles |
| Bus Voltage (typical) | $V_{bus}$ | **28 V, 50 V, or 100 V DC** | Standard regulated spacecraft bus |
| Battery Cell Voltage | $V_{cell}$ | 1.25 V (Ni-H₂), 3.6 V (Li-ion) | Nominal discharge voltage |
| End-of-Life Degradation | — | ~25–30% over 15 yrs | Solar array output loss due to radiation |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** The solar constant **1367 W/m²** is the single most-tested number in KTU Power System numericals. Memorize it!

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Power Subsystem

A satellite power system is composed of **three primary functional blocks**:

### 🔹 Block A: Power Generation (Source)
1. **Solar Array (SA)** — the primary energy source.
2. Mounted on the satellite body or deployed wings.
3. Composed of hundreds/thousands of individual **solar cells** connected in series-parallel.
4. Cell materials:
   - **Silicon (Si)** — older satellites, ~14% efficiency.
   - **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)** — modern satellites, ~18–22% efficiency.
   - **Multi-junction (InGaP/GaAs/Ge)** — state-of-the-art, **>30% efficiency**.

### 🔹 Block B: Power Storage (Buffer)
1. **Rechargeable Batteries** — energy reservoir for eclipse periods.
2. Technology generations:
   - **Ni-Cd** (obsolete, memory effect).
   - **Ni-H₂** (workhorse for 1990s GEO).
   - **Li-Ion** (current standard, high energy density).
3. Critical parameters: **Capacity (Ah), DOD (%), Cycle Life**.

### 🔹 Block C: Power Conditioning & Distribution (PCDU)
1. **PCDU = Power Control & Distribution Unit.**
2. Functions:
   - **Regulation** — maintains constant bus voltage despite varying load.
   - **Charge/Discharge Control** — manages battery current flow.
   - **Protection** — fuses, overcurrent/overvoltage trip circuits.
   - **Distribution** — switched, metered power to each subsystem.

## 2.2 Why the Bus Voltage is Regulated — The Core Physics

Solar array output varies with:
- **Illumination angle (incidence)** — cosine losses.
- **Temperature** — voltage drops as temperature rises.
- **Cell degradation** — gradual loss due to radiation.
- **Eclipse entry/exit** — sudden step from full Sun to full shade.

To prevent sensitive electronics from brownouts, the bus voltage is **clamped** to a constant level by the PCDU. This requires the PCDU to operate in one of three modes:

> [!NOTE]
> **Three PCDU Operating Regimes:**
> 1. **Sun Mode (Array-Only):** Solar array supplies the entire load *and* recharges the battery. Excess power is shunted through a **dissipative shunt regulator** (a heater that throws away surplus as heat).
> 2. **Eclipse Mode (Battery-Only):** Solar array output is zero. Battery alone supplies the full bus load.
> 3. **Transfer / Peak Mode (Hybrid):** Load exceeds array capability briefly (e.g., during eclipse exit or transmitter turn-on). Array + Battery share the load.

## 2.3 Operational Logic — Stepwise Power Flow

- **Step 1:** Photons strike the solar cells → **photovoltaic effect** releases electron-hole pairs → DC current flows.
- **Step 2:** Array output is fed to the **PCDU input bus** (unregulated, ~30–40 V).
- **Step 3:** **Shunt regulator** (a bank of Zener diodes or transistor switches) dumps excess current to maintain voltage ceiling.
- **Step 4:** **Battery Charge Regulator (BCR)** diverts controlled current to battery when State of Charge < 100%.
- **Step 5:** **Battery Discharge Regulator (BDR)** supplies bus during eclipse, acting as a DC-DC boost/buck converter.
- **Step 6:** **Power Distribution Unit (PDU)** routes regulated voltage to each subsystem via **latching relays** (no power consumed to hold state).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{SA} = G_{SC} \cdot A_{cell} \cdot \eta_{cell} \cdot \cos\theta \cdot ID$ | Solar array output power | Watts (W) |
| 2 | $P_{eclipse} = P_{load} \cdot T_{eclipse} / \eta_{batt}$ | Energy required from battery in eclipse | Watt-hours (Wh) |
| 3 | $E_{stored} = V_{batt} \cdot C_{batt} \cdot DOD_{max}$ | Usable energy stored in battery | Watt-hours (Wh) |
| 4 | $N_{cells,series} = V_{bus} / V_{cell}$ | Series cells needed to reach bus voltage | dimensionless |
| 5 | $N_{strings} = I_{load} / I_{cell}$ | Parallel strings needed to supply load current | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{cell} = P_{out} / P_{in} \times 100\%$ | Cell conversion efficiency | % |
| 7 | $P_{BOL} = P_{EOL} \cdot (1 + D)^{N_{yrs}}$ | EOL back-calculation from BOL requirement | W |
| 8 | $D = 1 - (1-d)^{N_{yrs}}$ | Total degradation factor (compound) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \frac{1}{C}\int_0^t I_{charge}\,d\tau - \frac{1}{C}\int_0^t I_{discharge}\,d\tau$ | Battery state of charge dynamics | % or Ah |
| 10 | $T_{eclipse,GEO} = \frac{2}{\omega_E} \arccos\left(\frac{R_E}{R_E+h}\right)$ | Eclipse duration for circular orbit | seconds |
| 11 | $P_{load,peak} = P_{avg} \cdot M_{factor}$ | Peak power from average using mission factor | W |
| 12 | $\theta = \arccos(\sin\phi \cdot \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cdot \cos\delta \cdot \cos H)$ | Solar incidence angle on tilted array | radians/deg |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical KTU Note:** In any table above, where you see vertical bars in the actual formula (such as $\vert I_{cell} \vert$), substitute the absolute value as plain text or use the magnitude without delimiters when transcribing to answer sheets. The Exam Section strictly uses magnitude symbols.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GEO Communication Satellites** (e.g., INSAT, Intelsat): Designed for **15-year EOL**, requiring 25–30% power margin at BOL.
- **LEO Earth Observation** (e.g., Cartosat, Sentinel): Higher eclipse frequency (15 orbits/day) → battery sized for 36 min/orbit, ~5500 cycles/year.
- **Mars Orbiter Mission (Mangalyaan, ISRO)**: Solar arrays sized for **1.4 AU** (further from Sun) → only ~590 W/m² available, requiring larger panels.
- **Interplanetary Missions**: Often use **RTGs (Radioisotope Thermoelectric Generators)** when solar flux is too weak (Jupiter, Saturn).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Solar Array Output Power (Detailed)

> **Problem Setup:** A satellite has a solar panel of area $A = 10 \text{ m}^2$ mounted normal to the Sun. The cell efficiency is $\eta = 18\%$. During solstice, the incidence angle is $\theta = 23.5°$ (worst-case). The intrinsic degradation $d = 2.5\%/\text{year}$. Find the **power available at the array terminals after 10 years**.

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs and constants.**

$$G_{SC} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$$
$$A = 10 \text{ m}^2$$
$$\eta_{cell} = 0.18$$
$$\theta = 23.5° = 0.4102 \text{ rad}$$
$$d = 0.025 \text{ per year}, \quad N_{yrs} = 10$$
$$ID = \text{intrinsic degradation factor} = (1-d)^{N_{yrs}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cosine loss factor.**

$$\cos\theta = \cos(23.5°) = 0.9171$$

This represents the **Lambertian projection loss** — only the perpendicular component of the solar flux effectively contributes to power generation.

**Step 3 — Compute the compound degradation factor over 10 years.**

$$ID = (1 - 0.025)^{10} = (0.975)^{10}$$

Let us expand this using the natural logarithm:

$$\ln(0.975) = -0.02532$$

Multiplying by 10:

$$10 \cdot \ln(0.975) = -0.2532$$

Exponentiating:

$$ID = e^{-0.2532} = 0.7765$$

Thus, after 10 years, the array retains **77.65%** of its original capability.

> **Engineering Insight:** The remaining 22.35% is lost primarily due to **charged-particle radiation** damaging the semiconductor lattice, creating recombination centers that reduce carrier lifetime.

**Step 4 — Substitute into the master power equation.**

$$P_{SA} = G_{SC} \cdot A \cdot \eta_{cell} \cdot \cos\theta \cdot ID$$

Plugging in:

$$P_{SA} = 1367 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.18 \cdot 0.9171 \cdot 0.7765$$

**Step 5 — Compute sequentially with intermediate rounding.**

$$1367 \cdot 10 = 13670 \text{ W}$$
$$13670 \cdot 0.18 = 2460.6 \text{ W}$$
$$2460.6 \cdot 0.9171 = 2256.4 \text{ W}$$
$$2256.4 \cdot 0.7765 = 1752.1 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{SA}(EOL, 10 \text{ yr}) \approx 1752 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 6 — Compare to Beginning of Life (BOL).**

$$P_{SA}(BOL) = 1367 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.18 \cdot 0.9171 \cdot 1.0 = 2256.4 \text{ W}$$

The **BOL/EOL ratio** is $2256.4 / 1752.1 = 1.288$, i.e., the array overproduces by **28.8%** at launch to compensate for the 10-year degradation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Sizing for Eclipse Operation

> **Problem Setup:** A GEO communication satellite requires a continuous bus load of $P_{load} = 2.5 \text{ kW}$ during eclipse. Maximum eclipse duration $T_e = 72 \text{ min} = 1.2 \text{ hr}$. Battery voltage $V_{batt} = 50 \text{ V}$, allowed depth of discharge $DOD_{max} = 70\%$, battery discharge efficiency $\eta_{batt} = 0.85$. Find the **required battery capacity in Ampere-hours (Ah)**.

**Step 1 — Compute the energy that must be delivered to the load during eclipse.**

$$E_{load} = P_{load} \cdot T_e = 2500 \cdot 1.2 = 3000 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 2 — Account for battery discharge inefficiency.**

The battery must store more energy than the load consumes because some is lost as heat in internal resistance.

$$E_{batt,required} = \frac{E_{load}}{\eta_{batt}} = \frac{3000}{0.85} = 3529.4 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to required Ah capacity using the bus voltage.**

$$C_{Ah} = \frac{E_{batt,required}}{V_{batt} \cdot DOD_{max}}$$

$$C_{Ah} = \frac{3529.4}{50 \cdot 0.70} = \frac{3529.4}{35} = 100.84 \text{ Ah}$$

$$\boxed{C_{batt} \approx 101 \text{ Ah}}$$

**Step 4 — Round up to a commercially available size.**

Standard Ni-H₂ or Li-Ion cells are available in sizes like 100 Ah, 110 Ah, 120 Ah. The engineer would select **110 Ah** to provide a small margin against unexpected load spikes or extended eclipse predictions.

> **Engineering Insight:** The $DOD_{max} = 70\%$ is **not arbitrary**. Pushing a Li-ion cell to 100% DOD dramatically reduces its cycle life. The 70% rule is a thermal-electrochemical compromise: deeper discharge = more energy per kg, but fewer total cycles.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Solar Cells Required

> **Problem Setup:** Bus voltage $V_{bus} = 28 \text{ V}$. Load current in sun mode $I_{load} = 12 \text{ A}$. Each Si solar cell produces $V_{cell} = 0.5 \text{ V}$ and $I_{cell} = 0.3 \text{ A}$ under AM0 illumination. Find the **number of cells in series per string** and the **number of parallel strings**.

**Step 1 — Cells in series (voltage buildup).**

$$N_{series} = \frac{V_{bus}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{28}{0.5} = 56 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 2 — Cells in parallel (current buildup).**

$$N_{parallel} = \frac{I_{load}}{I_{cell}} = \frac{12}{0.3} = 40 \text{ strings}$$

**Step 3 — Total cells.**

$$N_{total} = N_{series} \cdot N_{parallel} = 56 \cdot 40 = 2240 \text{ cells}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** In real engineering, you add **5–10% extra cells** per string to absorb radiation degradation and provide redundancy against cell failures. The KTU valuation pattern expects this margin to be mentioned.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Cell Technologies

| Property | Silicon (Si) | Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) | Multi-junction (InGaP/GaAs/Ge) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Efficiency (AM0) | 14–16% | 18–20% | **28–32%** |
| Temperature coefficient | $-0.45 \%/°C$ | $-0.23 \%/°C$ | $-0.18 \%/°C$ |
| Radiation tolerance | Low | **High** | Medium |
| Mass (W/kg) | ~25 | ~40 | **~60** |
| Cost per Watt | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Typical use | LEO, budget missions | GEO, defense | Modern GEO, deep-space |

> **Why KTU tests this:** It is a frequent 7-mark question: "Compare three solar cell technologies. Justify which is best for a 15-year GEO mission." The **answer is GaAs or multi-junction** due to combined efficiency + radiation tolerance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🧭 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — Satellite Power Subsystem

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete functional architecture of the on-board Power System, including power flow paths, control signals, and the PCDU's three operating modes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SA[Solar Array\nGaAs Cells 22 percent] -->|Primary DC| PCDU[PCDU Unit\nPower Conditioning]
    BATT[Battery Pack\nLi-ion 110 Ah] -->|Discharge Path| PCDU
    PCDU -->|Sun Mode| LOAD[Distribution Bus 28V]
    PCDU -->|Eclipse Mode| LOAD
    PCDU -->|Shunt Dump| HEATER[Shunt Heater Bank]
    PCDU -->|Charge Control| BATT
    BCR[Battery Charge Regulator] -.->|Sense Signal| PCDU
    BDR[Battery Discharge Regulator] -.->|Sense Signal| PCDU
    SHUNT[Shunt Regulator] -.->|Voltage Feedback| PCDU
    LOAD --> TX[TX Transponder 800W]
    LOAD --> RCVR[Receiver 50W]
    LOAD --> TT[C TT and C 100W]
    LOAD --> ADC[ADC and Control 150W]
    LOAD --> TH[Thermal Heaters 200W]
    PCDU -->|Telemetry| TM[Housekeeping TM]
```

### 4.2 Reading the Diagram

- **Solid arrows** = **power flow** (current carrying conductors).
- **Dotted arrows** = **control/sense signals** (low-power feedback lines).
- **Central hub:** PCDU is the *brain* of the power system.
- **Peripheral loads:** Transmitter, Receiver, TT&C, AOCS computer, Thermal heaters.

## 4.3 PCDU Internal State Machine — Mode Transition Logic

This diagram explains *how* the satellite decides which energy source to use.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SunMode
    SunMode --> EclipseMode : Sun sensor drops below threshold
    EclipseMode --> SunMode : Sun sensor exceeds threshold for 30 seconds
    SunMode --> TransferMode : Load exceeds array capacity
    TransferMode --> SunMode : Load falls below array capacity
    EclipseMode --> TransferMode : Eclipse exit with high load demand
    TransferMode --> EclipseMode : Sudden load drop at eclipse entry
    SunMode : Solar array powers all loads plus charges battery
    EclipseMode : Battery alone powers entire bus load
    TransferMode : Array plus battery share load during peaks
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The "30-second debounce" in the Sun Mode → Eclipse Mode transition is a real engineering feature. Without it, passing the satellite over the bright lunar reflection or the Sun's corona could falsely trigger eclipse mode and cause a momentary power glitch.

## 4.4 Solar Array Mechanical Configuration (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WING1[Wing 1 Panel] --> HARNESS1[Power Harness Connector]
    WING2[Wing 2 Panel] --> HARNESS1
    WING3[Wing 3 Panel] --> HARNESS2[Power Harness Connector]
    WING4[Wing 4 Panel] --> HARNESS2
    BODY[Body Mounted Cells] --> HARNESS3[Power Harness Connector]
    HARNESS1 --> SADG[Solar Array Drive Gear\nSun Tracking]
    HARNESS2 --> SADG
    HARNESS3 --> PCDU_IN[PCDU Input Bus]
    SADG --> PCDU_IN
    SADG -.->|Stepper Motor Control| AOCS[AOCS Computer]
```

> **Why this matters:** The **Solar Array Drive (SAD)** rotates the wings to track the Sun, maximizing $\cos\theta$ throughout the orbit. Without SAD, the array would only generate peak power at local noon and decay cosinusoidally through the orbit — wasting **~40% of available energy**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1 typically carries **15–20%** weightage in the End Semester Exam. Power System questions appear frequently as **Part A (3 marks)** conceptual items and **Part B (14 marks)** analytical problems.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define Depth of Discharge (DOD) for a satellite battery. Why is it restricted to 60–80% and not allowed to reach 100%?**

**Model Answer:**

> Depth of Discharge (DOD) is the percentage of the battery's nominal capacity that is withdrawn during one discharge cycle. It is computed as
> $$DOD = \frac{Q_{discharged}}{Q_{nominal}} \times 100\%$$
> where $Q$ is charge in Ampere-hours. For a Li-ion cell of 110 Ah, $DOD = 70\%$ means only 77 Ah are used per cycle.
>
> **Why restricted to 60–80%:** A 100% DOD drastically reduces the cycle life of a Li-ion cell due to electrode stress, electrolyte decomposition, and lithium plating at the anode. At 70% DOD, a typical Li-ion cell can deliver **>5000 cycles** in LEO; at 100% DOD, this drops to **<500 cycles**. For a 15-year GEO mission requiring only ~5500 cycles, 70% DOD offers a healthy safety margin.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**
**Explain the function of a shunt regulator in a satellite power system.**

**Model Answer:**

> The shunt regulator is a **voltage-limiting device** connected in parallel with the solar array. Its function is to **dissipate excess array current as heat** when the bus voltage tends to rise above the regulation set point (e.g., 28 V or 50 V). It is implemented as a bank of power transistors acting as a variable resistor, controlled by a voltage-sense feedback loop. The shunt regulator is the *primary* regulation method in modern spacecraft because it is **simple, highly efficient (no series voltage drop), and operates at high reliability**. The dissipated heat is sometimes deliberately dumped into the spacecraft thermal radiators.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Analytical Power Budget Problem (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** A GEO communication satellite operates at 36,000 km altitude. Its payload consists of 12 transponders each consuming 80 W, an AOCS computer at 150 W, TT&C at 90 W, thermal heaters at 250 W, and an attitude sensor array at 60 W. Calculate the **total sun-mode load** and the **total eclipse-mode load**, given that the thermal heaters are switched off during eclipse.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sun Mode Load.**

- Transponders: $12 \times 80 = 960$ W
- AOCS: $150$ W
- TT&C: $90$ W
- Heaters (active): $250$ W
- Sensors: $60$ W

$$P_{sun} = 960 + 150 + 90 + 250 + 60 = 1510 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Eclipse Mode Load.**

The thermal heaters are *off*; all other loads remain active.

$$P_{eclipse} = 960 + 150 + 90 + 0 + 60 = 1260 \text{ W}$$

> **(b) [7 Marks]** If the maximum eclipse duration for GEO is 1.2 hours and the allowed DOD is 70%, calculate the **required battery capacity in Ah** at a 50 V bus, assuming 85% discharge efficiency.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Energy required by the load during eclipse.**

$$E_{load} = P_{eclipse} \cdot T_e = 1260 \cdot 1.2 = 1512 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 2 — Account for battery inefficiency.**

$$E_{batt} = \frac{E_{load}}{\eta_{batt}} = \frac{1512}{0.85} = 1778.8 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Ah.**

$$C_{Ah} = \frac{E_{batt}}{V_{bus} \cdot DOD_{max}} = \frac{1778.8}{50 \cdot 0.70} = \frac{1778.8}{35} = 50.82 \text{ Ah}$$

$$\boxed{C_{batt} \approx 51 \text{ Ah (rounded up to 55 Ah commercial standard)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Marks Lost Here:**
> 1. Many students **forget to add the battery discharge efficiency denominator** (0.85). This single omission costs **3 marks**.
> 2. Many students **do not specify the final rounded-up value** with a brief engineering justification. KTU expects: "Round up to a commercial 55 Ah cell to provide margin." This is a **1-mark "engineering judgment"** point.
> 3. Some students confuse the **eclipse load** (heaters OFF) with the **sun load**. Carefully re-read: "thermal heaters are switched off during eclipse" — this is a deliberate trick in the problem statement. Marks lost: **2**.

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the **three operating modes** of a satellite PCDU: Sun Mode, Eclipse Mode, and Transfer Mode. For each mode, identify the energy source and the load being served.

**(a) Model Answer Outline (with expected bullet points):**

| Mode | Energy Source | Load Served | When Active |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sun Mode** | Solar Array (primary) | Full bus + Battery charging | All sunlit portions of orbit |
| **Eclipse Mode** | Battery only | Full bus (no charging) | During Earth's shadow |
| **Transfer Mode** | Solar Array + Battery (combined) | High transient load (e.g., eclipse exit, TX burst) | Peak demand exceeding array capacity |

A neat **flowchart or single-line diagram** showing the PCDU, battery, array, and load with arrows indicating power flow direction is **mandatory** for full 7 marks.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Silicon, Gallium Arsenide, and Multi-junction solar cells** for a 15-year GEO mission. Justify your selection.

**(b) Model Answer Key Points:**

1. **Efficiency:** Si (14–16%) < GaAs (18–20%) < Multi-junction (28–32%). Higher efficiency = smaller area = lower mass and stowed volume.
2. **Radiation tolerance:** GaAs > Multi-junction > Si. Critical for 15-year GEO where trapped electrons in the Van Allen belts degrade cells.
3. **Temperature coefficient:** Multi-junction has the lowest $-0.18\%/°C$, meaning less power loss in the hot GEO environment (~60°C).
4. **Cost per W:** Multi-junction is most expensive, but for a high-value GEO platform, the **mass savings and lifetime energy yield** justify the cost.
5. **Verdict:** **Multi-junction (or high-grade GaAs)** is the optimal choice for modern GEO. Si is suitable only for short-life LEO or student/amateur satellites.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. Students often list properties **without a verdict**. Always conclude with: *"For 15-year GEO, the recommended technology is ___"*. Marks lost: **2**.
> 2. Students confuse **temperature coefficient** (power loss per °C) with **degradation rate** (per year). These are different physical phenomena. Marks lost: **1**.
> 3. Diagrams missing in (a). Marks lost: **2**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 Final 60-Second Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU 2024):**

- ✅ **Solar constant = 1367 W/m²** (AM0, outside atmosphere). This number appears in **every** KTU numerical.
- ✅ **Master power equation:** $P_{SA} = G_{SC} \cdot A \cdot \eta \cdot \cos\theta \cdot ID \cdot (1 - M_{margin})$. Memorize it in this exact form.
- ✅ **Eclipse in GEO = 72 min/day max**; in LEO = 35 min/orbit. Battery sizing uses the worst-case 72 min.
- ✅ **Battery sizing formula:** $C_{Ah} = \frac{P_{load} \cdot T_e}{V_{bus} \cdot DOD_{max} \cdot \eta_{batt}}$ — three denominators are the **most-skipped** items in KTU papers.
- ✅ **PCDU modes:** Sun (array+charge), Eclipse (battery only), Transfer (array+battery peak).
- ✅ **DOD = 70% for Li-ion** — this is a thermal-electrochemical compromise for cycle life.
- ✅ **Cell count:** $N_{series} = V_{bus}/V_{cell}$, $N_{parallel} = I_{load}/I_{cell}$.
- ✅ **Shunt regulator** = parallel dissipative device; **Battery Discharge Regulator** = series DC-DC converter.
- ✅ **Solar Array Drive (SAD)** rotates panels to track the Sun — boosts effective $\cos\theta$ to near 1.0 throughout the orbit.
- ✅ **GaAs or Multi-junction** preferred for GEO; **Si** acceptable for LEO/amateur.
- ✅ **End-of-Life (EOL) design philosophy:** always design for EOL, not BOL. The 15–30% BOL margin compensates for radiation-induced degradation.
- ✅ **28 V, 50 V, 100 V** are the three standard spacecraft bus voltages. Higher voltage = lower $I^2R$ losses.
- ✅ **Solar Array Drive Gear (SADG)** and **Solar Array Drive Mechanism (SADM)** are the same thing — synonyms often used interchangeably in KTU papers.
- ✅ **Housekeeping telemetry** from PCDU includes: bus voltage, bus current, battery voltage, battery current, array current, shunt current, temperature of battery and array.
- ✅ **Ni-H₂ batteries** are pressure vessels — they store hydrogen gas at ~60 atm. Each cell is a sealed stainless-steel sphere.

> **🎓 Closing Note:** Power System design is **mission-shaping** — it constrains the satellite's mass, volume, lifetime, and payload. Every KTU problem in this topic is essentially a **trade-off** between mass, cost, and reliability. Master the four formulas above, and you will secure full marks in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Subsystem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Communication Subsystem # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is the Communication Subsystem? The **Communication Subsystem** (often called the **Payload** or **Communication Payload**) of a satellite is the on-board electronic assembly that receives the weak uplink radio signals from Earth stations, processes them (frequency translation, amplification, filtering, multiplexing), and re-transmits them back to Earth as downlink signals. It is the *functional heart* of any communication satellite — without it, the bus (structure, power, propulsion) is just an inert metal box in orbit. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Syllabus-aligned):** > The Communication Subsystem comprises the chain of RF components that accept the modulated RF carriers from the receiving antenna, perform low-noise amplification, frequency conversion, channelization, high-power amplification, and multiplexing, and finally deliver the combined RF output to the transmitting antenna for radiation back to Earth. The three primary sub-assemblies of the communication subsystem are: 1. **Receiving Section (Rx Chain):** LNA → Input Demultiplexer (IMUX filter bank) 2. **Processing Section (Transponder/Channelizer):** Frequency converter (Mixer + LO) → Channel amplifiers 3. **Transmitting Section (Tx Chain):** Output Multiplexer (OMUX) → High Power Amplifier (TWTA/SSPA) → Tx Antenna feed ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Satellite Repeater Tower in the Sky" Imagine a courier service office perched on top of a mountain: - **Inbox & Outbox trays** (multiplexers) sort letters by destination ZIP code (frequency channel). - **A magnifying glass** (Low Noise Amplifier) reads faint pencil marks and brightens them. - **A translator** (Mixer + Local Oscillator) converts the language (frequency) from French to English. - **A loudspeaker** (High Power Amplifier) broadcasts the message loud enough for the valley below to hear. The satellite's communication subsystem operates identically — it receives faint, noisy signals, amplifies them cleanly, shifts them to a different frequency (so uplink and downlink don't interfere), boosts them to high power, and beams them back to Earth. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit | |--------|----------|----------------------| | $k$ | Boltzmann's constant | **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** (or **−228.6 dBW/K/Hz**) | | $T_0$ | Standard reference noise temperature | **290 K** | | $c$ | Speed of light | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | | $f$ | Operating frequency | Hz (commonly 4, 6, 12, 14, 20, 30 GHz) | | $N_0$ | Noise spectral density | $kT$, in W/Hz | | $\text{EIRP}$ | Effective Isotropic Radiated Power | dBW | | $G/T$ | Figure of Merit | dB/K | > [!NOTE] > **Why Uplink ≠ Downlink Frequency?** The communication subsystem uses two different frequency bands (e.g., 6 GHz uplink / 4 GHz downlink for C-band) to **prevent echo and self-interference** between the satellite's own powerful transmitter and its sensitive receiver — a problem called *duplexer leakage*. ## 1.4 Block-Level Functional Overview The communication subsystem is built around one or more **transponders**. A transponder is a single channel (or band of channels) of the communication chain. Modern satellites carry **12 to 94 transponders** depending on the design. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Term — "Transponder":** A transponder is a single repeater channel that handles one portion of the uplink spectrum (typically 36 MHz or 54 MHz wide for FSS, or 27 MHz for BSS). A *single-conversion transponder* uses one mixer; a *double-conversion (regenerative) transponder* demodulates and remodulates the baseband signal for improved isolation and error performance. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Signal Flow Architecture The communication subsystem is architecturally divided into two segments: - **Receiver chain (Rx):** Antenna feed → LNA → IMUX → Down-converter → Channel amplifier - **Transmitter chain (Tx):** OMUX → HPA (TWTA/SSPA) → Tx antenna feed These are interconnected by the **translator** (frequency-conversion stage), which is the core of the transponder. ### 2.1.1 The Single-Conversion (Bent-Pipe) Transponder This is the workhorse architecture of 95% of commercial satellites. The signal undergoes exactly one frequency translation. **Operational Steps:** 1. **Reception:** The Rx antenna collects the uplink RF carriers across the full allocated uplink band. 2. **Low-Noise Amplification:** A highly sensitive LNA (NF ≈ 0.5–2 dB at Ku-band) boosts the signal by 30–60 dB while adding minimal noise. The LNA is the *single most critical* component for the satellite's **G/T** figure of merit. 3. **Input Demultiplexing (IMUX):** A bank of bandpass filters (cavity or dielectric resonator filters) separates the wideband input into individual transponder channels (e.g., 12 channels × 36 MHz = 432 MHz total). 4. **Frequency Down-Conversion:** Each channel is mixed with a local oscillator (LO) signal to shift the carrier from uplink frequency $f_{\text{UL}}$ to downlink frequency $f_{\text{DL}}$: $$f_{\text{IF}} = f_{\text{UL}} - f_{\text{LO}} \quad \text{(high-side injection)}$$ 5. **Channel Amplification:** Per-channel gain stages set the operating point for the HPA. 6. **Output Multiplexing (OMUX):** All channels are combined into the downlink feed manifold using a low-loss combiner. 7. **High-Power Amplification:** Either a **TWTA** (Travelling Wave Tube Amplifier) or **SSPA** (Solid-State Power Amplifier) provides 20–250 W of RF output per channel. 8. **Transmission:** The combined downlink signal is radiated by the Tx antenna. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Bent-Pipe"?** The signal is not demodulated; the satellite simply bends the RF pipe — receiving, shifting, amplifying, and re-broadcasting — exactly like a passive mirror with electronics. Modulation format, encryption, and baseband are transparent to the satellite. ### 2.1.2 The Double-Conversion (Regenerative) Transponder Used in advanced digital satellites (e.g., military, broadband internet constellations like Starlink v2 mini, Inmarsat). - First conversion: RF → IF - IF → Baseband (demodulation) - Signal processing (decoding, error correction, switching, re-routing) - Baseband → IF → RF (re-modulation, re-encoding) - Amplification and transmission **Advantages:** Better link C/N (typically 2–5 dB improvement), bit-error-rate regeneration, beam-to-beam onboard switching. **Disadvantages:** Complexity, cost, and limited to the modulation/coding format it was designed for. ## 2.2 The High Power Amplifier (HPA) — TWTA vs SSPA | Parameter | TWTA | SSPA | |-----------|------|------| | Device | Travelling Wave Tube (vacuum) | GaN / GaAs FET (solid state) | | Power | 20–250 W (typical) | 5–100 W | | Efficiency | 55–70% | 30–50% | | Bandwidth | Multi-octave | Narrower per device | | Linearity | Lower (intermodulation) | Higher | | Lifetime | ~15 years (limited by cathode) | >20 years | | Use case | High-power DTH, BSS, FSS | Mobile, L-band, smallsats | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Back-off and OBO:** The HPA is operated below its saturation point to control intermodulation products. The output back-off (OBO) is typically **3–6 dB** for multi-carrier operation. This is the single biggest reason satellites use only 30–50% of the HPA's rated power in practice. ## 2.3 Noise Figure and Noise Temperature — The LNA Trade-off The **Friis formula for cascaded noise figure** determines the overall receiver sensitivity: $$F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$$ This proves why the **LNA is always placed first** in the chain: its noise figure $F_1$ dominates, and any subsequent amplifier's noise contribution is divided by the LNA's high gain $G_1$. The equivalent noise temperature of the receiver is: $$T_{\text{e,total}} = T_1 + \frac{T_2}{G_1} + \frac{T_3}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** A typical Ku-band satellite LNA has $T_e \approx 150\text{ K}$, which corresponds to a noise figure of $F = 1 + T_e/T_0 = 1 + 150/290 \approx 1.52$ (or **1.8 dB**). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Communication Subsystem | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $F = 1 + \dfrac{T_e}{T_0}$ | Noise figure from noise temperature (dimensionless) | — | | 2 | $T_e = T_0 (F - 1)$ | Noise temperature from noise figure (with $T_0 = 290$ K) | K | | 3 | $F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}$ | Friis cascade noise figure | — | | 4 | $\text{EIRP} = P_T + G_T - L_{\text{feed}}$ | Effective isotropic radiated power | dBW | | 5 | $\text{FSPL} = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | Free-space path loss (linear) | — | | 6 | $\text{FSPL}_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$ | FSPL in dB | dB | | 7 | $\dfrac{C}{N_0} = \text{EIRP} + \dfrac{G}{T} - k - \text{FSPL} - L_{\text{other}}$ | Link carrier-to-noise density | dB-Hz | | 8 | $\dfrac{C}{N} = \dfrac{C}{N_0} - B$ | Carrier-to-noise ratio (B = bandwidth) | dB | | 9 | $\dfrac{G}{T} = G_{\text{antenna}} - L_{\text{feed}} - 10\log_{10}(T_{\text{sys}})$ | Figure of merit (Earth station side) | dB/K | | 10 | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Friis transmission equation | W | | 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{RF,out}}}{P_{\text{DC,in}}} \times 100\%$ | HPA/DC efficiency | % | | 12 | $\psi = \dfrac{\text{EIRP}}{4\pi d^2}$ | Power flux density at receiver | W/m² | | 13 | $f_{\text{IF}} = f_{\text{UL}} - f_{\text{LO}}$ | Frequency translation (high-side LO) | Hz | | 14 | $\Delta f_{\text{channel}} = 36\text{ MHz (FSS)}, 27\text{ MHz (BSS)}, 54\text{ MHz}$ | Standard transponder bandwidth | MHz | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL KTU NOTE:** Always use absolute value bars as `\vert F \vert` in LaTeX, never as a vertical pipe `|F|` inside markdown tables — this breaks the table rendering. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **G/T determines uplink performance:** A satellite with higher G/T can receive weaker Earth-station signals, allowing smaller ground antennas. - **EIRP determines downlink performance:** Higher EIRP means smaller receive dishes on Earth. - **IMUX/OMUX filter quality** sets the channel isolation (typically > 25 dB) preventing adjacent-channel interference. - **HPA efficiency** directly impacts DC power demand → which determines solar array and battery sizing on the satellite bus. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis Transmission Equation for the Satellite Link The Friis equation is the fundamental link-budget tool for satellite communication. We derive it from first principles using isotropic radiators. **Step 1 — Power density from an isotropic radiator at distance $d$:** A transmitter radiates total power $P_t$ uniformly in all directions. The surface area of a sphere of radius $d$ is $4\pi d^2$. Therefore, the power flux density at distance $d$ is: $$\psi = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$ **Step 2 — Effective aperture of a receiving antenna:** By electromagnetic theory, the effective area of any antenna is related to its gain $G_r$ and operating wavelength $\lambda$: $$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi} \quad \text{[m}^2\text{]}$$ **Step 3 — Power captured by the receiving antenna:** The received power is the flux density multiplied by the effective aperture: $$P_r = \psi \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$ **Step 4 — Symmetrically include transmitter gain:** If the transmitter is not isotropic but has gain $G_t$, the flux density in the receiver direction is enhanced by $G_t$: $$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^2$$ This is the **Friis Transmission Equation**. **Step 5 — Express in decibels for KTU calculations:** Taking $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides: $$P_r [dBW] = P_t [dBW] + G_t [dBi] + G_r [dBi] + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)$$ The last term is the free-space path loss in dB (negative sign): $$\text{FSPL}_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right) = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$$ Substituting $\lambda = c/f$ and $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz: $$\text{FSPL}_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Link C/N₀ Equation for the Communication Subsystem The carrier-to-noise spectral density ratio is the most important metric for any digital satellite link. **Step 1 — Start with the received carrier power $C$:** Using the Friis equation, applied to the Earth-station-to-satellite uplink (or vice versa): $$C = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2 \cdot L_{\text{other}}}$$ where $L_{\text{other}}$ includes atmospheric losses, feed losses, and pointing losses. **Step 2 — Express the noise power in the bandwidth $B$:** The thermal noise at the receiver input is: $$N = k \, T_{\text{sys}} \, B$$ **Step 3 — Form the C/N ratio:** $$\frac{C}{N} = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2 \, L_{\text{other}} \, k \, T_{\text{sys}} \, B}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to dB and rearrange (C/N₀ form):** Define $C/N_0 = (C/N) \cdot B$, and substitute $G_r/T_{\text{sys}}$ as the figure of merit: $$\left[\frac{C}{N_0}\right]_{dB\text{-}Hz} = \text{EIRP}_{dBW} + \left(\frac{G}{T}\right)_{dB/K} - k_{dBW/K/Hz} - \text{FSPL}_{dB} - L_{\text{other},dB}$$ where $k_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(1.38 \times 10^{-23}) = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz. **Step 5 — Final KTU-ready form:** $$\boxed{\left[\frac{C}{N_0}\right] = \text{EIRP} + \frac{G}{T} - \text{FSPL} - L_{\text{atm}} - L_{\text{pointing}} + 228.6}$$ This is the **single most important equation** for KTU satellite communication problems. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Noise Temperature Cascading For the receiver chain in the communication subsystem, the noise contributions add via Friis' formula. We derive the equivalent input noise temperature. **Step 1 — Consider a two-stage cascade** (LNA + Down-converter): Stage 1: LNA with gain $G_1$ and noise temperature $T_{e1}$ Stage 2: Mixer with gain $G_2$ (often $< 1$, i.e., conversion loss) and noise temperature $T_{e2}$ **Step 2 — Total output noise power** (referred to input for analysis): $$N_{\text{out}} = G_1 G_2 \, k \, B \, (T_{\text{source}} + T_{e1} + T_{e2}/G_1)$$ **Step 3 — Define the total equivalent noise temperature referred to the input:** $$T_{\text{e,total}} = T_{e1} + \frac{T_{e2}}{G_1}$$ This is the **Friis formula for noise temperature**, the dual of the noise figure cascade formula. **Step 4 — Verify equivalence with noise figure formula:** Since $F = 1 + T_e/T_0$ and $G_1$ is in linear scale: $$F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} = \left(1 + \frac{T_{e1}}{T_0}\right) + \frac{T_{e2}/T_0}{G_1} = 1 + \frac{T_{e1} + T_{e2}/G_1}{T_0}$$ Hence $T_{e,\text{total}} = T_{e1} + T_{e2}/G_1$ ✓ ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A satellite downlink operates at **12 GHz**. The satellite EIRP is **52 dBW**, the free-space path loss is **206 dB**, the ground station $G/T$ is **31 dB/K**, and the receiver bandwidth is **36 MHz**. Atmospheric and pointing losses total **2 dB**. Calculate: (a) $C/N_0$ in dB-Hz (b) $C/N$ in dB (c) $E_b/N_0$ for a 64 kbps digital carrier **Solution (exhaustive):** **Part (a) — $C/N_0$:** $$\left[\frac{C}{N_0}\right] = \text{EIRP} + \frac{G}{T} - \text{FSPL} - L_{\text{other}} + 228.6$$ Substituting: $$\left[\frac{C}{N_0}\right] = 52 + 31 - 206 - 2 + 228.6$$ $$\left[\frac{C}{N_0}\right] = 52 + 31 + 228.6 - 208 = 103.6 \text{ dB-Hz}$$ **Part (b) — $C/N$:** $$B = 36 \text{ MHz} \Rightarrow 10\log_{10}(36 \times 10^6) = 75.56 \text{ dB-Hz}$$ $$\left[\frac{C}{N}\right] = \left[\frac{C}{N_0}\right] - B_{dB} = 103.6 - 75.56 = 28.04 \text{ dB}$$ **Part (c) — $E_b/N_0$ for $R_b = 64$ kbps:** $$R_b = 64 \times 10^3 \text{ bps} \Rightarrow 10\log_{10}(64000) = 48.06 \text{ dB-bps}$$ $$\left[\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right] = \left[\frac{C}{N_0}\right] - R_{b,dB} = 103.6 - 48.06 = 55.54 \text{ dB}$$ **Interpretation:** This margin of 55.54 dB is enormous — far above the Shannon limit for QPSK (≈ 9.6 dB) or even 8-PSK (≈ 13 dB). The link is *uplink-limited* or *interference-limited*, not thermal-noise limited. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Single-Conversion (Bent-Pipe) Transponder The bent-pipe transponder is the most-examined architecture in KTU Module 1. This is the only way to achieve 15-year mission life with vacuum tubes having wear-out failure modes. ## 4.3 Functional Block — Redundancy Switch Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LNA_BANK["LNA Bank 4 for 2 Redundancy"] L1[LNA 1 Primary] --> SW1[Coaxial Switch Matrix] L2[LNA 2 Primary] --> SW1 L3[LNA 3 Standby] --> SW1 L4[LNA 4 Standby] --> SW1 end SW1 --> OUT1[Output to IMUX] SW1 --> OUT2[Output to IMUX] subgraph TWTA_BANK["TWTA Bank 4 for 2 Redundancy"] T1[TWTA 1 Primary] --> SW2[Waveguide Switch Matrix] T2[TWTA 2 Primary] --> SW2 T3[TWTA 3 Standby] --> SW2 T4[TWTA 4 Standby] --> SW2 end SW2 --> TX1[TX Feed] SW2 --> TX2[TX Feed] style L1 fill:#b3d9ff style L2 fill:#b3d9ff style L3 fill:#ffe6b3 style L4 fill:#ffe6b3 style T1 fill:#ffb380 style T2 fill:#ffb380 style T3 fill:#ffd9b3 style T4 fill:#ffd9b3 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a transponder in a communication satellite. Mention the standard bandwidth used in the FSS band.** **Model Answer:** A transponder is a single repeater channel within a satellite's communication subsystem that receives a portion of the uplink RF spectrum, amplifies it, translates its frequency, and re-transmits it on the downlink. It comprises an LNA, frequency converter (mixer + LO), IF amplifier, and HPA, with IMUX/OMUX filters for channelization. In the Fixed Satellite Service (FSS) band, the standard transponder bandwidth is **36 MHz** per channel. In the Broadcasting Satellite Service (BSS) band it is **27 MHz**, and modern high-throughput satellites use **54 MHz** wide channels or full-bandwidth "bent-pipe" payloads. (3 marks) --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State Friis transmission equation and explain the significance of each term.** **Model Answer:** $$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^2$$ - $P_r$ — power received at the Earth station or satellite (W) - $P_t$ — power transmitted by the source (W) - $G_t, G_r$ — transmit and receive antenna gains (dimensionless) - $\lambda$ — operating wavelength (m) - $d$ — slant range between antennas (m) The equation shows that received power decreases with the **square of distance and frequency**, which is why satellite links use high-gain narrow-beam antennas and high EIRP to compensate. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a **single-conversion (bent-pipe) transponder** used in a communication satellite. (7 marks) **(b)** A satellite operates at a downlink frequency of **4 GHz**. The EIRP is **38 dBW**, the slant range is **36,000 km**, the ground station antenna gain is **50 dBi**, the system noise temperature is **150 K**, and the receiver bandwidth is **30 MHz**. Calculate: (i) Free-space path loss in dB (ii) $C/N_0$ in dB-Hz (iii) $C/N$ in dB (iv) $E_b/N_0$ for a 2 Mbps data rate (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Block diagram and working (7 marks):** The bent-pipe transponder consists of: 1. **Receiving antenna feed** — collects uplink signals 2. **LNA** — low-noise pre-amplification (NF ≈ 1.5–2 dB) 3. **IMUX** — input multiplexer splits the wideband uplink into individual 36 MHz transponder channels 4. **Mixer + Local Oscillator** — translates the uplink frequency to downlink (e.g., 6 GHz → 4 GHz) 5. **IF Amplifier** — sets the operating level for the HPA, often with AGC 6. **TWTA / SSPA** — high-power amplification (50–200 W) 7. **OMUX** — combines all transponder channels for transmission 8. **Transmitting antenna feed** — radiates the downlink to Earth The signal is **not demodulated**; the satellite acts as a transparent frequency-shifting amplifier. **(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):** **(i) Free-space path loss:** $$\text{FSPL}_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(36000) + 20\log_{10}(4000)$$ $$= 32.45 + 91.126 + 72.04 = 195.62 \text{ dB}$$ **(ii) $C/N_0$:** $$\left[\frac{C}{N_0}\right] = \text{EIRP} + \frac{G}{T} - \text{FSPL} - L + 228.6$$ Compute $G/T$: $$G/T = 50 - 10\log_{10}(150) = 50 - 21.76 = 28.24 \text{ dB/K}$$ Substituting (assuming $L = 0$ for simplicity): $$\left[\frac{C}{N_0}\right] = 38 + 28.24 - 195.62 + 228.6 = 99.22 \text{ dB-Hz}$$ **(iii) $C/N$:** $$B = 30 \text{ MHz} \Rightarrow 10\log_{10}(30 \times 10^6) = 74.77 \text{ dB-Hz}$$ $$\left[\frac{C}{N}\right] = 99.22 - 74.77 = 24.45 \text{ dB}$$ **(iv) $E_b/N_0$:** $$R_b = 2 \text{ Mbps} \Rightarrow 10\log_{10}(2 \times 10^6) = 63.01 \text{ dB-bps}$$ $$\left[\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right] = 99.22 - 63.01 = 36.21 \text{ dB}$$ This margin is suitable for QPSK (≈ 9.6 dB required) with substantial coding gain. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Compare **TWTA and SSPA** as high-power amplifiers in communication satellites. Discuss the concept of output back-off. (7 marks) **(b)** Derive the **Friis formula for cascaded noise figure** for a two-stage receiver and explain why the LNA must be the first stage in a satellite receiver. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) TWTA vs SSPA (7 marks):** | Parameter | TWTA | SSPA | |-----------|------|------| | Active device | Travelling-wave tube (vacuum electronics) | GaN or GaAs FET (solid-state) | | RF output power | 50–250 W per channel | 5–100 W per channel | | DC-to-RF efficiency | 55–70% | 30–50% | | Bandwidth | Very wide (multi-octave) | Narrower (~10–15%) | | Linearity | Lower (more IM products) | Higher | | Operating life | ~15 years (cathode wear-out) | > 20 years (no wear-out) | | Cooling | Heavier (anode + collector) | Lightweight (conduction) | | Application | High-power FSS, BSS, DTH | Mobile, L-band, phased arrays | **Output Back-off (OBO):** The HPA is operated below its saturation point to control intermodulation distortion when multiple carriers share the amplifier. The OBO is defined as: $$\text{OBO}_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{sat}}}{P_{\text{op}}}\right)$$ Typical OBO is **3–6 dB** for multi-carrier operation, reducing the usable RF power to about **25–50%** of the saturated value. **(b) Derivation of cascaded noise figure (7 marks):** For a two-stage cascade with gains $G_1, G_2$ and noise figures $F_1, F_2$: **Step 1:** Output SNR of stage 1 alone: $$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_1 = \frac{G_1 S_{\text{in}}}{G_1 N_{\text{in}} F_1} = \frac{S_{\text{in}}}{N_{\text{in}} F_1}$$ **Step 2:** Stage 2 adds its own noise. The input noise to stage 2 is $G_1 N_{\text{in}} F_1$ (referred to its input). The output noise is: $$N_{\text{out,total}} = G_1 G_2 N_{\text{in}} F_1 + G_2 (F_2 - 1) N_{\text{in}}$$ **Step 3:** The total input-referred noise factor: $$F_{\text{total}} = \frac{N_{\text{out,total}}}{G_1 G_2 N_{\text{in}}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1}$$ **Step 4:** For $N$ stages: $$\boxed{F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots + \frac{F_N - 1}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{N-1}}}$$ **Why LNA is the first stage:** Since the noise contribution of every subsequent stage is divided by the gain of all preceding stages, the LNA's high gain ($G_1 \approx 30$–50 dB) suppresses the noise of the down-converter and IF stages to negligible levels. Placing a high-noise device first (e.g., a mixer with NF = 8 dB) would dominate the cascade and degrade $G/T$ by several dB. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make in KTU valuation:** > 1. **Forgetting the +228.6 term** in the $C/N_0$ equation — this single term accounts for nearly 50% of the marks in numerical link-budget problems. > 2. **Mixing up uplink and downlink frequencies** — always state which band you are computing. C-band uplink = 6 GHz, downlink = 4 GHz. Ku-band uplink = 14 GHz, downlink = 12 GHz. > 3. **Using noise figure in dB directly in Friis formula** — convert NF to linear scale first: $F_{\text{linear}} = 10^{NF_{dB}/10}$. > 4. **Confusing EIRP with transmitted power** — EIRP includes antenna gain: $\text{EIRP}_{dBW} = P_{T,dBW} + G_{T,dBi} - L_{\text{feed},dB}$. > 5. **Forgetting OBO** when computing HPA output power — multiply $P_{\text{sat}}$ by $10^{-\text{OBO}/10}$. > 6. **Sign errors in FSPL** — path loss is always **positive** when computed correctly (since $4\pi d / \lambda > 1$ for any realistic link). ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Communication subsystem = payload** of a satellite; converts uplink to downlink with minimal added noise and maximal power. - [x] **Bent-pipe transponder** is the most common — single frequency conversion, no demodulation. - [x] **LNA must be first stage** because of Friis cascade rule: $F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + (F_2-1)/G_1 + \cdots$ - [x] **HPA back-off** (3–6 dB) is mandatory to control intermodulation in multi-carrier operation. - [x] **IMUX and OMUX** use cavity bandpass filters with 25+ dB channel isolation. - [x] **Standard channel bandwidths:** 36 MHz (FSS), 27 MHz (BSS), 54 MHz (modern HTS). - [x] **EIRP** = $P_T + G_T - L_{\text{feed}}$ (dBW); G/T = $G_{\text{ant}} - 10\log_{10}(T_{\text{sys}})$ (dB/K). - [x] **C/N₀ master equation:** $\text{EIRP} + G/T - \text{FSPL} - L + 228.6$ dB-Hz. - [x] **FSPL (dB) = 32.45 + 20 log d(km) + 20 log f(MHz)** — memorize this constant. - [x] **Redundancy 4-for-2** for both LNA and TWTA banks is the industry standard for 15-year missions. - [x] **Regenerative transponder** offers 2–5 dB better C/N through baseband regeneration, at the cost of flexibility. - [x] **TWTA vs SSPA** trade-off: TWTA = higher power, lower linearity, finite life; SSPA = lower power, higher linearity, longer life. - [x] **Standard reference temperature** $T_0$ = 290 K; Boltzmann's constant $k$ = −228.6 dBW/K/Hz. - [x] **Uplink/downlink frequencies differ** to prevent duplexer leakage and self-interference.

topics.title: Satellite Antennas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Satellite Antennas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Satellite Antenna:** A microwave-frequency radiating/receiving device mounted on a spacecraft (or its terrestrial counterpart in the ground segment) that is responsible for **transmitting RF energy toward the Earth's surface** (downlink) and/or **intercepting RF energy arriving from the Earth** (uplink). In essence, it is the electromagnetic "lens and mouth" of the satellite, converting guided electrical signals into free-space propagating waves (and vice-versa) with controlled directivity, polarization, and gain.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the term *Satellite Antenna* specifically refers to the **high-gain, directional antenna subsystem** used at the satellite end of the link (as opposed to simple isotropic radiators). It must perform three simultaneous functions:
1. **Beam shaping** — concentrate radiated power into a narrow footprint covering the intended service zone.
2. **Beam steering** — point the beam accurately at the intended region on Earth.
3. **Polarization purity** — maintain either RHCP or LHCP to allow frequency reuse via orthogonal polarization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Flashlight Reflector:**
> Imagine a bare bulb glowing in a dark room. Its light spreads uniformly in all directions and is therefore very dim at any single point. Now place that same bulb at the focal point of a polished, curved mirror. The mirror gathers light from many directions and reflects it into a tight, forward-pointing cone. A distant object inside the cone now appears brilliantly bright, while everywhere else stays dark. **A satellite antenna does exactly the same job with radio waves instead of visible light.** The "bulb" is the feed horn (transmitter), and the "polished curved mirror" is the **parabolic reflector dish**. The narrow, intense cone of radio energy becomes the satellite's *footprint* on Earth.

### 1.3 Why Antennas Are Special in Satellites

| Constraint | Consequence on Antenna Design |
|---|---|
| **DC power is precious** (solar cells only) | Antenna must have **high gain** so transmitted power is not wasted into space. |
| **Mass & volume are strictly limited** | Reflectors must be light-weight, often deployable (mesh, ribbed). |
| **No maintenance possible** | High reliability, radiation-hardened, no field repair. |
| **Orbital motion causes pointing drift** | Antenna must be steerable (mechanical gimbals or electronic phased arrays). |
| **Frequency reuse** | Must support dual-polarization (e.g., linear H/V or RHCP/LHCP). |

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

- **Gain (G)** — dimensionless, expressed in dBi (relative to isotropic).
- **Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW / θ₃dB)** — measured in **degrees**.
- **Sidelobe Level (SLL)** — measured in **dB** below the main-beam peak.
- **Aperture Efficiency (ηₐ)** — dimensionless, between **0.55 and 0.70** for a typical parabolic reflector.
- **Antenna Noise Temperature (Tₐ)** — measured in **Kelvin (K)**.
- **Figure of Merit (G/T)** — measured in **dB/K**, decisive for receive quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two KTU-favourite "show-off" antennas are the **Parabolic Reflector** and the **Phased Array**. You must be able to draw, label, and derive the gain of both.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Satellite Antennas

Satellite antennas are broadly classified by their **radiation coverage pattern** and **physical construction**.

**A) By Coverage Pattern**

- **Isotropic Antenna** — theoretical, radiates equally in all directions. **Gain = 1 (0 dBi)**. Used only as a reference standard; never physically built.
- **Omnidirectional Antenna** — radiates uniformly in the azimuth plane but is directive in elevation. Used on **low-cost LEO/MEO satellites** and for **TT&C (Telemetry, Tracking, Command)** subsystems.
- **Directional (High-Gain) Antenna** — concentrates energy into a narrow beam aimed at Earth. Used for **communication payloads** on GEO/MEO/LEO birds.

**B) By Physical Construction**

1. **Wire Antennas (Dipole, Turnstile, Quadrifilar Helix)** — simple, low-gain (~2–6 dBi), used for omni coverage in VHF/UHF/S-band TT&C.
2. **Horn Antennas** — flared waveguide. Moderate gain (10–20 dBi), used as **feed elements** for reflectors and for telemetry at C-band.
3. **Parabolic Reflector Antennas** — high gain (20–50+ dBi). The **workhorse of satellite communication**.
4. **Phased Array Antennas** — electronic beam steering. Used for **multi-beam** and **on-board switching** satellites.
5. **Lens Antennas** — shaped dielectric lens to focus RF energy. Used in mm-wave and radar applications.
6. **Helical Antennas** — circular polarization, used for telemetry and GPS bands.

### 2.2 The Parabolic Reflector — Anatomy

A parabolic reflector uses a **paraboloid of revolution** as the reflecting surface. Its geometric property is the foundational "why" of its operation:

> [!NOTE]
> **Focal Property of a Parabola:** Every ray emanating from the *focus* and striking the parabolic surface is reflected into a path **parallel to the axis of symmetry**. Conversely, every plane wave arriving parallel to the axis is reflected and converges exactly at the focus.

**Key Geometric Parameters:**

- **D** — diameter of the dish aperture (mouth).
- **f** — focal length (distance from vertex to focus).
- **f/D ratio** — typically **0.25 to 0.6** for satellite dishes.
- **Depth of dish** = $D^2 / (16f)$.
- **Focal point** — where the *feed horn* is placed.

### 2.3 Antenna Parameters — The Six Pillars

**1. Gain (G)**
The ability of an antenna to concentrate radiated power in a desired direction compared to an isotropic radiator.

$$
G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2} = \eta_a \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2
$$

**2. Effective Aperture (Aₑ)**
The equivalent collecting area of the antenna for an incoming plane wave.

$$
A_e = \eta_a \, A_{physical} = \eta_a \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**3. Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW / θ₃dB)**
The angular width of the main beam between the two points where the power drops to **half (−3 dB)** of its peak.

$$
\theta_{3dB} \approx \frac{70 \lambda}{D} \text{ degrees}
$$

**4. Sidelobe Level (SLL)**
Magnitude of the largest unwanted lobe, expressed in dB below the main-beam peak. For satellite antennas, SLL is typically **−20 dB or better** to avoid interference with adjacent satellites in the geostationary arc.

**5. Polarization**
The orientation of the E-field vector. For satellite links, **circular polarization (RHCP/LHCP)** is preferred at C-band and above to avoid Faraday rotation effects in the ionosphere.

**6. Antenna Noise Temperature (Tₐ)**
The equivalent temperature that, applied at the antenna terminals, produces the same noise power as actually received from external sources (sky, ground, sidelobes).

### 2.4 EIRP and the G/T Figure of Merit

These are the two "golden" satellite system parameters that an examiner loves to test.

**EIRP (Equivalent Isotropically Radiated Power)** — measures the *transmit* strength:

$$
EIRP = P_t \, G_t \quad \text{[watts, also expressed in dBW]}
$$

**G/T (Figure of Merit)** — measures the *receive* sensitivity:

$$
\frac{G}{T} = \frac{G_r}{T_s} \quad \text{[dimensionless, expressed in dB/K]}
$$

where $T_s = T_a + T_{LNA}$ is the *system noise temperature* referred to the antenna terminals.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gain of parabolic dish | $G = \eta_a \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | dimensionless / dBi |
| 2 | Effective aperture | $A_e = \eta_a \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | m² |
| 3 | Gain-aperture relation | $G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | HPBW (beamwidth) | $\theta_{3dB} \approx \frac{70\lambda}{D}$ | degrees |
| 5 | First null beamwidth (FNBW) | $\theta_{FNBW} \approx \frac{140\lambda}{D}$ | degrees |
| 6 | EIRP | $EIRP = P_t \, G_t$ | W or dBW |
| 7 | Figure of merit | $G/T = G_r / T_s$ | 1/K or dB/K |
| 8 | System noise temp. | $T_s = T_a + T_{LNA} + \frac{T_{loss}}{G_{LNA}}$ | K |
| 9 | Free-space path loss | $FSPL = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Received power | $P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}$ | W |
| 11 | Friis transmission eqn. | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | W |
| 12 | Parabola depth | $d_{depth} = \frac{D^2}{16f}$ | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary values to memorise:**
> - $\eta_a$ (aperture efficiency) for a typical parabolic reflector: **0.55 ≤ ηₐ ≤ 0.70**.
> - $\eta_a$ for a uniformly illuminated circular aperture: **0.8333 (theoretical max)**.
> - In dBi: $G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10} G$.
> - 1 GHz → λ ≈ **0.3 m**; 10 GHz → λ ≈ **0.03 m**; 12 GHz → λ ≈ **0.025 m**.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GEO Communication Satellites (e.g., INSAT, Intelsat):** Use large deployable parabolic reflectors (up to **20 m** diameter at L-band for mobile satellites) to cover continents with one or multiple spot beams.
- **LEO Constellations (e.g., Starlink, OneWeb):** Use **phased-array flat panels** for fast electronic beam steering as the satellite zips across the sky.
- **Direct-to-Home TV (DTH):** The user-end dish is itself a small parabolic reflector following the same physics we are studying.
- **Radar & Remote Sensing:** Same parabolic geometry underpins weather radars, synthetic aperture radar (SAR), and deep-space communication antennas (the 70-m Deep Space Network dishes).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Gain of a Parabolic Reflector Antenna from Effective Aperture

**Starting Premise:** Gain is defined as the ratio of the maximum radiation intensity (power per unit solid angle) of the antenna to that of a lossless isotropic radiator.

**Step 1 — Write the gain in terms of effective aperture.**

The fundamental relationship between the gain of any antenna and its effective collecting area is:

$$
G = \frac{4\pi \, A_e}{\lambda^2}
$$

This equation connects the **geometric / electrical size** of an antenna (via $A_e$ and $\lambda$) to its **directive performance** (via $G$).

**Step 2 — Express the effective aperture in terms of the physical area.**

The effective aperture is always less than or equal to the physical mouth area of the dish, because not all of the incident plane wave is collected and re-radiated perfectly. We introduce the **aperture efficiency** $\eta_a$ (a number between 0 and 1):

$$
A_e = \eta_a \cdot A_{physical}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the physical area of a circular dish.**

For a circular parabolic reflector of diameter $D$, the physical mouth area is simply the area of a circle:

$$
A_{physical} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

Therefore:

$$
A_e = \eta_a \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine to obtain the gain expression.**

Substituting $A_e$ into the gain formula:

$$
G = \frac{4\pi}{\lambda^2} \cdot \eta_a \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the algebra.**

The factor of 4 in the denominator cancels with the 4 in the numerator:

$$
G = \eta_a \cdot \frac{\pi^2 D^2}{\lambda^2} = \eta_a \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,G = \eta_a \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Gain scales with the **square of $D/\lambda$** — doubling the dish diameter quadruples the gain, while doubling the frequency (halving the wavelength) also quadruples the gain.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Approximate HPBW of a Parabolic Reflector

**Step 1 — Start from the Fraunhofer (far-field) diffraction pattern.**

For a uniformly illuminated circular aperture, the radiation pattern is described by the **Airy function**. The first null of the pattern occurs at the angle:

$$
\sin \theta_{null} = 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the small-angle approximation.**

For narrow beams (small $\theta$), $\sin \theta \approx \theta$ (in radians):

$$
\theta_{null} \approx 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D} \text{ [radians]}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert radians to degrees.**

Since 1 radian = 180/π degrees:

$$
\theta_{null} \approx 1.22 \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D} = 69.88 \frac{\lambda}{D} \approx \frac{70 \lambda}{D} \text{ [degrees]}
$$

**Step 4 — Relate null-to-null to half-power beamwidth.**

For a typical parabolic pattern, the −3 dB points lie at roughly **half** the angle to the first null:

$$
\theta_{3dB} \approx \frac{1}{2} \theta_{null} \approx \frac{35 \lambda}{D} \text{ [degrees]}
$$

However, the empirical formula used universally in KTU texts and industry is:

$$
\boxed{\,\theta_{3dB} \approx \frac{70 \lambda}{D} \text{ [degrees]}\,}
$$

(The constant 70 lumps together the 1.22 factor, the radian-to-degree conversion, and the empirical scaling.)

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Gain and Beamwidth of a Ku-Band Satellite Dish

**Given:** A parabolic reflector antenna operating at **12 GHz** with diameter $D = 2.4 \text{ m}$ and aperture efficiency $\eta_a = 0.6$.

**Step 1 — Calculate the wavelength.**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{12 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}} = 0.025 \text{ m} = 2.5 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dimensionless ratio $D/\lambda$.**

$$
\frac{D}{\lambda} = \frac{2.4}{0.025} = 96
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gain (linear).**

$$
G = \eta_a \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 0.6 \times (\pi \times 96)^2
$$

Computing the intermediate product:

$$
\pi \times 96 = 301.59
$$

Squaring:

$$
(301.59)^2 = 90,956.5
$$

Multiplying by efficiency:

$$
G = 0.6 \times 90,956.5 = 54,574
$$

**Step 4 — Convert gain to dBi.**

$$
G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(54,574) = 10 \times 4.737 = 47.37 \text{ dBi}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the half-power beamwidth.**

$$
\theta_{3dB} = \frac{70 \lambda}{D} = \frac{70 \times 0.025}{2.4} = \frac{1.75}{2.4} = 0.729 \text{ degrees}
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Frequency | 12 GHz |
| Wavelength | 0.025 m (2.5 cm) |
| Gain (linear) | 54,574 |
| **Gain (dBi)** | **47.37 dBi** |
| **HPBW** | **0.73°** |

**Engineering Insight:** Such a 2.4 m dish at 12 GHz has nearly half a degree of beamwidth — wide enough to cover an entire sub-continent from GEO orbit (~36,000 km), and high enough in gain to close a DTH TV link with a few watts of satellite transmitter power.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Received Power and Friis Transmission Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the isotropic received power at distance d.**

If a transmitter of power $P_t$ radiated isotropically, the power flux density (power per unit area) at distance $d$ is:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Account for the directive gain of the transmitting antenna.**

A directional antenna with gain $G_t$ multiplies the flux density in the boresight direction:

$$
S = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the effective aperture of the receiving antenna.**

The power collected by the receiving antenna is:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_{e,r} = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot A_{e,r}
$$

**Step 4 — Use the gain-aperture identity for the receiver.**

From $A_{e,r} = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$, substituting:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\,P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2\,}
$$

This is the **Friis Transmission Equation** — the cornerstone of every satellite link budget.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Parabolic Reflector vs. Phased Array

| Feature | Parabolic Reflector | Phased Array |
|---|---|---|
| **Beam steering** | Mechanical (gimbals, slow) | **Electronic (microsecond re-pointing)** |
| **Gain** | Very high (40–60 dBi) | Moderate (20–35 dBi) per beam |
| **Bandwidth** | Very wide (multi-octave) | Narrow per element; overall moderate |
| **Multi-beam capability** | Difficult (one feed per beam → multiple feed cluster) | **Natural — many simultaneous beams** |
| **Mass & volume** | Heavy when large | Flat, conformal to satellite body |
| **Cost** | Moderate | High (electronics per element) |
| **Failure mode** | Single-point mechanical | Graceful degradation (some elements can fail) |
| **Typical use** | GEO comsats, DTH, deep space | LEO constellations, mobile satcom, military |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define EIRP. Why is it considered the most important transmit-side parameter in a satellite link?**

**Model Answer:**

> **EIRP (Equivalent Isotropically Radiated Power)** is defined as the product of the RF power delivered to the antenna terminals and the gain of the transmitting antenna in the direction of the receiver.

**Mathematically:**

$$
EIRP = P_t \times G_t
$$

It is expressed in **watts (W)** or more commonly in **dBW** using $EIRP_{dBW} = P_{t,dBW} + G_{t,dBi}$.

**Importance:** EIRP lumps together the two transmit-side variables (power and antenna gain) into a single quantity that describes *how strongly the satellite is "shouting" toward the ground*. The satellite link budget equation starts from EIRP, and regulatory bodies (ITU, FCC) directly limit the maximum EIRP a satellite may transmit to prevent interference between co-frequency birds in the geostationary arc. Higher EIRP allows smaller, cheaper ground dishes — a critical commercial factor for DTH TV and VSAT services.

*(Examiner tip: Always specify units — dBW or dBm. Do not confuse dBi and dBd; dBd = dBi − 2.15.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the focal property of a parabolic reflector. How is it used in satellite communication?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Focal Property of a Parabola:** Every ray emanating from the *focus* of a parabolic surface and striking the reflecting surface is reflected into a path **parallel to the axis of symmetry** of the parabola. Conversely, parallel rays arriving along the axis converge exactly at the focal point.

**Use in Satellite Communication:**

1. A **feed horn** placed at the focal point radiates spherical wavefronts; the parabolic dish converts these into a **collimated plane wave** (parallel beam) propagating along the axis — forming a high-gain directional beam toward Earth.
2. In *receive* mode, an incoming plane wave from Earth is focused back to the feed horn at the focal point, where a low-noise amplifier (LNA) boosts the weak signal.
3. This is the principle behind every DTH dish, VSAT terminal, and the large reflectors on GEO communication satellites.

*(Examiner tip: Mention the f/D ratio and the existence of offset-fed variants like the Cassegrain and Gregorian configurations for extra credit.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Choice 1]`**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **gain of a parabolic reflector antenna** in terms of its diameter and operating wavelength. **Define all symbols used.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GEO communication satellite uses a parabolic reflector of diameter **3.5 m** operating at a downlink frequency of **4 GHz** with an aperture efficiency of **0.6**. Compute:
  (i) the **gain in dBi**,
  (ii) the **half-power beamwidth** in degrees,
  (iii) the **EIRP in dBW** if the travelling-wave-tube amplifier (TWTA) delivers **40 W** to the antenna feed.
**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Gain:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. Summarised:

**Step 1:** Fundamental gain-aperture identity:

$$
G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Effective aperture = aperture efficiency × physical area:

$$
A_e = \eta_a \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and simplify:

$$
G = \eta_a \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2
$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{G = \eta_a \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2}
$$

**Symbol Definitions:**

- $G$ = antenna gain (dimensionless, also in dBi)
- $\eta_a$ = aperture efficiency (dimensionless, 0 ≤ ηₐ ≤ 1)
- $D$ = physical diameter of the dish aperture (metres)
- $\lambda$ = operating wavelength (metres)

**Conceptual Explanation:** Gain scales with the square of the ratio $D/\lambda$, meaning a larger dish or higher frequency dramatically increases the directive performance.

*(7 marks: 2 for stating the identity, 2 for substituting physical area, 2 for simplification, 1 for symbols and physical interpretation.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Given Data:**

- $D = 3.5$ m
- $f = 4$ GHz $= 4 \times 10^9$ Hz
- $\eta_a = 0.6$
- $P_t = 40$ W

**Step 1 — Calculate the wavelength:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^9} = 0.075 \text{ m} = 7.5 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the gain (linear):**

$$
G = \eta_a \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 0.6 \times \left(\frac{\pi \times 3.5}{0.075}\right)^2
$$

Intermediate calculations:

$$
\frac{\pi \times 3.5}{0.075} = \frac{10.996}{0.075} = 146.61
$$

Squaring:

$$
(146.61)^2 = 21,494.5
$$

Multiplying by $\eta_a$:

$$
G = 0.6 \times 21,494.5 = 12,897
$$

**Convert to dBi:**

$$
G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(12,897) = 10 \times 4.1105 = \mathbf{41.10 \text{ dBi}}
$$

**Step 3 — Half-power beamwidth:**

$$
\theta_{3dB} = \frac{70 \lambda}{D} = \frac{70 \times 0.075}{3.5} = \frac{5.25}{3.5} = \mathbf{1.5^\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — EIRP in dBW:**

First convert $P_t$ to dBW:

$$
P_{t,dBW} = 10 \log_{10}(40) = 10 \times 1.602 = 16.02 \text{ dBW}
$$

Then:

$$
EIRP_{dBW} = P_{t,dBW} + G_{t,dBi} = 16.02 + 41.10 = \mathbf{57.12 \text{ dBW}}
$$

---

### **Final Tabulated Answer for Q-A Part (b):**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Wavelength $\lambda$ | 0.075 m (7.5 cm) |
| **Gain $G$** | **41.10 dBi** |
| **HPBW $\theta_{3dB}$** | **1.5°** |
| **EIRP** | **57.12 dBW** |

*(7 marks: 2 for gain computation, 2 for beamwidth, 2 for EIRP, 1 for correct unit conversions.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice 2]`**

**(a)** With the aid of a **neat block diagram**, explain the construction and working of a **phased array antenna** used on-board a satellite. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** over a parabolic reflector antenna. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Friis transmission equation** for a satellite communication link. A satellite in GEO orbit at an altitude of **36,000 km** transmits an EIRP of **55 dBW** at **6 GHz** toward an Earth station whose antenna gain is **50 dBi** and system noise temperature is **150 K**. Calculate the **received power** $P_r$ and the **carrier-to-noise ratio (C/N)** in a bandwidth of **20 MHz**. **(Take Boltzmann constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.)** **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Phased Array Antenna:**

**Construction:** A phased array consists of an **ordered grid of identical radiating elements** (typically microstrip patches, dipoles, or waveguide slots) arranged in a 1-D linear or 2-D planar configuration. Each element is fed through a **programmable phase shifter** (and often a variable attenuator for amplitude tapering). A central **beam-steering computer** calculates the progressive phase difference $\Delta\phi$ to be applied across the array to point the beam in the desired direction. The corporate feed network distributes RF power to/from all elements. For active arrays, every element has its own T/R (Transmit/Receive) module containing a solid-state power amplifier and LNA.

**Working Principle:** When all elements radiate in-phase, the far-field contributions add constructively along the broadside direction. By progressively retarding the phase of successive elements by an amount:

$$
\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \sin \theta_0
$$

the main beam can be electronically steered to any angle $\theta_0$ within the array's scan range, without any mechanical motion.

**Block Diagram Reference:** See **Section 4.4** of these notes for the complete functional flow.

**Advantages over Parabolic Reflector:**

1. **Electronic (microsecond) beam steering** — ideal for LEO satellites that move rapidly across the sky.
2. **Simultaneous multiple beams** — one array can generate many independent beams pointing at different users.
3. **Conformal** — flat panels mount on the satellite body without a protruding dish.
4. **Graceful degradation** — if 5% of elements fail, performance drops only marginally.

**Limitations:**

1. **Lower gain per beam** (typically 20–35 dBi) compared to a large parabolic reflector.
2. **Scan losses** — gain drops as the beam is steered off broadside.
3. **Complex and expensive** — requires thousands of phase shifters and a powerful beam-steering computer.
4. **Limited bandwidth** per element; very wideband operation is harder than with a dish.

*(7 marks: 2 for construction, 2 for working, 1 for diagram, 2 for advantages/limitations.)*

---

**Part (b) — Friis Transmission and C/N Computation:**

**Derivation:** Refer to **Section 3.4** of these notes. The final result is:

$$
\boxed{P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2}
$$

Equivalently, in logarithmic form:

$$
P_{r,dBW} = EIRP_{dBW} + G_{r,dBi} - FSPL_{dB}
$$

where:

$$
FSPL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

- Altitude $d = 36{,}000 \text{ km} = 3.6 \times 10^7$ m
- $EIRP = 55$ dBW
- $f = 6$ GHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / 6 \times 10^9 = 0.05$ m
- $G_r = 50$ dBi
- $T_s = 150$ K
- $B = 20$ MHz
- $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K

**Step 2 — Compute Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL):**

$$
FSPL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi \times 3.6 \times 10^7}{0.05}\right)
$$

First compute the numerator:

$$
4\pi \times 3.6 \times 10^7 = 4 \times 3.1416 \times 3.6 \times 10^7 = 4.5239 \times 10^8
$$

Divide by $\lambda$:

$$
\frac{4.5239 \times 10^8}{0.05} = 9.0478 \times 10^9
$$

Take $20 \log_{10}$:

$$
20 \log_{10}(9.0478 \times 10^9) = 20 \times (9 + \log_{10}(9.0478)) = 20 \times (9 + 0.9567) = 20 \times 9.9567 = \mathbf{199.13 \text{ dB}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Received Power:**

$$
P_{r,dBW} = EIRP_{dBW} + G_{r,dBi} - FSPL_{dB} = 55 + 50 - 199.13 = \mathbf{-94.13 \text{ dBW}}
$$
