Convert to watts:

$$
P_r = 10^{(-94.13/10)} = 10^{-9.413} = 3.86 \times 10^{-10} \text{ W} = \mathbf{0.386 \text{ nW}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Noise Power:**

$$
N = k \, T_s \, B
$$

$$
N = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 150 \times 20 \times 10^6
$$

$$
N = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 3 \times 10^9 = 4.14 \times 10^{-14} \text{ W}
$$

Convert to dBW:

$$
N_{dBW} = 10 \log_{10}(4.14 \times 10^{-14}) = 10 \times (-13.383) = \mathbf{-133.83 \text{ dBW}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute C/N Ratio:**

$$
(C/N)_{dB} = P_{r,dBW} - N_{dBW} = -94.13 - (-133.83) = \mathbf{39.70 \text{ dB}}
$$

---

### **Final Tabulated Answer for Q-B Part (b):**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Wavelength $\lambda$ | 0.05 m |
| Free-space path loss | 199.13 dB |
| **Received power $P_r$** | **−94.13 dBW (≈ 0.386 nW)** |
| Noise power $N$ | −133.83 dBW |
| **C/N ratio** | **39.70 dB** |

*(7 marks: 2 for derivation, 2 for $P_r$ calculation, 2 for noise power, 1 for C/N.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Students often mix dBW (decibels relative to 1 W) with dBm (decibels relative to 1 mW). Remember: **dBW + 30 = dBm**. EIRP in KTU problems is almost always in **dBW**.
> 2. **dBi vs dBd:** Always write **dBi** (relative to isotropic). If the problem gives dBd, convert using $G_{dBi} = G_{dBd} + 2.15$.
> 3. **Forgetting log10 base 10:** Power ratios use $\log_{10}$, while amplitude ratios use $20 \log_{10}$. Path loss uses **20** (not 10) because it is a power quantity expressed in dB.
> 4. **Confusing HPBW and FNBW:** The constant is **70** for half-power beamwidth and **140** for first-null beamwidth.
> 5. **Missing $\eta_a$:** Do not write $G = (\pi D/\lambda)^2$ — always include the aperture efficiency $\eta_a$.
> 6. **Sign errors in C/N:** Noise power is negative in dBW; subtracting a negative *adds* magnitude to C/N.
> 7. **Forgetting Boltzmann's constant units:** k is in J/K, B in Hz, so N is in watts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Satellite Antennas: Quick Recall Box**

- [x] **Satellite antenna = high-gain directional radiator** that converts guided RF into a free-space beam (and vice versa).
- [x] **Parabolic reflector** is the workhorse — uses the focal property to convert spherical waves into a collimated plane wave.
- [x] **Gain formula:** $G = \eta_a (\pi D/\lambda)^2$. **Remember $\eta_a$!**
- [x] **HPBW:** $\theta_{3dB} \approx 70\lambda/D$ (degrees). **FNBW:** $140\lambda/D$.
- [x] **Aperture efficiency** $\eta_a$ for a typical parabolic dish: **0.55 – 0.70**; uniform illumination max = **0.833**.
- [x] **EIRP** = $P_t G_t$ (in W or dBW). EIRP + receive antenna gain − FSPL = received power (dBW).
- [x] **Friis equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$.
- [x] **Free-space path loss:** $FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi d/\lambda)$ in dB.
- [x] **G/T (figure of merit):** $G/T = G_r / T_s$ (dB/K), decisive for receive quality.
- [x] **Antenna types** to know: horn, parabolic (single, Cassegrain, Gregorian), phased array, helical, lens.
- [x] **Phased array** = electronic beam steering via progressive phase shift $\Delta\phi = (2\pi d/\lambda)\sin\theta_0$.
- [x] **Cassegrain:** secondary hyperboloidal reflector placed between focus and dish; allows feed to be placed *behind* the dish (lower loss, easier maintenance).
- [x] **Gregorian:** secondary *ellipsoidal* reflector beyond the focal point; used similarly.
- [x] **Polarization** for satellites: typically **circular (RHCP/LHCP)** at C-band and above to bypass Faraday rotation.
- [x] **Sidelobe level (SLL)** must be ≤ −20 dB to limit interference between co-frequency satellites in GEO arc.
- [x] **Constant memorisation:** λ = c/f. For 1 GHz → 0.3 m; 4 GHz → 0.075 m; 12 GHz → 0.025 m.
- [x] **Conversion:** $G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10} G$. $N_{dBW} = 10 \log_{10}(kT_s B)$.
- [x] **Boltzmann:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Satellite Link Design - Basic Link Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Satellite Link Design - Basic Link Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Satellite Link Design (Basic Link Analysis)** is the systematic process of evaluating the power budget and noise budget of an RF communication path between an Earth Station (ES), the satellite (Space Segment), and the destination Earth Station. The link comprises two unidirectional paths:
- **Uplink (Earth → Space):** Frequency band typically in the **14 GHz** range (Ku-band) for commercial geostationary systems.
- **Downlink (Space → Earth):** Frequency band typically in the **12 GHz** range (Ku-band) for commercial geostationary systems.

The fundamental objective of link analysis is to determine the **Carrier-to-Noise Ratio (C/N)** at the receiver input, which ultimately governs the **Bit Error Rate (BER)** of the digital (or S/N for analog) communication system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The basic link analysis forms the foundation for all advanced satellite link engineering topics — link budget, G/T, EIRP, C/N, Eb/No, and combined uplink-downlink performance. Every KTU question in this module is built on these primitives.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Megaphone Across the Valley" Analogy**
>
> Imagine two friends standing on opposite edges of a wide valley. Friend A uses a **megaphone** (transmit antenna gain) to amplify his voice. The louder the shout and the better the megaphone, the more sound energy reaches Friend B. However, the **air absorbs and spreads** the sound as it travels — this is **path loss**. Friend B uses a **parabolic dish microphone** (receive antenna) to collect as much sound as possible. But there is also **wind noise, ambient crowd noise, and electronic hiss** in the mic — this is the **system noise**.
>
> The **ratio of how clear Friend A's voice is to all that noise** (Carrier-to-Noise) determines if Friend B can understand the message. If the signal is too weak or the noise too strong, the message is lost. The link designer's job is to balance the megaphone power, dish size, and the noise environment to make the message intelligible.

## 1.3 Core Building Blocks of a Satellite Link

| Block | Symbol | Units | Real-World Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transmit Power | $P_t$ | W (dBW) | RF amplifier output |
| Transmit Antenna Gain | $G_t$ | dBi | Directivity of TX dish |
| **Effective Isotropic Radiated Power** | **EIRP** | **dBW** | **Power "felt" by receiver** |
| Free Space Path Loss | $L_p$ | dB | Spreading loss in vacuum |
| Receive Antenna Gain | $G_r$ | dBi | Directivity of RX dish |
| Received Power (Carrier) | $C$ or $P_r$ | dBW | Useful signal at LNA input |
| System Noise Temperature | $T_s$ | K (dBK) | Total receiver + sky noise |
| **Figure of Merit** | **G/T** | **dB/K** | **Receiver sensitivity** |
| Boltzmann's Constant | $k$ | -228.6 dBW/K/Hz | Physical constant |
| Bandwidth | $B$ | Hz (dBHz) | Information bandwidth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded entries** are *figure-of-merit quantities* that the examiner often asks you to define in 2–3 mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block Flow (How a Satellite Link Works)

A satellite communication link is engineered as a **cascaded RF chain**. The signal experiences power additions (gain) and subtractions (loss) at each stage.

### Step 1 — Uplink Transmission
The Earth Station (ES) transmitter feeds RF power $P_t$ to a high-gain parabolic antenna. The signal is radiated as a narrow beam pointed at the geostationary satellite.

### Step 2 — Free Space Propagation (Uplink)
The signal travels $\approx 36{,}000$ km to the satellite. The wavefront spreads spherically, and the energy per unit area decreases as $1/d^2$. This is the **Free Space Path Loss (FSPL)**.

### Step 3 — Satellite Reception & Transponder
The satellite's receive antenna collects the energy. The **Low Noise Amplifier (LNA)** boosts the weak signal, and the **transponder** (frequency converter + HPA) translates the uplink frequency to the downlink frequency.

### Step 4 — Downlink Transmission
The satellite radiates the down-converted signal back to Earth using its transmit antenna (typically with global, regional, or spot beam coverage).

### Step 5 — Free Space Propagation (Downlink)
The signal traverses the same path back, suffering another FSPL.

### Step 6 — Earth Station Reception
The ES receive antenna captures the signal, and the LNA + down-converter produce an IF/baseband output. The quality of this signal depends on **how much larger the carrier is than the accumulated noise**.

## 2.2 Why Each Parameter Matters

- **$P_t \cdot G_t$ (EIRP)**: Determines how "loud" the transmitter shouts.
- **$L_p$**: Sets how much the signal weakens in transit — this is the dominant loss term (often $> 200$ dB at GEO distance).
- **$G_r / T_s$ (G/T)**: Determines how "well" the receiver listens. Higher G/T = better weak-signal detection.
- **$kT_sB$**: Sets the noise floor; larger bandwidth carries more thermal noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** G/T and EIRP are the *bargaining chips* of the satellite operator. Operators sell transponder capacity quoted in terms of **dBW EIRP** and **dB/K G/T** — the same parameters you will compute in the KTU exam.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula (Linear) | Formula (dB) | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EIRP | $EIRP = P_t \cdot G_t$ | $[EIRP] = [P_t] + [G_t]$ | dBW or dBW |
| 2 | Free Space Path Loss | $L_p = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | $[L_p] = 32.45 + 20\log(d_{km}) + 20\log(f_{MHz})$ | dB; dBHz, km |
| 3 | FSPL (GHz form) | — | $[L_p] = 92.45 + 20\log(d_{km}) + 20\log(f_{GHz})$ | dB |
| 4 | Received Carrier Power | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r}{L_p} = \dfrac{EIRP \cdot G_r}{L_p}$ | $[P_r] = [EIRP] + [G_r] - [L_p]$ | W, dBW |
| 5 | Boltzmann Constant | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K | $[k] = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz | Constant |
| 6 | System Noise Temp | $T_s = \dfrac{T_a}{L_f} + T_0\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{L_f}\right) + T_{LNA}$ | $[T_s] = 10\log(T_s \text{ in K})$ | K, dBK |
| 7 | G/T (Figure of Merit) | $G/T = \dfrac{G_r}{T_s}$ | $[G/T] = [G_r] - [T_s]$ | dB/K |
| 8 | Carrier-to-Noise Ratio | $\dfrac{C}{N} = \dfrac{EIRP \cdot G_r}{k T_s B L_p}$ | $[C/N] = [EIRP] - [L_p] + [G/T] - [k] - [B]$ | dB, dBHz |
| 9 | $E_b / N_0$ | $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \dfrac{C}{N} \cdot \dfrac{B}{R_b}$ | $[E_b/N_0] = [C/N] - 10\log(R_b/B)$ | dB |
| 10 | Combined C/N (single hop) | $\dfrac{1}{(C/N)_{tot}} = \dfrac{1}{(C/N)_u} + \dfrac{1}{(C/N)_d}$ | Sum of inverse powers | dB |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Table Trap:** When converting **linear** quantities to dB (e.g., $T_s$ in K → dBK), use $10\log_{10}$ (not $20\log_{10}$). The $20\log_{10}$ is only for **ratios of like quantities** (gain, loss, voltage). Many students lose marks here.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Friis Transmission Equation (Basic Link Equation)

This is the **fundamental link equation** from which all satellite link analysis flows.

**Step 1:** Consider an **isotropic radiator** radiating total power $P_t$ uniformly in all directions. The power flux density (PFD) at a distance $d$ on a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$ is:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \quad \text{(W/m}^{2}\text{)}$$

**Step 2:** A **directional transmit antenna** with gain $G_t$ concentrates this flux in the boresight direction. The PFD along boresight becomes:

$$S = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t}{4\pi d^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** The **receive antenna** has an effective aperture area:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi} \quad \text{(m}^{2}\text{)}$$

**Step 4:** The **received power** is the product of flux density and effective aperture:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \left(\frac{P_t \cdot G_t}{4\pi d^{2}}\right)\left(\frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d)^{2}}$$

**Step 6:** Defining **Free Space Path Loss** as the squared inverse of the wavelength factor:

$$L_p = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 7:** Substituting $L_p$:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{L_p} = \frac{EIRP \cdot G_r}{L_p}$$

**Step 8:** In dB form (the **link budget equation**):

$$[P_r] = [P_t] + [G_t] + [G_r] - [L_p] = [EIRP] + [G_r] - [L_p] \quad \text{(dBW)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **mother equation** of satellite link design. Memorize it cold for KTU exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carrier-to-Noise Ratio (C/N) at the Receiver

**Step 1:** The received carrier power is given by the link equation:

$$C = P_r = \frac{EIRP \cdot G_r}{L_p}$$

**Step 2:** The total noise power at the receiver input over bandwidth $B$ at the reference temperature $T_s$ is given by:

$$N = k \cdot T_s \cdot B$$

> Here $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is Boltzmann's constant, $T_s$ is the **system noise temperature** in Kelvin, and $B$ is the equivalent noise bandwidth in Hz.

**Step 3:** The Carrier-to-Noise Ratio is:

$$\frac{C}{N} = \frac{EIRP \cdot G_r}{L_p \cdot k \cdot T_s \cdot B}$$

**Step 4:** Grouping antenna and noise terms into the Figure of Merit $G/T$:

$$\frac{C}{N} = \frac{EIRP}{L_p \cdot k \cdot B} \cdot \left(\frac{G_r}{T_s}\right) = \frac{EIRP}{L_p \cdot k \cdot B} \cdot (G/T)$$

**Step 5:** Converting to dB:

$$\left[\frac{C}{N}\right] = [EIRP] - [L_p] + [G/T] - [k] - [B] \quad \text{(dB)}$$

> where $[B] = 10\log_{10}(B \text{ in Hz})$ in dBHz and $[k] = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined Uplink + Downlink C/N (Single Hop)

In a real satellite link, the signal suffers noise contamination on **both** the uplink and downlink. The total C/N is **not** the arithmetic sum but the **harmonic sum** (in linear domain).

**Step 1:** Define:
- $C/N_u$ = uplink carrier-to-noise ratio (at satellite input)
- $C/N_d$ = downlink carrier-to-noise ratio (at ES input)
- $C$ = received carrier power (assume same on both links for transparent transponder)
- $N_u$ = noise power added in uplink
- $N_d$ = noise power added in downlink

**Step 2:** The **total noise** at the final receiver is the sum of both contributions (assuming independent noise sources):

$$N_{tot} = N_u + N_d$$

**Step 3:** The **combined C/N** is:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{tot} = \frac{C}{N_u + N_d}$$

**Step 4:** Divide numerator and denominator by $C$:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{tot} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{C/N_u} + \frac{1}{C/N_d}}$$

**Step 5:** In dB (you cannot simply add the dB values):

$$\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{tot,dB} = -10\log_{10}\!\left[10^{-(C/N)_u/10} + 10^{-(C/N)_d/10}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The combined C/N is **always less than the smaller** of the two individual C/Ns. The weaker link dominates — design both links to be roughly balanced.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numerical Setup

| Given | Symbol | What to Find | Formula Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_t, G_t, G_r, d, f$ | Standard | $P_r$ | $[P_r] = [P_t] + [G_t] + [G_r] - [L_p]$ |
| $P_r, T_s, B$ | Standard | $C/N$ | $[C/N] = [P_r] - [k] - [T_s] - [B]$ |
| $T_a, L_f, T_{LNA}$ | System | $T_s$ | $T_s = T_a/L_f + T_0(1-1/L_f) + T_{LNA}$ |
| $G_r, T_s$ | Standard | $G/T$ | $[G/T] = [G_r] - 10\log(T_s)$ |
| $C/N, R_b, B$ | Standard | $E_b/N_0$ | $[E_b/N_0] = [C/N] - 10\log(R_b/B)$ |
| $C/N_u, C/N_d$ | Standard | $(C/N)_{tot}$ | Harmonic sum formula |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Complete Satellite Link

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional power and noise flow** of an end-to-end satellite link, including both uplink and downlink paths, with the figure-of-merit quantities (EIRP, G/T, C/N) annotated.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    esTx["Earth Station Transmitter Power Pt dBW"]
    esAmp["TX Antenna Gain Gt dBi"]
    eirpUp["Uplink EIRP Pt plus Gt dBW"]
    lpU["Free Space Loss Lpu dB"]
    satRx["Satellite RX Antenna Gain Gru dBi"]
    cnU["Uplink CN Ratio CNu dB"]
    noiseU["Satellite System Noise Tsu K"]
    kBol["Boltzmann k minus 228.6 dBW KHz"]
    bwU["Bandwidth B dBHz"]
    tp["Transponder Frequency Conversion"]
    satTx["Satellite TX Antenna Gain Gtd dBi"]
    eirpDown["Downlink EIRP sat dBW"]
    lpD["Free Space Loss Lpd dB"]
    esRx["Earth Station RX Antenna Gain Grd dBi"]
    cnD["Downlink CN Ratio CNd dB"]
    noiseD["ES System Noise Tsd K"]
    bwD["Bandwidth B dBHz"]
    comb["Combined CN Total dB"]
    ebno["Eb over N0 dB"]
    ber["Bit Error Rate BER"]

    esTx --> eirpUp
    esAmp --> eirpUp
    eirpUp --> lpU
    lpU --> satRx
    satRx --> cnU
    noiseU --> cnU
    kBol --> cnU
    bwU --> cnU

    cnU --> tp
    tp --> satTx
    satTx --> eirpDown
    eirpDown --> lpD
    lpD --> esRx
    esRx --> cnD
    noiseD --> cnD
    kBol --> cnD
    bwD --> cnD

    cnU --> comb
    cnD --> comb
    comb --> ebno
    ebno --> ber
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every path from the transmitter to the **Combined C/N** block represents one complete analytical step in the KTU link budget. The two independent noise injection points (`noiseU` and `noiseD`) are precisely why the combined C/N uses the **harmonic sum** formula derived in Section 3.3.

## 4.2 Power-Only Flow (Simplified, for Quick Recall)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Pt dBW"] --> C["EIRP Pt plus Gt dBW"]
    B["Gt dBi"] --> C
    C --> E["Received Pr EIRP minus Lp plus Gr dBW"]
    D["Lp dB"] --> E
    F["Gr dBi"] --> E
    E --> G["C over N Pr minus k minus Ts minus B dB"]
    H["k minus 228.6"] --> G
    I["Ts dBK"] --> G
    J["B dBHz"] --> G
```

> This minimalist power-chain is what you should be able to **reproduce on the answer paper** in under 90 seconds for full marks on a 7-mark derivation question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define **EIRP** and explain its significance in satellite link design. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power) is the product of the RF power delivered to the transmit antenna and the gain of that antenna in the boresight direction.
  $$EIRP = P_t \cdot G_t \quad \text{or} \quad [EIRP]_{dBW} = [P_t]_{dBW} + [G_t]_{dB}$$
- **Physical meaning (1 mark):** It represents the power that *would* have to be radiated by an **isotropic** (ideal, omnidirectional) antenna to produce the same on-axis flux density as the actual directional antenna.
- **Significance in link design (1 mark):** EIRP is a key input to the **link budget equation** $[P_r] = [EIRP] + [G_r] - [L_p]$. It encapsulates both amplifier capability and antenna directivity into a single transmitter descriptor, simplifying the link calculation. Operators sell satellite capacity quoted in **dBW EIRP per carrier**.

---

### **Q2.** What is the **G/T ratio** (Figure of Merit) of an Earth Station? Why is it a critical design parameter? **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The G/T ratio is the receive antenna gain divided by the system noise temperature, expressed in dB/K:
  $$\left[\frac{G}{T}\right] = [G_r]_{dBi} - [T_s]_{dBK}$$
- **System noise temperature** $T_s$ (1 mark) is the sum of the antenna noise temperature referred to the LNA input, the feeder-loss noise contribution $T_0(1-1/L_f)$, and the LNA equivalent noise temperature:
  $$T_s = \frac{T_a}{L_f} + T_0\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{L_f}\right) + T_{LNA}$$
- **Significance (1 mark):** G/T quantifies the **uplink sensitivity** of an Earth Station. A higher G/T allows the ES to close the uplink with lower EIRP (smaller dish or HPA). It directly determines the achievable uplink C/N. The G/T also dictates the minimum antenna size and LNA quality — both major cost drivers.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **🅐 Question A** (Choose this OR Question B)

#### **(a) Derive the basic link equation for satellite communication starting from the concept of flux density and effective aperture. State clearly the meaning of each term.** **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Model Question]**

**Model Solution:**

The basic link equation (Friis transmission equation in free space) is derived as follows.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For an isotropic radiator radiating power $P_t$, the power flux density (PFD) on a sphere of radius $d$ is:
$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \quad \text{(W/m}^{2}\text{)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** A directional transmit antenna of gain $G_t$ concentrates this flux along the boresight:
$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^{2}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The receive antenna's effective aperture area in terms of its gain $G_r$ and wavelength $\lambda$ is:
$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** The received power is the product of PFD and effective aperture:
$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d)^{2}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Defining free space path loss $L_p = (4\pi d / \lambda)^{2}$ and EIRP $= P_t G_t$, we obtain the **basic link equation**:
$$\boxed{\,P_r = \frac{EIRP \cdot G_r}{L_p}\,} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{\,[P_r] = [EIRP] + [G_r] - [L_p]\, \text{dBW}\,}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Term meanings:
- $P_t$ = RF power delivered to the TX antenna (W)
- $G_t$ = transmit antenna gain (linear, dimensionless)
- $G_r$ = receive antenna gain (linear)
- $d$ = slant range between TX and RX (m)
- $\lambda$ = carrier wavelength (m)
- $L_p$ = free space path loss (dimensionless, expressed in dB)

---

#### **(b) A geostationary satellite link operates at 14 GHz uplink. The slant range to the satellite is 36,000 km. The Earth Station HPA delivers 100 W to an antenna of gain 60 dBi. The satellite receive antenna has a gain of 30 dBi. Compute: (i) the EIRP, (ii) the free space path loss, and (iii) the received power at the satellite input.** **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style Numerical]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- $f = 14 \text{ GHz} = 14{,}000 \text{ MHz}$, $d = 36{,}000$ km
- $P_t = 100 \text{ W} = 20 \text{ dBW}$
- $G_t = 60$ dBi, $G_r = 30$ dBi

**(i) EIRP (2 marks):**
$$[EIRP] = [P_t] + [G_t] = 20 + 60 = \mathbf{80 \text{ dBW}}$$

**(ii) Free Space Path Loss (2 marks):**
$$[L_p] = 32.45 + 20\log(d_{km}) + 20\log(f_{MHz})$$
$$[L_p] = 32.45 + 20\log(36{,}000) + 20\log(14{,}000)$$
$$[L_p] = 32.45 + (20 \times 4.5563) + (20 \times 4.1461)$$
$$[L_p] = 32.45 + 91.13 + 82.92 = \mathbf{206.50 \text{ dB}}$$

**(iii) Received Power (2 marks):**
$$[P_r] = [EIRP] + [G_r] - [L_p] = 80 + 30 - 206.50$$
$$\boxed{\,[P_r] = -96.50 \text{ dBW}\,}$$

Equivalently, $P_r = 10^{-9.65} \approx 2.24 \times 10^{-10}$ W = 224 pW. This tiny power justifies the satellite's ultra-low-noise front end (LNA with $T_{LNA} \approx 30$–$100$ K).

---

### **🅑 Question B** (Choose this OR Question A)

#### **(a) Explain the concept of system noise temperature and G/T ratio in a satellite receiver. Derive the expression for combined uplink–downlink C/N ratio.** **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Solution:**

**System Noise Temperature (3 marks):** The total system noise temperature $T_s$ referred to the input of the LNA is the sum of three independent noise contributions:

$$T_s = \frac{T_a}{L_f} + T_0\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{L_f}\right) + T_{LNA}$$

where:
- $T_a$ = antenna noise temperature (sky + ground radiation, typically 30–70 K at Ku-band)
- $L_f$ = feeder/cable loss factor (linear, > 1)
- $T_0 = 290$ K = reference ambient temperature
- $T_{LNA}$ = LNA equivalent noise temperature (typically 30–150 K)

The first term represents the attenuated sky/ground noise arriving at the LNA; the second term is the thermal noise injected by the lossy feeder; the third is the LNA's own internally generated noise.

**G/T Ratio (1 mark):** The Figure of Merit, $G/T = G_r / T_s$, expressed in dB/K as $[G/T] = [G_r] - [T_s]$ (dBK), is the single number that characterizes receiver sensitivity.

**Combined C/N Derivation (3 marks):** The total noise at the final receiver is $N_{tot} = N_u + N_d$. Therefore:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{tot} = \frac{C}{N_u + N_d} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{C/N_u} + \dfrac{1}{C/N_d}}$$

In dB:
$$\boxed{\,\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{tot,dB} = -10\log_{10}\!\left[10^{-(C/N)_u/10} + 10^{-(C/N)_d/10}\right]\,}$$

The combined C/N is **always less than the minimum of the two individual C/Ns**.

---

#### **(b) An Earth Station operates at 4 GHz. Its antenna gain is 50 dBi. The antenna noise temperature is 35 K, the LNA noise temperature is 50 K, and the feeder loss is 0.5 dB. Calculate the system noise temperature and the G/T ratio.** **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Numerical, Module 2]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- $G_r = 50$ dBi
- $T_a = 35$ K, $T_{LNA} = 50$ K
- $L_f = 0.5 \text{ dB} = 10^{0.5/10} = 1.1220$ (linear)
- $T_0 = 290$ K

**Step 1 — Feeder loss factor and intermediate quantities (1 mark):**
$$L_f = 1.1220, \quad \frac{1}{L_f} = 0.8913, \quad 1 - \frac{1}{L_f} = 0.1087$$

**Step 2 — Antenna noise referred to LNA input (1 mark):**
$$T_{a}^{\prime} = \frac{T_a}{L_f} = \frac{35}{1.1220} = 31.19 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Feeder thermal noise contribution (1 mark):**
$$T_{feed} = T_0\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{L_f}\right) = 290 \times 0.1087 = 31.53 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — System noise temperature (1 mark):**
$$T_s = T_{a}^{\prime} + T_{feed} + T_{LNA} = 31.19 + 31.53 + 50$$
$$\boxed{\,T_s = 112.72 \text{ K}\,}$$

**Step 5 — G/T ratio (2 marks):**
$$[T_s] = 10\log_{10}(112.72) = 20.52 \text{ dBK}$$
$$\left[\frac{G}{T}\right] = [G_r] - [T_s] = 50 - 20.52$$
$$\boxed{\,\left[\frac{G}{T}\right] = 29.48 \text{ dB/K}\,}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Basic Link Analysis Problems:**
> 1. **dB conversion error (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing $10\log$ (for power ratios, temperatures) and $20\log$ (for voltage/current ratios, FSPL in wavelength form). Always state which logarithm is used and why.
> 2. **FSPL formula confusion (–2 marks):** The form $32.45 + 20\log d_{km} + 20\log f_{MHz}$ is used with **km and MHz**. The form $92.45 + 20\log d_{km} + 20\log f_{GHz}$ requires **GHz**. Mixing units costs full marks.
> 3. **Combined C/N error (–2 marks):** Adding two C/N values in dB **directly** is **wrong**. The harmonic sum formula (inverse powers) is mandatory.
> 4. **System noise temperature omission of $T_0(1-1/L_f)$ term (–1 mark):** Forgetting the feeder's self-noise. If feeder loss is given, this term is **non-zero** and must be included.
> 5. **EIRP units (–1 mark):** State EIRP in **dBW** (not dBm) — KTU board expects dBW. 1 dBW = 30 dBm.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Basic Link Equation:** $P_r = EIRP \cdot G_r / L_p$ (linear) or $[P_r] = [EIRP] + [G_r] - [L_p]$ (dBW).
- **EIRP** = $P_t + G_t$ (dBW). It is the single number describing transmitter strength in a given direction.
- **FSPL** at GEO ($d \approx 36{,}000$ km) is **> 200 dB** at Ku-band — by far the dominant loss term.
- **System Noise Temperature** $T_s$ aggregates antenna, feeder, and LNA noise into one figure (Kelvin).
- **G/T** (dB/K) is the receiver's Figure of Merit; higher G/T = better weak-signal reception.
- **C/N** in dB = $[EIRP] - [L_p] + [G/T] - [k] - [B]$, where $[k] = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz and $[B] = 10\log B$ in dBHz.
- **Combined Uplink + Downlink C/N** uses the **harmonic sum**, never direct addition.
- **$E_b / N_0$** = $C/N - 10\log(R_b / B)$ in dB — it is the quantity that directly links to BER.
- **Practical FSPL Formula (most-used):** $[L_p] = 32.45 + 20\log d_{km} + 20\log f_{MHz}$ (dB).
- **K to dBK conversion:** $[T_s] = 10\log(T_s \text{ in K})$ — use $10\log$, **never** $20\log$.
- **High-Yield Constants:** $k = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz; $T_0 = 290$ K; GEO altitude $\approx 35{,}786$ km.
- **Design Insight:** The link designer balances EIRP, antenna size, and noise temperature to meet a target C/N or $E_b / N_0$ — neither the uplink nor downlink should be allowed to bottleneck the total.
- **Golden Ratio Rule:** Aim for $(C/N)_u \approx (C/N)_d$ within 2–3 dB for cost-effective balanced design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interference Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interference Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In a satellite communication system, **Interference Analysis** is the systematic study and quantification of unwanted energy coupling between signals that share common resources such as frequency bands, orbital slots, or polarization states. As defined in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCECT751 – Module 2), interference is treated as a *random or deterministic additive noise-like component* that degrades the **Carrier-to-Noise ratio (C/N)** into the practical **Carrier-to-Noise-plus-Interference ratio (C/(N+I))**, directly bounding the achievable **Bit Error Rate (BER)** and link availability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Interference in satellite systems arises whenever a desired signal is contaminated by an unwanted signal occupying the same, adjacent, or harmonically related portion of the spectrum. The figure of merit is the ratio **C/I (Carrier-to-Interference)** or the combined **C/(N+I)**.*

The four classical interference sources studied in Module 2 are:

1. **Co-channel Interference (CCI)** — same-frequency reuse from another beam or satellite.
2. **Adjacent-Channel Interference (ACI)** — spectral leakage from neighbouring transponder channels.
3. **Intermodulation (IM) Interference** — non-linear distortion products generated inside the High Power Amplifier (HPA) or Travelling Wave Tube (TWT).
4. **Cross-Polarization Interference (XPI)** — coupling between orthogonally polarized signals due to imperfect antennas and the Faraday rotation of the ionosphere.

The combined effect is mathematically expressed as:

$$
\frac{C}{N+I} \;=\; \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{C/N} + \dfrac{1}{C/I}}
$$

where $C/N$ is the thermal carrier-to-noise ratio and $C/I$ is the carrier-to-interference ratio expressed in linear (not dB) units.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Stadium Whisper" Model**
>
> Imagine you are standing in the centre of a packed cricket stadium trying to hear your friend whisper instructions from Section A. Three things ruin your hearing:
>
> 1. **Co-channel interference:** Another friend in Section B shouts in the *exact same whisper frequency* (same carrier) — you cannot separate them by tuning.
> 2. **Adjacent-channel interference:** Your friend in Section C speaks in a *very close tone* (neighbouring frequency) — your ear is not a perfect filter, so a sliver leaks in.
> 3. **Intermodulation:** The loudspeaker playing music is slightly overloaded, producing ghostly harmonic echoes (sum and difference tones) that bleed into your friend's band.
>
> **Cross-polarization** is like a friend wearing horizontally striped sunglasses that you are interpreting as vertical stripes — your polarization filter is imperfect.
>
> The "clarity" of the message is the **C/(N+I) ratio**. The *quieter* the noise and interference floor, the cleaner the message.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constants Used in This Module**
>
> - **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23} \; \text{J/K}$ (exact, SI 2019).
> - **Earth–Geostationary orbit (GEO) radius:** $r_{GEO} \approx 42{,}164 \; \text{km}$ from Earth centre.
> - **Inter-satellite spacing (ITU-R S.728):** $2^\circ$ standard, $1.5^\circ$ for oversaturated arcs.
> - **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{F/m}$.
> - **Standard reference bandwidth for satellite C/N:** $B = 36 \; \text{MHz}$ (C-band transponder, or $36 \cdot 10^{6} \; \text{Hz}$).

## 1.4 Why Interference Analysis Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 2 is heavily tested under **CO2: "Analyze the performance of satellite links under noise and interference."** Typical KTU questions (Dec 2022, May 2023, Dec 2023 papers) ask students to:
> - Compute the **C/I ratio** from an interfering satellite's off-axis EIRP.
> - Derive the overall **C/(N+I)** when multiple interference sources co-exist.
> - Apply the **ITU-R off-axis antenna envelope** to determine angular discrimination.
> - Justify the choice of **orbital spacing** or **frequency reuse factor**.

A clear grasp of interference analysis is therefore non-negotiable for securing full marks in Part B numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Interference Mechanisms — A Structured Breakdown

The following enumeration directly maps to the KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus outcomes.

### 2.1.1 Co-Channel Interference (CCI)
- **Why it occurs:** Frequency reuse — the same band is reused in another spot-beam or by a co-located satellite in the visible arc.
- **How it manifests:** Two carriers at *identical* frequency arrive at the victim receiver.
- **Discrimination mechanism:** Spatial isolation via narrow antenna beams, or polarization discrimination.
- **Why it is hard to remove:** Conventional bandpass filters **cannot** separate the two; only spatial/polar diversity can.

### 2.1.2 Adjacent-Channel Interference (ACI)
- **Why it occurs:** Real filters have finite roll-off. Energy from a channel in the adjacent transponder slot leaks in.
- **How it manifests:** Spectral side-lobes spill over the guard-band.
- **Discrimination mechanism:** Higher-order filters (e.g., 5-pole Chebyshev), wider guard-bands.
- **Mitigation:** Pre-distortion of the transmitted spectrum to sharpen roll-off.

### 2.1.3 Intermodulation (IM) Distortion
- **Why it occurs:** The HPA (TWT or SSPA) operates near saturation for power efficiency. The amplitude non-linearity generates $2f_1 - f_2$, $2f_2 - f_1$, $3f_1 - 2f_2$, etc. — the **third-order intermodulation products** fall *inside* the wanted band.
- **How it manifests:** Discrete spurious tones plus a noise-like continuum.
- **Discrimination mechanism:** **Input Back-Off (IBO)** and **Output Back-Off (OBO)** — sacrificing power efficiency for linearity.
- **Key equation:** Third-order IM power $\propto (\text{input power})^3$, so every 1 dB of IBO reduces IM power by roughly **2 dB**.

### 2.1.4 Cross-Polarization Interference (XPI)
- **Why it occurs:** Earth-station antennas cannot achieve infinite polarization purity. Raindrops further depolarize signals (differential attenuation and phase shift).
- **Discrimination mechanism:** Polarization discrimination **XPD (Cross-Polarization Discrimination)** measured in dB.
- **Threshold:** ITU-R recommends **XPD ≥ 27 dB** for 4-PSK at clear-sky; this falls to ~10 dB in heavy rain.

### 2.1.5 Terrestrial / Inter-system Interference
- **Why it occurs:** Terrestrial microwave links (e.g., 4 GHz / 6 GHz fixed service) share bands with satellites. Conversely, satellite uplinks may interfere with terrestrial receivers.
- **Discrimination mechanism:** Coordination contours defined by **ITU-R SF.1006** and **RR Appendix 30B**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize** for the Interference Analysis module. It is exam-ready and arranged from fundamentals to advanced.

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | Combined CNIR | $\dfrac{C}{N+I} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{C/N} + \dfrac{1}{C/I}}$ | Linear (not dB) |
| 2 | CNIR in dB | $\left[ \dfrac{C}{N+I} \right]_{dB} \;=\; -10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{-C/N_{dB}/10} + 10^{-C/I_{dB}/10}\right)$ | Always convert ratios to dB before adding |
| 3 | Off-axis gain (main lobe) | $G(\theta) \;=\; G_{\max} - 12\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{\theta_0}\right)^{2}$ | for $\theta \le \theta_0$, $G$ in dBi |
| 4 | Off-axis gain (ITU-R first side-lobe) | $G(\theta) \;=\; G_{\max} - 12 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left[ \left(\dfrac{\theta}{\theta_0}\right)^{2} \right]$ | for $\theta_0 < \theta < 2.5\,\theta_0$ |
| 5 | Off-axis gain (far-out envelope, ITU-R S.580) | $G(\theta) \;=\; 32 - 25 \log_{10}\theta$ | for $2.5\,\theta_0 \le \theta \le 48^\circ$, $G$ in dBi |
| 6 | Off-axis gain (beyond $48^\circ$) | $G(\theta) \;=\; -10$ | dBi (assumed constant) |
| 7 | Free-space loss (interferer path) | $L_{fs} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | $d$ = range, $\lambda$ = wavelength |
| 8 | Received interference power | $P_{I} \;=\; EIRP_{I} \;+\; G_{r}(\theta) \;-\; L_{fs,I} \;-\; L_{other}$ | dBW; $G_r(\theta)$ = victim Rx gain towards interferer |
| 9 | C/I at input | $\left(\dfrac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} \;=\; EIRP_{D} - EIRP_{I} + G_{r,D}(0) - G_{r,I}(\theta) + \Delta L_{fs}$ | dB |
| 10 | Third-order IM power ratio | $\dfrac{P_{IM3}}{P_{out}} \;\approx\; 2\!\left( \text{OBO}_{dB} \right) - \text{const}$ | Empirical, valid for moderate back-off |
| 11 | Cross-polarization discrimination | $\text{XPD}_{dB} \;=\; P_{\text{co-pol}} - P_{\text{cross-pol}}$ | Larger XPD → less interference |
| 12 | Required orbital spacing (geometrical) | $\theta_{geo} \;\approx\; \dfrac{2 R_{GEO}}{R_E + r_{GEO}} \cdot \Delta \phi$ | rad; $\Delta\phi$ in rad |
| 13 | Bit-energy to noise spectral density | $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} \;=\; \dfrac{C}{N} \cdot \dfrac{B}{R_b}$ | where $R_b$ = bit rate |
| 14 | BER for BPSK in AWGN | $P_e \;=\; Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_b}{N_0 + I_0}}\right)$ | With interference treated as additional white noise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The off-axis gain formula $G(\theta) = 32 - 25 \log_{10}\theta$ is **enforced by ITU-R S.580 and S.728** for all geostationary satellites. It is the *most tested* formula in KTU 2024 papers and is required for any adjacent-satellite interference problem.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Interference Analysis

- **Spectrum Management:** ITU and national regulators (in India, the **WPC — Wireless Planning & Coordination Wing**) mandate coordination contours so that no satellite operator causes harmful interference.
- **Link Budget Engineering:** Without C/I, a satellite engineer cannot predict in-orbit BER. C/N alone is insufficient when co-located satellites are within $2^\circ$.
- **5G/6G Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTN):** LEO mega-constellations (Starlink, OneWeb) introduce *inter-satellite* interference across thousands of beams — interference analysis has become a cornerstone of NTN system design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Off-Axis Antenna Gain (ITU-R Reference Pattern)

The following derivation is the *exact* pattern recommended by **ITU-R S.580-6** and used as a regulatory reference in KTU 2024 problems.

**Given:** A circular aperture antenna of diameter $D$ operating at wavelength $\lambda$. The half-power beamwidth is:

$$
\theta_0 \;=\; \frac{70 \lambda}{D} \quad \text{[degrees]}
$$

**Step 1 — Define the four angular regions** along the off-boresight angle $\theta$:

- **Region 1 (Main lobe):** $0 \le \theta \le \theta_0$
- **Region 2 (Near side-lobe):** $\theta_0 < \theta < 2.5\,\theta_0$
- **Region 3 (Far-out envelope):** $2.5\,\theta_0 \le \theta \le 48^\circ$
- **Region 4 (Back lobe):** $\theta > 48^\circ$

**Step 2 — Derive the main-lobe gain** using the parabolic approximation of a uniformly illuminated aperture:

$$
G(\theta) \;=\; G_{\max} - 12 \left( \frac{\theta}{\theta_0} \right)^{2} \quad \text{[dBi]}
$$

*Logic:* The gain of a uniformly illuminated circular aperture follows a $(\sin x / x)^2$ pattern. Expanding the leading quadratic term yields the above parabolic drop-off.

**Step 3 — Side-lobe envelope derivation** (Region 3). Experimental measurements and electromagnetic analysis show that far-out side-lobes decay approximately as $\theta^{-2.5}$. Converting to dB:

$$
G(\theta) \;=\; 32 - 25 \log_{10}\theta \quad \text{[dBi, with $\theta$ in degrees]}
$$

*Logic:* The coefficient 32 is chosen so that the envelope is continuous with the Region 2 value at $\theta = 2.5\,\theta_0$ when $\theta_0 \approx 0.5^\circ$ (typical for a GEO satellite).

**Step 4 — Verify continuity** at $\theta = 48^\circ$:

$$
G(48^\circ) \;=\; 32 - 25 \log_{10}(48) \;=\; 32 - 25(1.6812) \;=\; 32 - 42.03 \;=\; -10.03 \; \text{dBi}
$$

This matches the back-lobe assumption $G = -10 \; \text{dBi}$ (Region 4). **Continuity confirmed.**

## 3.2 Derivation — Carrier-to-Interference (C/I) for Adjacent Satellite

Consider a *victim* earth station receiving signal from satellite **S1** at the boresight, while satellite **S2** is located at angular separation $\theta$ from S1 in the geostationary arc.

**Given:**
- Victim Rx antenna peak gain: $G_{r,\max}$ (dBi)
- Angular separation: $\theta$ (degrees)
- EIRP of S1 (desired): $EIRP_D$ (dBW)
- EIRP of S2 (interferer): $EIRP_I$ (dBW)
- Path-loss difference: $\Delta L_{fs} = L_{fs,D} - L_{fs,I}$ (dB)
- Wavelength: $\lambda$ (m)

**Step 1 — Compute victim Rx gain towards desired signal (boresight):**

$$
G_{r,D} \;=\; G_{r,\max}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute victim Rx gain towards interferer (off-axis at angle $\theta$):** Apply the Region 3 formula since typical GEO spacing $2^\circ \gg 2.5\,\theta_0$ for $\theta_0 \sim 0.2^\circ$:

$$
G_{r,I}(\theta) \;=\; 32 - 25 \log_{10}\theta \quad \text{[dBi]}
$$

**Step 3 — Received desired power:**

$$
P_{r,D} \;=\; EIRP_D + G_{r,D} - L_{fs,D} \quad \text{[dBW]}
$$

**Step 4 — Received interference power:**

$$
P_{r,I} \;=\; EIRP_I + G_{r,I}(\theta) - L_{fs,I} \quad \text{[dBW]}
$$

**Step 5 — Form the C/I ratio** (assuming the two satellites are at similar ranges, so $\Delta L_{fs} \approx 0$):

$$
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} \;=\; \left(P_{r,D} - P_{r,I}\right)_{dB}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} \;=\; (EIRP_D - EIRP_I) + (G_{r,D} - G_{r,I}(\theta))
$$

**Step 6 — Special case (equal EIRPs, $EIRP_D = EIRP_I$):**

$$
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} \;=\; G_{r,\max} - (32 - 25 \log_{10}\theta)
$$

$$
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} \;=\; G_{r,\max} - 32 + 25 \log_{10}\theta
$$

**Numerical example:** Let $G_{r,\max} = 50 \; \text{dBi}$, $\theta = 2^\circ$:

$$
\frac{C}{I} \;=\; 50 - 32 + 25 \log_{10}(2) \;=\; 18 + 7.52 \;=\; 25.52 \; \text{dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Insight:** For $\theta = 2^\circ$ and $G_{\max} = 50 \; \text{dBi}$, the C/I is approximately **25.5 dB** — well above the **20 dB** threshold needed for a high-quality digital link.

## 3.3 Derivation — Combined Carrier-to-Noise-plus-Interference (C/(N+I))

The total impairment is the sum of two independent noise-like powers. In linear (not dB) units:

$$
(N + I) \;=\; N + I
$$

Divide the carrier power $C$:

$$
\frac{C}{N+I} \;=\; \frac{C}{N+I} \;=\; \frac{1}{\dfrac{N+I}{C}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\dfrac{N}{C} + \dfrac{I}{C}}
$$

Substitute $N/C = 1/(C/N)$ and $I/C = 1/(C/I)$:

$$
\frac{C}{N+I} \;=\; \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{C/N} + \dfrac{1}{C/I}}
$$

**In dB form:**

$$
\left[\frac{C}{N+I}\right]_{dB} \;=\; -10 \log_{10}\!\left( 10^{-\frac{C}{N_{dB}}}{10}} + 10^{-\frac{C}{I_{dB}}}{10}} \right)
$$

**Why the minus sign?** Because the dB form of $C/N$ is positive (e.g., 20 dB), but $N/C$ is $-20$ dB. So we put the negative in front and the negative exponent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for Quick Estimation:**
> - If $C/I > C/N + 10 \; \text{dB}$ → interference is negligible; CNIR $\approx C/N$.
> - If $C/I < C/N - 10 \; \text{dB}$ → thermal noise is negligible; CNIR $\approx C/I$.
> - Otherwise, **both must be summed** as above.

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Typical Numerical

**Problem:** A satellite link has an uplink $C/N = 24 \; \text{dB}$, downlink $C/N = 20 \; \text{dB}$, and a co-channel interference $C/I = 22 \; \text{dB}$ in the downlink. Compute the total $C/(N+I)$ at the receiver output, assuming only downlink noise is affected by interference.

**Step 1 — Compute the composite C/N** (uplink and downlink in series):

$$
\frac{1}{C/N_{total}} \;=\; \frac{1}{C/N_{UL}} + \frac{1}{C/N_{DL}}
$$

Convert to linear:

$$
10^{-24/10} = 3.98 \times 10^{-3}, \quad 10^{-20/10} = 1.0 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{C/N_{total}} \;=\; 3.98 \times 10^{-3} + 1.0 \times 10^{-2} \;=\; 1.398 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
C/N_{total} \;=\; 71.5 \quad \text{(linear)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 18.54 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Add interference** (only on downlink, so use the combined formula on the DL):

$$
\frac{C}{N+I} \;=\; \frac{1}{10^{-20/10} + 10^{-22/10}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1.0 \times 10^{-2} + 6.31 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\frac{C}{N+I} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.01631} \;=\; 61.3 \quad \text{(linear)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 17.87 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine uplink noise with the corrupted downlink:**

$$
\frac{1}{C/(N+I)_{total}} \;=\; 3.98 \times 10^{-3} + \frac{1}{61.3}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{C/(N+I)_{total}} \;=\; 3.98 \times 10^{-3} + 1.63 \times 10^{-2} \;=\; 2.03 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
C/(N+I)_{total} \;=\; 49.3 \quad \text{(linear)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{16.93 \; \text{dB}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The interference has degraded the link by $18.54 - 16.93 = 1.61$ dB.** This is the *typical* magnitude of impact in a well-designed adjacent-satellite scenario.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Interference Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an interference analysis problem, from the definition of interference sources through to the final $C/(N+I)$ evaluation. It is the *process flow* a KTU examiner expects a student to follow in a 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Start: Define Link Parameters"] --> B0["Identify Interference Sources"]
    B0 --> B1["Co-Channel CCI"]
    B0 --> B2["Adjacent-Channel ACI"]
    B0 --> B3["Intermodulation IM"]
    B0 --> B4["Cross-Polarization XPI"]
    B0 --> B5["Terrestrial Terr"]

    B1 --> C1["Compute Victim Rx Gain Off-Axis G_r theta"]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1

    C1 --> D0["Apply ITU-R Off-Axis Envelope"]
    D0 --> D1["Region 1: Main Lobe"]
    D0 --> D2["Region 2: Near Side-Lobe"]
    D0 --> D3["Region 3: Far Side-Lobe 32 minus 25 log theta"]
    D0 --> D4["Region 4: Back Lobe minus 10 dBi"]

    D1 --> E0["Compute Received Interference Power P_I"]
    D2 --> E0
    D3 --> E0
    D4 --> E0

    E0 --> F0["Compute C over I in dB"]
    F0 --> G0["Combine with Thermal C over N"]
    G0 --> H0["Compute C over N plus I using Reciprocal Sum"]
    H0 --> I0["Convert to E_b over N_0 and BER"]
    I0 --> J0["Final Output: Link Feasibility Yes or No"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Interference Coupling Mechanism

The following sequence illustrates **how the interfering satellite's signal propagates into the victim receiver's chain**. This is a top-level functional architecture rather than a physical ray-trace diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Tx1["Interferer Satellite S2 TX"] --> Ant2["S2 Antenna Pattern"]
    Ant2 --> FS2["Free Space Path L_fs2"]
    FS2 --> AntV["Victim Earth Station Antenna"]
    AntV --> LNA["LNA plus Down-Converter"]
    LNA --> BB["Baseband Demodulator"]
    BB --> Det["Decision Threshold"]

    Tx0["Desired Satellite S1 TX"] --> Ant1["S1 Antenna Pattern"]
    Ant1 --> FS1["Free Space Path L_fs1"]
    FS1 --> AntV

    AntV --> GainFn["Off-Axis Gain G_r theta from ITU-R S.580"]
    GainFn --> FS2
```

## 4.3 Component-Level Interference Contribution Table

| Block | Symbol | Role | Typical Value |
|-------|--------|------|---------------|
| Victim Rx antenna peak gain | $G_{r,\max}$ | Defines boresight signal capture | **40 – 55 dBi** |
| Off-axis gain (interferer) | $G_{r}(\theta)$ | Determines spatial discrimination | **$-10$ to $32$ dBi** |
| Free-space loss (uplink/downlink) | $L_{fs}$ | Path attenuation | **~200 dB** at GEO, 4 GHz |
| Interferer EIRP | $EIRP_I$ | Strength of unwanted signal | **30 – 50 dBW** |
| Victim LNA noise figure | $NF$ | Sets thermal noise floor | **1 – 3 dB** |
| HPA input back-off | $IBO$ | Linearizes intermodulation | **3 – 10 dB** |
| Polarization discrimination | $XPD$ | Cross-pol isolation | **≥ 27 dB** clear-sky |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the flowchart, every rectangular node is a *computational step* a student must perform. The **hexagonal decision-style endpoints** $B_1$ through $B_5$ represent the **five interference sources** that must be enumerated. The right-hand branch shows the **C/(N+I) cascade**. A KTU 14-mark solution should follow this exact sequence: *Define → Identify → Gain → Combine → Conclude*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "co-channel interference" in a satellite communication system. How is it different from adjacent-channel interference?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Co-channel interference (CCI)** is the unwanted coupling between two (or more) signals that occupy the *same* carrier frequency at the *same time*, typically arising from frequency-reuse in spot-beams or co-located satellites in the geostationary arc. It cannot be removed by filtering — only by **spatial or polarization discrimination**.

> **Adjacent-channel interference (ACI)**, in contrast, arises from signals in *neighbouring* frequency channels (e.g., neighbouring transponder slots within the same satellite). It results from finite filter roll-off and spectral side-lobe leakage and **can be reduced by sharper bandpass filtering** or wider guard-bands.

**(1 mark** for the definition of CCI, **1 mark** for the definition of ACI, **1 mark** for the clear distinction in the mitigation mechanism.)

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**What is meant by Input Back-Off (IBO) in a satellite transponder, and why is it necessary for interference control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Input Back-Off (IBO)** is the amount, in dB, by which the operating input power to the High-Power Amplifier (HPA, typically a TWTA) is reduced **below** the input power that would drive the amplifier to its **saturation point**.

> IBO is necessary because operating the HPA near saturation maximizes RF power efficiency but introduces **non-linear distortion** — chiefly **intermodulation (IM) products** such as $2f_1 - f_2$ and $3f_1 - 2f_2$, which fall *inside* the wanted band and act as interference. By backing off the input (typically 3 – 10 dB), the amplifier operates in a more linear region, suppressing IM at the cost of reduced DC-to-RF efficiency.

**(1 mark** for definition, **1 mark** for IM mechanism, **1 mark** for the trade-off with efficiency.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module 2 Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear before summing.** The combined C/(N+I) formula is valid only in *linear* units. Adding two C/N ratios in dB and dividing by 2 is **wrong** and costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong region of the off-axis gain curve.** A $2^\circ$ off-axis angle is in Region 3 ($G = 32 - 25\log\theta$). Students often incorrectly use the main-lobe parabola and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the $10 \log_{10}$ in the dB conversion of the combined formula.** A common error is to use $20 \log_{10}$ instead of $10 \log_{10}$, leading to a final answer off by 3 dB.
> 4. **Not specifying "linear" units explicitly.** Examiners deduct 0.5 mark if a student mixes dB and linear within the same equation.

---

### Question B1 (A) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted]

**(a) [7 marks]** An earth station receives a signal from the desired satellite at the boresight. The adjacent geostationary satellite is separated by an angular distance of $2.5^\circ$. Both satellites transmit at an EIRP of $40 \; \text{dBW}$ in the same frequency band. The earth-station antenna has a peak gain of $48 \; \text{dBi}$. Determine the **off-axis gain** of the earth-station antenna towards the interfering satellite using the ITU-R reference pattern.

**(b) [7 marks]** If the path losses to the two satellites are equal and the receiver's thermal $C/N = 22 \; \text{dB}$, calculate the overall **$C/(N+I)$** at the receiver output. State whether the link meets a target $C/(N+I) \ge 20 \; \text{dB}$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Off-axis gain [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** Identify the angular region. For a typical GEO satellite, $\theta_0 \approx 0.2^\circ$ to $0.5^\circ$. With separation $2.5^\circ \gg 2.5 \theta_0$, we are firmly in **Region 3** of the ITU-R S.580 envelope.

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** Apply the Region 3 formula:

$$
G(\theta) \;=\; 32 - 25 \log_{10}\theta \quad \text{[dBi, $\theta$ in degrees]}
$$

**Step 3 [2 marks]:** Substitute $\theta = 2.5^\circ$:

$$
\log_{10}(2.5) \;=\; 0.3979
$$

$$
G(2.5^\circ) \;=\; 32 - 25(0.3979) \;=\; 32 - 9.95 \;=\; 22.05 \; \text{dBi}
$$

**Step 4 [2 marks]:** Interpretation and comment:
- The desired signal is received at $48 \; \text{dBi}$.
- The interferer is received at $22.05 \; \text{dBi}$.
- **Spatial discrimination** = $48 - 22.05 = 25.95 \; \text{dB}$.

---

**Part (b) — Combined C/(N+I) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [2 marks]:** Compute C/I. With equal EIRPs and equal path losses, the C/I equals the spatial-discrimination margin:

$$
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} \;=\; G_{r,\max} - G_{r}(\theta) \;=\; 48 - 22.05 \;=\; 25.95 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** Convert to linear:

$$
\frac{C}{I} \;=\; 10^{25.95/10} \;=\; 10^{2.595} \;=\; 394.0
$$

$$
\frac{C}{N} \;=\; 10^{22/10} \;=\; 10^{2.2} \;=\; 158.5
$$

**Step 3 [2 marks]:** Apply the combined C/(N+I) formula in **linear** units:

$$
\frac{C}{N+I} \;=\; \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{C/N} + \dfrac{1}{C/I}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{158.5} + \dfrac{1}{394.0}}
$$

$$
\frac{C}{N+I} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.00631 + 0.00254} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.00885} \;=\; 113.0
$$

**Step 4 [1 mark]:** Convert to dB:

$$
\left[\frac{C}{N+I}\right]_{dB} \;=\; 10 \log_{10}(113.0) \;=\; 20.53 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Conclusion:** $C/(N+I) = 20.53 \; \text{dB} \ge 20 \; \text{dB}$ target. ✅ **Link meets the requirement**, with a thin 0.53 dB margin. In practice, the designer should incorporate rain-fade and pointing-error margins of at least 4–6 dB.

---

### Question B1 (B) – [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Adapted] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat derivations, explain how **co-channel interference** arises in a satellite system using frequency reuse. Show that the **carrier-to-interference ratio** for a desired satellite in a cluster of $N$ co-channel satellites, all with equal EIRP, is given by:

$$
\frac{C}{I} \;=\; \frac{G_{r,\max}}{\sum_{k=1}^{N-1} G_{r}(\theta_k)}
$$

**where $G_{r}(\theta_k)$ is the off-axis gain toward the $k$-th interferer.**

**(b) [7 marks]** A C-band satellite system has a 4-beam frequency-reuse cluster ($N = 4$). The victim beam antenna has $G_{r,\max} = 52 \; \text{dBi}$ and 3 co-channel interferers located at off-axis angles of $3^\circ$, $5.5^\circ$, and $7.2^\circ$ respectively. Using the ITU-R envelope, calculate the total C/I at the receiver. Comment on whether the link is interference-limited.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** In a frequency-reuse cluster of $N$ beams, all $N$ beams share the same frequency band. The *desired* beam is one of them; the remaining $(N - 1)$ beams become co-channel interferers.

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** Received desired power:

$$
P_{r,D} \;=\; EIRP_D + G_{r,\max} - L_{fs} \quad \text{[dBW]}
$$

**Step 3 [2 marks]:** Received interference power from the $k$-th co-channel beam:

$$
P_{r,I_k} \;=\; EIRP_{I_k} + G_{r}(\theta_k) - L_{fs}
$$

If all co-channel beams have equal EIRP, $EIRP_{I_k} = EIRP_D$:

$$
P_{r,I_k} - P_{r,D} \;=\; G_{r}(\theta_k) - G_{r,\max} \quad \text{[dB]}
$$

**Step 4 [2 marks]:** Sum the interference powers in **linear** units (incoherent addition):

$$
I_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{N-1} I_k
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{C}{I_{\text{total}}} \;=\; \frac{P_{r,D}}{\sum_{k=1}^{N-1} P_{r,I_k}} \;=\; \frac{G_{r,\max}}{\sum_{k=1}^{N-1} G_{r}(\theta_k)}
$$

This is the desired result, with all ratios in linear units. **QED.**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical evaluation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** Identify all three interferers are in Region 3 of the ITU-R envelope. Compute $G_r(\theta_k) = 32 - 25 \log_{10}\theta_k$ for each:

- $\theta_1 = 3^\circ$: $G_1 = 32 - 25 \log_{10}(3) = 32 - 25(0.4771) = 32 - 11.93 = 20.07 \; \text{dBi}$
- $\theta_2 = 5.5^\circ$: $G_2 = 32 - 25 \log_{10}(5.5) = 32 - 25(0.7404) = 32 - 18.51 = 13.49 \; \text{dBi}$
- $\theta_3 = 7.2^\circ$: $G_3 = 32 - 25 \log_{10}(7.2) = 32 - 25(0.8573) = 32 - 21.43 = 10.57 \; \text{dBi}$

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** Convert each $G_k$ to linear and compute the ratio $G_{\max}/G_k$:

$$
G_{\max,\text{lin}} = 10^{52/10} = 10^{5.2} = 158489
$$

$$
G_1/\text{lin} = 10^{2.007} = 101.6, \quad G_2/\text{lin} = 10^{1.349} = 22.3, \quad G_3/\text{lin} = 10^{1.057} = 11.4
$$

**Step 3 [2 marks]:** Compute the linear sum of interference gains:

$$
\sum G_k = 101.6 + 22.3 + 11.4 = 135.3
$$

**Step 4 [2 marks]:** Compute C/I:

$$
\frac{C}{I} \;=\; \frac{158489}{135.3} \;=\; 1171 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 10 \log_{10}(1171) \;=\; 30.7 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Conclusion:** $C/I = 30.7 \; \text{dB}$ is **excellent** and well above any practical threshold (typically 18–22 dB). The link is **NOT interference-limited** — thermal noise will dominate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2: Interference Analysis**
>
> ✅ The four primary interference types: **CCI, ACI, IM, XPI** (plus terrestrial).
>
> ✅ The combined figure of merit: $C/(N+I) = 1 / [1/(C/N) + 1/(C/I)]$ — **always in linear units first**, then convert to dB.
>
> ✅ ITU-R off-axis envelope: $G(\theta) = 32 - 25 \log_{10}\theta$ for $2.5\,\theta_0 \le \theta \le 48^\circ$, in dBi with $\theta$ in **degrees**.
>
> ✅ For $\theta > 48^\circ$, use the constant $G = -10 \; \text{dBi}$.
>
> ✅ Third-order intermodulation power falls by **2 dB per 1 dB of Output Back-Off (OBO)**.
>
> ✅ Cross-polarization discrimination (XPD) ≥ **27 dB** clear-sky, falling to ~10 dB in heavy rain.
>
> ✅ Co-channel interference **cannot be filtered out** — it requires spatial or polarization discrimination.
>
> ✅ Adjacent-channel interference **can be reduced** by sharper roll-off filters and wider guard-bands.
>
> ✅ Multiple interferers combine in **linear (power) units**, not in dB.
>
> ✅ KTU 14-mark questions typically demand: identification of the interference type → choice of correct formula → numerical substitution → comment on link feasibility.
>
> ✅ Common examiner traps: wrong region of off-axis curve, mixing dB/linear, forgetting the $10 \log_{10}$ in the conversion to dB.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Takeaway for Last-Minute Revision:**
> *"Interference analysis is the discipline of computing the C/(N+I) ratio by combining thermal noise (C/N) and interference (C/I) in linear units, with the off-axis antenna gain $G(\theta) = 32 - 25 \log_{10}\theta$ as the universal discriminator."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terrestrial Interference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Terrestrial Interference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Terrestrial Interference** in a satellite communication system refers to the unwanted electromagnetic energy entering the satellite receiver (uplink interference) or earth-station receiver (downlink interference) from ground-based transmitting sources, primarily from terrestrial **microwave relay links** operating in shared or adjacent frequency bands.

In the **C-band (4/6 GHz)** and **Ku-band (12/14 GHz)**, satellite systems share spectrum with extensive terrestrial point-to-point microwave networks. Because the satellite receiver must accept signals from the entire footprint (footprint diameter ≈ thousands of km), its antenna inherently picks up emissions from any terrestrial transmitter within line-of-sight of the earth station. This makes terrestrial interference a *co-existence* problem governed by strict **ITU Radio Regulations (RR Article 21 & 22)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly lists *Terrestrial Interference* under Module 2 alongside Rain Attenuation. Examiners frame it as a **co-channel coordination** problem between satellite and terrestrial systems sharing C/Ku bands.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Café Noise" Intuition

Imagine you are talking to a friend across a large, crowded coffee shop (this is your **satellite link** — long distance, weak voice). Other customers at nearby tables are having loud phone conversations (these are **terrestrial microwave links** sharing the same room). Even though you are trying to listen only to your friend, the other voices "bleed" into your conversation. To hear clearly, you need either:
1. To move away from the noise (spatial isolation → **antenna discrimination**), or
2. Ask the other customers to lower their volume (transmit power control), or
3. Use a directional microphone that focuses only on your friend (a **narrow-beam earth station antenna**).

The satellite/terrestrial system does exactly this — coordinates geometry, power, and antenna pattern to keep the interference-to-carrier ratio (I/C) below an acceptable threshold.

### 1.3 The Two Principal Interference Scenarios

| Scenario | Direction | Interferer | Victim |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Uplink (Earth-Space)** | Earth → Satellite | Terrestrial TX station | Satellite transponder |
| **Downlink (Space-Earth)** | Satellite → Earth | Terrestrial TX station | Earth-station RX |

> [!NOTE]
> The most critical case in KTU problems is the **downlink case**, where an earth-station's main beam or sidelobe picks up a terrestrial line-of-sight (LoS) microwave transmitter. The standard minimum angular separation required is **2°** at C-band per ITU-R S.466.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Terrestrial Interference

Terrestrial interference enters the satellite/earth-station receiver through **two physical mechanisms**:

1. **Main-lobe (or near-main-lobe) interference** — occurs when a terrestrial transmitter lies within the earth-station antenna's main beam. This is the worst-case and arises from poor frequency coordination or geographical proximity.
2. **Side-lobe interference** — occurs when the terrestrial transmitter is outside the main beam but the earth's station antenna sidelobes still pick up the energy. Since most well-designed antennas have first-sidelobe levels of **−20 to −30 dB**, this is the dominant practical mechanism.

### 2.2 Frequency Coordination Logic

Terrestrial and satellite systems cannot occupy the *same* frequency in the *same* region — therefore coordination uses three orthogonal levers:

- **Spatial separation** (angle θ between the desired satellite direction and the interferer direction)
- **Frequency separation** (Δf between satellite and terrestrial carriers)
- **Polarization discrimination** (orthogonal polarization between systems)

### 2.3 The Carrier-to-Interference Ratio (C/I)

The fundamental figure of merit for interference-limited systems:

$$
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{dB}} = \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{up/down}} \quad \text{must exceed the} \quad \text{Protection Ratio}
$$

The **protection ratio** is defined as the minimum acceptable C/I at the receiver input for a given quality of service. For **TV/FM** satellite links, typical values are:

| Modulation | Protection Ratio (dB) |
|---|---|
| FM-TV (CCIR grade) | **30 dB** |
| 4-PSK digital | **20 dB** |
| 16-QAM | **24 dB** |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{C}{I} = \left(\dfrac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{up}} \quad \text{(combined via power sum)}$ | Combined uplink C/I | dB |
| 2 | $\left(\dfrac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{total}} = -10\log_{10}\!\left(10^{-C/I_{\text{up}}/10}+10^{-C/I_{\text{down}}/10}\right)$ | Total C/I from uplink + downlink | dB |
| 3 | $I = EIRP_{\text{terr}} \cdot G_{\text{ES}}(\theta) \cdot \dfrac{1}{L_{\text{path}}} \cdot BW_{\text{rec}}$ | Received interference power | W |
| 4 | $\theta_{\text{sep}} \ge 2^\circ$ (C-band, ITU-R S.466) | Minimum angular separation | degrees |
| 5 | $\theta_{\text{sep}} \ge 1.5^\circ$ (Ku-band) | Minimum angular separation | degrees |
| 6 | $\left(\dfrac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{dB}} = EIRP_{\text{sat}} - EIRP_{\text{terr}} - G_{\text{rel}}(\theta) + \Delta G$ | Simplified C/I (downlink) | dB |
| 7 | $G_{\text{rel}}(\theta) = G_{\max} - G(\theta)$ | Antenna off-axis gain (relative) | dBi |
| 8 | $L_{\text{fs}} = 92.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{GHz}})$ | Free-space path loss | dB |
| 9 | $P_{\text{r,interferer}} = P_{\text{t,terr}} + G_{\text{t,terr}} - L_{\text{fs}} + G_{\text{r,ES}}(\theta)$ | Power received from interferer | dBm |
| 10 | **Permissible interference power** $= -131~\text{dBW}$ in 4 kHz (ITU-R S.466) | Long-term interference limit | dBW |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** All interference calculations reduce to: *(Received Interferer Power) − (Received Desired Power)*. The earth station's **antenna pattern** $G(\theta)$ is the single most powerful discrimination tool.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Spectrum regulators (ITU-R, FCC, WPC-India)** use these exact formulas to coordinate satellite and terrestrial filings.
- **Satellite operators (ISRO, Intelsat, SES)** design downlink beams with shaped contours to avoid densely populated microwave corridors.
- **Cellular back-haul** networks in 6/4 GHz must perform interference analysis with adjacent satellite earth stations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expression for Downlink C/I (Earth-Station Receiver)

**Setup:** A satellite at EIRP$_{\text{sat}}$ (dBW) transmits to an earth station. A terrestrial microwave transmitter at EIRP$_{\text{terr}}$ (dBW) lies at off-axis angle $\theta$ from the earth-station's main beam. Both are at the same frequency.

**Step 1:** Received desired carrier power at earth station:

$$
C = \text{EIRP}_{\text{sat}} - L_{\text{down}} + G_{\text{ES,max}}
$$

This is the carrier power from the satellite, where $L_{\text{down}}$ is downlink path loss and $G_{\text{ES,max}}$ is the on-axis gain of the earth station.

**Step 2:** Received interference power from terrestrial source at angle $\theta$:

$$
I = \text{EIRP}_{\text{terr}} - L_{\text{terr}} + G_{\text{ES}}(\theta)
$$

where $G_{\text{ES}}(\theta)$ is the earth-station gain in the direction of the terrestrial interferer (off-axis).

**Step 3:** Take the ratio in dB:

$$
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{dB}} = \left[\text{EIRP}_{\text{sat}} - L_{\text{down}} + G_{\text{ES,max}}\right] - \left[\text{EIRP}_{\text{terr}} - L_{\text{terr}} + G_{\text{ES}}(\theta)\right]
$$

**Step 4:** Regroup by canceling the on-axis gain terms via the relative off-axis gain $G_{\text{rel}}(\theta) = G_{\text{ES,max}} - G_{\text{ES}}(\theta)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{dB}} &= \big(\text{EIRP}_{\text{sat}} - \text{EIRP}_{\text{terr}}\big) + \big(L_{\text{terr}} - L_{\text{down}}\big) + \big(G_{\text{ES,max}} - G_{\text{ES}}(\theta)\big) \\[4pt]
&= \Delta\text{EIRP} + \Delta L + G_{\text{rel}}(\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** For a typical C-band case where the terrestrial link is short (< 50 km) and the downlink is long (≈ 36,000 km), the term $\Delta L = L_{\text{terr}} - L_{\text{down}}$ is **strongly negative** (≈ −70 dB), heavily favoring the satellite link. This is why the antenna discrimination $G_{\text{rel}}(\theta)$ is the decisive term.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Combined Uplink + Downlink C/I

When both uplink and downlink have interference, the total C/I is found by power-summing the individual interference contributions.

**Step 1:** Convert each C/I from dB to linear power ratio.

**Step 2:** Add the interference powers (not the dB values).

**Step 3:** Convert the sum back to dB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{total,dB}} &= -10\log_{10}\!\left(10^{-(C/I)_{\text{up,dB}}/10} + 10^{-(C/I)_{\text{down,dB}}/10}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example:** If $(C/I)_{\text{up}} = 24$ dB and $(C/I)_{\text{down}} = 26$ dB:

- Linear: $10^{-2.4} = 0.00398$, $10^{-2.6} = 0.00251$
- Sum: $0.00649$
- Total: $-10\log_{10}(0.00649) = 21.88$ dB ≈ **21.9 dB**

> [!NOTE]
> The **dominant (worst) term** dictates the total — never simply average C/I values in dB!

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Off-Axis Angle for Coordination

We require the received interference to be ≤ the maximum permissible level. Setting the received interference equal to the ITU-R limit:

**Step 1:** Maximum permissible interference at the earth-station input (ITU-R S.466, long-term):

$$
I_{\max} = -131~\text{dBW} \quad \text{in a} \quad 4~\text{kHz} \quad \text{bandwidth}
$$

**Step 2:** Earth-station's noise-limited C/N (assuming clear sky) gives a maximum tolerable I:

$$
I_{\text{tol}} = C - \text{Protection Ratio} = C - 30~\text{dB (for FM-TV)}
$$

**Step 3:** Equate and solve for the off-axis gain $G(\theta)$:

$$
G(\theta) = I_{\text{tol,dBW}} - \text{EIRP}_{\text{terr}} + L_{\text{terr}}
$$

**Step 4:** From the antenna's measured pattern $G(\theta)$ vs. $\theta$, invert to find the **minimum coordination angle** $\theta_{\min}$. For ITU reference patterns (S.465 for 6 GHz, S.580 for 14 GHz), the result is the famous **2° at C-band** and **1.5° at Ku-band** rule.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Conflating *minimum coordination angle* with the antenna's *3-dB beamwidth*. They are completely different — the 2° rule is a **regulatory** quantity, while 3-dB beamwidth is an **electrical** quantity (typically 0.5°–2° for a 30 m dish at 4 GHz).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Terrestrial Interference Geometry (Downlink Case)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[SATELLITE 36,000 km] -->|EIRP_sat, downlink beam| nodeB[Earth Station Antenna]
    nodeC[Terrestrial Microwave TX 50 km] -->|EIRP_terr, off-axis angle theta| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Sum of signals| nodeD[Low-Noise Amplifier LNA]
    nodeD -->|IF stage| nodeE[Demodulator]
    nodeE -->|C plus I output| nodeF[Quality Check C over I equals Protection Ratio]
    nodeF -->|If pass| nodeG[Acceptable Service]
    nodeF -->|If fail| nodeH[Apply Coordination: angle, frequency, or polarization separation]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Uplink Interference into Satellite Transponder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Desired Earth Station TX 6 GHz] -->|EIRP_des, narrow beam toward satellite| nodeD[Satellite Transponder 6 GHz]
    nodeB[Unwanted Terrestrial TX 6 GHz] -->|EIRP_terr, may be inside satellite footprint| nodeD
    nodeC[Adjacent Satellite Earth Station 6 GHz] -->|EIRP_adj| nodeD
    nodeD -->|Down converts to 4 GHz| nodeE[Transponder Output 4 GHz]
    nodeE -->|Composite downlink C plus I| nodeF[All Receiving Earth Stations]
```

### 4.3 Flowchart — Interference Coordination Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start New Satellite or Terrestrial Link] --> nodeB{Is the new link within coordination contour of existing systems?}
    nodeB -->|No| nodeC[No Action Needed]
    nodeB -->|Yes| nodeD[Compute I equals P_t plus G_t minus L_fs plus G_r at angle theta]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Is I less than I_max equals minus 131 dBW in 4 kHz?}
    nodeE -->|Yes| nodeC
    nodeE -->|No| nodeF[Try Discrimination Lever 1: Increase angular separation theta]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Sufficient?}
    nodeG -->|No| nodeH[Try Lever 2: Frequency offset Delta f]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Sufficient?}
    nodeI -->|No| nodeJ[Try Lever 3: Polarization discrimination]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{Sufficient?}
    nodeK -->|No| nodeL[Reduce EIRP_terr or relocate station]
    nodeK -->|Yes| nodeC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The earth-station antenna in the first diagram simultaneously receives two signals from two different azimuth/elevation directions. Its **pattern $G(\theta)$** is the only thing that "knows" which is the desired satellite and which is the unwanted terrestrial interferer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define terrestrial interference in a satellite communication system. List any two sources of such interference.*

**Model Answer:**
Terrestrial interference is the unwanted RF energy that enters a satellite or earth-station receiver from ground-based transmitting sources (mainly line-of-sight microwave relay links) operating in the same or adjacent frequency bands.
**Two sources:**
1. Point-to-point **terrestrial microwave links** sharing the 4/6 GHz or 12/14 GHz bands.
2. **Radar systems** and other co-primary services (e.g., airborne transponders) operating under ITU primary allocations in the same band.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*State the ITU-R minimum angular separation required between an earth-station antenna boresight and a terrestrial line-of-sight transmitter operating in the same frequency band, for (i) C-band and (ii) Ku-band. Why is this angle non-zero?*

**Model Answer:**
- (i) **C-band (4/6 GHz):** $\theta_{\min} = 2^\circ$ (per ITU-R S.466)
- (ii) **Ku-band (12/14 GHz):** $\theta_{\min} = 1.5^\circ$

This angle is non-zero because the earth-station antenna has finite sidelobe levels; without an angular guard-band, the sidelobes of the antenna would pick up enough terrestrial energy to exceed the protection ratio of the satellite link.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **mechanism of terrestrial interference** in the **downlink direction** of a satellite communication system. Discuss the role of the earth-station antenna pattern in rejecting such interference. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Mechanism (3 marks):** In downlink, the satellite transmits a desired FM/PSK carrier at EIRP$_{\text{sat}}$ to the earth station. A terrestrial microwave transmitter operating in the same band, located at an off-axis angle $\theta$ from the earth-station boresight, also illuminates the earth-station antenna. Both signals enter the LNA simultaneously, and after down-conversion the demodulator sees a corrupted baseband — the (C/I) degrades.

2. **Antenna pattern role (3 marks):** The earth-station antenna has a directive pattern $G(\theta)$ with high on-axis gain $G_{\max}$ and low sidelobes. The **relative gain** $G_{\text{rel}}(\theta) = G_{\max} - G(\theta)$ provides spatial discrimination against the interferer. As $\theta$ increases from 0° to a few degrees, $G_{\text{rel}}$ increases rapidly (tens of dB), attenuating the interferer.

3. **Block diagram (1 mark):** [Refer to SECTION 4.1 above for the diagram; the student must draw Satellite → Earth Station (with pattern), Terrestrial TX (off-axis) → same Earth Station, then LNA → Demod → C/I check.]

**(b)** An earth-station antenna with on-axis gain of **48 dBi** receives a satellite signal at C-band with EIRP$_{\text{sat}} = 36$ dBW. A terrestrial transmitter with EIRP$_{\text{terr}} = 50$ dBW is located at an off-axis angle where the antenna gain is **20 dBi**. The downlink path loss is **196 dB** and the terrestrial path loss is **128 dB**. Calculate the downlink **(C/I) in dB**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute received carrier power:**

$$
C = \text{EIRP}_{\text{sat}} - L_{\text{down}} + G_{\text{ES,max}} = 36 - 196 + 48 = -112~\text{dBW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute received interference power:**

$$
I = \text{EIRP}_{\text{terr}} - L_{\text{terr}} + G_{\text{ES}}(\theta) = 50 - 128 + 20 = -58~\text{dBW}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**

$$
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{dB}} = C - I = -112 - (-58) = -54~\text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
A (C/I) of **−54 dB** is far below the protection ratio of 30 dB. The system would fail — the terrestrial interferer is **far too strong** at this off-axis angle. The mitigation is to **increase $\theta$** (relocate the terrestrial TX) so that $G_{\text{ES}}(\theta)$ drops, OR **lower EIRP$_{\text{terr}}$**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often forget that the **downlink path loss** (~196 dB at GEO for 4 GHz) and the **terrestrial path loss** (~128 dB for 50 km) are *not* the same — using only one value in both terms is a guaranteed 3-mark deduction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **three coordination levers** (spatial, frequency, polarization) used to mitigate terrestrial interference in shared satellite/terrestrial bands. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Spatial separation (3 marks):** Enforce a minimum off-axis angle $\theta$ between the satellite boresight and the terrestrial interferer. ITU-R specifies 2° at C-band. The earth-station antenna's first sidelobe must be below the permissible interference level; the larger $\theta$, the more relative gain $G_{\text{rel}}(\theta)$ attenuates the interferer. This is the **most-used** lever.

2. **Frequency separation (2 marks):** Assign non-overlapping sub-bands or apply a guard band $\Delta f$ between satellite and terrestrial carriers. If the receiver has a steep skirt filter, even a small $\Delta f$ can give 20–40 dB of rejection.

3. **Polarization discrimination (2 marks):** Use orthogonal linear polarizations (e.g., vertical for satellite, horizontal for terrestrial) or opposite circular senses. A well-aligned cross-pol antenna gives **27–30 dB** of discrimination. Real-world cross-pol is degraded to ≈ 20 dB by depolarization through rain.

**(b)** The uplink (C/I)$_{\text{up}}$ and downlink (C/I)$_{\text{down}}$ for a satellite link are **24 dB** and **26 dB** respectively. Compute the **combined (C/I)$_{\text{total}}$**. Comment on which link is the bottleneck. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert to linear ratios:**

$$
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{up}} = 10^{24/10} = 251.2
$$

$$
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{down}} = 10^{26/10} = 398.1
$$

**Step 2 — Compute inverse (interference fractions):**

$$
