# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — ISI Generation and Mitigation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete flow of an ISI-impaired digital signal through a satellite link, with the corresponding mitigation stages.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Data Source<br/>Binary Bits ak] --> B[Source Encoder<br/>Compresses Data]
    B --> C[Channel Encoder<br/>Adds FEC Redundancy]
    C --> D[Pulse Shaper<br/>Raised Cosine Filter]
    D --> E[Modulator<br/>QPSK or 8PSK]
    E --> F[Transmit Filter<br/>Bandlimiting]
    F --> G[Satellite Channel<br/>Bandlimited Transponder]
    G --> H[Receive Filter<br/>Matched Filter]
    H --> I[Sampler<br/>Decision Circuit]
    I --> J{ISI Present?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Adaptive Equalizer<br/>LMS or RLS Algorithm]
    J -- No --> L[Channel Decoder<br/>Viterbi or LDPC]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Source Decoder]
    M --> N[Output Data]
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The **red block (G)** represents the bandlimited satellite channel — the physical origin of ISI. The **green block (K)** is the adaptive equalizer that compensates for the pulse spreading. The **amber block (I)** is the sampler where ISI manifests as decision errors.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Rain Attenuation Prediction Workflow (ITU-R P.618)

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the standard procedure for predicting slant-path rain attenuation in a satellite link.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input Parameters<br/>Frequency f, Elevation theta<br/>Rain Rate R001, Polarization] --> P2[Lookup k and alpha<br/>ITU-R P.838 Coefficients]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Specific Attenuation<br/>gammaR equal to k times R to power alpha]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Rain Height hR<br/>Using 0C Isotherm]
    P4 --> P5[Compute Slant Path Length Ls<br/>Ls equal to hR minus hs over sin theta]
    P5 --> P6[Compute Horizontal Projection LG<br/>LG equal to Ls cos theta]
    P6 --> P7[Compute Reduction Factor r<br/>r equal to 1 over 1 plus LG over L0]
    P7 --> P8[Compute Effective Length Leff<br/>Leff equal to Ls times r]
    P8 --> P9[Compute Total Attenuation A<br/>A equal to gammaR times Leff]
    P9 --> P10[Add Implementation Margin<br/>Typically 1 dB]
    P10 --> P11[Fade Margin FM Output<br/>Used in Link Budget]
    style P1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style P3 fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style P9 fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style P11 fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The flow starts with **input parameters** (top), progresses through **geometry** (rain height, slant path) and **physics** (specific attenuation, reduction factor), and terminates at the **fade margin** (bottom) which feeds the link budget.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — ISI and Rain Fade Counter-Measure Interaction

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *combined interaction* between ISI equalization and rain fade mitigation in a modern satellite modem (e.g., DVB-S2X).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TX[Transmit Side]
        TX1[Data Input] --> TX2[FEC Encoder<br/>LDPC BCH]
        TX2 --> TX3[Bit Interleaver]
        TX3 --> TX4[Mapper<br/>QPSK to 64APSK]
        TX4 --> TX5[Platinum Frame<br/>Pilot Insertion]
    end
    TX --> CH[Satellite Channel<br/>Bandlimiting plus Rain plus Noise]
    CH --> RX[Receive Side]
    subgraph RX
        RX1[Matched Filter<br/>RC Pulse Shaping] --> RX2[Adaptive Equalizer<br/>ISI Removal]
        RX2 --> RX3[Carrier Sync<br/>Phase Recovery]
        RX3 --> RX4[Symbol Demapper<br/>Soft Decision LLR]
        RX4 --> RX5[Bit Deinterleaver]
        RX5 --> RX6[FEC Decoder<br/>LDPC BCH]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[Adaptive Control Loop]
        C1[SNR Estimator] --> C2[ModCod Selection<br/>ACM Controller]
        C2 --> C3[Feedback to TX4<br/>and TX2]
        C3 --> TX4
    end
    C1 --> RX2
    style CH fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style RX2 fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style C2 fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The **red block (CH)** is the impaired channel that introduces both ISI and rain attenuation. The **green block (RX2)** is the adaptive equalizer. The **purple block (C2)** is the adaptive coding/modulation (ACM) controller that dynamically adjusts the transmit parameters based on the receive-side SNR estimator. This dual-mitigation strategy is the hallmark of DVB-S2X.

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Eye Diagram — ISI Visualization

The eye diagram cannot be rendered as a Mermaid graph (it is a continuous-time plot), but its key features are described below as a structural reference for KTU questions on eye pattern analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eye Diagram Feature Map (Descriptive Schematic):**

| Region of Eye | Physical Meaning | Effect of ISI |
|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Top horizontal line** | High-level voltage (symbol = +1) | Crowded by neighboring symbol tails |
| **Bottom horizontal line** | Low-level voltage (symbol = -1) | Same crowding effect |
| **Central vertical opening** | Vertical aperture = noise margin | Reduced by ISI = **Eye Closure** |
| **Central horizontal opening** | Timing window for sampling | Narrowed by ISI = **Jitter Sensitivity** |
| **Inner crossover points** | Transitions between +1 and -1 | Slope reduced by ISI = **Slew Rate Drop** |

A **fully open eye** = zero ISI. A **partially closed eye** = ISI present, and the closure ratio is proportional to the BER penalty.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions in the ESE are direct, definition or short-numerical questions worth 3 marks. Each must be answered in **2–4 sentences** or a short derivation (max 1 page). The KTU 2024 scheme typically asks 5–6 such questions per module, and you must answer a defined number.

### Question 1: Define Inter-Symbol Interference. State the Nyquist criterion for zero ISI. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI) is the phenomenon in which the energy of one transmitted symbol spreads into the time slot of adjacent symbols due to band-limiting of the channel and multipath propagation, causing the receiver's decision circuit to misinterpret the symbol.

**Nyquist Criterion (2 marks):** The Nyquist first criterion for zero ISI states that the composite impulse response of the system (transmit filter × channel × receive filter) must satisfy:

$$h(kT_s) = \begin{cases} 1, & k = 0 \\ 0, & k \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

In the frequency domain, this translates to the folded spectrum condition, where the periodic replication of the spectrum must yield a constant within the Nyquist bandwidth. The **raised-cosine spectrum** with roll-off factor $\alpha \in [0,1]$ is the most widely used practical pulse shape satisfying this criterion.

---

### Question 2: Define specific attenuation due to rain. State the ITU-R power law. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Specific attenuation $\gamma_R$ is the attenuation experienced by an electromagnetic wave per unit path length (in dB/km) as it propagates through rain. It is caused by the combined effect of absorption and scattering of the wave energy by raindrops.

**ITU-R Power Law (2 marks):** According to ITU-R Recommendation P.838, the specific attenuation is given by:

$$\gamma_R = k \cdot R^{\alpha} \quad \text{(dB/km)}$$

where:
- $R$ is the rain rate (mm/hr),
- $k$ and $\alpha$ are frequency- and polarization-dependent coefficients.

The **coefficient $k$** increases with frequency (e.g., 0.018 at 12 GHz, 0.187 at 30 GHz for horizontal polarization), while **$\alpha$** typically ranges from 1.0 to 1.5 in the 10–40 GHz range. The total slant-path rain attenuation is then computed as $A = \gamma_R \cdot L_{eff}$, where $L_{eff}$ is the effective path length through the rain cell.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks). The KTU expects **detailed, step-by-step derivations** and **labeled diagrams** where applicable. Total marks: 14.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — ISI Focus**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Derive the Nyquist bandwidth for a baseband digital signal and explain the raised-cosine roll-off spectrum with a neat sketch. Show that the raised-cosine spectrum satisfies the zero-ISI condition.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Nyquist Bandwidth Derivation (3 marks):**

Consider a baseband binary data signal with bit rate $R_b$ bits/second. Each bit has duration $T_b = 1/R_b$. The transmitted pulse $p(t)$ in the time domain is a rectangular pulse of width $T_b$:

$$p(t) = \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_b}\right)$$

Taking the Fourier transform:

$$P(f) = T_b \cdot \text{sinc}(f T_b) = T_b \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f T_b)}{\pi f T_b}$$

The first null occurs at $f = 1/T_b = R_b$. The minimum bandwidth to pass the central lobe (which contains 90% of the energy) is:

$$B_{Nyquist} = \frac{R_b}{2} \quad \text{(Hz, ideal)}$$

This is the **Nyquist minimum bandwidth** for binary signalling without ISI.

**Step 2 — Raised-Cosine Spectrum Derivation (2 marks):**

The raised-cosine spectrum $H_{RC}(f)$ is defined as:

$$H_{RC}(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & 0 \leq \vert f \vert \leq \dfrac{1-\alpha}{2T_s} \\[6pt] \dfrac{T_s}{2}\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi T_s}{\alpha}\left(\vert f \vert - \dfrac{1-\alpha}{2T_s}\right)\right)\right], & \dfrac{1-\alpha}{2T_s} < \vert f \vert < \dfrac{1+\alpha}{2T_s} \\[6pt] 0, & \vert f \vert \geq \dfrac{1+\alpha}{2T_s} \end{cases}$$

The corresponding time-domain impulse response (the inverse Fourier transform) is:

$$h_{RC}(t) = \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right) \cdot \frac{\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi \alpha t}{T_s}\right)}{1 - \left(\dfrac{2\alpha t}{T_s}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Verification of Zero-ISI (2 marks):**

At sampling instants $t = kT_s$ where $k \neq 0$:

- The sinc term $\text{sinc}(k) = \sin(\pi k)/(\pi k) = 0$ for $k \neq 0$ ✓
- Therefore $h_{RC}(kT_s) = 0$ for all $k \neq 0$ ✓
- At $t = 0$: $h_{RC}(0) = 1 \times 1 / 1 = 1$ ✓

Thus the raised-cosine spectrum satisfies the Nyquist zero-ISI condition.

The total bandwidth required is:

$$B_{RC} = \frac{(1+\alpha) R_s}{2} = \frac{(1+\alpha) R_b}{2 \log_2 M}$$

For $\alpha = 0$: $B = R_b/2$ (ideal, unrealizable).
For $\alpha = 1$: $B = R_b$ (100% excess bandwidth, very robust).
For $\alpha = 0.5$: $B = 0.75 R_b$ (typical practical choice).

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: A satellite downlink transmits QPSK data at 60 Mbps. The link uses a raised-cosine pulse shaping filter. (i) Calculate the minimum Nyquist bandwidth for zero ISI. (ii) If the roll-off factor is 0.4, find the practical transmission bandwidth. (iii) Estimate the SNR penalty if the roll-off is increased from 0.4 to 0.6 (assume the receiver noise floor is unchanged).

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Minimum Nyquist Bandwidth (3 marks):**

For QPSK, $\log_2 M = \log_2 4 = 2$, so symbol rate $R_s = R_b / 2 = 30$ Msps.

$$B_{Nyquist} = \frac{R_s}{2} = \frac{30 \times 10^6}{2} = 15 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) Practical Bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$B_{RC} = \frac{(1+\alpha) R_s}{2} = \frac{(1+0.4)(30 \times 10^6)}{2} = 21 \text{ MHz}$$

**(iii) SNR Penalty (2 marks):**

The bandwidth change is from $(1+0.4) = 1.4$ to $(1+0.6) = 1.6$. The ratio of new to old noise power (in linear) is:

$$\frac{N_{new}}{N_{old}} = \frac{1.6}{1.4} = 1.143$$

In dB: $10 \log_{10}(1.143) = 0.58$ dB.

So the SNR **decreases by 0.58 dB** when $\alpha$ changes from 0.4 to 0.6.

**Answer:** 15 MHz, 21 MHz, 0.58 dB SNR penalty.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Rain Attenuation Focus**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: With the help of a block diagram, explain the complete ITU-R P.618 procedure for computing the slant-path rain attenuation in a satellite link. Define all relevant parameters.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in SECTION 4.2 above.

**Step 2 — Procedure Description (5 marks):**

The ITU-R P.618 method for slant-path rain attenuation prediction involves the following sequential steps:

**Step A — Input Parameters:**
- Operating frequency $f$ (GHz)
- Earth station latitude $\phi$ and altitude $h_s$ (km)
- Satellite elevation angle $\theta$ (degrees)
- Polarization tilt $\tau$ (degrees from horizontal)
- Rain rate exceeded for 0.01% of an average year: $R_{0.01}$ (mm/hr)

**Step B — Specific Attenuation (ITU-R P.838):**
Using tabulated coefficients $k(f, \tau)$ and $\alpha(f, \tau)$:

$$\gamma_R = k R_{0.01}^{\alpha} \quad \text{(dB/km)}$$

**Step C — Effective Rain Height (ITU-R P.839):**

$$h_R = h_0 + 0.36 \text{ km}$$

where $h_0$ is the 0°C isotherm height (latitude-dependent).

**Step D — Slant Path Length:**

$$L_s = \frac{h_R - h_s}{\sin \theta} \quad \text{(km)}$$

**Step E — Horizontal Projection:**

$$L_G = L_s \cos \theta \quad \text{(km)}$$

**Step F — Reduction Factor:**

$$r = \frac{1}{1 + L_G / L_0} \quad \text{with } L_0 = 35 \text{ km}$$

**Step G — Effective Path Length:**

$$L_{eff} = L_s \cdot r \quad \text{(km)}$$

**Step H — Total Rain Attenuation:**

$$A_{0.01} = \gamma_R \cdot L_{eff} \quad \text{(dB)}$$

**Step I — Fade Margin Addition:**

$$FM = A_{0.01} + 1 \text{ dB (implementation margin)}$$

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: A satellite earth station is located at an elevation angle of 30°. The operating frequency is 12 GHz, and the rain rate exceeded for 0.01% of time is 95 mm/hr. Given the ITU-R P.838 coefficients for 12 GHz, horizontal polarization: $k = 0.0188$, $\alpha = 1.217$. The 0°C isotherm height is 4.5 km, and the station is at sea level. Compute the total slant-path rain attenuation.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\theta = 30°$, $f = 12$ GHz, $R_{0.01} = 95$ mm/hr
- $k = 0.0188$, $\alpha = 1.217$
- $h_0 = 4.5$ km, $h_s = 0.05$ km

**Step 1 — Effective Rain Height (1 mark):**

$$h_R = h_0 + 0.36 = 4.5 + 0.36 = 4.86 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2 — Slant Path Length (1 mark):**

$$L_s = \frac{h_R - h_s}{\sin \theta} = \frac{4.86 - 0.05}{\sin 30°} = \frac{4.81}{0.5} = 9.62 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3 — Horizontal Projection (1 mark):**

$$L_G = L_s \cos \theta = 9.62 \times \cos 30° = 9.62 \times 0.866 = 8.33 \text{ km}$$

**Step 4 — Specific Attenuation (1 mark):**

$$\gamma_R = k R^{\alpha} = 0.0188 \times (95)^{1.217}$$

Computing $(95)^{1.217}$:

$$\log_{10}(95) = 1.9777, \quad 1.217 \times 1.9777 = 2.4070$$
$$(95)^{1.217} = 10^{2.4070} = 255.3$$

Therefore:

$$\gamma_R = 0.0188 \times 255.3 = 4.80 \text{ dB/km}$$

**Step 5 — Reduction Factor (1 mark):**

$$r = \frac{1}{1 + L_G/L_0} = \frac{1}{1 + 8.33/35} = \frac{1}{1.238} = 0.808$$

**Step 6 — Effective Path Length (1 mark):**

$$L_{eff} = L_s \times r = 9.62 \times 0.808 = 7.77 \text{ km}$$

**Step 7 — Total Rain Attenuation (1 mark):**

$$A_{0.01} = \gamma_R \times L_{eff} = 4.80 \times 7.77 = 37.30 \text{ dB}$$

**Answer:** Total slant-path rain attenuation = **37.30 dB**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**

| Common Mistake | Marks Lost | Correction |
|----------------|------------|------------|
| Using $L_s$ instead of $L_{eff}$ in attenuation calculation | 1–2 | Always apply the reduction factor $r$ for non-uniform rain |
| Forgetting to convert $R_{0.01}$ from mm/hr to log scale correctly | 1 | Use $R^{\alpha}$ calculation carefully: $\log R$ first, then multiply by $\alpha$ |
| Using $h_0$ directly instead of $h_R$ | 1–2 | Add 0.36 km correction per ITU-R P.839 |
| Wrong sign or unit in $\sin \theta$ conversion | 0.5 | Ensure $\theta$ in degrees → decimal radians for $\sin/\cos$ |
| Forgetting to add implementation margin | 0.5 | FM = $A_{0.01}$ + 1 dB is standard |
| Confusing $\alpha$ (rain power law) with roll-off $\alpha$ (pulse shaping) | — | They are *different* parameters; clarify in answer |

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2**

- ✅ **ISI** = pulse spreading causing adjacent symbol interference; dominant in bandlimited channels.
- ✅ **Nyquist Zero-ISI Condition** in time: $h(kT_s) = 0$ for $k \neq 0$.
- ✅ **Nyquist Minimum Bandwidth** = $R_s/2 = R_b/(2\log_2 M)$.
- ✅ **Raised-Cosine Roll-off** spectrum adds $(1+\alpha)$ factor to bandwidth; $\alpha \in [0,1]$.
- ✅ **For $\alpha = 0$**: ideal brick-wall filter (unrealizable).
- ✅ **For $\alpha = 0.5$**: practical compromise, 50% excess bandwidth.
- ✅ **Eye Diagram** is the visual diagnostic; **eye closure = ISI penalty**.
- ✅ **Adaptive Equalizers**: LTE, DFE, MLSE — DFE preferred for satellite.
- ✅ **Rain Attenuation** = dominant impairment above 10 GHz (Ku, Ka, V bands).
- ✅ **Specific Attenuation**: $\gamma_R = k R^{\alpha}$ dB/km (ITU-R P.838).
- ✅ **Slant Path Length**: $L_s = (h_R - h_s)/\sin\theta$ km.
- ✅ **Reduction Factor**: $r = 1/(1 + L_G/L_0)$ with $L_0 = 35$ km.
- ✅ **Total Attenuation**: $A = \gamma_R \times L_{eff} = \gamma_R \times L_s \times r$.
- ✅ **Effective Rain Height**: $h_R = h_0 + 0.36$ km.
- ✅ **Kerala $R_{0.01}$** is significantly higher than the global average (≈120 mm/hr).
- ✅ **Fade Margin** = $A_{0.01}$ + implementation margin (typically +1 dB).
- ✅ **Rain fade mitigation**: UPC, ACM, site diversity, frequency diversity.
- ✅ **ISI mitigation**: pulse shaping (RC filter), adaptive equalization.
- ✅ **BER Sensitivity**: A 6 dB rain fade can increase BER by 5 orders of magnitude for QPSK.
- ✅ **Roll-off factor $\alpha = 0$** = ideal, $\alpha = 1$ = 100% excess, $\alpha = 0.5$ = common practice.
- ✅ **The rain rate exponent** $\alpha$ (in $kR^\alpha$) is NOT the same as the pulse-shaping roll-off $\alpha$ — do not confuse them in KTU answers.
- ✅ **ITU-R P-series recommendations** are mandatory references: P.618 (procedure), P.838 (specific attenuation), P.839 (rain height).
- ✅ **DVB-S2X** uses combined ACM + adaptive equalization for both rain and ISI resilience.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uplink Power Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Uplink Power Control # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Uplink Power Control (UPC)** is a critical fade-mitigation technique employed in satellite communication systems wherein the Earth Station's transmitted Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP) is dynamically adjusted in real-time to compensate for propagation impairments — primarily **rain attenuation**, **cloud attenuation**, and **tropospheric scintillation** — occurring on the uplink (Earth-to-Satellite) path. The fundamental objective is to maintain a **constant Power Flux Density (PFD)** or a **constant Carrier-to-Noise ratio (C/N)** at the satellite transponder input, irrespective of time-varying atmospheric conditions. In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, UPC is classified as an **adaptive link-availability enhancement technique** that falls under the broader umbrella of **Fades Mitigation Techniques (FMTs)** recommended by the **ITU-R** for Ka-band, Ku-band, and V-band satellite links operating above **10 GHz**. ### 1.1.1 Formal Statement of the Problem In a bent-pipe transponder satellite, the **Total C/N** at the satellite receiver output is governed by: $$\frac{1}{(C/N)_{total}} = \frac{1}{(C/N)_{up}} + \frac{1}{(C/N)_{down}} + \frac{1}{(C/N)_{im}}$$ where $(C/N)_{up}$, $(C/N)_{down}$, and $(C/N)_{im}$ are the uplink, downlink, and intermodulation carrier-to-noise ratios respectively. When uplink rain fade degrades $(C/N)_{up}$, the composite $(C/N)_{total}$ collapses. UPC counteracts this by **boosting Earth Station EIRP** by exactly the amount of the fade depth, thereby restoring the original flux density at the satellite. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — The Shouting-in-a-Storm Conversation:** > Imagine you are speaking to a friend standing **500 meters away** during a thunderstorm. On a clear day, you speak at a normal conversational volume and your friend hears you clearly. Suddenly, a heavy downpour begins. The rain drowns out your voice, and your friend can no longer hear you. > > What do you instinctively do? **You shout louder** — you increase your vocal power to overcome the rain noise. If the rain becomes even heavier, you shout even louder. Once the rain stops, you return to a normal volume. > > Uplink Power Control is **exactly this** — the Earth Station (the speaker) senses the "rain" (atmospheric fade) on the uplink path and automatically increases its transmitted power to ensure the satellite (your friend) always receives the signal at a constant level. This same principle governs the uplink from a **cellular handset** to a **base station** in terrestrial 4G/5G networks — known as **Open-Loop Power Control** in 3GPP specifications. --- ## 1.3 Why Uplink Power Control is Indispensable in KTU Module 2 > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Exam Favourite (High Weightage):** > UPC is consistently asked in KTU University examinations because it directly tests a student's grasp of **link-budget arithmetic**, **fade dynamics**, and **transponder loading discipline**. The technique becomes mandatory at frequencies above **10 GHz** where rain attenuation can exceed **10 dB** at **0.01% availability**. ### 1.3.1 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Memory) - **Operating Frequency Threshold:** $f \ge 10 \text{ GHz}$ (Ka, Ku, V bands) - **Typical Rain Attenuation:** $A_{rain} = 1 \text{ to } 20 \text{ dB}$ (depending on rain rate and zone) - **Beacon Frequency:** Often a separate low-frequency CW beacon (e.g., **12.5 GHz**, **20 GHz**) - **UPC Dynamic Range:** Typically **10 dB to 15 dB** of HPA back-off margin - **Standard Units:** **dBW** (absolute power), **dBi** (antenna gain), **dB/K** (G/T figure of merit) - **ITU-R Rain Zone Reference:** **K, N, P, Q** (tropical and equatorial regions of India fall in zones **N** and **P**) --- ## 1.4 Classification of Uplink Power Control Schemes | **Scheme** | **Operating Principle** | **Response Time** | **Accuracy** | |---|---|---|---| | **Open-Loop UPC** | Earth Station estimates fade from beacon downlink; adjusts uplink accordingly | Slow (seconds) | Moderate | | **Closed-Loop UPC** | Satellite measures uplink level and telemands correction back to Earth Station | Fast (ms) | High | | **Hybrid UPC** | Combines both with predictive filtering | Adaptive | Very High | These classifications are the foundation for the deep theoretical analysis in the next section. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fundamental Need for UPC — A C/N Degradation Walkthrough Consider a transparent (bent-pipe) satellite transponder shared by multiple carriers. The **saturation EIRP** of the transponder is fixed, and the satellite operator typically runs the transponder at a **back-off** of **3 dB to 6 dB** to avoid intermodulation products. The uplink rain fade manifests itself in **four cascading effects**: 1. **Reduction in received carrier power** $C_{up}$ at the satellite. 2. **Degradation of uplink C/N**, i.e., $(C/N)_{up} \downarrow$. 3. **Transponder operating point shifts** toward the linear region (if no UPC) — sometimes called **"back-off breathing"**. 4. **Composite link C/N** at the Earth station receiver collapses, leading to **burst errors**, **frame loss**, and eventual **link outage**. The *purpose* of UPC is to **inject exactly the lost dB back into the uplink EIRP**, so that $C_{up}$ at the satellite transponder input is restored to its clear-sky value. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition of UPC Operation > [!NOTE] > **Operating Logic — Six Canonical Steps:** 1. **Step 1 — Sensing the Fade:** The Earth Station monitors a **downlink beacon signal** transmitted by the satellite at a known frequency (often the satellite's own telemetry beacon, e.g., **11.7 GHz** or **12.5 GHz** for Ku-band). 2. **Step 2 — Fade Estimation:** The received beacon level $P_{beacon,Rx}$ is compared with its **clear-sky reference level** $P_{beacon,clear}$. The difference in dB is the **downlink fade depth** $A_{down}$. 3. **Step 3 — Uplink Fade Inference (Asymmetry Correction):** Since rain cells on uplink and downlink are spatially correlated (especially in a single-hop GEO link), the **uplink fade** $A_{up}$ is inferred by applying a **frequency-scaling factor** and a **rain-cell geometry correction factor**. 4. **Step 4 — Look-Up Table (LUT) Update:** A **pre-computed fade-vs-EIRP-correction table** is consulted. Each row maps an estimated $A_{up}$ value to a required HPA drive increase $\Delta P_{HPA}$. 5. **Step 5 — HPA Drive Adjustment:** The High-Power Amplifier (HPA) — typically a **Travelling Wave Tube Amplifier (TWTA)** or a **Solid-State Power Amplifier (SSPA)** — is commanded to increase its output power by $\Delta P_{HPA} = A_{up} + M_{margin}$. 6. **Step 6 — Feedback Verification:** The new uplink signal level is verified at the satellite via **telemetry**, and a closed-loop correction is applied if any residual error exists. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize All Eight Equations Below.** | **#** | **Equation** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | **F1** | $PFD = EIRP / (4 \pi d^2)$ | Power Flux Density at satellite from isotropic radiator | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | **F2** | $PFD_{dBW/m^2} = EIRP_{dBW} - 10\log_{10}(4\pi d^2)$ | PFD in logarithmic scale | $\text{dBW/m}^2$ | | **F3** | $A_{up}(f_1) = A_{up}(f_2) \cdot (f_1 / f_2)^{\alpha}$ | Frequency scaling of rain attenuation (ITU-R P.838) | dB | | **F4** | $\Delta P_{HPA} = A_{up} + A_{margin} - A_{backoff}$ | Required HPA power boost to compensate uplink fade | dB | | **F5** | $(C/N)_{up} = EIRP_{es} - A_{up} + G/T_{sat} - k - B - L_{other}$ | Uplink C/N with rain fade included | dB | | **F6** | $A_{clear-sky} - A_{rain} = \Delta_{UPC}$ | Net UPC correction required to restore clear-sky level | dB | | **F7** | $\eta_{up} = A_{up} / A_{down}$ | Uplink-to-downlink fade correlation ratio (typically 0.8 to 1.2) | dimensionless | | **F8** | $P_{out,HPA,max} = P_{sat} - BO_{HPA}$ | Max HPA output = saturation power minus back-off | dBW | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Pipe Escaping Rule Applied:** The above table uses `\vert` and `\mid` in place of any bare $\vert$ character to preserve the markdown table structure. Do not write $A \le B$ inside table cells — write $A \leq B$ instead. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Where UPC is Deployed in Industry Today:** > - **ISRO's GSAT and INSAT Series:** Ka/Ku-band uplinks from master control facilities (Hasan, Bhopal, Dharwad) use UPC for monsoonal rain resilience. > - **Hughes Jupiter and ViaSat High-Throughput Satellites (HTS):** Employ **Closed-Loop UPC** on user-terminal gateways at Ka-band. > - **DTH (Direct-To-Home) Operators (Tata Play, Dish TV):** Their Earth Station teleports in Mumbai and Bengaluru use UPC to maintain **100.000% transponder availability** even during cyclonic cloud cover. > - **5G Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTN):** 3GPP Release 17 and 18 use **Open-Loop UPC** between UE (smartphone) and LEO satellites for uplink rain-fade mitigation. --- ## 2.5 Inter-relationship Between UPC and Transponder Loading A subtle but frequently-examined point: when the uplink is restored via UPC, the satellite HPA **sees the same input back-off**, so the transponder's intermodulation noise does not change. This is the **"no transponder-penalty"** property of well-designed UPC — a point that examiners love to test. Conversely, if UPC is **not** applied, the satellite HPA is *under-driven* during fades, and the **output back-off** drops, which can paradoxically **decrease intermodulation** but at the cost of severely reduced $(C/N)_{up}$. This trade-off is the classic "UPC or no UPC" decision in **link-budget engineering**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Uplink Power Flux Density at the Satellite We begin with the **Friis transmission equation in free space**, then specialize to the satellite geometry. **Step 1:** The transmitted power flux density at distance $d$ from an isotropic radiator of EIRP is: $$ PFD = \frac{EIRP}{4\pi d^{2}} $$ **Textual Logic:** This is just the surface area of a sphere ($4\pi d^2$) spreading the radiated isotropically. Power per unit area falls off as $1/d^2$. **Step 2:** Convert to logarithmic form (base 10), because all satellite link budgets are calculated in dB: $$ PFD_{dBW/m^{2}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{EIRP}{4\pi d^{2}}\right) $$ **Step 3:** Split the logarithm using the identity $10\log_{10}(a/b) = 10\log_{10}(a) - 10\log_{10}(b)$: $$ PFD_{dBW/m^{2}} = 10 \log_{10}(EIRP) - 10 \log_{10}(4\pi d^{2}) $$ **Step 4:** Recognize the two terms as **EIRP in dBW** and the **free-space spreading loss in dB** (denoted $L_{fs}$): $$ PFD_{dBW/m^{2}} = EIRP_{dBW} - L_{fs,dB} $$ where $$ L_{fs,dB} = 10 \log_{10}(4\pi) + 20 \log_{10}(d) = 10.99 + 20 \log_{10}(d) $$ **Step 5:** For a **Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO)** satellite at altitude $h = 35786 \text{ km}$, the slant range $d$ varies from approximately **35786 km** (zenith) to about **41000 km** (at the edge of coverage at $81.3°$ elevation). The free-space loss at **14 GHz** (typical Ku uplink) for zenith is: $$ L_{fs,dB} = 10.99 + 20 \log_{10}(3.5786 \times 10^{7}) = 10.99 + 20 \times 7.5536 = 10.99 + 151.07 = 162.06 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 6:** Therefore, for an Earth Station transmitting $EIRP_{es} = 60 \text{ dBW}$ at the edge of the beam: $$ PFD_{dBW/m^{2}} = 60 - 162.06 = -102.06 \text{ dBW/m}^{2} $$ **Step 7 (Now Injecting UPC):** If the uplink path suffers a rain fade of $A_{up} = 8 \text{ dB}$, the received PFD at the satellite becomes: $$ PFD_{rain} = -102.06 - 8 = -110.06 \text{ dBW/m}^{2} $$ **Step 8 (UPC Compensation):** To restore the clear-sky PFD, the Earth Station must increase its EIRP by exactly $A_{up}$: $$ EIRP_{new} = 60 + 8 = 68 \text{ dBW} $$ giving $$ PFD_{restored} = 68 - 162.06 - 8 = -102.06 \text{ dBW/m}^{2} \quad \checkmark $$ The clear-sky flux density is fully recovered. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uplink C/N With and Without UPC The general uplink C/N equation for a satellite link is: $$ (C/N)_{up} = EIRP_{es} - L_{fs} + (G/T)_{sat} - k - B - L_{other} - A_{up} $$ **Step 1:** Define all terms in their clear-sky values: - $EIRP_{es} = 60 \text{ dBW}$ - $L_{fs} = 206.5 \text{ dB}$ (at 14 GHz, 40000 km slant range) - $(G/T)_{sat} = 5 \text{ dB/K}$ - $k = -228.6 \text{ dBW/K/Hz}$ (Boltzmann constant) - $B = 36 \text{ MHz} = 75.56 \text{ dBHz}$ - $L_{other} = 1 \text{ dB}$ (feed, pointing, polarization losses) - $A_{up} = 0$ (clear sky) **Step 2:** Compute the clear-sky uplink C/N: $$ (C/N)_{up,clear} = 60 - 206.5 + 5 - (-228.6) - 75.56 - 1 $$ **Step 3:** Group positive and negative terms: $$ (C/N)_{up,clear} = (60 + 5 + 228.6) - (206.5 + 75.56 + 1) $$ **Step 4:** Evaluate both groups: $$ \text{Positive Sum} = 293.6 \quad ; \quad \text{Negative Sum} = 283.06 $$ **Step 5:** Subtract: $$ (C/N)_{up,clear} = 293.6 - 283.06 = 10.54 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 6:** Now introduce an 8 dB uplink rain fade, with **NO UPC**: $$ (C/N)_{up,rain,noUPC} = 10.54 - 8 = 2.54 \text{ dB} $$ This is **8 dB below** the clear-sky value, almost certainly below the **QPSK threshold** of about **5 dB C/N** for a target BER of $10^{-6}$. The link would be **out of service**. **Step 7:** Now apply UPC, increasing EIRP by exactly 8 dB: $$ EIRP_{UPC} = 60 + 8 = 68 \text{ dBW} $$ **Step 8:** Recompute uplink C/N with the boosted EIRP and the same 8 dB fade: $$ (C/N)_{up,rain,withUPC} = 68 - 206.5 + 5 - (-228.6) - 75.56 - 1 - 8 $$ **Step 9:** Group again: $$ = (68 + 5 + 228.6) - (206.5 + 75.56 + 1 + 8) $$ **Step 10:** Evaluate: $$ = 301.6 - 291.06 = 10.54 \text{ dB} \quad \checkmark $$ The clear-sky uplink C/N is **fully restored** to 10.54 dB. > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for Board Exams:** Notice how the $A_{up}$ term in the numerator (EIRP) and denominator (fade) cancel out exactly. This algebraic identity is the **mathematical proof** that UPC works: it makes the net C/N **independent of uplink rain fade**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frequency Scaling of Rain Attenuation (ITU-R P.838) Often, the Earth Station monitors a **beacon frequency** that is different from the **uplink traffic frequency**. The relationship between the two attenuations is given by: $$ A(f_1) = A(f_2) \cdot \left(\frac{f_1}{f_2}\right)^{\alpha} $$ **Step 1:** Define beacon frequency $f_2 = 12.5 \text{ GHz}$, beacon fade $A(f_2) = 5 \text{ dB}$, exponent $\alpha = 1.72$ (typical for moderate rain at 30 mm/hr). **Step 2:** Compute the fade at the uplink frequency $f_1 = 14 \text{ GHz}$: $$ A(14) = 5 \cdot \left(\frac{14}{12.5}\right)^{1.72} $$ **Step 3:** Compute the frequency ratio: $$ \frac{14}{12.5} = 1.12 $$ **Step 4:** Raise to the power 1.72: $$ (1.12)^{1.72} = e^{1.72 \cdot \ln(1.12)} = e^{1.72 \cdot 0.1133} = e^{0.1949} = 1.2152 $$ **Step 5:** Multiply: $$ A(14) = 5 \cdot 1.2152 = 6.076 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 6:** Round to engineering precision: $A(14) \approx 6.08 \text{ dB}$. This is the value that the UPC control loop must inject back into the Earth Station HPA drive level to compensate for uplink fade at 14 GHz, even though the beacon was measured at 12.5 GHz. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use UPC | **System Scenario** | **Apply UPC?** | **Justification** | **Estimated Benefit** | |---|---|---|---| | C-band uplink (4/6 GHz) with high-availability requirement | **Optional** | Rain fade < 1 dB for 99.99% availability in most of India | Marginal (< 1 dB) | | Ku-band uplink (12/14 GHz), DTH teleport | **Highly Recommended** | Rain fade 2 to 6 dB for 99.9% in Kerala, Karnataka | **3 to 6 dB** | | Ka-band gateway (20/30 GHz), HTS | **Mandatory** | Fade 5 to 20 dB even at 99.5% availability | **5 to 15 dB** | | V-band feeder link (50 GHz) | **Essential** | Fade 10+ dB at 99% availability in tropical zones | **10+ dB** | | L-band mobile satellite (1.5/1.6 GHz) | **Not Required** | Frequency below rain-fade threshold | 0 dB | This matrix is a **direct translation of the KTU Module 2 syllabus statement** that UPC is mandatory above 10 GHz and becomes increasingly indispensable at higher frequencies. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Uplink Power Control Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Earth Station Modem

Monitor] --> C4[Sat Telemetry
Encoder] C4 -->|Command Link
TDM Telemetry| C5[ES Command
Decoder] C5 --> C2 end O3 -->|Uplink| Sat((GEO Satellite)) C2 -->|Uplink| Sat Sat -->|Downlink| O1 Sat -->|Downlink| C3 Sat -->|Downlink| C1 ``` **Architectural Insight:** - **Open-Loop** relies solely on the ES receiving its own beacon; no telemetry feedback is used. - **Closed-Loop** uses the satellite's measurement of the actual uplink level, encoded in the satellite's telemetry beacon, and sent back to the ES. - The **closed-loop** is **more accurate** but **slower** due to telemetry round-trip latency (typically **250 ms to 600 ms** for GEO). - The **open-loop** is **faster** but **less accurate** because it does not directly sense the uplink condition. - **Hybrid systems** use both: open-loop for fast reaction, closed-loop for fine correction. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Note to Students:** KTU Part A questions test **Recall and Understanding** (Bloom Level L1–L2). The answer should be concise, **3 to 4 sentences**, with one **key equation** or **diagram** if applicable. --- ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Model Question] > **"Define Uplink Power Control. Mention two situations in which UPC becomes mandatory in satellite communication."** **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Uplink Power Control (UPC)** is an adaptive fade-mitigation technique in which the Earth Station's transmitted EIRP is dynamically increased in proportion to the uplink rain attenuation, in order to maintain a constant **Power Flux Density** at the satellite receiver. > > **Situation 1:** Operating frequency above **10 GHz** (Ku-band, Ka-band, V-band), where rain attenuation exceeds **2 dB** even at 99.9% link availability — for example, **ISRO's GSAT-15 Ku-band DTH uplinks**. > > **Situation 2:** High-throughput satellite (HTS) gateway links operating at **Ka/V band** with stringent **99.99% availability** SLAs — for example, **Hughes Jupiter gateways** during monsoon over Kerala and Karnataka. **Mark Split (KTU Pattern):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two situations. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Model Question] > **"Differentiate between Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Uplink Power Control in two points."** **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!TIP] > **Open-Loop UPC:** The Earth Station estimates uplink fade from its **received downlink beacon level** and adjusts its HPA drive accordingly. It is **fast** but does not directly sense the actual uplink condition at the satellite. > > **Closed-Loop UPC:** The **satellite itself measures** the uplink carrier level and telemands a correction back to the Earth Station via the telemetry channel. It is **highly accurate** but slower due to the **GEO round-trip latency** of approximately **240 ms**. > > **Hybrid UPC:** Combines the speed of open-loop with the accuracy of closed-loop using a predictive digital filter. **Mark Split:** 1 mark for open-loop, 1 mark for closed-loop, 1 mark for the comparative observation. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks and offers **internal choice** between two questions. Each question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, each carrying **7 marks**. You may use diagrams, flowcharts, and step-by-step derivations. --- ### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern] > **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of **Closed-Loop Uplink Power Control** in a Ku-band satellite link. Discuss the role of the **beacon receiver** and the **look-up table** in the control loop. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** An Earth Station operating at **14 GHz** transmits an EIRP of **60 dBW**. The slant range to a GEO satellite is **38,000 km**. The uplink experiences a rain fade of **6 dB** during a heavy monsoon downpour. The satellite's G/T is **+3 dB/K**, the bandwidth is **36 MHz**, and the system noise temperature is **500 K**. Compute: > - **(i)** The clear-sky uplink **C/N** in dB. > - **(ii)** The uplink **C/N during rain without UPC**. > - **(iii)** The minimum HPA EIRP boost required to restore the clear-sky C/N. > - **(iv)** State the **final uplink C/N after UPC** is applied. > **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** > **Block Diagram (2 marks):** > Draw a block diagram showing the following flow: **Earth Station Modem → Up-Converter → HPA (variable gain) → Antenna → Uplink path (with rain fade $A_{up}$) → Satellite transponder**. The feedback path is: **Satellite beacon transmitter → Earth Station Beacon Receiver (LNA + detector) → UPC Controller (with Look-Up Table) → HPA gain command**. > > **Operation (3 marks):** > 1. The satellite transmits a **CW beacon** at a known frequency (often 12.5 GHz) with a stable EIRP. (0.5 mark) > 2. The Earth Station beacon receiver continuously measures the received beacon level $P_{Rx,beacon}$. (0.5 mark) > 3. The UPC controller compares $P_{Rx,beacon}$ with the **clear-sky reference** to determine the **downlink fade** $A_{down}$. (0.5 mark) > 4. The look-up table (pre-computed using ITU-R rain models) converts $A_{down}$ into the corresponding **uplink fade** $A_{up}$ at the operating frequency using the **frequency-scaling law**. (1 mark) > 5. The controller issues a command to the HPA to increase its drive by $A_{up} + M_{margin}$ dB. (0.5 mark) > > **Role of Beacon Receiver and Look-Up Table (2 marks):** > The beacon receiver is the **sensor** of the closed loop; without it, the controller has no real-time information about the propagation channel. The look-up table is the **pre-trained knowledge base** that maps beacon fade measurements to uplink correction commands; it eliminates the need for computationally expensive real-time rain-cell modeling. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Given Data:** - $EIRP_{es} = 60 \text{ dBW}$ - $f = 14 \text{ GHz}$ - $d = 38{,}000 \text{ km} = 3.8 \times 10^{7} \text{ m}$ - $A_{up,rain} = 6 \text{ dB}$ - $(G/T)_{sat} = +3 \text{ dB/K}$ - $B = 36 \text{ MHz}$ **Step 1: Free-Space Path Loss (1 mark):** $$ L_{fs,dB} = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi) + 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) $$ $$ = 32.45 + 20 \log_{10}(3.8 \times 10^{7}) + 20 \log_{10}(14 \times 10^{9}) $$ $$ = 32.45 + 171.60 + 142.92 = 206.97 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 2: Clear-Sky Uplink C/N (2 marks):** $$ (C/N)_{up,clear} = EIRP_{es} - L_{fs} + (G/T)_{sat} - k - B $$ $$ = 60 - 206.97 + 3 - (-228.6) - 10\log_{10}(36 \times 10^{6}) $$ $$ = 60 - 206.97 + 3 + 228.6 - 75.56 $$ $$ = 291.6 - 282.53 = 9.07 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 3: Uplink C/N in Rain Without UPC (1 mark):** $$ (C/N)_{up,rain,noUPC} = 9.07 - 6 = 3.07 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 4: Required HPA Boost (1 mark):** $$ \Delta EIRP_{UPC} = A_{up} = 6 \text{ dB} $$ $$ EIRP_{new} = 60 + 6 = 66 \text{ dBW} $$ **Step 5: Uplink C/N After UPC (2 marks):** $$ (C/N)_{up,rain,UPC} = 66 - 206.97 + 3 + 228.6 - 75.56 - 6 $$ $$ = 297.6 - 288.53 = 9.07 \text{ dB} \quad \checkmark $$ The clear-sky C/N of **9.07 dB** is fully restored. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Forgetting the $20 \log_{10}(f)$ term** in free-space loss. Marks lost: 1. > 2. **Using $4\pi d^2$ in numerator** instead of denominator. Marks lost: 1. > 3. **Not converting 36 MHz to dBHz properly** — many students write 36 dBHz instead of **75.56 dBHz**. Marks lost: 1. > 4. **Forgetting to subtract the 6 dB rain fade** in Step 3 even though "no UPC" implies it is uncompensated. Marks lost: 0.5. > 5. **Not verifying the answer** in Step 5 (it should exactly equal Step 2). Marks lost: 0.5. --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Alternative Internal Choice] > **(a)** Explain the **classification of Uplink Power Control schemes** in detail. Compare the advantages and disadvantages of **Open-Loop**, **Closed-Loop**, and **Hybrid UPC** in terms of accuracy, response time, complexity, and cost. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** An Earth Station at Mumbai uses a beacon receiver to estimate uplink fade. The beacon frequency is **11.7 GHz** and the measured beacon fade is **4.5 dB**. The uplink traffic frequency is **14 GHz**. Using the ITU-R frequency-scaling law with exponent **$\alpha = 1.72$**, compute: > - **(i)** The estimated **uplink fade** at 14 GHz. > - **(ii)** The required **HPA EIRP boost** in dB. > - **(iii)** If the HPA has a saturation EIRP of **75 dBW** and is currently operating at **3 dB output back-off**, determine whether the **HPA can deliver the required boost** without saturating. > - **(iv)** If the HPA cannot deliver the boost, propose **two alternative fade-mitigation techniques**. > **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** **Classification (3 marks):** > **Category 1 — Open-Loop UPC:** Beacon-based estimation at the ES itself. > **Category 2 — Closed-Loop UPC:** Satellite-based measurement with telemetry feedback. > **Category 3 — Hybrid UPC:** Combines both, often with a Kalman filter for predictive smoothing. **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | **Parameter** | **Open-Loop** | **Closed-Loop** | **Hybrid** | |---|---|---|---| | **Accuracy** | Moderate (±2 dB) | High (±0.3 dB) | Very High (±0.2 dB) | | **Response Time** | Fast (10 ms) | Slow (250 to 600 ms) | Adaptive (50 ms) | | **Complexity** | Low | High (requires telemetry) | Very High | | **Cost** | Low | Moderate | High | | **Telemetry Required** | No | Yes | Yes | | **Suitable For** | LEO/MEO links | GEO links with telemetry | Mission-critical GEO | **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1: Frequency-Scaled Uplink Fade (2 marks):** $$ A(14) = A(11.7) \cdot \left(\frac{14}{11.7}\right)^{1.72} $$ $$ \frac{14}{11.7} = 1.1966 $$ $$ (1.1966)^{1.72} = e^{1.72 \cdot \ln(1.1966)} = e^{1.72 \cdot 0.1794} = e^{0.3086} = 1.3615 $$ $$ A(14) = 4.5 \cdot 1.3615 = 6.13 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 2: Required HPA Boost (1 mark):** $$ \Delta EIRP_{UPC} = 6.13 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 3: HPA Headroom Check (2 marks):** The HPA is currently operating with a 3 dB back-off, so its current output is: $$ EIRP_{current} = 75 - 3 = 72 \text{ dBW} $$ After UPC boost, the required output becomes: $$ EIRP_{required} = 72 + 6.13 = 78.13 \text{ dBW} $$ This **exceeds the HPA's saturation EIRP of 75 dBW** by **3.13 dB**. > **Conclusion (1 mark):** The HPA **cannot deliver the required boost** without saturating. The boost must be **clamped** to the available headroom of **3 dB**, leaving a residual fade of **3.13 dB** uncompensated. **Step 4: Alternative Fade-Mitigation Techniques (1 mark):** > 1. **Adaptive Coding and Modulation (ACM):** Switch to a more robust waveform (e.g., from 8PSK to QPSK) to tolerate the residual fade. > 2. **Uplink Diversity (Site Diversity):** Use a second Earth Station at a geographically separated location (e.g., one in Mumbai and one in Pune) and select the clearer path. > 3. **Data Rate Reduction:** Lower the symbol rate to reduce the SNR threshold, gaining approximately **2 to 3 dB**. > 4. **Transponder Power Re-allocation:** Request the satellite operator to allocate more transponder power (if available) via carrier-shaping adjustments. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Alternative Choice B:** > 1. **Confusing beacon frequency with traffic frequency** in the scaling equation. Marks lost: 1. > 2. **Using $\alpha = 1$ instead of 1.72** (treating rain fade as frequency-independent). Marks lost: 1. > 3. **Forgetting to check the HPA saturation limit** — many students stop at "6.13 dB boost needed" without verifying feasibility. Marks lost: 1. > 4. **Naming only one alternative technique** instead of two. Marks lost: 0.5. > 5. **Forgetting the 3 dB back-off** when computing the available headroom. Marks lost: 1. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day** - **Definition:** UPC is the **dynamic adjustment of Earth Station EIRP** to compensate for uplink propagation impairments and maintain **constant PFD at the satellite**. - **Mandatory Frequency Threshold:** Above **10 GHz** (Ku, Ka, V bands) where rain attenuation becomes significant. - **Two Main Schemes:** **Open-Loop** (beacon-based, fast, less accurate) and **Closed-Loop** (telemetry-based, slower, more accurate). - **Key Frequency-Scaling Formula:** $A(f_1) = A(f_2) \cdot (f_1/f_2)^{\alpha}$, with $\alpha \approx 1.72$ for moderate rain. - **Master Equation:** $\Delta EIRP_{UPC} = A_{up}$ — the **mathematical identity** that makes UPC work. - **Free-Space Loss at 14 GHz, GEO:** Approximately **206 dB** (always remember this number). - **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = -228.6 \text{ dBW/K/Hz}$. - **HPA Headroom:** UPC dynamic range is **limited by the HPA back-off** — typically **10 dB to 15 dB** for SSPA, up to **20 dB** for TWTA. - **Transponder Loading:** Well-designed UPC does **not change the transponder intermodulation** because the input back-off is preserved. - **Rain Zones of India:** Kerala, Karnataka, Konkan, Northeast India fall under **ITU-R Rain Zone N and P** — most fade-prone regions in the country. - **Industry Deployments:** ISRO GSAT/INSAT DTH, Hughes Jupiter HTS, ViaSat, 5G NTN (3GPP Rel-17/18). - **Alternative Techniques (when UPC saturates):** **ACM, Uplink Diversity, Data Rate Reduction, Transponder Power Boost**. - **KTU Exam Tip:** Always **show the algebraic cancellation** of $A_{up}$ in the C/N derivation — this is what the examiner looks for as proof of UPC's effectiveness. - **Real-Time Latency Budget:** GEO round-trip is **~240 ms**; LEO round-trip is **~10 ms** — this determines whether closed-loop UPC is feasible in LEO NTN scenarios. - **Beacon Frequencies in ISRO Satellites:** Typically **12.5 GHz** for Ku-band and **20 GHz** for Ka-band. - **Power Flux Density Limit at Satellite:** Per **ITU-R S.672**, the PFD at the GEO satellite must not exceed **-138 dBW/m²/4 kHz** for co-polar signals below **10.7 GHz** — UPC must comply with this regulatory limit. > **End of Notes — Module 2 / Uplink Power Control**

topics.title: System Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Availability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**System Availability** in satellite communication is defined as the *probability* or *fraction of time* that the communication system (including space segment, ground segment, and link) is operational and delivering services within the specified performance tolerances (e.g., BER, EIRP, G/T) over a long observation period.

Mathematically, it is the ratio of the **uptime** (the duration the system is actually performing its intended function) to the **total scheduled mission time** (uptime + downtime).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** System availability is a key **reliability-engineering metric** that quantifies the operational readiness of a satellite communication link. It is the combined effect of *hardware reliability*, *mean time to repair (MTTR)*, and the *redundancy architecture* implemented at the spacecraft and earth-station levels.

The standard telecom industry targets for satellite systems are:

| Service Tier | Target Availability | Allowed Downtime per Year |
|--------------|--------------------|---------------------------|
| Consumer broadband | **99.5 %** | ~43.8 hours |
| VSAT / Enterprise | **99.9 %** | ~8.76 hours |
| Mission-critical / Defense | **99.99 %** | ~52.6 minutes |
| Deep-space / Safety-of-life | **99.999 %** | ~5.26 minutes |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a public bus service. The schedule says the bus runs 24 hours a day, but every now and then it breaks down, goes to the garage, and is unavailable for hours or days.

- **Reliability** → *How long does the bus run before it breaks down the first time?* (this is the **MTBF**)
- **Maintainability** → *How quickly can the garage fix it?* (this is the **MTTR**)
- **Availability** → *What percentage of the time is the bus actually on the road carrying passengers?*

If the bus runs for 1000 hours and then needs 2 hours in the garage, the cycle repeats. Its **availability = 1000/1002 ≈ 99.8 %**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Same logic for a satellite:** the spacecraft, transponder, or earth-station equipment is "running" (uptime = MTBF) and periodically fails or is taken offline for repair (downtime = MTTR). System availability is the long-run fraction of time it is *actually working*.

### 1.3 Why System Availability is a *Module-2 Pivotal Concept*

In KTU Module 2 we study the **link budget, propagation impairments, modulation, coding, and the engineering trade-offs** required to build a working satellite link. All these sub-systems must *together* stay operational with high probability, otherwise the carefully designed **C/N, Eb/N0, and link margin** become meaningless during outage windows. Therefore, availability closes the loop on system engineering: it ties the **electrical design (Module 2)** to the **operational reliability (Module 3 and beyond)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Availability

System availability is governed by **three engineering quantities**, each tied to a physical design decision:

1. **Reliability (R)** – probability that a component performs its function under stated conditions for a given duration. Governed by component quality, derating, and radiation hardening.
2. **Maintainability (M)** – probability that a failed system is restored to working order within a given time. Governed by repair crew, spares, and access to the failed unit.
3. **Redundancy Architecture** – the topology (series, parallel, k-out-of-n) of backups. Governed by mass, power, and cost budgets on the spacecraft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to remember**
> - MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) – hours
> - MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) – hours
> - MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) – for *non-repairable* items, hours
> - Failure rate $\lambda$ = 1 / MTBF – per hour
> - Availability expressed as a *dimensionless ratio* between 0 and 1, OR as a *percentage*.

### 2.2 Inherent Availability — The Working Formula

For a *single repairable* component:

$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

For a *non-repairable* item (e.g., a chip on a deep-space probe that cannot be serviced):

$$A(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \qquad \lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** To *raise* availability you can either (a) *increase MTBF* by using better, derated, radiation-hardened components, OR (b) *decrease MTTR* by adding redundancy switches, hot-standby spares, and faster repair logistics. Both are costly; engineering is the art of trade-off.

### 2.3 Effect of Redundancy Topology

| Topology | Availability Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------|
| **Series** (all units must work) | $A_s = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdots A_n$ | The *weakest link* dominates; multiplied reliability collapses fast. |
| **Active Parallel (1-out-of-2)** | $A_p = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)$ | System survives if *either* unit survives. Big boost in availability. |
| **Standby Parallel (1-out-of-2 with switch)** | $A_{sb} = A_1 + (1 - A_1)\,A_2\,A_{sw}$ | Includes the *switch* reliability $A_{sw}$. |
| **k-out-of-n** | $A_{k/n} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} A^{i}(1-A)^{n-i}$ | At least k of n units must work; common in transponder banks. |

> [!TIP]
> **Always watch the pipes in markdown tables.** The KTU 2024 Scheme uses typeset math. In the formula sheet we render the absolute value of a quantity $x$ as $\vert x \vert$ — never the unescaped `|x|` form, because the bare pipe breaks markdown table columns.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | LaTeX | Meaning / Units |
|---|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Steady-state availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le A \le 1$ |
| 2 | Unavailability | $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Also called *outage probability* |
| 3 | Failure rate | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failures per hour |
| 4 | Reliability (exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of *no* failure by time $t$ |
| 5 | Series availability | $A_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | All units must work |
| 6 | Parallel availability | $A_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | At least one unit works |
| 7 | k-out-of-n availability | $A_{k/n} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i}A^{i}(1-A)^{n-i}$ | At least k of n work |
| 8 | Overall link availability | $A_{link} = A_{sat} \cdot A_{ES1} \cdot A_{ES2} \cdot A_{prop}$ | Series composition of segments |
| 9 | Approximation: high availability | $U \approx n \cdot U_i$ | n identical series stages with $U_i \ll 1$ |
| 10 | Annual downtime | $T_{down} = (1 - A) \times 8760$ | Hours of outage per year |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite operators (Intelsat, SES, ISRO)** quote availability figures in service-level agreements (SLAs) to enterprise customers.
- **Insurance and risk assessment** for satellite launches uses availability projections to compute expected revenue loss.
- **Mission planning** for deep-space probes uses *non-repairable* availability, because a remote failure cannot be fixed.
- **Network architects** combine link availability, rain-fade margin, and equipment MTBF to size the *overall* end-to-end service availability of VSAT networks and broadcast DTH systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Steady-State Availability $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$

Consider a *single, repairable* unit observed over a very long horizon $T \rightarrow \infty$. The unit alternates between an "operating" state and a "failed-under-repair" state.

**Step 1 — Define the observation cycle.**
A complete cycle consists of one operating period of mean duration MTBF, followed by one repair period of mean duration MTTR. The mean cycle length is:

$$
T_{cycle} = MTBF + MTTR
$$

**Step 2 — Define uptime fraction.**
Within one cycle, the system is operational for MTBF hours and non-operational for MTTR hours. Availability is the *fraction* of the cycle the system is up:

$$
A = \frac{\text{Operating time}}{\text{Total time}} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 3 — Express failure rate in terms of MTBF.**
By definition the (constant) failure rate is the reciprocal of MTBF:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine the two results.**
Substituting $\lambda$ back into the expression for $A$ we obtain the equivalent reliability-engineering form:

$$
A = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + MTTR} = \frac{1}{1 + \lambda \cdot MTTR}
$$

This is the **canonical "A = 1/(1 + λ·MTTR)"** form that KTU frequently tests.

**Step 5 — Unavailability series approximation (for high-availability systems).**
For a high-reliability link, $MTTR \ll MTBF$, hence $A \approx 1 - \lambda \cdot MTTR$. The unavailability becomes:

$$
U = 1 - A \approx \frac{MTTR}{MTBF} = \lambda \cdot MTTR
$$

This is the *small-downtime* engineering approximation, valid whenever $U \le 0.1$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A Ku-band VSAT earth station has an MTBF of 8000 hours and an MTTR of 4 hours. The on-board transponder has MTBF = 50 000 h and MTTR = 720 h (waiting for next eclipse window + ground-commanded switch). The propagation segment experiences a 0.2 % annual outage due to rain fade. Compute (a) earth-station availability, (b) transponder availability, (c) overall link availability, and (d) expected annual downtime in minutes.

**Part (a) — Earth station availability**

$$
A_{ES} = \frac{8000}{8000 + 4} = \frac{8000}{8004} = 0.9995
$$

So $A_{ES} = 99.95\,\%$.

**Part (b) — Transponder availability**

$$
A_{TX} = \frac{50\,000}{50\,000 + 720} = \frac{50\,000}{50\,720} = 0.98580
$$

So $A_{TX} \approx 98.58\,\%$.

**Part (c) — Overall link availability (series composition)**

$$
A_{link} = A_{ES} \cdot A_{TX} \cdot A_{prop} = 0.9995 \times 0.9858 \times 0.998
$$

Intermediate steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.9995 \times 0.9858 &= 0.98531 \\
0.98531 \times 0.998 &= 0.98334
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $A_{link} \approx 0.98334$, or **98.33 %** overall availability.

**Part (d) — Annual downtime**

$$
T_{down} = (1 - A_{link}) \times 8760 = 0.01666 \times 8760 = 145.94 \text{ hours}
$$

Converting to minutes:

$$
T_{down} = 145.94 \times 60 = 8756 \text{ minutes per year}
$$

This shows that the *transponder* dominates the outage budget — exactly the diagnostic insight KTU examiners reward.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Series System | Parallel (1-out-of-2) System |
|--------|---------------|------------------------------|
| Availability expression | $A_s = \prod A_i$ | $A_p = 1 - (1-A_1)(1-A_2)$ |
| If both $A_i = 0.9$ | $A_s = 0.81$ | $A_p = 0.99$ |
| If both $A_i = 0.99$ | $A_s = 0.9801$ | $A_p = 0.9999$ |
| Failure behaviour | Any one fails → system fails | One must work → system works |
| Design implication | Strengthen *every* link | Add a *redundant* unit + switch |
| Typical application | End-to-end link budget chain | TWTAs, transponders, power supplies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold observation:** redundancy is *only* beneficial when $A_i > 0.5$. If a single unit has $A_i = 0.3$, even a parallel pair gives $A_p = 0.51$ — usually *not* worth the mass and power cost. Always check this threshold before recommending redundancy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Availability — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional flow of all sub-systems whose availabilities combine to form the end-to-end link availability.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER TERMINAL] --> B[EARTH STATION A]
    B --> C[UPLINK PATH]
    C --> D[TRANSPONDER PAYLOAD]
    D --> E[DOWNLINK PATH]
    E --> F[EARTH STATION B]
    F --> G[USER NETWORK]

    H[POWER SUPPLY] --> D
    I[TT AND C SUBSYSTEM] --> D
    J[REDUNDANCY SWITCH MATRIX] --> D
    K[REPAIR AND OPS TEAM] --> B
    L[REPAIR AND OPS TEAM] --> F

    M[RAIN FADE MARGIN MODULE] --> C
    M --> E

    N[MTBF DATABASE] --> O[AVAILABILITY ENGINE]
    P[MTTR DATABASE] --> O
    O --> Q[REPORTED A LINK]
```

**How to read the diagram**

- The **forward chain** (A → G) is the *user data path*. Each box has its own availability; the chain's *series* product is the link availability.
- The **payload (D)** receives auxiliary services from the power supply, TT&C, and the redundancy switch matrix — these are the *internal* sub-systems whose availabilities are *combined in parallel* inside the spacecraft.
- **Repair teams (K, L)** shorten the MTTR for earth-station outages.
- The **rain-fade margin module (M)** is the propagation-side compensation block that protects the link against the *propagation availability* term.
- The **availability engine (O)** is the design/operations tool that ingests MTBF/MTTR databases and reports the calculated $A_{link}$.

### 4.2 MTTR / MTBF — Renewal Cycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OPERATIONAL
    OPERATIONAL --> FAILED : failure event
    FAILED --> OPERATIONAL : repair complete
    OPERATIONAL --> OPERATIONAL : continue working
    FAILED --> FAILED : repair in progress

    note right of OPERATIONAL : Mean residence time = MTBF
    note right of FAILED : Mean residence time = MTTR
```

**Interpretation**

- The system begins in the **OPERATIONAL** state.
- A random failure event (rate $\lambda = 1/MTBF$) transitions it to the **FAILED** state.
- A repair team (rate $\mu = 1/MTTR$) transitions it back to **OPERATIONAL**.
- The *steady-state probability* of being in the OPERATIONAL state is exactly the availability $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.

### 4.3 Series vs Parallel Redundancy — Topology Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S[Series topology]
        S1[Unit 1] --> S2[Unit 2] --> S3[Unit 3] --> S4[Output]
    end

    subgraph P[Parallel topology 1 out of 2]
        P1[Unit A]
        P2[Unit B]
        P1 --> PSW[OR switch] --> P3[Output]
        P2 --> PSW
    end
```

**Reading the topology**

- In the **series** chain, the failure of *any single* unit (1, 2, or 3) breaks the path.
- In the **parallel** chain, *either* A or B can deliver the signal; the OR switch selects the live unit. The switch itself has its own availability $A_{sw}$, which must be included for an exact analysis.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define *System Availability* in the context of a satellite communication link. State its mathematical expression in terms of MTBF and MTTR.

**Model Answer:**
System availability is the probability that a satellite communication system is operational and meeting its performance specifications at any given instant of time over its mission life. It is the ratio of the total *uptime* to the total scheduled *mission time*.

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

where MTBF = mean time between failures and MTTR = mean time to repair. It is expressed as a *dimensionless fraction* (0 to 1) or as a *percentage*. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between *reliability* and *availability* of a satellite transponder. Why is availability usually higher than reliability over a long mission?

**Model Answer:**
*Reliability* $R(t)$ is the probability that a component performs its intended function *without failure* for a stated duration $t$ — it assumes no repair is performed and only decreases with time. *Availability* is the long-run fraction of time the system is operational, *including the effect of repairs and redundancy*. Because availability credits the time the system spends in the *repaired* state, it is always greater than or equal to the instantaneous reliability over a long mission. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice between A and B)

> **Common Stem:** A C-band satellite link consists of the following sub-systems with the stated MTBF and MTTR values. Compute all required availabilities and comment on which sub-system is the bottleneck. (Use $8760$ hours per year.)

| Sub-system | MTBF (h) | MTTR (h) |
|------------|----------|----------|
| Earth station Tx/Rx | 6000 | 6 |
| Transponder (TWTA + LNA) | 40 000 | 600 |
| Propagation (rain + gaseous) | 90 000 | 12 |

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Determine the steady-state availability of each sub-system and the **overall end-to-end link availability**. Identify the bottleneck segment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Earth station availability.

$$
A_1 = \frac{6000}{6000 + 6} = \frac{6000}{6006} = 0.999001 \;\; (99.900\,\%)
$$

Step 2 — Transponder availability.

$$
A_2 = \frac{40\,000}{40\,000 + 600} = \frac{40\,000}{40\,600} = 0.985222 \;\; (98.522\,\%)
$$

Step 3 — Propagation availability.

$$
A_3 = \frac{90\,000}{90\,000 + 12} = \frac{90\,000}{90\,012} = 0.999867 \;\; (99.987\,\%)
$$

Step 4 — Overall link availability (series composition).

$$
A_{link} = A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3 = 0.999001 \times 0.985222 \times 0.999867
$$

Intermediate product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.999001 \times 0.985222 &= 0.984236 \\
0.984236 \times 0.999867 &= 0.984107
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $A_{link} \approx 0.9841$, or **98.41 %** end-to-end availability.

Step 5 — Bottleneck identification.
Comparing unavailabilities:
$U_1 = 9.99 \times 10^{-4}$, $U_2 = 1.478 \times 10^{-2}$, $U_3 = 1.33 \times 10^{-4}$.

The **transponder** dominates by more than an order of magnitude and is therefore the bottleneck. Recommendation: add an on-board *redundant* TWTA in cold-standby and shorten the MTTR by pre-positioning the switch command sequence. **(Full 7 marks)**

**(b)** If a fully redundant *cold-standby* TWTA is added (with switch reliability $A_{sw} = 0.999$ and identical MTBF = 40 000 h, MTTR = 600 h for each), compute the **new transponder availability** and the **new overall link availability**. By how many hours per year has the downtime been reduced? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Single TWTA availability.

$$
A_{TWTA} = \frac{40\,000}{40\,600} = 0.985222
$$

Step 2 — Cold-standby (1-out-of-2 with switch) availability.

$$
A_{TX,new} = A_{TWTA} + (1 - A_{TWTA}) \cdot A_{TWTA} \cdot A_{sw}
$$

Substituting:

$$
A_{TX,new} = 0.985222 + (0.014778)(0.985222)(0.999)
$$

Compute the second term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.014778)(0.985222) &= 0.014560 \\
(0.014560)(0.999) &= 0.014545
\end{aligned}
$$

So

$$
A_{TX,new} = 0.985222 + 0.014545 = 0.999767
$$

Step 3 — New overall link availability.

$$
A_{link,new} = A_1 \times A_{TX,new} \times A_3 = 0.999001 \times 0.999767 \times 0.999867
$$

Intermediate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.999001 \times 0.999767 &= 0.998769 \\
0.998769 \times 0.999867 &= 0.998637
\end{aligned}
$$

So $A_{link,new} \approx 0.99864$, or **99.86 %** end-to-end.

Step 4 — Annual downtime comparison.

Old: $T_{down,old} = (1 - 0.984107) \times 8760 = 139.4$ hours.
New: $T_{down,new} = (1 - 0.998637) \times 8760 = 11.9$ hours.

Reduction:

$$
\Delta T = 139.4 - 11.9 = 127.5 \text{ hours per year}
$$

Conclusion: redundancy cuts annual downtime by **≈ 127.5 hours**, raising the link from *consumer-grade* to *enterprise-grade* availability. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Derive, from first principles, the steady-state availability expression $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ for a single repairable unit. State the assumption on failure rate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. Consider a single repairable unit observed over a long horizon $T \to \infty$.
2. The unit alternates between OPERATIONAL (mean duration MTBF) and FAILED (mean duration MTTR) states.
3. A *renewal cycle* has total length $T_{cycle} = MTBF + MTTR$.
4. Steady-state availability = (time in OPERATIONAL) / (total time) = $MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
5. Assumption: failure rate $\lambda$ is **constant** (memoryless / exponential distribution), and the repair rate $\mu$ is also constant.
6. Equivalent form using $\lambda = 1/MTBF$ and $\mu = 1/MTTR$ is $A = \mu / (\lambda + \mu)$.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 1-out-of-2 active-redundant earth-station pair where each unit has $A_1 = A_2 = 0.95$ and the changeover switch has $A_{sw} = 0.99$, compute the **system availability**. If a 1-out-of-3 arrangement is used instead (with identical unit availability and a perfect switch), what is the new system availability? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 — 1-out-of-2 parallel availability.

$$
A_{p,2} = 1 - (1 - 0.95)(1 - 0.95)(1 - 0.99)
$$

Intermediate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 - 0.95)(1 - 0.95) &= 0.0025 \\
0.0025 \times 0.01 &= 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \\
A_{p,2} &= 1 - 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 0.999975
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — 1-out-of-3 (perfect switch) availability.

$$
A_{p,3} = 1 - (1 - 0.95)^3 = 1 - (0.05)^3 = 1 - 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = 0.999875
$$

Wait — careful. The expression for 1-out-of-3 is $1 - (1-A)^3$.

$$
(1 - 0.95)^3 = (0.05)^3 = 0.000125
$$

Hence

$$
A_{p,3} = 1 - 0.000125 = 0.999875
$$

Step 3 — Comparison.

$A_{p,2} = 0.999975$ vs $A_{p,3} = 0.999875$.

The 1-out-of-2 with a slightly imperfect switch *outperforms* a 1-out-of-3 with a perfect switch in this case because adding a *third* unit gives *another* path to fail. Lesson: more redundancy is not always better when units are identical — diminishing returns must be evaluated.

**(Full 7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in the KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Series vs parallel mix-up.** End-to-end link availability is *always* the product of segment availabilities (series). Redundancy *within* a segment is parallel. Students often multiply redundancies as if they were series and obtain absurdly low availabilities.
> 2. **Forgetting the switch reliability.** Cold-standby availability requires the *changeover switch* term. Ignoring it is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Unit confusion between hours and minutes.** MTTR is usually given in hours; convert carefully when computing annual downtime in minutes.
> 4. **Using the wrong approximation.** The formula $U \approx n \cdot U_i$ is valid only for *identical* series stages each with $U_i \ll 1$. Do not apply it to parallel systems.
> 5. **Markdown rendering errors.** Always use $\vert x \vert$ (or `\vert`) — never bare pipes — inside KTU formula tables. A broken column is treated as incomplete presentation by examiners.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **System availability** = fraction of mission time the link is operational; it is the *operational* counterpart of reliability.
- **Canonical formula:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) = 1 / (1 + \lambda MTTR)$.
- **Series composition** for end-to-end links: $A_{link} = \prod A_i$ — the *weakest segment dominates*.
- **Parallel (redundancy) composition:** $A_{p} = 1 - \prod (1 - A_i)$ — boosts availability but requires a *reliable switch*.
- **k-out-of-n formula:** $A_{k/n} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} A^{i}(1-A)^{n-i}$ — useful for transponder banks.
- **Unavailability approximation:** $U_{link} \approx \sum U_i$ for small $U_i$ — convenient for SLAs.
- **Annual downtime:** $T_{down} = (1 - A) \times 8760$ hours/year.
- **Engineering levers** to raise availability: (i) better/derated components (raise MTBF), (ii) faster repair logistics (lower MTTR), (iii) parallel redundancy + high-reliability switch.
- **Threshold rule:** redundancy is *only* cost-effective if the *single-unit* availability exceeds **0.5**; below that, the math stops helping.
- **Real-world benchmarks:** 99.5 % consumer, 99.9 % enterprise, 99.99 % military, 99.999 % safety-of-life — always quote the *target* before sizing redundancy.
- **KTU exam hot-button:** expect a numerical problem combining *three* sub-system availabilities in series, plus a *one-unit-redundancy* sub-question — practice this hybrid pattern until it is second nature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Design for Link Without Frequency Reuse and System Design for Link With Frequency Reuse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Design for Link Without Frequency Reuse and System Design for Link With Frequency Reuse

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Satellite Link Design** is the rigorous engineering process of computing the carrier-to-noise ratio (C/N), carrier-to-interference ratio (C/I), and the resultant carrier-to-noise-plus-interference ratio (C/(N+I)) at the receiver output, while satisfying the quality-of-service (QoS) constraints of bit-error-rate (BER) and availability for a specified modulation/coding scheme.

The design is bifurcated based on the spectral resource management strategy:

1. **System Design for Link Without Frequency Reuse (Single Carrier / Non-Reuse Link):** A link architecture where the available bandwidth of a transponder is assigned to a single carrier (or a non-overlapping group), so that each frequency band is used only once in the coverage zone. The performance bottleneck is purely **thermal noise** (uplink C/Nᵤ and downlink C/Nₐ).

2. **System Design for Link With Frequency Reuse:** A link architecture in which the same frequency band (or polarization) is reused within the beam or across multiple beams to multiply system capacity. The performance bottleneck is governed by **co-channel interference (CCI)** characterized by C/I, in addition to thermal noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Module 2 focuses on the *satellite link design equations*, transponder modeling, and the *frequency reuse factor* `n` (typically `n = 2, 3, 4, 7`). The reuse factor determines the cluster size `N` in a cellular analogy and the resulting co-channel interference ratio.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Water-Pipeline System**
> 
> - **Without Frequency Reuse:** Imagine a city supplied by a single, very wide water pipe (one transponder, one carrier). Every drop of water is "clean" and only suffers friction loss (thermal noise). To serve more customers, you simply build a wider pipe. The total throughput is limited by pipe diameter, but the water quality is perfect.
> 
> - **With Frequency Reuse:** Now imagine doubling the capacity by laying *two parallel pipes carrying the SAME type of water* in the same trench, one carrying horizontally-polarized water and the other vertically-polarized water (orthogonal polarization reuse). At the receiving house, a smart filter separates the two. If the filter is imperfect, "cross-contamination" occurs — this is **co-channel interference**, quantified by the Cross-Polarization Discrimination (XPD).

The engineering trade-off is therefore: **reuse gains you capacity (more bits/Hz/km²) but introduces a noise floor (C/I) that degrades link quality unless you design antennas, polarizers, and beam isolations meticulously.**

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded)

- **Boltzmann's constant:** $k = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (exactly, by SI definition)
- **Carrier frequency bands:** L (~1.5 GHz), S (~2.5 GHz), C (~4/6 GHz), Ku (~12/14 GHz), Ka (~20/30 GHz)
- **Frequency Reuse Factor (`n`):** Number of times a frequency is reused within the system
- **Cluster size (`N`):** Number of cells in a frequency reuse cluster (related to `n` by $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ in hexagonal cells)
- **Cross-Polarization Discrimination (XPD):** typically **27 dB to 35 dB** for well-designed Ku-band systems

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Link Without Frequency Reuse

In a non-reuse (single carrier per transponder) link, the analysis decomposes into three cascaded stages:

1. **Uplink (Earth Station → Satellite):** The earth station's EIRP and the satellite's figure-of-merit G/Tₛ define the uplink C/Nᵤ.
2. **Satellite Transponder:** Adds intermodulation noise if multiple carriers are present; for single-carrier case, the transponder is treated as a transparent amplifier with a given gain and noise figure.
3. **Downlink (Satellite → Earth Station):** The satellite's EIRP and the earth station's G/Tₑ define the downlink C/Nₐ.

The total C/N is the *power sum* (not dB sum) of the two contributions:

$$[C/N]_{T}^{-1} = [C/N]_{U}^{-1} + [C/N]_{D}^{-1}$$

### 2.2 Operational Logic of Link With Frequency Reuse

When the same frequency is reused (e.g., dual-polarization or multi-beam), a new impairment appears: **co-channel interference** from the orthogonal channel. Two design parameters dominate:

- **XPD (Cross-Polarization Discrimination):** Measures how well the antenna rejects the orthogonal polarization. Higher XPD → lower interference.
- **C/I (Carrier-to-Interference Ratio):** Determines the "interference-limited" performance.

The combined metric for a reuse link is:

$$[C/(N+I)]^{-1} = [C/N]^{-1} + [C/I]^{-1}$$

For multi-beam reuse, **beam-to-beam isolation** (side-lobe coupling) and **angular separation** determine C/I.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are in **decibels (dB)** unless explicitly noted. Brackets `[x]` denote dB-value of `x`.

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression (dB form) | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---------------------|----------------------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Free-space path loss | $[L_p] = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$ | dB | f: MHz, d: km |
| 2 | Uplink C/N | $[C/N]_U = [EIRP]_{ES} + [G/T]_S - [L_{pU}] - [k] - [B]$ | dB | $[k] = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz |
| 3 | Downlink C/N | $[C/N]_D = [EIRP]_S + [G/T]_{ES} - [L_{pD}] - [k] - [B]$ | dB | Same as uplink, mirrored |
| 4 | Total C/N (no reuse) | $[C/N]_T = -10\log_{10}(10^{-[C/N]_U/10} + 10^{-[C/N]_D/10})$ | dB | Power sum rule |
| 5 | C/I from XPD | $[C/I] = [XPD] + \Delta_{pol}$ | dB | $\Delta_{pol}$ = polarization isolation margin |
