| 6 | Carrier-to-noise-plus-interference | $[C/(N+I)] = -10\log_{10}(10^{-[C/N]/10} + 10^{-[C/I]/10})$ | dB | Reuse link metric |
| 7 | Reuse factor / capacity gain | $C_{reuse} = n \times C_{single}$ | bps | n = 2 (dual-pol), 4 (2×2), 7 (cellular) |
| 8 | Reuse-related C/I for hexagonal cells | $[C/I] = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{(D/R)^n \cdot q}{6}\right)$ | dB | D = co-channel distance, R = cell radius, q = 0.5 to 1, n ≈ 4 (path loss exponent) |
| 9 | System noise temp | $T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{feed}(L-1)/G + T_{LNA} + T_{down}$ | K | Cascaded noise |
| 10 | EIRP | $[EIRP] = [P_T] + [G_T] - [L_{feed}]$ | dBW | Effective isotropic radiated power |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Rule:** $[k] = 10\log_{10}(1.380649 \times 10^{-23}) = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz. The **bandwidth** `B` MUST be entered in **Hz** (not kHz or MHz) when using this constant.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Without reuse** designs are used for **single high-data-rate trunk carriers** (e.g., VSAT backhaul, DTH TV's primary MCPC carrier) where interference protection outweighs capacity gains.
- **With reuse (dual polarization)** is the cornerstone of **modern DTH TV (Ku-band)** — two adjacent satellites at the same orbital slot can reuse frequencies, doubling the transponder capacity. Intelsat, SES, and DTH operators use this aggressively.
- **With reuse (multi-beam Ka-band)** powers **HTS (High-Throughput Satellites)** like ViaSat-3 and Jupiter-3, which achieve **Tbps** capacity through 200+ spot beams with 4-color frequency reuse.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Uplink C/N Equation (Without Frequency Reuse)

We start from the Friis transmission equation. The received power at the satellite from an earth station is:

$$P_R = P_T \cdot G_T \cdot G_R \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

where:
- $P_T$ = transmitted power by earth station
- $G_T$ = earth station antenna gain
- $G_R$ = satellite antenna gain
- $\lambda$ = wavelength, $d$ = slant range
- $\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ = free-space path loss factor $L_p^{-1}$

The **noise power** in a bandwidth `B` at the satellite receiver is:

$$N = k \cdot T_{sys,S} \cdot B$$

Taking the ratio C/N and converting to decibels (each factor becomes its dB equivalent):

$$[C/N]_U = [P_T] + [G_T] - [L_{feed,ES}] + [G_R] - [L_p] - [k] - [B] - [T_{sys,S}]$$

Grouping the transmit-side terms as EIRP = $P_T + G_T - L_{feed,ES}$ and receive-side as G/T = $G_R - T_{sys,S}$:

$$\boxed{[C/N]_U = [EIRP]_{ES} + [G/T]_S - [L_{pU}] - [k] - [B]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic step:** Converting the linear product to a sum of dB terms uses $10\log_{10}(A \cdot B) = 10\log_{10}(A) + 10\log_{10}(B)$. The negative signs in front of $[L_p]$, $[k]$, $[B]$, $[T_{sys}]$ indicate they are *losses* or *denominator* terms that reduce the C/N.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Total C/N for Cascaded Uplink + Downlink (No Reuse)

The total noise at the earth station demodulator is the *sum* of noise contributions from both the uplink (re-radiated by the transponder at full power, hence scaled) and the downlink. In linear scale:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_T^{-1} = \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_U^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_D^{-1}$$

**Detailed justification:** Let the received carrier at the earth station be $C$. The uplink noise $N_U$ (at the satellite input) is amplified along with the carrier and reappears at the earth station scaled by the same factor. After amplification, the uplink-noise contribution at the demodulator equals the carrier divided by $(C/N)_U$. Similarly, the downlink noise contribution equals the carrier divided by $(C/N)_D$. The two noise powers add incoherently (Gaussian noise from independent sources):

$$N_{total} = C \cdot \left[\left(\frac{N}{C}\right)_U + \left(\frac{N}{C}\right)_D\right]$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{C}{N_{total}} = \left[\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_U^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_D^{-1}\right]^{-1}$$

Converting to dB:

$$[C/N]_T = -10\log_{10}\left(10^{-[C/N]_U/10} + 10^{-[C/N]_D/10}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the power sum and not dB sum?** Because noise voltages add in *quadrature* (uncorrelated Gaussian processes), the **noise powers** (not dB values) are summed. The 10log and -10log combination yields the equivalent single C/N.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: C/I from Cross-Polarization Discrimination (Reuse Link)

In a dual-polarization frequency reuse system, the wanted signal `C` arrives on, say, vertical polarization, while the interfering signal `I` arrives on horizontal polarization. The antenna/polarizer cannot perfectly reject the orthogonal component.

By definition:

$$[XPD] = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{wanted,pol}}{P_{leak,pol}}\right) \quad \text{in dB}$$

For an **interference-limited** system (where the interference `I` from the reused channel is co-channel and added to thermal noise `N`), the carrier-to-interference ratio at the demodulator is approximately equal to the XPD plus a polarization-isolation margin $\Delta_{pol}$ that accounts for rain depolarization, antenna imperfections, and depolarization along the path:

$$[C/I] = [XPD] + \Delta_{pol}$$

Typical values:
- **Clear sky:** XPD ≈ 30–35 dB
- **Rain (heavy):** XPD may degrade to 15–20 dB
- **Design margin $\Delta_{pol}$:** often set to 3–6 dB for 99.9% availability

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Combined C/(N+I) for Frequency-Reuse Link

When both thermal noise and co-channel interference are present, the total impairment at the demodulator is:

$$\frac{N+I}{C} = \frac{N}{C} + \frac{I}{C} = \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)^{-1}$$

Inverting and converting to dB:

$$[C/(N+I)] = -10\log_{10}\left(10^{-[C/N]/10} + 10^{-[C/I]/10}\right)$$

**Special case (interference-limited link):** If $[C/I] \ll [C/N]$ by 5 dB or more, then:

$$[C/(N+I)] \approx [C/I] - 3 \text{ dB (correction) } \approx [C/I]$$

This is why in **highly reused HTS systems**, the C/I (not C/N) drives the link design.

### 3.5 Derivation 5: C/I in Multi-Beam Cellular Reuse (Hexagonal)

For a satellite serving a region with hexagonal cells, the co-channel interference from six neighboring cells using the same frequency must be summed. If the path loss exponent is $n$ (typically 2 for free-space, 3–4 for mobile land mobile satellite):

$$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{1}{6} \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^n \cdot \frac{1}{q}$$

where:
- $D$ = distance between co-channel cell centers
- $R$ = cell radius
- $q$ = traffic activity factor (0 < q ≤ 1)
- The factor 6 is the number of first-tier co-channel interferers

In dB:

$$[C/I] = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{(D/R)^n}{6q}\right)$$

The **reuse distance ratio** $D/R$ is related to the **cluster size N** by:

$$\frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$$

So:

$$[C/I] = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{(3N)^{n/2}}{6q}\right)$$

For N = 7, n = 4, q = 0.5:

$$[C/I] = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{(21)^2}{3}\right) = 10\log_{10}(147) \approx 21.7 \text{ dB}$$

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A Ku-band downlink operates at 12 GHz over a slant range of 38,000 km. Satellite EIRP = 52 dBW, Earth station G/T = 30 dB/K, bandwidth = 36 MHz. Calculate the downlink C/N.

**Step 1: Free-space path loss**

$$[L_p] = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(12000) + 20\log_{10}(38000)$$

$$= 32.45 + 81.58 + 91.60 = 205.63 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2: Apply C/N equation**

$$[C/N]_D = [EIRP]_S + [G/T]_{ES} - [L_p] - [k] - [B]$$

- $[B] = 10\log_{10}(36 \times 10^6) = 75.56$ dBHz
- $[k] = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz

$$[C/N]_D = 52 + 30 - 205.63 - (-228.6) - 75.56$$

$$= 52 + 30 - 205.63 + 228.6 - 75.56 = 29.41 \text{ dB}$$

This is typical for a DTH link — sufficient for QPSK at FEC 3/4 with margin.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Link Without Frequency Reuse

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Earth Station Transmitter] --> B[Uplink fU]
    B --> C[Satellite Transponder LNA + HPA]
    C --> D[Downlink fD]
    D --> E[Earth Station Receiver]
    E --> F[Demodulator and Baseband]

    subgraph NoiseSources1[Noise Contributions]
        N1[Uplink Thermal Noise N_U]
        N2[Downlink Thermal Noise N_D]
    end

    N1 --> C
    N2 --> E
    C --> G[(C/N)_T Computation]
    E --> G
    G --> H[BER and QoS Check]

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style E fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style C fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style G fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style H fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Link With Frequency Reuse (Dual Polarization)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Tx1[Transmit Side A - Vertical Pol]
        A1[ES-A TX EIRP] --> A2[Vertical Feed]
    end

    subgraph Tx2[Transmit Side B - Horizontal Pol]
        B1[ES-B TX EIRP] --> B2[Horizontal Feed]
    end

    A2 --> C[Satellite Antenna Common Aperture]
    B2 --> C

    C --> D1[Pol Filter V - 30 dB XPD]
    C --> D2[Pol Filter H - 30 dB XPD]

    D1 --> E1[Rx V - Wanted C plus Cross-Pol Leakage I]
    D2 --> E2[Rx H - Wanted C plus Cross-Pol Leakage I]

    E1 --> F1[(C/N plus C/I Computation)]
    E2 --> F2[(C/N plus C/I Computation)]

    F1 --> G1[BER Check on Channel A]
    F2 --> G2[BER Check on Channel B]

    style C fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style D1 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style D2 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style F1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style F2 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style G1 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style G2 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Reuse Factor vs C/I Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Capacity Demand Mbps] --> Q{Choose Reuse Strategy}
    Q -->|n equals 1| R1[Single Carrier No Reuse]
    Q -->|n equals 2| R2[Dual Polarization]
    Q -->|n equals 4| R3[2-Polar plus 2-Beam]
    Q -->|n equals 7| R4[Hexagonal 7-Cell Cluster]

    R1 --> S1[C/N only - C/I equals infinity]
    R2 --> S2[C/I equals XPD 30 dB]
    R3 --> S3[C/I equals min XPD, Beam Isolation 25 dB]
    R4 --> S4[C/I equals 21 dB for n equals 4]

    S1 --> T1[Link Margin M1]
    S2 --> T2[Link Margin M2 equals M1 minus 3 dB]
    S3 --> T3[Link Margin M3 equals M1 minus 5 dB]
    S4 --> T4[Link Margin M4 equals M1 minus 8 dB]

    T1 --> U[Select Strategy meeting BER and Availability]
    T2 --> U
    T3 --> U
    T4 --> U

    style P fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style Q fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style U fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

### 4.4 End-to-End Link Budget (Without Reuse) — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[ES Power Pt dBW] --> A1[EIRP ES]
    G1[ES Antenna Gain Gt dB] --> A1
    F1[ES Feed Loss Lf dB] --> A1

    A1 --> UPLINK
    LP1[Path Loss LpU dB] --> UPLINK
    T1[Satellite System Temp Tsys K] --> UPLINK
    B1[Bandwidth B Hz] --> UPLINK

    UPLINK --> CN_U[(C/N)_U dB]
    CN_U --> TRANS[Transponder Gain G_T dB]
    TRANS --> CN_D_Input

    P2[Satellite Power PtS dBW] --> A2[EIRP Sat]
    G2[Satellite Antenna Gain GtS dB] --> A2
    A2 --> CN_D_Input
    LP2[Path Loss LpD dB] --> CN_D_Input
    T2[ES System Temp T_E K] --> CN_D_Input
    B2[Bandwidth B Hz] --> CN_D_Input

    CN_D_Input --> CN_D[(C/N)_D dB]

    CN_U --> COMBINE
    CN_D --> COMBINE
    COMBINE --> TOTAL[(C/N)_T dB]

    style UPLINK fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style CN_D_Input fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style COMBINE fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style TOTAL fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**  
Define **frequency reuse** in a satellite communication system. Why is dual-polarization reuse preferred over spatial frequency reuse in geostationary DTH systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Frequency reuse is the technique of employing the same frequency band multiple times within the satellite's coverage area to increase the total system capacity without requiring additional spectrum allocation. It is implemented via:
1. **Spatial reuse:** using multiple narrow spot beams pointing to different geographical regions, each beam reuses the full frequency band.
2. **Polarization reuse:** using orthogonal polarizations (LHCP/RHCP or V/H) within the same beam, allowing two independent carriers on the same frequency.

Dual-polarization is preferred in DTH because geostationary satellites are bandwidth-limited (only ~500 MHz usable in Ku-band per orbital slot), and a single CONUS beam can serve all subscribers. Polarization reuse effectively **doubles** the transponder capacity without requiring new spectrum. Spatial reuse at GEO would need very large deployable antennas (≥10 m) to form multiple spot beams, increasing cost and complexity. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**  
What is **Cross-Polarization Discrimination (XPD)**? How does rain affect XPD, and what design margin is typically kept to compensate?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **XPD** is the ratio, in dB, of the wanted co-polar signal power to the unwanted cross-polar leaked signal power at the receiver after the polarization filter. It quantifies the antenna's ability to discriminate between the two orthogonal polarizations. **(1 mark)**
- **Rain effect:** Raindrops, being oblate spheroids, act as **differential phase shifters and attenuators** between the two polarization components, rotating the polarization ellipse and causing depolarization. Heavy rain (rain rate > 25 mm/hr) can degrade XPD from 30 dB (clear sky) to 15–20 dB. **(1 mark)**
- **Design margin:** A polarization isolation margin $\Delta_{pol}$ of **3–6 dB** is typically included in the link budget to ensure 99.9% link availability in the presence of rain-induced depolarization. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module is tested as a 14-mark question with internal choice. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each. Show all derivations and final numerical answers with units.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — System Design for Link Without Frequency Reuse

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the equation for the downlink carrier-to-noise ratio (C/N) of a satellite link. List the contributing factors and identify which term dominates for a typical Ku-band DTH link.

**Model Solution:**

The downlink C/N is derived from the Friis transmission equation.

The carrier power received at the earth station is:

$$C = P_{T,S} \cdot G_{T,S} \cdot G_{R,ES} \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2 \cdot L_{other}^{-1}$$

where $L_{other}$ includes atmospheric losses, pointing losses, and feed losses.

The noise power in the receiver bandwidth `B` with system noise temperature $T_{sys,ES}$ is:

$$N = k \cdot T_{sys,ES} \cdot B$$

Forming the C/N ratio in dB:

$$[C/N]_D = [P_{T,S}] + [G_{T,S}] + [G_{R,ES}] - [L_p] - [L_{other}] - [k] - [B] - [T_{sys,ES}]$$

Grouping transmit terms as EIRP = $[P_T] + [G_T] - [L_{feed}]$ and receive terms as G/T = $[G_R] - [T_{sys}]$:

$$\boxed{[C/N]_D = [EIRP]_S + [G/T]_{ES} - [L_p] - [L_{other}] - [k] - [B]}$$

**Contributing factors (with typical Ku-band DTH values):**
- EIRP of satellite: **+52 dBW** (large antenna + TWTA)
- G/T of earth station: **+22 dB/K** (60 cm dish, LNB NF 0.7 dB)
- Free-space path loss: **+205.6 dB** (12 GHz, 38,000 km) — **dominant loss**
- Boltzmann's constant: **-228.6 dBW/K/Hz**
- Bandwidth: **+75.6 dBHz** (36 MHz transponder)

The **free-space path loss is the dominant term** (~205 dB), accounting for nearly all the loss budget. Therefore, increasing satellite EIRP (via higher power TWTA or larger antenna) is the most effective way to improve C/N. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A C-band satellite link has the following parameters: Uplink: EIRP_ES = 80 dBW, G/T_S = -5 dB/K, f_U = 6 GHz, range = 40,000 km, B = 30 MHz. Downlink: EIRP_S = 40 dBW, G/T_ES = 20 dB/K, f_D = 4 GHz. Calculate the total C/N. Comment on the uplink vs. downlink balance.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Uplink C/N**

Path loss at 6 GHz over 40,000 km:

$$[L_{pU}] = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(6000) + 20\log_{10}(40000)$$

$$= 32.45 + 55.56 + 92.04 = 180.05 \text{ dB}$$

$$[B] = 10\log_{10}(30 \times 10^6) = 74.77 \text{ dBHz}$$

$$[C/N]_U = 80 + (-5) - 180.05 - (-228.6) - 74.77$$

$$= 80 - 5 - 180.05 + 228.6 - 74.77 = 48.78 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2: Downlink C/N**

Path loss at 4 GHz over 40,000 km:

$$[L_{pD}] = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(4000) + 20\log_{10}(40000)$$

$$= 32.45 + 52.04 + 92.04 = 176.53 \text{ dB}$$

$$[C/N]_D = 40 + 20 - 176.53 - (-228.6) - 74.77$$

$$= 40 + 20 - 176.53 + 228.6 - 74.77 = 37.30 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3: Total C/N (power sum rule)**

$$[C/N]_T = -10\log_{10}\left(10^{-48.78/10} + 10^{-37.30/10}\right)$$

$$= -10\log_{10}\left(10^{-4.878} + 10^{-3.730}\right)$$

$$= -10\log_{10}\left(1.324 \times 10^{-5} + 1.862 \times 10^{-4}\right)$$

$$= -10\log_{10}\left(1.994 \times 10^{-4}\right) = 37.00 \text{ dB}$$

**Comment:** The total C/N (37.00 dB) is essentially equal to the downlink C/N (37.30 dB), with only a 0.3 dB degradation. This is a **downlink-limited** system. The uplink C/N is 11.5 dB higher, so making the uplink "perfect" would yield only a 0.3 dB improvement — an inefficient design trade. To balance, the engineer should either increase satellite EIRP or decrease the satellite's noise figure, OR can intentionally de-rate the uplink (smaller ES antenna) to save cost. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — System Design for Link With Frequency Reuse

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the carrier-to-noise-plus-interference (C/(N+I)) expression for a dual-polarization frequency-reuse link. Define XPD and discuss the design factors that improve XPD in practice.

**Model Solution:**

In a dual-polarization reuse system, two independent carriers share the same frequency band using orthogonal polarizations (e.g., V and H, or LHCP and RHCP). At the receiver, the desired signal C is corrupted by both:
- **Thermal noise N** from the receiver electronics and sky noise
- **Co-channel interference I** due to imperfect polarization discrimination

The two are uncorrelated and add as powers:

$$\frac{N+I}{C} = \frac{N}{C} + \frac{I}{C}$$

Inverting:

$$\frac{C}{N+I} = \left[\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)^{-1}\right]^{-1}$$

In dB:

$$\boxed{[C/(N+I)] = -10\log_{10}\left(10^{-[C/N]/10} + 10^{-[C/I]/10}\right)}$$

**XPD (Cross-Polarization Discrimination)** is defined as the ratio (in dB) of the power received in the desired polarization to the power leaked into the orthogonal polarization:

$$[XPD] = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{co-pol}}{P_{cross-pol}}\right)$$

The C/I at the demodulator is approximately:

$$[C/I] = [XPD] + \Delta_{pol}$$

**Design factors that improve XPD:**
1. **Antenna design:** Use a high-quality feed (e.g., corrugated horn) with very low cross-pol in the main beam (≤ -30 dB).
2. **Polarizer network:** A well-designed orthomode transducer (OMT) and polarizer with cross-pol rejection > 35 dB.
3. **Depolarization mitigation:** Use circular polarization (rain depolarization affects LHCP/RHCP symmetrically) rather than linear.
4. **Adaptive compensation:** In modern systems, an adaptive cross-pol canceller (XPIC) can improve XPD by an additional 15–20 dB at the cost of complexity.
5. **Link margin:** A 4–6 dB depolarization margin is reserved in the link budget to combat rain-induced XPD degradation. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Ku-band downlink uses dual-polarization frequency reuse. The following parameters are given:
- Clear-sky C/N = 22 dB
- Clear-sky XPD = 30 dB
- Polarization margin $\Delta_{pol}$ = 3 dB
- Under heavy rain, XPD degrades to 18 dB

Calculate:
(i) Clear-sky C/(N+I)
(ii) C/(N+I) under heavy rain
(iii) The required satellite EIRP increase to restore clear-sky C/(N+I) under rain.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Clear-sky C/(N+I):**

$[C/I]_{clear} = 30 + 3 = 33$ dB

Since $[C/I] = 33$ dB is much greater than $[C/N] = 22$ dB, the system is **noise-limited** (not interference-limited) in clear sky.

$$[C/(N+I)]_{clear} = -10\log_{10}\left(10^{-22/10} + 10^{-33/10}\right)$$

$$= -10\log_{10}\left(10^{-2.2} + 10^{-3.3}\right) = -10\log_{10}\left(6.310 \times 10^{-3} + 5.013 \times 10^{-4}\right)$$

$$= -10\log_{10}(6.811 \times 10^{-3}) = 21.67 \text{ dB} \approx 21.7 \text{ dB}$$

This is essentially the C/N itself — interference has negligible effect in clear sky.

**(ii) C/(N+I) under heavy rain:**

$[C/I]_{rain} = 18 + 3 = 21$ dB

Now C/I (21 dB) is *less than* C/N (22 dB), so interference begins to dominate.

$$[C/(N+I)]_{rain} = -10\log_{10}\left(10^{-22/10} + 10^{-21/10}\right)$$

$$= -10\log_{10}\left(6.310 \times 10^{-3} + 7.943 \times 10^{-3}\right)$$

$$= -10\log_{10}(1.425 \times 10^{-2}) = 18.46 \text{ dB}$$

**(iii) Required EIRP increase:**

To restore clear-sky C/(N+I) of 21.7 dB under rain, we need the *new* C/N (with rain attenuation included) to give a C/(N+I) of 21.7 dB when combined with C/I of 21 dB.

Let the rain-attenuated C/N be $[C/N]_{rain-eff}$. We need:

$$21.7 = -10\log_{10}\left(10^{-[C/N]_{rain-eff}/10} + 10^{-21/10}\right)$$

$$10^{-2.17} = 10^{-[C/N]_{rain-eff}/10} + 7.943 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$6.761 \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-[C/N]_{rain-eff}/10} + 7.943 \times 10^{-3}$$

Wait — this gives a negative value for the first term, which is impossible. This means under heavy rain, the interference floor is so high that we **cannot** restore clear-sky C/(N+I) by simply increasing EIRP. We need to **mitigate the interference** (e.g., via XPIC) or accept the lower C/(N+I).

Re-evaluating: We must accept a worse C/(N+I) or apply interference cancellation. If we insist on increasing EIRP, the new required C/N must exceed C/I by enough that the sum is 21.7 dB. Solving numerically:

Setting $10^{-[C/N]_{new}/10} \ll 10^{-2.17}$:

Approximately, we need $[C/N]_{new}$ such that $-10\log_{10}(10^{-[C/N]_{new}/10} + 10^{-2.1}) = 21.7$

This requires $[C/N]_{new}$ around 22.5 dB, so the required EIRP increase is ~0.5 dB only after the *link* attenuation is included. Practically, the **rain attenuation** on the carrier (often 6–10 dB at Ku) is the dominant effect, and the satellite has limited EIRP margin. Hence uplink power control (UPC) and adaptive coding are used. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the -10 log form for combining C/N and C/I:** Many students write $[C/N]_T = [C/N]_U + [C/N]_D$ in dB. This is **WRONG** by ~3 dB. Always use the power-sum rule: $-10\log_{10}(10^{-a/10} + 10^{-b/10})$.
> 
> 2. **Mixing k and Boltzmann's constant units:** $[k] = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz requires `B` in **Hz**. If you use MHz (i.e., $[B] = 10\log_{10}(30 \text{ MHz}) = 74.77$ dB is correct *only if* you remember to use Hz-relative unit "dBHz"). A common error yields 60 dB answers.
> 
> 3. **Confusing G/T with gain alone:** G/T = $G - T_{sys}$ in dB, NOT $G + T$. A typical 30 dB/K figure of merit does **not** mean 30 dB gain with 1 K noise; it means the ratio.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring the polarization margin $\Delta_{pol}$:** Many students compute C/I = XPD directly. KTU examiners expect the additional 3–6 dB polarization margin in the link budget — failing to include it loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 5. **Reverse calculation errors for "increase EIRP to restore" type questions:** Always convert dB to linear, sum linearly, then convert back to dB. Don't try to "add dB values" at the input of a -10 log.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to mention that the cluster size N = 7 is the *minimum* for 21 dB C/I in cellular-like reuse:** This is a frequent KTU question — students may compute 21 dB but not state that N=7 is the canonical cluster size.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> 
> - [x] **Link Without Reuse:** C/N equation = EIRP + G/T − Lp − k − B (dB)
> - [x] **Cascading rule:** Total C/N uses **inverse-power sum**, not dB sum
> - [x] **Frequency Reuse Definition:** Same frequency band used multiple times via polarization/space/beam
> - [x] **Reuse Factor `n`:** Number of times frequency is reused (n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 7 are canonical)
> - [x] **XPD:** Co-pol to cross-pol ratio, typically 30 dB clear sky, 18 dB heavy rain
> - [x] **C/I equation:** $[C/I] = [XPD] + \Delta_{pol}$ (include 3–6 dB margin)
> - [x] **Combined metric:** $[C/(N+I)] = -10\log_{10}(10^{-C/N/10} + 10^{-C/I/10})$
> - [x] **Interference-limited regime:** When $[C/I] < [C/N]$ by 3+ dB
> - [x] **Noise-limited regime:** When $[C/N] < [C/I]$ by 3+ dB → reuse penalty is small
> - [x] **Path loss formula:** $L_p = 32.45 + 20\log f_{MHz} + 20\log d_{km}$ (dB)
> - [x] **Boltzmann's constant:** $-228.6$ dBW/K/Hz — always use Hz for bandwidth
> - [x] **Hexagonal cluster:** $D/R = \sqrt{3N}$; for N=7, C/I ≈ 21.7 dB (with n=4, q=0.5)
> - [x] **Dominant loss term:** Free-space path loss (~205 dB at Ku-band GEO) drives EIRP requirements
> - [x] **HTS reuse:** Modern Ka-band HTS use 4-color (quad-polarization) reuse for 200+ spot beams
> - [x] **XPIC:** Adaptive cross-pol interference canceller can boost XPD by 15–20 dB
> - [x] **Polarization types:** LHCP/RHCP more rain-robust than V/H linear (symmetric depolarization)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📡 Module 3 — Introduction to Radar Systems

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RADAR** stands for **R**adio **D**etection **A**nd **R**anging. It is an electromagnetic system for the detection and location of reflecting objects (targets) such as aircraft, ships, vehicles, people, terrain, and weather formations, by transmitting an electromagnetic signal and interpreting the echoes returned from those objects.

According to **Skolnik's standard definition** (the canonical reference adopted universally in radar engineering):

> *“Radar is an electromagnetic sensor used for the detection and location of objects. It operates by transmitting EM energy, intercepting energy reflected from targets, and extracting target information from the reflected signal.”*

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, Module 3 specifically introduces the foundational principles of radar — the **radar equation**, the **radar block diagram**, the **classification of radars**, and basic operational parameters that bridge the gap between communication theory (covered in earlier modules on satellite communication) and the sensing/detection aspect of RF engineering.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — “The Bat's Sonar”

Imagine you are standing in a dark, foggy valley and shout *"HELLO!"*. The sound wave travels outward, hits the opposite mountain, and the **echo** comes back to your ears. By measuring how long the echo took to return, you can estimate how far the mountain is.

A **bat** does this thousands of times per second using ultrasonic waves to hunt insects in pitch darkness.

**A radar does exactly the same — but with radio waves instead of sound.**

| Parameter | Human/Bat (Sonar) | Radar System |
|---|---|---|
| Wave type | Acoustic (sound) | Electromagnetic (radio/microwave) |
| Speed | ~340 m/s | ~3 × 10⁸ m/s |
| Range | Meters to kilometers | Kilometers to thousands of km |
| Medium | Air | Air / Space |
| Source | Voice / mouth | Antenna + Transmitter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Just as your brain can determine *direction* (by moving your head) and *distance* (by echo time), a radar can determine **range** (via time delay), **velocity** (via Doppler shift), **angle** (via antenna pointing), and even **size/shape** (via radar cross-section analysis).

## 1.2 Why Radar? — Motivation for the Module

Radar is preferred over optical/infrared sensors because:

1. **All-weather operation** — Microwaves penetrate rain, fog, smoke, dust, and clouds that would blind a camera.
2. **Long-range detection** — Can detect targets hundreds or thousands of km away.
3. **Day and night capability** — Independent of sunlight.
4. **Range measurement directly** — Time delay gives distance precisely.
5. **Velocity measurement via Doppler** — Built-in capability via frequency shift.

### 1.3 Operating Principle — The Three-Step Process

The fundamental operating sequence of any radar is universal:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — TRANSMIT:** A high-power RF pulse (or CW signal) is radiated by the antenna into free space.
>
> **Step 2 — ILLUMINATE & SCATTER:** The wavefront travels outward, hits a target, and induces **surface currents** on the target. The target reradiates energy back (scattering).
>
> **Step 3 — RECEIVE & PROCESS:** A small portion of the scattered energy returns, is captured by the same (or a separate) antenna, amplified, and processed to extract target **range, velocity, angle, and signature**.

## 1.4 Essential Radar Parameters — The Language of Radar

For Module 3 (Introduction), you must internalize the following terminology:

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier Frequency | $f_c$ | The RF frequency used (typically **1 GHz – 40 GHz**) |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = c/f_c$ where **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| Peak Transmitted Power | $P_t$ | Highest instantaneous power during the pulse |
| Pulse Width | $\tau$ | Duration of the transmitted pulse (seconds) |
| Pulse Repetition Frequency | $PRF = f_r$ | Pulses transmitted per second (Hz) |
| Pulse Repetition Interval | $PRI = 1/PRF$ | Time between successive pulses |
| Duty Cycle | $d$ | $d = \tau \cdot PRF = \tau / PRI$ |
| Antenna Gain | $G$ | Directional amplification factor of antenna |
| Effective Aperture | $A_e$ | $A_e = G\lambda^2 / 4\pi$ |
| Radar Cross Section | $\sigma$ | Apparent echoing area of the target (m²) |
| Range | $R$ | Distance from radar to target |
| Noise Bandwidth | $B$ | Receiver bandwidth (Hz) |
| Noise Figure | $F$ | Receiver degradation factor (≥ 1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024 Exams:** You **must** know the relationship $\lambda = c/f$ and $A_e = G\lambda^2/4\pi$ fluently — these appear in nearly every radar equation problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📊 Deep Theoretical Analysis — Radar Foundations

## 2.1 The Electromagnetic Nature of Radar

Radar operates in the **microwave region** of the EM spectrum because microwaves offer the ideal trade-off between:
- **Atmospheric propagation** (low attenuation in clear air at certain bands)
- **Resolution capability** (smaller $\lambda$ → finer angular resolution)
- **Target interaction** (physical features of typical targets are comparable to $\lambda$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Radar Frequency Bands (IEEE Designation):**
> - **L-band:** 1–2 GHz (long-range surveillance, e.g., ATC)
> - **S-band:** 2–4 GHz (medium-range surveillance, weather radar)
> - **C-band:** 4–8 GHz (tracking, weather)
> - **X-band:** 8–12 GHz (fire-control, marine radar)
> - **Ku-band:** 12–18 GHz (satellite, police radar)
> - **Ka-band:** 26–40 GHz (millimeter-wave radar, short-range)

## 2.2 Classification of Radar Systems

This is a **favourite KTU question topic** — expect a "classify radar systems with examples" question in Part A.

### A. Based on Waveform Type

| Type | Full Form | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **CW Radar** | Continuous Wave | Transmits continuously; cannot directly measure range; excellent Doppler (velocity) measurement |
| **Pulsed Radar** | Pulsed | Transmits short bursts; measures range via time delay; most common form |
| **FMCW Radar** | Frequency Modulated CW | Frequency varies linearly with time; measures both range and velocity |
| **MOPA Radar** | Master Oscillator Power Amplifier | Coherent pulsed radar with separate oscillator and amplifier |

### B. Based on Function

- **Primary Radar** — Detects targets by their own reflected signal (passive reflection).
- **Secondary Radar** — Target carries a transponder that replies (e.g., aircraft transponder in ATC).
- **Surveillance Radar** — Searches for new targets over a wide volume.
- **Tracking Radar** — Continuously follows a specific target.
- **Imaging Radar (SAR)** — Synthetic Aperture Radar for high-resolution images.
- **Weather Radar** — Detects precipitation, storms.
- **Missile Guidance Radar** — Homing seeker in missile.

### C. Based on Platform

- Ground-based, Airborne, Ship-borne, Space-based, Missile-borne

## 2.3 The Radar Range Equation — Heart of Radar Theory

The radar equation relates the **received echo power** at the radar receiver to the radar parameters, target properties, and range. It is the single most important equation in radar engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two-Way (Monostatic) Radar Equation:**
>
> $$\boxed{P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot R^4 \cdot L_s}}$$
>
> where $L_s$ represents system losses.

### 2.3.1 Interpretation of Each Term

- **$P_r$** — Received echo power (Watts). Extremely small — typically **femtowatts ($10^{-15}$ W)** to picowatts ($10^{-12}$ W).
- **$P_t$** — Peak transmitted power — can range from **kW to MW** for long-range radars.
- **$G$** — Antenna gain (appears **squared** because the same antenna transmits AND receives; gain is applied on both the outward and return paths).
- **$\lambda^2$** — Wavelength squared (inversely proportional — shorter wavelengths yield larger echoes).
- **$\sigma$** — Radar Cross Section (RCS) of the target in m².
- **$(4\pi)^3$** — Geometric spreading losses over three spherical paths (one Tx + one Rx + one back to target).
- **$R^4$** — Range to the **fourth power** — the famous "$R^4$ problem" of radar. Doubling range requires **16×** the transmitted power!

### 2.3.2 Maximum Range Equation

Setting $P_r = P_{r,\min}$ (the **minimum detectable signal**, MDS), the **maximum radar range** is:

$$R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot k \cdot T_0 \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{\min}} \right]^{\frac{1}{4}}$$

where the **noise floor** is given by:

$$P_n = k \cdot T_0 \cdot B \cdot F$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant You Must Memorize:**
> **Boltzmann's Constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> **Standard Temperature:** $T_0 = 290$ K

### 2.3.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ | Range from round-trip time $\Delta t$ |
| 2 | $\lambda = c / f_c$ | Wavelength from carrier frequency |
| 3 | $A_e = G\lambda^2 / 4\pi$ | Effective aperture |
| 4 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | Radar equation (no losses) |
| 5 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4 L_s}$ | With system losses |
| 6 | $R_{\max} = \left[\dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_0 B F (S/N)_{\min}}\right]^{1/4}$ | Maximum detection range |
| 7 | $P_n = k T_0 B F$ | Thermal noise power |
| 8 | $d = \tau \cdot PRF$ | Duty cycle |
| 9 | $\text{Avg Power} = P_t \cdot d$ | Average transmitted power |
| 10 | $f_d = 2 v_r f_c / c$ | Doppler shift for radial velocity $v_r$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Range Ambiguity Warning (KTU favourite):** Maximum unambiguous range is $R_{un} = c / (2 \cdot PRF)$. If actual target range exceeds $R_{un}$, the radar will display a false (aliased) range. This is **why pulsed radars cannot use arbitrarily high PRF** — there is a fundamental trade-off between unambiguous range and unambiguous Doppler.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Radar Equation

This derivation is **mandatory knowledge** for KTU Part B questions.

## 3.1 Derivation — From Transmitter to Receiver

### Step 1: Power Density at the Target

A directive antenna with transmit gain $G$ focuses the radiated power $P_t$ along its boresight direction. The **power density** (power per unit area) at a range $R$ in the far field is:

$$S_1 = \frac{P_t \cdot G}{4\pi R^2}$$

> **Explanation:** The isotropic radiator would spread $P_t$ uniformly over a sphere of area $4\pi R^2$. The directive antenna multiplies this density by the gain $G$, concentrating the energy.

### Step 2: Power Intercepted by the Target

The target intercepts a portion of this incident power according to its **radar cross section $\sigma$** (in m²). The total power scattered (intercepted and reradiated) is:

$$P_{\text{scattered}} = S_1 \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \cdot G \cdot \sigma}{4\pi R^2}$$

> **Explanation:** RCS $\sigma$ is **not** the geometric area. It is an *equivalent* area that, if it scattered isotropically, would return the same echo strength.

### Step 3: Power Density of the Echo at the Radar

The scattered power re-radiates **isotropically** (a simplification standard in radar theory — real targets scatter directionally, but $\sigma$ already captures this). At distance $R$ back at the radar, the echo power density is:

$$S_2 = \frac{P_{\text{scattered}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \cdot G \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}$$

### Step 4: Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna

The receiving antenna has an **effective aperture** $A_e$ that intercepts a fraction of this echo power density:

$$P_r = S_2 \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \cdot G \cdot \sigma \cdot A_e}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}$$

### Step 5: Substitute $A_e = G \lambda^2 / 4\pi$

Since the **same antenna** is typically used for transmit and receive (monostatic radar), its gain $G$ is the same. Substituting the antenna relation:

$$A_e = \frac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

we obtain:

$$\boxed{P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot R^4}}$$

This is the **basic monostatic radar equation**.

### Step 6: Maximum Range Form (with Noise Floor)

For the radar to *detect* the target, the received echo must exceed the minimum detectable signal:

$$P_r \geq P_{r,\min} = k \cdot T_0 \cdot B \cdot F \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\min}$$

Equating $P_r = P_{r,\min}$ and solving for $R$:

$$R^4 = \frac{P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot k \cdot T_0 \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{\min}}$$

Taking the fourth root gives the **maximum detectable range**:

$$R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot k \cdot T_0 \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{\min}} \right]^{1/4}$$

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A pulsed radar operates at **10 GHz**, transmits a peak power of **1 MW**, with antenna gain of **40 dB**. A target with RCS = **1 m²** is at a range of **100 km**. System losses are negligible. Calculate the received echo power.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Convert gain from dB to linear scale:

$$G_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(G) \Rightarrow G = 10^{40/10} = 10^4$$

**Step 2:** Compute wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_c} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the radar equation:

$$P_r = \frac{(10^6) \cdot (10^4)^2 \cdot (0.03)^2 \cdot 1}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot (10^5)^4}$$

**Step 4:** Numerator:

$$N = 10^6 \cdot 10^8 \cdot 9 \times 10^{-4} = 9 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 5:** Denominator — compute $(4\pi)^3$:

$$(4\pi)^3 = (12.566)^3 \approx 1984 \approx 1.984 \times 10^3$$

$$(10^5)^4 = 10^{20}$$

$$D = 1.984 \times 10^3 \cdot 10^{20} = 1.984 \times 10^{23}$$

**Step 6:** Final result:

$$P_r = \frac{9 \times 10^{10}}{1.984 \times 10^{23}} \approx 4.54 \times 10^{-12} \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_r \approx 4.54 \text{ pW (picowatts)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The radar transmitted **1 MW** but only received **~4.5 picowatts** — a dynamic range of nearly **240 dB**! This is why radar receivers must use highly sensitive low-noise amplifiers (LNAs).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting $R_{\max}$

| Parameter | Increase by factor | Effect on $R_{\max}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $P_t$ ↑ | 16× | $R_{\max}$ doubles |
| $G$ ↑ | 4× | $R_{\max}$ doubles |
| $\lambda$ ↑ | 16× | $R_{\max}$ doubles |
| $\sigma$ ↑ | 16× | $R_{\max}$ doubles |
| $(S/N)_{\min}$ ↓ | 16× (lower threshold) | $R_{\max}$ doubles |
| $T_0$, $B$, $F$ ↓ | 16× (less noise) | $R_{\max}$ doubles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** To double range you must improve **each of the factors in the numerator by 16×**, OR reduce the noise floor by 16×. This is the "$R^4$ law" and is the fundamental challenge that drives modern radar technology (e.g., phased arrays, pulse compression, AESA).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams — Radar System Architecture

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Pulsed Radar

Below is the **Mermaid block diagram** showing the standard KTU-recommended architecture of a monostatic pulsed radar system:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TIMING["Timing and Control Unit"]
        TCU1[Trigger Pulse Generator]
        TCU2[PRF and Range Clock]
    end

    subgraph TRANS["Transmitter Chain"]
        XMIT1[Modulator]
        XMIT2[Power Oscillator or MOPA]
        XMIT3[Duplexer or TR Switch]
    end

    subgraph ANTGRP["Antenna Subsystem"]
        ANT1[Parabolic or Phased Array Antenna]
        ANT2[Rotating Mount for Surveillance]
    end

    subgraph RECV["Receiver Chain"]
        RECV1[LNA Low Noise Amplifier]
        RECV2[Mixer and Local Oscillator]
        RECV3[IF Amplifier]
        RECV4[Matched Filter Detector]
    end

    subgraph DSP["Signal Processing and Display"]
        DSP1[Range Gate and Doppler Processor]
        DSP2[MTI or Pulse Doppler Filter Bank]
        DSP3[Threshold Detection CFAR]
        DSP4[PPI or RHI Display]
    end

    TCU1 --> XMIT1
    XMIT1 --> XMIT2
    XMIT2 --> XMIT3
    XMIT3 --> ANT1
    ANT1 --> ANT2
    ANT2 -.Echo Return.-> ANT1
    ANT1 --> RECV1
    RECV1 --> RECV2
    RECV2 --> RECV3
    RECV3 --> RECV4
    RECV4 --> DSP1
    DSP1 --> DSP2
    DSP2 --> DSP3
    DSP3 --> DSP4
    TCU2 --> DSP1
    XMIT3 -.Blocks Tx Leakage.-> RECV1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Duplexer (TR Switch) Function:** It allows the single antenna to be used for both transmission (very high power) and reception (extremely weak echo) by isolating the receiver during transmission — without it, the 1 MW transmit pulse would instantly destroy the sensitive LNA.

## 4.2 Radar Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RADAR["RADAR Systems Classification"]
    RADAR --> A["Based on Waveform"]
    RADAR --> B["Based on Function"]
    RADAR --> C["Based on Platform"]

    A --> A1["CW Radar Continuous Wave"]
    A --> A2["Pulsed Radar"]
    A --> A3["FMCW Radar"]
    A --> A4["Pulse Doppler Radar"]

    B --> B1["Primary Radar Passive Echo"]
    B --> B2["Secondary Radar Transponder"]
    B --> B3["Tracking Radar"]
    B --> B4["Surveillance or Search Radar"]
    B --> B5["Imaging SAR"]

    C --> C1["Ground Based"]
    C --> C2["Airborne AEW"]
    C --> C3["Ship Borne Naval"]
    C --> C4["Space Based"]
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow — Time-Domain View of Echo Reception

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant TX as Transmitter
    participant ANT as Antenna
    participant TGT as Target
    participant RX as Receiver

    TX->>ANT: Transmit Pulse at t = 0
    ANT->>TGT: Wavefront travels outward
    Note over TGT: Wave hits target at t = R/c
    TGT-->>ANT: Echo returns after delay
    Note over ANT: Echo arrives at t = 2R/c
    ANT->>RX: Capture weak echo
    RX->>RX: Amplify, mix, filter
    Note over RX: Measure round trip time = 2R/c
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The factor of 2 in $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$** accounts for the **round-trip** path: the wave travels distance $R$ outward *and* distance $R$ back. This is a classic exam pitfall — students often forget to divide by 2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank — Module 3 Introduction

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the term "Radar". List any four applications of radar systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Radar** (Radio Detection and Ranging) is an electromagnetic system that uses reflected radio waves to detect the presence, position, velocity, and nature of distant objects.

**Four Applications:**
1. **Air Traffic Control (ATC)** — Surveillance of aircraft around airports.
2. **Weather Monitoring** — Detection of storms, precipitation, wind shear.
3. **Military Surveillance** — Early warning, fire-control, missile guidance.
4. **Marine Navigation** — Ship collision avoidance, harbour surveillance.
5. **Remote Sensing** — Earth observation via Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR).
6. **Speed Enforcement** — Police Doppler radar for vehicle speed measurement.

*(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Distinguish between CW radar and pulsed radar.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | CW Radar | Pulsed Radar |
|---|---|---|
| Transmission | Continuous signal | Short pulses with silent listening period |
| Range measurement | Not directly possible | Easily measured via time delay |
| Velocity measurement | Excellent (Doppler) | Requires coherent processing |
| Peak power requirement | Low (continuous) | Very high (peak power) |
| Transmitter | Simple oscillator | Modulator + high-power oscillator |
| Range ambiguity | None (continuous) | Present beyond $R_{un} = c/(2 \cdot PRF)$ |
| Example | Police speed gun | Air surveillance radar |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the **monostatic radar equation** in terms of transmitted power $P_t$, antenna gain $G$, wavelength $\lambda$, range $R$, target RCS $\sigma$, and system loss $L_s$. Explain each term clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pulsed radar operates at **5 GHz** with peak transmitted power of **500 kW**, antenna gain of **30 dB**, and PRF of **1 kHz**. If the pulse width is **1 µs** and the target RCS is **10 m²**, calculate:
- (i) the duty cycle,
- (ii) the average transmitted power, and
- (iii) the maximum unambiguous range. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes. Marks distributed as:
- **1 mark** — Power density at target $S_1 = P_t G / (4\pi R^2)$
- **1 mark** — Power scattered by target $P_s = S_1 \sigma$
- **1 mark** — Echo power density at radar $S_2 = P_s / (4\pi R^2)$
- **1 mark** — Power captured $P_r = S_2 \cdot A_e$
- **1 mark** — Substitution of $A_e = G\lambda^2/(4\pi)$
- **1 mark** — Final equation with losses: $P_r = P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma / [(4\pi)^3 R^4 L_s]$
- **1 mark** — Explanation of $R^4$ dependence (two-way path) and significance

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Duty Cycle:**
$$d = \tau \cdot PRF = (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1 \times 10^3) = 10^{-3} = 0.1\%$$

**(ii) Average Power:**
$$P_{\text{avg}} = P_t \cdot d = 500 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-3} = 500 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Maximum Unambiguous Range:**
$$R_{un} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 1000} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ m} = 150 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{R_{un} = 150 \text{ km}, \quad P_{\text{avg}} = 500 \text{ W}, \quad d = 0.1\%}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a pulsed radar system**. Describe the role of the **duplexer** and the **matched filter**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain any **four applications of radar** with one example for each. Discuss why microwaves are preferred over optical frequencies for radar. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Block Diagram & Key Subsystems (7 marks):**
- **2 marks** — Block diagram with transmitter, duplexer, antenna, receiver, display (refer Section 4.1)
- **2 marks** — Duplexer role: protects LNA during transmission; isolates Tx/Rx paths; allows single antenna
- **2 marks** — Matched filter role: maximizes SNR at the receiver output; matched to pulse shape; optimal detection in AWGN
- **1 mark** — Conclusion on coherence

**(b) Applications & Microwave Justification (7 marks):**

**Four Applications:**
1. **Air Traffic Control (ATC) Radar** — e.g., ASR (Airport Surveillance Radar)
2. **Weather Radar** — e.g., Doppler weather radar at 3 GHz for precipitation
3. **Military Fire-Control Radar** — e.g., track-while-scan radar
4. **Marine Radar** — e.g., X-band navigation radar on ships
5. **Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)** — e.g., ISRO's RISAT satellite
6. **Automotive Radar** — e.g., 77 GHz adaptive cruise control

*(Any 4 with examples fetch 4 marks)*

**Why Microwaves over Optical (3 marks):**
1. **All-weather penetration** — Microwaves pass through fog, rain, dust; optical waves scatter heavily.
2. **Day/night independence** — Optical requires illumination (sun or laser); microwaves carry their own illumination.
3. **Long-range propagation** — Microwaves have lower atmospheric attenuation at certain bands (e.g., 35 GHz, 94 GHz "windows").
4. **Doppler sensitivity** — Microwave frequencies give measurable Doppler shifts for typical target velocities.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in range equation $R = c\Delta t / 2$ — the most common error. Marks deducted: **1–2 per question**.
>
> 2. **Confusing peak and average power** in the radar equation — $P_t$ must always be **peak** transmitted power. Many students incorrectly substitute average power. Marks deducted: **1–2**.
>
> 3. **Mixing up $G$ vs $G^2$** — In monostatic radar, gain is squared because the same antenna is used for transmit and receive. Marks deducted: **1**.
>
> 4. **Not converting dB to linear** — Always convert $G_{dB} = 10\log_{10}G$ to linear before substituting into the radar equation. Marks deducted: **2**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting system losses $L_s$** — If losses are mentioned in the problem, they must appear in the denominator of the final equation. Marks deducted: **1**.
>
> 6. **Unit errors** — Always express range in metres, frequency in Hz, power in Watts before computing.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 Introduction:**
>
> - ✅ **RADAR** = **R**adio **D**etection **A**nd **R**anging
> - ✅ Standard frequency bands: **L, S, C, X, Ku, Ka** (must know IEEE designations)
> - ✅ Range equation: $R = c\Delta t / 2$ (round-trip factor!)
> - ✅ Wavelength: $\lambda = c / f_c$
> - ✅ Effective aperture: $A_e = G\lambda^2 / (4\pi)$
> - ✅ **Monostatic Radar Equation:** $P_r = P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma / [(4\pi)^3 R^4 L_s]$
> - ✅ **Maximum Range:** $R_{\max}^4 \propto P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma$
> - ✅ Noise floor: $P_n = k T_0 B F$ with $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $T_0 = 290$ K
> - ✅ Duty cycle: $d = \tau \cdot PRF$; Average power: $P_{\text{avg}} = P_t \cdot d$
> - ✅ Maximum unambiguous range: $R_{un} = c / (2 \cdot PRF)$
> - ✅ Doppler shift: $f_d = 2 v_r f_c / c$
> - ✅ Duplexer protects the receiver during transmission
> - ✅ Matched filter maximizes output SNR
> - ✅ Key radar blocks: Modulator → Power Amp → Duplexer → Antenna → LNA → Mixer → IF → Detector → Display
> - ✅ Classification: CW vs Pulsed; Primary vs Secondary; Surveillance vs Tracking
> - ✅ The "$R^4$ problem" — the defining challenge of radar engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When a problem mentions "monostatic", immediately recall $G^2$. When it says "bistatic" (separate Tx and Rx antennas), use $G_t \cdot G_r$ instead of $G^2$. This distinction has appeared in KTU supplementary exams previously.
