# Module 3 — Range in Radar Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In radar engineering, **Range** is defined as the *slant distance* between the radar antenna and the target, measured along the line of sight (LOS) using the two-way (round-trip) propagation time of the electromagnetic wave. Because the transmitted pulse must travel to the target and the echo must return to the receiver, the range is computed as:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot t_R}{2}$$

where $c$ is the velocity of light (**3 × 10⁸ m/s**) and $t_R$ is the measured round-trip time delay between transmission and reception of the echo.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The term "Range" encompasses three distinct sub-concepts that are routinely asked in university examinations:
> 1. **Maximum Detectable Range ($R_{max}$)** — governed by the Radar Range Equation.
> 2. **Minimum Detectable Range ($R_{min}$)** — governed by pulse width and receiver recovery time.
> 3. **Maximum Unambiguous Range ($R_u$)** — governed by the Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are standing at the edge of a deep canyon and you shout "Hello!" toward the opposite wall. You hear the echo after a few seconds. If you know the speed of sound and the time delay of the echo, you can easily calculate the width of the canyon.

> A radar works **exactly the same way**, but instead of sound waves, it uses **high-frequency electromagnetic (EM) waves** traveling at the speed of light, and instead of your ears, it uses a highly sensitive receiver.

| Real-world Analogy | Radar Equivalent |
|--------------------|------------------|
| Shouting into a canyon | Transmitting an RF pulse |
| Sound wave | Electromagnetic wave |
| Time delay of echo | Round-trip time $t_R$ |
| Canyon wall | Radar target (aircraft, ship) |
| Loudness of echo | Received signal power $P_r$ |
| Loudness of your shout | Transmitted power $P_t$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of **2** in the range equation is critical — the EM wave must make a *round trip*. A student who forgets the factor of 2 will get **double the actual range**, a classic KTU valuation pitfall.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|-------|------|
| Speed of light | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸** | m/s |
| Boltzmann constant | $k$ | **1.38 × 10⁻²³** | J/K |
| Standard temperature | $T_0$ | **290** | K |
| Thermal noise floor | $kT_0$ | **4 × 10⁻²¹** | W/Hz |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Range Concepts — Structured Logic

**A) Maximum Range ($R_{max}$)** — The *furthest* distance at which a target can be detected.
- Determined by **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** at the receiver.
- Depends on transmitted power, antenna gain, wavelength, target cross-section, and noise.
- Computed using the **Radar Range Equation** (Barton, Skolnik form).

**B) Minimum Range ($R_{min}$)** — The *closest* distance at which a target can be detected.
- Determined by **pulse width ($\tau$)** and **receiver recovery time**.
- The receiver needs a "blanking period" to recover from the transmitted pulse before it can listen for echoes.
- $R_{min} = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$

**C) Maximum Unambiguous Range ($R_u$)** — The distance beyond which range ambiguity occurs.
- Determined by **Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI = $T_p = 1/f_r$)**.
- $R_u = \dfrac{c \cdot T_p}{2} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_r}$
- If a target is beyond $R_u$, its echo from one pulse may arrive *after* the next pulse is transmitted, causing the radar to misread the range.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Increasing the PRF improves $R_u$ but introduces *Doppler ambiguities*. Decreasing PRF reduces Doppler ambiguity but worsens range ambiguity. This is a **fundamental radar design dilemma** frequently tested in KTU exams.

---

### 2.2 The Radar Range Equation (RRE) — Logical Step-by-Step

The Radar Range Equation is the single most important equation in radar theory. It relates the received echo power to the physical and electrical parameters of the radar.

**Step 1 — Power Density at the Target**
When the radar radiates power $P_t$ through an antenna of gain $G$, the power density (power per unit area) at a slant range $R$ is the transmitted power spread over the surface of a sphere of radius $R$:

$$S_{target} = \frac{P_t \cdot G}{4 \pi R^2}$$

**Step 2 — Power Intercepted by the Target**
The target intercepts a portion of this power based on its **radar cross-section ($\sigma$)**, measured in square meters (m²). The intercepted power is:

$$P_{intercepted} = S_{target} \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \cdot G \cdot \sigma}{4 \pi R^2}$$

**Step 3 — Power Density of the Echo Back at the Radar**
The target re-radiates the intercepted power isotropically (assumed). The echo power density at the radar receiver, located at range $R$ from the target, is:

$$S_{echo} = \frac{P_{intercepted}}{4 \pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \cdot G \cdot \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}$$

**Step 4 — Echo Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna**
The receiving antenna has an effective aperture $A_e$, related to gain by $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^2}{4 \pi}$. The received power is:

$$P_r = S_{echo} \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \cdot G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R^4}$$

**Step 5 — Setting $P_r$ Equal to the Minimum Detectable Signal ($S_{min}$)**
For detection, the received echo must exceed the receiver's noise floor:

$$S_{min} = k T_s B_n \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{min}$$

where $T_s$ is the system noise temperature, $B_n$ is the noise bandwidth, and $(S/N)_{min}$ is the minimum signal-to-noise ratio for reliable detection (typically 13 dB for a Swerling I model).

**Step 6 — Solving for the Maximum Range**

$$\boxed{R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **fourth-root dependence** is the most striking feature of the RRE. To **double the range**, you must increase the transmitted power by a factor of **16**! This is why modern radars use high gain antennas and high frequencies rather than brute-force power.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot t_R}{2}$ | Range from time delay | m, s |
| 2 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | Echo power received | W |
| 3 | $R_{max} = \left[\dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$ | Maximum detectable range | m |
| 4 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$ | Minimum detectable range | m |
| 5 | $R_u = \dfrac{c}{2 f_r} = \dfrac{c \, T_p}{2}$ | Maximum unambiguous range | m |
| 6 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 B}$ | Range resolution (for FMCW/chirp) | m |
| 7 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$ | Range resolution (for pulse radar) | m |
| 8 | $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^2}{4 \pi}$ | Effective aperture of antenna | m² |
| 9 | $S_{min} = k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}$ | Minimum detectable signal | W |
| 10 | $NF = \dfrac{S/N_{in}}{S/N_{out}} = 1 + \dfrac{T_e}{T_0}$ | Noise figure relation | dimensionless |

> **Critical Reminder on LaTeX:** All absolute values and norms in the formulas above (such as the speed of light $c$) are written using $\vert$ or \mid where needed. **Never use raw `|` inside a markdown table cell** as it breaks the table parser.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Military Surveillance Radars:** The RRE determines how far an aircraft or missile can be tracked.
- **Air Traffic Control (ATC):** Primary surveillance radars rely on $R_{max}$ to manage aircraft separation.
- **Weather Rad radars:** $R_{max}$ defines the storm coverage radius; $R_u$ limits the maximum measurable precipitation range.
- **Automotive Radar (77 GHz):** Modern cars use compact radars whose $R_{max}$ and $R_{min}$ are critical for adaptive cruise control and collision avoidance.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Radar Range Equation

We start from first principles using energy conservation and spherical wave propagation.

**Step 1: Power radiated by an isotropic antenna**

For an isotropic radiator, the power density at distance $R$ is the total power distributed over the surface of a sphere of radius $R$:

$$S_{iso} = \frac{P_t}{4 \pi R^2}$$

**Step 2: Power density from a directive antenna of gain G**

A directive antenna concentrates the power in a preferred direction. The power density along the boresight becomes:

$$S_{dir} = \frac{P_t \, G}{4 \pi R^2}$$

> *Logic:* Gain $G$ represents the concentration factor compared to an isotropic radiator.

**Step 3: Power intercepted by the target**

The target, characterized by its radar cross-section $\sigma$ (units: m²), intercepts a portion of the incident wave equal to $\sigma$ times the incident power density:

$$P_{int} = S_{dir} \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4 \pi R^2}$$

**Step 4: Re-radiation of the echo**

The target re-radiates the intercepted power. Under the *isotropic re-radiation assumption*, the echo spreads over a sphere of radius $R$ centered on the target. The echo power density at the radar is:

$$S_{echo} = \frac{P_{int}}{4 \pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4 \pi R^2)(4 \pi R^2)} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}$$

**Step 5: Power captured by the receiving antenna**

The receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ captures the echo:

$$P_r = S_{echo} \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma \, A_e}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}$$

**Step 6: Substitute the antenna gain-aperture relation**

From antenna theory, the effective aperture and gain are related by:

$$A_e = \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4 \pi}$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4} \cdot \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4 \pi} \\[6pt]
P_r &= \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 \, R^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **monostatic radar equation** (transmitter and receiver share the same antenna, which is the most common radar configuration).

**Step 7: Setting $P_r = S_{min}$ for the threshold of detection**

For a target to be just detectable, the received echo power must equal the minimum detectable signal:

$$P_r = S_{min} = k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}$$

Equating:

$$\frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 \, R_{max}^4} = k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}$$

**Step 8: Solve algebraically for $R_{max}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max}^4 &= \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 \, k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}} \\[6pt]
R_{max} &= \left[\frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 \, k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Dimensional check:** 
- Numerator units: W · (dimensionless)² · m² · m² = W·m⁴
- Denominator units: (dimensionless) · J/K · K · Hz · (dimensionless) = J/Hz = W·s = W·s
- Since Hz = 1/s, denominator = W (after Hz inverse)
- Ratio: m⁴ → fourth root = m ✓

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Range

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect on $R_{max}$ | Doubling effect on $R_{max}$ |
|-----------|--------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Transmitted power | $P_t$ | Direct 4th root | × 1.19 (≈ 19% increase) |
| Antenna gain | $G$ | Square root | × 1.19 |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | Square root | × 1.19 |
| Target RCS | $\sigma$ | 4th root | × 1.19 |
| System noise temp | $T_s$ | Inverse 4th root | × 0.84 (≈ 16% decrease) |
| Required SNR | $(S/N)_{min}$ | Inverse 4th root | × 0.84 |
| Noise bandwidth | $B_n$ | Inverse 4th root | × 0.84 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "What is the effect of doubling the transmitted power on $R_{max}$?", the correct answer is $R_{max}$ increases by a factor of $2^{1/4} \approx 1.189$. Many students incorrectly answer "doubles", losing 1–2 marks.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of Radar Range Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trigger Generator] --> B[Pulse Modulator]
    B --> C[Transmitter Power Amplifier]
    C --> D[Duplexer]
    D --> E[Antenna System with Gain G]
    E --> F[Target at Slant Range R]
    F --> E
    E --> D
    D --> G[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    G --> H[Matched Filter Receiver]
    H --> I[Signal Processor and Detector]
    I --> J{Range Computation Block}
    K[PRF Clock 1 over Tp] --> A
    J --> L[Output Range c times tR divided by 2]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Radar Range Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Transmitted Power Pt] --> B[Antenna Gain G]
    B --> C[Free Space Path Loss 1 over 4 pi R squared]
    C --> D[Target RCS sigma]
    D --> E[Return Path Loss 1 over 4 pi R squared]
    E --> F[Receiver Aperture Ae]
    F --> G[Received Power Pr]
    H[Noise Floor k Ts Bn] --> I[Threshold Smin]
    I --> J{Comparison Pr vs Smin}
    G --> J
    J -->|Pr greater than Smin| K[Target Detected at Range R]
    J -->|Pr less than Smin| L[Target Not Detected]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Range Ambiguity Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Echo Received] --> B{Is tR less than PRI Tp}
    B -->|Yes| C[Range is Unambiguous]
    B -->|No| D[Range is Ambiguous]
    C --> E[Compute R = c tR divided by 2]
    D --> F[Apply Staggered PRF Technique]
    F --> G[Resolve True Range]
    D --> H[Or Use Multiple PRF Measurement]
    H --> G
```

### 4.4 Minimum Range vs Maximum Range Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Radar Operating Range] --> B[Minimum Range Rmin]
    A --> C[Maximum Range Rmax]
    A --> D[Unambiguous Range Ru]
    B --> B1[Determined by Pulse Width tau]
    B --> B2[Rmin equals c tau divided by 2]
    C --> C1[Determined by RRE fourth root law]
    C --> C2[Function of Pt G lambda sigma and noise]
    D --> D1[Determined by PRF]
    D --> D2[Ru equals c divided by 2 fr]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "Maximum Unambiguous Range" of a radar. Derive the expression for it in terms of PRF.*

**Model Answer:**
The maximum unambiguous range $R_u$ is the largest slant range for which a returned echo from a target can be unambiguously associated with the most recently transmitted pulse, with no confusion from echoes of earlier pulses.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } T_p &= \frac{1}{f_r} \text{ be the Pulse Repetition Interval} \\[6pt]
\text{Time available for one round trip} &= T_p \\[6pt]
\text{Since the wave travels distance } 2R_u \text{ in time } T_p: \\[6pt]
2 R_u &= c \, T_p = \frac{c}{f_r} \\[6pt]
R_u &= \frac{c}{2 f_r} = \frac{c \, T_p}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

If a target lies beyond $R_u$, its echo arrives after the next pulse has been transmitted, causing **range ambiguity** — the radar will display a falsely short range.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the significance of the Radar Range Equation (RRE). What is the dependence of $R_{max}$ on transmitted power?*

**Model Answer:**
The Radar Range Equation relates the maximum detectable range of a radar to its design and target parameters:

$$R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Significance:**
- It is the *fundamental design equation* for any radar system.
- It enables trade-off analysis between hardware parameters (power, gain, frequency) and detection performance.
- It quantifies the **fourth-root dependence** — radar range is *insensitive* to power increases.

**Dependence on $P_t$:** $R_{max} \propto P_t^{1/4}$. To double the range, transmitted power must increase by a factor of $2^4 = 16$.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Starting from first principles, derive the Radar Range Equation for a monostatic pulsed radar. Clearly state all assumptions used. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A pulsed surveillance radar operates at 10 GHz with peak transmitted power of 1 MW, antenna gain of 40 dB, pulse width of 2 µs, PRF of 1 kHz, and system noise temperature of 600 K. The antenna noise temperature is 150 K. The minimum SNR for detection is 13 dB. A target of RCS 1 m² is to be detected. Compute:
(i) The maximum detectable range $R_{max}$
(ii) The minimum detectable range $R_{min}$
(iii) The maximum unambiguous range $R_u$
(iv) State whether the target can be detected at 200 km. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Monostatic radar (transmitter and receiver share the same antenna).
2. Target re-radiates isotropically (no angular dependence).
3. Free-space propagation (no atmospheric attenuation or ground clutter).
4. The radar cross-section $\sigma$ is constant during the observation.
5. The receiver is noise-limited with thermal noise of equivalent noise bandwidth $B_n$.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Power density at the target from a directive antenna of gain $G$:

$$S_{target} = \frac{P_t G}{4 \pi R^2}$$

Step 2 — Power intercepted by the target of RCS $\sigma$:

$$P_{int} = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{4 \pi R^2}$$

Step 3 — Echo power density at the radar after isotropic re-radiation from the target:

$$S_{echo} = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}$$

Step 4 — Power captured by the receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_e$:

$$P_r = S_{echo} \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G \sigma A_e}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}$$

Step 5 — Apply the gain-aperture relation $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^2}{4 \pi}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 6 — For threshold detection, set $P_r = S_{min} = k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \left[\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the monostatic Radar Range Equation. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given data:**
- $f = 10$ GHz → $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8)/(10 \times 10^9) = 0.03$ m = 3 cm
- $P_t = 1$ MW = $10^6$ W
- $G = 40$ dB → $G_{linear} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4$
- $\tau = 2$ µs = $2 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- $f_r = 1$ kHz = $10^3$ Hz
- $T_s = 600$ K
- $T_{ant} = 150$ K (not needed unless using noise figure)
- $(S/N)_{min} = 13$ dB → $(S/N)_{min,linear} = 10^{1.3} \approx 19.95$
- $\sigma = 1$ m²
- $B_n \approx 1/\tau = 1/(2 \times 10^{-6}) = 5 \times 10^5$ Hz

**(i) Maximum detectable range $R_{max}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma \\
&= 10^6 \cdot (10^4)^2 \cdot (0.03)^2 \cdot 1 \\
&= 10^6 \cdot 10^8 \cdot 9 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1 \\
&= 9 \times 10^{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= (4\pi)^3 \cdot k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min} \\
&= (12.566)^3 \cdot (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \cdot 600 \cdot (5 \times 10^5) \cdot 19.95 \\
&\approx 1984 \cdot (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \cdot 600 \cdot (5 \times 10^5) \cdot 19.95 \\
&\approx 1984 \cdot 1.38 \cdot 600 \cdot 5 \times 10^{5-23+0.9988} \\
&\approx 1984 \cdot 1.38 \cdot 600 \cdot 5 \cdot 10^{-17.0} \\
&\approx 1.638 \times 10^{-13}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max}^4 &= \frac{9 \times 10^{10}}{1.638 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 5.49 \times 10^{23} \\[6pt]
R_{max} &= (5.49 \times 10^{23})^{1/4} \approx 8.59 \times 10^5 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
R_{max} &\approx 859 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Minimum detectable range $R_{min}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= \frac{c \, \tau}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \\
&= \frac{600}{2} = 300 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Maximum unambiguous range $R_u$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_u &= \frac{c}{2 f_r} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \cdot 10^3} \\
&= 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ m} = 150 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Detection of target at 200 km:**

- $R_{max} \approx 859$ km → target is within power-limited range.
- However, $R_u = 150$ km → 200 km > $R_u$ → **range ambiguous** and **unmeasurable unambiguously**.

**Conclusion:** The target **cannot be uniquely detected** at 200 km, even though it is within $R_{max}$, because the range exceeds the unambiguous range. The radar would display a *folded* (ambiguous) range.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of range ambiguity in a pulsed radar. With the help of a clear time-domain diagram description, derive the expression for maximum unambiguous range. Discuss two methods to resolve range ambiguities in practice. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A radar system has the following specifications: peak power 250 kW, antenna gain 30 dB, operating frequency 5.6 GHz, system noise temperature 500 K, noise bandwidth 500 kHz, minimum SNR 20 dB, and target RCS 2 m².
(i) Calculate the maximum range.
(ii) What must be the new transmitted power to triple the maximum range?
(iii) If the PRF is 800 Hz, determine the maximum unambiguous range and comment on whether $R_{max}$ can be uniquely observed. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Concept of Range Ambiguity:**
In a pulsed radar, pulses are transmitted periodically with a Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI) of $T_p = 1/f_r$. The radar assumes that every received echo corresponds to the *most recently transmitted* pulse. If a target lies at a range $R > R_u = cT_p/2$, the echo arrives *after* the next pulse is transmitted. The radar will mistakenly assign this echo to the second pulse, showing a falsely short range. This is called **range ambiguity**.

**Derivation:**

The maximum time interval between two successive transmitted pulses is $T_p = 1/f_r$. In this interval, the EM wave can travel a maximum one-way distance of $c T_p$ and round-trip distance of $2 R_u$. For unambiguous measurement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 R_u &= c T_p = \frac{c}{f_r} \\[6pt]
\boxed{R_u &= \frac{c}{2 f_r}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Methods to Resolve Range Ambiguity:**

1. **Staggered PRF (Pulse Repetition Frequency) Technique:**
   The PRF is varied between successive pulses (e.g., using two or more PRF values cyclically). True range can be reconstructed by solving the resulting system of ambiguous range equations. This is a common technique in modern surveillance radars.

2. **Multiple PRF Measurement:**
   Echoes are measured at two or more distinct PRF values. The *true* range is the one that is consistent across all measurements, whereas ambiguous ranges differ. The Chinese Remainder Theorem can be applied for two-PRF systems.

3. **PRI Switching with Phase Coding:**
   The transmitted pulse train is phase-coded (e.g., Barker codes) so that the receiver can correlate incoming echoes with the *correct* transmission time, even if echoes overlap from multiple PRIs.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- $P_t = 250$ kW = $2.5 \times 10^5$ W
- $G = 30$ dB → $G_{lin} = 10^3 = 1000$
- $f = 5.6$ GHz → $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8)/(5.6 \times 10^9) = 0.05357$ m
- $T_s = 500$ K
- $B_n = 500$ kHz = $5 \times 10^5$ Hz
- $(S/N)_{min} = 20$ dB → $(S/N)_{min,lin} = 100$
- $\sigma = 2$ m²
- $f_r = 800$ Hz (for part iii)

**(i) Maximum range:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \left[\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4} \\[6pt]
R_{max} &= \left[\frac{(2.5 \times 10^5)(10^6)(0.05357)^2 (2)}{(1984)(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(500)(5 \times 10^5)(100)}\right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute numerator:
$(2.5 \times 10^5)(10^6) = 2.5 \times 10^{11}$
$(0.05357)^2 = 2.870 \times 10^{-3}$
Numerator = $(2.5 \times 10^{11})(2.870 \times 10^{-3})(2) = 1.435 \times 10^9$

Compute denominator:
$(1984)(1.38 \times 10^{-23}) = 2.738 \times 10^{-20}$
$(2.738 \times 10^{-20})(500)(5 \times 10^5)(100) = 2.738 \times 10^{-20} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{10} = 6.845 \times 10^{-10}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max}^4 &= \frac{1.435 \times 10^9}{6.845 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.097 \times 10^{18} \\[6pt]
R_{max} &= (2.097 \times 10^{18})^{1/4} = (2097 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} \\[6pt]
R_{max} &\approx 6.78 \times 10^4 \text{ m} \approx 67.8 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Power required to triple the range:**

Since $R_{max} \propto P_t^{1/4}$, for $R_{max}' = 3 R_{max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3 &= \left(\frac{P_t'}{P_t}\right)^{1/4} \\[6pt]
\frac{P_t'}{P_t} &= 3^4 = 81 \\[6pt]
P_t' &= 81 \times 250 \text{ kW} = 20{,}250 \text{ kW} = 20.25 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Maximum unambiguous range and check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_u &= \frac{c}{2 f_r} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \cdot 800} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1600} \\[6pt]
R_u &= 1.875 \times 10^5 \text{ m} = 187.5 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Since $R_{max} \approx 67.8$ km $< R_u = 187.5$ km, every detectable target lies within the unambiguous range. The radar can therefore uniquely observe the maximum range without ambiguity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $R = ct/2$ — students who write $R = ct$ will get **double the correct range**, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Unit conversion mistakes** — GHz to Hz, kW to W, dB to linear. KTU examiners specifically test unit conversion.
> 3. **Confusing $(4\pi)^3$ with $(4\pi)^2$** — the cube comes from the two-way path and the antenna aperture. Memorize: $\text{(4π)}^3 \approx 1984$.
> 4. **Mixing up $T_s$ and $T_{ant}$** — for noise-limited systems use the **system** noise temperature $T_s = T_{ant} + T_{receiver}$.
> 5. **Not checking $R_u$ vs $R_{max}$** — many KTU questions deliberately give values where $R_{max} > R_u$, requiring the student to identify the unambiguous range as the *actual* limit.
> 6. **Ignoring the matched-filter approximation** $B_n \approx 1/\tau$ — for a pulse radar the matched filter bandwidth equals the inverse of the pulse width; failing to apply this loses 1 mark in numerical problems.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Range** in a monostatic radar is computed as $R = c t_R / 2$, where the factor **2** is non-negotiable.
- The **Radar Range Equation** is $R_{max} = \left[\dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$.
- The **fourth-root dependence** on $P_t$ and $\sigma$ means that to **double the range**, you must increase transmitted power (or RCS) by a factor of **16**.
- **Minimum range** is set by pulse width: $R_{min} = c\tau/2$.
- **Maximum unambiguous range** is set by PRF: $R_u = c/(2f_r)$.
- For pulse radar, **range resolution** is $\Delta R = c\tau/2$; for FMCW/chirp, it is $\Delta R = c/(2B)$.
- The **antenna gain-aperture relation** $A_e = G\lambda^2/(4\pi)$ is essential in RRE derivation.
- The **constant $(4\pi)^3 \approx 1984$** is a frequently tested numerical value in KTU numericals.
- To resolve **range ambiguities**, use **staggered PRF**, **multiple PRF**, or **PRI phase coding**.
- Always verify whether the target is within **both** $R_{max}$ and $R_u$ before claiming successful detection.
- The **system noise temperature** $T_s$ includes both antenna and receiver contributions: $T_s = T_{ant} + T_{rec}$.
- For a **matched filter receiver**, the noise bandwidth equals the inverse of the pulse width: $B_n \approx 1/\tau$.
- The **free-space path loss** in radar involves a $(4\pi R^2)^2 = (4\pi)^2 R^4$ two-way factor, not the usual one-way $(4\pi R^2)$.
- A **monostatic radar** uses a single antenna for transmit and receive (via a duplexer); a **bistatic radar** uses separate antennas and the equation is modified accordingly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radar Waveforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radar Waveforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radar Waveform:** A *radar waveform* is the time-varying electromagnetic signal — characterized by its amplitude, frequency, phase, and modulation structure — that is transmitted by a radar system into the propagation medium and subsequently processed upon reception to extract target information such as **range (R)**, **radial velocity (v_r)**, and **radar cross-section (RCS / σ)**.

The waveform is the fundamental design choice that determines a radar's ability to:
- Resolve targets in **range** (delay domain).
- Resolve targets in **velocity** (Doppler domain).
- Achieve a specific **energy budget** for a given peak power.
- Operate unambiguously in the presence of clutter and noise.

### 1.2 The Four Canonical Radar Waveform Families

| Family | Transmitter State | Measurable Quantity | Power Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CW (Continuous Wave)** | Always ON, no modulation | Doppler (velocity) only | Requires high average power |
| **Pulsed CW (Unmodulated Pulse)** | Pulses of constant frequency | Range (via delay) | Low average power |
| **LFM Chirp (Linear FM Pulse)** | Linear frequency sweep inside pulse | Range (after matched filter) | Pulse compression |
| **Phase-Coded Pulse (Barker / PRN)** | Binary phase modulation inside pulse | Range (after matched filter) | Pulse compression |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a radar waveform as your "voice" when shouting into a foggy canyon.**
> - A **short, sharp shout (narrow pulse)** lets you hear an echo from a distant cliff — you know **how far** it is (range), but you cannot tell if the cliff is moving toward you.
> - A **continuous humming tone (CW)** tells you if the cliff is moving (Doppler shift in your hum), but you cannot tell which cliff replied (no range resolution).
> - A **sweeping whistle (chirp)** — from low pitch to high pitch — acts like a fingerprint; even a faint echo is recognizable because only the *true* echo matches the exact sweep pattern. This is **pulse compression**.
> - A **shouted binary code "10111001" (Barker code)** gives every echo a unique signature that the receiver can correlate against the original.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light (vacuum):** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Speed of light (standard KTU exam value):** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Round-trip time for range R:** $t_R = \dfrac{2R}{c}$
- **Carrier frequencies of interest:** L-band (~1–2 GHz), S-band (~2–4 GHz), C-band (~4–8 GHz), X-band (~8–12 GHz).
- **Standard received SNR threshold for detection:** $\text{SNR}_{\min} \geq 13$ dB for $P_d = 0.9$, $P_{fa} = 10^{-6}$ (Swerling I / non-fluctuating case).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pulsed Radar Waveform — Mathematical Description

A single rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ (seconds) and carrier frequency $f_c$ (Hz) is mathematically written as:

$$
s(t) = A \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - \tau/2}{\tau}\right) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Why this matters:** The radar receiver must extract $\tau$ (range resolution), $T_{PRI}$ (unambiguous range), and the phase/frequency evolution (Doppler). Every waveform design choice alters one of these three.

### 2.2 The Three Governing Parameters of Any Pulse Train

1. **Pulse Width $\tau$ (s):** Sets the *range resolution* of the radar.
2. **Pulse Repetition Interval $T_{PRI}$ (s):** Sets the *unambiguous range* and *unambiguous Doppler*.
3. **Carrier Frequency $f_c$ (Hz):** Sets the operating band and Doppler sensitivity.

### 2.3 Why Pulsed Beats Pure CW? — The "Duty Cycle" Trick

If the transmitter is ON only a fraction $d$ (the duty cycle) of the time, the **average transmitted power** is:

$$
P_{avg} = d \cdot P_{peak} = \frac{\tau}{T_{PRI}} \cdot P_{peak}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the foundational trade-off in radar: high peak power + low duty cycle achieves a required energy-per-pulse $E_p = P_{peak} \cdot \tau$ without burning the tube. The **radar range equation** uses $P_{avg}$, so high duty cycle = long detection range.

### 2.4 The Matched Filter — Optimal Linear Processor

> [!NOTE]
> A **matched filter** is the linear time-invariant filter whose impulse response is a time-reversed, conjugated copy of the transmitted signal. It **maximizes the output SNR** at the sampling instant in white Gaussian noise.

Its frequency response is:

$$
H(f) = K \cdot S^*(f) \cdot e^{-j2\pi f t_0}
$$

Its output peak SNR is:

$$
\text{SNR}_{out} = \frac{2E}{\mathcal{N}_0}
$$

where $E$ is the signal energy and $\mathcal{N}_0$ is the noise PSD.

### 2.5 Pulse Compression — Stretching Resolution Without Stretching Pulse

To get **fine range resolution** (small $\delta_R$) one would naively need a *very narrow* pulse. But a narrow pulse has *low energy* (since $E_p = P_{peak}\tau$). **Pulse compression** decouples resolution from energy:

- Transmit a **long pulse** (high energy).
- Modulate it (LFM or phase code) so its **bandwidth $B$ is large**.
- The matched-filter output has a *compressed* width of $\approx 1/B$, which is much shorter than $\tau$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time-Bandwidth Product (TBP):** $TBP = \tau \cdot B$. The **compression ratio** = TBP. A chirp of $\tau = 20\,\mu$s and $B = 10$ MHz has TBP = 200:1 — the matched filter compresses a 20 µs pulse into a 50 ns effective pulse.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Range from round-trip delay | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot t_R}{2}$ | meters |
| 2 | Pulse energy | $E_p = P_{peak} \cdot \tau$ | joules |
| 3 | Average power | $P_{avg} = d \cdot P_{peak} = \dfrac{\tau}{T_{PRI}} P_{peak}$ | watts |
| 4 | Duty cycle | $d = \dfrac{\tau}{T_{PRI}}$ | dimensionless, $0 < d \le 1$ |
| 5 | Unambiguous range | $R_u = \dfrac{c \cdot T_{PRI}}{2}$ | meters |
| 6 | Pulse Repetition Frequency | $f_{PRF} = \dfrac{1}{T_{PRI}}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Unambiguous Doppler | $\vert f_D \vert \le \dfrac{f_{PRF}}{2}$ | Hz (Nyquist) |
| 8 | Doppler frequency for radial velocity $v_r$ | $f_D = \dfrac{2 v_r f_c}{c}$ | Hz |
| 9 | Range resolution (uncompressed pulse) | $\delta_R = \dfrac{c \tau}{2}$ | meters |
| 10 | Range resolution (compressed pulse) | $\delta_R = \dfrac{c}{2B}$ | meters |
| 11 | Time-Bandwidth Product | $TBP = \tau \cdot B$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Compression ratio | $CR = TBP$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Chirp frequency-vs-time slope (LFM) | $\mu = \dfrac{B}{\tau}$ | rad/s² or Hz/s |
| 14 | Instantaneous chirp frequency | $f_i(t) = f_c \pm \mu t$ | Hz |
| 15 | Matched-filter output peak SNR | $\text{SNR}_{out} = \dfrac{2 E_p}{\mathcal{N}_0}$ | dimensionless (linear) |
| 16 | Noise bandwidth of matched filter | $B_n = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ | Hz (for rectangular pulse) |
| 17 | Maximum unambiguous velocity | $v_{r,max} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot f_{PRF}}{4}$ | m/s |
| 18 | Blind speed (Doppler ambiguity) | $v_{blind} = n \cdot \dfrac{\lambda \cdot f_{PRF}}{2}$, $n = 1,2,\dots$ | m/s |
| 19 | Range-Doppler coupling in LFM | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \cdot f_D}{2 \mu}$ | meters (apparent range error) |
| 20 | Barker code length / SNR gain | Processing gain = $10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB | $N$ = code length |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Waveform | Real Radar That Uses It |
|---|---|
| **CW** | Police radar guns (speed only), continuous-wave illuminators |
| **Pulsed (no modulation)** | Classical search radars, primary surveillance radars (PSR) |
| **LFM Chirp** | Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), weather radar, long-range surveillance |
| **Phase-coded (Barker-13, PRN)** | GPS ranging codes, modern AESA radars (AN/APG-81) |
| **Costas frequency hopping** | High-range-Doppler ambiguity rejection |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Range Resolution from Pulse Width

**Goal:** Show that $\delta_R = \dfrac{c \tau}{2}$ for an uncompressed pulse.

**Step 1 — Define range resolution.** Two point targets separated by $\delta R$ are just resolved if their echo pulses are separated in time by at least the pulse width $\tau$:

$$
\Delta t \ge \tau
$$

**Step 2 — Relate time separation to range separation.** Since the wave travels out *and back*:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2 \cdot \delta R}{c}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two and solve for $\delta R$:**

$$
\frac{2 \delta R}{c} \ge \tau \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \delta R \ge \frac{c \tau}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — State the resolution:**

$$
\boxed{\delta_R = \frac{c \tau}{2}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 1 µs pulse gives $\delta_R = 150$ m. A 1 ns pulse gives $\delta_R = 0.15$ m. This is why high-resolution radars use *short* pulses *or* pulse compression.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Unambiguous Range from PRI

**Goal:** Derive $R_u = \dfrac{c \cdot T_{PRI}}{2}$.

**Step 1 — Maximum unambiguous time between pulses.** If the first echo from a target at range $R$ arrives *before* the next pulse is transmitted, the receiver can associate it correctly. The condition is:

$$
t_{echo} \le T_{PRI}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the round-trip time:**

$$
\frac{2 R}{c} \le T_{PRI}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R$:**

$$
\boxed{R_u = \frac{c \cdot T_{PRI}}{2} = \frac{c}{2 f_{PRF}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU-favorite):** For $f_{PRF} = 1$ kHz:

$$
R_u = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^3} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ m} = 150 \text{ km}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Blind Speed from Doppler Ambiguity

**Goal:** Show that blind speeds are integer multiples of $\dfrac{\lambda \, f_{PRF}}{2}$.

**Step 1 — Doppler from radial velocity:**

$$
f_D = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 2 — Nyquist criterion for the sampler that measures $f_D$:**

$$
\vert f_D \vert \le \frac{f_{PRF}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the *first* maximum unambiguous velocity (which is the *first* blind speed, since at this velocity the echo Doppler is aliased to zero):**

$$
\frac{2 v_{blind,1}}{\lambda} = \frac{f_{PRF}}{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad v_{blind,1} = \frac{\lambda \, f_{PRF}}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to all integer multiples of $f_{PRF}/2$:**

$$
\boxed{v_{blind,n} = n \cdot \frac{\lambda \, f_{PRF}}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots}
$$

**Numerical sanity check:** $\lambda = 0.1$ m (S-band), $f_{PRF} = 1$ kHz:

$$
v_{blind,1} = \frac{0.1 \times 1000}{2} = 50 \text{ m/s} = 180 \text{ km/h}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Matched-Filter Output SNR (Sklar's Classic Result)

**Goal:** Show that the matched filter yields $\text{SNR}_{out} = \dfrac{2E}{\mathcal{N}_0}$ at the sampling instant $t = t_0$.

**Step 1 — Filter output at $t = t_0$:**

$$
y(t_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) X(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0} \, df
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute matched-filter response $H(f) = K \cdot S^*(f) e^{-j2\pi f t_0}$:**

$$
y(t_0) = K \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert S(f) \vert^2 X(f) \, df
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality to the integral split into signal and noise contributions.**

*Signal part* at $t_0$ (using Parseval):

$$
y_s(t_0) = K \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert S(f) \vert^2 \, df = K E
$$

*Noise variance*:

$$
\sigma_n^2 = K^2 \cdot \frac{\mathcal{N}_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert S(f) \vert^2 \, df = K^2 \cdot \frac{\mathcal{N}_0 E}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the SNR ratio:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{out} = \frac{y_s^2(t_0)}{\sigma_n^2} = \frac{K^2 E^2}{K^2 \cdot \frac{\mathcal{N}_0 E}{2}} = \boxed{\frac{2E}{\mathcal{N}_0}}
$$

**Insight:** The matched-filter output SNR depends *only* on the signal energy $E$ and the noise spectral density $\mathcal{N}_0$. It is **independent of the waveform shape** — but the *resolution* and *ambiguity* properties do depend on the shape. This is why pulse compression is so powerful: you can boost $E$ (by lengthening $\tau$) without losing resolution.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Linear FM (LFM / Chirp) Instantaneous Frequency

**Goal:** Show that a chirp has instantaneous frequency that varies linearly with time over a bandwidth $B$ in pulse width $\tau$.

**Step 1 — Chirp phase function (quadratic phase):**

$$
\psi(t) = 2\pi \left( f_c t + \frac{\mu t^2}{2} \right), \quad -\frac{\tau}{2} \le t \le \frac{\tau}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous frequency is the time derivative of phase divided by $2\pi$:**

$$
f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \frac{d\psi}{dt} = f_c + \mu t
$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions: at $t = -\tau/2$, $f_i = f_c - B/2$; at $t = +\tau/2$, $f_i = f_c + B/2$. Solve for $\mu$:**

$$
\mu = \frac{B}{\tau}
$$

**Step 4 — Final chirp signal:**

$$
\boxed{s(t) = A \cos\!\left[ 2\pi \left( f_c t + \frac{B t^2}{2 \tau} \right) \right], \quad -\frac{\tau}{2} \le t \le \frac{\tau}{2}}
$$

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6: Range-Doppler Coupling in LFM

**Goal:** Quantify the apparent range error introduced when an LFM target has a non-zero Doppler.

**Step 1 — Echo from a target at range $R$ with Doppler $f_D$:**

$$
r(t) = a \cdot s(t - t_R) \cdot e^{j 2\pi f_D (t - t_R)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $s(t)$ from §3.5 and pass through the matched filter.** The matched filter has a quadratic phase $H(f)$ that conjugates $S(f)$.

**Step 3 — The output peak, for an echo with Doppler $f_D$, occurs at a *shifted* time delay:**

$$
\Delta t = -\frac{f_D}{\mu}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert time shift to apparent range shift:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta R = \frac{c \, \Delta t}{2} = -\frac{c \, f_D}{2 \mu} = -\frac{c \, f_D \, \tau}{2 B}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 1 kHz Doppler in a $\tau = 10$ µs, $B = 10$ MHz chirp gives:

$$
\Delta R = -\frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^7} = -150 \text{ m}
$$

This is the **range-Doppler coupling** that is the curse of LFM and must be corrected in SAR processing.

---

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A pulsed radar has $\tau = 2\,\mu$s, $f_{PRF} = 2$ kHz, $f_c = 10$ GHz, $P_{peak} = 1$ MW. Find (a) duty cycle, (b) average power, (c) unambiguous range, (d) first blind speed, (e) range resolution (no compression), (f) range resolution if the pulse is LFM with $B = 20$ MHz.

**Solution:**

**(a) Duty cycle:**

$$
d = \tau \cdot f_{PRF} = (2 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^3) = 4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.4\%
$$

**(b) Average power:**

$$
P_{avg} = d \cdot P_{peak} = (4 \times 10^{-3})(10^6) = 4 \text{ kW}
$$

**(c) Unambiguous range:**

$$
R_u = \frac{c}{2 f_{PRF}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2 \times 10^3} = 7.5 \times 10^4 \text{ m} = 75 \text{ km}
$$

**(d) Wavelength and first blind speed:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f_c} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10^{10}} = 0.03 \text{ m}
$$

$$
v_{blind,1} = \frac{\lambda \cdot f_{PRF}}{2} = \frac{0.03 \times 2000}{2} = 30 \text{ m/s} = 108 \text{ km/h}
$$

**(e) Uncompressed range resolution:**

$$
\delta_R = \frac{c \tau}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 300 \text{ m}
$$

**(f) Compressed range resolution:**

$$
\delta_R^{comp} = \frac{c}{2B} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 20 \times 10^6} = 7.5 \text{ m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The LFM upgrade improves range resolution by a factor of **40** (300 m → 7.5 m) while keeping the same pulse energy.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Pulsed Radar Transmitter/Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Master Timing Clock] --> A2[PRF Generator]
    A2 --> A3[Modulator Switch]
    A3 --> A4[High Voltage Power Supply]
    A4 --> A5[Power Amplifier / Magnetron]
    A5 --> A6[Duplexer]
    A6 --> A7[Antenna]
    A7 --> A8[Target in Space]
    A8 --> A7
    A7 --> A6
    A6 --> A9[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    A9 --> A10[Matched Filter]
    A10 --> A11[Envelope Detector]
    A11 --> A12[Threshold Detector]
    A12 --> A13[Range Bin Counter]
    A13 --> A14[Display / Tracker]
    A1 -.sync.-> A10
    A1 -.sync.-> A13
```

**Reading the diagram:** The top chain (master clock → PRF generator → modulator → power amp) *generates* the waveform. The bottom chain (LNA → matched filter → envelope detector → threshold) *processes* the echo. Dashed lines show timing/synchronization, which is critical: the matched filter is *time-aligned* to the transmitted pulse via the master clock.

---

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Pulse Compression Receiver (LFM Chirp)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Received Echo r of t] --> B2[Quadrature Downconversion to Baseband]
    B2 --> B3[Matched Filter H of f = S conjugate of f]
    B3 --> B4[Compressed Pulse of width 1 over B]
    B4 --> B5[Range Bin Sampling]
    B5 --> B6[CFAR Threshold Detection]
    B6 --> B7{Detection above threshold?}
    B7 -- Yes --> B8[Declare Target at Range R = c tau over 2]
    B7 -- No --> B9[Discard Cell]
    B6 --> B10[Side-Lobe Windowing Hanning or Hamming]
    B10 --> B11[Reduced Side-Lobe Output]
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing a Radar Waveform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1{What is the primary mission?}
    C1 -- Velocity measurement only --> C2[CW Doppler Radar]
    C1 -- Range and velocity --> C3{Doppler spread of targets?}
    C3 -- Low spread --> C4[Single PRF Pulsed Radar]
    C3 -- High spread --> C5[Staggered PRF or MTI]
    C1 -- Long range with fine range resolution --> C6{Energy budget?}
    C6 -- Limited peak power --> C7[LFM Chirp Pulse Compression]
    C6 -- Low sidelobe required --> C8[Phase-Coded Barker or PRN]
    C1 -- Doppler-only with range rejection --> C9[FM CW FMCW for short range]
```

---

### 4.4 Conceptual Schematic — LFM Chirp Time-Frequency Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Time t = minus tau over 2] --> D2[Frequency f = fc minus B over 2]
    D2 -- Linear sweep up --> D3[Time t = plus tau over 2]
    D3 --> D4[Frequency f = fc plus B over 2]
    D4 --> D5[Matched filter collapses to short pulse of width 1 over B]
```

**Reading:** A linear frequency sweep over the interval $\left[ -\tau/2, +\tau/2 \right]$ from $f_c - B/2$ to $f_c + B/2$ is "unfolded" by the matched filter into a narrow pulse of width $\approx 1/B$.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define the term "matched filter" in the context of radar receivers. State the peak SNR obtained at its output.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **matched filter** is a linear time-invariant filter whose impulse response is the time-reversed and conjugated replica of the known transmitted signal $s(t)$, i.e., $h(t) = K \cdot s^*(t_0 - t)$. It is the **optimal linear filter** for detecting a known signal in additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN), maximizing the output **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** at the sampling instant. The peak SNR at the filter output is:

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR}_{out} = \frac{2E_p}{\mathcal{N}_0}}
$$

where $E_p$ is the received signal energy and $\mathcal{N}_0$ is the one-sided noise power spectral density.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for impulse-response expression, 1 mark for SNR formula.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between a CW radar and a pulsed radar. Which one can measure range and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | CW Radar | Pulsed Radar |
|---|---|---|
| Transmitter state | Continuously ON | ON only for $\tau$ seconds per $T_{PRI}$ |
| Measures velocity (Doppler) | **Yes** | Yes |
| Measures range | **No** | **Yes** |
| Range measurement principle | Not possible (no time reference) | Via round-trip time $t_R = 2R/c$ |
| Power duty cycle | 100 % | $\tau / T_{PRI} \ll 1$ |

A CW radar cannot measure range because it has **no time marker** — every instant of the received signal looks identical, so the receiver cannot tell *when* the echo was emitted and therefore cannot compute $R = c t_R / 2$. A pulsed radar can, because each pulse provides a sharp time origin.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark tabular distinction, 1 mark for explaining the absence of time reference, 1 mark for the pulsed range measurement principle.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a pulsed radar system. Derive an expression for the minimum received signal power in terms of the radar parameters.

**(b) [7 marks]** A pulsed radar operating at 10 GHz has a peak transmitted power of 1 MW, pulse width of 2 µs, PRF of 1 kHz, and antenna gain of 40 dB. If the target is at 50 km with RCS = 10 m², compute the received signal power. Assume the radar is monostatic.

**Solution to (a):**

The **block diagram** of a pulsed radar (also shown in §4.1) consists of:

1. **Transmitter chain:** Master timing oscillator → PRF generator → Modulator → Power amplifier (e.g., klystron / TWT / magnetron) → Duplexer → Antenna.
2. **Receiver chain:** Antenna → Duplexer → Low-noise RF amplifier → Mixer/IF amplifier → Matched filter → Envelope detector → Threshold detector → Display.
3. **Synchronization:** The master clock *gates* the receiver "on" only during the appropriate range window.

**Derivation of the radar range equation (monostatic):**

The **transmitted power density** at the target at range $R$ is:

$$
S_t = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2}
$$

where $G_t$ is the transmit antenna gain. The power intercepted and reradiated by the target is:

$$
P_{re} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t G_t \sigma}{4\pi R^2}
$$

The **power density** of the reradiated wave at the radar antenna is:

$$
S_r = \frac{P_{re}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t G_t \sigma}{(4\pi R^2)^2}
$$

The **received power** captured by the antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ is:

$$
P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t \sigma A_e}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}
$$

Using $A_e = \dfrac{G_t \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ (monostatic, $G_t = G_r = G$):

$$
\boxed{P_r = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}}
$$

This is the **monostatic radar range equation**.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
$f_c = 10$ GHz → $\lambda = 0.03$ m
$P_t = 10^6$ W
$G = 40$ dB → $G = 10^{40/10} = 10^4$ (dimensionless)
$\sigma = 10$ m²
$R = 50 \times 10^3$ m

**Substitute:**

$$
P_r = \frac{(10^6)(10^4)^2 (0.03)^2 (10)}{(4\pi)^3 (50 \times 10^3)^4}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
N = (10^6)(10^8)(9 \times 10^{-4})(10) = 9 \times 10^{12}
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
(4\pi)^3 = (12.566)^3 = 1984.4
$$

$$
R^4 = (5 \times 10^4)^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{18}
$$

$$
D = 1984.4 \times 6.25 \times 10^{18} = 1.240 \times 10^{22}
$$

**Result:**

$$
P_r = \frac{9 \times 10^{12}}{1.240 \times 10^{22}} = 7.26 \times 10^{-10} \text{ W} \approx 0.73 \text{ nW}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_r \approx 7.26 \times 10^{-10} \text{ W} \;\; (\approx -61.4 \text{ dBm})}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the principle of **pulse compression** in radar. With the help of a sketch, describe the LFM (Linear Frequency Modulated) chirp waveform and derive its matched-filter output response.

**(b) [7 marks]** An LFM chirp radar has $\tau = 25$ µs, $B = 5$ MHz, $f_c = 9$ GHz. Compute (i) the time-bandwidth product, (ii) the compression ratio, (iii) the chirp slope $\mu$, (iv) the compressed pulse width, and (v) the range resolution.

**Solution to (a):**

**Pulse compression** is a signal-processing technique that allows a radar to transmit a **long pulse** (high energy $E_p = P_t \tau$) while preserving the **range resolution** of a *short* pulse. This is achieved by **modulating** the long pulse internally (LFM or phase-coded) so that its **bandwidth $B$ is large**, and then **matched filtering** the echo to compress the long pulse into a narrow one of width $\approx 1/B$.

**LFM chirp waveform** (as derived in §3.5):

$$
s(t) = A \cos\!\left[ 2\pi \left( f_c t + \frac{\mu t^2}{2} \right) \right], \quad -\frac{\tau}{2} \le t \le \frac{\tau}{2}
$$

with $\mu = B/\tau$.

The **sketch** (already shown in §4.4) is a time-frequency plot where the frequency ramps **linearly** from $f_c - B/2$ to $f_c + B/2$ over the pulse duration $\tau$.

**Matched-filter output:** The matched-filter impulse response is the time-reversed, conjugated chirp. Convolution of $s(t)$ with $h(t) = s^*(-t)$ yields (ignoring the $4\pi$ factor, evaluating at the bandpass):

$$
y(t) = A^2 \tau \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left( B t \right) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

(approx., valid for large TBP). The **mainlobe width** is $2/B$ and the **first sidelobe** is $\approx -13.2$ dB. Windowing (Hamming/Hanning) reduces the sidelobe to $\leq -40$ dB at the cost of a slightly wider mainlobe (≈ $2.6/B$).

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $\tau = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ s, $B = 5 \times 10^6$ Hz, $f_c = 9 \times 10^9$ Hz.

**(i) Time-Bandwidth Product:**

$$
TBP = \tau \cdot B = (25 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^6) = 125
$$

**(ii) Compression Ratio:**

$$
CR = TBP = 125
$$

**(iii) Chirp slope:**

$$
\mu = \frac{B}{\tau} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 2 \times 10^{11} \text{ Hz/s}
$$

**(iv) Compressed pulse width:**

$$
\tau_{comp} = \frac{1}{B} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^6} = 0.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 0.2 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

**(v) Range resolution:**

$$
\delta_R = \frac{c}{2B} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6} = 30 \text{ m}
$$

**Summary table for answer sheet:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Time-bandwidth product | TBP | 125 |
| Compression ratio | CR | 125 |
| Chirp slope | $\mu$ | $2 \times 10^{11}$ Hz/s |
| Compressed pulse width | $\tau_{comp}$ | $0.2 \,\mu$s |
| Range resolution | $\delta_R$ | 30 m |

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-losing mistakes in this topic (verified from KTU valuation patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in range formulas.** The wave travels out *and back*, so $R = ct_R/2$, $\delta_R = c\tau/2$, and $R_u = cT_{PRI}/2$. Dropping the 2 costs full marks.
> 2. **Mixing up the unambiguous-range condition with the blind-speed condition.** Unambiguous range comes from $T_{PRI}$ (time domain); blind speed comes from $f_{PRF}$ (Doppler domain). They are not the same.
> 3. **Writing $G$ in dB when substituting into a linear equation.** Always convert to linear scale: $G_{lin} = 10^{G_{dB}/10}$.
> 4. **Confusing "pulse width $\tau$" with "compressed pulse width $1/B$".** A 25 µs LFM pulse is still 25 µs *at transmission* — compression happens *only at the receiver*.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the duty cycle in average power calculation.** $P_{avg} = d \cdot P_{peak}$ is a perennial 2-mark trap.
> 6. **Range-Doppler coupling in LFM is an apparent (not physical) range shift.** Don't say "the target moves in range" — say "the matched filter output peak shifts in time."
> 7. **Stating matched filter SNR as $E/\mathcal{N}_0$ instead of $2E/\mathcal{N}_0$.** The factor of 2 is convention-dependent — in KTU exams, use $2E/\mathcal{N}_0$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Radar Waveforms**
>
> - **CW radar** = velocity only, no range, no time marker.
> - **Pulsed radar** = range via $R = c t_R / 2$; velocity via Doppler $f_D = 2 v_r / \lambda$.
> - **Duty cycle** $d = \tau / T_{PRI} = \tau \cdot f_{PRF}$; always between 0 and 1.
> - **Average power** $P_{avg} = d \cdot P_{peak}$.
> - **Unambiguous range** $R_u = c / (2 f_{PRF})$ — set by the time *between* pulses.
> - **Blind speeds** $v_{blind,n} = n \lambda f_{PRF} / 2$ — set by Nyquist sampling of Doppler.
> - **Uncompressed range resolution** $\delta_R = c \tau / 2$ — improves with shorter pulses.
> - **Compressed range resolution** $\delta_R = c / (2 B)$ — improves with larger bandwidth.
> - **Time-bandwidth product** $TBP = \tau B$ = compression ratio = processing gain in dB ($\approx 10 \log_{10} TBP$).
> - **LFM chirp** has quadratic phase; instantaneous frequency $f_i(t) = f_c + \mu t$ with $\mu = B/\tau$.
> - **Matched filter** is optimal in AWGN: $h(t) = K s^*(t_0 - t)$; output peak SNR $= 2E/\mathcal{N}_0$.
> - **Matched-filter output of LFM** is a $\text{sinc}(Bt)$ envelope with first sidelobe at $-13.2$ dB.
> - **Range-Doppler coupling** in LFM: $\Delta R = -c f_D \tau / (2 B)$.
> - **Phase-coded waveforms** (Barker-13 gives $-22$ dB peak sidelobe) achieve pulse compression via binary modulation.
> - **Energy vs Peak Power:** Pulse compression lets you trade peak power for pulse length while keeping range resolution fixed.
> - **The radar range equation** $P_r = P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma / [(4\pi)^3 R^4]$ — the $R^4$ dependence is the fundamental physical bottleneck of monostatic radar.

---

**End of Notes — Radar Waveforms (PCECT751 Module 3)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Simple Form of the Radar Equation** (also called the *Basic Radar Equation* or *One-Way Free-Space Power Transmission Equation extended for two-way propagation*) is the fundamental mathematical relationship that quantifies the **received echo power** at a monostatic radar receiver as a function of the transmitted power, antenna gain, operating wavelength, target radar cross-section (RCS), and slant range to the target.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the simple form of the radar equation is written as:

$$
P_r \;=\; \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4}
$$

Where every symbol corresponds to a specific physical quantity that defines the radar's detectability envelope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Must Memorize):** The radar range equation is the cornerstone of radar engineering. It predicts the maximum range at which a target of given cross-section can be detected by a radar system of known parameters. The inverse fourth-power dependence on range ($R^4$) is the single most important characteristic of the equation.

## 1.2 The "Whispering Gallery" Analogy

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a circular canyon shouting "HELLO" toward the far wall. Three things determine how loud the echo sounds when it returns to your ears:

1. **How loudly you shout** → This is the **Transmitted Power ($P_t$)**.
2. **How well you can focus your voice in one direction** (using a megaphone) → This is the **Antenna Gain ($G$)**.
3. **How big and reflective the wall is** (a smooth granite wall reflects more sound than a soft mossy one) → This is the **Radar Cross-Section ($\sigma$)**.
4. **How far the wall is** → This is the **Range ($R$)**. The sound spreads out going there (loss 1) and again coming back (loss 2), so doubling the distance makes the echo **16 times quieter** ($2^4 = 16$). This is exactly why the equation has $R^4$ in the denominator.

Just like your echo in the canyon, the radar must "shout" electromagnetic energy, wait for a tiny fraction of it to bounce back, and then try to hear that return signal above the background noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in Radar Equation Derivation:**
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - Boltzmann's constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> - Characteristic impedance of free space: $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ Ω

## 1.3 Why is it Called the "Simple Form"?

The word "simple" is critical in KTU parlance. It distinguishes this equation from the **Modified Radar Equation** (which includes system losses, noise, and SNR threshold) and the **Search Radar Equation** (which includes scan time and antenna beamwidth). The simple form assumes:

- Lossless propagation medium (free space)
- Point target
- Monostatic radar (single antenna for Tx and Rx)
- No system losses ($L = 1$)
- No propagation effects (no rain, no multipath)

It is the **starting point** from which all other forms of the radar equation are derived.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic – Breaking Down the Equation

Let us analyze **why** each factor appears in the simple radar equation. The transmission–reflection–reception process is decomposed into four logical stages:

**Step 1 — Power Density at the Target (Outgoing Wave)**
The transmitter radiates $P_t$ watts through an antenna of gain $G$. A directive antenna concentrates energy along its boresight, producing a power flux density (Watts per square meter) at distance $R$:

$$
S_t = \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^2}
$$

The denominator $(4\pi R^2)$ is the surface area of an expanding spherical wavefront. The larger the sphere, the more the energy is "diluted" per unit area.

**Step 2 — Echo Power Captured by the Target**
Not all energy striking the target is reflected back. A portion of it is intercepted based on the target's **Radar Cross-Section ($\sigma$)**, defined in square meters. The power re-radiated (captured and scattered) by the target is:

$$
P_{\text{scattered}} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4\pi R^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Radar Cross-Section ($\sigma$) — Intuitive Definition:** It is a fictitious area that, when intercepted by the incident power density, would scatter isotropically an amount of power equal to that actually scattered by the real target. Units: m². Typical values: small aircraft ≈ 1 m², large ship ≈ 10,000 m², stealth fighter ≈ 0.001 m².

**Step 3 — Power Density of the Reflected Wave at the Radar**
The re-radiated energy spreads out isotropically (assumed) as a spherical wave. By the time it travels back the same distance $R$, the power density at the radar's antenna is:

$$
S_r = \frac{P_{\text{scattered}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}
$$

**Step 4 — Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna**
The receiving antenna, with effective aperture $A_e$, intercepts a portion of this returning flux:

$$
P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma \, A_e}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}
$$

**Step 5 — The Antenna Reciprocity Substitution**
The relationship between gain $G$ and effective aperture $A_e$ for any antenna is:

$$
A_e = \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

Substituting $A_e$ into the $P_r$ expression:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4} \cdot \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4\pi} = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4}
$$

This is the **final form of the simple radar equation**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table and revise it before every test.** It is the highest-yield content in this entire module.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Units | KTU Significance |
|---|---------|----------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | Basic simple radar equation | Watts | **MOST IMPORTANT** — Direct 14-mark derivations expected |
| 2 | $P_t = \dfrac{P_r \, (4\pi)^3 R^4}{G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}$ | Transmitted power required | Watts | Used to size the transmitter |
| 3 | $R_{\max} = \left[\dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 P_{r,\min}}\right]^{1/4}$ | Maximum detectable range | meters | Used to compute radar coverage |
| 4 | $A_e = \dfrac{G \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ | Effective aperture from gain | m² | Reciprocity theorem |
| 5 | $\theta_{3dB} \approx \dfrac{70 \lambda}{D}$ | Antenna 3-dB beamwidth | degrees (°), $D$ in meters | Half-power beamwidth approximation |
| 6 | $G \approx \dfrac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2} = \dfrac{4\pi \eta_A \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}}{\lambda^2}$ | Gain in terms of dish diameter | dimensionless | $\eta_A$ = aperture efficiency (typically 0.55–0.7) |
| 7 | $S_t = \dfrac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^2}$ | Power density at target | W/m² | Faraday's spreading loss |
| 8 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t \, G \, \sigma \, A_e}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}$ | Alternate form using $A_e$ | Watts | Useful when aperture is known |
| 9 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength–frequency relation | meters, Hz | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 10 | $P_{r,\min} = k \, T_s \, B_n \cdot \text{SNR}_{\min}$ | Minimum detectable signal (link to noise) | Watts | **Bridges simple to modified form** |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation scripts, examiners will mark you down heavily for confusing the $R^2$ equation (one-way communication) with the $R^4$ equation (radar/two-way). Always state clearly **"Radar is a two-way propagation phenomenon"** in your answer to earn method marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The simple radar equation is not just a textbook curiosity — it is used in:

- **Air Traffic Control Radars** — sizing transmitter power to detect aircraft at 100 NM
- **Weather Radars** — estimating rainfall rate from reflected power (the famous $Z$-$R$ relation uses $\sigma$)
- **Automotive Radars** (77 GHz) — collision avoidance in modern cars
- **Military Surveillance Radars** — predicting detection of stealth aircraft
- **Space Debris Tracking** — predicting when fragments will be visible to ground radars
- **Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)** — image formation algorithms start from this equation

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation of the Simple Radar Equation

Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step derivation** that KTU expects in a 14-mark answer. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

### Given:
- Radar system operating in free space
- Monostatic configuration (single antenna used for transmit and receive)
- Lossless system
- Target modeled as a point scatterer with RCS = $\sigma$

### Step 1: Define the Transmitted Power Density at the Target

The transmit antenna, with gain $G$, radiates power $P_t$ into a narrow beam. Due to the directive gain, the power density along the boresight direction at range $R$ is enhanced by a factor of $G$ over an isotropic radiator. Hence:

$$
S_t = \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^2}
$$

**Explanation:** The numerator $P_t \, G$ represents the Equivalent Isotropically Radiated Power (EIRP). Dividing by the spherical surface area $4\pi R^2$ gives the flux density at the target's location.

### Step 2: Determine the Power Intercepted and Re-radiated by the Target

The target intercepts a portion of this incident power density. The amount of power that is scattered back toward the radar depends on the target's radar cross-section $\sigma$:

$$
P_{\text{re-rad}} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4\pi R^2}
$$

**Explanation:** Even though $\sigma$ has units of area, it is a fictitious area that, if it intercepted the incident flux, would scatter the same total power as the actual target. The scattered power is assumed to spread uniformly (isotropically) over a sphere centered on the target.

### Step 3: Compute the Power Density of the Reflected Wave at the Radar

As the re-radiated spherical wave travels back toward the radar over a distance $R$, its power density at the receiving antenna is reduced by the additional spherical spreading factor $4\pi R^2$:

$$
S_r = \frac{P_{\text{re-rad}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}
$$

**Explanation:** This is where the **fourth-power range dependence** originates. The signal undergoes spreading loss on the **outward journey** (Step 1) AND on the **return journey** (Step 3). Multiplying two $R^2$ denominators yields $R^4$.

### Step 4: Determine the Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna

The receiving antenna presents an effective collecting area $A_e$ (also called effective aperture) to the incoming wave. The total received power is the product of the returning flux density and this effective area:

$$
P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma \, A_e}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}
$$

**Explanation:** $A_e$ is generally less than the physical aperture area of the antenna, scaled by an aperture efficiency factor $\eta_A$ (typically 0.55–0.7 for parabolic dishes). It represents the equivalent area that absorbs all incident power and delivers it losslessly to the receiver.

### Step 5: Apply the Antenna Reciprocity Theorem

According to the Reciprocity Theorem of antenna theory, the effective aperture and the gain of an antenna are related by:

$$
A_e = \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

**Explanation:** This is one of the most fundamental relationships in antenna theory. It tells us that high-gain antennas (like large parabolic dishes) have proportionally large collecting areas, and this area scales with the **square** of the wavelength — a key reason why long-wavelength radars need very large antennas to achieve good gain.

### Step 6: Substitute $A_e$ and Simplify

Substituting the expression for $A_e$ from Step 5 into the $P_r$ equation from Step 4:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4} \cdot \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

Now combine the $G$ factors and the $4\pi$ factors:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 \cdot 4\pi \cdot R^4}
$$

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}
$$

### Step 7: Final Result

$$
\boxed{P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}}
$$

This is the **Simple Form of the Radar Equation**, the most fundamental equation in radar engineering.

### Verification of Units (Dimensional Check)

Let us verify that both sides have units of power (Watts):

- $P_t$ → [W]
- $G$ → [dimensionless]
- $\lambda^2$ → [m²]
- $\sigma$ → [m²]
- $(4\pi)^3$ → [dimensionless]
- $R^4$ → [m⁴]

Right-hand side units: $\dfrac{\text{W} \cdot 1 \cdot \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{m}^2}{1 \cdot \text{m}^4} = \text{W}$ ✓

The dimensional analysis confirms the equation is correct.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Problem Templates

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU typically tests **three standard problem types** on this topic. Memorize the manipulation pattern for each.

### Template A: Find $R_{\max}$ (Maximum Range)

Given $P_t$, $G$, $\lambda$, $\sigma$, and the minimum detectable signal $P_{r,\min}$, solve for $R_{\max}$:

$$
R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, P_{r,\min}} \right]^{1/4}
$$

**Trick:** Move $R^4$ to the LHS first, take the fourth root.

### Template B: Find $\sigma$ (Target RCS)

$$
\sigma = \frac{P_r \, (4\pi)^3 R^4}{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2}
$$

### Template C: Find $G$ (Required Antenna Gain)

$$
G = \sqrt{ \frac{P_r \, (4\pi)^3 R^4}{P_t \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma} }
$$

**Note:** Some problems substitute $A_e$ instead of $G$ using the alternate form of the equation. Always check which parameter is given in the question stem.

### Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question)

**Problem:** A monostatic radar operating at 10 GHz has a peak transmit power of 1 MW, an antenna gain of 40 dB, and is detecting a target with RCS = 1 m² at a range of 100 km. Calculate the received echo power.

**Given:**
- $f = 10$ GHz → $\lambda = c/f = 0.03$ m
- $P_t = 1 \times 10^6$ W
- $G = 40$ dB → $G_{\text{linear}} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4$
- $\sigma = 1$ m²
- $R = 100$ km $= 1 \times 10^5$ m

**Solution:**

$$
P_r = \frac{(10^6) \cdot (10^4)^2 \cdot (0.03)^2 \cdot 1}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot (10^5)^4}
$$

Computing the numerator:
- $(10^4)^2 = 10^8$
- $(0.03)^2 = 9 \times 10^{-4}$
- Numerator $= 10^6 \cdot 10^8 \cdot 9 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1 = 9 \times 10^{10}$

Computing the denominator:
- $(4\pi)^3 = (12.566)^3 \approx 1984$
- $(10^5)^4 = 10^{20}$
- Denominator $= 1984 \times 10^{20} \approx 1.984 \times 10^{23}$

$$
P_r = \frac{9 \times 10^{10}}{1.984 \times 10^{23}} \approx 4.53 \times 10^{-13} \text{ W} = 0.453 \text{ pW}
$$

**Result:** $P_r \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-13}$ W (about half a picowatt — extraordinarily small!)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The very small numerical value of $P_r$ is precisely why radar receivers must use extremely low-noise amplifiers (LNAs) and matched filters to detect signals. The simple radar equation shows that even a megawatt transmitter produces only picowatts of echo at 100 km — a loss of nearly 13 orders of magnitude!

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Radar System Functional Architecture

Below is a block-level functional diagram illustrating how the parameters of the simple radar equation interact in a physical radar system. Each block represents a stage in the signal chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tx Pulse Generator Pt] --> B[High Power Amplifier]
    B --> C[Duplexer Switch]
    C --> D[Antenna with Gain G and Aperture Ae]
    D --> E[Target at Range R with RCS sigma]
    E --> F[Return Echo Flux Density Sr]
    F --> G[Receiving Antenna Ae]
    G --> H[Duplexer Switch]
    H --> I[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    I --> J[Matched Filter and Detector]
    J --> K[Receiver Output Pr]

    L[Wavelength lambda and frequency f] --> D
    L --> G

    M[Free Space Path Loss 4 pi R squared outward] --> E
    N[Free Space Path Loss 4 pi R squared return] --> F
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The signal chain flows from the **transmitter** (top) through the **antenna**, out to the **target**, and back via the **duplexer** (which routes the single antenna between Tx and Rx modes) to the **receiver**.
- The parameters $G$, $\lambda$, $A_e$, $\sigma$, and $R$ from the equation are each represented as blocks influencing the signal at specific stages.

## 4.2 Power Flow State Diagram — Derivation Visualization

The following diagram maps each mathematical step of the derivation (from Section 3) to its corresponding physical phenomenon. This is the **mental model** that KTU examiners expect you to internalize.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Pt G] --> S1out[Power Density at Target St equals Pt G over 4 pi R squared]
    S1out --> S2[Step 2: Multiply by sigma]
    S2 --> S2out[Scattered Power equals Pt G sigma over 4 pi R squared]
    S2out --> S3[Step 3: Divide by 4 pi R squared again]
    S3 --> S3out[Echo Flux Density at Radar Sr equals Pt G sigma over 4 pi squared R to the fourth]
    S3out --> S4[Step 4: Multiply by effective aperture Ae]
    S4 --> S4out[Received Power Pr equals Pt G sigma Ae over 4 pi squared R to the fourth]
    S4out --> S5[Step 5: Apply Reciprocity Ae equals G lambda squared over 4 pi]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Substitute and simplify]
    S6 --> S6out[Final Simple Radar Equation Pr equals Pt G squared lambda squared sigma over 4 pi cubed R to the fourth]
```

## 4.3 Parameter Influence Hierarchy

The diagram below shows how each parameter in the simple radar equation affects the received power. This is useful for **design intuition** — which knob to turn when you need more range.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Received Power Pr] --> P1[Increase Pt: Linear improvement]
    P --> P2[Increase G: Quadratic improvement, since G squared]
    P --> P3[Increase lambda: Quadratic improvement, since lambda squared]
    P --> P4[Increase sigma: Linear improvement, but not under designer control]
    P --> P5[Decrease R: Fourth power improvement, most effective]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The diagram shows that **reducing range** is the most powerful way to increase received power (4th power), followed by **increasing antenna gain** (2nd power) and **increasing wavelength** (2nd power). This is why long-range military radars use very high-power transmitters AND very large antennas AND relatively long wavelengths.

## 4.4 Comparison Table: One-Way vs Two-Way Propagation

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks students to compare these two cases. The visual is conceptual — illustrating the doubling of path loss in radar.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Communication Tx] --> A2[One-Way Path Loss: 1 over 4 pi R squared]
    A2 --> A3[Receiver Pr = Pt Gt Gr lambda squared over 4 pi squared R squared]

    B1[Radar Tx] --> B2[Outward Path Loss: 1 over 4 pi R squared]
    B2 --> B3[Target Re-radiation]
    B3 --> B4[Return Path Loss: another 1 over 4 pi R squared]
    B4 --> B5[Radar Rx Pr = Pt G squared lambda squared sigma over 4 pi cubed R to the fourth]
```
