# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Define Radar Cross-Section (RCS). What is the RCS of a small sphere of radius $a$ and a flat plate of area $A$ oriented perpendicular to the radar line of sight?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Radar Cross-Section (RCS), denoted by $\sigma$, is a measure of how detectable an object is by radar. It is defined as the fictitious area that, when intercepted by the incident power density, would scatter isotropically an amount of power equal to that actually scattered by the real target. It has units of square meters (m²) and is a property of the target's geometry, material, and orientation relative to the radar.

**Standard values (1 mark):**
- **Small sphere** of radius $a$: $\sigma = \pi a^2$ (geometric optics value, independent of wavelength for $a \gg \lambda$).
- **Flat plate** of area $A$ oriented normal to LOS: $\sigma = \dfrac{4\pi A^2}{\lambda^2}$ (very large for plates much larger than a wavelength).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: Why does the simple radar equation have an $R^4$ dependence while a communication link equation has only an $R^2$ dependence?**

**Model Answer:**

In a **communication link**, the signal travels **only one way** (from transmitter to receiver). The free-space path loss is given by the Friis transmission equation, with $R^2$ in the denominator:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 \, R^2}
$$

In a **radar system**, the signal travels **two ways** — outward to the target and back after reflection. The power density at the target drops by $\dfrac{1}{4\pi R^2}$ on the way out, and the reflected power density drops by **another** $\dfrac{1}{4\pi R^2}$ on the way back. Multiplying the two $R^2$ losses yields the characteristic $R^4$ dependence:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4}
$$

This makes radar detection much more range-sensitive than communication: doubling the range reduces the received power by a factor of 16 in radar but only 4 in communication.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** For each 14-mark question, you must answer **either** the full Question A **or** the full Question B. Internal choice means the same module appears with two variants. Pick the variant you find easier.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Derive the simple form of the radar equation, clearly stating all assumptions. Define each term in the equation.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Free space propagation (no atmospheric attenuation)
2. Monostatic radar (single antenna for Tx and Rx)
3. Point target modeled by a single RCS value $\sigma$
4. Lossless system ($L = 1$)
5. No noise or clutter considered (simple form only)
6. Target re-radiates isotropically
7. Far-field conditions apply

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Power density radiated by the transmit antenna at range $R$:

$$
S_t = \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Power scattered by the target with radar cross-section $\sigma$:

$$
P_{\text{scat}} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4\pi R^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Echo power density at the radar after return path:

$$
S_r = \frac{P_{\text{scat}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}
$$

**Step 4:** Received power collected by antenna of effective aperture $A_e$:

$$
P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma \, A_e}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}
$$

**Step 5:** Using reciprocity $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}$:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4} \cdot \frac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi} = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}
$$

**Definition of terms (1 mark):**
- $P_t$: Peak transmitted power (W)
- $P_r$: Received echo power (W)
- $G$: Antenna gain (dimensionless)
- $\lambda$: Operating wavelength (m)
- $\sigma$: Target radar cross-section (m²)
- $R$: Slant range to target (m)

---

**Q: (b) A monostatic radar operates at 5 GHz with peak transmitted power of 2 MW and an antenna gain of 30 dB. It detects a target with RCS = 10 m² at a range of 50 km. Calculate (i) the wavelength, (ii) the antenna effective aperture, and (iii) the received echo power.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $f = 5$ GHz $= 5 \times 10^9$ Hz
- $P_t = 2 \times 10^6$ W
- $G = 30$ dB → $G_{\text{linear}} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000$
- $\sigma = 10$ m²
- $R = 50$ km $= 5 \times 10^4$ m
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Part (i): Wavelength (1 mark)**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^9} = 0.06 \text{ m} = 6 \text{ cm}
$$

**Part (ii): Effective Aperture (2 marks)**

$$
A_e = \frac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi} = \frac{1000 \times (0.06)^2}{4\pi} = \frac{1000 \times 0.0036}{12.566}
$$

$$
A_e = \frac{3.6}{12.566} \approx 0.2865 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Part (iii): Received Power (4 marks)**

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}
$$

Numerator:
- $P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma = (2 \times 10^6)(10^3)^2(0.06)^2(10)$
- $= 2 \times 10^6 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 0.0036 \cdot 10$
- $= 2 \times 10^6 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 0.036 = 7.2 \times 10^{10}$

Denominator:
- $(4\pi)^3 \approx 1984$
- $R^4 = (5 \times 10^4)^4 = 625 \times 10^{16} = 6.25 \times 10^{18}$
- Denominator $= 1984 \times 6.25 \times 10^{18} = 1.24 \times 10^{22}$

$$
P_r = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{10}}{1.24 \times 10^{22}} \approx 5.81 \times 10^{-12} \text{ W} \approx 5.81 \text{ pW}
$$

**Result:** $P_r \approx 5.8 \times 10^{-12}$ W (about 6 picowatts)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Explain the concept of antenna gain and effective aperture. Derive the relation $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}$.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Antenna Gain (2 marks):** Gain $G$ of an antenna is the ratio of the maximum power flux density radiated by the antenna in a given direction to the power flux density that would be produced by an isotropic radiator fed with the same total input power. It is a dimensionless quantity, often expressed in dB.

**Effective Aperture (1 mark):** Effective aperture $A_e$ is the equivalent area of the antenna through which all incoming power is captured and delivered to the receiver output terminals (assuming 100% efficiency in the equivalent model). It is related to the physical aperture $A_p$ by $A_e = \eta_A \cdot A_p$ where $\eta_A$ is the aperture efficiency (typically 0.55–0.7).

**Derivation (4 marks):** Consider an isotropic radiator transmitting power $P_t$. The power density at distance $R$ is:

$$
S_{\text{iso}} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi R^2}
$$

A directive antenna with gain $G$ concentrates this same power to produce a higher density at the boresight:

$$
S_{\text{dir}} = \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^2}
$$

By the Reciprocity Theorem, if the same antenna is used for reception, the received power is:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e
$$

Equating the two-way link through Friis equation for a matched pair of identical antennas separated by $R$:

$$
\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2
$$

For identical antennas ($G_t = G_r = G$):

$$
\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G^2 \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2
$$

The same ratio using effective aperture:

$$
\frac{P_r}{P_t} = \frac{A_e^2}{(\lambda R)^2} \cdot \text{(constants)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A_e = \frac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

---

**Q: (b) A surveillance radar with antenna diameter 2 m operates at 10 GHz with peak power 1 MW. If the minimum detectable signal is $10^{-13}$ W and target RCS is 2 m², find the maximum range. Assume aperture efficiency = 0.6.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 2$ m → $A_p = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi$ m²
- $f = 10$ GHz → $\lambda = 0.03$ m
- $P_t = 10^6$ W
- $P_{r,\min} = 10^{-13}$ W
- $\sigma = 2$ m²
- $\eta_A = 0.6$ → $A_e = 0.6 \times \pi \approx 1.885$ m²

**Step 1: Compute Gain (2 marks)**

$$
G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2} = \frac{4\pi \times 1.885}{(0.03)^2} = \frac{23.69}{0.0009} \approx 26320
$$

In dB: $G_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(26320) \approx 44.2$ dB

**Step 2: Apply Simple Radar Equation for $R_{\max}$ (4 marks)**

$$
R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, P_{r,\min}} \right]^{1/4}
$$

Numerator:
- $P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma = 10^6 \times (26320)^2 \times (0.03)^2 \times 2$
- $(26320)^2 \approx 6.93 \times 10^8$
- $(0.03)^2 = 9 \times 10^{-4}$
- Numerator $= 10^6 \times 6.93 \times 10^8 \times 9 \times 10^{-4} \times 2$
- $= 10^6 \times 6.93 \times 10^8 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-3}$
- $\approx 1.247 \times 10^{12}$

Denominator:
- $(4\pi)^3 \approx 1984$
- $P_{r,\min} = 10^{-13}$
- Denominator $= 1984 \times 10^{-13} = 1.984 \times 10^{-10}$

$$
\frac{\text{Num}}{\text{Den}} = \frac{1.247 \times 10^{12}}{1.984 \times 10^{-10}} = 6.285 \times 10^{21}
$$

$$
R_{\max} = (6.285 \times 10^{21})^{1/4} = (6.285)^{1/4} \times (10^{21})^{1/4}
$$

$$
R_{\max} \approx 1.582 \times 10^{5.25} = 1.582 \times 1.778 \times 10^5 \approx 281,400 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Final Result (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{R_{\max} \approx 281 \text{ km}}
$$

**Verification using $A_e$ form:**

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma \, A_e}{(4\pi)^2 R^4} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R^4 = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma \, A_e}{(4\pi)^2 P_{r,\min}}
$$

This should yield the same result when computed. KTU expects the method marks for the correct approach, not minor numerical errors.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Students 5–7 Marks Each in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear scale.** A 30 dB gain is **1000**, not 30. Always write: $G_{\text{linear}} = 10^{G_{\text{dB}}/10}$.
> 2. **Mixing up frequency and wavelength.** $\lambda = c/f$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. Watch for GHz vs MHz confusion.
> 3. **Forgetting to raise G to the power 2.** This is the **most common mistake**. In the radar equation, the gain appears as $G^2$ because the same antenna transmits and receives. Writing $G$ instead of $G^2$ gives wrong answers.
> 4. **Wrong exponent when solving for $R_{\max}$.** Some students take the square root instead of the fourth root. Always isolate $R^4$ on one side, then take the **fourth root** using the $\cdot^{1/4}$ operation.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention assumptions.** KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for stating "free space," "monostatic," "no losses," etc. Skipping them loses easy marks.
> 6. **Confusing $(4\pi)^2$ with $(4\pi)^3$.** The denominator has $(4\pi)^3$, not $(4\pi)^2$. One-way communication uses $(4\pi)^2$ but radar uses $(4\pi)^3$ because of the two-way spherical spreading.
> 7. **Not stating units in the final answer.** KTU deducts 0.5 marks if you write "5.81" instead of "5.81 pW" or "5.81 × 10⁻¹² W".

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — 60-Second Rapid Recap:**
>
> ✅ The simple radar equation: $P_r = \dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$
>
> ✅ Why $R^4$? **Two-way propagation** — outward spreading + return spreading
>
> ✅ Why $G^2$? **Monostatic radar** — same antenna used for both Tx and Rx
>
> ✅ Why $\lambda^2$? Comes from the reciprocity relation $A_e = G\lambda^2/(4\pi)$
>
> ✅ RCS ($\sigma$) has units of m². **Not under designer control.**
>
> ✅ The **fourth root** is taken when solving for $R_{\max}$.
>
> ✅ EIRP (Equivalent Isotropically Radiated Power) $= P_t \cdot G$ — appears in Step 1 of derivation.
>
> ✅ **Free space assumption** — no rain, no multipath, no atmospheric attenuation.
>
> ✅ The simple form has **no system losses** ($L = 1$) and **no SNR threshold** — those appear in the **modified radar equation**.
>
> ✅ Most common RCS values to remember: small fighter ≈ 1 m², large bomber ≈ 100 m², ship ≈ 10⁴ m², stealth aircraft ≈ 0.001 m².
>
> ✅ Antenna gain in dB → linear: $G = 10^{G_{\text{dB}}/10}$.
>
> ✅ Reciprocity theorem: $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ — also gives: $G = \dfrac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$.
>
> ✅ **Simple form is the foundation** — every more complex radar equation (search radar, track radar, SAR) reduces to this form under limiting conditions.
>
> ✅ KTU 2024 emphasis: derivation (7 marks) + numerical problem (7 marks) — always be ready for **both** in a single 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radar Block Diagram and Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radar Block Diagram and Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radar (Radio Detection and Ranging)** is an electromagnetic-based detection and ranging system that operates by transmitting a high-frequency RF pulse toward a target and analyzing the **echo signal** reflected from that target. From the time delay and frequency/phase characteristics of the returned echo, the system determines the target's **range, angular position, radial velocity, and signature.**

A **Radar Block Diagram** is the canonical functional architecture that interconnects the major sub-systems of a radar — namely the **Waveform Generator, Transmitter, Duplexer, Antenna, Receiver, Signal Processor, and Display** — in a closed transmit–receive loop. According to KTU 2024 syllabus conventions, mastering this block diagram is the foundational gateway to understanding pulsed radar, CW radar, MTI radar, and tracking radar subsystems.

### Key Subsystem Roles

| Subsystem | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Waveform Generator / Timer | Produces the master trigger pulse that synchronizes the transmitter, receiver gate, and display sweep. |
| Transmitter | Generates high-power RF carrier pulses at microwave frequencies (typically **L, S, C, X, Ku** bands). |
| Duplexer (T/R Switch) | Routes the transmitted pulse to the antenna and protects the receiver during transmission. |
| Antenna | A highly directional parabolic reflector that launches the pulse and collects the weak echo. |
| Receiver (Superheterodyne) | Low-noise amplification, down-conversion, matched filtering, and detection of the echo. |
| Signal Processor | Extracts target information — range, Doppler, clutter rejection. |
| Display | Visualizes target echo on PPI / A-Scope / B-Scope. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Echo in a Dark Cave"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump:** Imagine shouting inside a pitch-dark cave. If you shout "HELLO!", the time it takes for the echo to return tells you how far the cave wall is. If the echo is **loud**, the wall is **close** and **hard**; if the echo is **soft**, the wall is either **far** or made of soft material. Radar works on exactly this principle — but instead of sound, it shouts with **invisible radio waves** and listens for the radio "echo."

This analogy maps perfectly to radar engineering:

| Cave Analogy Element | Radar Counterpart |
|----------------------|-------------------|
| Your voice (shout) | **Transmitted RF pulse** |
| Speed of sound | **Speed of light (c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s)** |
| Echo return time | **Round-trip time delay (tR)** |
| Loudness of echo | **Echo strength / RCS (σ)** |
| Cave wall material | **Target's Radar Cross Section** |
| Your ears | **Receiver (LNA + Mixer + Detector)** |
| Mouth-to-ear switch (you can't listen while shouting) | **Duplexer (T/R Switch)** |

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
> - **Boltzmann's constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
> - **Standard noise temperature:** $T_0 = 290 \text{ K}$
> - **Operating wavelength band:** $\lambda = 1 \text{ cm to } 1 \text{ m}$ (300 MHz – 30 GHz)
> - **Pulse duration range:** $\tau = 0.1 \text{ μs to } 10 \text{ μs}$
> - **PRF range:** $f_r = 100 \text{ Hz to } 100 \text{ kHz}$

---

## 1.4 Operating Frequency Bands — Quick Reference

| Band | Frequency | Wavelength | Typical Use |
|------|-----------|------------|-------------|
| **HF** | 3–30 MHz | 10–100 m | Over-the-horizon radar |
| **VHF/UHF** | 30 MHz – 1 GHz | 0.3–10 m | Long-range surveillance |
| **L-band** | 1–2 GHz | 15–30 cm | Air traffic control (long range) |
| **S-band** | 2–4 GHz | 7.5–15 cm | Terminal approach radar |
| **C-band** | 4–8 GHz | 3.75–7.5 cm | Weather radar |
| **X-band** | 8–12 GHz | 2.5–3.75 cm | Fire-control, marine radar |
| **Ku-band** | 12–18 GHz | 1.67–2.5 cm | Missile guidance |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A pulsed radar operates on the **echo-ranging principle**. The complete operational cycle consists of six sequential steps.

1. **Pulse Generation (t = 0)**: The waveform generator (typically a multivibrator or digital timing circuit) produces a sharp rectangular trigger pulse of width $\tau$ (pulse duration).
2. **RF Carrier Modulation**: This trigger gates a high-power microwave oscillator (magnetron, klystron, or TWT). The transmitter outputs a burst of RF energy: $s_t(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ for duration $\tau$.
3. **Antenna Radiation via Duplexer**: The duplexer switches the transmitter to the antenna. The antenna concentrates the energy into a narrow beam with gain $G$ and beamwidth $\theta \approx \lambda/D$.
4. **Echo Reception (t = tR)**: After travelling to the target at range $R$ and back, the echo arrives delayed by $t_R = 2R/c$. The antenna captures the reflected wave.
5. **Receiver Processing**: The duplexer routes the faint echo (often $-90$ to $-120$ dBm) to a superheterodyne receiver — LNA → Mixer → IF Amplifier → Detector.
6. **Display & Decision**: The detected video pulse is sent to a CRT display. The horizontal sweep is calibrated to range: range = (sweep time × c)/2.

---

## 2.2 The Radar Range Equation — Engineering Backbone

> [!NOTE]
> The **Radar Range Equation** is the single most important equation in radar engineering. It relates the **maximum detectable range** of a radar to its transmitted power, antenna characteristics, target radar cross-section, and receiver sensitivity.

### 2.2.1 Derivation Logic (The "Why")

- The transmitter radiates power $P_t$ through an antenna of gain $G$. At distance $R$, the power density (power per unit area) on the target is:
$$P_{\text{density}} = \frac{P_t G}{4\pi R^2}$$
- This is the **inverse-square spreading** of the wave.
- The target intercepts this power and re-radiates it. The amount of power re-radiated back toward the radar is governed by the **Radar Cross Section (RCS, $\sigma$)**, measured in square meters. Effective backscattered power = $P_t G \sigma / (4\pi R^2)$.
- This re-radiated power again spreads spherically as it returns. The power density back at the radar antenna is:
$$P_{\text{ret}} = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{(4\pi R^2)^2} = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{16 \pi^2 R^4}$$
- The receiving antenna captures this with effective aperture $A_e = G \lambda^2 / (4\pi)$. Therefore, the received echo power is:
$$P_r = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$$
- For the radar to detect the target, $P_r \geq S_{\min}$, the **minimum detectable signal**.

### 2.2.2 Final Form — Maximum Range

$$\boxed{R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k \, T_0 \, B_n \, F \, (S/N)_{\min}} \right]^{1/4}}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{c \, t_R}{2}$ | Range from echo delay | m |
| 2 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | Received echo power | W |
| 3 | $R_{\max} = \left[ \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{\min}} \right]^{1/4}$ | Max detection range (no-noise form) | m |
| 4 | $R_{\max} = \left[ \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_0 B_n F (S/N)_{\min}} \right]^{1/4}$ | Full radar range equation | m |
| 5 | $G = \dfrac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$ | Antenna gain from effective aperture | dimensionless |
| 6 | $S_{\min} = k T_0 B_n F (S/N)_{\min}$ | Minimum detectable signal | W |
| 7 | $\theta_{3\text{dB}} \approx \dfrac{70 \lambda}{D}$ | Antenna 3-dB beamwidth | degrees |
| 8 | $\text{PRF} = \dfrac{1}{T_p}$ | Pulse Repetition Frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $R_{\text{unamb}} = \dfrac{c}{2 \, \text{PRF}}$ | Unambiguous range | m |
| 10 | $\text{Duty cycle} = \tau \cdot \text{PRF}$ | Fraction of time transmitting | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{Avg power} = P_t \cdot \tau \cdot \text{PRF}$ | Average transmitted power | W |
| 12 | $A_e = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \eta_a$ | Effective aperture of parabolic dish | m² |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The radar range equation is used directly in **system design trade-offs**:
> - If a designer doubles $P_t$, the range increases only by $2^{1/4} \approx 1.19$ — i.e., **a 19% range boost requires 4× power.** This is why radar systems favour **high antenna gain** and **low receiver noise figure** over brute-force transmitter power.
> - Choosing a **shorter wavelength** (higher frequency) increases atmospheric attenuation and rainfall loss — this is why weather-penetrating radars (e.g., ATC) operate at L-band, while precision tracking radars use X-band.
> - The RCS of a **stealth aircraft** (F-22 ≈ 0.0001 m²) is engineered to be ~$10^{6}$× smaller than a conventional fighter (~1 m²) — drastically shrinking $R_{\max}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Radar Range Equation

### Step 1 — Power Density at the Target

The transmitter radiates power $P_t$ through an antenna of gain $G$. By definition of antenna gain, the power density in the direction of maximum radiation at distance $R$ is:

$$S_{\text{target}} = \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^2} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$

> **Logic:** A lossless isotropic radiator would distribute $P_t$ uniformly over a sphere of area $4\pi R^2$, giving $P_t/(4\pi R^2)$. Gain $G$ simply multiplies this by $G$ in the main beam direction.

---

### Step 2 — Power Intercepted and Re-Radiated by the Target

The target intercepts $S_{\text{target}}$ and re-radiates power governed by its **radar cross-section** $\sigma$ (m²), defined such that:

$$P_{\text{re-rad}} = S_{\text{target}} \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4\pi R^2}$$

> **Logic:** RCS is a fictitious area that, if the target scattered isotropically, would produce the same back-scattered power density at the radar as the actual target does in its actual direction.

---

### Step 3 — Power Density of Echo Back at the Radar

The re-radiated power spreads isotropically from the target. At the radar antenna (distance $R$ away), the echo power density is:

$$S_{\text{echo}} = \frac{P_{\text{re-rad}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi R^2)^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{16 \pi^2 R^4}$$

---

### Step 4 — Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna

The antenna has an **effective aperture** $A_e$ (m²) that captures this power density:

$$P_r = S_{\text{echo}} \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma \, A_e}{16 \pi^2 R^4}$$

---

### Step 5 — Substituting the Gain–Aperture Relation

Using $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}$:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{16 \pi^2 R^4} \cdot \frac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi} = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{64 \pi^3 R^4}$$

Since $64 = 4^3$:

$$\boxed{P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}}$$

---

### Step 6 — Maximum Range Condition

For detection, $P_r \geq S_{\min}$. Equating $P_r = S_{\min}$ and solving for $R = R_{\max}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\min} &= \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R_{\max}^4} \\
R_{\max}^4 &= \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{\min}} \\
R_{\max} &= \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{\min}} \right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Step 7 — Substituting the Noise-Limited $S_{\min}$

The minimum detectable signal in thermal noise is:

$$S_{\min} = k \, T_0 \, B_n \, F \, \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\min}$$

Plugging in:

$$\boxed{R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k \, T_0 \, B_n \, F \, (S/N)_{\min}} \right]^{1/4}}$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Illustration — Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A pulsed radar operates at **10 GHz** with peak power $P_t = 1 \text{ MW}$, antenna gain $G = 40 \text{ dB}$, target RCS $\sigma = 1 \text{ m}^2$, receiver noise figure $F = 3 \text{ dB}$, bandwidth $B_n = 1 \text{ MHz}$, and required SNR $= 13 \text{ dB}$. Find $R_{\max}$.

**Step A — Convert dB values to linear:**

$$\begin{aligned}
G &= 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10000 \\
F &= 10^{3/10} \approx 1.995 \\
(S/N)_{\min} &= 10^{13/10} \approx 19.95
\end{aligned}$$

**Step B — Compute wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ cm}$$

**Step C — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma \\
  &= (10^6) \times (10^4)^2 \times (0.03)^2 \times 1 \\
  &= 10^6 \times 10^8 \times 9 \times 10^{-4} \\
  &= 9 \times 10^{10}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step D — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= (4\pi)^3 \times k T_0 B_n F (S/N)_{\min} \\
  &= (4\pi)^3 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 290 \times (10^6) \times 1.995 \times 19.95 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Computing each piece:

$$\begin{aligned}
(4\pi)^3 &= 1973.92 \\
k T_0 B_n &= 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \times 10^6 = 4.002 \times 10^{-15} \\
F \cdot (S/N)_{\min} &= 1.995 \times 19.95 \approx 39.80 \\
D &= 1973.92 \times 4.002 \times 10^{-15} \times 39.80 \\
  &= 1973.92 \times 1.593 \times 10^{-13} \\
  &\approx 3.144 \times 10^{-10}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step E — Form the ratio and take the fourth root:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{N}{D} &= \frac{9 \times 10^{10}}{3.144 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.863 \times 10^{20} \\
R_{\max} &= (2.863 \times 10^{20})^{1/4} \\
        &= (28.63)^{1/4} \times 10^5 \\
        &\approx 2.316 \times 10^5 \text{ m} \\
        &\approx 231.6 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $R_{\max} \approx 231.6 \text{ km}$. This is a typical long-range air-surveillance radar performance figure, validating our derivation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Range Sensitivity to Parameters

| Parameter Doubled | $R_{\max}$ Scaling | % Increase |
|-------------------|---------------------|------------|
| $P_t \rightarrow 2P_t$ | $2^{1/4} \approx 1.189$ | **+18.9%** |
| $G \rightarrow 2G$ | $2^{1/2} \approx 1.414$ | **+41.4%** |
| $\lambda \rightarrow 2\lambda$ | $2^{1/4} \approx 1.189$ | **+18.9%** |
| $\sigma \rightarrow 2\sigma$ | $2^{1/4} \approx 1.189$ | **+18.9%** |
| $F \rightarrow 2F$ (worse noise) | $2^{-1/4} \approx 0.841$ | **−15.9%** |
| $(S/N)_{\min} \rightarrow 2 (S/N)_{\min}$ | $2^{-1/4} \approx 0.841$ | **−15.9%** |

> **Engineering insight:** Antenna gain has the *strongest* leverage ($R_{\max} \propto G^{1/2}$), followed by wavelength, RCS, and power — all under a fourth-root dependence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pulsed Radar Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WG[Waveform Generator and Timer] -->|Trigger pulse| TX
    WG -->|Sync sweep| DSP[Display Unit]
    WG -->|Receiver gate| RX[Receiver Superheterodyne]

    TX[Transmitter Magnetron or Klystron] -->|High power RF pulse| DUP
    DUP[Duplexer T and R Switch] -->|Radiate| ANT[Antenna Parabolic Reflector]
    ANT -->|Target echo| DUP
    DUP -->|Weak echo to RX| RX
    RX -->|IF and Video| DSP
    DSP -->|Target blip| OP[Operator]

    ANT -.->|Beam to target| TGT[Target Aircraft Ship]
    TGT -.->|Reflected echo| ANT

    style WG fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style TX fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style DUP fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style ANT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style RX fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style DSP fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style TGT fill:#B0B0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style OP fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Yellow node (WG):** Time-base master — synchronizes everything.
> - **Red node (TX):** High-power transmitter — gated by the WG.
> - **Sky-blue node (DUP):** Duplexer — routes TX↔ANT and ANT↔RX.
> - **Green node (ANT):** Common transmit/receive aperture.
> - **Purple node (RX):** Superheterodyne receiver — LNA → Mixer → IF → Detector.
> - **Orange node (DSP):** Display unit — PPI / A-scope presentation.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Duplexer (T/R Switch) Internal Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TXIN[Transmitter RF Input High Power] --> TR1[TR Tube Gas Discharge]
    TR1 --> ANT2[Antenna Port]

    ANT2 --> ARC[ARC Tube Receiver Protector]
    ARC --> LNA1[LNA Low Noise Amplifier]
    LNA1 --> RXOUT[Receiver IF Output]

    TR1 -.->|Ionized during TX| BLOCK[Blocks receiver path]
    ARC -.->|Cold during RX| PASS[Passes weak echo]

    style TXIN fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style TR1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style ANT2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style ARC fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style LNA1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style RXOUT fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style BLOCK fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style PASS fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> **Function:** The **TR (Transmit-Receive) tube** is a gas-discharge switch that ionizes rapidly when high-power TX power arrives, creating a short circuit that protects the LNA. The **ARC (Anti-Receiver-Cutoff) tube** protects the receiver from leakage during transmit and arcs only when dangerously high power leaks through.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Superheterodyne Receiver Signal Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RFIN[Echo RF at f_c] --> LNA[LNA 30 dB Gain]
    LNA --> MIX[Mixer]
    LO[Local Oscillator at f_LO] --> MIX
    MIX --> IF[IF Amplifier at 30 or 60 MHz]
    IF --> DET[Envelope Detector Diode]
    DET --> VID[Video Amplifier]
    VID --> DSP[Display and Signal Processor]

    style RFIN fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style LNA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style MIX fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style LO fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style IF fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style DET fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style VID fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style DSP fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> **Operation:** The mixer heterodynes the echo (at $f_c$) with a local oscillator (at $f_{LO}$) to produce an **Intermediate Frequency (IF)** at $f_{IF} = \vert f_c - f_{LO} \vert$, typically **30 MHz or 60 MHz**. Down-conversion allows high-gain amplification at a fixed, easily-filtered frequency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Radar. With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of a pulsed radar system.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Radar stands for **RAdio Detection And Ranging**. It is an electromagnetic system for the detection and location of objects (targets) by transmitting RF energy and analyzing the reflected echo.

**Operation (Block Diagram in words):**

1. The **Waveform Generator** produces a timing pulse.
2. This triggers the **Transmitter** (magnetron/klystron) which generates a high-power RF pulse.
3. The **Duplexer** routes the pulse to the **Antenna**, which radiates it as a narrow beam toward the target.
4. The **echo** reflected from the target is captured by the same antenna, passed through the duplexer to the **Receiver** (LNA → Mixer → IF → Detector).
5. The detected video pulse is sent to the **Display** which shows the target's range based on the echo time-delay $R = c t_R / 2$.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a Duplexer? Why is it essential in a radar receiver?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Duplexer** (or T/R switch) is a fast-acting switching device that allows a single antenna to be used for both **transmission** and **reception** in a pulsed radar.

**Why essential:**
- During transmission, the duplexer **disconnects the receiver** so that high-power TX pulses (MW range) do not burn out the sensitive LNA.
- During reception (between TX pulses), the duplexer **connects the antenna to the receiver** so that weak echoes (≈ −100 dBm) can be amplified.
- It is implemented using **TR tubes** (gas discharge) and **ARC tubes** (anti-receiver-cutoff protection).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the functional block diagram of a pulsed radar system. Discuss the role of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the radar range equation and explain how the maximum range depends on transmitted power, antenna gain, wavelength, and target RCS. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)**

| Block | Role | Mark Weightage |
|-------|------|----------------|
| **Waveform Generator** | Generates master trigger pulses of width $\tau$ at PRF $f_r$; synchronizes TX, RX gate, and display sweep. | 1 |
| **Transmitter** | Produces high-power microwave pulses — usually a **magnetron** (non-coherent) or **klystron/TWT** (coherent); output: $P_t \approx 1$ kW – 10 MW peak. | 1.5 |
| **Duplexer** | Routes TX power to antenna and protects receiver using TR/ARC gas-discharge tubes. | 1 |
| **Antenna** | Parabolic reflector with feed horn; provides high gain $G$ and narrow beamwidth $\theta \approx 70\lambda/D$. | 1 |
| **Receiver (Superheterodyne)** | LNA → Mixer (down-converts to IF at 30/60 MHz) → IF amplifier → Detector → Video amp. | 1.5 |
| **Display** | PPI (plan position indicator), A-scope, or B-scope — visualizes echo as blip with $R = ct_R/2$. | 1 |

#### **Solution to (b) — Derivation of Radar Range Equation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Power density at target** (1 mark):

$$S_t = \frac{P_t G}{4\pi R^2}$$

**Step 2 — Re-radiated power using RCS $\sigma$** (1 mark):

$$P_{\sigma} = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{4\pi R^2}$$

**Step 3 — Echo power density back at radar** (1 mark):

$$S_e = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{(4\pi R^2)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Received power using effective aperture** (1 mark):

$$P_r = \frac{P_t G \sigma A_e}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting $A_e = G\lambda^2 / (4\pi)$** (1 mark):

$$P_r = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$$

**Step 6 — Setting $P_r = S_{\min} = k T_0 B_n F (S/N)_{\min}$ and solving** (1 mark):

$$R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_0 B_n F (S/N)_{\min}} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 7 — Dependence Analysis** (1 mark):

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\max} &\propto P_t^{1/4} \\
R_{\max} &\propto G^{1/2} \\
R_{\max} &\propto \lambda^{1/2} \\
R_{\max} &\propto \sigma^{1/4}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **superheterodyne radar receiver**. Why is down-conversion to IF preferred over direct RF amplification? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A radar operating at **5 GHz** has a peak transmitted power of **500 kW**, antenna gain of **35 dB**, target RCS of **2 m²**, receiver bandwidth of **2 MHz**, noise figure of **5 dB**, and minimum SNR of **12 dB**. Calculate the maximum detection range. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to (a) — Superheterodyne Receiver Block Diagram (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (described in words, since diagrams were drawn in Section 4.3):**

**RF Amplifier (LNA)** → **Mixer** + **Local Oscillator** → **IF Amplifier (30/60 MHz)** → **Second Mixer + Second LO** (optional double-conversion) → **Detector** → **Video Amplifier** → **Display**.

**Marks Distribution:**

| Topic | Marks |
|-------|-------|
| Block diagram and description of each stage | 3 |
| Function of LNA (improves SNR), Mixer (down-conversion), IF (fixed-freq high gain), Detector (envelope), Video (display drive) | 2 |
| Why IF preferred: stable gain at fixed freq, easier filtering, better selectivity, lower cost than tunable RF amp | 2 |

**Why IF is preferred over direct RF amplification (2 marks):**
- RF amplifiers at microwave frequencies (GHz) are **noisy and hard to tune** over wide bands.
- IF at 30/60 MHz can be amplified with **high gain, low noise, and excellent selectivity** using fixed, well-designed LC/ceramic filters.
- This results in **better sensitivity** and **selectivity**, which is critical for detecting faint echoes amid clutter.

#### **Solution to (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given data:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f &= 5 \text{ GHz} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^9} = 0.06 \text{ m} \\
P_t &= 5 \times 10^5 \text{ W} \\
G &= 10^{35/10} = 3162.3 \\
\sigma &= 2 \text{ m}^2 \\
B_n &= 2 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} \\
F &= 10^{5/10} = 3.162 \\
(S/N)_{\min} &= 10^{12/10} = 15.85
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Numerator** (2 marks):

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma \\
  &= (5 \times 10^5) \times (3162.3)^2 \times (0.06)^2 \times 2 \\
  &= (5 \times 10^5) \times (1 \times 10^7) \times (3.6 \times 10^{-3}) \times 2 \\
  &= 3.6 \times 10^{10}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Denominator** (2 marks):

$$\begin{aligned}
k T_0 B_n &= 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \times 2 \times 10^6 \\
         &= 8.004 \times 10^{-15} \\
F \cdot (S/N)_{\min} &= 3.162 \times 15.85 \approx 50.12 \\
(4\pi)^3 &= 1973.92 \\
D &= 1973.92 \times 8.004 \times 10^{-15} \times 50.12 \\
  &= 1973.92 \times 4.012 \times 10^{-13} \\
  &\approx 7.92 \times 10^{-10}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio and fourth root** (2 marks):

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{N}{D} &= \frac{3.6 \times 10^{10}}{7.92 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 4.545 \times 10^{19} \\
R_{\max} &= (4.545 \times 10^{19})^{1/4} \\
        &= (4.545)^{1/4} \times 10^{4.75} \\
        &\approx 1.46 \times 5.623 \times 10^4 \\
        &\approx 82.1 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer** (1 mark):

$$\boxed{R_{\max} \approx 82.1 \text{ km}}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake areas where students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in range calculation:** Students often write $R = c \cdot t_R$ instead of the correct $R = c \cdot t_R / 2$ because they forget that the echo travels to the target **and back** (round-trip).
>
> 2. **Mixing up dB and linear values:** When $G = 35$ dB is given, students frequently plug in $G = 35$ directly into the equation. **Always convert to linear first:** $G_{\text{linear}} = 10^{G_{\text{dB}}/10}$.
>
> 3. **Wrong exponent of 4 in $R_{\max}$:** A common slip is writing $R_{\max} \propto (P_t)^{1/2}$ instead of $(P_t)^{1/4}$. The fourth root comes from the $R^4$ dependence in the denominator.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to include the noise figure $F$** in $S_{\min}$: Students often write $S_{\min} = k T_0 B_n (S/N)_{\min}$ and miss the factor $F$ — losing 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Confusing RCS with antenna aperture:** RCS $\sigma$ is in m² (target's effective echoing area); $A_e$ is in m² (antenna's effective receiving area) — they appear in *different* positions in the equation.
>
> 6. **Block diagrams without proper arrows or labels:** Examiners deduct marks for block diagrams missing direction arrows, signal labels (e.g., "RF pulse", "echo"), or functional annotations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Radar Block Diagram & Operation**

- **RADAR = RAdio Detection And Ranging** — detects targets by analysing RF echoes.
- **Block diagram sequence:** Waveform Generator → Transmitter → Duplexer → Antenna → (target) → Antenna → Duplexer → Receiver → Display.
- **Pulse cycle:** TX pulse of width $\tau$ sent every $T_p = 1/\text{PRF}$.
- **Range formula:** $R = c \, t_R / 2$ — divide by 2 for round-trip.
- **Radar Range Equation:**

$$R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k \, T_0 \, B_n \, F \, (S/N)_{\min}} \right]^{1/4}$$

- **Fourth-root dependence:** Doubling any of $\{P_t, \lambda, \sigma\}$ gives only $\approx 19\%$ range boost; doubling $G$ gives $41\%$ boost.
- **Duplexer (TR/ARC tubes):** Protects LNA during TX; routes echo to RX during RX.
- **Superheterodyne receiver:** LNA → Mixer → IF (30/60 MHz) → Detector → Video → Display. IF preferred for stable, high-gain, low-noise amplification.
- **RCS (Radar Cross Section):** Fictitious target area in m² that determines back-scatter.
- **Antenna gain-aperture relation:** $G = 4\pi A_e / \lambda^2$.
- **Noise-limited $S_{\min}$:** $S_{\min} = k T_0 B_n F (S/N)_{\min}$.
- **Constants to memorize:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $T_0 = 290$ K.
- **Operating bands:** L, S, C, X, Ku — higher freq = finer resolution but more attenuation.
- **Exam tip:** Always **convert dB to linear** before substituting into the radar equation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radar Frequencies and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radar Frequencies and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radar Frequencies** refer to the specific portions of the electromagnetic spectrum allocated for the transmission, reception, and processing of Radio Detection and Ranging (RADAR) signals. These frequencies span from **High Frequency (HF, 3–30 MHz)** all the way up to the **millimeter-wave band (>300 GHz)**, with each sub-band exhibiting unique propagation, atmospheric, and target-reflection characteristics that govern its suitability for a given radar application.

The choice of operating frequency is one of the **most critical design decisions** in any radar system, since it directly influences:
- Maximum unambiguous range
- Range and angular resolution
- Atmospheric attenuation
- Target Radar Cross Section (RCS)
- Antenna size, gain, and beamwidth
- Clutter and noise behaviour

### 1.2 The IEEE Standard Radar Frequency Band Designations

The **Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE)** has standardised radar frequency bands using letter codes. These letter designations are used internationally and are essential to memorise for the KTU examination.

| Band | Frequency Range | Wavelength Range | Primary Applications |
|------|----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| HF | 3 – 30 MHz | 10 – 100 m | Over-the-horizon (OTH) surveillance |
| VHF | 30 – 300 MHz | 1 – 10 m | Long-range surveillance, early warning |
| UHF | 300 – 1000 MHz | 0.3 – 1 m | Surface surveillance, foliage penetration |
| L | 1 – 2 GHz | 15 – 30 cm | Air traffic control, long-range tracking |
| S | 2 – 4 GHz | 7.5 – 15 cm | Medium-range surveillance, weather radar |
| C | 4 – 8 GHz | 3.75 – 7.5 cm | Fire control, weather, satellite altimetry |
| X | 8 – 12 GHz | 2.5 – 3.75 cm | Missile guidance, marine radar, SAR |
| Ku | 12 – 18 GHz | 1.67 – 2.5 cm | High-resolution mapping, police radars |
| K | 18 – 27 GHz | 1.11 – 1.67 cm | Cloud detection, atmospheric studies |
| Ka | 27 – 40 GHz | 0.75 – 1.11 cm | Short-range fire control, airport surveillance |
| V | 40 – 75 GHz | 0.4 – 0.75 cm | Millimeter-wave, automotive radar |
| W | 75 – 110 GHz | 0.27 – 0.4 cm | Experimental, high-resolution imaging |
| mm | 110 – 300 GHz | 0.1 – 0.27 cm | Sub-millimetre, experimental radar |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Whistle Spectrum":**
> Think of radar frequencies as different "whistles" a person can blow:
> - **Low-frequency (HF/VHF) whistles** are deep, travel extremely far, but cannot "see" tiny objects. They bend around hills and obstacles.
> - **Mid-frequency (S/C band) whistles** are balanced — good range and reasonable detail.
> - **High-frequency (X/Ku/Ka band) whistles** are sharp and can spot a coin on the road, but they fade quickly in fog and rain.
>
> In radar engineering, you always trade **range penetration for resolution**. The lower the frequency, the farther the signal travels and the better it penetrates weather, but the poorer the resolution. The higher the frequency, the sharper the image you can build, but the more the atmosphere absorbs it.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants Used in Radar Frequency Analysis

- Speed of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Free-space impedance: **η₀ = 377 Ω**
- Boltzmann's constant: **k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- Wavelength–frequency relation: **λ = c / f**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU Module 3, students must be able to (a) list IEEE radar bands, (b) explain why each band is preferred for a given application, and (c) discuss the effect of atmospheric attenuation on the choice of frequency.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Each Radar Band

The choice of radar frequency is not arbitrary. It is the result of a multi-objective optimisation balancing **physics, engineering, and economics**. The principal decision drivers are explained below.

#### 2.1.1 Atmospheric Attenuation

As radar waves propagate through the atmosphere, they suffer attenuation due to **gaseous absorption (O₂, H₂O vapour)** and **hydrometeor scattering (rain, snow, fog)**.

- **Resonance peaks of water vapour (H₂O):** ~22 GHz, ~183 GHz
- **Resonance peak of oxygen (O₂):** ~60 GHz, ~118 GHz

These peaks create **"attenuation windows"** — narrow frequency regions where the signal is almost completely absorbed. Designers deliberately avoid these.

#### 2.1.2 Target Detection vs. Resolution

The **maximum unambiguous range (R_max)** is:

$$R_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot T_{PRI}}{2}$$

The **range resolution (ΔR)** is:

$$\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2B}$$

where **B** is the signal bandwidth. Since B is limited by the carrier frequency and practical hardware, a higher carrier frequency generally allows a wider fractional bandwidth and therefore **finer resolution**.

#### 2.1.3 Antenna Size Constraint

The **antenna gain** is:

$$G = \dfrac{4\pi A_{e}}{\lambda^{2}}$$

where **A_e** is the effective aperture. For a given physical antenna size, a **shorter wavelength (higher frequency) produces more gain** and a **narrower beamwidth**, giving better angular accuracy.

#### 2.1.4 Clutter and Scattering

For the same target, the **Radar Cross Section (RCS, σ)** varies with frequency:

- **Rayleigh region (λ >> target size):** σ ∝ f⁴
- **Resonance region (λ ≈ target size):** σ oscillates strongly
- **Optical region (λ << target size):** σ approaches geometric cross section

> [!NOTE]
> At low frequencies (HF/VHF), stealth aircraft are more detectable because they are in the Rayleigh region where their surface coatings are ineffective.

### 2.2 Atmospheric Attenuation Curves — Behaviour

| Frequency | Clear-air attenuation (dB/km) | Rain attenuation @ 4 mm/h (dB/km) | Practical horizon |
|-----------|-------------------------------|------------------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 GHz (L) | ~0.01 | ~0.0001 | All-weather |
| 10 GHz (X) | ~0.01 | ~0.5 | Rain-limited |
| 35 GHz (Ka) | ~0.2 | ~5 | Short ranges in rain |
| 60 GHz (V) | ~15 | ~10 | "Atmospheric black-out" |
| 94 GHz (W) | ~0.5 | ~7 | Short-range, all-weather |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The 60 GHz band is heavily absorbed by oxygen and is reserved for **short-range, covert, or inter-satellite** links where signal containment is desirable (e.g., secure military comms, mm-wave 5G). The 35 GHz "K-band" is similarly rain-sensitive.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength from frequency | m |
| 2 | $f = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$ | Frequency from wavelength | Hz |
| 3 | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c \, T_{PRI}}{2}$ | Max unambiguous range | m |
| 4 | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \, f_{PRF}}$ | Max range in terms of PRF | m |
| 5 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2B}$ | Range resolution | m |
| 6 | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{k \lambda}{D}$ | Beamwidth (antenna) | rad |
| 7 | $G = \dfrac{4\pi A_{e}}{\lambda^{2}}$ | Antenna gain | numeric (dB) |
| 8 | $A_{e} = \rho_{a} A$ | Effective aperture | m² |
| 9 | $P_{r} = \dfrac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4}}$ | Radar range equation (simplified) | W |
| 10 | $\sigma \propto f^{4}$ | Rayleigh-region RCS dependence | m² |
| 11 | $L_{atm}(dB) = \alpha(f) \cdot R$ | Atmospheric attenuation (one-way) | dB |
| 12 | $Doppler \, shift: f_{d} = \dfrac{2 v_{r} f_{c}}{c}$ | Velocity measurement | Hz |
| 13 | $D = \dfrac{v_{r}}{f_{d}} \cdot \dfrac{2 f_{c}}{c}$ | Velocity resolution (FFT) | m/s |
| 14 | $SNR = \dfrac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4} k T_{0} B F \, L}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio at receiver | numeric (dB) |

> **Note on Formula 12:** The factor **2** appears for monostatic radar (single antenna TX/RX) because the wave travels to the target and back. For bistatic radar, the factor becomes 1.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Typical Frequency | Why this frequency? |
|------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Air-traffic-control (long-range) | L-band (1–2 GHz) | Low attenuation, large volume coverage |
| Weather radar (precipitation) | S-band (2.7 GHz) and C-band (5.6 GHz) | Balance of attenuation vs. resolution; rain scattering is strong |
| Marine navigation radar | X-band (9.4 GHz) | Small antennas on ships, good rain-penetration vs. resolution |
| Police speed gun | Ka-band (35.5 GHz), K-band (24 GHz) | Short range, very high Doppler accuracy, small antenna |
| Missile seeker | W-band (94 GHz), Ka-band (35 GHz) | Small antenna fits in missile nose, very high resolution |
| Automotive ACC / AEB | 76–77 GHz (W-band) | Small, cheap, high resolution for short ranges |
| Airborne SAR | X, C, L bands | Frequency chosen based on desired penetration & resolution |
| Spaceborne altimeter | Ku, Ka, C bands | Trade-off between ionospheric delay and ocean surface roughness |
| Over-the-horizon radar | HF (3–30 MHz) | Sky-wave or surface-wave reflection over the horizon |
| Foliage-penetrating SAR | UHF / VHF | Long wavelength can penetrate leaves and tree canopy |
| Drone detection radar | Ku / Ka / W | Small targets need short wavelengths |

### 2.5 The Seven Classic Radar Sub-Systems (Frequency-Aware View)

A modern radar consists of seven functional sub-systems; each one is **frequency-dependent**:

1. **Transmitter** – Tube (twt, klystron) for high power at S/X; solid-state for lower power at C/X.
2. **Waveguide / feed network** – Waveguide size scales with λ; oversized at VHF, very small at W-band.
3. **Duplexer** – Uses ferrite circulators; precise behaviour at high frequencies.
4. **Antenna** – Reflector, phased array, or slotted waveguide; gain ∝ 1/λ².
5. **Receiver** – Low-noise amplifier must operate at the chosen f with adequate B.
6. **Signal processor** – FFT/MTI/CFAR; sampled at f_s ≥ 2B (Nyquist).
7. **Display / data unit** – Modern AESA radars digitise at the element level.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Unambiguous Range from Pulse Repetition Interval

> The KTU 2024 syllabus requires you to derive the radar range equation components from first principles. Below is the full derivation for the **maximum unambiguous range (R_max)**.

**Step 1 — Definition of Pulse Repetition Interval**

A pulsed radar transmits a burst of duration **τ**, then waits for a time interval **T_PRI** before transmitting the next pulse. The **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** is the reciprocal:

$$PRF = f_{PRF} = \dfrac{1}{T_{PRI}}$$

**Step 2 — Echo Return Constraint**

For the echo from a target at range **R** to be received before the next pulse is transmitted, the round-trip time-of-flight must satisfy:

$$t_{RT} = \dfrac{2R}{c} \leq T_{PRI}$$

**Step 3 — Solving for R**

Rearranging the inequality for the maximum range:

$$R \leq \dfrac{c \, T_{PRI}}{2}$$

Therefore, the **maximum unambiguous range** is:

$$R_{max} = \dfrac{c \, T_{PRI}}{2} = \dfrac{c}{2 \, f_{PRF}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitution of c**

Substituting **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**:

$$R_{max} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \, f_{PRF}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{8}}{f_{PRF}} \;\; \text{[metres]}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example**

If a radar operates at **f_PRF = 1 kHz**:

$$R_{max} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{8}}{1 \times 10^{3}} = 1.5 \times 10^{5} \text{ m} = 150 \text{ km}$$

> This shows that **low-PRF radars** (long PRI) give long unambiguous range but suffer from **Doppler ambiguities**, while **high-PRF radars** give excellent velocity measurement but suffer from **range ambiguities**. This trade-off is the famous *MTI/PRF dilemma*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Antenna Beamwidth vs. Frequency

**Step 1 — Antenna Aperture and Beamwidth**

For a uniformly illuminated rectangular aperture of physical width **D**, the half-power beamwidth in radians is:

$$\theta_{3dB} = \dfrac{0.886 \, \lambda}{D}$$

For engineering estimation, the constant 0.886 is often rounded to **0.88** or even **1**, so the approximate form used in practice is:

$$\Delta\theta \approx \dfrac{\lambda}{D} \;\; [\text{radians}]$$

**Step 2 — Expressing in Wavelength**

Since $\lambda = c/f$:

$$\Delta\theta = \dfrac{c}{f \, D}$$

**Step 3 — Angular Resolution**

The angular resolution (in radians) at range **R** is:

$$\delta x = R \cdot \Delta\theta = \dfrac{R \, c}{f \, D}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Comparison**

| Frequency | λ (m) | D = 1 m antenna: Δθ | D = 1 m antenna: cross-range at 10 km |
|-----------|-------|---------------------|---------------------------------------|
| 1 GHz (L) | 0.30 | 0.30 rad ≈ 17.2° | 3000 m |
| 10 GHz (X) | 0.03 | 0.03 rad ≈ 1.72° | 300 m |
| 35 GHz (Ka) | 0.0086 | 0.0086 rad ≈ 0.49° | 86 m |
| 94 GHz (W) | 0.0032 | 0.0032 rad ≈ 0.18° | 32 m |

> **Engineering Insight:** For a given antenna size, raising the frequency by 10× improves angular accuracy by 10×. This is why **mm-wave automotive radars** (76–77 GHz) can fit a small antenna on a bumper and still resolve lanes of cars.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: The Monostatic Radar Range Equation

**Step 1 — Power Density at the Target**

The power transmitted by the radar is **P_t**. Through an antenna of gain **G_t**, the power density at the target (range R) is:

$$S_{1} = \dfrac{P_{t} G_{t}}{4\pi R^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Echo Power Re-radiated by Target**

The target re-radiates power equal to the incident density multiplied by the **Radar Cross Section (σ)**:

$$P_{re} = S_{1} \cdot \sigma = \dfrac{P_{t} G_{t} \, \sigma}{4\pi R^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Power Density of Echo Back at Radar**

The re-radiated power spreads uniformly over a sphere of radius **R**:

$$S_{2} = \dfrac{P_{re}}{4\pi R^{2}} = \dfrac{P_{t} G_{t} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{2} R^{4}}$$

**Step 4 — Power Captured by Receiving Antenna**

The receiving antenna has effective aperture:

$$A_{e} = \dfrac{G_{r} \, \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$$

So the received power is:

$$P_{r} = S_{2} \cdot A_{e} = \dfrac{P_{t} G_{t} G_{r} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4}}$$

**Step 5 — Monostatic Case (G_t = G_r = G)**

For a single-antenna (monostatic) radar:

$$\boxed{\,P_{r} = \dfrac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4}}\,}$$

> **Note on λ² dependence:** Higher frequency → smaller λ → **less** received power (for the same R, G, σ). This is the fundamental physical reason that **mm-wave radars need high gain, high power, or short range** to work at all. It is a counter-intuitive but crucial result.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Frequency vs. Application Trade-Off

The following decision matrix summarises the engineering trade-offs:

| Decision Criterion | Low Frequency (HF/VHF/UHF) | Mid Frequency (L/S/C) | High Frequency (X/Ku) | Very High Frequency (Ka/W/mm) |
|--------------------|----------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|---------------------------------|
| **Atmospheric loss** | Negligible | Low | Moderate | High (rain, gas) |
| **Target RCS** | Rayleigh region, σ rises as f⁴ | Resonance region | Optical region | Strong specular, may drop |
| **Range** | Very long | Long | Medium | Short |
| **Resolution** | Poor | Medium | Good | Excellent |
| **Antenna size for given gain** | Very large | Medium | Compact | Very small |
| **Doppler accuracy** | Lower (Δf_d ∝ f) | Good | Very good | Excellent |
| **Hardware cost** | High (vacuum tubes) | Medium | Moderate | High |
| **Stealth vs. detection** | Stealth coatings fail | Mid | Mid | Coatings work well |
| **Susceptibility to clutter** | Less (sea, rain) | Moderate | Rain clutter heavy | Severe |
| **Typical application** | OTH, AEW | ATC, weather | Marine, SAR | Missile, automotive |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Sizing a Surveillance Radar

> **Problem (KTU 2024 sample pattern, 14 marks style):**
> A coastal surveillance radar operates at **f = 9.4 GHz (X-band)**. The maximum desired range is **R = 50 km**, and the target RCS is **σ = 1 m²** (a small boat). The antenna diameter is **D = 2 m**, and the noise figure is **F = 3 dB**. The required SNR at the detector output is **SNR_min = 13 dB**. The system temperature is **T₀ = 290 K**. Compute:
> (a) The minimum transmitted power.
> (b) The maximum unambiguous range if the PRF is 2 kHz.
> (c) The 3-dB beamwidth.

**Solution (a) — Transmitted Power**

Step 1 — Compute wavelength:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{9.4 \times 10^{9}} = 0.0319 \text{ m} \approx 3.19 \text{ cm}$$

Step 2 — Antenna gain (efficiency assumed 0.55):

$$A_{e} = \rho_{a} \cdot \pi (D/2)^{2} = 0.55 \times \pi \times 1^{2} = 1.728 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$G = \dfrac{4\pi A_{e}}{\lambda^{2}} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 1.728}{(0.0319)^{2}} = 21315 \approx 43.3 \text{ dB}$$

Step 3 — Noise bandwidth (assume matched filter, B = 1/τ = 5 MHz):

$$N = k T_{0} B F = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \times 5 \times 10^{6} \times 2$$

$$N = 4.002 \times 10^{-14} \text{ W} \approx -104 \text{ dBm}$$

Step 4 — Required signal power (SNR_min = 13 dB, factor 20):

$$P_{r,min} = 20 \times N = 8.0 \times 10^{-13} \text{ W} = -91 \text{ dBm}$$

Step 5 — Solve the radar equation for P_t:

$$P_{t} = \dfrac{P_{r,min} \, (4\pi)^{3} R^{4}}{G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}$$

Plugging R = 5 × 10⁴ m:

$$P_{t} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^{-13} \times 1984 \times (5 \times 10^{4})^{4}}{(21315)^{2} \times (0.0319)^{2} \times 1}$$

$$P_{t} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^{-13} \times 1984 \times 6.25 \times 10^{18}}{4.543 \times 10^{8} \times 1.018 \times 10^{-3} \times 1}$$

$$P_{t} = \dfrac{9.92 \times 10^{6}}{4.625 \times 10^{5}} \approx 21.4 \text{ W}$$

**Result:** A modest 21 W transmitter suffices — confirming that X-band at 9.4 GHz is very efficient for short-range surveillance.

**Solution (b) — Maximum Unambiguous Range**

$$R_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \, f_{PRF}} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{3}} = 7.5 \times 10^{4} \text{ m} = 75 \text{ km}$$

The 50 km requirement is satisfied with margin.

**Solution (c) — Beamwidth**

$$\Delta\theta = \dfrac{0.88 \, \lambda}{D} = \dfrac{0.88 \times 0.0319}{2} = 0.01404 \text{ rad} \approx 0.80^{\circ}$$

Excellent angular accuracy for a 2 m antenna at X-band.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
