$$F = \frac{G k T_0 B + N_{added}}{G k T_0 B} = 1 + \frac{N_{added}}{G k T_0 B}$$

**Step 3 — Express added noise as an equivalent input temperature.**

By definition, the equivalent input noise temperature $T_e$ is the fictitious input temperature that, when multiplied by $k$ and $B$ and amplified by $G$, would produce the same added noise:

$$N_{added} = G \cdot k T_e B$$

Substituting back:

$$F = 1 + \frac{G k T_e B}{G k T_0 B} = 1 + \frac{T_e}{T_0}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T_e$.**

$$\boxed{T_e = (F - 1) T_0 \quad \text{with} \quad T_0 = 290 \text{ K}}$$

This derivation shows that every noisy two-port can be modeled as a noiseless two-port with a noise source of temperature $T_e$ at its input — a hugely powerful abstraction.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Friis' Noise Figure Formula (Cascade)

**Step 1 — Consider two cascaded stages.**

Let Stage 1 have noise factor $F_1$, gain $G_1$, and added noise $N_{1,added} = G_1 k (F_1 - 1) T_0 B$. Same for Stage 2.

**Step 2 — Compute output noise of the cascade.**

Output noise $N_o$ comes from:
- Input noise $N_i$ amplified by $G_1 G_2$: $\quad G_1 G_2 k T_0 B$
- Stage 1 added noise amplified by $G_2$: $\quad G_1 G_2 (F_1 - 1) k T_0 B$
- Stage 2 added noise: $\quad G_2 (F_2 - 1) k T_0 B$

Therefore:

$$N_o = G_1 G_2 k T_0 B \left[ 1 + (F_1 - 1) + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the noise factor definition.**

$$F_{total} = \frac{N_o}{G_1 G_2 k T_0 B} = 1 + (F_1 - 1) + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1}$$

$$\boxed{F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1}}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to N stages by induction.**

Adding Stage 3 with gain $G_3$ in front of the existing two-stage cascade (gain $G_1 G_2$, noise factor $F_{12} = F_1 + (F_2 - 1)/G_1$):

$$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}$$

By induction for N stages:

$$F_{total} = F_1 + \sum_{i=2}^{N} \frac{F_i - 1}{\prod_{j=1}^{i-1} G_j}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

#### Example 1: Thermal Noise Power Across a Band

A satellite receiver has an LNA with bandwidth $B = 36$ MHz operating at $T = 290$ K. Find the thermal noise power.

**Solution:**

$$N = k T B = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) (290) (36 \times 10^6)$$

$$N = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \times 3.6 \times 10^7$$

$$N = 1.4407 \times 10^{-13} \text{ W}$$

Converting to dBm:

$$N_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(1.4407 \times 10^{-13} / 10^{-3}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.4407 \times 10^{-10})$$

$$N_{dBm} = -98.4 \text{ dBm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The value −98.4 dBm is the noise floor of a typical satellite TV receiver. The received signal must be **above this** to be decoded — hence the need for high-gain antennas and low-noise amplifiers.

#### Example 2: Noise Figure to Noise Temperature

An LNA has NF = 1.2 dB. Find its equivalent noise temperature.

**Solution:**

$$F = 10^{1.2/10} = 10^{0.12} = 1.318$$

$$T_e = (F - 1) \cdot 290 = (0.318)(290) = 92.2 \text{ K}$$

#### Example 3: Friis Cascade — Three-Stage Receiver

A receiver has:
- Stage 1 (LNA): $G_1 = 20$ dB, $F_1 = 1.5$ dB
- Stage 2 (Mixer): $G_2 = −6$ dB, $F_2 = 6$ dB
- Stage 3 (IF Amp): $G_3 = 30$ dB, $F_3 = 2$ dB

Find overall noise figure in dB.

**Solution — convert to linear values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
G_1 &= 10^{20/10} = 100 \\
G_2 &= 10^{-6/10} = 0.2512 \\
G_3 &= 10^{30/10} = 1000 \\
F_1 &= 10^{1.5/10} = 1.4125 \\
F_2 &= 10^{6/10} = 3.981 \\
F_3 &= 10^{2/10} = 1.585
\end{aligned}$$

Apply Friis:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{total} &= F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} \\
&= 1.4125 + \frac{3.981 - 1}{100} + \frac{1.585 - 1}{100 \times 0.2512} \\
&= 1.4125 + 0.02981 + 0.02329 \\
&= 1.4656
\end{aligned}$$

Converting back to dB:

$$NF_{total} = 10 \log_{10}(1.4656) = 1.66 \text{ dB}$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice that the LNA's noise figure (1.5 dB) dominates — the mixer and IF amp contribute less than 0.06 dB combined. **This confirms the LNA-first design principle.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Cascaded Noisy Receiver with Noise Sources

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Ant["Antenna with Sky Noise Tsky"] --> LNAin["Noise Source Trec1 Added by LNA"]
    LNAin --> LNA["LNA Block Gain G1 Noise Factor F1"]
    LNA --> Mixin["Noise Source Trec2 Added by Mixer"]
    Mixin --> Mix["Mixer Block Gain G2 Noise Factor F2"]
    Mix --> IFin["Noise Source Trec3 Added by IF Amplifier"]
    IFin --> IF["IF Amplifier Block Gain G3 Noise Factor F3"]
    IF --> Out["Receiver Output C over N Ratio"]

    Sys["System Noise Temp Tsys = Tant plus Trec1 plus Trec2 over G1 plus Trec3 over G1G2"] -.computed.-> Out
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Flow — Noise Power Budget

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Thermal Agitation of Electrons in Conductors"] --> B["Johnson Nyquist Noise Source 4kTR per Hz"]
    C["Discrete Charge Flow in Junctions"] --> D["Shot Noise Source 2qIdc per Hz"]
    E["Imperfections and Traps in Semiconductors"] --> F["Flicker 1 divided by f Noise"]
    G["Cosmic Background and Galactic Emission"] --> H["Antenna Sky Noise Tsky"]
    I["Ground Pickup via Side Lobes"] --> J["Antenna Ground Noise Tground"]

    B --> K["Total Receiver Noise Budget"]
    D --> K
    F --> K
    H --> K
    J --> K

    K --> L["Compute Tsys and NF"]
    L --> M["Link Budget and G over T Figure of Merit"]
```

### 4.3 Noise Figure vs Noise Temperature Reference Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NF["Noise Figure NF in dB"] --> Conv1["Convert to linear F equals 10 power NF by 10"]
    Conv1 --> Te["Compute Te equals F minus 1 times 290 K"]
    Te --> Back1["Back to NF equals 10 log F plus 1 times Te by 290"]
    Back1 --> NF

    style NF fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Conv1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Te fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Back1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Noise Figure and Equivalent Noise Temperature of a receiver. Derive the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

**Noise Figure (F):** The noise figure of a two-port network is the ratio of input signal-to-noise ratio to output signal-to-noise ratio:

$$F = \frac{SNR_{in}}{SNR_{out}}$$

**Equivalent Noise Temperature (T_e):** It is the fictitious temperature that, when applied at the input of an ideal noiseless network with the same gain, would produce the same output noise power as the actual noisy network.

**Relationship:** A noisy network adds $N_{added} = G k T_e B$ to the input noise $G k T_0 B$. Therefore:

$$F = 1 + \frac{T_e}{T_0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_e = (F - 1) T_0$$

where $T_0 = 290$ K. **(3 marks)** *(1 mark for definitions, 2 marks for derivation)*

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is thermal noise? Write the expression for thermal noise power available from a resistor.**

**Model Answer:**

Thermal noise (Johnson-Nyquist noise) is the random noise generated due to the thermal agitation of charge carriers (electrons) in a conductor or resistor at any temperature above absolute zero.

The available thermal noise power delivered to a matched load over a bandwidth $B$ at temperature $T$ is:

$$N = kTB$$

where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is Boltzmann's constant. **(3 marks)** *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for explanation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Derive the Friis formula for the noise factor of a cascaded receiver system. A satellite receiver has three stages with the following parameters:**

- **Stage 1 (LNA):** Gain $G_1 = 15$ dB, Noise Figure $F_1 = 2$ dB
- **Stage 2 (Mixer):** Gain $G_2 = −8$ dB, Noise Figure $F_2 = 7$ dB
- **Stage 3 (IF Amplifier):** Gain $G_3 = 25$ dB, Noise Figure $F_3 = 3$ dB

**Find the overall noise figure and equivalent noise temperature of the cascade.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Friis Formula (7 marks):**

Refer to Section 3.3 for the full step-by-step derivation. Outline:

1. Define noise factor for a single stage: $F_i = 1 + N_{i,added}/(G_i k T_0 B)$.
2. Write the output noise power of two cascaded stages as the sum of input noise amplified, plus added noise from Stage 1 amplified by Stage 2, plus added noise of Stage 2.
3. Apply the definition $F_{total} = N_o / (G_1 G_2 k T_0 B)$ to get:

$$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1}$$

4. Generalize by induction to N stages:

$$F_{total} = F_1 + \sum_{i=2}^{N} \frac{F_i - 1}{\prod_{j=1}^{i-1} G_j}$$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Convert all parameters to linear scale.**

$$\begin{aligned}
G_1 &= 10^{15/10} = 31.623 \\
G_2 &= 10^{-8/10} = 0.1585 \\
G_3 &= 10^{25/10} = 316.23 \\
F_1 &= 10^{2/10} = 1.585 \\
F_2 &= 10^{7/10} = 5.012 \\
F_3 &= 10^{3/10} = 1.995
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply Friis formula.**

$$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{total} &= 1.585 + \frac{5.012 - 1}{31.623} + \frac{1.995 - 1}{31.623 \times 0.1585} \\
&= 1.585 + 0.127 + 0.1986 \\
&= 1.911
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Convert overall noise figure to dB.**

$$NF_{total} = 10 \log_{10}(1.911) = 2.81 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4: Compute equivalent noise temperature.**

$$T_e = (F - 1) T_0 = (1.911 - 1) \times 290 = 264.2 \text{ K}$$

**Result:** $NF_{total} = 2.81$ dB and $T_e = 264.2$ K. **(14 marks total)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Explain the concept of system noise temperature in a satellite communication receiver. A satellite ground station has an antenna noise temperature $T_{ant} = 50$ K and a receiver with equivalent noise temperature $T_e = 150$ K operating at 4 GHz with a bandwidth of 30 MHz. Compute:**
- **(i) The total system noise temperature**
- **(ii) The thermal noise power at the receiver input**
- **(iii) The C/N ratio if the received carrier power is $C = −120$ dBW**

**(b) Discuss the significance of the G/T ratio as a figure of merit for satellite ground stations.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept and Numerical Computation (10 marks):**

**System Noise Temperature Concept (3 marks):**
The system noise temperature $T_{sys}$ lumps together all noise sources (external antenna noise + internal receiver noise) into a single equivalent input temperature. It allows us to compute the total input-referred noise power as simply $N = k T_{sys} B$.

**(i) Total System Noise Temperature (2 marks):**

$$T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_e = 50 + 150 = 200 \text{ K}$$

**(ii) Thermal Noise Power (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= k T_{sys} B \\
&= (1.38 \times 10^{-23})(200)(30 \times 10^6) \\
&= 8.28 \times 10^{-14} \text{ W} \\
&= 10 \log_{10}(8.28 \times 10^{-14}) \text{ dBW} \\
&= -130.82 \text{ dBW}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Carrier-to-Noise Ratio (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{N} &= C \text{(dBW)} - N \text{(dBW)} \\
&= -120 - (-130.82) = 10.82 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) G/T Ratio Significance (4 marks):**

The G/T ratio is the **figure of merit** of a satellite earth station, defined as:

$$\frac{G}{T} = \frac{Antenna\ Gain}{System\ Noise\ Temperature} \quad \text{in dB/K}$$

**Significance:**
1. **Captures both gain and noise in one number** — a higher G/T means the station can detect weaker signals.
2. **Standardized comparison metric** — used by Intelsat, ITU, and ISRO to specify earth station performance categories.
3. **Directly affects link margin** — appears in the C/N equation: $C/N = EIRP + G/T - Path\ Loss - kB$.
4. **Drives design trade-offs** — large dish (high G) vs. cryogenically cooled LNA (low T) both improve G/T, but with different cost/operational implications.

For our example, with $G = 50$ dB and $T_{sys} = 200$ K = 23 dB/K, the figure of merit is $G/T = 50 - 23 = 27$ dB/K. **(14 marks total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **dB ↔ Linear conversion errors** are the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. Always show the step $G_1 = 10^{15/10} = 31.62$, never assume the value.
> 2. **Confusing noise factor (linear, F) with noise figure (dB, NF)**. Marks deducted for using NF directly in Friis formula without conversion.
> 3. **Forgetting the bandwidth** in the $kTB$ formula — many students write $kT$ alone.
> 4. **Reference temperature confusion**: $T_e = (F-1)T_0$ uses $T_0 = 290$ K, but actual system temperature is $T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_e$ — do **not** mix them up.
> 5. **In Friis formula**, the second term uses the gain of the **preceding** stage, not the current one. This is a classic trap.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Thermal noise power** in a bandwidth $B$ at temperature $T$: $N = kTB$, with $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- **Magic constant to memorize**: $kT_0 = 4 \times 10^{-21}$ W/Hz $= -204$ dBW/Hz at $T_0 = 290$ K.
- **Noise Figure (NF)** = SNR degradation through a stage; **Noise Temperature (T_e)** = equivalent input temperature representation.
- **Conversion formulas**: $F = 1 + T_e/T_0$ and $T_e = (F-1)T_0$ with $T_0 = 290$ K.
- **Friis formula** states that the first stage dominates: $F_{total} = F_1 + (F_2-1)/G_1 + \ldots$
- **System noise temperature**: $T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_e$, drives the entire link budget.
- **C/N ratio** at output: $C/N = C / (k T_{sys} B)$ — the central equation for satellite link design.
- **Design rule**: LNA first, LNA low-noise, LNA high-gain. This single principle governs satellite/radar receiver design.
- **Practical NF values**: Satellite LNA ≈ 0.3–0.8 dB; Radar front-end ≈ 1–2 dB; Cellular base station LNA ≈ 1.5–2.5 dB.
- **Practical T_e values**: Cryogenic LNA ≈ 4–20 K; Room-temperature LNA ≈ 30–150 K; Cable/filters alone ≈ 100–300 K.
- **Don't confuse** noise factor (linear) with noise figure (dB). **Don't confuse** input noise $kT_0B$ with output noise $GkT_0B$ in Friis analysis.
- The **G/T figure of merit** ties together antenna gain and system noise temperature into a single number that determines link performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modified Radar Range Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modified Radar Range Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Modified Radar Range Equation** is a comprehensive extension of the classical radar range equation that incorporates real-world non-idealities such as **receiver noise**, **system losses**, **signal processing gains**, and **pulse integration effects**. While the standard radar range equation assumes an ideal, noiseless receiver, the modified form provides a practical framework for predicting the **maximum detectable range** of a radar system under actual operating conditions, where the minimum detectable signal must compete against the noise floor of the receiver.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this equation is treated as the **engineering-grade formulation** that bridges theoretical detectability and practical radar design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCECT751 – Module 3)**
> The Modified Radar Range Equation must be studied in the context of *receiver noise characteristics*, *noise figure*, *system temperature*, and *integration of pulses*. The examiner expects students to derive the SNR-based form from the classical energy/power radar equation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a busy market, trying to listen to your friend call your name from a distance. Several factors determine whether you can hear them:

- **Volume of their voice** → Analogous to *transmitted power (Pt)*
- **Direction they are facing** → *Antenna gain (G)*
- **Distance between you** → *Target range (R)*
- **Background market noise** → *Receiver noise (kT₀B)*
- **How clearly your ears pick up sounds** → *Noise Figure (F)*
- **Whether you are listening for a split second or for several seconds** → *Pulse integration (nE or M)*

The **Modified Radar Range Equation** works exactly like this. The "whisper" of an echo from a distant aircraft must be louder than the combined effect of market noise, imperfect hearing, and short listening time. The equation mathematically determines the maximum distance at which the echo is still "hearable."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants (Memorize for KTU Exam):**
> - Boltzmann's constant: **k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
> - Reference temperature: **T₀ = 290 K**
> - Speed of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**

### 1.3 Why "Modify" the Standard Equation?

The standard radar range equation only computes the *received signal power*. In real engineering practice, we need to compare this signal against the *noise floor* to determine **detectability**. The modification introduces:

1. **Noise Power Term (kT₀B)**: Establishes the noise floor.
2. **Noise Figure (F)**: Models receiver imperfections.
3. **System Losses (Ls)**: Captures atmospheric, plumbing, and processing losses.
4. **Integration Improvement Factor (Ii)**: Rewards longer observation times.
5. **Minimum SNR (S/N)min**: Sets the detection threshold.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework – Step-by-Step Logic

The derivation begins from the **classical radar range equation** for received echo power:

$$P_{r} = \frac{P_{t} \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot R^{4}}$$

To make this practically useful, we apply the following logical modifications:

- **Step 1 – Introduce the Minimum Detectable Signal**: The received power must exceed a minimum threshold, governed by the receiver's noise floor and the required SNR for detection.

- **Step 2 – Incorporate Receiver Noise**: The noise power is given by the **kTB** relation, where **B** is the receiver's equivalent noise bandwidth. Since real receivers are not ideal, the **noise figure F** is introduced to account for internally generated noise.

- **Step 3 – Add System Losses**: A multiplicative loss factor **Ls** captures all real-world inefficiencies (transmission line losses, antenna pattern losses, signal processing losses, atmospheric attenuation, beam-shape loss).

- **Step 4 – Apply Pulse Integration**: When M pulses are coherently integrated, the SNR improves by a factor approximately equal to M (coherent) or √M (non-coherent). This is the **Integration Improvement Factor (Ii)**.

- **Step 5 – Equate and Solve for Range**: Set the received SNR equal to the minimum required SNR and solve for **Rmax**, the maximum detectable range.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Expression** | **Standard Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Received Echo Power | Pr | $\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | Watts (W) |
| Noise Power | N | $k \cdot T_0 \cdot B$ | Watts (W) |
| Receiver Noise Power (with NF) | N | $k \cdot T_0 \cdot B \cdot F$ | Watts (W) |
| Minimum Detectable Signal | Smin | $k \cdot T_0 \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{min}$ | Watts (W) |
| Modified Radar Range (Ideal) | Rmax | $\left[\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_0 B F (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$ | Meters (m) |
| Modified Radar Range (with losses) | Rmax | $\left[\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_0 B F (S/N)_{min} L_s}\right]^{1/4}$ | Meters (m) |
| Modified Radar Range (with integration) | Rmax | $\left[\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma \cdot I_i \cdot n E}{(4\pi)^3 k T_0 B F (S/N)_{min} L_s}\right]^{1/4}$ | Meters (m) |
| Integration Improvement (Coherent) | Ii | ≈ M (number of pulses) | Dimensionless |
| Integration Improvement (Non-Coherent) | Ii | ≈ √M | Dimensionless |
| Antenna Gain vs. Aperture | G | $\frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$ | Dimensionless |
| Wavelength from frequency | λ | $c / f$ | Meters (m) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Point:** Many students incorrectly use the *transmitted* wavelength when both transmit and receive paths exist. For a monostatic radar (single antenna for Tx/Rx), the gain appears as **G²** since the same antenna is used twice.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Modified Radar Range Equation is the **workhorse formula** used by:

- **Air Traffic Control (ATC) Radars** – to determine coverage volume
- **Military Surveillance Radars** – for detection range predictions
- **Weather Radars (Doppler)** – to size transmitters for storm penetration
- **Automotive Radars (77 GHz)** – for adaptive cruise control safety margins
- **Spaceborne SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar)** – for Earth observation mission planning
- **Marine Navigation Radars** – for shipboard installation trade-offs

In all these systems, the modified equation is used during the **system design phase** to perform **link-budget analysis** — a critical step in radar engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Modified Radar Range Equation

We will systematically derive the modified form from first principles, ensuring every algebraic transition is shown.

---

#### **Step 1: Start with the Classical (Bistatic-style) Power Equation**

The classical monostatic radar received power from a point target at range R is:

$$P_{r} = \frac{P_{t} \cdot G_{t} \cdot G_{r} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{2} \cdot R^{2} \cdot (4\pi) \cdot R^{2}}$$

**Explanation:** The transmitted signal undergoes *inverse-square spreading* to reach the target, gets *re-radiated* with radar cross-section σ, and undergoes *inverse-square spreading* again on the return path. The wavelength-squared term arises from the effective aperture of the receive antenna.

For a monostatic radar where the same antenna is used for transmit and receive, Gt = Gr = G, and the two-way antenna gain factor becomes:

$$P_{r} = \frac{P_{t} \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot R^{4}}$$

---

#### **Step 2: Define the Noise Power at the Receiver Input**

Thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) noise at the receiver input is governed by:

$$N = k \cdot T_{0} \cdot B$$

**Explanation:** Here, k is Boltzmann's constant (1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K), T₀ is the standard reference temperature (290 K), and B is the receiver's equivalent noise bandwidth in Hertz.

---

#### **Step 3: Account for Receiver Imperfections via Noise Figure**

A real receiver amplifies both the signal and adds its own internal noise. The **noise figure F** (linear, not in dB) is defined as:

$$F = \frac{(S/N)_{in}}{(S/N)_{out}}$$

**Explanation:** F ≥ 1. An ideal receiver has F = 1. A typical radar receiver has F between 2 and 10 (i.e., 3 dB to 10 dB).

The effective total noise power at the receiver output is therefore:

$$N_{eff} = k \cdot T_{0} \cdot B \cdot F$$

---

#### **Step 4: Define the Minimum Detectable Signal**

The smallest signal power that can be reliably detected is when the output SNR equals the minimum required SNR for a chosen probability of detection (Pd) and probability of false alarm (Pfa):

$$S_{min} = (S/N)_{min} \cdot N_{eff} = k \cdot T_{0} \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{min}$$

---

#### **Step 5: Equate Echo Power to Minimum Detectable Signal**

For maximum range, the received echo power must just equal the minimum detectable signal:

$$\frac{P_{t} \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot R_{max}^{4}} = k \cdot T_{0} \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{min}$$

---

#### **Step 6: Solve Algebraically for Rmax**

Rearranging to isolate Rmax:

$$R_{max}^{4} = \frac{P_{t} \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot k \cdot T_{0} \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{min}}$$

Taking the fourth root (since the exponent of R is 4):

$$R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_{t} \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot k \cdot T_{0} \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$$

This is the **first complete form** of the modified radar range equation.

---

#### **Step 7: Incorporate System Losses (Ls)**

Real systems suffer from various losses. Multiplying the denominator by Ls (where Ls ≥ 1):

$$R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_{t} \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot k \cdot T_{0} \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{min} \cdot L_{s}}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Explanation:** Ls aggregates transmission line losses, antenna pattern losses, atmospheric absorption, signal processing losses, and beam-shape losses. A typical value is Ls ≈ 10 dB to 20 dB.

---

#### **Step 8: Incorporate Pulse Integration (nE · Ii)**

When the radar observes the target over multiple pulses, integration improves SNR. Let M be the number of integrated pulses, and nE be the duty cycle / efficiency. The integration improvement factor Ii is:

- Coherent integration: Ii = M
- Non-coherent integration: Ii ≈ √M (approximate)

The fully modified form becomes:

$$R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_{t} \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma \cdot n_{E} \cdot I_{i}}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot k \cdot T_{0} \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{min} \cdot L_{s}}\right]^{1/4}$$

---

#### **Step 9: Final Compact Form**

The general KTU-ready form is often written as:

$$R_{max}^{4} = \frac{P_{t} \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma \cdot n_{E} \cdot I_{i}}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot k \cdot T_{0} \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{min} \cdot L_{s}}$$

This equation is the **engineering-grade Modified Radar Range Equation** tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Insight Table

| **Modification** | **Physical Reason** | **Effect on Range** |
|---|---|---|
| Add kT₀B | Thermal noise in receiver | Reduces Rmax |
| Add F (Noise Figure) | Receiver internal noise | Reduces Rmax |
| Add (S/N)min | Required detection quality | Reduces Rmax |
| Add Ls | Real-world losses | Reduces Rmax |
| Add nE · Ii | Multiple pulse integration | Increases Rmax |
| Use G² | Same antenna for Tx and Rx | Doubled gain benefit |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The R⁴ dependence means that **doubling the transmitted power** increases the range by only a factor of 2^(1/4) ≈ 1.189 (a 19% increase). This is why radar engineers focus on improving the noise figure and integration techniques — they yield much larger gains for the same cost.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram – Modified Radar Range Equation Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PT - Transmitted Power] --> B[Transmit Path Gain G]
    B --> C[Two-Way Propagation Loss 1 over 4 pi R squared]
    C --> D[Target RCS sigma]
    D --> E[Receive Path Gain G]
    E --> F[PR - Received Echo Power]
    F --> H{SNR Comparator}
    I[k T0 B F - Noise Power Floor] --> H
    J[SNR min - Detection Threshold] --> H
    K[Ls - System Losses] --> H
    L[nE - Duty Cycle and Ii - Integration] --> H
    H --> M[Detection Decision]
    M --> N[Target Detected at Range Rmax]
    H --> O[No Detection - Out of Range]
    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style I fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style H fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
    style M fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
    style N fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style O fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
```

### 4.2 Component-Level Block Diagram – Link-Budget View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX[Transmitter Chain]
        TX1[PT - Power Amplifier] --> TX2[Antenna Gain G]
    end
    subgraph CH[Channel - Free Space]
        CH1[Path Loss 1 over 4 pi R squared] --> CH2[Target Scattering sigma]
        CH2 --> CH3[Return Path Loss 1 over 4 pi R squared]
    end
    subgraph RX[Receiver Chain]
        RX1[Antenna Gain G] --> RX2[Noise Figure F]
        RX2 --> RX3[Bandwidth B - Filter]
        RX3 --> RX4[Integration M pulses]
        RX4 --> RX5[Threshold Detector]
    end
    TX --> CH
    CH --> RX
    RX5 --> DEC{Detection Yes or No}
    style TX fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style CH fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style RX fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style DEC fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

### 4.3 Term Dependency Graph (Conceptual Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BASE[Classical Radar Range Equation] --> NOISE[Add k T0 B - Noise Term]
    BASE --> NF[Add F - Noise Figure]
    NOISE --> LOSS[Add Ls - System Losses]
    NF --> LOSS
    LOSS --> INT[Add nE and Ii - Integration]
    INT --> FINAL[Modified Radar Range Equation - Complete Form]
    style BASE fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style FINAL fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:3px
    style INT fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first diagram shows the *signal-flow architecture* from transmitter to detection decision.
> - The second diagram visualizes the *link budget* as a chain of system blocks.
> - The third diagram shows the *hierarchical evolution* from the classical equation to the modified form.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define the Modified Radar Range Equation and explain why the standard radar range equation must be modified for practical applications.**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Modified Radar Range Equation is the practical form of the classical radar range equation that incorporates **receiver noise, noise figure, system losses, and pulse integration effects** to predict the maximum detectable range of a radar under real operating conditions.

The standard equation **P_r = (P_t · G² · λ² · σ) / (4π)³ · R⁴** only computes the received signal power but does not account for the noise floor of the receiver. In practice, the received echo must exceed the receiver's noise power (kT₀BF) by a minimum SNR ratio. Hence, the equation is **modified** to include terms for noise, losses, and integration to provide a realistic estimate of maximum range.

---

#### **Q2. What is the role of the Noise Figure (F) in the Modified Radar Range Equation?**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2025 Pattern]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Noise Figure (F)** is a dimensionless factor (linear, not in dB) that quantifies the **degradation of the signal-to-noise ratio** caused by the receiver's internal noise generation. It is defined as:

$$F = \frac{(S/N)_{in}}{(S/N)_{out}}$$

In the modified radar range equation, F appears in the denominator as a multiplicative factor along with the thermal noise term (kT₀B). A higher F (worse receiver) reduces the maximum detectable range. An **ideal noiseless receiver** has F = 1, while practical radar receivers have F = 2 to 10 (3 dB to 10 dB).

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student must answer **ONE** of the two alternatives provided.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2025 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the Modified Radar Range Equation starting from the classical radar range equation, clearly showing the inclusion of receiver noise, noise figure, system losses, and pulse integration. State all standard assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pulsed radar operates at 10 GHz with peak transmitted power of 1 MW, antenna gain of 40 dB, target RCS of 1 m², receiver noise figure of 5 dB, and receiver bandwidth of 1 MHz. The minimum SNR required is 13 dB. If 100 pulses are integrated coherently, calculate the maximum detectable range. Assume system losses of 10 dB. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION – Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Start with the classical monostatic radar equation* (1 mark):

$$P_{r} = \frac{P_{t} \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot R^{4}}$$

*Introduce the noise floor* (1 mark): Thermal noise at receiver input is **N = k · T₀ · B**, where k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K, T₀ = 290 K, and B is the receiver bandwidth.

*Apply the Noise Figure* (1 mark): The effective noise at the receiver output is **N_eff = k · T₀ · B · F**, where F is the linear noise figure.

*Define minimum detectable signal* (1 mark): **S_min = (S/N)_min · k · T₀ · B · F**.

*Equate echo power to S_min* (1 mark):

$$\frac{P_{t} \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot R_{max}^{4}} = k \cdot T_{0} \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{min}$$

*Solve for Rmax* (1 mark):

$$R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_{t} \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot k \cdot T_{0} \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$$

*Include system losses and integration* (1 mark):

$$R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_{t} \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma \cdot n_{E} \cdot I_{i}}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot k \cdot T_{0} \cdot B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{min} \cdot L_{s}}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Assumptions:** Point target, monostatic radar, far-field conditions, free-space propagation, matched filter receiver, and integration of identical pulses.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**

- f = 10 GHz → λ = c/f = 3 × 10⁸ / 10 × 10⁹ = 0.03 m
- P_t = 1 MW = 10⁶ W
- G = 40 dB → Linear G = 10^(40/10) = 10⁴
- σ = 1 m²
- F = 5 dB → Linear F = 10^(5/10) ≈ 3.162
- B = 1 MHz = 10⁶ Hz
- (S/N)_min = 13 dB → Linear (S/N)_min = 10^(13/10) ≈ 19.95
- L_s = 10 dB → Linear L_s = 10^(10/10) = 10
- M = 100 pulses, coherent integration → I_i = 100
- Assume n_E = 1 (100% duty)

**Substitute into the formula (1 mark):**

$$R_{max}^{4} = \frac{10^{6} \cdot (10^{4})^{2} \cdot (0.03)^{2} \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \cdot 100}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 290 \cdot 10^{6} \cdot 3.162 \cdot 19.95 \cdot 10}$$

**Compute numerator (1 mark):**

$$Num = 10^{6} \cdot 10^{8} \cdot 9 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 100 = 9 \times 10^{12}$$

**Compute denominator (1 mark):**

$$(4\pi)^{3} = (12.566)^{3} \approx 1984$$

$$1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 290 = 4.002 \times 10^{-21}$$

$$4.002 \times 10^{-21} \cdot 10^{6} = 4.002 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$4.002 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 3.162 \cdot 19.95 \cdot 10 = 2.525 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$Den = 1984 \cdot 2.525 \times 10^{-12} \approx 5.01 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Compute Rmax⁴ (1 mark):**

$$R_{max}^{4} = \frac{9 \times 10^{12}}{5.01 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.797 \times 10^{21}$$

**Take fourth root (1 mark):**

$$R_{max} = (1.797 \times 10^{21})^{1/4}$$

Computing step-by-step:
- √(1.797 × 10²¹) = 4.239 × 10¹⁰
- √(4.239 × 10¹⁰) = 2.059 × 10⁵

$$R_{max} \approx 2.06 \times 10^{5} \text{ m} = 206 \text{ km}$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** **Rmax ≈ 206 km**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the significance of **pulse integration** in the Modified Radar Range Equation. Differentiate between coherent and non-coherent integration with relevant equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A radar system has the following parameters: P_t = 500 kW, f = 5 GHz, G = 30 dB, σ = 2 m², F = 3 dB, B = 2 MHz, (S/N)_min = 10 dB, L_s = 6 dB. If the radar integrates 50 pulses non-coherently, determine the maximum range. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION – Question B:**

**(a) Pulse Integration Discussion (7 marks):**

*Definition (1 mark):* Pulse integration is the technique of accumulating the returns from multiple pulses to improve the SNR before threshold detection.

*Significance (1 mark):* It directly increases the maximum detectable range by boosting the effective SNR without requiring higher transmitted power.

*Coherent Integration (2 marks):* Retains phase information; provides **M-fold SNR improvement**, where M is the number of pulses.

$$I_{i,coh} = M$$

*Non-coherent Integration (2 marks):* Discards phase; only magnitudes are summed. Improvement is approximately:

$$I_{i,non-coh} \approx \sqrt{M}$$

*Comparison (1 mark):* Coherent integration is more efficient but requires phase coherence and complex hardware. Non-coherent integration is simpler but yields lower improvement. The Modified Radar Range Equation uses the factor **nE · Ii** in the numerator to account for this gain.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**

- P_t = 5 × 10⁵ W
- f = 5 × 10⁹ Hz → λ = 3 × 10⁸ / 5 × 10⁹ = 0.06 m
- G = 30 dB → G = 10³ = 1000
- σ = 2 m²
- F = 3 dB → F = 10^(0.3) ≈ 1.995
- B = 2 × 10⁶ Hz
- (S/N)_min = 10 dB → (S/N)_min = 10
- L_s = 6 dB → L_s = 10^(0.6) ≈ 3.981
- M = 50, non-coherent → Ii = √50 ≈ 7.07

**Substitute (1 mark):**

$$R_{max}^{4} = \frac{5 \times 10^{5} \cdot (10^{3})^{2} \cdot (0.06)^{2} \cdot 2 \cdot 7.07}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 290 \cdot 2 \times 10^{6} \cdot 1.995 \cdot 10 \cdot 3.981}$$

**Numerator (1 mark):**

$$Num = 5 \times 10^{5} \cdot 10^{6} \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2 \cdot 7.07 = 2.545 \times 10^{10}$$

**Denominator (1 mark):**

$$1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 290 \cdot 2 \times 10^{6} = 8.004 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$8.004 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.995 \cdot 10 \cdot 3.981 = 6.355 \times 10^{-13}$$

$$Den = 1984 \cdot 6.355 \times 10^{-13} \approx 1.261 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Compute Rmax⁴ (1 mark):**

$$R_{max}^{4} = \frac{2.545 \times 10^{10}}{1.261 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.018 \times 10^{19}$$

**Fourth root (1 mark):**

- √(2.018 × 10¹⁹) = 4.493 × 10⁹
- √(4.493 × 10⁹) = 6.703 × 10⁴

$$R_{max} \approx 6.7 \times 10^{4} \text{ m} = 67 \text{ km}$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** **Rmax ≈ 67 km**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **dB vs. Linear Confusion:** Students often forget to convert dB values to linear before substituting. Noise figure, antenna gain, and SNR **MUST** be linear in the formula.
> 2. **Wavelength Error:** Using wavelength in cm or mm inconsistently. Always convert to **meters** before substituting.
> 3. **Forgetting G²:** For monostatic radar, gain is squared. Using G instead of G² gives a range error of a factor of √2.
> 4. **Missing (4π)³:** Dropping the 4π³ factor is a frequent 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Integration Factor:** Using Ii = M even for non-coherent integration. Use **√M** for non-coherent.
> 6. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing MHz with Hz or km with m. The fourth root will explode if units are mixed.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Modified Radar Range Equation** extends the classical form by adding **kT₀B**, **F**, **Ls**, and **(nE · Ii)** terms.
- ✅ The standard form is: **Rmax = [(P_t · G² · λ² · σ) / ((4π)³ · k · T₀ · B · F · (S/N)min · Ls)]^(1/4)**.
- ✅ With integration: **Multiply numerator by nE · Ii** to boost the range.
- ✅ **k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** and **T₀ = 290 K** are the constants to memorize.
- ✅ Always convert **dB to linear** using **10^(dB/10)** for power-related quantities.
- ✅ **R⁴ dependence** means doubling P_t gives only a **19%** increase in range.
- ✅ Coherent integration is **M times** more effective than non-coherent for the same number of pulses.
- ✅ The modified equation is used in **link-budget analysis** for all radar systems — from automotive to military.
- ✅ Always include **system losses (Ls)** — forgetting them gives an overly optimistic range prediction.
- ✅ **Minimum SNR** depends on Pd, Pfa, and detection scheme (e.g., Swerling target models).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Doppler Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Doppler Effect # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **PCECT751 – Satellite and Radar Communication**, the **Doppler Effect** refers to the apparent change (shift) in the observed frequency of an electromagnetic wave caused by the **relative radial motion** between the transmitter and the receiver. When the relative distance between them is decreasing, the received frequency rises; when it is increasing, the received frequency falls. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 Definition** > The Doppler shift $f_d$ is the difference between the transmitted frequency $f_0$ and the received (echo) frequency $f_r$, generated purely by **target/source radial velocity** $v_r$ along the line of sight. In radar (two-way propagation), the shift is **doubled** because the signal traverses the path twice. The phenomenon is formally captured by the equations: $$f_r = f_0 \left( \frac{c \pm v_r}{c \mp v_s} \right)$$ where $c \approx \mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \; m/s}$ is the speed of light, $v_s$ is the source velocity, and $v_r$ is the receiver/target velocity (positive for approach, negative for recession). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Picture (The "Ambulance Siren" Analogy)** > > Imagine standing on a roadside as an ambulance passes by. As it approaches, the siren sounds **high-pitched** because successive sound wave crests are squeezed closer together. As it recedes, the pitch **drops** because the crests are stretched apart. The ambulance's actual siren frequency never changed — only your **perceived** frequency did, because of relative motion. > > In radar, the **target plays the role of the moving siren**: it receives a wave at one frequency and re-radiates (reflects) it back. If the target is moving toward the radar, the returned echo has a slightly higher frequency. The radar then mixes this echo with a reference to extract the small **Doppler frequency $f_d$**, which is directly proportional to the target's radial velocity. ## 1.3 Why Doppler Effect Matters in SatCom & Radar | Domain | Engineering Utility of Doppler Effect | |---|---| | **CW Doppler Radar** | Detects moving targets (vehicles, humans) and measures radial velocity | | **MTI Radar** | Filters out stationary clutter (buildings, land) using Doppler discrimination | | **Police Speed Gun** | Computes vehicle speed as $v = \dfrac{c \, f_d}{2 f_0}$ | | **LEO Satellites (Iridium, Starlink)** | Massive Doppler shifts (up to ±48 kHz at L-band) require real-time pre-compensation | | **GPS Navigation** | Relativistic + classical Doppler must be corrected for accurate ranging | | **Doppler Weather Radar** | Measures precipitation radial velocity to predict storms | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note**: Doppler-based velocity extraction is the **second primary function** of a radar (range being the first). In satellite systems, the **inverse Doppler problem** is solved by the receiver's AFC (Automatic Frequency Control) loop to keep the carrier locked. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown 1. **Transmitter emits a CW (Continuous Wave) signal** at carrier frequency $f_0$. No modulation is needed for pure Doppler extraction. 2. **Signal propagates** through free space at speed $c$ toward the target. 3. **Target intercepts the wave** and, in the target's reference frame, observes a shifted frequency due to the target's own radial motion $v_r$ (this is the first Doppler compression/expansion). 4. **Target re-radiates** (passive reflection for cooperative targets, or active transponder response). The reflected wave carries the shifted frequency back to the radar. 5. **Radar receives the echo** at frequency $f_r = f_0 + f_d$. The same relative motion again shifts the frequency, producing the **second** Doppler effect. 6. **Receiver mixer** beats the received echo $f_r$ with a sample of the transmitted $f_0$, extracting the **Doppler audio frequency** $f_d$, which is in the audible range (Hz to kHz) for typical radar targets. 7. **Velocity computation**: $v_r = \dfrac{c \, f_d}{2 f_0}$, since each leg of the journey contributes equally. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" of the Factor of 2 in Radar** > Radar is a **two-way** system — the wave hits the target, then bounces back. Each one-way journey adds a Doppler shift of $\Delta f$. Because the target is the "receiver" on the way out and the "transmitter" on the way back, both shifts are equal and additive, hence the **$2 \times$** factor. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table** — these equations cover ~80% of KTU Doppler questions. | # | Formula | Description / Application | Units / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f_d = f_0 \cdot \dfrac{v_r}{c}$ | One-way Doppler shift (small velocity approximation) | Valid when $v_r \ll c$ | | 2 | $f_d = 2 \cdot \dfrac{v_r}{\lambda}$ | **Radar Doppler shift** (two-way, fundamental) | $\lambda = c / f_0$ | | 3 | $f_d = 2 \cdot \dfrac{v_r \cdot f_0}{c}$ | Alternate form using frequency directly | Hz | | 4 | $v_r = \dfrac{c \cdot f_d}{2 f_0}$ | Radial velocity from measured Doppler | m/s | | 5 | $f_{d,\max} = f_0 \cdot \dfrac{v_{\text{orbital}}}{c}$ | Max Doppler for a LEO satellite at closest approach | Hz | | 6 | $f_d = f_0 \cdot \dfrac{v_s \cos\theta}{c}$ | Satellite Doppler (with elevation angle $\theta$) | $\theta$ from radial | | 7 | $\Delta f_d = f_0 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta v}{c}$ | Doppler rate of change for non-circular pass | Hz/s | | 8 | $f_r = f_0 \pm f_d$ | Received frequency: **+** for approach, **−** for recession | Hz | | 9 | $S = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{f_d}{f_0} \right)$ | Doppler ratio in dB (rarely used, but appears in analysis) | dB | **Physical Constants to Memorize:** - Speed of light: $c = \mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \; m/s}$ - Speed of sound: $a = \mathbf{343 \; m/s}$ (for acoustic doppler only) - Standard radar X-band: $f_0 = \mathbf{10 \; GHz} \Rightarrow \lambda = \mathbf{0.03 \; m = 3 \; cm}$ - LEO orbital speed: $v_{\text{orb}} \approx \mathbf{7.8 \; km/s}$ ## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Traffic Radars**: A police radar at 10.5 GHz detects $f_d = 707$ Hz for a 36 km/h (10 m/s) car — well within audio detection range. - **Starlink Satellites** orbit at ~550 km with $v \approx 7.6$ km/s. At 12 GHz Ku-band downlink, $f_{d,\max} \approx 304$ kHz — the user terminal must track and compensate in real time. - **Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)**: Exploits platform Doppler history to synthesize a large antenna aperture. - **Air Traffic Control (ATC) Radars**: Use Doppler to discriminate aircraft from ground clutter at airports. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Doppler Shift (One-Way, Classical) Consider a transmitter at rest emitting at wavelength $\lambda_0$ and frequency $f_0 = c / \lambda_0$. **Step 1** — Number of crests emitted per second by the stationary source: $$N_{\text{emit}} = f_0 \; \text{cycles per second}$$ **Step 2** — Consider the source now moving with velocity $v_s$ **toward** the receiver. In one second, the source moves a distance $v_s$ closer to the receiver. The last crest of that second is now only a distance $(c - v_s) \cdot T_0$ behind the source, where $T_0 = 1/f_0$ is the period. **Step 3** — All $f_0$ crests of that second are now compressed into a spatial length of $(c - v_s)/f_0$. The new wavelength observed is: $$\lambda_r = \frac{c - v_s}{f_0}$$ **Step 4** — The observed frequency at the (stationary) receiver is the wave speed divided by the new wavelength: $$f_r = \frac{c}{\lambda_r} = \frac{c}{(c - v_s)/f_0} = f_0 \cdot \frac{c}{c - v_s}$$ **Step 5** — Using the identity $\dfrac{1}{1 - v_s/c} = 1 + \dfrac{v_s}{c} + \left(\dfrac{v_s}{c}\right)^2 + \ldots$, and the fact that $v_s \ll c$ for typical radar targets (e.g., 100 m/s vs $3 \times 10^8$ m/s): $$f_r \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_s}{c}\right)$$ **Step 6** — The Doppler shift is therefore: $$f_d = f_r - f_0 \approx f_0 \cdot \frac{v_s}{c}$$ This is the **one-way** non-relativistic Doppler formula. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Radar Doppler Shift (Two-Way, Monostatic) For a monostatic radar (transmitter and receiver co-located) and a target moving with radial velocity $v_r$: **Step 1** — The wave emitted by the radar at $f_0$ hits the target. From the target's reference frame, the incident wave has frequency: $$f_1 = f_0 \cdot \frac{c}{c - v_r} \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)$$ **Step 2** — The target re-radiates (reflects) at $f_1$ back toward the radar. Now the radar acts as a receiver moving *toward* the approaching wave (because the target is approaching, the wave-front spacing as seen by the radar is further compressed): $$f_2 = f_1 \cdot \frac{c}{c - v_r} \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)^2$$ **Step 3** — Expand the square using the binomial approximation $(1 + x)^2 \approx 1 + 2x$ for small $x = v_r/c$: $$f_2 \approx f_0 \left(1 + 2 \frac{v_r}{c}\right)$$ **Step 4** — Therefore, the round-trip Doppler shift is: $$f_d = f_2 - f_0 = 2 f_0 \cdot \frac{v_r}{c}$$ **Step 5** — Substituting $c = f_0 \lambda$, the most compact form is: $$\boxed{f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}}$$ This is the **most-quoted formula in KTU Module 4** for radar Doppler problems. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Satellite Doppler (LEO with Elevation Angle) For a LEO satellite moving with orbital velocity $v_s$ at elevation angle $\theta$ from the ground station: **Step 1** — The radial component of the satellite's velocity along the line of sight to the ground station is: $$v_r = v_s \cos\theta$$ **Step 2** — The one-way Doppler shift is: $$f_d = f_0 \cdot \frac{v_s \cos\theta}{c}$$ **Step 3** — Maximum Doppler occurs when $\theta = 0°$ (satellite directly overhead, moving tangentially — actually, for a horizontal pass this happens at the closest approach, where the velocity is fully radial): $$f_{d,\max} = \frac{f_0 \cdot v_s}{c}$$ **Step 4** — Numerical example: A LEO satellite with $v_s = 7.5 \; \text{km/s}$ transmitting at $f_0 = 1.6 \; \text{GHz}$ (GPS L1): $$f_{d,\max} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^9 \times 7.5 \times 10^3}{3 \times 10^8} = 40{,}000 \; \text{Hz} = 40 \; \text{kHz}$$ **Step 5** — Doppler rate (rate of change of Doppler with time) for a circular LEO orbit at constant $v_s$: $$\frac{df_d}{dt} = -\frac{f_0 \cdot v_s^2 \sin\theta}{c \cdot R_{\text{range}}}$$ This rate is what causes the rapid frequency drift in LEO satellite handovers. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Problem**: An X-band radar ($f_0 = 10 \; \text{GHz}$) tracks a target. The mixer output (audio Doppler) reads $f_d = 1.5 \; \text{kHz}$. Find: - (a) The wavelength of the transmitted signal - (b) The radial velocity of the target - (c) The frequency of the received echo **Solution**: (a) The wavelength: $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03 \; \text{m} = 3 \; \text{cm}$$ (b) Radial velocity using $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda$: $$v_r = \frac{f_d \cdot \lambda}{2} = \frac{1500 \times 0.03}{2} = 22.5 \; \text{m/s}$$ (c) Received echo frequency: $$f_r = f_0 + f_d = 10 \times 10^9 + 1500 \approx 10.0000015 \; \text{GHz}$$ The shift is **microscopic** relative to the carrier, but easily extractable with a stable reference oscillator. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Doppler Processing Block Diagram (CW Doppler Radar) The figure below illustrates the canonical architecture of a **Continuous-Wave (CW) Doppler Radar**, which is the simplest system that exploits the Doppler effect. The same waveform is transmitted and used as the local oscillator reference; the mixer extracts the small Doppler frequency $f_d$ from the received echo. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Oscillator
f0 Carrier] --> B[Power Amplifier
and Duplexer] B -->|Transmitted wave
at f0| C[Antenna
Radiating Element] C -->|Propagating EM wave
at speed c| D[Moving Target
radial velocity vr] D -->|Reflected echo
at fr = f0 plus fd| C C -->|Received echo| E[Low Noise Amplifier] E --> F[Mixer Multiplier
Reference f0] A --> F F -->|Beat product
at fd| G[Bandpass Filter
audio range] G --> H[Doppler Processor
and Display] H --> I[Radial Velocity
vr Output] H --> J[Audio Output
Headphones] classDef source fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 classDef path fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 classDef receive fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#2e8b57,color:#000 classDef out fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 class A,B source class C,D path class E,F,G receive class H,I,J out ``` **Reading the diagram**: - The **left branch** (yellow) is the transmit chain: oscillator → amplifier → antenna. - The **central branch** (blue) shows free-space propagation to the moving target and the return echo. - The **right branch** (green) is the receive chain: LNA → mixer (which multiplies echo with reference) → bandpass filter to isolate the audio Doppler tone. - The **red branch** shows the user outputs: numerical velocity and an audio monitor (the operator can literally *hear* the Doppler shift of a moving car). ## 4.2 Satellite Doppler Geometry Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Ground Station
on Earth Surface] -->|Line of sight
range R| B[LEO Satellite
at elevation angle theta] B -->|Orbital velocity
vs tangential| C[Velocity Vector
decomposed into components] C -->|Radial component
vr = vs cos theta| A C -->|Tangential component
vt = vs sin theta| D[Perpendicular to LOS] classDef sat fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 classDef gs fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 classDef vec fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#2e8b57,color:#000 classDef perp fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 class B,C sat class A gs class D perp ``` **Reading the diagram**: - The **ground station** (yellow) is the reference frame of the observer. - The **satellite** (blue) moves tangentially, but only the radial component of velocity (along the line of sight) causes Doppler shift. - The **velocity vector** (green) decomposes into a Doppler-producing radial part and a non-Doppler tangential part (red). - As the satellite passes overhead, $\theta$ changes from $0°$ to $90°$ to $180°$, causing the Doppler signature to **flip sign** at the point of closest approach (the "zero-Doppler" point). ## 4.3 MTI Radar Doppler Filter Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Radar Echoes
Clutter plus Target] --> B[Coherent Oscillator
Reference f0] B --> C[Phase Detector
I and Q Outputs] A --> C C --> D[Clutter Rejection Filter
Notch at 0 Hz] D --> E[Moving Target
Indicator Output] E --> F[Display Scope
or DSP] classDef in fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 classDef out fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#2e8b57,color:#000 class A,B in class C,D proc class E,F out ``` > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Mapping for MTI Radar** > Stationary clutter (buildings, trees) reflects at exactly $f_0$ → zero Doppler. Moving targets (aircraft) produce non-zero $f_d$ → they pass through the notch filter and appear on the display. This is **Doppler discrimination** in its purest form, a recurring KTU Module 4 topic. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: Define the Doppler Effect. State the formula for the Doppler frequency shift in a monostatic radar and explain the significance of the factor '2'.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Doppler Effect is the apparent change in the observed frequency of an electromagnetic wave when there is relative motion between the source and the observer. In a monostatic radar system, the Doppler frequency shift is given by: $$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$$ The factor **'2'** arises because the radar signal undergoes a **two-way journey**: the wave is first Doppler-shifted as it travels **from radar to target** (first leg), and then Doppler-shifted again as the reflected echo travels **from target back to radar** (second leg). Since both shifts are equal in magnitude and same in sign (for an approaching target), they add up, producing a total shift that is twice the one-way value. This factor must always be included in **radar** Doppler calculations; it is absent in one-way satellite links unless the satellite retransmits on a different frequency (a non-coherent transponder). --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: A radar operating at 10 GHz observes a Doppler shift of 2 kHz from a moving target. Calculate the radial velocity of the target.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Given**: $f_0 = 10 \; \text{GHz} = 10 \times 10^9 \; \text{Hz}$, $f_d = 2 \; \text{kHz} = 2000 \; \text{Hz}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$. **Step 1** — Compute wavelength: $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03 \; \text{m}$$ **Step 2** — Apply radar Doppler formula: $$v_r = \frac{f_d \cdot \lambda}{2} = \frac{2000 \times 0.03}{2} = 30 \; \text{m/s}$$ **Step 3** — Convert to km/h for clarity: $$v_r = 30 \times \frac{18}{5} = 108 \; \text{km/h}$$ The target is moving with a radial velocity of **30 m/s (108 km/h)** toward the radar. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Derive the expression for the Doppler frequency shift in a monostatic CW radar. State clearly the assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A police radar operates at 24 GHz (K-band). It measures a Doppler shift of 1.886 kHz from a car. Calculate: (i) the wavelength, (ii) the radial velocity of the car in m/s and km/h, and (iii) whether the car is approaching or receding. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Assumptions:** - The target velocity $v_r$ is much less than the speed of light ($v_r \ll c$), so relativistic effects can be ignored. - The target moves only along the radial (line-of-sight) direction; transverse motion produces no Doppler shift. - Free-space propagation, no atmospheric refraction. - Perfectly coherent radar (single oscillator serves both transmit and receive). **Derivation:** Consider a stationary radar transmitting at $f_0$ and a target moving with radial velocity $v_r$ toward the radar. **Step 1** — In the target's frame, the incident wave frequency is Doppler-shifted: $$f_1 = f_0 \left(\frac{c}{c - v_r}\right) \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)$$ **Step 2** — The target re-radiates at $f_1$ back to the radar. The radar, now acting as a moving receiver, sees the echo at: $$f_2 = f_1 \left(\frac{c + v_r}{c}\right) \approx f_1 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)$$ **Step 3** — Substituting $f_1$: $$f_2 \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)^2 \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{2 v_r}{c}\right)$$ (using the approximation $(1 + x)^2 \approx 1 + 2x$ for $x \ll 1$). **Step 4** — The Doppler shift is: $$f_d = f_2 - f_0 = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$ **Result:** $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ — the **fundamental radar Doppler equation**. **(Full 7 marks if all steps and assumptions are clearly written.)** --- **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** $f_0 = 24 \; \text{GHz} = 24 \times 10^9 \; \text{Hz}$, $f_d = 1.886 \; \text{kHz} = 1886 \; \text{Hz}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$. **(i) Wavelength:** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{24 \times 10^9} = 0.0125 \; \text{m} = 1.25 \; \text{cm} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ **(ii) Radial velocity:** $$v_r = \frac{f_d \cdot \lambda}{2} = \frac{1886 \times 0.0125}{2} = 11.79 \; \text{m/s}$$ Convert to km/h: $$v_r = 11.79 \times 3.6 = 42.44 \; \text{km/h} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$ **(iii) Direction:** By convention, a **positive** Doppler shift means the received frequency is **higher** than the transmitted. This occurs only when the target is **approaching** the radar. Hence, the car is **approaching** the police radar. **(2 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Doppler Problems** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2**: Students often write $v_r = f_d \cdot \lambda$ instead of $v_r = f_d \cdot \lambda / 2$. This is the **#1 reason for mark deduction** in radar Doppler problems. **Always** include the factor 2 for monostatic radar. > 2. **Unit errors**: Mixing GHz with kHz without converting to a common base (Hz). Always work in **base SI units** (Hz, m/s). > 3. **Sign confusion**: A positive $f_d$ ⇒ approach; negative $f_d$ ⇒ recession. Marks are lost if direction is not identified. > 4. **Using speed of sound (343 m/s)**: This is the *acoustic* Doppler constant. For *radar* and *satellites*, always use $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. > 5. **Omitting assumptions** in derivations: KTU 2024 explicitly tests the *reasoning* behind the approximations. Always state the small-velocity assumption. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a CW Doppler radar with a neat block diagram. Discuss its main limitation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A LEO satellite at an altitude of 600 km communicates with a ground station at 2.2 GHz (S-band uplink). The satellite's orbital velocity is 7.56 km/s. Assuming the satellite is at the point of closest approach (radial alignment), calculate: (i) the maximum Doppler shift, (ii) the received frequency if the transmitted is exactly 2.2 GHz, and (iii) comment on why this shift matters for a 64-QAM modulated carrier. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — CW Doppler Radar (7 marks):** **Principle of Operation:** A **Continuous-Wave (CW) Doppler Radar** transmits an unmodulated, uninterrupted sinusoidal carrier at frequency $f_0$. Because there is no pulse modulation, the radar **cannot measure range** (no time reference). However, it **can** measure the radial velocity of a target by extracting the small Doppler shift $f_d$ superimposed on the reflected echo. **Working:** - The transmitted signal $f_0$ serves as the **local oscillator** for the receiver mixer (the radar is fully coherent). - The received echo from a moving target is at $f_0 \pm f_d$. - The mixer multiplies echo and reference; the output is a beat note at $f_d$ (plus a high-frequency term filtered out). - The Doppler audio tone is amplified and presented to an operator through headphones or a frequency counter. - The target's radial velocity is computed as $v_r = f_d \cdot \lambda / 2$. **Block Diagram** (refer to SECTION_4.1 above for the Mermaid version): Oscillator → PA → Duplexer → Antenna → Target (radial $v_r$) → Echo → LNA → Mixer (with reference) → Audio Filter → Headphones / Counter. **Main Limitation:** - The CW Doppler radar **cannot measure target range** because there is no time marker (no pulse) on the transmitted waveform. Hence it cannot distinguish between two targets at the same Doppler frequency but at different distances. - It also suffers from **leakage** of the transmitted signal directly into the receiver (since the transmitter is always on), which can saturate the receiver unless isolation is very high. **(Full 7 marks with diagram)** --- **Part (b) — LEO Satellite Doppler (7 marks):** **Given:** $v_s = 7.56 \; \text{km/s} = 7560 \; \text{m/s}$, $f_0 = 2.2 \; \text{GHz} = 2.2 \times 10^9 \; \text{Hz}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$. **(i) Maximum Doppler shift:** $$f_{d,\max} = \frac{f_0 \cdot v_s}{c} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^9 \times 7560}{3 \times 10^8}$$ $$f_{d,\max} = \frac{1.6632 \times 10^{13}}{3 \times 10^8} = 55{,}440 \; \text{Hz} \approx 55.4 \; \text{kHz} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$ **(ii) Received frequency:** Since the satellite is at closest approach, the relative motion is **fully radial** and the receiver sees a higher frequency: $$f_r = f_0 + f_{d,\max} = 2{,}200{,}000{,}000 + 55{,}440 = 2{,}200{,}055{,}440 \; \text{Hz} \approx 2.20005544 \; \text{GHz} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ **(iii) Comment on 64-QAM carrier:** A 64-QAM signal has a symbol rate of, say, 1 Msymbol/s, occupying roughly 1 MHz of bandwidth. The carrier frequency itself must be locked to within a fraction of the symbol rate (typically $< 1\%$ of the symbol rate) for the demodulator to correctly interpret phase and amplitude. A Doppler shift of **55.4 kHz is ~5.5% of 1 MHz**, which is too large for the receiver carrier recovery loop to track without compensation. Therefore, the ground station must employ **Automatic Frequency Control (AFC)** or predict the Doppler using the satellite's orbital elements (TLE) and pre-compensate the local oscillator. Failure to correct this Doppler will cause severe **phase rotation** of the 64-QAM constellation, resulting in symbol errors. **(2 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Satellite vs Radar Doppler** > For **one-way** satellite links, there is **no factor of 2** in the Doppler shift (unless the satellite uses a frequency-translating transponder and the comparison is between the original uplink and the resulting downlink). For **two-way radar**, the factor of 2 is mandatory. Examiners often test this distinction in Module 4 problems. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Doppler Effect (Module 4)** - ✅ **Core formula (Radar)**: $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$ — always includes the **factor of 2**. - ✅ **Core formula (One-way SatCom)**: $f_d = \dfrac{v_r f_0}{c}$ — **no factor of 2** for direct reception. - ✅ **Velocity extraction**: $v_r = \dfrac{c \, f_d}{2 f_0}$ for radar. - ✅ **Direction sign convention**: $f_d > 0$ ⇒ target approaching; $f_d < 0$ ⇒ target receding. - ✅ **LEO Doppler magnitude**: typically **40 kHz at L-band (1.6 GHz)**, scales linearly with carrier frequency. - ✅ **Approximations valid when** $v_r \ll c$ — true for all radar targets (cars, aircraft, ships). - ✅ **CW Doppler radar limitation**: cannot measure range (no time marker). - ✅ **MTI radar principle**: stationary clutter at 0 Hz is filtered out; moving targets pass through. - ✅ **Speed of light**: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (radar/SatCom); $a = 343$ m/s is for *sound* only. - ✅ **X-band radar**: $f_0 \approx 10 \; \text{GHz}$, $\lambda = 3 \; \text{cm}$ — common KTU exam frequency. - ✅ **Derived quantity**: Doppler rate $\dfrac{df_d}{dt}$ matters for LEO handover timing. **Key Distinctions to Memorize:** | Parameter | Radar | Satellite (LEO) | |---|---|---| | Propagation | Two-way (Tx + Rx) | One-way (downlink) | | Factor of 2 | **Yes** | **No** | | Typical $f_d$ | Hz to kHz | kHz to hundreds of kHz | | Primary use | Velocity / MTI | Carrier recovery / AFC | | Range info | Yes (pulsed) | Yes (ranging) | **Mnemonic for the Master Formula**: **"Radar Rounds Twice"** — the signal goes out and comes back, so the Doppler shift is **twice** the one-way value.

topics.title: CW Radar - Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** CW Radar - Block Diagram # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of CW Radar ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Continuous Wave (CW) Radar** is a type of radar system that transmits a **continuous, uninterrupted electromagnetic wave** (typically a sinusoidal carrier at microwave/RF frequencies) and continuously receives the echo reflected from a target. Unlike pulsed radar, CW radar has **100% duty cycle**, meaning the transmitter never switches off while the system is operational. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A CW radar transmits and receives continuous wave signals simultaneously, relying on the **Doppler Effect** to detect moving targets. Since there is no time reference pulse, a simple CW radar can measure only the **radial velocity** of a target and not its range." The two principal variants covered in Module 4 are: | Variant | Measurement Capability | Modulation Type | |---|---|---| | **Simple CW Radar** | Velocity only | Unmodulated CW carrier | | **FM-CW Radar** | Range + Velocity | Frequency Modulated CW | ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Ambulance Siren":** When an ambulance approaches you, its siren pitch sounds *higher* than when it moves away. This pitch change is the **Doppler Effect**. A CW radar works on the same principle: it continuously sends out a pure tone (the siren), and when that tone bounces off a moving car, aircraft, or ship, the reflected tone comes back at a *slightly different pitch*. By comparing the transmitted and received pitches, the radar instantly knows **how fast** the target is moving — even in total darkness or fog. Think of the radar as throwing a musical note at a wall. If the wall is **stationary**, you hear the same note back. If the wall is **moving towards you**, the note comes back pitched up; if it moves **away**, it sounds lower. The frequency difference is proportional to the speed. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Speed of electromagnetic wave in free space:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ - **Doppler frequency shift** is the fundamental observable in CW radar - Typical CW radar operating frequencies: **X-band (8–12 GHz)**, **K-band (18–27 GHz)**, **W-band (75–110 GHz)** for automotive applications - Antenna isolation (leakage between Tx and Rx) is a critical physical metric, typically required to be **> 80 dB** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Doppler Shift When a CW radar transmits a pure sinusoidal signal of frequency $f_0$, the received signal reflected from a moving target undergoes a **frequency shift** proportional to the **radial velocity** of the target relative to the radar. This is the **Doppler Effect** applied to electromagnetic waves. ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. **Transmission Stage:** The CW oscillator (often a Gunn diode or Klystron) generates a stable sinusoidal carrier $f_0$ that is routed to the transmitting antenna. 2. **Propagation:** The wave travels to the target at speed $c$. 3. **Reflection:** The target acts as a secondary radiator. If the target is moving, it imposes a Doppler shift on the reflected wave. 4. **Reception:** The receiving antenna captures the Doppler-shifted echo. 5. **Heterodyning (Mixing):** The received signal is mixed with a sample of the transmitted signal in a **coherent detector (mixer)** to extract the small frequency difference — the Doppler frequency $f_d$. 6. **Display:** The Doppler frequency is processed and displayed as velocity on the radar screen or sent to a tracking system. > [!NOTE] > **Why mix with the transmitted signal?** The Doppler shift is a *tiny* frequency change compared to the carrier (e.g., a 10 GHz carrier shifted by only 1 kHz). It is impossible to measure such a tiny shift directly. By mixing, we translate the high microwave frequencies down to a manageable **audio-frequency Doppler tone** that can be amplified and analyzed. ## 2.2 Architectural Subsystems A CW radar block diagram contains the following functional blocks: - **CW Oscillator (Source):** Generates the carrier signal (Gunn / Klystron / Dielectric Resonator Oscillator). - **Power Divider (Directional Coupler):** Splits the CW signal into two paths — one to the Tx antenna and another to the mixer as the local oscillator (LO) reference. - **Transmitting Antenna:** Radiates the CW signal toward the target. - **Receiving Antenna:** Captures the weak reflected signal. - **Circulator / Duplexer (in monostatic systems):** Allows a single antenna to be used for both Tx and Rx by providing high isolation. - **Mixer (Coherent Detector):** Multiplies the received RF with the LO to produce the difference (Doppler) frequency at the IF output. - **IF Amplifier:** Amplifies the low-frequency Doppler signal to a usable level. - **Detector / Signal Processor:** Extracts the Doppler frequency and converts it to velocity. - **Display / Output:** Shows the radial velocity of detected targets. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they form 70% of CW Radar numerical problems in KTU exams.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | Condition / Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Doppler Frequency** | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$ | $v_r$ = radial velocity (m/s), $f_0$ = carrier freq (Hz), $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | Valid for $v_r \ll c$ | | 2 | Doppler in terms of wavelength | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength (m) | Equivalent form, useful at high freq | | 3 | **Radial Velocity** | $v_r = \dfrac{f_d \, \lambda}{2}$ | $f_d$ in Hz, $\lambda$ in m | Result in m/s | | 4 | Beat frequency (FM-CW) | $f_b = \dfrac{2 R \, \Delta f}{T_m \, c} = \dfrac{2 R \, f_m \, \Delta f}{c}$ | $R$ = range, $\Delta f$ = freq deviation, $T_m$ = mod period, $f_m$ = mod rate | For sawtooth / triangular modulation | | 5 | Maximum unambiguous range (FM-CW) | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \, \Delta f}$ | — | Set by frequency sweep width | | 6 | Range resolution (FM-CW) | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 \, \Delta f}$ | — | Directly tied to bandwidth | | 7 | Transmitted Power Average = Peak (CW) | $P_{avg} = P_{peak}$ | — | Always true for CW | | 8 | Mixer output IF frequency | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{Rx} - f_{LO} \vert$ | — | After heterodyning | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** CW Doppler radars are the heart of **police speed guns**, **anti-missile proximity fuses**, **athletic speed measurement**, **door openers**, **traffic enforcement**, **ballistic missile early-warning**, and modern **automotive adaptive cruise control (ACC)**. The block diagram in this module is the foundation of all such systems. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the CW Radar Doppler Frequency Equation ### Setup and Physical Model Let the radar be at the origin $O$ and the target be at a range $R(t)$ that varies with time. The transmitted CW signal is: $$ s_t(t) = A_t \cos(2 \pi f_0 t) $$ where $A_t$ is the amplitude and $f_0$ is the carrier frequency. ### Step 1 — Wave Travel to the Target The signal travels a distance $R(t)$ to reach the target. The time taken for the wavefront to reach the target is: $$ t_1 = \frac{R(t)}{c} $$ At the instant of reflection, the phase of the arriving signal is delayed by $\phi_1 = 2 \pi f_0 \, t_1$ relative to the transmitted phase. ### Step 2 — Reflection and Return Path The target reflects the wave (modeled as a perfect point scatterer with reflection coefficient $\sigma$). The reflected signal then travels back the same distance $R(t)$ to the radar. The total two-way propagation time is: $$ t_2 = \frac{2 R(t)}{c} $$ ### Step 3 — Phase of the Received Echo The instantaneous phase of the received echo is: $$ \phi_r(t) = 2 \pi f_0 \left( t - \frac{2 R(t)}{c} \right) $$ The received signal can therefore be written as: $$ s_r(t) = A_r \cos\!\left[ 2 \pi f_0 t - \frac{4 \pi f_0 R(t)}{c} \right] $$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Instantaneous Frequency The instantaneous frequency of a signal is the time derivative of its phase divided by $2\pi$: $$ f_{inst}(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[ 2 \pi f_0 t - \frac{4 \pi f_0 R(t)}{c} \right] $$ Expanding the derivative: $$ f_{inst}(t) = f_0 - \frac{2 f_0}{c} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$ ### Step 5 — Identify the Doppler Frequency The rate of change of range $\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$ is by definition the **radial velocity** $v_r$ (with the convention that an approaching target gives a *negative* derivative, i.e., $v_r < 0$ for approach). The received signal frequency therefore differs from the transmitted by: $$ f_{inst} = f_0 - \frac{2 f_0 v_r}{c} $$ The **Doppler frequency shift** is the magnitude of this difference: $$ f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} $$ Using $c = f_0 \lambda$, this is equivalently: $$ f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} $$ Both forms are the canonical KTU results. ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A CW police radar operates at **10.525 GHz**. A car is approaching the radar at a speed of **108 km/h**. Calculate (a) the Doppler frequency, and (b) the Doppler frequency if the car moves away at the same speed. (Assume $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.) ### (a) Approaching Car **Step 1 — Convert speed to SI units:** $$ v_r = 108 \, \tfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} = 108 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 30 \, \text{m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the Doppler formula:** $$ f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 \times 30 \times 10.525 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate:** $$ f_d = \frac{6.315 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^8} = 2105 \, \text{Hz} = 2.105 \, \text{kHz} $$ ### (b) Receding Car (same speed magnitude) For a receding target, $v_r$ is taken negative in the sign convention, but the **magnitude** of the Doppler shift is identical: $$ \vert f_d \vert = 2.105 \, \text{kHz} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Insight:** A common KTU trap is forgetting the factor of **2** in the numerator. The factor of 2 arises because the Doppler shift happens **twice** — once when the target "sees" the approaching wave, and again when the radar "sees" the approaching reflector. ## 3.3 FM-CW Beat Frequency Derivation (Sketch) For a triangularly modulated FM-CW radar with deviation $\Delta f$ and modulation period $T_m$, the beat frequency at the mixer output is: $$ f_b = \frac{2 R \, \Delta f}{T_m \, c} $$ For sawtooth modulation, only the up-ramp or down-ramp portion is used. The factor of $\Delta f$ in the numerator indicates that **larger frequency deviation gives larger beat frequency for the same range**, which improves SNR. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Simple Bistatic CW Radar > [!NOTE] > **Bistatic configuration** uses two physically separated antennas. A **monostatic CW radar** uses a single antenna with a circulator/duplexer for Tx/Rx isolation. The block diagram below illustrates the bistatic case (clearer for pedagogical purposes). ```mermaid graph LR osc[CW OSCILLATOR
Gunn Klystron DRO] --> div{POWER DIVIDER
Directional Coupler} div --> txant[TX ANTENNA
Radiates CW Wave] div --> mixer[MIXER
Coherent Detector] txant -.-> target[TARGET
Moving Reflector] target -.-> rxant[RX ANTENNA
Receives Echo] rxant --> mixer mixer --> ifamp[IF AMPLIFIER
Audio Band] ifamp --> det[DETECTOR
Frequency Discriminator] det --> disp[DISPLAY
Velocity Readout] det --> out[AUDIO OUTPUT
Speaker Headphones] ``` ### Block-by-Block Functional Description - **CW OSCILLATOR:** Produces a stable, low-noise continuous carrier (typical power: 10–100 mW for short-range police radar; up to kilowatts for military systems). - **POWER DIVIDER:** Splits the carrier into two coherent copies, ensuring the Tx and LO signals are phase-locked. - **TX ANTENNA:** Narrow-beam directional antenna (parabolic dish or horn) that radiates the CW wave toward the target. - **TARGET:** Any moving object (vehicle, aircraft, ship, weather scatterer). Stationary clutter reflects at the same frequency and is rejected. - **RX ANTENNA:** Captures the weak Doppler-shifted echo. - **MIXER:** Multiplies Rx and LO signals; output contains sum and difference frequencies. A low-pass filter retains only the difference (Doppler) frequency. - **IF AMPLIFIER:** Boosts the audio-frequency Doppler signal (typically 100 Hz to 100 kHz) to a usable level. - **DETECTOR:** Extracts the Doppler frequency value, usually via zero-crossing counting or FFT. - **DISPLAY:** Converts the Doppler frequency to a velocity reading in km/h or m/s. ## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Monostatic CW Radar with Circulator ```mermaid graph TD oscM[CW SOURCE] --> cir[CIRCULATOR
3 Port Ferrite Device] cir --> antM[SHARED ANTENNA
Tx and Rx] antM --> targetM[TARGET] targetM --> antM cir --> mixM[MIXER] oscM --> mixM mixM --> proc[SIGNAL PROCESSOR
FFT Filter Bank] proc --> vel[VELOCITY OUTPUT] proc --> clut[CLUTTER REJECTION
DC Notch Filter] clut --> vel ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why a circulator?** In a monostatic system, the transmitted signal power leaking directly into the receiver (antenna back-scatter, mismatches) can be **80 dB or more** stronger than the desired target echo.
