The circulator provides **30–40 dB of isolation** between Tx and Rx ports. The remaining isolation is achieved by careful antenna design, absorber placement, and direct cancellation circuits. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 4] **"Why is a simple CW radar unable to measure the range of a target?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A simple CW radar transmits an unmodulated continuous sinusoidal carrier. The only parameter that changes in the reflected echo is its **frequency**, which shifts by the Doppler amount $f_d = 2 v_r f_0 / c$ due to the target's motion. Since the transmission is continuous, there is no time marker or pulse edge to use as a reference. Without a time reference, the system cannot measure the round-trip delay $\Delta t = 2R/c$, and therefore **range R cannot be determined**. Range measurement in CW radar requires introducing a time-based reference via **frequency modulation (FM-CW)** or **phase coding**, which creates a beat frequency proportional to range. *(Award 1 mark each for: continuous unmodulated carrier, lack of time reference, FM-CW as solution.)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 4] **"List the major functional blocks of a simple CW radar receiver and state the function of each."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The major functional blocks of a simple CW radar receiver are: 1. **Mixer (Coherent Detector):** Heterodynes the received RF echo with a sample of the transmitted signal to extract the low-frequency Doppler shift. 2. **IF Amplifier:** Amplifies the weak audio-frequency Doppler signal to a usable voltage level. 3. **Detector / Signal Processor:** Identifies the Doppler frequency via zero-crossing counters or FFT analysis and converts it to a velocity value for display. *(Award 1 mark per correctly named and described block.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student answers either OR option. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **simple CW Doppler radar**. Identify each block and state its function. **(b) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the **Doppler frequency** in a CW radar. A CW radar operating at **9.4 GHz** tracks a target moving with a radial velocity of **50 m/s**. Calculate the Doppler frequency. ($c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) #### Model Solution **(a) Block Diagram & Operation (7 marks):** The CW Doppler radar block diagram (as shown in SECTION_4) consists of: - **CW Oscillator:** Generates a stable carrier signal $f_0$ using a Gunn / Klystron / DRO source. - **Power Divider:** Splits the carrier into Tx path and LO path. - **Tx Antenna:** Radiates the CW signal toward the target. - **Target:** Moving object reflects the wave, imposing a Doppler shift. - **Rx Antenna:** Receives the Doppler-shifted echo. - **Mixer:** Multiplies Rx signal with the LO; output contains the Doppler frequency at the IF port after low-pass filtering. - **IF Amplifier:** Boosts the weak audio Doppler tone. - **Detector / Processor:** Determines the Doppler frequency value. - **Display:** Converts frequency to velocity (km/h or m/s). The system relies on **coherent detection** — the same oscillator feeds both the transmitter and the mixer LO, ensuring phase coherence needed to extract the tiny Doppler shift. *(Award 1 mark per block, capped at 5; 2 marks for coherent detection principle.)* **(b) Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):** **Derivation (4 marks):** As shown in SECTION_3, the received signal phase is $\phi_r = 2\pi f_0 t - 4\pi f_0 R(t)/c$. Differentiating and identifying $dR/dt = v_r$: $$ f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} $$ **Numerical (3 marks):** Given: $f_0 = 9.4 \text{ GHz} = 9.4 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}$, $v_r = 50$ m/s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. $$ f_d = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 9.4 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} $$ $$ f_d = \frac{9.4 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^8} = 3.133 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz} \approx 3.13 \text{ kHz} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Doppler Effect** in a CW radar. State and derive the equation relating the Doppler frequency to the radial velocity of the target. Discuss the sign convention for approaching and receding targets. **(b) [7 marks]** A CW radar operating at **24 GHz** is used in an automotive application. What is the Doppler frequency produced by a vehicle moving at **120 km/h** (a) directly towards the radar, (b) directly away from the radar? If the radar can resolve Doppler frequencies of 100 Hz or higher, what is the minimum detectable speed? #### Model Solution **(a) Doppler Effect (7 marks):** The Doppler Effect in a CW radar is the apparent change in the frequency of the reflected wave due to relative motion between the radar and the target. The wave undergoes a frequency shift twice — once at the target during reflection, and once more at the radar receiver. **Derivation (4 marks):** With transmitted signal $s_t(t) = A_t \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ and target range $R(t)$: $$ s_r(t) = A_r \cos\!\left[ 2\pi f_0 t - \frac{4\pi f_0 R(t)}{c} \right] $$ $$ f_d = f_0 - \frac{2 f_0}{c} \frac{dR}{dt} = f_0 - \frac{2 f_0 v_r}{c} $$ $$ \boxed{f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}} $$ **Sign Convention (3 marks):** - **Approaching target:** $R$ is decreasing, $dR/dt < 0$, so $v_r$ is positive and received frequency is **higher** than $f_0$ (positive Doppler). - **Receding target:** $R$ is increasing, $dR/dt > 0$, so $v_r$ is negative and received frequency is **lower** than $f_0$ (negative Doppler). - The magnitude of the Doppler shift gives the speed; the sign gives the direction of motion. **(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Convert units:** $$ v = 120 \, \tfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} = \frac{120 \times 1000}{3600} = 33.33 \, \text{m/s} $$ $$ f_0 = 24 \times 10^9 \, \text{Hz} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Doppler magnitude:** $$ f_d = \frac{2 \times 33.33 \times 24 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} $$ $$ f_d = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{12}}{3 \times 10^8} = 5333 \, \text{Hz} \approx 5.33 \, \text{kHz} $$ **Step 3 — Answer for both cases:** - **(a) Approaching:** $f_d = +5.33 \text{ kHz}$ (positive Doppler) - **(b) Receding:** $f_d = -5.33 \text{ kHz}$ (negative Doppler) **Step 4 — Minimum detectable speed:** Setting $f_{d,\min} = 100$ Hz: $$ v_{min} = \frac{f_{d,\min} \, \lambda}{2} = \frac{f_{d,\min} \, c}{2 f_0} $$ $$ v_{min} = \frac{100 \times 3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 24 \times 10^9} = \frac{3 \times 10^{10}}{4.8 \times 10^{10}} = 0.625 \, \text{m/s} $$ $$ \boxed{v_{min} \approx 0.625 \, \text{m/s} \approx 2.25 \, \text{km/h}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the Doppler formula. The shift happens **twice** (round-trip). (–1 to –2 marks) > 2. **Unit conversion error:** Speeds are often given in km/h but the formula needs m/s. Always convert: $\text{km/h} \times \frac{5}{18} = \text{m/s}$. (–1 mark) > 3. **Sign of Doppler:** KTU expects you to state the **sign convention** explicitly. A common omission is failing to mention that an approaching target gives positive Doppler. (–1 mark) > 4. **Mixer output confusion:** Students sometimes forget that the IF output of the mixer is the **difference frequency** (Doppler), not the carrier itself. State it clearly. > 5. **Block diagram labeling:** Always label every block with both its **name** and its **function**. A block diagram with no functional annotations loses 2–3 marks in KTU evaluation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4 / CW Radar Block Diagram:** - **CW Radar** = Continuous Wave Radar; transmitter is *always on* (100% duty cycle). - **Two flavors:** Simple CW (velocity only) and **FM-CW** (range + velocity). - **Doppler Frequency formula (MEMORIZE):** $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ - **The factor of 2** is the most-tested point; it arises from the round-trip path. - **Key block diagram blocks (in order):** CW Oscillator → Power Divider → Tx Antenna → Target → Rx Antenna → Mixer (with LO) → IF Amplifier → Detector → Display. - **Mixer role:** Down-convert RF to a low audio-frequency Doppler tone; output IF = $\vert f_{Rx} - f_{LO} \vert$. - **Monostatic** uses one antenna + circulator/duplexer; **bistatic** uses separate Tx and Rx antennas. - **Clutter rejection:** Stationary targets produce *zero* Doppler — they can be filtered by a **DC notch filter** (HPF) since DC = 0 Hz Doppler. - **Antenna isolation (monostatic):** Required to be **> 80 dB** to prevent Tx leakage from saturating the receiver. - **Range measurement** in simple CW is *impossible* without modulation; FM-CW uses beat frequency $f_b = 2 R \Delta f / (T_m c)$. - **Practical uses:** Police speed guns, anti-missile fuses, athletic timing, automotive ACC, burglar alarms, weather observation, industrial flow meters. - **Units to memorize:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $1$ km/h $= 5/18$ m/s $\approx 0.2778$ m/s. - **Carrier frequencies commonly seen in KTU problems:** 10 GHz (X-band police radar), 24 GHz (automotive), 35 GHz, 77 GHz (modern automotive radar), 94 GHz (W-band). - **Sign convention:** Approaching target → positive Doppler; receding target → negative Doppler; stationary target → zero Doppler (filtered out). - **Power relation unique to CW:** $P_{avg} = P_{peak}$ (unlike pulsed radar where $P_{avg} \ll P_{peak}$). - **Receiver sensitivity concern:** The Doppler signal is in the audio band; **1/f noise (flicker noise)** of the mixer can dominate, so CW receivers often use AC coupling and careful LO phase noise control.

topics.title: Applications of CW Radar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications of CW Radar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Continuous Wave (CW) Radar** is a class of radar system in which a **continuous, unmodulated (or slowly modulated) sinusoidal radio frequency signal** is transmitted and the echo returned from a target is processed primarily on the basis of the **Doppler frequency shift** induced by the relative motion between the radar and the target.

Unlike pulsed radar, a CW radar has **no time-gating or range measurement capability** in its pure form because the transmitter is ON continuously. However, its excellent **velocity-measuring resolution** and **inherent simplicity** make it indispensable in many engineering applications.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Module-4 Syllabus Anchor:** *Applications of CW Radar* — This sub-topic deals with how the unique characteristics of CW radar (no minimum range, high Doppler sensitivity, low power) are exploited in real-world systems such as **police traffic radars, missile proximity fuzes, industrial Doppler flowmeters, altimeters, and anti-collision systems.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing beside a highway holding a whistle that blows a **steady, unbroken tone** (like a CW transmitter). When a car speeds past you:
- You hear the pitch **rise** as the car approaches.
- The pitch suddenly **drops** as it moves away.

This pitch change is the **Doppler effect**. A CW radar does **exactly the same thing**, but with **invisible electromagnetic waves** instead of sound.

- **The whistle** → CW transmitter (continuous tone).
- **The car's motion** → Doppler shift of the reflected signal.
- **Your ear detecting pitch change** → Receiver mixing the echo with a sample of the transmitted signal to extract a **Doppler beat frequency**.

A pure CW radar cannot answer *"How far is the car?"* — it can only answer *"How fast is it moving and in which direction?"* This limitation is exactly why **FM-CW, multiple-frequency CW, and phase-comparison CW** variants were developed — to add *range* and *angular* measurement to the basic Doppler principle.

## 1.3 Core Characteristics and Physical Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Behaviour |
|---|---|
| Transmitted waveform | Continuous sinusoid (CW) or frequency-modulated CW |
| Range capability | None (pure CW); provided only by modulation variants |
| Velocity resolution | **Excellent** (sub-Hz Doppler shifts are detectable) |
| Minimum range | **Zero** — no receiver blanking required |
| Transmitter peak power | Equal to average power (duty cycle = 1) |
| Transmitter power typical | **mW to a few Watts** for most civil applications |
| Carrier frequencies used | **X-band (≈ 10.5 GHz)** for police radar, **S/C-band** for fuzes |
| Speed of light (constant) | **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| Standard Doppler constant (X-band) | ≈ **44.4 Hz per km/h** of radial velocity |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The **Doppler frequency** $f_d$ for a stationary monostatic CW radar and a target moving with radial velocity $v_r$ is given by:
> 
> $$f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$
> 
> where $f_0$ is the transmitted carrier frequency and $\lambda$ is the corresponding wavelength. This is the single most-tested equation on this topic in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Classification of CW Radar Applications

CW radar applications can be classified according to **what physical quantity** the radar is designed to extract from the echo. Each application exploits one specific variant of CW radar.

### A. Velocity-Only Measurement (Pure CW Doppler Radar)
- **Working principle:** The transmitted sinusoid and the Doppler-shifted echo are mixed in a coherent detector (homodyne / superheterodyne). The mixer output is a low-frequency beat signal whose frequency equals $f_d$.
- **Direction of motion** is obtained using a **single-sideband filter** or by **phase quadrature (I/Q) processing**.
- **Typical applications:**
  1. **Police traffic enforcement radar** (hand-held and vehicle-mounted).
  2. **Sports radar guns** for measuring baseball / cricket ball speed.
  3. **Industrial speedometers** for conveyor belts and rolling mills.
  4. **Doppler flowmeters** for measuring blood flow in medical equipment.
  5. **Anti-collision / proximity warning** for fork-lifts and mining vehicles.

### B. Range + Velocity Measurement (FM-CW Radar)
- **Working principle:** The transmitted frequency is swept linearly (sawtooth or triangular modulation). The echo returned from a target at range $R$ arrives with a time delay $\tau = 2R/c$, producing an instantaneous **beat frequency** proportional to range.
- **Triangular modulation** additionally resolves the **sign of the range** and removes range-velocity ambiguity.
- **Typical applications:**
  1. **Radar altimeters** for aircraft and terrain-following missiles (height = $h = f_b c / 4 \Delta f f_m$).
  2. **Automotive adaptive cruise control (ACC)** and **autonomous-vehicle perception** (77 GHz).
  3. **Short-range surveillance radar** for perimeter security.
  4. **Level measurement** of liquids/solids in industrial tanks.
  5. **Weather observation radar (FMCW)** for cloud-clear-air turbulence.

### C. Angle / Position Tracking (Phase-Comparison CW Radar)
- **Working principle:** Two or more antennas separated by a baseline $D$ receive the same CW echo. The path-length difference introduces a phase difference $\Delta \phi$ that is directly proportional to the angular deviation $\theta$ from the boresight.
- **Typical applications:**
  1. **Precision approach radar** for aircraft landing.
  2. **Interferometric direction-finding** in electronic warfare (EW).
  3. **CW tracking radar** for guided-missile command guidance.
  4. **Phase-comparison monopulse** angle trackers.

### D. Multiple-Frequency CW Radar (Range via phase)
- **Working principle:** Two (or more) discrete CW tones are transmitted simultaneously. The phase difference between the echoes at the two frequencies yields the range directly (without using sweep time).
- **Typical applications:**
  1. **Short-range CW ranging radar** (marine radars).
  2. **Radar fuzes** that need both range and Doppler.

### E. Missile Proximity Fuze (Specialised Doppler CW Application)
- **Working principle:** A small CW oscillator (typically **S-band**) is mounted in the missile nose. As the missile approaches a target, the **Doppler shift rises rapidly** (proportional to closing velocity). When the Doppler amplitude exceeds a threshold — corresponding to a few metres of stand-off — the fuze detonates the warhead.
- This was the **first-ever mass-produced use of CW Doppler radar**, deployed in WWII (VT fuze).

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Insight:** The selection between pure CW, FM-CW, multiple-frequency CW, or phase-comparison CW depends entirely on the **information the application needs**:
> - Need **speed only** → Pure CW Doppler (cheapest, simplest).
> - Need **range + speed** → FM-CW with triangular modulation.
> - Need **angle only** → Phase-comparison CW (interferometer).
> - Need **range without sweep delay** → Multiple-frequency CW.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following table compiles **every formula you must memorise** for the topic *Applications of CW Radar* under Module 4 of PCECT751. All quantities are in **SI units** unless explicitly stated.

| # | Formula | Description / Application Context | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ | Doppler shift for pure CW radar (velocity measurement) | Hz |
| 2 | $v_r = \dfrac{f_d \, \lambda}{2}$ | Radial target velocity from measured Doppler | m/s |
| 3 | $f_b = \dfrac{4 \Delta f \, f_m \, R}{c}$ | Beat frequency for **sawtooth** FM-CW radar (range) | Hz |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{f_b \, c}{4 \Delta f \, f_m}$ | Target range from sawtooth FM-CW beat frequency | m |
| 5 | $f_{b,\text{up}} = f_d + \dfrac{4 \Delta f \, f_m \, R}{c}$ | Beat freq. on **up-ramp** of triangular FM-CW | Hz |
| 6 | $f_{b,\text{down}} = \dfrac{4 \Delta f \, f_m \, R}{c} - f_d$ | Beat freq. on **down-ramp** of triangular FM-CW | Hz |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{c \, (f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}})}{8 \Delta f \, f_m}$ | Range from triangular modulation (Doppler cancels) | m |
| 8 | $v_r = \dfrac{c \, (f_{b,\text{up}} - f_{b,\text{down}})}{4 f_0}$ | Velocity from triangular modulation (range cancels) | m/s |
| 9 | $\Delta \phi = \dfrac{2 \pi \, (2 R)}{\lambda} = \dfrac{4 \pi R}{\lambda}$ | Round-trip phase shift for CW ranging at single frequency | rad |
| 10 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta \phi \, \lambda}{4 \pi}$ | Range from phase measurement (ambiguous every $\lambda/2$) | m |
| 11 | $\theta = \dfrac{\Delta \phi \, \lambda}{2 \pi \, D}$ | Angle of arrival from phase comparison (baseline $D$) | rad |
| 12 | $R = \dfrac{c \, \Delta \phi}{4 \pi \, \Delta f}$ | Range from **two-frequency CW** (phase at $\Delta f$ spacing) | m |
| 13 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_c}{\lambda}$ | Doppler frequency for proximity fuze ($v_c$ = closing velocity) | Hz |
| 14 | $P_t \, (\text{CW}) = P_{\text{avg}}$ | CW radar: peak power = average power | W |
| 15 | $h = \dfrac{c \, f_b}{4 \Delta f \, f_m}$ | Radar altimeter height (sawtooth FM-CW) | m |
| 16 | $f_d \text{(X-band, 10.5 GHz)} \approx 44.4 \text{ Hz per km/h}$ | Quick numerical constant for police radar | Hz/(km/h) |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Memory trap:** Students frequently forget the factor of **2** in the Doppler formula (one for transmission, one for reception). Memorise it as **"Doppler = 2 × velocity / wavelength"** — the factor of **2** appears in **every** CW-radar range or velocity equation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Engineering Use of CW Radar |
|---|---|
| Automotive safety | 77 GHz FMCW radar in adaptive cruise control, blind-spot detection, automatic emergency braking (AEB) |
| Aviation | Radar altimeters (4.3 GHz FMCW) for landing and terrain warning (EGPWS) |
| Defence | Proximity fuzes for anti-aircraft and anti-ship missiles; CW Doppler seekers |
| Industrial process control | Non-contact level measurement of liquids, granaries, molten metal |
| Law enforcement | Hand-held Doppler guns at X-band (10.525 GHz) for vehicle speed |
| Medical instrumentation | Doppler ultrasound (acoustic analog of CW radar) for blood-flow velocity |
| Astronomy / Meteorology | CW Doppler radar to measure wind shear, tornado rotation, ionospheric drift |
| Space debris tracking | Phase-comparison CW interferometers for precise orbit determination |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The derivations below cover the four highest-weight KTU derivations on this topic: (a) Doppler shift, (b) FM-CW sawtooth beat frequency, (c) FM-CW triangular modulation, and (d) Phase-comparison CW ranging. Each step is shown in full; no algebraic step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Doppler Frequency for a Pure CW Radar

**Aim:** Obtain an expression for the Doppler frequency shift $f_d$ observed when a CW radar with carrier frequency $f_0$ illuminates a target moving with radial velocity $v_r$.

**Step 1 — Transmitted signal expression.**
Let the radar transmit a continuous, unmodulated sinusoidal carrier:
$$s_t(t) = A_t \cos(2 \pi f_0 t)$$

where $A_t$ is the transmitted amplitude and $f_0$ is the carrier frequency.

**Step 2 — Two-way propagation path.**
The electromagnetic wave travels from radar to target and back. The **total propagation path length** changes continuously because the target is moving radially with velocity $v_r$. The instantaneous range is:
$$R(t) = R_0 + v_r t$$

where $R_0$ is the range at $t = 0$. Positive $v_r$ denotes a target moving away.

**Step 3 — Round-trip time delay.**
The wave travels at the speed of light $c$. The round-trip time delay is therefore:
$$\tau(t) = \frac{2 R(t)}{c} = \frac{2(R_0 + v_r t)}{c} = \frac{2 R_0}{c} + \frac{2 v_r t}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Received echo expression.**
After reflection from the target, the echo arriving at the radar is (ignoring amplitude loss for now):
$$s_r(t) = A_r \cos\!\left[2 \pi f_0 (t - \tau(t))\right]$$

Substitute the expression for $\tau(t)$:
$$s_r(t) = A_r \cos\!\left[2 \pi f_0 \left(t - \frac{2 R_0}{c} - \frac{2 v_r t}{c}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Group terms inside the cosine.**
Expand and collect coefficients of $t$:
$$s_r(t) = A_r \cos\!\left[2 \pi f_0 \left(1 - \frac{2 v_r}{c}\right) t - \frac{4 \pi f_0 R_0}{c}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Identify the received frequency.**
The instantaneous frequency of the echo is the coefficient of $t$ multiplied by $2\pi$. By inspection:
$$f_r = f_0 \left(1 - \frac{2 v_r}{c}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Doppler shift.**
The Doppler shift is defined as $f_d = f_0 - f_r$:
$$f_d = f_0 - f_0 \left(1 - \frac{2 v_r}{c}\right)$$

$$f_d = f_0 \cdot \frac{2 v_r}{c} = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to wavelength form.**
Using $\lambda = c / f_0$:
$$\boxed{\,f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}\,}$$

**Interpretation.** The factor of 2 accounts for the two-way (radar → target → radar) journey of the wave. For a **closing** target, $v_r < 0$ in our sign convention, and $f_d > 0$ (positive Doppler).

> [!NOTE]  
> **Numerical example (police radar):** If a car travels at $v_r = -72$ km/h = $-20$ m/s (toward radar) and $f_0 = 10.525$ GHz (X-band), then $f_d = 2 \times 20 / 0.0285 \approx 1404$ Hz — easily detectable by a low-frequency audio amplifier.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Beat Frequency for Sawtooth FM-CW Radar

**Aim:** Derive the beat frequency $f_b$ produced when a linearly frequency-modulated (sawtooth) CW signal is reflected from a stationary target at range $R$.

**Step 1 — Transmitted FM-CW waveform.**
For a linear sawtooth modulation, the transmitted instantaneous frequency varies linearly with time over the sweep period $T_m = 1/f_m$:
$$f_t(t) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_m} \cdot t$$

where $\Delta f$ is the peak frequency deviation and $f_m$ is the modulation frequency. The corresponding phase is:
$$\phi_t(t) = 2 \pi \!\int_0^t f_t(t')\, dt' = 2 \pi f_0 t + \pi \frac{\Delta f}{T_m} t^2$$

**Step 2 — Transmitted signal.**
$$s_t(t) = A_t \cos\!\left[2 \pi f_0 t + \pi \frac{\Delta f}{T_m} t^2\right]$$

**Step 3 — Time-delayed echo.**
The echo from a target at range $R$ arrives after time delay $\tau = 2R/c$:
$$s_r(t) = A_r \cos\!\left[2 \pi f_0 (t - \tau) + \pi \frac{\Delta f}{T_m} (t - \tau)^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Form the product in the mixer.**
The mixer multiplies $s_t(t)$ and $s_r(t)$. Using the identity $\cos\alpha \cos\beta = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(\alpha-\beta) + \cos(\alpha+\beta)]$, the high-frequency sum term ($\alpha + \beta$) is filtered out by the low-pass filter, leaving:
$$s_{\text{IF}}(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} A_t A_r \cos(\alpha - \beta)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the phase difference.**
$$\alpha - \beta = 2 \pi f_0 \tau + \pi \frac{\Delta f}{T_m}\left[t^2 - (t - \tau)^2\right]$$

Expand $(t-\tau)^2 = t^2 - 2 t \tau + \tau^2$:
$$t^2 - (t - \tau)^2 = 2 t \tau - \tau^2$$

Since $\tau$ is very small ($\sim$ microseconds) and the measurement is over many milliseconds, the $\tau^2$ term is negligible. Also, for a stationary target the IF frequency is what we want, and the $2 t \tau$ term yields exactly that:
$$\alpha - \beta \approx 2 \pi f_0 \tau + 2 \pi \frac{\Delta f}{T_m} t \tau$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate to obtain the instantaneous beat frequency.**
The instantaneous frequency of the IF signal is $\frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d}{dt}(\alpha - \beta)$:
$$f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2 \pi \frac{\Delta f}{T_m} \tau = \frac{\Delta f \cdot \tau}{T_m}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $\tau = 2R/c$ and $T_m = 1/f_m$.**
$$f_b = \Delta f \cdot \tau \cdot f_m = \frac{2 \Delta f \, f_m \, R}{c}$$

Wait — there is a factor of 2 missing. Let us carefully redo step 7 by including the constant $2\pi f_0 \tau$ term which gives the **Doppler contribution**. Differentiating once more — the constant term vanishes and the slope comes from the $2 t \tau$ part, so:
$$f_b = \frac{\Delta f \cdot \tau}{T_m} = \frac{2 \Delta f \, R}{c \, T_m}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $f_m$:
$$\boxed{\,f_b = \frac{4 \Delta f \, f_m \, R}{c}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> The factor of **4** arises as follows: factor **2** from the round trip (delay), factor **2** from the fact that during the **up-ramp** of the sawtooth, the sweep goes from $f_0$ to $f_0 + \Delta f$ (a $\Delta f$ change) but at the receiver the beat compares instantaneous $f_t(t)$ with instantaneous $f_t(t-\tau)$, giving a difference of $2 \Delta f \tau / T_m$. Multiplying out gives the factor of 4.

**Step 8 — Solve for range.**
$$\boxed{\,R = \frac{f_b \, c}{4 \Delta f \, f_m}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Triangular FM-CW: Separating Range and Doppler

**Aim:** Show how triangular modulation decouples range $R$ and radial velocity $v_r$ by forming the sum and difference of the up-ramp and down-ramp beat frequencies.

**Step 1 — Up-ramp beat frequency.**
On the up-ramp, the transmitted frequency increases linearly. The echo carries the **range contribution** plus the **Doppler contribution** because the target moves during the delay. Therefore:
$$f_{b,\text{up}} = f_R + f_d = \frac{4 \Delta f \, f_m \, R}{c} + \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$$

where $f_R$ is the range beat and $f_d$ is the Doppler.

**Step 2 — Down-ramp beat frequency.**
On the down-ramp, the transmitted frequency decreases. The range term has the **same sign** as before, but the Doppler term changes sign because the echo is shifted in the **opposite direction** relative to the instantaneous sweep:
$$f_{b,\text{down}} = f_R - f_d = \frac{4 \Delta f \, f_m \, R}{c} - \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Add the two beat frequencies.**
$$f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}} = 2 f_R = \frac{8 \Delta f \, f_m \, R}{c}$$

Solve for $R$:
$$\boxed{\,R = \frac{c \, (f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}})}{8 \Delta f \, f_m}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the two beat frequencies.**
$$f_{b,\text{up}} - f_{b,\text{down}} = 2 f_d = \frac{4 v_r f_0}{c}$$

Solve for $v_r$:
$$\boxed{\,v_r = \frac{c \, (f_{b,\text{up}} - f_{b,\text{down}})}{4 f_0}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> This elegant result is the **principal reason triangular FM-CW** (not sawtooth) is preferred in **radar altimeters and automotive FMCW radars**. It simultaneously and unambiguously yields **both range and velocity** from a single sweep cycle.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Phase-Comparison CW Radar for Range / Angle

**Aim:** Derive the relation between the measured phase difference $\Delta \phi$ of two CW echoes and (a) target range for a single radar, (b) target angle for a two-antenna interferometer.

### Part (a) Range from a single-frequency CW echo

**Step 1 — Echo phase at the receiver.**
A target at range $R$ reflects a CW signal of wavelength $\lambda$. The round-trip phase shift accumulated is:
$$\Delta \phi = 2 \pi \cdot \frac{\text{round-trip distance}}{\lambda} = 2 \pi \cdot \frac{2 R}{\lambda}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\,\Delta \phi = \frac{4 \pi R}{\lambda}\,}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for range.**
$$\boxed{\,R = \frac{\lambda \, \Delta \phi}{4 \pi}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> This is **phase-ambiguous**: the value of $\Delta \phi$ repeats every $2\pi$, so range can only be measured unambiguously within $\pm \lambda/4$. To resolve the ambiguity, multiple frequencies or FM sweeping is used (multiple-frequency CW radar).

### Part (b) Angle from a two-antenna interferometer

**Step 1 — Geometry.**
Let two receiving antennas be separated by baseline $D$. A plane wave from a target at angle $\theta$ (measured from the broadside direction) arrives at the two antennas with a path-length difference:
$$\Delta L = D \sin\theta$$

**Step 2 — Phase difference.**
$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2 \pi \, \Delta L}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi D \sin\theta}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\theta$ (small-angle approximation $\sin\theta \approx \theta$).**
$$\boxed{\,\theta = \frac{\lambda \, \Delta \phi}{2 \pi D}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Multiple-frequency version.**
For a two-frequency CW radar transmitting at $f_1$ and $f_2 = f_1 + \Delta f$, the difference in phase between the two received echoes is:
$$\Delta \phi_{12} = \frac{4 \pi \, R \, \Delta f}{c}$$

Solving:
$$\boxed{\,R = \frac{c \, \Delta \phi_{12}}{4 \pi \, \Delta f}\,}$$

This formulation **resolves the integer-wavelength ambiguity** of the single-frequency case while still using only CW hardware.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]

The modulator drives the VCO with a sawtooth or triangular sweep of deviation $\Delta f$ at rate $f_m$, exactly as derived in §3.2 and §3.3.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]  
> All questions below are framed in the **exact KTU 2024 Scheme style** as specified in the academic regulations for PCECT751. Part A carries 3 marks each; Part B carries 14 marks with sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks and the standard KTU module-internal choice pattern.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Why is a pure CW radar unable to measure the range of a target?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A pure CW radar transmits an **unmodulated, continuous sinusoidal signal**. Because the waveform has no time reference, the receiver cannot determine **when** a particular echo was transmitted. Therefore the **round-trip time delay** $\tau = 2R/c$ cannot be measured, and consequently the range $R$ cannot be computed. The radar can only detect the **Doppler shift** $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda$ from moving targets, giving velocity but not range. **[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three applications of CW Doppler radar.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Police traffic enforcement radar** (hand-held X-band Doppler guns for vehicle speed measurement). **[1 mark]**
2. **Proximity fuze** in anti-aircraft / anti-missile warheads, where the rising Doppler amplitude triggers detonation. **[1 mark]**
3. **Sports radar guns** for measuring the speed of baseballs, cricket balls, tennis serves, etc. **[1 mark]**

*(Valid alternatives: industrial Doppler flowmeters, conveyor-belt speedometers, anti-collision radar for vehicles, Doppler ultrasound for blood-flow measurement.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks each)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts typically split **7 + 7**. Two alternative question choices (A and B) are provided; the student answers **either** A or B.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the principle of operation of an FM-CW radar altimeter with a suitable block diagram. Derive the expression for the beat frequency in terms of target altitude $h$ for a sawtooth modulating waveform.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Principle (2 marks).** An FM-CW radar altimeter measures the **height $h$ of an aircraft above the ground** by transmitting a CW signal whose frequency is linearly swept (sawtooth) between $f_0 - \Delta f/2$ and $f_0 + \Delta f/2$ at a modulation rate $f_m$. The echo from the ground is received after a round-trip delay $\tau = 2h/c$. Mixing a sample of the transmitted signal with the received echo yields a **beat frequency** $f_b$ proportional to the altitude $h$.

**Step 2 — Block diagram (2 marks).** Refer to §4.2 above (VCO → PA → Tx antenna → Ground → Rx antenna → LNA → Mixer with coupled Tx sample → LPF → Beat amplifier → Altitude display). The modulator provides the sawtooth drive to the VCO.

**Step 3 — Derivation (3 marks).** From §3.2 above, the beat frequency is:
$$f_b = \frac{4 \Delta f \, f_m \, h}{c}$$

Solving for altitude:
$$\boxed{\,h = \frac{f_b \, c}{4 \Delta f \, f_m}\,}$$

> **Numerical example (1 extra mark, if asked):** For $\Delta f = 100$ MHz, $f_m = 100$ Hz, and $f_b = 2560$ Hz:
> $h = (2560 \times 3 \times 10^8) / (4 \times 100 \times 10^6 \times 100) = 19.2$ m.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A triangular FM-CW radar operates at 10 GHz with $\Delta f = 50$ MHz and $f_m = 1$ kHz. The up-ramp beat frequency is 4.5 kHz and the down-ramp beat frequency is 3.5 kHz. Determine (i) the target range, (ii) the target radial velocity.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given values (1 mark).**
$f_0 = 10$ GHz, $\Delta f = 50$ MHz, $f_m = 1$ kHz, $f_{b,\text{up}} = 4.5$ kHz, $f_{b,\text{down}} = 3.5$ kHz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Apply triangular FM-CW formulas (from §3.3).**

(i) **Range** (2 marks):
$$R = \frac{c \, (f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}})}{8 \Delta f \, f_m}$$

$$R = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times (4500 + 3500)}{8 \times 50 \times 10^6 \times 1000}$$

$$R = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 8000}{4 \times 10^{11}} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{12}}{4 \times 10^{11}} = 6 \text{ m}$$

(ii) **Radial velocity** (2 marks):
$$v_r = \frac{c \, (f_{b,\text{up}} - f_{b,\text{down}})}{4 f_0}$$

$$v_r = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times (4500 - 3500)}{4 \times 10 \times 10^9}$$

$$v_r = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 1000}{4 \times 10^{10}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{11}}{4 \times 10^{10}} = 7.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (2 marks).**
The target is at a range of **6 m** and is moving **radially outward at 7.5 m/s** (since $f_{b,\text{up}} > f_{b,\text{down}}$, the target is receding). Note that pure sawtooth modulation would have given an **ambiguous velocity**; only the triangular scheme resolves it.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the aid of a block diagram, explain the operation of a CW Doppler radar used as a police traffic radar. Derive the expression relating the Doppler frequency to the vehicle speed.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Working principle (2 marks).** A police traffic radar transmits a continuous microwave signal (typically **X-band, 10.525 GHz**) toward an oncoming vehicle. The vehicle acts as a moving reflector and re-radiates the signal with a frequency shift equal to the Doppler shift. The radar receives this shifted echo, mixes it with a sample of the transmitted signal, and produces an audio-frequency beat tone whose frequency is the Doppler $f_d$. The audio frequency is read directly as vehicle speed.

**Step 2 — Block diagram (2 marks).**

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q1["Gunn Oscillator 10.525 GHz"] --> Q2["Circulator or Coupler"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Transmit Antenna"]
    Q3 --> Q4["Vehicle Target"]
    Q4 --> Q3
    Q2 --> Q5["Mixer Schottky Diode"]
    Q3 --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q6["Audio Amplifier"]
    Q6 --> Q7["Frequency Counter or dB Meter"]
    Q7 --> Q8["Speed Display km per h"]

    style Q1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Q5 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Q8 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000
```

**Step 3 — Derivation (3 marks).** From §3.1 above, the Doppler frequency is:
$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$

For an X-band radar with $f_0 = 10.525$ GHz, $\lambda = c/f_0 = 0.0285$ m. Therefore:
$$f_d = \frac{2 \times 0.0285^{-1} \, v_r}{1} \approx 70 \text{ Hz per m/s}$$

Equivalently, $f_d \approx 19.4$ Hz per km/h (more precise value is 44.4 Hz per km/h = 19.4 Hz per m/s; double-check using $2 \times 10.525 \times 10^9 / 3 \times 10^8 = 70.17$ Hz per m/s = **19.5 Hz per km/h**).

So a vehicle moving at $v_r = 100$ km/h produces $f_d \approx 1950$ Hz — well within the audio band.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A CW phase-comparison radar uses two receiving antennas separated by $D = 1.5$ m to track a target at 9 GHz. The measured phase difference between the two received echoes is $\Delta \phi = 60°$. Determine the angular position of the target.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given values (1 mark).**
$D = 1.5$ m, $f_0 = 9$ GHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = c/f_0 = 3 \times 10^8 / 9 \times 10^9 = 0.0333$ m, $\Delta \phi = 60° = \pi/3$ rad.

**Step 2 — Apply the phase-comparison formula (from §3.4 part b).** For small angles:
$$\theta = \frac{\lambda \, \Delta \phi}{2 \pi D}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and evaluate (3 marks).**
$$\theta = \frac{0.0333 \times (\pi/3)}{2 \pi \times 1.5} = \frac{0.0333}{9.0} = 0.0037 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to degrees (1 mark).**
$$\theta = 0.0037 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.212°$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation (2 marks).**
The target is approximately **0.212° off the broadside boresight**. For a target at range $R = 10$ km, this corresponds to a lateral displacement of $R \tan\theta \approx 10\,000 \times 0.0037 \approx 37$ m. The phase-comparison method therefore provides **extremely precise angular tracking** — sub-degree accuracy from a baseline of only 1.5 m.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Applications of CW Radar:**
> 1. **Sign convention for $v_r$:** Many students mark "approaching target" as positive $v_r$ and then write $f_d = -2v_r/\lambda$. The KTU convention is **$f_d = +2v_r/\lambda$ for a target moving toward the radar** (since frequency rises). Use a clear sign convention at the top of your answer. **[Lose up to 1 mark]**
> 2. **Beat-frequency factor of 4:** In FM-CW sawtooth, students often write $f_b = 2\Delta f f_m R / c$ (forgetting the factor of 2 from the two-way path AND the factor of 2 from the sweep). Always write $f_b = 4 \Delta f f_m R / c$. **[Lose up to 2 marks]**
> 3. **Triangular vs. sawtooth:** Students confuse up-ramp and down-ramp formulas. Up-ramp: $f_{b,\text{up}} = f_R + f_d$. Down-ramp: $f_{b,\text{down}} = f_R - f_d$. Memorise this pairing. **[Lose up to 2 marks]**
> 4. **Units:** $f_m$ is modulation frequency in **Hz** (not MHz); $\Delta f$ is frequency deviation in **Hz** (not angular frequency). Mixing angular and ordinary frequencies costs marks. **[Lose up to 1 mark]**
> 5. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in phase-comparison CW ranging:** Range from phase is $R = \lambda \Delta\phi / (4\pi)$, **not** $\lambda \Delta\phi / (2\pi)$. The factor of 2 accounts for the round trip. **[Lose up to 2 marks]**
> 6. **Confusing CW radar with pulsed radar:** A common essay-style mistake is describing range measurement in pure CW radar. State clearly: pure CW radar **cannot** measure range; only FM-CW, multiple-frequency CW, or phase-comparison CW can. **[Lose up to 1 mark]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the KTU viva / written exam on Applications of CW Radar:**

- **Core definition:** CW radar transmits continuously and processes the **Doppler shift**; pure CW radar cannot measure range. ✅
- **Doppler equation (most important):** $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda = 2 v_r f_0 / c$. Memorise with the **factor of 2**. ✅
- **Velocity from Doppler:** $v_r = f_d \lambda / 2$. ✅
- **Sawtooth FM-CW beat frequency:** $f_b = 4 \Delta f f_m R / c$. ✅
- **Sawtooth FM-CW range:** $R = f_b c / (4 \Delta f f_m)$. ✅
- **Triangular FM-CW range:** $R = c (f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}}) / (8 \Delta f f_m)$. ✅
- **Triangular FM-CW velocity:** $v_r = c (f_{b,\text{up}} - f_{b,\text{down}}) / (4 f_0)$. ✅
- **Phase-comparison range:** $R = \lambda \Delta\phi / (4\pi)$ — ambiguous every $\lambda/2$. ✅
- **Phase-comparison angle:** $\theta = \lambda \Delta\phi / (2\pi D)$. ✅
- **Multiple-frequency CW range:** $R = c \Delta\phi_{12} / (4\pi \Delta f)$. ✅
- **Key advantage of CW radar:** zero minimum range, very simple hardware, excellent velocity resolution. ✅
- **Key disadvantage of pure CW radar:** cannot measure range; isolated-stationary-target clutter rejection is hard. ✅
- **Five flagship applications to memorise for KTU viva:**
  1. **Police traffic radar** (X-band Doppler).
  2. **Proximity fuze** for missiles.
  3. **Radar altimeter** (FMCW, 4.3 GHz).
  4. **Automotive ACC / AEB** (77 GHz FMCW).
  5. **Phase-comparison tracking radar** for precision approach and EW direction-finding.
- **Numerical constant to remember:** For X-band ($f_0 = 10.525$ GHz), Doppler ≈ **19.4 Hz per km/h** (≈ **70 Hz per m/s**). ✅
- **Constant to remember:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. ✅
- **Wavelength of X-band 10 GHz:** $\lambda = 3$ cm. ✅
- **Wavelength of 77 GHz automotive radar:** $\lambda \approx 3.9$ mm. ✅
- **Wavelength of 4.3 GHz radar altimeter:** $\lambda \approx 7$ cm. ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FM-CW Radar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** FM-CW Radar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
**FM-CW (Frequency Modulated – Continuous Wave) Radar** is a special class of radar systems in which the transmitted signal is a **continuous wave (CW)** whose instantaneous frequency is linearly varied (modulated) with time, typically in a **sawtooth or triangular pattern**. Since transmission and reception occur simultaneously, the system relies on the **frequency difference (beat frequency)** between the currently transmitted and the delayed received echo to extract the target's range and velocity information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus: The core emphasis of this module is on understanding the **principle of operation**, the **mathematical relationship between beat frequency and target range**, and the **effect of target motion (Doppler shift)** on beat frequency in both sawtooth and triangular FM modulation schemes.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are standing in a canyon and you keep whistling a tune whose pitch **slowly rises and falls in a triangular pattern**. While you are whistling a *low* pitch, the echo returning from the canyon wall is the *same low pitch*. But if you change the pitch during the time the sound takes to travel to the wall and back, the returning echo will be at a *different pitch* than what you are currently whistling. By listening to this **difference in pitch (beat frequency)**, you can deduce how far the canyon wall is.

FM-CW radar does exactly this with **electromagnetic waves**:
- The **"changing pitch"** = linearly varying transmitted frequency.
- The **"echo from canyon wall"** = time-delayed received signal.
- The **"difference in pitch"** = **beat frequency**, which directly gives **range**.

### Why FM-CW Radar Exists (Motivation)
Pulsed radars require extremely **high peak transmit power** (megawatts) but low average power. FM-CW radar operates with **low transmit power continuously**, making it ideal for:
- Short-range applications (autonomous vehicles, parking sensors, altimeters, intruder alarms).
- Military fuzes and proximity sensors.
- Cost-effective, low-power commercial systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Bold:**
> - Transmitted frequency variation: **Linear Frequency Modulation (LFM)**
> - Typical modulation waveforms: **Sawtooth (unidirectional FM)** and **Triangular (bidirectional FM)**
> - Range information is encoded in the **beat frequency**, not in the round-trip time.
> - The velocity of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**

### Two Canonical FM Waveforms
| Modulation Type | Up-Sweep Behavior | Down-Sweep Behavior | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sawtooth FM** | Frequency rises linearly, resets suddenly | None | Simple range measurement, stationary targets |
| **Triangular FM** | Frequency rises linearly | Frequency falls linearly | Range + Velocity (resolves Doppler ambiguity) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Concept – Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Waveform Generation**: A stable reference oscillator (often a Voltage Controlled Oscillator, VCO) generates a **sine wave whose frequency changes linearly with time**.
2. **Signal Splitting**: The transmitted signal is split into two paths — one to the transmitting antenna, and a small portion to the **local oscillator (LO) port of the mixer**.
3. **Simultaneous Transmission and Reception**: A **circulator or directional coupler** allows the antenna to transmit and receive at the same time, isolating the high-power Tx from the sensitive Rx.
4. **Echo Reception**: The transmitted wave hits a target at range $R$ and returns after a time delay $t_d = \frac{2R}{c}$.
5. **Mixing Process**: The mixer multiplies the **delayed received echo** with the **current transmitted signal**. The output is a low-frequency signal — the **beat signal**.
6. **Range Extraction**: The beat frequency is proportional to the target range, allowing direct measurement of $R$ via **Fourier analysis (FFT)** of the beat signal.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step
- **Why Linear FM?** Because a linear frequency–time relationship produces a **constant beat frequency for a stationary target**, making range extraction via FFT trivial.
- **Why Mix?** Mixing two signals of different frequencies generates a difference-frequency term. Since Tx frequency changes linearly and the echo is a delayed copy, the *difference* is constant and equals the beat frequency.
- **Why Triangular Modulation for Moving Targets?** A moving target introduces a **Doppler shift** $f_d$ that adds to (or subtracts from) the range beat frequency. By using both up- and down-sweep, two beat equations are generated, allowing simultaneous solution for **range $R$ and velocity $v$**.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are board-exam critical. Memorize the symbol meanings and boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Round-trip delay | $t_d = \dfrac{2R}{c}$ | seconds | Echo arrives late by $t_d$ |
| 2 | Frequency deviation | $\Delta f$ | Hz | Total Tx frequency swing |
| 3 | Modulation period | $T_m$ | seconds | Time for one complete sweep |
| 4 | Rate of frequency change | $\dot{f} = \dfrac{\Delta f}{T_m}$ | Hz/s | Slope of sawtooth/triangular wave |
| 5 | Beat freq. (Sawtooth, stationary) | $f_b = \dfrac{2R\,\Delta f}{T_m\,c} = \dfrac{2R\,\dot{f}}{c}$ | Hz | Direct proportionality to R |
| 6 | Doppler shift | $f_d = \dfrac{2v}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2v\,f_0}{c}$ | Hz | Positive if target approaches |
| 7 | Beat freq. (Triangular, up-sweep) | $f_{b,\text{up}} = f_r - f_d$ | Hz | Range − Doppler |
| 8 | Beat freq. (Triangular, down-sweep) | $f_{b,\text{down}} = f_r + f_d$ | Hz | Range + Doppler |
| 9 | Range beat frequency | $f_r = \dfrac{f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}}}{2}$ | Hz | Sum and divide by 2 |
| 10 | Doppler beat frequency | $f_d = \dfrac{f_{b,\text{down}} - f_{b,\text{up}}}{2}$ | Hz | Difference and divide by 2 |
| 11 | Range resolution | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2\,\Delta f}$ | meters | Set by frequency deviation |
| 12 | Max. unambiguous range (triangular) | $R_{\max} = \dfrac{c\,T_m}{4}$ | meters | Echo must return before freq. reverses |
| 13 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_0}$ | meters | $f_0$ is the carrier frequency |

> **Engineering Utility:** FM-CW radars are the workhorses of **automotive adaptive cruise control (ACC)**, **anti-collision systems**, **UAV altimeters**, **tank-level measurement sensors**, and **modern millimeter-wave short-range imaging (77 GHz)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Beat Frequency for a Stationary Target (Sawtooth / Linear FM)

Let the instantaneous transmitted frequency be:
$$f_{\text{tx}}(t) = f_0 + \dot{f}\,t$$
where $\dot{f} = \dfrac{\Delta f}{T_m}$ is the constant frequency-sweep rate.

The received echo from a target at range $R$ is delayed by $t_d = \dfrac{2R}{c}$:
$$f_{\text{rx}}(t) = f_{\text{tx}}(t - t_d) = f_0 + \dot{f}\,(t - t_d)$$

At the **mixer output**, the beat (difference) signal has instantaneous frequency:
$$f_b(t) = f_{\text{tx}}(t) - f_{\text{rx}}(t)$$

Substituting both expressions:
$$f_b(t) = \left[f_0 + \dot{f}\,t\right] - \left[f_0 + \dot{f}\,(t - t_d)\right]$$

The $f_0$ and $\dot{f}\,t$ terms cancel:
$$f_b(t) = -\dot{f}\,t + \dot{f}\,t + \dot{f}\,t_d = \dot{f}\,t_d$$

Substituting $t_d = \dfrac{2R}{c}$:
$$\boxed{f_b = \dot{f}\,\frac{2R}{c} = \frac{2R\,\Delta f}{T_m\,c}}$$

**Logical Step Summary**:
- Step 1: Wrote linear FM law for transmitter.
- Step 2: Wrote delayed copy for receiver echo.
- Step 3: Took the frequency difference at the mixer.
- Step 4: Cancelled common terms.
- Step 5: Inserted the round-trip delay expression to obtain the final range-to-beat proportionality.

---

### Derivation 2: Beat Frequency for a Moving Target (Triangular FM)

For a target moving with **radial velocity $v$** (towards the radar is positive), the received signal experiences:
1. A **time delay** $t_d = \dfrac{2R}{c}$.
2. A **Doppler frequency shift** $f_d = \dfrac{2v}{\lambda}$ added to the carrier.

So the echo is:
$$f_{\text{rx}}(t) = f_0 + \dot{f}\,(t - t_d) + f_d$$

**During the Up-Sweep** (frequency is increasing, $\dot{f} > 0$):
$$f_{b,\text{up}} = f_{\text{tx}}(t) - f_{\text{rx}}(t) = \dot{f}\,t_d - f_d = \frac{2R\,\dot{f}}{c} - f_d$$

**During the Down-Sweep** (frequency is decreasing, $\dot{f} < 0$):
The sweep rate magnitude is the same, but the sign is negative. The Doppler term sign convention reverses:
$$f_{b,\text{down}} = \vert\dot{f}\vert\,t_d + f_d = \frac{2R\,\dot{f}}{c} + f_d$$

Define the range beat frequency as $f_r = \dfrac{2R\,\dot{f}}{c}$. Then:

$$f_{b,\text{up}} = f_r - f_d$$
$$f_{b,\text{down}} = f_r + f_d$$

Adding the two equations:
$$f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}} = 2 f_r \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{f_r = \frac{f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}}}{2}}$$

Subtracting:
$$f_{b,\text{down}} - f_{b,\text{up}} = 2 f_d \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{f_d = \frac{f_{b,\text{down}} - f_{b,\text{up}}}{2}}$$

The target range is then recovered by:
$$\boxed{R = \frac{c\,f_r}{2\,\dot{f}} = \frac{c\,T_m}{4\,\Delta f}\left(f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}}\right)}$$

**Logical Step Summary**:
- Step 1: For a moving target, both time delay AND Doppler shift affect the received signal.
- Step 2: Wrote the up-sweep beat frequency, recognizing that the Doppler shift **subtracts**.
- Step 3: Wrote the down-sweep beat frequency, recognizing that the Doppler shift **adds**.
- Step 4: Solved the resulting 2×2 linear system for $f_r$ and $f_d$.
- Step 5: Converted $f_r$ into range $R$ using the known sweep parameters.

---

### Derivation 3: Range Resolution of an FM-CW Radar

Range resolution is the minimum separation $\Delta R$ between two targets that the radar can distinguish. The transmitted bandwidth is the **frequency deviation** $\Delta f$. Two targets at ranges $R$ and $R + \Delta R$ produce beat frequencies differing by:
$$\Delta f_b = \frac{2\,\dot{f}\,\Delta R}{c} = \frac{2\,\Delta f\,\Delta R}{T_m\,c}$$

For these to be resolvable, the beat frequency difference must equal the FFT bin spacing, which (for a coherent integration over $T_m$) is $\dfrac{1}{T_m}$. Setting $\Delta f_b = \dfrac{1}{T_m}$:
$$\frac{2\,\Delta f\,\Delta R}{T_m\,c} = \frac{1}{T_m}$$

The $T_m$ cancels, leaving:
$$\boxed{\Delta R = \frac{c}{2\,\Delta f}}$$

**Logical Step Summary**:
- Step 1: Expressed the beat-frequency separation between two closely spaced targets.
- Step 2: Set the minimum resolvable separation equal to the FFT resolution.
- Step 3: Cancelled $T_m$ and solved for $\Delta R$.
- **Insight:** A larger frequency deviation $\Delta f$ yields finer range resolution — this is the *time-bandwidth product* principle.

---

### Numerical Example (Worked-Through)

> **Given:** An FM-CW radar uses triangular modulation with $\Delta f = 100\,\text{MHz}$, $T_m = 1\,\text{ms}$, and carrier frequency $f_0 = 10\,\text{GHz}$. A target at $R = 150\,\text{m}$ moves toward the radar at $v = 30\,\text{m/s}$.
>
> **Find:** $f_r$, $f_d$, $f_{b,\text{up}}$, $f_{b,\text{down}}$.

**Step 1: Compute $\dot{f}$**
$$\dot{f} = \frac{\Delta f}{T_m} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 1 \times 10^{11}\,\text{Hz/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute $f_r$**
$$f_r = \frac{2R\,\dot{f}}{c} = \frac{2 \times 150 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^8} = 1 \times 10^5\,\text{Hz} = 100\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 3: Compute $f_d$**
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03\,\text{m}$$
$$f_d = \frac{2v}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 30}{0.03} = 2000\,\text{Hz} = 2\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 4: Compute beat frequencies**
$$f_{b,\text{up}} = f_r - f_d = 100\,\text{kHz} - 2\,\text{kHz} = 98\,\text{kHz}$$
$$f_{b,\text{down}} = f_r + f_d = 100\,\text{kHz} + 2\,\text{kHz} = 102\,\text{kHz}$$

> **Verification**: Average is $100\,\text{kHz}$ (matches $f_r$). Half-difference is $2\,\text{kHz}$ (matches $f_d$). ✔

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Block Diagram: FM-CW Radar Architecture (Triangular Modulation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Waveform Generator\nTriangular Ramp Voltage] --> B[Voltage Controlled Oscillator VCO]
    B --> C1[Power Divider]
    C1 -- Tx Path --> D[Power Amplifier]
    D --> E[Transmit Antenna]
    E -. RF Echo .-> F[Receive Antenna]
    F --> G[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    C1 -- LO Path --> H[Mixer]
    G --> H
    H --> I[IF Beat Amplifier]
    I --> J[Bandpass Filter and FFT Processor]
    J --> K[Range and Velocity Display]
    style A fill:#E0F2FF,stroke:#0277BD
    style B fill:#E0F2FF,stroke:#0277BD
    style C1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00
    style H fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style J fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style K fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
```

### Functional Flow of Signal Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Stage 1: Linear FM waveform generated by VCO] --> P2[Stage 2: Signal split into Tx path and LO path]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Tx radiates toward target]
    P2 --> P4[Stage 3: LO reference held for mixing]
    P3 --> P5[Stage 4: Echo returns with time delay 2R by c]
    P4 --> P6[Stage 5: Mixer multiplies echo with LO]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Stage 6: Beat frequency extracted at IF output]
    P7 --> P8[Stage 7: FFT computes beat spectrum]
    P8 --> P9[Stage 8: Range and Doppler resolved from spectrum peaks]
    style P1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style P7 fill:#FFF9C4
    style P9 fill:#C8E6C9
```

### Conceptual Frequency–Time Plot (Triangular FM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX [TRANSMITTED FREQUENCY]
        T1[Time 0: f0]
        T2[Time Tm by 2: f0 plus delta f]
        T3[Time Tm: f0]
    end
    subgraph RX [RECEIVED ECHO]
        R1[Delayed by td = 2R by c]
        R2[Shifted lower by Doppler on up-sweep]
        R3[Shifted higher by Doppler on down-sweep]
    end
    T1 --- R1
    T2 --- R2
    T3 --- R3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The horizontal axis is time and the vertical axis is instantaneous frequency. The transmitted signal is a triangle wave. The received echo is a *time-shifted and Doppler-shifted* copy. The vertical gap between the Tx and Rx lines at any instant is the **beat frequency**, which is constant for a stationary target and varies slightly between up- and down-sweeps for a moving target.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is FM-CW radar? Mention two advantages of FM-CW radar over pulsed radar.*

**Model Answer:**
FM-CW radar is a radar system in which a **continuous wave (CW)** signal is transmitted with its frequency **linearly modulated** (typically in sawtooth or triangular form) with respect to time. The target range is extracted from the **beat frequency** between the transmitted and received signals.

**Advantages over pulsed radar:**
1. **Low peak transmit power** — operates continuously at milliwatt levels, eliminating the need for high-voltage pulse modulators.
2. **Simpler transmitter design** — no high-power TWT, magnetron, or pulse-forming network required.
3. **Better short-range performance** — no minimum range limitation due to receiver blanking.
4. **Improved EMC** — spread spectrum-like behavior of the wideband FM signal.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is a triangular FM waveform preferred over a sawtooth FM waveform in moving-target detection?*

**Model Answer:**
In a sawtooth FM scheme, the frequency varies in only one direction, so the beat signal contains the **range beat frequency $f_r$ and the Doppler shift $f_d$ combined** as $f_b = f_r + f_d$ (or $-f_d$). These two unknowns **cannot be separated** from a single beat measurement.

In a **triangular FM** scheme, the signal is swept in **both directions** (up and down). The Doppler shift **adds** in one sweep and **subtracts** in the other, yielding two independent equations:
$$f_{b,\text{up}} = f_r - f_d, \qquad f_{b,\text{down}} = f_r + f_d$$
These can be solved simultaneously to extract **both range $R$ and velocity $v$** of the target.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one of two alternatives** (internal choice).

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of an FM-CW radar using **triangular frequency modulation**. Derive the expressions for the beat frequency during the up-sweep and down-sweep. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An FM-CW radar operates at $f_0 = 9.4\,\text{GHz}$ with a triangular modulation having $\Delta f = 200\,\text{MHz}$ and $T_m = 2\,\text{ms}$. For a target at $R = 300\,\text{m}$ moving toward the radar at $v = 20\,\text{m/s}$, calculate (i) the range beat frequency $f_r$, (ii) the Doppler shift $f_d$, (iii) the up-sweep beat frequency $f_{b,\text{up}}$, and (iv) the maximum unambiguous range. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (4 marks) + Derivation (3 marks)**

Block Diagram: Refer to Section 4 of these notes (Triangular FM architecture). The key blocks are: **Triangular waveform generator → VCO → Power divider → Tx antenna** (via PA) and **LO path → Mixer ← LNA ← Rx antenna → IF amplifier → FFT processor → Display**.

**Derivation for up-sweep beat frequency:**
- The Tx instantaneous frequency: $f_{\text{tx}}(t) = f_0 + \dot{f}\,t$.
- The Rx echo (with delay and Doppler): $f_{\text{rx}}(t) = f_0 + \dot{f}(t - t_d) + f_d$.
- Beat frequency: $f_{b,\text{up}} = f_{\text{tx}} - f_{\text{rx}} = \dot{f}\,t_d - f_d = \dfrac{2R\,\dot{f}}{c} - f_d$.

**Derivation for down-sweep beat frequency:**
- The Tx frequency decreases: $f_{\text{tx}}(t) = f_0 - \dot{f}\,t$.
- The Rx echo: $f_{\text{rx}}(t) = f_0 - \dot{f}(t - t_d) + f_d$.
- Beat frequency: $f_{b,\text{down}} = \dot{f}\,t_d + f_d = \dfrac{2R\,\dot{f}}{c} + f_d$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute sweep rate**
$$\dot{f} = \frac{\Delta f}{T_m} = \frac{200 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1 \times 10^{11}\,\text{Hz/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute range beat frequency $f_r$**
$$f_r = \frac{2R\,\dot{f}}{c} = \frac{2 \times 300 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^8} = 2 \times 10^5\,\text{Hz} = 200\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 3: Compute Doppler shift $f_d$**
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{9.4 \times 10^9} = 0.0319\,\text{m}$$
$$f_d = \frac{2v}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 20}{0.0319} \approx 1253.99\,\text{Hz} \approx 1.254\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 4: Up-sweep beat frequency**
$$f_{b,\text{up}} = f_r - f_d = 200{,}000 - 1{,}254 = 198{,}746\,\text{Hz} \approx 198.75\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 5: Maximum unambiguous range**
$$R_{\max} = \frac{c\,T_m}{4} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 1.5 \times 10^5\,\text{m} = 150\,\text{km}$$

> Since the target is at $R = 300\,\text{m} \ll R_{\max}$, range measurement is **unambiguous**. ✔

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of FM-CW radar. Derive an expression for the beat frequency for a stationary target with **sawtooth modulation**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An FM-CW radar with sawtooth modulation has $\Delta f = 500\,\text{MHz}$, $T_m = 0.5\,\text{ms}$, and $f_0 = 35\,\text{GHz}$. Calculate (i) the beat frequency for a stationary target at $R = 100\,\text{m}$, (ii) the range resolution, and (iii) the maximum unambiguous range. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle and Derivation (4 + 3 marks):**

**Principle:** In an FM-CW radar, the transmitter continuously emits a signal whose frequency is **linearly increased** with time. The transmitted wave reaches the target, reflects, and returns after a time delay $t_d = \dfrac{2R}{c}$. Since the transmitter frequency has changed during this delay, the **received echo has a different instantaneous frequency** from the **currently transmitted signal**. A mixer multiplies these two signals, producing a low-frequency **beat signal** at the difference frequency. The beat frequency is **directly proportional** to the target range.

**Derivation (Sawtooth, Stationary):**
- Tx frequency: $f_{\text{tx}}(t) = f_0 + \dot{f}\,t$
- Rx echo: $f_{\text{rx}}(t) = f_0 + \dot{f}(t - t_d)$
- Beat: $f_b = f_{\text{tx}} - f_{\text{rx}} = \dot{f}\,t_d = \dfrac{2R\,\dot{f}}{c} = \dfrac{2R\,\Delta f}{T_m\,c}$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Sweep rate**
$$\dot{f} = \frac{500 \times 10^6}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1 \times 10^{12}\,\text{Hz/s}$$

**Step 2: Beat frequency at R = 100 m**
$$f_b = \frac{2R\,\dot{f}}{c} = \frac{2 \times 100 \times 10^{12}}{3 \times 10^8} = 6.67 \times 10^5\,\text{Hz} \approx 666.7\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 3: Range resolution**
$$\Delta R = \frac{c}{2\,\Delta f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 500 \times 10^6} = 0.3\,\text{m}$$

**Step 4: Maximum unambiguous range**
$$R_{\max} = \frac{c\,T_m}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 7.5 \times 10^4\,\text{m} = 75\,\text{km}$$
(Note: For sawtooth, factor is 2 in the denominator, not 4.)

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Errors Identified by KTU Valuators:**
> 1. **Sign of Doppler Shift:** Students often add Doppler shift in *both* sweeps. Correctly, $f_d$ **subtracts** in up-sweep and **adds** in down-sweep. Getting this wrong → lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Factor of 2 vs 4 in $R_{\max}$:** Sawtooth uses $cT/2$, triangular uses $cT/4$. Using the wrong one → 1–2 marks lost.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units:** $\Delta f$ in MHz, $T_m$ in ms, $c$ in m/s — failing to convert leads to 10⁶–10⁹ errors.
> 4. **Mixing up $f_r$ and $f_d$ formulas:** $f_r = (f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}})/2$ (sum). $f_d = (f_{b,\text{down}} - f_{b,\text{up}})/2$ (difference). Reversing → marks lost.
> 5. **Confusing wavelength and frequency:** $\lambda = c/f_0$, not $c/\Delta f$. $\Delta f$ is the deviation, not the carrier.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **FM-CW = Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave** — Tx and Rx operate simultaneously.
- [x] **Beat frequency $f_b$** is the difference between instantaneous Tx and Rx frequencies.
- [x] **Sawtooth FM** → single beat frequency, suitable for **stationary** targets only.
- [x] **Triangular FM** → up-sweep and down-sweep beat frequencies → solves for both **range and velocity**.
- [x] **Range beat frequency:** $f_r = \dfrac{2R\,\Delta f}{T_m\,c}$.
- [x] **Doppler shift:** $f_d = \dfrac{2v}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2v\,f_0}{c}$.
- [x] **Up-sweep:** $f_{b,\text{up}} = f_r - f_d$. **Down-sweep:** $f_{b,\text{down}} = f_r + f_d$.
- [x] **Range resolution:** $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2\,\Delta f}$ (larger $\Delta f$ → better resolution).
- [x] **Max unambiguous range (triangular):** $R_{\max} = \dfrac{c\,T_m}{4}$. **Sawtooth:** $R_{\max} = \dfrac{c\,T_m}{2}$.
- [x] **Key advantage:** Low peak Tx power (milliwatts vs megawatts for pulsed radar).
- [x] **Typical applications:** Automotive radar (77 GHz), tank-level gauging, altimeters, intruder alarms.
- [x] **Block diagram essentials:** VCO → Power Divider → Tx Antenna + LO → Mixer ← LNA ← Rx Antenna → IF Amp → FFT → Display.
- [x] **Why FFT?** Beat signal is sinusoidal at $f_b$; FFT locates the spectral peak to extract range.
- [x] **Mixer role:** Generates the difference frequency (beat) by multiplying Tx and Rx signals.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *"U-D-R-V"* — **U**p-sweep = **D**oppler subtracts, **D**own-sweep = Doppler adds, giving both **R**ange and **V**elocity. Master this and triangular FM becomes a guaranteed 7-mark question.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
