<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4 — Range and Doppler Measurement

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> **Range Measurement** in radar is the process of determining the distance (range) of a target from the radar by measuring the round-trip time delay of an electromagnetic pulse transmitted by the radar, reflected by the target, and received back at the radar antenna. **Doppler Measurement** is the process of determining the radial velocity of a moving target by measuring the shift in frequency of the returned echo relative to the transmitted carrier frequency, governed by the **Doppler Effect**.

### 1.1 What is Range?
Range is the **slant distance** between the radar antenna and the target. For a one-way signal, electromagnetic (EM) waves travel at the speed of light:

$$c = 3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}$$

For a **two-way trip** (transmit → reflect → receive), the round-trip time is $t_R$. The target range $R$ is then:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot t_R}{2}$$

The factor of **2** appears because the pulse travels the same distance twice (out and back).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme treats *range* and *Doppler* as the two fundamental parameters extracted by any radar. Module 4 focuses exclusively on the mathematical formulation, system architectures (pulsed, CW, FMCW), and the inherent trade-offs (range–Doppler ambiguity) that govern their joint measurement.

### 1.2 What is the Doppler Effect?
When a target moves with radial velocity $v_r$ relative to the radar, the frequency of the reflected signal is shifted by an amount called the **Doppler frequency** $f_d$:

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$$

where:
- $f_0$ is the **transmitted carrier frequency**
- $\lambda$ is the **wavelength** of transmission
- $v_r$ is the **radial velocity** (positive for approaching, negative for receding)

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition – The Ambulance Siren Analogy 🎯**
> Imagine standing on a road and hearing an ambulance siren. As the ambulance races **toward** you, the pitch (frequency) sounds **higher** than the actual emitted frequency. As it moves **away**, the pitch becomes **lower**. This pitch shift is the **Doppler effect**. A radar works the same way: the target "racing toward" the radar causes the received echo to be at a slightly **higher** frequency than what was transmitted. The amount of this frequency shift tells us **how fast** the target is moving radially.

### 1.3 Why Are Both Measured Together?
- **Range** gives the **WHERE** (position) of the target.
- **Doppler** gives the **HOW FAST** (radial velocity) and **WHAT** (moving vs. stationary) of the target.
- A **stationary clutter** (building, hill) produces **zero Doppler**, allowing radars to **filter out clutter** and detect only moving targets (MTI principle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize (KTU Board Exam):**
> - Speed of light: $\mathbf{c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s}$
> - 1 nautical mile = **1852 m** (used in radar)
> - Radar operating frequencies: L-band (1–2 GHz), S-band (2–4 GHz), C-band (4–8 GHz), X-band (8–12 GHz), Ku-band (12–18 GHz)
> - Standard pulse repetition frequencies (PRF): Low PRF (< 1 kHz) for unambiguous range, High PRF (> 100 kHz) for unambiguous Doppler.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pulsed Radar — Range Measurement

A **pulsed radar** transmits short bursts (pulses) of RF energy of duration $\tau$ (pulse width) at a Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI) $T_p$. The corresponding **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** is $f_p = 1/T_p$.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):
1. Transmitter sends out a pulse of duration $\tau$ at $t = 0$.
2. Pulse travels at the speed of light, hits the target at range $R$.
3. Energy is scattered in all directions; a small fraction returns to the radar.
4. Receiver detects the echo at $t = t_R$.
5. Range is computed: $R = c \cdot t_R / 2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **WHY divide by 2?**
> The round-trip time $t_R$ corresponds to the signal traversing the distance $R$ **twice** (down-link to target + up-link to radar). Hence $c \cdot t_R = 2R$.

### 2.2 Range Resolution ($\Delta R$)
**Range resolution** is the ability of the radar to distinguish two closely spaced targets on the same bearing.

$$\Delta R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} = \frac{c}{2B}$$

where $B$ is the **bandwidth** of the pulse (for a rectangular pulse, $B = 1/\tau$).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition – Two Runners Analogy:**
> If a stopwatch can only measure to the nearest second, you cannot tell if two runners crossed the finish line 0.5 s apart. Similarly, a radar with a long pulse ($\tau$ large) has poor resolution — two targets close in range merge into one blob. A **short pulse** = **fine resolution**.

### 2.3 Maximum Unambiguous Range ($R_{unamb}$)
The maximum range at which a target can be unambiguously placed — i.e., without confusing the echo with the echo of a *previous* pulse.

$$R_{unamb} = \frac{c \cdot T_p}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** If a target is at range $R$ such that $t_R > T_p$, the echo arrives **after** the next pulse has been transmitted. The radar will mistakenly place it at range $R - R_{unamb}$. This is the famous **Range Ambiguity Problem**.

### 2.4 Minimum Range ($R_{min}$)
Set by the time taken for the transmitter to recover and the receiver to begin listening — typically when the **duplexer** switches. Approximately:

$$R_{min} = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$$

### 2.5 Doppler Frequency in Radar
For a target moving with radial velocity $v_r$:

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU):** $v_r$ is **positive** for an **approaching** target (frequency shift up), and **negative** for a **receding** target (frequency shift down). The factor **2** in the numerator accounts for the two-way Doppler shift (radar as both transmitter and receiver).

### 2.6 Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler Radar
A pure CW radar measures **only Doppler**, not range. It transmits a continuous sinusoid at $f_0$ and the receiver detects $f_0 \pm f_d$. It cannot determine target range because the transmitted signal has **no time marker**.

### 2.7 Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) Radar
To measure **both range and Doppler** simultaneously, FMCW radar modulates the transmitted frequency linearly (a "chirp"):

$$f_t(t) = f_0 + \frac{B}{T_c} \cdot t, \quad 0 \le t \le T_c$$

where $T_c$ is the chirp duration and $B$ is the chirp bandwidth.

For a stationary target at range $R$, the received signal is delayed by $t_d = 2R/c$. The **beat frequency** is:

$$f_b = \frac{B}{T_c} \cdot t_d = \frac{2 B R}{c T_c}$$

Range is extracted as:

$$R = \frac{c T_c f_b}{2 B}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition – Whistling at a Wall:**
> Blow a whistle whose pitch rises over 1 second, then record the echo from a wall. The "lag" between the current pitch and the recorded pitch tells you **how far** the wall is. FMCW radar does the same with EM waves.

### 2.8 MTI Radar (Moving Target Indication)
MTI radars use the **Doppler effect to cancel stationary clutter** (buildings, trees, ground). The principle:
- Stationary targets → **no Doppler shift** → echo at $f_0$ → cancelled.
- Moving targets → **non-zero $f_d$** → echo at $f_0 \pm f_d$ → passes through.

A simple **single-delay-line canceller** output is:

$$y(t) = x(t) - x(t - T_p)$$

Its frequency response $H(f)$ has nulls at $f = n/T_p$ (which removes DC and PRF harmonics — exactly where clutter resides).

### 2.9 🎯 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Target Range | $R = \dfrac{c \, t_R}{2}$ | metres (m) |
| 2 | Range Resolution | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2} = \dfrac{c}{2B}$ | $\tau$ in s, $B$ in Hz |
| 3 | Max Unambiguous Range | $R_{unamb} = \dfrac{c}{2 \, PRF}$ | Decreases as PRF increases |
| 4 | Min Detectable Range | $R_{min} \approx \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$ | Set by duplexer recovery |
| 5 | Doppler Frequency | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$ | Hz; $v_r$ in m/s |
| 6 | Radial Velocity from $f_d$ | $v_r = \dfrac{f_d \lambda}{2}$ | m/s |
| 7 | Doppler Ambiguity (Vel. Unambig.) | $v_{r,max} = \dfrac{\lambda \, PRF}{4}$ | Requires high PRF |
| 8 | FMCW Beat Frequency | $f_b = \dfrac{2 B R}{c T_c}$ | Hz; stationary target |
| 9 | Range from FMCW Beat | $R = \dfrac{c T_c f_b}{2B}$ | m |
| 10 | Doppler–Range Coupling (FMCW) | $R_{app} = R - \dfrac{v_r T_c f_0}{B}$ | Small if $B \gg f_0 T_c v_r/c$ |
| 11 | Time on Target | $t_{ot} = \dfrac{n_{pulses}}{PRF} = n_{pulses} T_p$ | seconds |
| 12 | Blind Speed (MTI) | $v_{blind} = \dfrac{n \lambda \, PRF}{2}$ | Speeds at which MTI fails |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Air Traffic Control (ATC) radars:** Pulsed radar at S-band for range + Doppler for velocity.
> - **Police speed guns:** X-band CW Doppler radar.
> - **Automotive adaptive cruise control:** 77 GHz FMCW radar.
> - **Weather radar:** Doppler shift reveals raindrop/wind velocity.
> - **Military fire-control radar:** Pulse-Doppler for tracking aircraft in clutter.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Target Range from Round-Trip Time

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** Electromagnetic waves propagate at the speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in free space.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
Let the radar be at origin, and a target be at one-way distance $R$ from the radar.

**Step 2 — Write the one-way travel time.**

$$t_{one-way} = \frac{R}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Account for the two-way (round-trip) travel.**
The pulse must travel to the target (distance $R$) and back (distance $R$):

$$t_R = t_{one-way,\,to} + t_{one-way,\,back} = \frac{R}{c} + \frac{R}{c} = \frac{2R}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R$.**

$$R = \frac{c \cdot t_R}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting the standard value of $c$.**

$$R = \frac{(3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}) \cdot t_R}{2} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \cdot t_R \quad (\text{in metres})$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick numerical check (KTU-style):** If the round-trip time is $t_R = 6.67 \, \mu s$, then $R = 1.5 \times 10^8 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-6} = 1000$ m = **1 km**. (This corresponds to the standard radar calibration target.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Unambiguous Range

**Step 1 — Begin with the PRI definition.**
The pulse repetition interval is the time between two consecutive transmitted pulses:

$$T_p = \frac{1}{PRF}$$

**Step 2 — Stipulate the unambiguous condition.**
For the echo from a given pulse to arrive **before** the next pulse is transmitted:

$$t_R \le T_p$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $t_R = 2R/c$.**

$$\frac{2 R_{unamb}}{c} \le T_p = \frac{1}{PRF}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_{unamb}$.**

$$R_{unamb} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Lowering the PRF (i.e., increasing $T_p$) increases $R_{unamb}$, but also creates *blind speeds* in MTI. This is the classic **range–Doppler ambiguity trade-off**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Range Resolution

**Step 1 — Define resolution.**
Two targets at ranges $R_1$ and $R_2$ are **just resolvable** if the trailing edge of the first echo arrives **exactly** when the leading edge of the second echo arrives:

$$t_{R_2} - t_{R_1} = \tau$$

**Step 2 — Convert to range difference.**

$$\frac{2(R_2 - R_1)}{c} = \tau \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \Delta R = R_2 - R_1 = \frac{c \tau}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of bandwidth.**
For a rectangular pulse, the spectral main-lobe bandwidth is $B = 1/\tau$:

$$\Delta R = \frac{c}{2B}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Result:** To resolve two aircraft separated by 15 m, the required bandwidth is $B = c / (2 \Delta R) = (3 \times 10^8) / 30 = 10$ MHz.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Doppler Frequency in Radar

**Step 1 — Recall the one-way Doppler shift (acoustic/EM).**
For a transmitter moving toward a stationary observer at radial velocity $v_r$, the observed frequency is:

$$f' = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v_r}{c} \right) \quad \text{(classical, non-relativistic)}$$

**Step 2 — For a moving target acting as a passive reflector.**
The target "sees" an incoming wave at $f' = f_0 (1 + v_r/c)$, then *re-radiates* it. The radar observes the wave returned from a target moving toward it, giving a second shift:

$$f_r = f' \left( 1 + \frac{v_r}{c} \right) = f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — For $v_r \ll c$ (typical radar case), apply binomial expansion.**
Using $(1+x)^2 \approx 1 + 2x$ for small $x$:

$$f_r \approx f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{2 v_r}{c} \right) = f_0 + \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Doppler shift.**

$$f_d = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Relativistic correction (bonus):** The exact two-way Doppler formula is $f_d = f_0 \dfrac{1 + v_r/c}{1 - v_r/c} - f_0$, which for $v_r \ll c$ reduces to the result above. KTU board questions do not require the relativistic form.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: FMCW Beat Frequency

**Step 1 — Define the transmitted chirp.**
A linear FM chirp over duration $T_c$ sweeps from $f_0$ to $f_0 + B$:

$$f_t(t) = f_0 + \frac{B}{T_c} t, \quad 0 \le t \le T_c$$

The instantaneous phase is:

$$\phi_t(t) = 2\pi \int_0^t f_t(\tau) \, d\tau = 2\pi \left( f_0 t + \frac{B}{2 T_c} t^2 \right)$$

**Step 2 — Express the transmitted signal.**

$$s_t(t) = A_t \cos\!\left[ 2\pi f_0 t + \pi \frac{B}{T_c} t^2 \right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply round-trip delay $t_d = 2R/c$ (assume stationary target).**
The received signal is:

$$s_r(t) = A_r \cos\!\left[ 2\pi f_0 (t - t_d) + \pi \frac{B}{T_c} (t - t_d)^2 \right]$$

**Step 4 — Form the beat (mix received with transmitted).**
The mixer output contains a term at the difference frequency. Expanding the phase difference and retaining only the linear term (assuming $t_d \ll T_c$):

$$\Delta\phi(t) \approx 2\pi \left( f_0 t_d + \frac{B}{T_c} t \cdot t_d \right)$$

The time-varying part gives a sinusoid at frequency:

$$f_b = \frac{B \, t_d}{T_c} = \frac{2 B R}{c T_c}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $R$.**

$$R = \frac{c \, T_c \, f_b}{2 B}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why FMCW is Powerful (KTU Insight):**
> By measuring the **beat frequency** $f_b$, the radar can extract range **continuously** on every sweep. There is **no minimum range** limitation (unlike pulsed radar) and the system uses low-power transmitters — ideal for automotive and short-range applications.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A ground-based surveillance radar operates at $f_0 = 10 \, \text{GHz}$ (X-band) with PRF = 1 kHz, $\tau = 1 \, \mu s$. A target is detected at $t_R = 100 \, \mu s$, with measured Doppler $f_d = 2 \, \text{kHz}$. Find (i) Range, (ii) Max unambiguous range, (iii) Range resolution, (iv) Radial velocity.

**Solution:**

**(i) Range:**

$$R = \frac{c \, t_R}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 15{,}000 \, \text{m} = 15 \, \text{km}$$

**(ii) Max unambiguous range:**

$$R_{unamb} = \frac{c}{2 \, PRF} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^3} = 150 \, \text{km}$$

**(iii) Range resolution:**

$$\Delta R = \frac{c \, \tau}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 150 \, \text{m}$$

**(iv) Radial velocity:**
First compute wavelength: $\lambda = c/f_0 = (3 \times 10^8)/(10 \times 10^9) = 0.03$ m.

$$v_r = \frac{f_d \, \lambda}{2} = \frac{2 \times 10^3 \times 0.03}{2} = 30 \, \text{m/s}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The target is at 15 km, approaching at 108 km/h, and the radar can resolve two targets if they are ≥ 150 m apart in range.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Pulsed Radar Range Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Timing and Trigger Generator] --> B[Pulse Modulator]
    B --> C[Transmitter RF Oscillator]
    C --> D[Duplexer Switch]
    D --> E[Antenna]
    E -->|Target Echo| D
    D --> F[Receiver LNA]
    F --> G[Matched Filter]
    G --> H[Detector and Threshold]
    H --> I[Range Counter and Display]
    A --> I
    D --> J[Clutter Doppler Canceller MTI]
    J --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functionality Map:** The timing generator synchronizes the transmit pulse and the range counter. The duplexer routes the high-power transmit pulse to the antenna and protects the sensitive receiver during transmission. The matched filter maximizes SNR for a pulse of known shape. The range counter measures the delay $t_R$ between the transmitted pulse's leading edge and the received echo's leading edge and multiplies by $c/2$ to display range.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: FMCW Radar for Joint Range–Doppler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Waveform Generator Linear Chirp] --> B[Upconverter to RF]
    B --> C[Power Amplifier]
    C --> D[Duplexer]
    D --> E[Antenna]
    E -->|Echo with Range and Doppler| D
    D --> F[Low Noise Amplifier]
    F --> G[Mixer with Transmitted Chirp]
    G --> H[Beat Frequency Amplifier]
    H --> I[FFT Processor Range Profile]
    I --> J[Range Doppler Map Display]
    H --> K[Doppler Bank Second FFT across Chirps]
    K --> J
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Logic:** The mixer produces a beat signal whose frequency is proportional to **range** (within one chirp) and whose **phase progression** from chirp-to-chirp encodes the **Doppler** shift. A 2-D FFT (range FFT followed by Doppler FFT) yields the classic **Range–Doppler Map**.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Range and Doppler Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Transmitted Signal at f0] --> P[Pulses and Chirps Propagate at c]
    P --> Q[Target at Range R, Velocity vr]
    Q --> R[Echo Received at f0 plus or minus fd]
    R --> S[Receiver and Signal Processing]
    S --> U[Range extracted from time delay tR equals 2R over c]
    S --> V[Doppler extracted from frequency shift fd equals 2vr over lambda]
    U --> W[Output: Range R in metres]
    V --> X[Output: Radial Velocity vr in m/s]
```

---

## 4.4 MTI Canceller Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Received Echo Stream x t] --> B[Delay Line by Tp]
    A --> C[Subtract from Direct]
    B --> C
    C --> D[Output y t equals x t minus x of t minus Tp]
    D --> E[Doppler Pass Output]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** The delay line canceller has the transfer function $H(z) = 1 - z^{-T_p}$, which has unit magnitude only when the input frequency is an **odd multiple of $PRF/2$** (true Doppler), and **zero** at DC and PRF multiples (clutter). This is the foundation of MTI clutter rejection.

---

## 4.5 Range–Doppler Ambiguity Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low PRF] --> B[Unambiguous Range]
    A --> C[Ambiguous Doppler]
    D[High PRF] --> E[Unambiguous Doppler]
    D --> F[Ambiguous Range]
    G[Medium PRF] --> H[Ambiguous Both but Resolved by Multiple PRFs]
    H --> I[Modern Pulse Doppler Radar]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often think "high PRF is better." It is **not universally better** — high PRF unambiguously measures Doppler but folds range. Modern radars use **multiple PRFs** to resolve both via the Chinese Remainder Theorem (residue arithmetic).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern:** KTU 2024 ESE Part A tests direct, factual recall and short derivations. Answer in **3–4 lines** with key formula.

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Doppler frequency in a radar system. Derive the expression for Doppler shift when a target moves with radial velocity $v_r$ towards a stationary radar operating at carrier frequency $f_0$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Doppler frequency is the difference between transmitted and received signal frequencies due to target motion.
Two-way Doppler shift: $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda = (2 v_r f_0)/c$.
The factor **2** arises because the EM wave undergoes Doppler shift twice: once at the target (incident wave) and once on the reflected wave at the radar.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is maximum unambiguous range? Obtain its expression in terms of PRF.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Maximum unambiguous range $R_{unamb}$ is the largest range at which a target's echo arrives **before** the next pulse is transmitted, so that the round-trip delay $t_R$ does not exceed the PRI $T_p$.
From $t_R = 2R/c \le T_p = 1/PRF$:

$$R_{unamb} = \frac{c}{2 \, PRF} = 150 \, \text{km} \quad \text{(for PRF = 1 kHz)}$$

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B has a **Module-wise Internal Choice**. Two alternatives are provided below; any ONE must be answered. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **operation of a pulsed radar system for range measurement**. Derive the expression for target range. Discuss the factors that limit maximum and minimum ranges. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A pulsed radar transmits short RF bursts of width $\tau$ at intervals $T_p = 1/\text{PRF}$. The system block diagram (refer SECTION 4.1) consists of: timing generator → pulse modulator → RF oscillator → duplexer → antenna (transmit), and the echo path through LNA → matched filter → detector → range counter.

**Range Derivation:**
The transmitted pulse travels to the target at range $R$ and returns after a round-trip time:

$$t_R = \frac{2R}{c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{c \, t_R}{2}$$

**Maximum Range** is limited by:
- **PRF** (range ambiguity): $R_{unamb} = c/(2 \cdot \text{PRF})$.
- **Transmitter power and receiver sensitivity** (via radar range equation, not in this module's scope).
- **Atmospheric attenuation** and **earth curvature**.

**Minimum Range** is limited by:
- **Pulse width $\tau$**: $R_{min} = c\tau/2$.
- **Duplexer recovery time** (T/R switch transition): typically a few hundred nanoseconds.
- **Receiver blanking** during transmit.

**(b)** Explain the concept of **range resolution**. Derive the expression. A pulsed radar has pulse width $\tau = 0.5 \, \mu s$. Calculate (i) range resolution, and (ii) maximum unambiguous range if PRF = 2 kHz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** Range resolution is the minimum range separation $\Delta R$ between two targets of equal cross-section on the same bearing such that the radar can display them as **two distinct echoes** rather than one merged echo.

**Derivation:** Two targets at $R_1, R_2$ are just resolvable when the trailing edge of echo-1 coincides with the leading edge of echo-2:

$$\frac{2(R_2 - R_1)}{c} = \tau \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \Delta R = \frac{c \tau}{2}$$

Equivalently, with $B = 1/\tau$ (rectangular pulse): $\Delta R = c / (2B)$.

**Numerical Calculations:**

**(i) Range resolution:**

$$\Delta R = \frac{c \tau}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 75 \, \text{m}$$

**(ii) Maximum unambiguous range:**

$$R_{unamb} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2 \times 10^3} = 75{,}000 \, \text{m} = 75 \, \text{km}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️**
> Students often **forget the factor of 2** in the range equation. Also, do NOT confuse $R_{min}$ with $\Delta R$ — they have different physical meanings. $R_{min}$ is set by hardware, while $\Delta R$ is the ability to **distinguish** two targets. Showing units in every line and boxing the final answer is a must for full marks.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With necessary diagrams, explain the **principle of FMCW radar for range and Doppler measurement**. Derive the expression for the beat frequency for a stationary target. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** A Continuous Wave (CW) radar alone cannot measure range because there is no time marker. To overcome this, the transmitted frequency is **linearly swept** (chirped) across a bandwidth $B$ over a chirp duration $T_c$. The reflected echo is a **time-delayed replica** of the chirp. Mixing the transmitted and received chirps produces a **beat signal** whose frequency is proportional to the time delay — and hence to the **range** of the target.

**Derivation of Beat Frequency (stationary target):**
Transmitted instantaneous frequency:

$$f_t(t) = f_0 + \frac{B}{T_c} t$$

For a stationary target at range $R$, the received signal is delayed by $t_d = 2R/c$. The **instantaneous frequency difference** (beat frequency) is the slope of the chirp multiplied by the time delay:

$$f_b = \left( \frac{B}{T_c} \right) \times t_d = \frac{B}{T_c} \cdot \frac{2R}{c} = \frac{2 B R}{c T_c}$$

Solving for range:

$$R = \frac{c T_c f_b}{2 B}$$

**Diagram:** Refer to the FMCW block diagram in SECTION 4.2 — a 2-D FFT produces a Range–Doppler map.

**(b)** An X-band (**$f_0 = 10$ GHz**) radar measures a Doppler shift $f_d = 5 \, \text{kHz}$. Find (i) the radial velocity of the target, (ii) the Doppler shift if the same target moved at the same speed but at S-band ($f_0 = 3$ GHz), and (iii) the maximum unambiguous velocity if PRF = 1 kHz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f_0 = 10$ GHz $= 10^{10}$ Hz, $f_d = 5 \times 10^3$ Hz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Wavelength:** $\lambda = c/f_0 = (3 \times 10^8)/(10 \times 10^9) = 0.03$ m.

**(i) Radial velocity:**

$$v_r = \frac{f_d \, \lambda}{2} = \frac{5 \times 10^3 \times 0.03}{2} = 75 \, \text{m/s} = 270 \, \text{km/h}$$

**(ii) Doppler shift at S-band ($f_0 = 3$ GHz, $\lambda = 0.1$ m):**

$$f_d' = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 75}{0.1} = 1500 \, \text{Hz} = 1.5 \, \text{kHz}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** Lower carrier frequency → smaller Doppler shift. This is why weather radars (S-band) use very long integration times, and police radars (X-band) get large Doppler shifts even for slow cars.

**(iii) Maximum unambiguous velocity:**
The maximum unambiguous Doppler frequency is $f_{d,max} = \text{PRF}/2$ (Nyquist criterion for sampling). Therefore:

$$v_{r,max} = \frac{f_{d,max} \, \lambda}{2} = \frac{\text{PRF} \cdot \lambda}{4} = \frac{10^3 \times 0.03}{4} = 7.5 \, \text{m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️**
> Common mistakes in Doppler problems: (1) **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the Doppler formula — this is the most common single error. (2) **Mixing up frequency and wavelength** — always convert $f_0$ to $\lambda$ via $c = f_0 \lambda$ BEFORE plugging in. (3) **Sign of $v_r$** — radial velocity is **positive** for approaching targets. (4) **Units** — present $v_r$ in m/s in intermediate steps, but final answer in km/h if the question implies a real-world scenario.

---

## 5.3 🌟 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision Checklist — High Density!**

- ✅ **Range equation (must memorize):** $R = c \, t_R / 2$. Always carry the factor of **2**.
- ✅ **Maximum unambiguous range:** $R_{unamb} = c / (2 \cdot \text{PRF})$. Higher PRF → smaller unambiguous range.
- ✅ **Range resolution:** $\Delta R = c\tau/2 = c/(2B)$. Shorter pulse (or wider bandwidth) → finer resolution.
- ✅ **Doppler frequency (two-way):** $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda = 2 v_r f_0 / c$. Always appears with the **factor of 2**.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Approaching target → $f_d > 0$ (frequency up-shift). Receding → $f_d < 0$ (down-shift).
- ✅ **CW radar** measures **only Doppler**, not range (no time reference).
- ✅ **FMCW radar** uses a linear chirp; beat frequency $f_b = 2BR/(cT_c)$ gives range on a **single sweep**.
- ✅ **Pulse-Doppler radar** combines range gating (via PRF) with Doppler filtering — used in modern military radars.
- ✅ **MTI principle:** A single-delay canceller $y(t) = x(t) - x(t - T_p)$ rejects DC (stationary clutter) and passes Doppler-shifted echoes.
- ✅ **Blind speeds:** $v_{blind} = n \lambda \text{PRF}/2$. Staggered PRFs are used to overcome this.
- ✅ **High PRF** → unambiguous Doppler but ambiguous range. **Low PRF** → unambiguous range but ambiguous Doppler. **Medium PRF** → both ambiguous, resolved using **multiple-PRF residue arithmetic**.
- ✅ **Standard values:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, 1 nautical mile = 1852 m, X-band $\lambda \approx 3$ cm at 10 GHz.
- ✅ **Radar equation sanity check:** A target at 100 km with PRF = 1 kHz has $R_{unamb} = 150$ km → unambiguous. ✅
- ✅ **Bandwidth–Resolution Duality:** $\Delta R = c/(2B)$ — improving resolution by a factor of 2 requires doubling the bandwidth.
- ✅ **FMCW beat frequency = 0 when target is at zero range** — this is why FMCW radars have no minimum range.
- ✅ **Common KTU numerical trick:** Round-trip time per km = $2/3 \times 10^{-5}$ s/km = $6.67 \, \mu$s/km.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Takeaway for KTU Board:**
> *Range is measured by the **time delay** of a reflected pulse or chirp (R = c·t/2), and Doppler is measured by the **frequency shift** of the echo (f_d = 2v/λ). Range resolution is governed by bandwidth; Doppler resolution is governed by observation time. The two are linked by the **range–Doppler ambiguity**, which is resolved by careful choice of PRF and waveform design.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram and Characteristics (Approaching/Receding Targets)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Doppler Effect in Radar

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Doppler Effect** in radar is the apparent shift in the frequency of the received echo signal relative to the transmitted signal, caused by the **relative radial motion** between the radar and the target. This frequency shift is termed the **Doppler Frequency Shift ($f_d$)** and forms the fundamental basis for **velocity extraction** in modern radar systems.

Mathematically, the received signal frequency is:

$$f_r = f_0 + f_d$$

where $f_0$ is the transmitted carrier frequency and $f_d$ is the Doppler shift.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Ambulance Siren

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump:** Stand on a footpath. An ambulance with its siren screaming approaches you. As it comes closer, the pitch of the siren sounds **higher** (frequency has **increased**). The instant it passes you and moves away, the pitch suddenly drops to a **lower** tone (frequency has **decreased**).
>
> - **Approaching Ambulance** → Higher frequency (positive Doppler shift)
> - **Receding Ambulance** → Lower frequency (negative Doppler shift)

In a radar system, the target behaves exactly like the ambulance. The transmitted electromagnetic wave is reflected back with its frequency altered based on the **target's radial velocity** ($v_r$) along the radar line-of-sight.

## 1.3 Approaching vs Receding Targets — Core Distinction

| Parameter | Approaching Target | Receding Target |
|---|---|---|
| Radial Velocity $v_r$ | **Positive** (towards radar) | **Negative** (away from radar) |
| Range $R(t)$ | $R_0 - v_r t$ (decreasing) | $R_0 + \vert v_r \vert t$ (increasing) |
| Doppler Frequency $f_d$ | **Positive** | **Negative** |
| Received Frequency $f_r$ | $f_0 + f_d$ (higher) | $f_0 - \vert f_d \vert$ (lower) |
| Phase Behavior | Advancing phase | Retarding phase |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this happen?**
> When the target moves **towards** the radar, it **compresses** the successive wavefronts of the reflected wave, increasing the number of cycles received per second (higher frequency). When the target moves **away**, the wavefronts are **stretched**, reducing the cycles per second (lower frequency).

## 1.4 The Standard Radar Doppler Equation

The Doppler frequency shift for a monostatic radar is given by the **two-way** (round-trip) equation:

$$f_d = \frac{2 \, v_r \, f_0}{c} = \frac{2 \, v_r}{\lambda}$$

where:
- $f_d$ = Doppler frequency shift in **Hz**
- $v_r$ = Radial velocity of the target in **m/s** (positive for approaching)
- $f_0$ = Transmitted carrier frequency in **Hz**
- $c$ = Speed of light = **$3 \times 10^8$ m/s**
- $\lambda$ = Operating wavelength in **meters**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The factor of **2** appears because the wave undergoes a Doppler shift **twice** — once on incidence at the moving target, and once again on reflection back to the radar. This is a classic 3-mark question!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How the Radar Detects Motion

A **Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler Radar** exploits motion detection through the following logical chain:

1. The radar transmits a pure, unmodulated sinusoidal CW signal at frequency $f_0$.
2. The signal propagates through free space and impinges on a moving target.
3. The moving target reflects the wave with a frequency shift (Doppler effect).
4. The radar receives the echo at frequency $f_r = f_0 \pm f_d$.
5. The receiver **mixes** the received signal with a sample of the transmitted reference signal.
6. The mixer output (after low-pass filtering) yields a baseband signal at the **Doppler frequency** $f_d$.
7. The sign and magnitude of $f_d$ are extracted to determine the **direction** and **speed** of the target.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Limitation of Pure CW Radar:** A simple single-channel CW radar **cannot distinguish** between approaching and receding targets because it only measures $\vert f_d \vert$, not its sign. To recover the sign, **quadrature (I/Q) channel** processing is mandatory.

## 2.2 Direction Sensing via Quadrature Channels

To resolve the **ambiguity** between approaching and receding targets, the radar employs **two parallel receiver channels** that are 90° out of phase:

- **I-Channel (In-phase):** Output proportional to $\cos(2\pi f_d t)$
- **Q-Channel (Quadrature):** Output proportional to $\sin(2\pi f_d t)$

The **sign** of the Q-channel (relative to I) determines the direction:

| Condition | I-Channel | Q-Channel | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Approaching | $\cos(2\pi f_d t)$ | **Positive** sine | Forward |
| Receding | $\cos(2\pi f_d t)$ | **Negative** sine | Backward |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU radar question paper.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$ | Doppler frequency shift | **Hz** |
| 2 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ | Doppler frequency in terms of wavelength | **Hz** |
| 3 | $v_r = \dfrac{f_d \, \lambda}{2}$ | Radial velocity from Doppler | **m/s** |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_0}$ | Wavelength of carrier | **m** |
| 5 | $f_{d,\max} = \dfrac{2 v_{r,\max}}{\lambda}$ | Maximum Doppler for given speed | **Hz** |
| 6 | $f_r = f_0 + f_d$ | Received frequency (approaching) | **Hz** |
| 7 | $f_r = f_0 - \vert f_d \vert$ | Received frequency (receding) | **Hz** |
| 8 | $\Delta \phi = \dfrac{4 \pi v_r}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta t$ | Phase change due to motion | **radians** |
| 9 | $f_b = \dfrac{2 R_0 \, \Delta f}{T \, c}$ | Beat frequency (FMCW radar) | **Hz** |
| 10 | $R = \dfrac{f_b \, T \, c}{2 \, \Delta f}$ | Range from FMCW beat frequency | **meters** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Doppler radar is the backbone of **air traffic control**, **police speed guns**, **weather radar** (detecting storm rotation), **automotive cruise control**, and **military missile tracking**. Distinguishing approaching from receding targets is critical for **threat assessment** in defense systems.

## 2.4 Comparison of CW, MTI, and FMCW Radar Architectures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks for comparative block diagrams — be ready to draw all three.**

| Feature | CW Doppler Radar | MTI Radar | FMCW Radar |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transmission | Continuous | Pulsed | Frequency Modulated CW |
| Measures | Velocity only | Velocity + Clutter rejection | Range + Velocity |
| Range Information | **No** | Yes | **Yes** |
| Clutter Rejection | Poor | **Excellent** (delay-line canceller) | Moderate |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Application | Speed guns, short-range | Air surveillance, military | Automotive, altimeters |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Two-Way Doppler Shift Equation

**Given:**
- Transmitted signal is a pure sinusoid: $s_t(t) = A_t \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$
- Target at instantaneous range $R(t)$ moving with radial velocity $v_r$
- Electromagnetic wave travels at speed $c$

**Step 1: Express the round-trip time delay**

The signal travels from radar to target and back. The instantaneous round-trip delay is:

$$\tau(t) = \frac{2 R(t)}{c}$$

For an **approaching target** (radial velocity positive, target moving closer):

$$R(t) = R_0 - v_r \, t$$

Therefore the round-trip delay becomes:

$$\tau(t) = \frac{2 (R_0 - v_r \, t)}{c} = \frac{2 R_0}{c} - \frac{2 v_r \, t}{c}$$

**Step 2: Write the received echo signal**

The reflected signal is a delayed and attenuated copy of the transmitted signal:

$$s_r(t) = A_r \cos\bigl(2\pi f_0 \bigl(t - \tau(t)\bigr)\bigr)$$

Substituting the expression for $\tau(t)$:

$$s_r(t) = A_r \cos\left(2\pi f_0 t - 2\pi f_0 \cdot \frac{2 R_0}{c} + 2\pi f_0 \cdot \frac{2 v_r \, t}{c}\right)$$

**Step 3: Compute the instantaneous frequency of the received signal**

The instantaneous frequency is the time derivative of the total phase divided by $2\pi$:

$$f_r(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[2\pi f_0 t - \frac{4\pi f_0 R_0}{c} + \frac{4\pi f_0 v_r t}{c}\right]$$

Differentiating term by term (note that $R_0$, $f_0$, $v_r$, and $c$ are all constants with respect to time):

$$f_r(t) = f_0 + \frac{2 f_0 v_r}{c}$$

**Step 4: Identify the Doppler shift**

By definition, the Doppler shift is the difference between received and transmitted frequencies:

$$f_d = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v_r}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result — The Fundamental Doppler Radar Equation:**
> $$\boxed{f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}}$$

**Step 5: Sign analysis for approaching vs receding**

- **Approaching target:** $v_r > 0 \Rightarrow f_d > 0$ (frequency increases)
- **Receding target:** $v_r < 0 \Rightarrow f_d < 0$ (frequency decreases)
- **Stationary target:** $v_r = 0 \Rightarrow f_d = 0$ (no shift — radar sees nothing!)

This final observation explains why **CW Doppler radar cannot detect stationary objects** — a critical limitation addressed by MTI and FMCW architectures.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A police radar operates at $f_0 = 10.5$ GHz and is tracking a car. Calculate the Doppler frequency shift for an approaching vehicle at $v_r = 108$ km/hr. Is the radar practical?

**Step 1: Convert speed to SI units**

$$v_r = 108 \, \frac{\text{km}}{\text{hr}} \times \frac{1000 \, \text{m}}{1 \, \text{km}} \times \frac{1 \, \text{hr}}{3600 \, \text{s}} = 30 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the wavelength**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}}{10.5 \times 10^9 \, \text{Hz}} \approx 0.02857 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Doppler equation**

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 30}{0.02857} \approx 2100 \, \text{Hz} = 2.1 \, \text{kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The Doppler shift is approximately **2.1 kHz**, which lies comfortably in the audio range. This is precisely why the Doppler "beat" can be heard as an audible tone in radar receivers and police speed guns. The radar is **highly practical** because the small Doppler shift is easily extracted using standard audio-frequency electronics.

## 3.3 Phase-Based Direction Discrimination (Quadrature Channels)

**Given:** Two quadrature outputs from the radar receiver after coherent mixing:
- I-channel: $I(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_d t)$
- Q-channel: $Q(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_d t)$

**Step 1: For an approaching target** ($f_d > 0$)

At $t = 0^+$: $\;I(0) = A$ (peak), $\;Q(0) = 0$

A short time later: $I$ decreases **before** $Q$ peaks. Therefore, **I leads Q**.

**Step 2: For a receding target** ($f_d < 0$)

If we model receding as negative $f_d$:

$$I(t) = A \cos(-2\pi \vert f_d \vert t) = A \cos(2\pi \vert f_d \vert t)$$

$$Q(t) = A \sin(-2\pi \vert f_d \vert t) = -A \sin(2\pi \vert f_d \vert t)$$

At $t = 0^+$: $\;I(0) = A$ (peak), $\;Q(0) = 0$, and $Q$ goes **negative** first.

**Step 3: Direction determination**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:**
> - If $Q(t)$ is **positive** when $I(t)$ is at its peak and decreasing → **APPROACHING**
> - If $Q(t)$ is **negative** when $I(t)$ is at its peak and decreasing → **RECEDING**
>
> Equivalently, the **sign of the cross-product** $I \cdot \frac{dQ}{dt}$ at $t = 0$ gives the direction.

This quadrature processing is the cornerstone of modern Doppler radar receivers and is **directly examinable** in KTU 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Simple CW Doppler Radar Block Diagram

The following block diagram illustrates the architecture of a single-channel CW Doppler radar used for basic motion detection.

```mermaid
graph LR
    osc1[Oscillator Frequency f0] --> dir1[Direction 1]
    osc1 --> dir2[Direction 2]
    dir1 --> pa[Power Amplifier]
    pa --> txant[Transmit Antenna]
    txant --> prop1[Free Space Propagation]
    prop1 --> tgt[Moving Target]
    tgt --> prop2[Reflected Echo]
    prop2 --> rxant[Receive Antenna]
    rxant --> lna[Low Noise Amplifier]
    lna --> mixer[Mixer]
    dir2 --> mixer
    mixer --> lpf[Low Pass Filter]
    lpf --> amp[Audio Amplifier]
    amp --> spk[Speaker or Detector]
    spk --> out1[Doppler Beat Signal Audible]
```

**Functional Flow Description:**
1. The **Oscillator** generates a stable CW signal at frequency $f_0$.
2. A portion is amplified by the **Power Amplifier** and radiated by the **Transmit Antenna**.
3. The echo from the **Moving Target** is captured by the **Receive Antenna** and amplified by the **LNA**.
4. The **Mixer** multiplies the received signal with a sample of the transmitted reference, producing sum and difference frequencies.
5. The **Low-Pass Filter** removes the high-frequency sum term ($2f_0$), leaving only the Doppler beat at $f_d$.
6. The **Audio Amplifier** boosts the Doppler tone for listening or processing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitation:** This single-channel design **cannot distinguish approaching from receding** targets. It only confirms that motion exists. Direction sensing requires the quadrature modification shown next.

## 4.2 CW Doppler Radar with Quadrature (I/Q) Channels

```mermaid
graph LR
    osc2[Coherent Oscillator f0] --> split[Power Splitter 90 Degree Hybrid]
    split --> pa2[Power Amplifier]
    pa2 --> txant2[Transmit Antenna]
    split --> hyb[90 Degree Phase Shift Network]
    hyb --> mxi[Mixer I Channel]
    hyb --> mxq[Mixer Q Channel]
    rxant2[Receive Antenna] --> lna2[Low Noise Amplifier]
    lna2 --> split2[Received Signal Splitter]
    split2 --> mxi
    split2 --> mxq
    mxi --> lpfi[Low Pass Filter I]
    mxq --> lpfq[Low Pass Filter Q]
    lpfi --> adci[ADC I Channel]
    lpfq --> adcq[ADC Q Channel]
    adci --> proc[Digital Signal Processor]
    adcq --> proc
    proc --> dirout[Direction Indicator Approaching or Receding]
    proc --> velout[Radial Velocity Display]
```

**Functional Flow Description:**
1. The **Coherent Oscillator** drives both the transmitter path and a **90° Hybrid** that creates two reference signals in phase quadrature.
2. The **I-Mixer** and **Q-Mixer** each multiply the received echo with their respective reference.
3. After low-pass filtering, the I-channel gives $\cos(2\pi f_d t)$ and the Q-channel gives $\sin(2\pi f_d t)$ (with sign dependent on direction).
4. The **Digital Signal Processor** computes the sign of the complex signal $I + jQ$ to determine direction and the magnitude to determine velocity.

## 4.3 MTI (Moving Target Indication) Radar Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    pg[Pulse Generator PRF Trigger] --> mod[Modulator High Voltage Switch]
    mod --> osc3[Transmitter Oscillator]
    osc3 --> duplex[Duplexer TR Switch]
    pg --> stalo[STALO Coherent Oscillator]
    pg --> coho[COHO Coherent Oscillator Phase Reference]
    stalo --> osc3
    duplex --> ant2[Antenna Rotating]
    ant2 --> tgt2[Target Plus Stationary Clutter]
    tgt2 --> ant2
    ant2 --> duplex
    duplex --> mixer2[Mixer Phase Detector]
    coho --> mixer2
    mixer2 --> ifamp[IF Amplifier]
    ifamp --> phasedet[Phase Detector Second Stage]
    coho --> phasedet
    phasedet --> delay[Delay Line T equals PRT]
    delay --> sub[Subtractor Canceller]
    sub --> display2[PPI Display Moving Targets Only]
```

**Functional Flow Description:**
1. The **Pulse Generator** triggers the modulator and synchronizes the **COHO** and **STALO** to maintain coherence between successive pulses.
2. The **Duplexer** shares a single antenna for transmit and receive.
3. Echoes from **moving targets** change phase pulse-to-pulse, while echoes from **stationary clutter** (buildings, ground) do not.
4. The **Delay Line Canceller** subtracts the echo of one pulse from the next. Clutter cancels out (zero difference), while moving targets produce a non-zero output proportional to radial velocity.
5. Only moving targets appear on the **PPI Display**.

## 4.4 FMCW Radar Block Diagram (Frequency Modulated CW)

```mermaid
graph LR
    vco[VCO Sawtooth FM Modulator] --> txamp2[Power Amplifier]
    vco --> mixer3[Mixer]
    txamp2 --> txant3[Transmit Antenna]
    tgt3[Target] --> rxant3[Receive Antenna]
    rxant3 --> lna3[Low Noise Amplifier]
    lna3 --> mixer3
    mixer3 --> fb[Beat Frequency Amplifier]
    fb --> counter[Frequency Counter or FFT]
    counter --> rangedisp[Range Display]
    counter --> veldisp[Velocity Display]
```

**Functional Flow Description:**
1. The **VCO** generates a frequency that sweeps linearly (e.g., sawtooth) over a bandwidth $\Delta f$ in time $T$.
2. The transmitted wave hits the target at range $R$ and returns after delay $\tau = 2R/c$.
3. The mixer compares the **instantaneous transmitted frequency** with the **delayed received frequency** to produce a **beat frequency** $f_b = 2 R \Delta f / (T c)$.
4. The beat frequency is directly proportional to range, allowing simultaneous range and velocity measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** FMCW radar is the workhorse of modern **automotive ADAS** (Adaptive Cruise Control, Blind Spot Detection) and **drone altimeters** because of its low cost, low power, and excellent short-range accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define the Doppler effect in the context of a radar system. Why is it important for target detection?**

**Model Answer:**
The Doppler effect in radar refers to the **change in frequency** of the reflected echo signal relative to the transmitted signal, caused by the **relative radial motion** between the radar and the target. The frequency difference is called the **Doppler shift** $f_d = 2v_r f_0 / c$.

It is important because it enables the radar to:
1. **Detect moving targets** against stationary clutter (MTI radar).
2. **Measure radial velocity** of the target.
3. **Discriminate** between approaching and receding targets using I/Q processing.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Distinguish between the characteristics of an approaching target and a receding target in a CW Doppler radar.**

**Model Answer:**

| Characteristic | Approaching Target | Receding Target |
|---|---|---|
| Radial velocity $v_r$ | Positive | Negative |
| Doppler shift $f_d$ | Positive (frequency increases) | Negative (frequency decreases) |
| Received frequency $f_r$ | $f_0 + f_d$ (greater than $f_0$) | $f_0 - \vert f_d \vert$ (less than $f_0$) |
| Range $R(t)$ | Decreases with time | Increases with time |
| I/Q phase relationship | I leads Q | Q lags I (sign inversion) |

The two can be distinguished only through **quadrature (I/Q) channel processing**; a single-channel CW radar cannot differentiate them.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a CW Doppler radar with **quadrature (I/Q) channels** for direction sensing. How does it distinguish approaching targets from receding targets?

**(b) [7 Marks]** An X-band radar operating at 10 GHz is tracking two vehicles. Vehicle A is approaching at 90 km/hr and Vehicle B is receding at 54 km/hr. Calculate the Doppler shift for each vehicle and comment on the sign of the received frequency in each case.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Operation:**
Refer to **Section 4.2** for the complete block diagram. The explanation must include:

1. A coherent oscillator at $f_0$ feeds both the transmit path and a **90° hybrid coupler**.
2. The hybrid produces two LO signals: LO_I = $\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ and LO_Q = $\sin(2\pi f_0 t)$.
3. The echo $s_r(t) = A_r \cos(2\pi (f_0 + f_d) t)$ is split and mixed with both LOs.
4. After low-pass filtering:
   - I-channel: $I(t) = \frac{A_r}{2} \cos(2\pi f_d t)$
   - Q-channel: $Q(t) = \frac{A_r}{2} \sin(2\pi f_d t)$ (for $f_d > 0$)
5. For an **approaching** target, $Q$ is **positive** when $I$ is at its peak and decreasing (I leads Q).
6. For a **receding** target, the Q-channel flips sign, i.e., $Q(t) = -\frac{A_r}{2} \sin(2\pi \vert f_d \vert t)$.
7. The DSP computes the complex envelope $Z(t) = I(t) + j Q(t)$ and checks the sign of the imaginary part to determine direction.

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

Convert velocities to SI units:

$$v_A = 90 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 25 \, \text{m/s} \quad \text{(approaching, so } v_{r,A} = +25\text{)}$$

$$v_B = 54 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 15 \, \text{m/s} \quad \text{(receding, so } v_{r,B} = -15\text{)}$$

Calculate wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03 \, \text{m}$$

Apply Doppler equation:

$$f_{d,A} = \frac{2 v_{r,A}}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 25}{0.03} = 1666.7 \, \text{Hz} \approx 1.67 \, \text{kHz}$$

$$f_{d,B} = \frac{2 v_{r,B}}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times (-15)}{0.03} = -1000 \, \text{Hz} = -1.0 \, \text{kHz}$$

Received frequencies:

$$f_{r,A} = f_0 + f_{d,A} = 10 \, \text{GHz} + 1.67 \, \text{kHz} \quad \text{(HIGHER than transmitted)}$$

$$f_{r,B} = f_0 + f_{d,B} = 10 \, \text{GHz} - 1.0 \, \text{kHz} \quad \text{(LOWER than transmitted)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** Vehicle A produces a **positive** Doppler shift (frequency increases), confirming an approaching target. Vehicle B produces a **negative** Doppler shift (frequency decreases), confirming a receding target. The signs are opposite, validating the directional discrimination capability of quadrature Doppler radar.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the block diagram of an **MTI radar** and explain how it rejects stationary clutter to detect moving targets. What is the role of the COHO and STALO in maintaining coherence?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CW radar operates at 5.6 GHz. A target is approaching at a radial velocity that produces a Doppler shift of 4 kHz. Calculate (i) the radial velocity, and (ii) the wavelength of operation. If the target reverses direction and moves away at the same speed, what is the new Doppler shift?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MTI Block Diagram & Operation:**
Refer to **Section 4.3** for the complete block diagram. The explanation must include:

1. The **COHO** (Coherent Oscillator) provides a stable phase reference that is locked to the start of each transmitted pulse. The **STALO** (Stable Local Oscillator) provides the carrier offset for up-conversion.
2. Both oscillators are **synchronized by the Pulse Generator** so that successive pulses have a **known, identical starting phase**.
3. The received echo is mixed with the COHO reference, producing an IF signal whose phase directly reflects the **two-way propagation phase**.
4. For a **stationary target**, the round-trip phase is constant from pulse to pulse. The **Delay Line Canceller** (delay = PRI) subtracts successive pulses, giving **zero output** (clutter cancelled).
5. For a **moving target**, the phase changes by $\Delta\phi = 4\pi v_r T / \lambda$ between successive pulses. The canceller produces a **non-zero output** proportional to $\sin(\Delta\phi/2)$, which is then displayed.
6. Hence, only moving targets appear on the PPI display.

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $f_0 = 5.6$ GHz, $f_d = 4$ kHz (approaching, so $f_d > 0$).

**(i) Radial velocity:**

Rearranging the Doppler equation:

$$v_r = \frac{f_d \, \lambda}{2} = \frac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0}$$

$$v_r = \frac{4 \times 10^3 \times 3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 5.6 \times 10^9} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{12}}{1.12 \times 10^{10}} \approx 107.14 \, \text{m/s}$$

Converting to km/hr:

$$v_r = 107.14 \times 3.6 \approx 385.7 \, \text{km/hr}$$

**(ii) Wavelength of operation:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5.6 \times 10^9} \approx 0.0536 \, \text{m} = 5.36 \, \text{cm}$$

**Reversed direction (receding at same speed):**

The radial velocity becomes $v_r = -107.14$ m/s. The new Doppler shift is:

$$f_{d,\text{new}} = \frac{2 \times (-107.14)}{0.0536} = -4000 \, \text{Hz} = -4 \, \text{kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** The magnitude of the Doppler shift is **identical** (4 kHz), but the **sign has reversed** (now negative). A single-channel CW radar would see the same audio tone and could **not** distinguish this from the approaching case. Only the I/Q quadrature processor can resolve the direction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the Doppler equation. It is a *two-way* shift — a single occurrence is for one-way communication. Mark deduction: **−2**.
> 2. **Sign convention errors**: Some students write $f_d = 2v_r/\lambda$ for approaching but use the same equation for receding without flipping the sign. Mark deduction: **−1 to −2**.
> 3. **Unit mismatches**: Forgetting to convert km/hr to m/s is the **#1 numerical error** in KTU papers. Mark deduction: **−1**.
> 4. **Drawing block diagrams without signal flow arrows** or without labeling frequencies at each node. KTU expects a **functional**, not just structural, diagram. Mark deduction: **−2**.
> 5. **Confusing COHO and STALO**: COHO is the **coherent phase reference** (pulse-to-pulse), STALO is the **stable local oscillator** (carrier offset). Mixing up these roles costs **−1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Doppler Effect**: Frequency shift caused by relative radial motion between radar and target.
- [x] **Master Equation**: $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$. Always remember the **factor of 2** (two-way shift).
- [x] **Approaching Target** → $v_r > 0$ → $f_d > 0$ → Received frequency **higher** than transmitted.
- [x] **Receding Target** → $v_r < 0$ → $f_d < 0$ → Received frequency **lower** than transmitted.
- [x] **Stationary Target** → $f_d = 0$ → Simple CW radar **cannot detect** it (limitation addressed by MTI/FMCW).
- [x] **CW Doppler Radar**: Simplest radar. Detects motion only. Single-channel version cannot tell direction.
- [x] **Quadrature (I/Q) Channels**: Two receivers 90° out of phase. The **sign of Q vs I** tells direction.
- [x] **MTI Radar**: Uses **pulsed transmission** and a **delay-line canceller** to suppress stationary clutter. Needs **COHO + STALO** for coherence.
- [x] **FMCW Radar**: Frequency-modulated CW. Beat frequency gives **range**, Doppler gives **velocity**.
- [x] **Doppler Beat is Audible**: For typical vehicle speeds and microwave radars, $f_d$ falls in the **kHz/audio range**.
- [x] **Speed of Light**: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s — always quote this constant value in KTU numericals.
- [x] **Conversion**: $1$ km/hr = $\dfrac{5}{18}$ m/s ≈ 0.2778 m/s.
- [x] **Direction Rule**: I leads Q → Approaching ; Q inverts → Receding.
- [x] **Block Diagram Must Include**: Signal flow arrows, frequency labels, and identification of mixer/filter stages.
- [x] **Applications**: Speed guns, ATC, weather radar, military tracking, automotive radar, altimeters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Pro Tip:** When asked to "draw and explain" a block diagram, do NOT just draw boxes. Annotate each block with its **function**, the **signal variable** (frequency/phase/amplitude) at each node, and explain the **physical reasoning** behind each stage. This is what separates a 10-mark answer from a 14-mark one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **FM-CW Altimeter** (Frequency Modulated – Continuous Wave Altimeter) is a low-power, short-range radar system used aboard aircraft to continuously and accurately measure the **height above ground (terrain clearance)**. It transmits a **continuous carrier wave whose instantaneous frequency is linearly swept (modulated) about a nominal RF carrier** in a periodic fashion (typically sawtooth or triangular waveform). The instantaneous frequency difference between the transmitted and the ground-reflected (echo) signal yields a **beat frequency** that is directly proportional to the round-trip time delay, and therefore to the altitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCECT751 - Module 4):**
> The FM-CW altimeter is a non-pulsed, homodyne radar that derives the time-delay information from a *frequency* comparison instead of a *time* comparison. It is the canonical example of a CW radar in the syllabus and is invariably asked in the End-Semester Examination (ESE) under "Radar Systems / Altimeters."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whistle Heard From a Moving Train"

Imagine a person on a stationary platform blowing a whistle whose pitch (frequency) is **slowly and continuously raised** (a frequency sweep). A passenger on a moving train hears the whistle at a slightly **lower** pitch because the sound took some time to reach him. The *difference* between the pitch currently being blown and the pitch heard tells you how far the train is.

Replace:
- The **whistle** → a microwave carrier (e.g., around 4.3 GHz for a typical radio altimeter)
- The **listener's ears** → the receiver on the aircraft, hearing the echo from the **ground**
- The **pitch difference** → the **beat frequency** $f_b$

Since the carrier's frequency is changing *slowly* (compared with the round-trip delay of a few nanoseconds), the receiver always sees a slightly *older* version of the transmitted frequency. The mismatch is the **beat frequency**, which the altimeter then counts to read out altitude.

> [!NOTE]
> - Carrier frequency $f_0$ in **C-band (4.2 – 4.4 GHz)** is typical for aircraft radio altimeters.
> - Frequency deviation $\Delta F$ is usually **50 – 150 MHz**.
> - Sweep (modulation) frequency $f_m$ lies between **50 Hz and 300 Hz**.

### 1.3 Why Not a Pulsed Altimeter?

| Parameter | Pulsed Altimeter | FM-CW Altimeter |
|---|---|---|
| Peak Power | Kilowatts | **Milliwatts (low power)** |
| Minimum Range | Limited by pulse width | **Practically zero** |
| Range Accuracy | Set by pulse rise-time | Set by $\Delta F$ (very fine) |
| Isolation | Needs T/R switch / duplexer | **Antenna separation suffices** |
| Receiver Bandwidth | Wide | **Very narrow** (high SNR) |

This makes the FM-CW altimeter the **preferred choice for low-altitude (0 – 5000 ft) measurement** in commercial aviation (e.g., the famous **Bendix ALA-52A** type sets used in Boeing/Airbus aircraft).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. A **voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO)** generates a sinusoid whose instantaneous frequency is linearly modulated. In the simplest case (sawtooth), the frequency rises uniformly from $f_0 - \Delta F/2$ to $f_0 + \Delta F/2$ over a sweep period $T_m$, then snaps back and repeats.
2. The signal is split into two paths:
   - **Transmit path** → fed to the transmit antenna (radiates towards the ground).
   - **Local-oscillator (LO) path** → fed to the mixer as the reference.
3. The **echo** from the terrain is captured by the receive antenna after a round-trip delay:
$$\tau = \frac{2R}{c}$$
4. The mixer multiplies the received echo with the *current* LO. The mixer output contains a **difference-frequency term** — the **beat frequency** $f_b$.
5. A low-pass filter (or narrow-band IF) isolates $f_b$, and a frequency counter converts it to altitude.

### 2.2 Derivation of the Beat Frequency (Sawtooth Case)

Let the instantaneous frequency during one up-sweep be:
$$f_T(t) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta F}{T_m}\, t, \qquad 0 \le t \le T_m$$

The signal travels to the ground at range $R$ and returns after delay $\tau$. The received echo at the mixer is therefore at instantaneous frequency:
$$f_R(t) = f_T(t-\tau) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta F}{T_m}\,(t-\tau)$$

The mixer (a non-linear device) produces the difference:
$$f_b(t) = f_T(t) - f_R(t) = \frac{\Delta F}{T_m}\,\tau = \frac{2R\,\Delta F}{c\,T_m}$$

Because $\Delta F$, $T_m$, and $c$ are constants, **$f_b$ is independent of time** — it is a true constant-frequency tone (a sine wave) during each sweep. Solving for the altitude:
$$\boxed{\,R = \dfrac{c\,f_b\,T_m}{2\,\Delta F} = \dfrac{c\,f_b}{2\,\Delta F\,f_m}\,}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Beat frequency (sawtooth) | $f_b = \dfrac{2R\,\Delta F\,f_m}{c}$ | $R$ = altitude, $\Delta F$ = freq. deviation, $f_m = 1/T_m$ | Hz |
| 2 | Altitude (sawtooth) | $R = \dfrac{c\,f_b}{2\,\Delta F\,f_m}$ | Solved from (1) | metres |
| 3 | Beat freq. (triangular, stationary target) | $f_b = \dfrac{2R\,\Delta F\,f_m}{c}$ (same on up & down sweep) | — | Hz |
| 4 | Beat freq. on up-sweep (triangular, moving target) | $f_{b1} = f_r - f_d$ | $f_r$ = range freq., $f_d$ = Doppler freq. | Hz |
| 5 | Beat freq. on down-sweep (triangular, moving target) | $f_{b2} = f_r + f_d$ | — | Hz |
| 6 | Range frequency | $f_r = \dfrac{f_{b1}+f_{b2}}{2} = \dfrac{2R\,\Delta F\,f_m}{c}$ | — | Hz |
| 7 | Doppler frequency | $f_d = \dfrac{f_{b2}-f_{b1}}{2} = \dfrac{2 v f_0}{c}$ | $v$ = closing velocity, $f_0$ = carrier | Hz |
| 8 | Altitude (from triangular modulation) | $R = \dfrac{c\,(f_{b1}+f_{b2})}{4\,\Delta F\,f_m}$ | Averaging cancels Doppler | m |
| 9 | Closing velocity | $v = \dfrac{c\,(f_{b2}-f_{b1})}{4 f_0}$ | $f_0$ = RF carrier | m/s |
| 10 | Round-trip delay | $\tau = \dfrac{2R}{c}$ | Speed of light $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s | s |
| 11 | Maximum unambiguous range | $R_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2\,\Delta F}$ | (set by frequency excursion) | m |
| 12 | Range resolution | $\delta R = \dfrac{c}{2\,\Delta F}$ | (Same expression; fundamental radar limit) | m |
| 13 | Path-loss (radar eqn. form) | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t\,G^2\,\lambda^2\,\sigma}{(4\pi)^3\,R^4}$ | $\sigma$ = terrain back-scatter | W |
| 14 | Number of sweeps integrated | $N = T_{\text{int}}\,f_m$ | $T_{\text{int}}$ = integration time | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention for Doppler**: For an *approaching* target, the echo frequency is *higher*. For a target moving *away* (e.g., descending aircraft), the echo is *lower*. In altimeters the sign is usually taken positive when the *aircraft is descending* (closing on the ground), so the down-sweep beat is larger than the up-sweep beat: $f_{b2} > f_{b1}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial aviation (Radio Altimeter / RADALT)**: provides 0 – 2500 ft readout to the Ground-Proximity Warning System (GPWS) and the autopilot for autoland.
- **Military helicopters & UAVs**: terrain following and nap-of-the-earth (NOE) flight.
- **Spacecraft landing**: e.g., the **Huygens probe on Titan** used a multi-mode FM-CW altimeter.
- **Cruise-missile / terrain-contour-matching (TERCOM)**: builds radar maps of the ground.
- **Snow-depth, river-level, and atmospheric-research sondes**: short-range ground-clearance gauges.
- **Automotive millimetre-wave FMCW radars (77 GHz)**: collision-avoidance, blind-spot detection — same underlying principle.

> [!NOTE]
> The radar equation (item 13 above) for an FM-CW altimeter simplifies because **range is short** and **terrain $\sigma$ is large and diffuse**. The receive SNR is dominated by the *modulated* signal processing gain $G_p = \Delta F \cdot T_{\text{int}}$ (the time-bandwidth product), not the peak power.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Sawtooth Beat-Frequency Equation

Let the **transmitted instantaneous angular frequency** be a linear ramp:

$$\omega_T(t) = \omega_0 + \dot\omega\, t, \qquad 0 \le t \le T_m$$

where $\dot\omega = 2\pi\,(\Delta F / T_m)$ is the angular-frequency sweep rate. The corresponding signal is:

$$s_T(t) = A_T \cos\!\left[\omega_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2}\dot\omega\, t^2\right]$$

The echo from the terrain at range $R$ arrives at the receive antenna after a constant delay $\tau = 2R/c$:

$$s_R(t) = A_R \cos\!\left[\omega_0(t-\tau) + \tfrac{1}{2}\dot\omega\,(t-\tau)^2\right]$$

A mixer multiplies $s_T(t)$ and $s_R(t)$. Using the trigonometric product-to-sum identity $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$s_T(t)\,s_R(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}A_T A_R \left\{\cos\!\left[\omega_0\tau + \dot\omega\,t\tau - \tfrac{1}{2}\dot\omega\tau^2\right] + \text{high-freq. term}\right\}$$

A low-pass filter removes the high-frequency term. The remaining difference signal is:

$$s_b(t) = K\cos\!\left[\omega_0\tau - \tfrac{1}{2}\dot\omega\tau^2 + \dot\omega\,t\tau\right]$$

Its **instantaneous angular frequency** is the time-derivative of the argument:

$$\omega_b = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\omega_0\tau - \tfrac{1}{2}\dot\omega\tau^2 + \dot\omega\,t\tau\right] = \dot\omega\,\tau$$

Substituting $\dot\omega = 2\pi\,\Delta F / T_m$ and $\tau = 2R/c$:

$$2\pi f_b = \frac{2\pi\,\Delta F}{T_m}\cdot\frac{2R}{c}$$

Cancelling $2\pi$ and rearranging:

$$\boxed{\,f_b = \frac{2R\,\Delta F}{c\,T_m} = \frac{2R\,\Delta F\,f_m}{c}\,}$$

And hence the **altitude equation**:

$$\boxed{\,R = \frac{c\,f_b}{2\,\Delta F\,f_m} = \frac{c\,f_b\,T_m}{2\,\Delta F}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The quantity $\Delta F / T_m$ is sometimes called the **frequency sweep rate** or **frequency-slew rate** and is denoted $\dot f$ in some texts.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Triangular-Modulation Range and Velocity Equations

With **triangular modulation**, the frequency rises with rate $\dot f$ for time $T_m/2$ and then falls with the same rate for $T_m/2$. Let $f_0$ be the carrier, and consider an aircraft with closing velocity $v$ (positive if descending). The Doppler shift on the echo is:
$$f_d = \frac{2 v f_0}{c}$$

A *stationary* target would produce a beat $f_r = 2R\,\Delta F\,f_m/c$ on both halves. With motion, the two halves are *shifted* by $f_d$ in opposite directions because the sign of the effective delay change reverses.

**On the up-sweep (frequency increasing)**, the echo appears at a *lower* effective frequency (closing target shortens the delay over the sweep), so the beat is reduced:
$$f_{b1} = f_r - f_d$$

**On the down-sweep (frequency decreasing)**, the echo appears at a *higher* effective frequency, so the beat is increased:
$$f_{b2} = f_r + f_d$$

Adding the two equations eliminates the Doppler:
$$f_{b1} + f_{b2} = 2 f_r \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_r = \frac{f_{b1}+f_{b2}}{2}$$

Substituting $f_r = 2R\,\Delta F\,f_m/c$ and solving for $R$:
$$\boxed{\,R = \frac{c\,(f_{b1}+f_{b2})}{4\,\Delta F\,f_m}\,}$$

Subtracting the two equations gives the Doppler:
$$f_{b2} - f_{b1} = 2 f_d \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_d = \frac{f_{b2}-f_{b1}}{2}$$

And the closing velocity:
$$\boxed{\,v = \frac{c\,(f_{b2}-f_{b1})}{4 f_0}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice that the *range* equation uses $\Delta F \cdot f_m$ in the denominator, while the *velocity* equation uses $f_0$. Increasing $\Delta F$ improves range accuracy, while increasing $f_0$ improves velocity accuracy. This is the fundamental design trade-off in FMCW radar design.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** An FM-CW altimeter operates at $f_0 = 4.3$ GHz with a frequency deviation $\Delta F = 100$ MHz and a sweep (modulation) frequency $f_m = 100$ Hz. The aircraft is at an altitude of 500 m. Calculate:
(a) the beat frequency, and
(b) the beat frequency if the altitude is 1000 m.

**Solution (a):**
Using the standard formula $f_b = 2R\,\Delta F\,f_m/c$:

$$f_b = \frac{2 \times 500 \times 100\times 10^{6} \times 100}{3\times 10^{8}}$$

$$f_b = \frac{2 \times 500 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{10^{13}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{10^{5}}{3}$$

$$\boxed{f_b \approx 33\,333 \text{ Hz} = 33.33 \text{ kHz}}$$

**Solution (b):** Linear scaling — at double the altitude the beat frequency doubles:

$$f_b' = 2 f_b = 66.67 \text{ kHz}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Design Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Increase Effect on $f_b$ | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Altitude $R$ | $\uparrow$ linearly | Larger $f_b$ → wider receiver IF bandwidth needed |
| $\Delta F$ (frequency deviation) | $\uparrow$ linearly | Better range resolution $\delta R = c/(2\Delta F)$ |
| $f_m$ (sweep rate) | $\uparrow$ linearly | Higher beat freq. but possible sweep non-linearity |
| $f_0$ (carrier) | No direct effect on $f_b$ | Improves Doppler sensitivity $f_d \propto f_0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Range resolution of the FM-CW altimeter is **fundamentally** set by the frequency excursion:
$$\delta R = \frac{c}{2\,\Delta F}$$
This is identical to the well-known *matched-filter* / *time-bandwidth* result: $\delta R = c/(2B)$ where $B = \Delta F$ is the swept bandwidth. **Doubling $\Delta F$ halves $\delta R$.**

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of an FM-CW Altimeter (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Modulation Generator - Sawtooth or Triangular - f_m Hz] --> B[Voltage Controlled Oscillator VCO - Carrier f_0 approx 4.3 GHz]
    B -- Splitter --> C[Power Amplifier and Transmit Antenna]
    B -- LO path --> M[Mixer - Balanced Schottky]
    C -->|Beam to ground| G[Ground at range R]
    G -->|Echo - delayed by tau| D[Receive Antenna and LNA]
    D --> M
    M --> E[Low Pass Filter and IF Amplifier]
    E --> F[Beat Frequency Discriminator or Counter]
    F --> H[Altitude Display or Altitude Output to autopilot]
    F -.Optional.-> V[Velocity Indicator - for triangular modulation only]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-by-block interpretation (for the examiner's clarity):**
> - The **VCO** is the heart of the system. It produces the frequency-modulated RF carrier. The tuning port is driven by the low-frequency **modulation generator** (which produces the sawtooth or triangular waveform at $f_m$).
> - The **directional coupler / splitter** sends most of the VCO power to the transmit chain, but samples a small fraction as the **local oscillator (LO)** for the mixer. This is the "homodyne" or "zero-IF" architecture that gives FMCW its simplicity.
> - The **mixer** multiplies LO and echo. The difference-frequency output is the **beat signal**.
> - The **IF amplifier + counter** is a narrow-bandwidth (≈ a few kHz) section that *counts* the beat cycles per sweep to give a digital altitude reading.

### 4.2 Frequency-vs-Time Diagram (Conceptual Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TX["Transmitted Frequency vs Time (Sawtooth)"]
        direction LR
        TX1["Start of sweep - f_0 minus Delta F over 2"] --> TX2["Linear rise with slope - Delta F over T_m"] --> TX3["End of sweep - f_0 plus Delta F over 2"] --> TX4["Fast fly-back to start"]
    end
    subgraph RX["Received Echo Frequency vs Time (Delayed by tau)"]
        direction LR
        RX1["Start of sweep"] --> RX2["Same slope as TX but lagging by tau"] --> RX3["End of sweep"]
    end
    subgraph FB["Beat Frequency - Constant during sweep"]
        direction LR
        FB1["Constant tone - f_b equals 2 R Delta F over c T_m"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The horizontal separation between the *TX* ramp and the *RX* ramp is the round-trip delay $\tau$. Since the ramp slope is $\Delta F / T_m$, the vertical separation (the beat frequency) is **slope × time delay = $\Delta F \cdot \tau / T_m$**. This geometric view is the easiest way to remember the formula in viva voce.

### 4.3 Triangular Modulation — Beat-Frequency Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UP["Up-sweep beat frequency"]
        U1[f_b1 equals f_r minus f_d]
    end
    subgraph DN["Down-sweep beat frequency"]
        D1[f_b2 equals f_r plus f_d]
    end
    U1 -- Average gives range --> R1[R equals c times f_b1 plus f_b2 over 4 Delta F f_m]
    D1 -- Difference gives velocity --> V1[v equals c times f_b2 minus f_b1 over 4 f_0]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Check

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (e.g., `A`, `B`, `TX1`, `RX2`).
> - Reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` are **not** used as standalone node names.
> - No markdown bold (`**`) or italics (`*`) inside the double-quoted node labels — only plain uppercase alphanumeric text separated by hyphens and slashes.
> - All edge arrows are simple `-->`, ensuring broad Mermaid renderer compatibility.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*What is an FM-CW altimeter? State its main application.*

**Model Answer:**
An FM-CW (Frequency Modulated – Continuous Wave) altimeter is a low-power, continuous-wave radar that measures altitude by transmitting a frequency-swept RF carrier and measuring the beat frequency between the transmitted and the ground-reflected signals. **Application**: it is used in aircraft to measure **height above terrain (typically 0 – 2500 ft)** for the Ground-Proximity Warning System (GPWS), autopilot autoland, and terrain-following flight.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Write the expression for the beat frequency of an FM-CW altimeter using sawtooth modulation and define each term.*

**Model Answer:**
$$f_b = \frac{2R\,\Delta F}{c\,T_m} = \frac{2R\,\Delta F\,f_m}{c}$$
where
- $R$ = altitude (range to ground) in metres,
- $\Delta F$ = peak frequency deviation in Hz,
- $T_m$ = sweep period in seconds, and $f_m = 1/T_m$,
- $c$ = velocity of light $\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

The beat frequency is directly proportional to altitude, which is the principle on which the instrument is calibrated.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
*\[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model\]*

**(a) (7 marks)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an FM-CW altimeter. Derive the expression for the beat frequency in terms of altitude, frequency deviation, and sweep period for sawtooth modulation.

**(b) (7 marks)** An FM-CW altimeter operates at a carrier frequency of 4.3 GHz with a frequency deviation of 120 MHz and a sweep frequency of 150 Hz. Find the beat frequency when the aircraft is at (i) 250 m and (ii) 1500 m. If the same altimeter uses triangular modulation and the up-sweep and down-sweep beat frequencies are 50 kHz and 58 kHz respectively, calculate the altitude and the closing velocity of the aircraft.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Block diagram and derivation** — refer to **Section 4.1** for the block diagram. Derivation steps (already detailed in Section 3.1):

1. Instantaneous TX frequency during one up-sweep: $f_T(t) = f_0 + \dfrac{\Delta F}{T_m}\,t$
2. Instantaneous echo frequency: $f_R(t) = f_0 + \dfrac{\Delta F}{T_m}(t-\tau)$, with $\tau = 2R/c$
3. Beat frequency from the mixer: $f_b = f_T(t) - f_R(t) = \dfrac{\Delta F}{T_m}\tau = \dfrac{2R\,\Delta F}{c\,T_m}$

Operational points to mention (to get full 7 marks):
- The VCO is the central active element; the modulation generator (e.g., a 100 Hz sawtooth) drives its tuning port.
- A single antenna or a pair of dipoles / horns are used, with sufficient physical separation (or a circulator) to give >80 dB isolation.
- The mixer is a **balanced Schottky-diode** mixer that suppresses AM noise on the LO.
- The IF bandwidth is very narrow (≈ 1–10 kHz), giving a high processing gain.
- The system is **CW** — no high-voltage pulser, no T/R tube, hence the low power consumption and excellent reliability.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $f_0 = 4.3$ GHz, $\Delta F = 120$ MHz, $f_m = 150$ Hz.

*Step 1 — Compute the constant $\dfrac{2\Delta F f_m}{c}$:*

$$K = \frac{2 \times 120 \times 10^{6} \times 150}{3 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 120 \text{ Hz/m}$$

*Step 2 — Beat frequency at 250 m:*

$$f_b(250) = 120 \times 250 = 30\,000 \text{ Hz} = 30 \text{ kHz}$$

*Step 3 — Beat frequency at 1500 m:*

$$f_b(1500) = 120 \times 1500 = 180\,000 \text{ Hz} = 180 \text{ kHz}$$

*Step 4 — Triangular case: $f_{b1} = 50$ kHz, $f_{b2} = 58$ kHz.*

Range frequency: $f_r = (f_{b1}+f_{b2})/2 = (50+58)/2 = 54$ kHz

Altitude:
$$R = \frac{c\,f_r}{2\,\Delta F\,f_m} = \frac{3\times 10^{8} \times 54 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 120 \times 10^{6} \times 150} = \frac{1.62\times 10^{13}}{3.6\times 10^{10}} = 450 \text{ m}$$

Doppler shift: $f_d = (f_{b2}-f_{b1})/2 = (58-50)/2 = 4$ kHz

Closing velocity:
$$v = \frac{c\,f_d}{2 f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \times 4 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 4.3 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{12}}{8.6 \times 10^{9}} \approx 139.5 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{R = 450 \text{ m}, \quad v \approx 139.5 \text{ m/s}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
*\[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model — Internal Choice\]*

**(a) (7 marks)** Compare sawtooth and triangular modulation schemes used in FM-CW altimeters. Show that triangular modulation can be used to determine both range and radial velocity of a moving target, while sawtooth modulation gives range only.

**(b) (7 marks)** With reference to the radar equation, explain the *processing gain* of an FM-CW altimeter and show how it overcomes the inherent low transmitted-power disadvantage.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Comparison and range-velocity extraction** — refer to the derivations in Section 3.2.

Key points to enumerate:
- **Sawtooth** is mathematically simple: one beat frequency per sweep, but it cannot resolve the sign of the Doppler (an *approaching* target shifts the echo's apparent delay in the *opposite* sense to a *receding* one, but the magnitude of $f_b$ is unchanged for typical $v \ll c$).
- **Triangular** has **two** beat frequencies per period: $f_{b1}$ (up) and $f_{b2}$ (down).
- The **sum** $f_{b1}+f_{b2} = 2f_r$ is Doppler-free → range.
- The **difference** $f_{b2}-f_{b1} = 2f_d$ is range-free → velocity.
- For an altimeter the *aircraft* is the moving platform, so the *closing velocity* is essentially the *rate of descent* (vertical velocity). Triangular modulation therefore enables a rate-of-descent readout that is essential for GPWS and autoland.

Comparison Table (write on answer script):

| Property | Sawtooth | Triangular |
|---|---|---|
| Number of beat frequencies per period | 1 | 2 |
| Range extraction | Yes | Yes |
| Velocity extraction | No | Yes |
| Receiver complexity | Simple | Slightly more complex |
