| Used in commercial RADALT | Yes (most) | Yes (modern, with $v$ readout) |

**(b) Processing gain of an FM-CW altimeter** — start from the radar equation:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t\,G^2\,\lambda^2\,\sigma}{(4\pi)^3\,R^4}$$

Although $P_t$ is small (≈ 1 W or less), the *bandwidth* of the transmitted signal is very large (≈ $\Delta F = 100$ MHz) and the *integration* time is also long (≈ 1 s). The matched-filter (or coherent integration) processing gain is:

$$G_p = \Delta F \cdot T_{\text{int}}$$

For $\Delta F = 100$ MHz and $T_{\text{int}} = 1$ s, $G_p = 10^{8}$ (= 80 dB). This compensates the low $P_t$ and yields a usable SNR even with milliwatt transmitters. The processing gain is essentially the *time-bandwidth product* of the linear FM sweep, identical to the gain of a chirp pulse-compression radar.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Most common mistake in numericals**: forgetting to convert MHz → Hz and kHz → Hz. **1 mark lost per such error.**
> 2. **Common derivation trap**: students often write $f_b = \Delta F \cdot \tau$ and forget the factor $1/T_m$ that arises from the *slope* of the ramp. **Always** state the slope explicitly.
> 3. **Sign of Doppler**: in the up-sweep, an *approaching* target gives $f_{b1} = f_r - f_d$, **not** $f_r + f_d$. Markers expect the *physical* argument (closing target shortens the delay) and will deduct 1–2 marks for a sign error.
> 4. **Confusing $f_m$ and $1/T_m$**: both are valid; just be consistent.
> 5. **Range resolution $\delta R = c/(2\Delta F)$** is often asked as a one-liner — do not forget it is *independent* of $f_m$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Core equation (must memorise)**: $f_b = \dfrac{2R\,\Delta F\,f_m}{c}$ and $R = \dfrac{c\,f_b}{2\,\Delta F\,f_m}$.
- ✅ **Carrier band**: typically **C-band (4.2 – 4.4 GHz)** for civil aviation radio altimeters; **Ka-band (35 GHz)** for military/precision.
- ✅ **Modulation waveforms**: **sawtooth** (range only) and **triangular** (range + velocity).
- ✅ **Triangular equations**: $R = c(f_{b1}+f_{b2})/(4\Delta F f_m)$; $v = c(f_{b2}-f_{b1})/(4 f_0)$.
- ✅ **Round-trip delay**: $\tau = 2R/c$ — note factor 2 (radar, not lidar).
- ✅ **Range resolution**: $\delta R = c/(2\Delta F)$ — set by the *frequency deviation*, not the carrier.
- ✅ **Maximum unambiguous range**: $R_{\max} = c/(2\Delta F)$ — note it equals the resolution. This is a *fundamental* property of FM-CW.
- ✅ **Processing gain**: $G_p = \Delta F \cdot T_{\text{int}}$ (time-bandwidth product), typically 70 – 90 dB.
- ✅ **Speed of light**: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ **Key advantage**: low peak power (mW range), no T/R switch, very high range accuracy, excellent low-altitude performance.
- ✅ **Key limitation**: cannot resolve multiple targets at the same range (a single isolated return is required) — for that, **pulsed FMCW (chirp)** is used.
- ✅ **Real-world names to mention in viva**: *Bendix ALA-52*, *Honeywell Mark II radio altimeter*, *TERCOM altimeter*.
- ✅ **Examiner-favourite link**: FM-CW altimeter is the *continuous-wave counterpart* of the *pulsed* radar; both use $R = c\tau/2$ but in FM-CW the delay is converted to a *frequency* difference.
- ✅ **Mini-formula-card for last-minute revision**:

| Quantity | Expression |
|---|---|
| $f_b$ (sawtooth) | $2R\Delta F f_m / c$ |
| $R$ (sawtooth) | $c f_b / (2\Delta F f_m)$ |
| $f_r$ | $(f_{b1}+f_{b2})/2$ |
| $f_d$ | $(f_{b2}-f_{b1})/2$ |
| $R$ (triangular) | $c(f_{b1}+f_{b2})/(4\Delta F f_m)$ |
| $v$ (triangular) | $c(f_{b2}-f_{b1})/(4 f_0)$ |
| $\delta R$ | $c/(2\Delta F)$ |
| $G_p$ | $\Delta F T_{\text{int}}$ |

> **All the best for your KTU End-Semester Examination!** Stay calm, draw the block diagram first, derive carefully, and double-check all unit conversions — that is the KTU board-exam winning strategy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Frequency CW Radar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multiple Frequency CW Radar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Multiple Frequency CW Radar** is a variant of the Continuous-Wave (CW) radar system that simultaneously transmits two or more discrete sinusoidal carrier frequencies ($f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n$) and exploits the **phase difference** of the corresponding Doppler-shifted echo signals to determine the unambiguous range of a target, while simultaneously measuring its radial velocity through the Doppler frequency shift.

Unlike pulsed radar (which uses time-of-flight) or single-frequency CW radar (which can only measure velocity), the multiple-frequency CW radar overcomes the fundamental range-measurement limitation of the standard CW radar by using **phase comparison** between the echoes of two closely-spaced carriers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Two-Clock Stopwatch" Analogy**
> Imagine you are blindfolded in a stadium and a friend fires two cap-guns at slightly different times. You can only hear the **time between the two shots arriving back to you**. By knowing the exact time-interval at which the two cap-guns were fired, and measuring the time-interval at which their echoes return, you can deduce **how far away your friend is**. The "stretching" of the gap between the two echoes (which is actually a *phase shift* at radio frequency) tells you the distance. This is exactly how multiple-frequency CW radar works — it transmits two tones and compares their **relative phase** upon return.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Exists — The Range-Ambiguity Problem

| Radar Type | Range Measurement | Velocity Measurement | Minimum Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pulsed Radar | ✅ Yes (via time delay) | ❌ Difficult | Large (pulse-width limited) |
| Single-Freq. CW Radar | ❌ No (no time reference) | ✅ Yes (Doppler) | Essentially Zero |
| **Multiple-Freq. CW Radar** | ✅ **Yes (via phase difference)** | ✅ **Yes (via Doppler)** | ✅ **Essentially Zero** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants used in this Module:**
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \; \text{m/s}$
> - Wavelength $\lambda = c/f_0$
> - Standard X-band frequency: $f_0 \approx 10 \; \text{GHz}$ (for typical radar examples)

## 1.4 Module-Level Context (KTU 2024 — Module 4)

This topic builds upon the MTI (Moving Target Indication) and pulsed radar concepts covered earlier in Module 4, and serves as a **bridge** to advanced topics like **FM-CW radar** (covered subsequently). The KTU 2024 scheme specifically lists "Multiple Frequency CW Radar" as a high-weightage topic for both Continuous Internal Evaluation and the End Semester Examination.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Logic Flow

The multiple-frequency CW radar operates on **four** sequential engineering principles:

1. **Dual-Tone Transmission**: A transmitter radiates a composite signal containing two (or more) sinusoidal carriers, $f_1$ and $f_2$, chosen such that their frequency separation $\Delta f = f_2 - f_1$ is much smaller than either carrier individually.
2. **Round-Trip Delay ($\tau$)**: The transmitted wave traverses the line-of-sight distance $R$ to the target and back, producing a time delay:
$$\tau = \frac{2R}{c}$$
3. **Phase Shift per Carrier**: Each carrier component suffers a phase shift proportional to its frequency and the round-trip delay. The phase of the $i$-th echo is:
$$\phi_i = 2\pi f_i \tau = \frac{4\pi f_i R}{c}$$
4. **Differential Phase Detection**: A **phase detector** in the receiver measures the **difference** between the two phase shifts, $\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1$. Because $\Delta f$ is small, this difference $\Delta\phi$ is small enough to be measurable in real-time and is **directly proportional to the range $R$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Design**
> Why use *multiple* frequencies? A single CW signal provides no timing reference — the receiver has no way to know when the wave was transmitted. By sending two frequencies, we create an **internal phase reference** whose *difference* encodes the range. This is the same mathematical idea used in **interferometry** in radio astronomy.

## 2.2 Signal Model (Transmitted, Received, and Beat)

The transmitted signal is the sum of two unmodulated sinusoids:

$$s_t(t) = A_1 \cos(2\pi f_1 t) + A_2 \cos(2\pi f_2 t)$$

The echo from a target at range $R(t)$ and radial velocity $v$ is:

$$s_r(t) = k_1 A_1 \cos\!\big[2\pi f_1(t - \tau)\big] + k_2 A_2 \cos\!\big[2\pi f_2(t - \tau)\big]$$

where $\tau = 2R(t)/c$ and $k_1, k_2$ are the propagation/reflection loss factors. For a **moving** target, $f_i$ is replaced by $f_i + f_{d,i}$ (Doppler-shifted), where:

$$f_{d,i} = \frac{2v f_i}{c}$$

After homodyne mixing (multiplying received signal with a local oscillator copy of the transmitted signal) and low-pass filtering, the **baseband beat signal** is recovered. The phase of this beat signal — measured at the receiver — yields the desired range information.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table** — Memorize these for Part A and Part B. Units are shown explicitly.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Round-trip time delay | $\tau = \dfrac{2R}{c}$ | seconds, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 2 | Phase shift at $f_i$ | $\phi_i = 2\pi f_i \tau = \dfrac{4\pi f_i R}{c}$ | radians |
| 3 | Differential phase | $\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 = \dfrac{4\pi (f_2 - f_1) R}{c} = \dfrac{4\pi \Delta f \, R}{c}$ | radians, $0 \le \Delta\phi < 2\pi$ |
| 4 | Range from $\Delta\phi$ | $R = \dfrac{c \, \Delta\phi}{4\pi \Delta f}$ | metres |
| 5 | Max unambiguous range | $R_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{c}{2 \Delta f}$ | metres (when $\Delta\phi$ wraps past $2\pi$) |
| 6 | Range resolution | $\delta R = \dfrac{c}{4\pi \Delta f} \cdot \delta\phi_{\text{min}}$ | metres, $\delta\phi_{\text{min}}$ is phase-measurement accuracy |
| 7 | Doppler frequency (carrier) | $f_d = \dfrac{2v f_0}{c}$ | Hz, $f_0$ is the carrier frequency |
| 8 | Radial velocity | $v = \dfrac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0}$ | m/s |
| 9 | Apparent range error due to $v$ | $\Delta R_v = \dfrac{c \, f_d}{2 \Delta f}$ | metres (correction term) |
| 10 | Choice of $\Delta f$ | $\Delta f < f_0$ (typically $\Delta f \ll f_0$) | Ensures $f_1, f_2$ propagate identically |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Multiple-frequency CW radar is the workhorse of **aircraft radio altimeters** (operating at $f_0 \approx 4.3$ GHz with $\Delta f \approx 100$ kHz giving $R_{\text{max}} = 1500$ m), **automotive anti-collision radar** (77 GHz, FMCW variant), and **missile proximity fuzes**.

## 2.4 The "Apparent Range" Correction Term

When the target is moving, the Doppler shift $f_d$ at the carrier frequency causes an **additional phase accumulation** that is indistinguishable from a true range change. This introduces a correction term:

$$R_{\text{measured}} = \frac{c \, \Delta\phi}{4\pi \Delta f} = R_{\text{true}} + \frac{c \, f_d}{2 \Delta f}$$

To resolve this, the KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize that the system must use the **simultaneously measured Doppler frequency** to apply this correction. The corrected range is:

$$R_{\text{true}} = R_{\text{measured}} - \frac{c \, f_d}{2 \Delta f}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Range from Differential Phase

**Goal:** Show rigorously that the target range $R$ can be obtained from the phase difference $\Delta\phi$ of two transmitted carriers.

**Step 1 — Setup the transmitted signal.**
A two-frequency CW radar simultaneously transmits two carriers of frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$, with $f_2 > f_1$. The composite transmitted signal is:

$$s_t(t) = A_1 \cos(2\pi f_1 t) + A_2 \cos(2\pi f_2 t)$$

**Step 2 — Express the round-trip delay.**
A target at line-of-sight range $R$ reflects the signal back. The two-way propagation time is:

$$\tau = \frac{2R}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Write the received echo signal.**
The echo arrives with delay $\tau$ and a small amplitude scaling factor $k$ (accounting for radar equation losses):

$$s_r(t) = k A_1 \cos\!\big[2\pi f_1(t - \tau)\big] + k A_2 \cos\!\big[2\pi f_2(t - \tau)\big]$$

**Step 4 — Coherent (homodyne) demodulation.**
The receiver multiplies $s_r(t)$ with two local-oscillator replicas at $f_1$ and $f_2$, then low-pass filters. The baseband outputs are:

$$b_1(t) = \frac{kA_1}{2} \cos(2\pi f_1 \tau)$$
$$b_2(t) = \frac{kA_2}{2} \cos(2\pi f_2 \tau)$$

**Step 5 — Extract the phase of each baseband signal.**
The phase of $b_1(t)$ is $\phi_1 = 2\pi f_1 \tau$ and the phase of $b_2(t)$ is $\phi_2 = 2\pi f_2 \tau$.

**Step 6 — Form the phase difference.**
The phase detector at the receiver computes:

$$\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 = 2\pi(f_2 - f_1)\tau = 2\pi \Delta f \cdot \tau$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $\tau = 2R/c$.**
Combining steps 6 and 2:

$$\Delta\phi = 2\pi \Delta f \cdot \frac{2R}{c} = \frac{4\pi \Delta f \, R}{c}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $R$.**
Rearranging for the unknown range $R$:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{c \, \Delta\phi}{4\pi \Delta f}}$$

**Step 9 — Maximum unambiguous range.**
Since a phase meter can only resolve $\Delta\phi$ in the principal interval $[0, 2\pi)$, the system becomes ambiguous when $\Delta\phi$ exceeds $2\pi$. Setting $\Delta\phi = 2\pi$:

$$2\pi = \frac{4\pi \Delta f \, R_{\text{max}}}{c} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{R_{\text{max}} = \frac{c}{2\Delta f}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Range Resolution

The smallest measurable change in range is governed by the smallest measurable change in phase, $\delta\phi_{\text{min}}$. Differentiating the range equation:

$$R = \frac{c \, \Delta\phi}{4\pi \Delta f} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{dR}{d(\Delta\phi)} = \frac{c}{4\pi \Delta f}$$

Hence the **range resolution** is:

$$\boxed{\delta R = \frac{c \, \delta\phi_{\text{min}}}{4\pi \Delta f}}$$

If $\delta\phi_{\text{min}} = 0.1$ rad (a typical practical phase-meter accuracy), then:

$$\delta R = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 0.1}{4\pi \Delta f} \approx \frac{2.387 \times 10^{6}}{\Delta f} \;\;\text{[metres, with } \Delta f \text{ in Hz]}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Apparent Range Error Due to Target Motion

**Step 1 — Doppler shift at the carrier.**
A target moving with radial velocity $v$ produces a Doppler shift at each carrier. At the central carrier $f_0 = (f_1 + f_2)/2$:

$$f_d = \frac{2v f_0}{c}$$

**Step 2 — Phase accumulation due to Doppler.**
Over an observation time $T$, this Doppler shift causes a phase rotation of $\Delta\phi_d = 2\pi f_d T$.

**Step 3 — Convert to apparent range error.**
Using the range-phase relation $R = c\Delta\phi / (4\pi \Delta f)$:

$$\Delta R_v = \frac{c \, \Delta\phi_d}{4\pi \Delta f} = \frac{c \cdot 2\pi f_d T}{4\pi \Delta f} = \frac{c f_d T}{2 \Delta f}$$

For short observation times (so that the Doppler phase is small), this term is small and can be compensated by subtracting the measured Doppler contribution:

$$R_{\text{true}} = R_{\text{measured}} - \frac{c f_d}{2 \Delta f} \cdot T_{\text{coherent}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Problem)

> **Problem:** A two-frequency CW radar transmits at $f_1 = 9.40$ GHz and $f_2 = 9.41$ GHz. The phase detector measures $\Delta\phi = 60^\circ$. Find: (a) the target range, (b) the maximum unambiguous range, and (c) the range resolution assuming $\delta\phi_{\text{min}} = 0.05$ rad.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Range:**

Convert the phase difference: $\Delta\phi = 60^\circ = \pi/3$ rad $\approx 1.0472$ rad.
Frequency separation: $\Delta f = 9.41 - 9.40 = 0.01$ GHz $= 10^{7}$ Hz.
Apply the range formula:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\phi}{4\pi \Delta f} = \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(1.0472)}{4\pi \times 10^{7}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{3.1416 \times 10^{8}}{1.2566 \times 10^{8}} \\
  &= 2.5 \;\;\text{metres}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) — Maximum Unambiguous Range:**

$$R_{\text{max}} = \frac{c}{2 \Delta f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^{7}} = 15 \;\;\text{metres}$$

**Part (c) — Range Resolution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta R &= \frac{c \, \delta\phi_{\text{min}}}{4\pi \Delta f} = \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(0.05)}{4\pi \times 10^{7}} \\
         &= \frac{1.5 \times 10^{7}}{1.2566 \times 10^{8}} \\
         &\approx 0.119 \;\;\text{metres} \approx 12 \;\;\text{cm}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** $R = 2.5$ m is less than $R_{\text{max}} = 15$ m, so the result is **unambiguous** — the system can definitively resolve this target.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Effect on $R_{\text{max}}$ | Effect on $\delta R$ | Effect on Phase Noise Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $\Delta f$ | Decreases ($R_{\text{max}} \propto 1/\Delta f$) | Improves ($\delta R \propto 1/\Delta f$) | Decreases (better SNR) |
| Increase $f_0$ (carrier) | Unchanged | Unchanged | Improves angular resolution |
| Increase observation $T$ | Unchanged | Unchanged (limited by $\delta\phi_{\text{min}}$) | Improves (narrower FFT bin) |
| Increase transmitter power | Unchanged | Unchanged | Improves SNR |
| Decrease $\delta\phi_{\text{min}}$ | Unchanged | Improves | Requires better hardware |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice of $\Delta f$ is the **central design trade-off** in a multiple-frequency CW radar. A large $\Delta f$ gives excellent range resolution but shortens the maximum unambiguous range. Real-world designs (e.g., radio altimeters) select $\Delta f$ such that $R_{\text{max}}$ exceeds the maximum expected target range with margin.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Receiver Signal Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the functional architecture of a two-frequency CW radar receiver, showing how the two echoes are mixed, phase-detected, and processed to yield both range and velocity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Antenna Common Port"] --> B["Duplexer Circulator"]
    B --> C["Low Noise Amplifier LNA"]
    C --> D1["Bandpass Filter BPF1 at f1"]
    C --> D2["Bandpass Filter BPF2 at f2"]
    D1 --> E1["Mixer M1 with LO1 at f1"]
    D2 --> E2["Mixer M2 with LO2 at f2"]
    E1 --> F1["Low Pass Filter LPF1 for f1 baseband"]
    E2 --> F2["Low Pass Filter LPF2 for f2 baseband"]
    F1 --> G["Phase Detector PD"]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H["Range Computer and Display"]
    F1 --> I["Doppler Frequency Discriminator"]
    F2 --> I
    I --> J["Velocity Computer and Display"]
    H --> K["Correction Logic Subtractor"]
    I --> K
    K --> H
    L["Reference Oscillator at f0"] --> M1["Frequency Synthesizer"]
    L --> M2["Frequency Synthesizer"]
    M1 --> E1
    M2 --> E2
    L --> N1["Power Divider"]
    N1 --> E1
    N1 --> E2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The reference oscillator drives two frequency synthesizers that produce the local oscillators at $f_1$ and $f_2$. A power divider feeds the same LOs to the two mixers. The mixers down-convert each echo to baseband, where the phase detector extracts the differential phase (for range) and the discriminator extracts the Doppler frequency (for velocity). The correction logic removes the apparent range bias caused by target motion.

## 4.2 Functional-Block Architecture of the Complete Two-Frequency CW Radar

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX["Transmitter Section"] --> ANT["Common Transmit Receive Antenna"]
    ANT --> TGT["Moving Target at Range R and Velocity v"]
    TGT --> ANT
    ANT --> RX["Receiver Section"]
    TX --- OSC1["Reference Oscillator f0 and Frequency Synthesizer"]
    OSC1 --> RX
    RX --> DSP["Signal Processor and Display Unit"]
    DSP --> OUTR["Range Output R in metres"]
    DSP --> OUTV["Velocity Output v in m per second"]
    subgraph TXBLOCK["Transmitter Block"]
        direction TB
        OSC1 --> AMP1["Power Amplifier Chain"]
        AMP1 --> COMP["Combiner Network"]
    end
    subgraph RXBLOCK["Receiver Block"]
        direction TB
        LNA["LNA and Preselector"] --> DUAL["Dual Channel Downconverter"]
        DUAL --> BB["Dual Channel Baseband Processor"]
        BB --> PD["Phase Detector and Doppler Extractor"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Operational Timeline (Conceptual Phases)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Multiple Frequency CW Radar Operational Sequence
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Transmit
    Continuous Dual-Tone Transmission   :active, t1, 0, 10
    section Receive
    Echo Capture and Amplification     :r1, 1, 9
    section Process
    Dual-Channel Coherent Demodulation :p1, 2, 8
    Phase Difference Computation       :p2, 3, 7
    Doppler Extraction                 :p3, 3, 7
    Range and Velocity Computation     :p4, 4, 6
    section Output
    Display and Track Update           :o1, 5, 5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Educational Interpretation:** Unlike pulsed radar, the multiple-frequency CW radar has **no transmit/receive switching cycle**. The transmission is continuous, and the receiver operates **simultaneously** with the transmitter — hence the need for a duplexer/circulator to provide isolation. This is the chief reason CW radars have essentially **zero minimum range**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a multiple-frequency CW radar. Why is it preferred over a single-frequency CW radar for range measurement? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A multiple-frequency CW radar transmits two carriers $f_1$ and $f_2$ simultaneously. The echo phase difference $\Delta\phi = 4\pi(f_2 - f_1)R/c$ is measured and converted to range as $R = c\Delta\phi/(4\pi\Delta f)$. A single-frequency CW radar cannot measure range because it has no internal phase reference — there is no second frequency to create a measurable phase difference. The multiple-frequency version overcomes this by exploiting the differential phase of the two carriers. *(3 marks: block diagram 1.5, explanation 1, justification 0.5)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: A two-frequency CW radar operates at $f_1 = 9.0$ GHz and $f_2 = 9.001$ GHz. Calculate the maximum unambiguous range. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The maximum unambiguous range is given by $R_{\text{max}} = c/(2\Delta f)$, where $\Delta f = 9.001 - 9.000 = 0.001$ GHz $= 10^6$ Hz.

$$R_{\text{max}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^6} = 150 \;\text{metres}.$$

The maximum unambiguous range is **150 m**. *(3 marks: formula 1, substitution 1, answer 1)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the range measured by a two-frequency CW radar in terms of the transmitted frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ and the measured phase difference $\Delta\phi$. State clearly the role of the phase detector. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A two-frequency CW radar transmits at $f_1 = 10.0$ GHz and $f_2 = 10.0001$ GHz. The receiver measures a phase difference of $45^\circ$ between the two echo signals. Determine:
  (i) The target range.
  (ii) The maximum unambiguous range.
  (iii) The range resolution for a phase-measurement accuracy of $0.1$ rad. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**
The transmitted signal is $s_t(t) = A_1\cos(2\pi f_1 t) + A_2\cos(2\pi f_2 t)$. After reflection from a target at range $R$, the round-trip delay is $\tau = 2R/c$, producing received phases $\phi_1 = 2\pi f_1 \tau$ and $\phi_2 = 2\pi f_2 \tau$. The phase detector outputs the difference $\Delta\phi = 2\pi(f_2 - f_1)\tau = 4\pi(f_2 - f_1)R/c$. Rearranging:

$$R = \frac{c \, \Delta\phi}{4\pi (f_2 - f_1)}.$$

The phase detector acts as the **range transducer**: it converts the round-trip delay into a measurable voltage proportional to the phase difference, which is then converted to range. *(Full marks: setup 1.5, phase extraction 1.5, derivation 2, phase-detector role 1, final formula 1)*

**(b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

Given: $f_1 = 10.0$ GHz, $f_2 = 10.0001$ GHz, $\Delta\phi = 45^\circ = \pi/4$ rad $\approx 0.7854$ rad, $\Delta f = 10^{5}$ Hz, $\delta\phi_{\text{min}} = 0.1$ rad.

**(i) Range:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{c \, \Delta\phi}{4\pi \Delta f} = \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(0.7854)}{4\pi \times 10^{5}} \\
  &= \frac{2.3562 \times 10^{8}}{1.2566 \times 10^{6}} \\
  &= 187.5 \;\;\text{metres}.
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Maximum unambiguous range:**

$$R_{\text{max}} = \frac{c}{2\Delta f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^{5}} = 1500 \;\text{metres}.$$

**(iii) Range resolution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta R &= \frac{c \, \delta\phi_{\text{min}}}{4\pi \Delta f} = \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(0.1)}{4\pi \times 10^{5}} \\
         &= \frac{3 \times 10^{7}}{1.2566 \times 10^{6}} \\
         &\approx 23.87 \;\;\text{metres}.
\end{aligned}$$

*(Marks: (i) 2.5, (ii) 2, (iii) 2.5)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the operational principle of a multiple-frequency CW radar with the help of a block schematic. Discuss how the range and velocity of a target are simultaneously determined. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the limitations of multiple-frequency CW radar in detail. An X-band radar operates at $f_0 = 10$ GHz with a frequency offset of $\Delta f = 200$ kHz. Compute: (i) the maximum unambiguous range, (ii) the range resolution if the phase measurement accuracy is $0.05$ rad, and (iii) the apparent range error due to a target moving with radial velocity $v = 300$ m/s, assuming a coherent integration time of $T = 10$ ms. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Operation and Block Schematic (7 Marks):**
A multiple-frequency CW radar transmits two continuous carriers at $f_1$ and $f_2$ through a common antenna via a circulator/duplexer. The echo signals are received, amplified by an LNA, and split into two channels by bandpass filters centred at $f_1$ and $f_2$. Each channel is coherently demodulated (homodyne) using a local oscillator at the respective frequency, and the baseband outputs are passed to a phase detector and a Doppler discriminator.

- **Range** is obtained from the differential phase: $R = c\Delta\phi/(4\pi \Delta f)$.
- **Velocity** is obtained from the Doppler frequency at the carrier: $v = f_d c / (2 f_0)$, where $f_d$ is measured at the discriminator output.

The two measurements are independent and simultaneous, allowing real-time tracking of moving targets. *(Marks: schematic 2.5, range principle 1.5, velocity principle 1.5, integration 1.5)*

**(b) — Limitations and Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Limitations:** Phase ambiguity if target range exceeds $R_{\text{max}}$; phase noise limits resolution; requires precise frequency synthesizers; vulnerable to motion-induced range error; more complex than single-frequency CW.

**Numerical:** Given $f_0 = 10^{10}$ Hz, $\Delta f = 2 \times 10^5$ Hz, $\delta\phi_{\text{min}} = 0.05$ rad, $v = 300$ m/s, $T = 10^{-2}$ s.

**(i) Maximum unambiguous range:**

$$R_{\text{max}} = \frac{c}{2\Delta f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^5} = 750 \;\text{metres}.$$

**(ii) Range resolution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta R &= \frac{c \, \delta\phi_{\text{min}}}{4\pi \Delta f} = \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(0.05)}{4\pi \times 2 \times 10^5} \\
         &= \frac{1.5 \times 10^{7}}{2.5133 \times 10^{6}} \\
         &\approx 5.97 \;\;\text{metres}.
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Apparent range error due to motion:**
First compute the Doppler frequency:

$$f_d = \frac{2v f_0}{c} = \frac{2 \times 300 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 10^8} = 2 \times 10^{4} \;\text{Hz} = 20 \;\text{kHz}.$$

Then the apparent range error over the integration time $T$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta R_v &= \frac{c \, f_d \, T}{2 \Delta f} = \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(2 \times 10^{4})(10^{-2})}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} \\
           &= \frac{6 \times 10^{10}}{4 \times 10^{5}} \\
           &= 1.5 \times 10^{5} \;\;\text{metres} = 150 \;\text{km}.
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - The Doppler-induced range error can be **enormous** if not corrected. Students often forget to include $T$ in the formula and lose 1–2 marks.
> - Always state the **assumptions** (e.g., "assuming $\Delta\phi$ lies in $[0, 2\pi)$") before plugging numbers in.
> - **Do not** confuse $f_0$ (the carrier) with $\Delta f$ (the separation) — the Doppler shift uses $f_0$, the range equation uses $\Delta f$.
> - The KTU 2024 scheme expects unit consistency: convert GHz to Hz, ° to rad, and show all steps in the aligned equations.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Multiple Frequency CW Radar**

- **Definition**: A CW radar that transmits two (or more) carriers to enable range measurement via differential phase comparison.
- **Range Formula**: $R = c\Delta\phi/(4\pi\Delta f)$ — the central equation of this module.
- **Max Unambiguous Range**: $R_{\text{max}} = c/(2\Delta f)$ — set by the $2\pi$ periodicity of the phase detector.
- **Range Resolution**: $\delta R = c\,\delta\phi_{\text{min}}/(4\pi\Delta f)$ — improves with larger $\Delta f$ and finer phase measurement.
- **Velocity Measurement**: Uses Doppler shift at the carrier: $v = f_d c/(2f_0)$ — independent of $\Delta f$.
- **Motion-Induced Range Error**: $\Delta R_v = c f_d T/(2\Delta f)$ — must be subtracted using the measured Doppler.
- **Zero Minimum Range**: Because the transmitter and receiver operate simultaneously (no pulse), there is essentially no blind range.
- **Continuous Transmission**: No T/R switchover → requires a high-isolation duplexer/circulator.
- **Key Trade-off**: Larger $\Delta f$ gives better $\delta R$ and $R_{\text{max}}$ paradoxically decreases — designer must balance.
- **Phase Detected in $[0, 2\pi)$**: Phase meters wrap around; the radar becomes ambiguous beyond $R_{\text{max}}$.
- **Applications to Remember**: Radio altimeters, proximity fuzes, short-range surveillance, automotive radar (FMCW variant).
- **Common Exam Trap**: Forgetting to convert $\Delta\phi$ from degrees to radians (always convert before substitution).
- **Common Exam Trap**: Confusing $R_{\text{max}}$ (set by $2\pi$ phase wrap) with maximum **detectable** range (set by SNR and radar equation).
- **Connect to Module Context**: This topic bridges to **FM-CW radar** (next module) where $\Delta f$ is swept continuously rather than being a fixed offset.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MTI and Pulse Doppler Radar: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MTI and Pulse Doppler Radar: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is MTI Radar?

**MTI (Moving Target Indication) Radar** is a type of pulse radar that uses the **Doppler Effect** to discriminate between *stationary* and *moving* targets. It exploits the fact that echoes from stationary objects (clutter) maintain a constant phase from pulse to pulse, whereas echoes from moving targets exhibit a progressive phase shift proportional to the target's radial velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *MTI Radar is a coherent pulse radar system in which successive returned pulses from a given range cell are compared in phase (or frequency) to detect and extract moving targets embedded in a stationary clutter background.*

## 1.2 What is Pulse Doppler Radar?

**Pulse Doppler Radar (PDR)** is an advanced coherent radar that combines the principles of **pulse radar** (for range measurement via time delay) and **continuous-wave Doppler radar** (for velocity measurement via frequency shift). It transmits a coherent burst of pulses and uses a bank of narrow-band filters (Doppler filters) to separate targets based on their radial velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Both MTI and Pulse Doppler radars are classified as **Coherent Radars** — meaning the transmitted signal's *phase* is preserved as a reference for comparing the phase of the received echo. This phase coherence is the *sine qua non* of MTI/PDR operation.

## 1.3 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

Imagine standing beside a railway crossing listening to a train approaching with its horn blowing:
- The **horn pitch (frequency)** appears to rise as the train approaches and fall as it moves away — this is the **Doppler Effect**.
- If the train is **stationary** (e.g., a parked engine at the platform), the pitch is unchanged.
- A radar operates identically but with **electromagnetic waves** instead of sound. MTI radar is essentially an "electronic ear" that listens for *changing* pitches and ignores the unchanging background hum of buildings, trees, and terrain.

## 1.4 Why Do We Need MTI / Pulse Doppler Radar?

In real-world scenarios, radar echoes are dominated by **unwanted reflections from stationary objects** (called *clutter*):
- Ground terrain (land clutter)
- Sea waves (sea clutter)
- Rain, chaff, and birds (weather/volumetric clutter)
- Buildings and other man-made structures

These clutter echoes can be **60–90 dB stronger** than the desired target echo, making small moving targets (aircraft, ships, vehicles) virtually invisible. MTI/PDR systems are designed to suppress clutter and reveal the moving target.

## 1.5 Fundamental Physical Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Use in Radar |
|----------|--------|-------|--------------|
| Speed of light | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | Round-trip time-of-flight, range calculation |
| Boltzmann constant | $k$ | **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | Receiver thermal noise calculation |

> [!TIP]
> Always remember: in radar, electromagnetic waves travel at $c$ and undergo a **two-way** journey (transmit + receive). Hence range is computed as $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$, not $c \cdot \Delta t$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of MTI Radar — Step-by-Step Logic

The MTI radar operates on a beautifully simple principle: **phase comparison between successive pulses**. Let us break this down:

1. **Transmission of Coherent Pulses:** The transmitter generates a *coherent* train of RF pulses at a stable carrier frequency $f_0$. Each pulse has a known initial phase reference (e.g., from a stable local oscillator — STALO).

2. **Echo Reception:** Each transmitted pulse produces echoes from both clutter and targets. The receiver captures these echoes, preserving the phase information.

3. **Phase Storage (Delay Line):** The received echo of pulse #1 is *delayed* by exactly one Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI) so that it can be compared with echo of pulse #2.

4. **Subtraction (Cancellation):** A subtractor (delay-line canceller) computes:  
$$\text{Output} = \text{Current pulse echo} - \text{Previous pulse echo (delayed)}$$

5. **Clutter Cancellation:** For a stationary clutter patch, the phase of successive echoes is **identical** (assuming coherent oscillator), so subtraction yields **zero** output.

6. **Target Detection:** A moving target produces a **different phase** in successive echoes due to its changing range. Subtraction produces a **non-zero residual** — this is the target indication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "phase difference" and not "amplitude difference"?** Because amplitude can be very similar between clutter and a small target. Phase is far more sensitive to motion — even a few millimeters of target movement produces a measurable phase shift.

## 2.2 The Doppler Frequency Concept

When a target moves with radial velocity $v_r$ toward/away from the radar, the received frequency $f_r$ differs from the transmitted frequency $f_0$ by the **Doppler frequency shift** $f_d$:

$$f_d = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$$

Where:
- $v_r$ = radial velocity (positive = approaching, negative = receding)
- $\lambda = c/f_0$ = wavelength of transmitted signal
- $f_0$ = transmitted carrier frequency

The factor of **2** arises because the wave undergoes a Doppler shift **twice**: once on transmit-path (radar → target) and once on receive-path (target → radar).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ | Doppler frequency shift | Hz |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_0}$ | Wavelength from carrier frequency | m |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Target range from round-trip delay | m |
| 4 | $R_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$ | Maximum unambiguous range | m |
| 5 | $R_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | Minimum range (limited by pulse width $\tau$) | m |
| 6 | $v_{\text{blind}} = \dfrac{n \cdot \lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{2}$ | Blind speed ($n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$) | m/s |
| 7 | $\Delta \phi = \dfrac{4 \pi v_r \cdot \text{PRI}}{\lambda} = 2 \pi f_d \cdot \text{PRI}$ | Phase change per PRI between echoes | radians |
| 8 | $\text{CA} = \dfrac{\text{Input SCR}}{\text{Output SCR}}$ | Clutter Attenuation (figure of merit) | ratio (dB) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** In equation 7, students often forget that the phase change between two successive pulses involves a **two-way** path difference, hence the $4\pi$ factor (not $2\pi$). This is the single most common mistake in Doppler/MTI derivations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Air Traffic Control (ATC):** MTI/PDR radars track aircraft against ground clutter.
- **Military Surveillance:** Detection of moving vehicles, low-flying aircraft, and missiles in heavy clutter.
- **Weather Radar:** Pulse Doppler distinguishes precipitation motion (radial wind velocity).
- **Marine Navigation:** Detecting ships against sea-wave clutter.
- **Police Speed Guns:** A simplified CW Doppler variant of the same principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Doppler Frequency Equation

**Statement:** When a target moves with radial velocity $v_r$ relative to a radar operating at wavelength $\lambda$, the received echo undergoes a frequency shift $f_d = 2v_r/\lambda$.

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry
Consider a stationary radar at origin $O$ and a target $T$ moving directly away with radial velocity $v_r$. At time $t = 0$, the target is at range $R_0$. At time $t$, the target's range is:

$$R(t) = R_0 + v_r \cdot t$$

### Step 2 — Express the transmitted wave
The radar transmits a sinusoidal electromagnetic wave of frequency $f_0$:

$$E_{\text{tx}}(t) = E_0 \cos(2 \pi f_0 t + \phi_0)$$

where $\phi_0$ is an arbitrary initial phase (we will keep it for completeness).

### Step 3 — Compute the round-trip time delay
The wave travels from radar to target and back at speed $c$, so the total one-way path is $2R(t)$ and the time delay is:

$$\tau(t) = \frac{2 R(t)}{c} = \frac{2(R_0 + v_r t)}{c}$$

### Step 4 — Write the received echo
The received echo is the transmitted wave evaluated at delayed time $t - \tau(t)$:

$$E_{\text{rx}}(t) = E_0' \cos\!\left[ 2 \pi f_0 \left(t - \frac{2(R_0 + v_r t)}{c}\right) + \phi_0 \right]$$

### Step 5 — Expand and group time terms
Rearranging inside the cosine:

$$E_{\text{rx}}(t) = E_0' \cos\!\left[ 2 \pi f_0 t \left(1 - \frac{2 v_r}{c}\right) - \frac{4 \pi f_0 R_0}{c} + \phi_0 \right]$$

### Step 6 — Identify the effective received frequency
The received frequency is the coefficient of $t$ in the cosine argument, divided by $2\pi$:

$$f_r = f_0 \left(1 - \frac{2 v_r}{c}\right)$$

### Step 7 — Compute the Doppler shift
By definition, $f_d = f_0 - f_r$ (the difference between transmitted and received frequency):

$$f_d = f_0 - f_0 \left(1 - \frac{2 v_r}{c}\right) = \frac{2 v_0 v_r}{c}$$

Since $v_0 = f_0$ is the carrier frequency:

$$f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v_r}{c}$$

### Step 8 — Convert to wavelength form
Using $\lambda = c/f_0$, we substitute $c = f_0 \lambda$ into the denominator:

$$f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v_r}{f_0 \lambda} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** $f_d$ is **positive** for an *approaching* target and **negative** for a *receding* target. Many textbooks write $f_d = -2v_r/\lambda$ by defining $v_r$ as positive for receding motion. Always check the convention used in your reference.

## 3.2 Derivation of Phase Change Between Successive Pulses

In an MTI radar, two successive pulses are separated in time by one PRI, where $\text{PRI} = 1/\text{PRF}$. The phase of pulse #1 echo is $\phi_1$ and pulse #2 echo is $\phi_2 = \phi_1 + \Delta\phi$, where:

$$\Delta\phi = 2 \pi f_d \cdot \text{PRI} = 2 \pi f_d \cdot \frac{1}{\text{PRF}}$$

Substituting $f_d = 2v_r/\lambda$:

$$\Delta\phi = 2 \pi \cdot \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{PRF}} = \frac{4 \pi v_r}{\lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta\phi = \frac{4 \pi v_r}{\lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}\,}$$

For a *stationary* target, $v_r = 0$, so $\Delta\phi = 0$ → the canceller output is **zero** (clutter cancelled). For a *moving* target, $\Delta\phi \neq 0$ → a residual appears at the canceller output.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Maximum Unambiguous Range

A pulse transmitted at time $t_0$ must return and be fully received *before* the next pulse is transmitted. The next pulse goes out at $t_0 + \text{PRI}$. Therefore, the maximum two-way time-of-flight is PRI:

$$\Delta t_{\text{max}} = \text{PRI} = \frac{1}{\text{PRF}}$$

Using $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$:

$$R_{\text{max}} = \frac{c \cdot \text{PRI}}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$$

$$\boxed{\,R_{\text{max}} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}\,}$$

If echoes from a target arrive *after* the next pulse is transmitted, they are mistakenly assigned to the wrong range cell — this is called **range ambiguity**.

## 3.4 Derivation of Blind Speed

A **blind speed** is the radial velocity at which a moving target produces a phase shift of exactly $2\pi n$ radians ($n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$) between successive pulses. In that case, $\Delta\phi$ modulo $2\pi$ equals zero, and the target looks identical to a stationary clutter — the MTI canceller *cannot detect it*.

Setting $\Delta\phi = 2\pi n$:

$$\frac{4 \pi v_r}{\lambda \cdot \text{PRF}} = 2 \pi n$$

Solving for $v_r$:

$$v_r = \frac{n \cdot \lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,v_{\text{blind},n} = \frac{n \lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{2} \quad \text{for } n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Blind speeds are the *Achilles heel* of single-PRF MTI radars. The standard solution is **PRF staggering** — using multiple PRFs so that no single target can be blind to all of them simultaneously.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** An MTI radar operates at $f_0 = 10\,\text{GHz}$ with PRF = 1 kHz. A target moves toward the radar at $v_r = 100\,\text{m/s}$. Compute: (i) wavelength, (ii) Doppler frequency, (iii) phase change per PRI, (iv) first three blind speeds, (v) maximum unambiguous range.

**Given:** $f_0 = 10 \times 10^9\,\text{Hz}$, PRF = $10^3\,\text{Hz}$, $v_r = 100\,\text{m/s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$.

### (i) Wavelength

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03\,\text{m} = 3\,\text{cm}$$

### (ii) Doppler frequency

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 100}{0.03} = 6666.67\,\text{Hz} \approx 6.67\,\text{kHz}$$

### (iii) Phase change per PRI

$$\text{PRI} = \frac{1}{\text{PRF}} = \frac{1}{1000} = 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{ms}$$

$$\Delta\phi = 2 \pi f_d \cdot \text{PRI} = 2 \pi \times 6666.67 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta\phi = 41.89\,\text{rad} = 6.667 \times 2\pi\,\text{rad}$$

(Note: $6.667$ complete cycles of $2\pi$ — equivalent to $0.667$ cycle beyond a full wrap, hence the target is detectable but close to a blind speed.)

### (iv) Blind speeds

$$v_{\text{blind},n} = \frac{n \lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{2} = \frac{n \times 0.03 \times 1000}{2} = 15 n\,\text{m/s}$$

- $n = 1$: $v_{\text{blind},1} = 15\,\text{m/s}$
- $n = 2$: $v_{\text{blind},2} = 30\,\text{m/s}$
- $n = 3$: $v_{\text{blind},3} = 45\,\text{m/s}$

### (v) Maximum unambiguous range

$$R_{\text{max}} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 1000} = 1.5 \times 10^5\,\text{m} = 150\,\text{km}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always show the *unit cancellation* explicitly in KTU derivations. Examiners allot partial marks for unit consistency.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an MTI Radar System

The following Mermaid diagram traces the full signal flow of a coherent MTI radar, showing how the *coherent oscillator (COHO)* maintains phase reference, the *delay line* stores the previous pulse echo, and the *subtractor* cancels clutter.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STALO Stable Local Oscillator] --> B[COHO Coherent Oscillator]
    B -->|Reference phase| G[Phase Detector Mixer]
    B -->|Carrier| C[Power Amplifier Transmitter]
    C -->|RF Pulse| D[Duplexer Antenna]
    D -->|Transmit to space| E[TARGET Moving Object]
    D -->|Receive echo| F[RF LNA Receiver Front End]
    E -->|Echo with Doppler shift| F
    F -->|Amplified echo| G
    G -->|I and Q baseband| H[Delay Line Storage]
    H -->|Previous pulse echo| I[SUBTRACTOR Canceller]
    G -->|Current pulse echo| I
    I -->|Clutter cancelled residual| J[MTI Filter Bank]
    J -->|Moving target indication| K[Display and Tracker]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00
    style C fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style D fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style E fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style I fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style J fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#558B2F
    style K fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
```

### Block-by-Block Explanation

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **STALO** | Generates the highly stable RF carrier reference (e.g., at 10 GHz). |
| **COHO** | Generates the coherent IF/oscillator signal whose phase is locked to STALO; provides phase reference for each transmitted pulse. |
| **Power Amplifier** | Boosts the RF pulse to high power (kW–MW range) for transmission. |
| **Duplexer** | Switches the antenna between transmitter (TX) and receiver (RX) with high isolation. |
| **Receiver (LNA + Mixer)** | Amplifies weak echoes and down-converts to baseband/IF while preserving phase. |
| **Delay Line** | Stores the echo of pulse #N for exactly one PRI, so pulse #N+1 can be compared with it. |
| **Subtractor** | Computes (current pulse) − (delayed previous pulse); outputs zero for clutter. |
| **MTI Filter Bank** | Further Doppler filtering and target detection. |
| **Display** | PPI or A-scope shows only the moving target. |

## 4.2 Comparison Flow: MTI vs Pulse Doppler Radar

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q{Coherent Pulsed Radar} --> M[MTI Radar]
    Q --> P[Pulse Doppler Radar]

    M --> M1[Single or Few PRF values]
    M --> M2[Delay line canceller one stage or two stage]
    M --> M3[Low to Medium PRF]
    M --> M4[Detects moving targets in clutter]
    M --> M5[Suffers from blind speeds]

    P --> P1[Multiple PRF staggered]
    P --> P2[Doppler filter bank FFT based]
    P --> P3[High PRF or Medium PRF or Low PRF variants]
    P --> P4[Measures radial velocity unambiguously]
    P --> P5[Resolves blind speed problem via PRF agility]
    P --> P6[Better clutter suppression via narrow band filters]

    style M fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style P fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style Q fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Flow of Clutter Cancellation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Tx as Transmitter
    participant Clutter as Stationary Clutter
    participant Target as Moving Target
    participant Rx as Receiver
    participant Canceller as Delay Line Canceller

    Tx->>Clutter: Pulse N transmitted
    Clutter->>Rx: Echo N (phase 0 degrees)
    Tx->>Target: Pulse N transmitted
    Target->>Rx: Echo N (phase 0 deg + small shift)
    Note over Rx: Pulse N stored in delay line

    Tx->>Clutter: Pulse N+1 transmitted
    Clutter->>Rx: Echo N+1 (phase 0 degrees, identical)
    Tx->>Target: Pulse N+1 transmitted
    Target->>Rx: Echo N+1 (phase shifted by Delta phi)
    Rx->>Canceller: Feed current echo N+1
    Canceller->>Canceller: Subtract stored echo N from N+1
    Canceller-->>Canceller: Clutter output is ZERO
    Canceller-->>Canceller: Target output is NON ZERO residual
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define MTI Radar and state its primary purpose. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
MTI (Moving Target Indication) Radar is a coherent pulse radar that uses the **Doppler Effect** to distinguish moving targets from stationary clutter. Its primary purpose is to **suppress unwanted echoes from stationary objects** (ground, buildings, sea waves) and to **detect small moving targets** (aircraft, vehicles) that would otherwise be masked by strong clutter returns. It achieves this by comparing the *phase* of successive pulse echoes — clutter has identical phase from pulse to pulse (cancels out), while moving targets exhibit progressive phase shift (producing a non-zero residual).

### Question 2: What is meant by "blind speed" in MTI radar? How is it related to PRF? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Blind speed is the radial velocity of a target at which the MTI canceller fails to detect it, because the phase change between successive pulses is an exact integer multiple of $2\pi$ radians. The relation is:

$$v_{\text{blind}} = \frac{n \lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

Blind speed is **directly proportional to PRF** — higher PRF raises the blind speed (so slow targets are still detectable), but lower maximum unambiguous range. This is the fundamental *range-velocity ambiguity trade-off* in MTI design.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an MTI radar. Discuss the role of the COHO and the delay-line canceller. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The MTI radar consists of the following functional blocks: STALO, COHO, transmitter, duplexer, antenna, receiver, delay line canceller, and display. (Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1.)

1. **STALO** (Stable Local Oscillator) produces a stable RF carrier, say at frequency $f_{\text{STALO}}$, which determines the radar's operating frequency.

2. **COHO** (Coherent Oscillator) is phase-locked to the STALO and provides a *coherent reference* for each transmitted pulse. Every transmitted pulse begins with the same initial phase, ensuring that the phase of the received echo can be meaningfully compared with the phase of the previous pulse. **Without COHO, the radar would be non-coherent, and MTI operation would be impossible.**

3. The transmitter generates RF pulses, which are radiated via the duplexer and antenna.

4. Echoes return from clutter and targets. The receiver amplifies and down-converts them to a frequency where phase comparison is convenient (typically baseband or low IF).

5. The **delay-line canceller** stores the echo of pulse #N for exactly one PRI, and then subtracts it from the echo of pulse #N+1. Mathematically:

$$y(t) = x(t) - x(t - T)$$

where $T = \text{PRI}$. For stationary clutter, $x(t) = x(t-T)$, so $y = 0$. For a moving target, $x(t) \neq x(t-T)$, so $y \neq 0$.

6. The non-zero residual is the target indication, displayed on the PPI/A-scope.

**Role of COHO:** Provides a *fixed phase reference* for every pulse; without it, transmitted pulses would have random starting phases and the canceller would be useless.

**Role of Delay-Line Canceller:** Acts as a *high-pass filter in the Doppler domain*, rejecting DC and low-frequency components (clutter) and passing high-frequency Doppler shifts (moving targets).

---

**(b) Derive the expression for Doppler frequency in radar. Explain its significance and compute the Doppler frequency for a target moving at 250 m/s toward a radar operating at 6 GHz. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.1 for the full step-by-step derivation.)

The final result is:

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$$

**Significance:**
- It is the fundamental quantity that MTI/PDR radars exploit to detect moving targets.
- Its magnitude is directly proportional to the radial velocity — hence the radar can also *measure* velocity.
- It is extremely small compared to the carrier (e.g., a 10 GHz radar with a 100 m/s target gives $f_d \approx 6.67$ kHz — a ratio of 1.5 million to 1), which is why *coherent detection* and *sensitive phase measurement* are essential.

**Numerical Computation:**
Given: $v_r = 250\,\text{m/s}$, $f_0 = 6 \times 10^9\,\text{Hz}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$.

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^9} = 0.05\,\text{m}$$

$$f_d = \frac{2 \times 250}{0.05} = \frac{500}{0.05} = 10{,}000\,\text{Hz} = 10\,\text{kHz}$$

**Answer:** $f_d = 10\,\text{kHz}$ (positive → target is approaching).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Pulse Doppler Radar. Compare it with MTI Radar in terms of PRF usage, clutter rejection, and velocity measurement capability. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Pulse Doppler Radar Block Diagram:**

A Pulse Doppler radar has the same basic front-end as an MTI radar (STALO → COHO → Transmitter → Antenna → Receiver), but the *back-end processing* is fundamentally different. After coherent down-conversion, the I and Q baseband signals are sampled and processed by a **Doppler Filter Bank** — typically implemented via an **FFT (Fast Fourier Transform)** over multiple coherent pulses. The output of the FFT is a Doppler spectrum, where each filter bin corresponds to a specific velocity range.

**Operation:**
1. A burst of $N$ coherent pulses (e.g., 8, 16, 32, or 64 pulses) is transmitted.
2. Echoes from each range cell are sampled coherently.
3. The samples across the $N$ pulses are FFT'd.
4. The FFT output is a Doppler spectrum for that range cell.
5. Targets with different radial velocities fall into different FFT bins.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | MTI Radar | Pulse Doppler Radar |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| PRF usage | Single / few PRF values | Multiple PRFs (staggered) for unambiguous velocity |
| Clutter rejection | Single-stage delay-line canceller | Narrow-band Doppler filter bank (FFT) — much sharper rejection |
| Velocity measurement | Qualitative (presence/absence) | Quantitative (precise velocity from FFT bin) |
| Blind speed problem | Severe | Mitigated by PRF staggering |
| Computational complexity | Low (analog delay line) | High (digital FFT) |
| Typical application | Simple ground-based radar | Modern ATC, military, weather radar |

---

**(b) An MTI radar operates at 9 GHz with PRF = 2 kHz. Compute: (i) wavelength, (ii) maximum unambiguous range, (iii) first two blind speeds, (iv) Doppler frequency for a target moving at 80 m/s. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f_0 = 9 \times 10^9\,\text{Hz}$, PRF = $2000\,\text{Hz}$, $v_r = 80\,\text{m/s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$.

### (i) Wavelength

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{9 \times 10^9} = 0.0333\,\text{m} \approx 3.33\,\text{cm}$$

### (ii) Maximum unambiguous range

$$R_{\text{max}} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2000} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4000} = 7.5 \times 10^4\,\text{m} = 75\,\text{km}$$

### (iii) First two blind speeds

$$v_{\text{blind},n} = \frac{n \lambda \cdot \text{PRF}}{2} = \frac{n \times 0.0333 \times 2000}{2} = 33.3 n\,\text{m/s}$$

- $n = 1$: $v_{\text{blind},1} = 33.3\,\text{m/s}$
- $n = 2$: $v_{\text{blind},2} = 66.6\,\text{m/s}$

### (iv) Doppler frequency for $v_r = 80$ m/s

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 80}{0.0333} = \frac{160}{0.0333} \approx 4800\,\text{Hz} = 4.8\,\text{kHz}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\lambda = 0.0333\,\text{m}$
- $R_{\text{max}} = 75\,\text{km}$
- $v_{\text{blind},1} = 33.3\,\text{m/s}$, $v_{\text{blind},2} = 66.6\,\text{m/s}$
- $f_d = 4.8\,\text{kHz}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mark-Loss Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Many students write $f_d = v_r/\lambda$ instead of $f_d = 2v_r/\lambda$. This single omission costs **2 marks** in a 7-mark derivation question.
> 2. **Mixing up $\text{PRF}$ and $\text{PRI}$:** $\text{PRF}$ has units of Hz, $\text{PRI}$ has units of seconds. Substituting one for the other in $R_{\text{max}} = c/(2 \cdot \text{PRF})$ is a common error.
> 3. **Wrong sign convention for $v_r$:** Failing to mention that $f_d$ is positive for approaching targets loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Missing the "stationary clutter" explanation in the canceller question:** Many students derive the equation but forget to *explain physically* why subtraction cancels clutter. Always include the qualitative reasoning.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- MTI and Pulse Doppler radars are **coherent radars** — they require a **COHO (Coherent Oscillator)** phase-locked to the **STALO** to maintain a fixed phase reference across pulses.
- MTI radars cancel clutter using a **delay-line canceller** that subtracts successive pulse echoes.
- Stationary clutter → zero phase change → cancels out. Moving target → non-zero phase change → produces residual.
- The fundamental Doppler equation is: $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda$ (note the **factor of 2** for two-way path).
- Phase change per PRI: $\Delta\phi = 4\pi v_r / (\lambda \cdot \text{PRF})$ (note the **factor of $4\pi$** for two-way path × two-pulse interval).
- Maximum unambiguous range: $R_{\text{max}} = c / (2 \cdot \text{PRF})$.
- Minimum range: $R_{\text{min}} = c \tau / 2$ (limited by pulse width $\tau$).
- Blind speed: $v_{\text{blind},n} = n \lambda \cdot \text{PRF} / 2$ — a fundamental limitation of single-PRF MTI radars.
- **PRF staggering** is the standard solution to the blind speed problem.
- **Range–velocity ambiguity trade-off:** Higher PRF → larger blind speeds but smaller $R_{\text{max}}$; lower PRF → larger $R_{\text{max}}$ but smaller blind speeds.
- Pulse Doppler radars use an **FFT-based Doppler filter bank** for much sharper clutter rejection than single-stage MTI cancellers.
- Pulse Doppler radars can **quantitatively measure** radial velocity, while classical MTI radars only *detect* the presence of motion.
- Always state the **sign convention** explicitly: $f_d > 0$ for approaching, $f_d < 0$ for receding.
- The KTU syllabus weight for Module 4 typically includes MTI, Pulse Doppler, and CW Doppler FMCW radars — each is a frequent **14-mark long answer** topic in the End Semester Exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radar (Radio Detection and Ranging)** is an electromagnetic system that operates by transmitting high-frequency EM energy toward a target, intercepting the small fraction of energy scattered (echoed) back by the target, and extracting from this echo signal information about the target's **range, angular position, velocity, and other characteristics**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, **Module 4 — Principle** establishes the foundational operating philosophy of a primary radar system. A *primary radar* is one in which the target itself does not cooperate — it merely reflects a portion of the incident EM wave back toward the radar receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCECT751, Module 4):**
> "Principle of operation of radar — basic block diagram, frequencies, applications, simple form of radar range equation, minimum detectable signal, maximum unambiguous range, integration of radar pulses, radar cross-section of targets, antenna parameters, system losses, propagation effects on radar."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Imagine standing at the edge of a deep, dark canyon. You shout *"Hello!"* into the void. After a brief moment, you hear your voice come back as a faint **echo**. By measuring how long the echo took to return, you can deduce how far away the canyon wall is — **without seeing it**.

**Radar does exactly this — but with invisible EM waves instead of sound.**

| Human Echo | Radar System |
|---|---|
| Mouth (shouts) | Transmitter (emits EM pulse) |
| Sound wave | Radio/Microwave EM wave |
| Canyon wall (reflects) | Target (aircraft, ship, vehicle) |
| Ear (listens) | Receiver (detects echo) |
| Round-trip time | $t = \frac{2R}{c}$ → gives Range $R$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - Boltzmann's constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> - Standard reference temperature: $T_0 = 290$ K

## 1.3 Block-Level Operational Summary

A radar system is built around five principal functional blocks:
1. **Transmitter** — generates high-power RF pulse
2. **Duplexer / T-R Switch** — shares the antenna between TX and RX
3. **Antenna** — radiates pulse, collects echo
4. **Receiver** — amplifies and processes the tiny return echo
5. **Display / Signal Processor** — displays target information (PPI, A-scope, digital)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Operation — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Pulse Generation**: The transmitter generates a high-power RF pulse of duration $\tau$ (pulse width) at a carrier frequency $f_c$ (typically 1–40 GHz).
2. **Radiation via Antenna**: The pulse is routed through a duplexer to a highly directive antenna that radiates the energy in a narrow beam toward the surveillance volume.
3. **Target Illumination**: A portion of the EM wave strikes the target. The target intercepts a fraction of the incident power density and reradiates (scatters) it in all directions.
4. **Echo Capture**: The radar antenna captures an extremely weak scattered echo whose power depends on target range $R$, RCS $\sigma$, and antenna parameters.
5. **Range Measurement**: The receiver measures the round-trip time delay $\Delta t$. Since EM waves travel at $c$, the slant range is $R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$. The factor of 2 accounts for the *out-and-back* path.
6. **Velocity Measurement**: If the carrier is coherent, the Doppler frequency shift $f_d = \frac{2v_r}{\lambda}$ in the echo yields the radial velocity $v_r$.
7. **Angular Measurement**: The antenna's beamwidth and pointing direction give the target's azimuth and elevation.

## 2.2 The "Why & How" Behind Each Step

- **Why a pulse?** A short pulse allows precise time-of-arrival measurement → accurate ranging.
- **Why directive antenna?** Concentrates transmitted energy (high gain) AND collects echoes from a narrow angular sector → improves angular resolution.
- **Why duplexer?** Single antenna shared between transmit (megawatts) and receive (microwatts) → protects receiver from burnout.
- **Why carrier frequencies in microwave band?** Atmospheric attenuation is low, antennas of practical size give high gain, target RCS behaves predictably.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** most commonly tested in KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions on the topic *"Principle"*.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Units | Condition / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Range of a target | $R = \dfrac{c \, \Delta t}{2}$ | km, m | $\Delta t$ is round-trip delay |
| 2 | Maximum unambiguous range | $R_{unamb} = \dfrac{c}{2 \, f_p}$ | km | $f_p$ = PRF (pulse repetition frequency) |
| 3 | Alternate form of $R_{unamb}$ | $R_{unamb} = \dfrac{c \, T_p}{2}$ | km | $T_p$ = pulse repetition interval |
| 4 | Range resolution | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$ | m | $\tau$ = pulse width |
| 5 | Doppler frequency shift | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ | Hz | $v_r$ = radial velocity |
| 6 | Velocity (from Doppler) | $v_r = \dfrac{f_d \, \lambda}{2}$ | m/s | One-way Doppler = $v/\lambda$ |
| 7 | Simple Radar Range Eqn | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4}$ | W | Monostatic radar with same TX/RX gain |
| 8 | Minimum detectable signal | $S_{min} = k T_s B_n \left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{min}$ | W | $T_s$ = system noise temp, $B_n$ = noise BW |
| 9 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (radar) | $\dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4 \, k T_s B_n F}$ | dimensionless | $F$ = noise figure |
| 10 | Antenna effective aperture | $A_e = \dfrac{G \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ | m² | Relates gain & aperture |
| 11 | Antenna beamwidth (approx) | $\theta \approx \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | radians | $D$ = antenna diameter |
| 12 | Carrier frequency ↔ wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | m | Useful inverse relation |
| 13 | PRF ↔ PRI | $f_p = \dfrac{1}{T_p}$ | Hz | Where $T_p$ is PRI |
| 14 | Duty cycle | $d = \tau \, f_p$ | dimensionless | $\tau$ = pulse width, $f_p$ = PRF |
| 15 | Average transmitted power | $P_{av} = P_t \, \tau \, f_p$ | W | Average over many pulses |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Radar Range Equation:** *"Power, Gain squared, Lambda squared, Sigma, divided by Range to the fourth power"* → $\dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$. The **fourth-power law** is the single most-tested fact in KTU radar problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Air Traffic Control (ATC)**: Primary surveillance radars at airports operate at S-band (2–4 GHz) for long-range aircraft detection.
- **Weather Radar**: Doppler weather radars at C-band (4–8 GHz) measure precipitation and wind shear.
- **Military Surveillance**: L-band (1–2 GHz) over-the-horizon radars detect ballistic missiles at thousands of km.
- **Marine Navigation**: Marine radars at X-band (8–12 GHz) provide short-range, high-resolution ship detection.
- **Automotive Radar**: 77 GHz FMCW radars enable adaptive cruise control and collision avoidance.
- **Space Debris Tracking**: Phased-array radars monitor orbital debris as small as 1 cm in LEO.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Range From Time Delay

The fundamental principle of a pulsed radar is that electromagnetic energy travels at the speed of light. If a pulse is transmitted at time $t_0$ and the echo is received at $t_0 + \Delta t$, the wave has traversed a round-trip distance of $2R$.

$$
\text{Distance traveled} = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

Setting this equal to the round-trip path $2R$:

$$
2R = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

Solving for the one-way slant range to the target:

$$
\boxed{R = \frac{c \, \Delta t}{2}}
$$

**Physical reasoning:** EM waves travel at $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. In one microsecond ($\Delta t = 10^{-6}$ s), light travels 300 m. Since the wave must go out *and come back*, the target is at half that distance, i.e., 150 m. Hence the factor of $\dfrac{1}{2}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Simple Radar Range Equation (One-Way Path Loss Concept)

Consider a radar transmitting power $P_t$ through an antenna of gain $G$. The power flux density at range $R$ is:

$$
\text{Power density at target} = \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^{2}}
$$

A target of radar cross-section $\sigma$ intercepts and reradiates this flux isotropically. The echo power density back at the radar antenna aperture $A_e$ is:

$$
\text{Echo power density} = \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^{2}} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{4\pi R^{2}}
$$

The antenna captures this flux over its effective aperture $A_e$:

$$
P_r = \left(\frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^{2}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\sigma}{4\pi R^{2}}\right) \cdot A_e
$$

Using the relation $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^{2}} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{4\pi R^{2}} \cdot \frac{G \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}
$$

Multiplying and simplifying the constants:

$$
\boxed{P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, R^{4}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental radar equation** in its simplest form. It shows the **$R^{4}$ dependence** — doubling the range reduces the received power by a factor of **16** (i.e., ~12 dB).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Minimum Detectable Signal (MDS)

A radar receiver must extract the target echo from thermal noise. The available noise power at the receiver input is given by the Nyquist–Johnson relation:

$$
N = k \, T_s \, B_n
$$

Where:
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant (1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K)
- $T_s$ = system noise temperature (K)
- $B_n$ = receiver noise bandwidth (Hz)

For reliable detection, the signal-to-noise ratio must exceed a threshold defined by the minimum required SNR, denoted $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{min}$ (typically 12–14 dB for classical detection).

Therefore, the minimum detectable signal power is:

$$
\boxed{S_{min} = k \, T_s \, B_n \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{min}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Maximum Unambiguous Range

Targets at greater ranges produce echoes that arrive *after* the next transmit pulse. If the round-trip delay exceeds the pulse repetition interval $T_p$, the echo is mistakenly assigned to a closer range (range ambiguity).

The maximum round-trip time allowed without ambiguity is exactly $T_p$:

$$
\Delta t_{max} = T_p = \frac{1}{f_p}
$$

Using the range equation derived earlier:

$$
R_{unamb} = \frac{c \, T_p}{2} = \frac{c}{2 f_p}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{unamb} = \frac{c}{2 \, f_p}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** For $f_p = 1$ kHz, $R_{unamb} \approx 150$ km. For $f_p = 10$ kHz, $R_{unamb} \approx 15$ km. Higher PRF → shorter unambiguous range but better velocity resolution.

---

## 3.5 Combined Radar Equation with Receiver Noise

Substituting $S_{min}$ for $P_r$ in the radar equation and solving for SNR:

$$
\frac{S}{N} = \frac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, R^{4} \, k \, T_s \, B_n \, F}
$$

Rearranging for maximum detection range $R_{max}$:

$$
\boxed{R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, k \, T_s \, B_n \, F \, (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}}
$$

This is the **most general form of the simple radar range equation** for KTU board-level numerical problems.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Model Problem)

> **Problem:** A monostatic radar operating at $f = 10$ GHz with peak power $P_t = 1$ MW, antenna gain $G = 40$ dB, and target RCS $\sigma = 1$ m² detects a target at range $R = 50$ km. Compute (a) the wavelength, (b) the received echo power, and (c) verify if the SNR exceeds $S_{min} = 10^{-12}$ W given noise bandwidth $B_n = 1$ MHz and system noise temperature $T_s = 600$ K.

### Step 1 — Compute wavelength

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10 \times 10^{9}} = 0.03 \, \text{m} \; (\text{i.e., 3 cm})
$$

### Step 2 — Convert antenna gain from dB to linear

$$
G_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} G \quad \Rightarrow \quad G = 10^{40/10} = 10^{4} = 10000
$$

### Step 3 — Compute received echo power

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, R^{4}}
$$

Substitute values:
- $P_t = 1 \times 10^{6}$ W
- $G^{2} = (10^{4})^{2} = 10^{8}$
- $\lambda^{2} = (0.03)^{2} = 9 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- $\sigma = 1$ m²
- $(4\pi)^{3} = (4\pi)^{3} = 1984.4$
- $R^{4} = (50 \times 10^{3})^{4} = 6.25 \times 10^{18}$ m⁴

$$
P_r = \frac{(10^{6})(10^{8})(9 \times 10^{-4})(1)}{(1984.4)(6.25 \times 10^{18})}
$$

$$
P_r = \frac{9 \times 10^{10}}{1.24 \times 10^{22}} = 7.26 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{W}
$$

### Step 4 — Compare with $S_{min}$

$$
P_r = 7.26 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{W} \quad \text{vs.} \quad S_{min} = 1.0 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{W}
$$

Since $P_r > S_{min}$, the target is **detectable** with a positive SNR margin.

> [!NOTE]
> This example illustrates the extreme dynamic range in radar: transmitting **1 MW** yet receiving only **~7 picowatts** — a 17-orders-of-magnitude span handled by sensitive receivers and high-gain antennas.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Pulsed Radar

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX[TRANSMITTER SECTION]
        Mod[Modulator / Timer]:::gen --> Osc[Stable Local Oscillator fc]:::gen
        Osc --> PA[High Power RF Amplifier]:::gen
        PA --> Pul[RF Pulse Output Pt tau]:::gen
    end

    DUP[Duplexer T-R Switch]:::switch

    Pul --> DUP
    ANT[Directional Antenna Gain G]:::ant
    DUP --> ANT

    subgraph TARGET[TARGET SPACE]
        TGT[TARGET at slant range R]:::tgt
    end
    ANT -.Transmit Pulse.-> TGT
    TGT -.Backscatter echo Pr.-> ANT

    subgraph RX[RECEIVER SECTION]
        LNA[Low Noise Amplifier]:::rx
        MIX[Mixer Down Converter]:::rx
        IF[IF Amplifier and Matched Filter]:::rx
        DET[Envelope / Coherent Detector]:::rx
        DSP[Signal Processor and Display PPI]:::rx
    end

    ANT --> DUP
    DUP --> LNA
    LNA --> MIX
    MIX --> IF
    IF --> DET
    DET --> DSP

    OSC -.Coherent LO Reference.-> MIX

    classDef gen fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,color:#000
    classDef switch fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#ca8a04,color:#000
    classDef ant fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef tgt fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000
    classDef rx fill:#e0e1dd,stroke:#495057,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> The **duplexer (T-R Switch)** is the unsung hero — it isolates the gigawatt-level transmit pulse from the picowatt-level receive path, preventing receiver burnout. The **local oscillator** is shared between TX and RX for **coherence**, which is essential for Doppler and pulse-doppler processing.

## 4.2 Mermaid Timing Diagram — Range Measurement Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[T equals 0: TX Pulse transmitted]:::txpulse --> B0[Wave propagates outward at c]
    B0 --> C0[At time Delta t equals 2R over c: Echo arrives]
    C0 --> D0[Receiver measures Delta t]
    D0 --> E0[Compute R equals c Delta t divided by 2]
    E0 --> F0[Display target at range R on PPI or A-scope]:::display

    classDef txpulse fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#9d4e15,color:#000
    classDef display fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Principles Discussed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Radar Principle] --> B[Transmitter]
    A --> C[Duplexer]
    A --> D[Antenna]
    A --> E[Target]
    A --> F[Receiver]
    A --> G[Display]
    B --> B1[Peak Power Pt]
    B --> B2[Pulse Width tau]
    B --> B3[PRF fp]
    C --> C1[T-R Switch Isolation]
    D --> D1[Gain G]
    D --> D2[Beamwidth theta]
    E --> E1[RCS sigma]
    E --> E2[Slant Range R]
    E --> E3[Radial Velocity vr]
    F --> F1[Noise Figure F]
    F --> F2[Bandwidth Bn]
    F --> F3[MDS Smin]
    G --> G1[Range from time delay]
    G --> G2[Velocity from Doppler fd]
    G --> G3[Angle from beam pointing]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

> **Q1:** Define the term **Radar Cross-Section (RCS)** of a target. Mention its typical units and the typical RCS value of a small fighter aircraft.

**Model Answer:**
> **Radar Cross-Section (RCS or σ)** is the hypothetical area that, if it scattered the incident electromagnetic power isotropically, would produce the same echo power density at the radar as the actual target. It is a measure of how detectable an object is by radar.
>
> - **Units:** Square meters (m²), often expressed in dBsm where $\sigma_{dBsm} = 10 \log_{10}(\sigma / 1\,\text{m}^{2})$.
> - **Typical small fighter RCS:** $\sigma \approx 1\,\text{m}^{2}$ (≈ 0 dBsm).
> - **Stealth aircraft (e.g., F-22):** $\sigma \approx 0.0001\,\text{m}^{2}$ (≈ −40 dBsm).
> - **Large commercial airliner:** $\sigma \approx 100\,\text{m}^{2}$ (≈ 20 dBsm).

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

> **Q2:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **principle of operation of a pulse radar**.

**Model Answer (concise version):**
> A **pulse radar** transmits short-duration high-power RF pulses via a directional antenna. The duplexer connects the antenna to the transmitter during the pulse and to the receiver during the listening interval.
>
> 1. The transmitter emits a pulse of width $\tau$ and peak power $P_t$.
> 2. The pulse travels at speed $c$ toward the target at range $R$.
> 3. The target reflects a small fraction of incident energy, characterized by RCS $\sigma$.
> 4. The echo returns after time delay $\Delta t = \dfrac{2R}{c}$.
> 5. The receiver amplifies the echo, and the signal processor computes:
>    - **Range** $R = \dfrac{c \Delta t}{2}$
>    - **Velocity** from Doppler shift $f_d = \dfrac{2v_r}{\lambda}$
>    - **Angle** from antenna pointing.
>
> Block Diagram: Refer to Section 4.1 above.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> In Part B problems on radar principle, students **commonly lose 2–4 marks** for the following mistakes:
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $R = c \Delta t / 2$. Always remember the *round-trip* path.
> 2. **Not converting antenna gain from dB to linear** when substituting into the radar equation. A 30 dB gain is **1000**, not 30.
> 3. **Mixing up PRF and PRI**. PRF is in Hz, PRI is in seconds. $f_p = 1 / T_p$.
> 4. **Dropping the system loss factor $L$** when present in the equation. Always check if $L > 1$ is given.
> 5. **Wrong units for wavelength** (cm vs. m). Always convert to meters before computation.

---

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`**

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a block diagram, explain the principle of operation of a radar system. List any four applications of radar.

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram:** (as shown in Section 4.1 above)

**Principle:**
1. The **transmitter** generates a high-power RF pulse of carrier frequency $f_c$ and pulse width $\tau$, modulated by the modulator/timer.
2. The pulse passes through the **duplexer (T-R switch)** to the **directional antenna**, which radiates it as a narrow beam.
3. The EM wave travels at $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and illuminates the **target**.
4. The target, characterized by radar cross-section $\sigma$, **scatters** a tiny fraction of the incident energy back toward the radar.
5. The antenna captures this echo, and the duplexer routes it to the **receiver**.
6. The **receiver** amplifies the weak echo via LNA, down-converts via mixer (using the same LO for coherence), passes it through matched filter and envelope detector.
7. The **signal processor** extracts target parameters:
   - Range $R = \dfrac{c \Delta t}{2}$
   - Velocity $v_r = \dfrac{f_d \lambda}{2}$
   - Angular position from antenna boresight.
8. The processed signal is displayed on **PPI (Plan Position Indicator)** or **A-scope**.

**Four Applications of Radar:**
