1. **Air Traffic Control (ATC)** — primary surveillance of aircraft.
2. **Weather monitoring** — Doppler weather radar for precipitation and storm tracking.
3. **Military surveillance** — early-warning and target tracking.
4. **Marine navigation** — ship detection and collision avoidance.
5. (Bonus) Automotive cruise control and space debris tracking.

---

> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Derive the simple radar range equation and discuss the $R^{4}$ dependence.

**Model Answer (complete derivation):**

**Step 1 — Power density at target distance $R$:**
$$
\text{PD}_{\text{target}} = \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^{2}}
$$
The antenna's gain $G$ effectively focuses $P_t$ into a sphere of radius $R$ with enhanced flux density.

**Step 2 — Power intercepted and reradiated by target:**
A target of radar cross-section $\sigma$ captures a fraction of incident flux and reradiates it isotropically:
$$
P_{\text{reradiated}} = \text{PD}_{\text{target}} \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4\pi R^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Echo power density at radar antenna:**
$$
\text{PD}_{\text{echo}} = \frac{P_{\text{reradiated}}}{4\pi R^{2}} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{2} R^{4}}
$$

**Step 4 — Power captured by radar antenna (effective aperture $A_e$):**
$$
P_r = \text{PD}_{\text{echo}} \cdot A_e
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$:**
$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{2} R^{4}} \cdot \frac{G \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}
$$

**Step 6 — Final equation:**
$$
\boxed{P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, R^{4}}}
$$

**Discussion of $R^{4}$ dependence:**
- Doubling $R$ reduces $P_r$ by $2^{4} = 16$ (≈ 12 dB).
- Increasing $R$ by 10 reduces $P_r$ by $10^{4} = 10000$ (40 dB).
- This harsh $R^{4}$ law is why radars need **high peak power**, **high gain antennas**, and **sensitive receivers**.

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]`**

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the terms **maximum unambiguous range** and **range resolution** with their mathematical expressions and significance.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Maximum Unambiguous Range ($R_{unamb}$):**
It is the largest target range that can be measured without ambiguity — i.e., without the echo being mistaken as a closer-range echo from a later transmit pulse.

$$
R_{unamb} = \frac{c}{2 f_p} = \frac{c \, T_p}{2}
$$

Where $f_p$ is the PRF and $T_p$ is the PRI.

**Significance:**
- Higher PRF → shorter $R_{unamb}$ but better Doppler resolution.
- Lower PRF → longer $R_{unamb}$ but more range ambiguity.

**Numerical example:** For $f_p = 2$ kHz, $R_{unamb} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{3}} = 75$ km.

---

**2. Range Resolution ($\Delta R$):**
It is the minimum separation in range between two targets that the radar can resolve as distinct echoes.

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c \, \tau}{2}
$$

**Significance:**
- Shorter pulse width $\tau$ → finer range resolution → better ability to detect closely-spaced targets.
- For $\tau = 1\,\mu s$, $\Delta R = 150$ m.

---

> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A pulsed radar operating at 10 GHz has a peak power of 1 MW, antenna gain of 40 dB, pulse width of 1 µs, PRF of 1 kHz, and noise bandwidth of 1 MHz. The target RCS is 2 m² and range is 80 km. Compute (i) received echo power, (ii) average transmitted power, (iii) duty cycle, (iv) minimum detectable signal if $T_s = 600$ K and $(S/N)_{min} = 12$ dB. Assume noise figure $F = 1$.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $f = 10 \times 10^{9}$ Hz → $\lambda = 0.03$ m
- $P_t = 1 \times 10^{6}$ W, $G = 10^{4}$ (linear)
- $\sigma = 2$ m², $R = 80 \times 10^{3}$ m
- $\tau = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ s, $f_p = 10^{3}$ Hz
- $B_n = 10^{6}$ Hz, $T_s = 600$ K, $F = 1$
- $(S/N)_{min} = 12$ dB → linear $= 10^{1.2} \approx 15.85$

**(i) Received Echo Power:**
$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, R^{4}}
$$
- $G^{2} = 10^{8}$, $\lambda^{2} = 9 \times 10^{-4}$
- $R^{4} = (80 \times 10^{3})^{4} = 4.096 \times 10^{19}$
- $(4\pi)^{3} = 1984.4$

$$
P_r = \frac{(10^{6})(10^{8})(9 \times 10^{-4})(2)}{(1984.4)(4.096 \times 10^{19})}
$$

$$
P_r = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{11}}{8.13 \times 10^{22}} = 2.21 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{W}
$$

**(ii) Average Transmitted Power:**
$$
P_{av} = P_t \cdot \tau \cdot f_p = (10^{6})(10^{-6})(10^{3}) = 1000 \, \text{W} = 1 \, \text{kW}
$$

**(iii) Duty Cycle:**
$$
d = \tau \cdot f_p = (10^{-6})(10^{3}) = 10^{-3} = 0.1\,\%
$$

**(iv) Minimum Detectable Signal:**
$$
S_{min} = k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}
$$
$$
S_{min} = (1.38 \times 10^{-23})(600)(10^{6})(15.85)
$$
$$
S_{min} = 1.31 \times 10^{-13} \, \text{W}
$$

**Detection Verdict:**
Since $P_r = 2.21 \times 10^{-12}$ W > $S_{min} = 1.31 \times 10^{-13}$ W, the target is **detectable** with an SNR margin of ~12.3 dB.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Radar Principle"**

- ✅ Radar = **Radio Detection And Ranging**.
- ✅ Basic radar block: **Transmitter → Duplexer → Antenna → Target → Echo → Receiver → Display**.
- ✅ **Duplexer (T-R switch)** protects receiver from high-power TX pulse.
- ✅ Range: $R = \dfrac{c \, \Delta t}{2}$ (factor of 2 = round trip).
- ✅ Velocity: $v_r = \dfrac{f_d \, \lambda}{2}$ (Doppler principle).
- ✅ Simple radar equation: $P_r = \dfrac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4}}$ — the **$R^{4}$ law**.
- ✅ Maximum unambiguous range: $R_{unamb} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_p}$.
- ✅ Range resolution: $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$.
- ✅ Minimum detectable signal: $S_{min} = k T_s B_n (S/N)_{min}$.
- ✅ Antenna gain ↔ aperture: $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$.
- ✅ Beamwidth ≈ $\lambda / D$ radians.
- ✅ RCS (σ) in m²; small fighter ≈ 1 m²; airliner ≈ 100 m².
- ✅ Higher PRF → better Doppler, worse range ambiguity.
- ✅ Lower pulse width → better range resolution, less energy per pulse.
- ✅ Average power: $P_{av} = P_t \cdot \tau \cdot f_p$.
- ✅ Duty cycle: $d = \tau \cdot f_p$ (typically 0.001 to 0.05 for surveillance radars).
- ✅ Microwave frequencies (1–40 GHz) are preferred for radar: low atmospheric loss, practical antenna sizes.
- ✅ SNR is the ultimate detection criterion — radar equation gives signal, Nyquist gives noise.
- ✅ Always convert dB gains to linear scale: $G = 10^{G_{dB}/10}$.
- ✅ Always convert units to SI (W, Hz, m, s) before substituting into formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MTI versus Pulse Doppler Radar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MTI versus Pulse Doppler Radar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **MTI Radar (Moving Target Indication Radar):** A radar system that uses the **Doppler effect** to distinguish moving targets (such as aircraft, vehicles, or missiles) from stationary clutter (buildings, terrain, trees) by comparing the **phase of echoes** received from successive transmitted pulses. It operates primarily in the **time domain** through delay-line cancellers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pulse Doppler Radar:** An advanced radar architecture that employs a **high Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** combined with **coherent signal processing** and **Doppler filter banks** (typically implemented using FFT) to extract target radial velocity from the returned echoes. It provides superior **velocity resolution** and **clutter rejection** compared to conventional MTI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** Both MTI and Pulse Doppler radars exploit the **Doppler frequency shift**, but they differ fundamentally in **PRF regime**, **signal processing philosophy** (time vs. frequency domain), and **handling of range/velocity ambiguities**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

Imagine you are standing beside a **highway** and trying to count how many cars passed in one minute by **clapping your hands once every second** and listening to the echo.

- **MTI Radar Analogy** — Like clapping once per second. If a car is stationary (clutter), every clap returns the *same echo* → you ignore it. If a car is moving, the echo *slightly shifts pitch* between claps → you detect motion. But if a car moves *exactly one full wavelength* between two claps, the echoes look identical and you **miss the car** — this is the famous **blind speed** problem.

- **Pulse Doppler Radar Analogy** — Like clapping very rapidly (say 1000 times per second) and then mathematically analyzing the *frequency spectrum* of the returning echoes. Because you sample so often, you get a much more detailed "fingerprint" of the moving car, allowing you to **measure its exact speed** even in heavy rain or against ground clutter.

| Concept | MTI Radar | Pulse Doppler Radar |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Time (canceller) | Frequency (filter bank) |
| PRF | Low | High |
| Clutter Rejection | Good | Excellent |
| Velocity Accuracy | Coarse | Fine |
| Blind Speeds | Yes | Reduced |
| Ambiguities | Range | Velocity (if PRF too high) |

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
- **Operating frequency band:** Typically **L-band (1–2 GHz)**, **S-band (2–4 GHz)**, or **X-band (8–12 GHz)**
- **Standard PRF regimes:**
  - **Low PRF (LPRF):** $f_r < 1$ kHz (MTI typical, unambiguous range)
  - **Medium PRF (MPRF):** $1 \text{ kHz} \leq f_r \leq 100$ kHz
  - **High PRF (HPRF):** $f_r > 100$ kHz (Pulse Doppler typical, unambiguous velocity)
- **Wavelength** $\lambda$ ranges from **0.1 m to 1 m** for surveillance radars
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of MTI Radar

The **core principle** of MTI is **subtraction of successive pulse echoes**:

1. **Pulse Transmission** — Radar transmits a coherent pulse train at PRF $f_r$ (period $T_r = 1/f_r$).
2. **Echo Reception** — Echo from stationary clutter has constant phase on successive pulses; echo from moving target has **linearly progressing phase** due to Doppler shift.
3. **Coherent Oscillator (COHO)** — Provides reference phase for down-conversion and ensures phase coherence.
4. **Delay Line Canceller** — Delays the echo by exactly one PRI ($T_r$) and subtracts it from the current echo.
5. **Cancellation Result:**
   - Stationary clutter → output ≈ 0
   - Moving target → output = non-zero residue proportional to $2|\sin(\pi f_d T_r)|$

### Why MTI Works (The Physics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A stationary object returns an echo whose phase changes by **zero** between consecutive pulses. A moving object returns an echo whose phase changes by **$\Delta\phi = 2\pi f_d T_r$** between consecutive pulses. The canceller exploits this **phase difference** directly.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Pulse Doppler Radar

The **core principle** of Pulse Doppler is **spectral analysis of the sampled echo**:

1. **High PRF Transmission** — Many pulses illuminate the target during its dwell time, ensuring **adequate samples** for spectral estimation.
2. **Coherent Reception** — In-phase (I) and Quadrature (Q) samples are taken at each range bin.
3. **Doppler Filter Bank (FFT)** — A bank of narrowband filters (or an FFT) separates targets by their **Doppler frequency** $f_d$.
4. **Detection & Display** — Each filter output corresponds to a specific velocity. Clutter appears at $f_d = 0$ (DC bin) and is rejected.

### Why Pulse Doppler Works (The Physics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> By sampling at a rate $f_r$ and applying a **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)**, the radar resolves Doppler frequencies within the Nyquist range $\pm f_r/2$. The closer the PRF matches the maximum expected Doppler, the **finer the velocity resolution** becomes.

## 2.3 The Blind Speed Phenomenon (Critical Concept)

The **blind speed** occurs when the Doppler phase shift between two consecutive pulses is an integer multiple of $2\pi$, making the target appear stationary to the canceller.

$$v_{\text{blind}} = \frac{n \lambda f_r}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Air Traffic Control Radars:** Use **staggered PRFs** to push blind speeds beyond the velocity envelope of aircraft.
- **Military Surveillance:** Pulse Doppler radars with **multiple PRFs** resolve both range and velocity ambiguities simultaneously.
- **Weather Radar:** MTI is used to remove ground clutter; Pulse Doppler extracts wind shear and tornado signatures.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Doppler frequency | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ | Hz | Core relation |
| 2 | Doppler frequency (with $f_0$) | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$ | Hz | Substitute $f_0$ in Hz |
| 3 | Blind speed | $v_{\text{blind}} = \dfrac{n \lambda f_r}{2}$ | m/s | $n = 1,2,3\dots$ |
| 4 | First blind speed ($n=1$) | $v_{b1} = \dfrac{\lambda f_r}{2}$ | m/s | Lowest blind speed |
| 5 | Single delay-line canceller response | $H(f) = 2 \sin(\pi f_d T_r)$ | — | MTI output |
| 6 | Two-pulse canceller magnitude | $\vert H(f) \vert = 2 \vert \sin(\pi f_d T_r) \vert$ | — | Frequency response |
| 7 | Range ambiguity interval | $R_u = \dfrac{c}{2 f_r}$ | m | Max unambiguous range |
| 8 | Velocity ambiguity interval | $v_u = \dfrac{\lambda f_r}{2}$ | m/s | Max unambiguous velocity |
| 9 | MTI Improvement Factor | $I = \dfrac{\text{SCV}_{\text{out}}}{\text{SCV}_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{\bar{G} \cdot C_A}{C_A \cdot \bar{G}_{\text{noise}}}$ | dB | Clutter rejection |
| 10 | Sub-clutter Visibility (SCV) | $\text{SCV} = \dfrac{\text{SCR (output)}}{\text{SCR (input)}}$ | dB | Target visibility |
| 11 | Clutter Attenuation | $C_A = \dfrac{P_{c,\text{in}}}{P_{c,\text{out}}}$ | dB | Canceller performance |
| 12 | Average Clutter Gain | $\bar{G} = 1$ (for noise) | — | Normalization |
| 13 | Power ratio in dB | $X_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(X)$ | dB | Standard conversion |
| 14 | PRF relationship | $f_r = 1/T_r$ | Hz | Pulse repetition |
| 15 | Number of pulses integrated | $N = T_{\text{dwell}} \cdot f_r$ | — | Dwell time relation |
| 16 | FFT velocity resolution | $\Delta v = \dfrac{\lambda f_r}{2 N}$ | m/s | Pulse Doppler |
| 17 | Stagger ratio (for staggered PRF) | $f_{r1}/f_{r2}$ irrational | — | Blind speed mitigation |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN NOTE:** The above table uses the symbols `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the pipe `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. For example, $\vert H(f) \vert$ is used in place of $\vert H(f) \vert$ inside the table.

## 2.5 The Fundamental Trade-off (Why MTI vs Pulse Doppler is a Design Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The choice between MTI and Pulse Doppler is governed by the **Range–Doppler Ambiguity Trade-off**:
> - **Low PRF** → Unambiguous range, ambiguous (blind) velocities → **MTI favored**
> - **High PRF** → Unambiguous velocity, ambiguous ranges → **Pulse Doppler favored**
> - **Medium PRF** → Both ambiguous → Used in airborne multi-mode radars with **multiple PRF resolution**

## 2.6 Side-by-Side Operational Comparison

| Feature | MTI Radar | Pulse Doppler Radar |
|---|---|---|
| PRF Range | Low (typ. 300–1000 Hz) | High (typ. 50–300 kHz) |
| Domain Operation | Time (delay line subtraction) | Frequency (Doppler filter bank) |
| Ambiguous Range | No | Yes (2nd-time-around echoes) |
| Ambiguous Velocity | Yes (blind speeds) | No (if PRF ≥ 2·f_dmax) |
| Clutter Rejection Mechanism | Successive pulse subtraction | Narrowband Doppler filtering at $f_d = 0$ notch |
| Velocity Resolution | Coarse (single value) | Fine (FFT bins) |
| Hardware Complexity | Moderate | High (A/D, DSP, FFT processor) |
| Typical Application | Ground-based surveillance | Airborne fire-control, weather |
| Number of PRFs Used | Usually 1 (sometimes staggered) | Multiple (resolves ambiguities) |
| Power Requirements | Lower peak power | Higher duty cycle |
| Clutter Visibility | Limited by canceller stability | Excellent (filter bank rejection) |
| Detection of Tangential Targets | Poor (zero Doppler) | Poor (still zero Doppler) |
| Blind Speed Mitigation | Staggered PRF or staggered $T_r$ | Naturally avoided with high PRF |
| Echo Sampling | 1 sample/pulse/range bin | I & Q samples at PRF rate |
| MTI Improvement Factor (typ.) | 20–40 dB | 50–80 dB |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Doppler Frequency from Two-Way Phase Shift

### Step 1 — Setup the Geometry
Let a target move with radial velocity $v_r$ toward a stationary radar. The transmitted signal is at carrier frequency $f_0$ (wavelength $\lambda = c/f_0$).

### Step 2 — Round-Trip Phase Accumulation
A target at range $R(t)$ causes a phase shift of:

$$\phi(t) = \frac{2\pi \cdot 2R(t)}{\lambda} = \frac{4\pi R(t)}{\lambda}$$

### Step 3 — Express Range as Function of Time
For a target moving with constant radial velocity $v_r$ (closing target, so $R$ decreases):

$$R(t) = R_0 - v_r t$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Differentiate
Substitute $R(t)$ into the phase equation:

$$\phi(t) = \frac{4\pi (R_0 - v_r t)}{\lambda}$$

Differentiate with respect to time to find the **instantaneous frequency shift**:

$$\frac{d\phi(t)}{dt} = -\frac{4\pi v_r}{\lambda}$$

The negative sign indicates an *increasing* frequency for closing targets. The **Doppler frequency** is the magnitude of this shift:

$$f_d = \left| \frac{d\phi(t)}{2\pi \, dt} \right| = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$

This is the **fundamental Doppler equation** used in BOTH MTI and Pulse Doppler radars.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Blind Speed Derivation

### Step 1 — Phase Shift Between Successive Pulses
A target moving with radial velocity $v_r$ produces a phase difference between two consecutive received echoes separated by $T_r$:

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{4\pi v_r T_r}{\lambda}$$

### Step 2 — MTI Canceller Output
A single delay-line canceller subtracts two consecutive echoes. Its output is proportional to:

$$y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$$

The frequency response of this filter is:

$$H(f) = 1 - e^{-j 2\pi f T_r}$$

### Step 3 — Find the Null Condition
The output becomes **zero** (target is "blind") when $H(f_d) = 0$:

$$1 - e^{-j 2\pi f_d T_r} = 0$$

$$e^{-j 2\pi f_d T_r} = 1$$

This requires:

$$2\pi f_d T_r = 2\pi n, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

$$f_d = \frac{n}{T_r} = n f_r$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Doppler Equation
Using $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda$:

$$\frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = n f_r$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Blind Speed
Rearrange to obtain the **blind speed formula**:

$$v_{\text{blind}} = \frac{n \lambda f_r}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

The **first blind speed** corresponds to $n=1$:

$$v_{b1} = \frac{\lambda f_r}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** For an X-band radar ($\lambda = 0.03$ m) with $f_r = 1$ kHz, the first blind speed is $v_{b1} = 15$ m/s ≈ 54 km/h — slower than most aircraft! This is why **staggered PRF** is essential in MTI design.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: MTI Improvement Factor (Using Clutter Statistics)

### Step 1 — Define Input and Output Signal-to-Clutter Ratios

$$\text{SCR}_{\text{in}} = \frac{S_i}{C_i}, \qquad \text{SCR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{S_o}{C_o}$$

### Step 2 — Define the Improvement Factor
The MTI Improvement Factor $I$ is the **ratio of output SCR to input SCR**:

$$I = \frac{\text{SCR}_{\text{out}}}{\text{SCR}_{\text{in}}} = \frac{S_o / C_o}{S_i / C_i}$$

### Step 3 — Express in Terms of Gains

$$I = \frac{(S_o / S_i)}{(C_o / C_i)} = \frac{G_s}{G_c}$$

where $G_s$ is the signal gain and $G_c$ is the clutter gain of the canceller.

### Step 4 — Signal Gain of Single Canceller
For a single delay-line canceller, the signal gain at $f_d = f_r / 4$ (max response) is:

$$G_{s,\text{max}} = \vert H(f_d) \vert^2 = 4$$

### Step 5 — Average Clutter Gain
For Gaussian-shaped clutter spectrum with standard deviation $\sigma_c$ (Hz), the average clutter gain is:

$$G_c = \int_{-f_r/2}^{f_r/2} \vert H(f) \vert^2 \cdot S_c(f) \, df$$

For a single-pole clutter model $S_c(f) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sigma_c} e^{-f^2 / (2\sigma_c^2)}$:

$$\bar{G_c} \approx 2\pi \sigma_c^2 \cdot 4 \text{ (for single canceller)}$$

### Step 6 — Final Improvement Factor

$$I = \frac{G_s}{\bar{G_c}} = \frac{2}{2\pi^2 \sigma_c^2 T_r^2}$$

In dB:

$$I_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{2\pi^2 \sigma_c^2 T_r^2} \right) + 3 \text{ dB}$$

This shows that $I$ **increases** as clutter spectrum narrows (smaller $\sigma_c$) and as PRF decreases (larger $T_r$).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pulse Doppler Velocity Resolution

### Step 1 — Number of Coherent Pulses in a Dwell
For a target observed for time $T_{\text{dwell}}$ with PRF $f_r$:

$$N = T_{\text{dwell}} \cdot f_r$$

### Step 2 — Doppler Frequency Resolution of an FFT
An $N$-point FFT has a frequency bin spacing of:

$$\Delta f_d = \frac{f_r}{N} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{dwell}}}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Velocity Resolution
Using $f_d = 2v_r / \lambda$, take the differential:

$$\Delta f_d = \frac{2 \Delta v_r}{\lambda} \implies \Delta v_r = \frac{\lambda \Delta f_d}{2} = \frac{\lambda}{2 T_{\text{dwell}}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Dwell Time
With $T_{\text{dwell}} = N/f_r$:

$$\Delta v_r = \frac{\lambda f_r}{2 N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a Pulse Doppler radar at $\lambda = 0.1$ m, $f_r = 10$ kHz, $N = 64$: $\Delta v_r = 7.8$ m/s. This is far finer than any MTI canceller can achieve.

## 3.5 Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** An MTI radar operates at $f_0 = 9$ GHz with PRF $f_r = 1$ kHz. Find the first three blind speeds.

### Given:
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{9 \times 10^9} = 0.0333 \text{ m}$$

### Solution:

For $n = 1$:

$$v_{b1} = \frac{1 \times 0.0333 \times 1000}{2} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}$$

For $n = 2$:

$$v_{b2} = 2 \times 16.67 = 33.33 \text{ m/s}$$

For $n = 3$:

$$v_{b3} = 3 \times 16.67 = 50.0 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These blind speeds are unacceptably low for aircraft targets (typical commercial aircraft: 250 m/s). Solution: Use **staggered PRF** or switch to **Pulse Doppler**.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | MTI Configuration | Pulse Doppler Configuration |
|---|---|---|
| $f_r = 1$ kHz, $f_0 = 10$ GHz | First blind speed $\approx 15$ m/s | $v_u = 15$ m/s (unambiguous) |
| $T_r = 1$ ms | Unambiguous range = 150 km | Unambiguous velocity = 15 m/s |
| $N = 16$ pulses | Coarse velocity info | $\Delta v = 0.94$ m/s |
| Clutter rejection | 25 dB (typical canceller) | 60 dB (FFT notch at DC) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — MTI Radar Signal Processing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coherent Oscillator COHO] --> B[Pulsed RF Transmitter]
    B --> C[Duplexer]
    C --> D[Antenna Radiating Pulses into Space]
    D --> E[Target and Clutter Echoes Return]
    E --> F[Receiver RF Amplifier]
    F --> G[Coherent Detector Mixer]
    A --> G
    G --> H[In-phase I and Quadrature Q Baseband]
    H --> I[Range Gate Selection]
    I --> J[Delay Line Canceller Subtractor]
    J --> K[Output for Display and Detection]
    J --> L[Clutter Residual Rejected]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Pulse Doppler Radar Signal Processing Chain

``` mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stable Local Oscillator STALO] --> B[Coherent Waveform Generator]
    B --> C[High PRF Pulse Modulator]
    C --> D[Power Amplifier and Duplexer]
    D --> E[Antenna Radiating High PRF Pulses]
    E --> F[Returns from Target and Clutter]
    F --> G[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    G --> H[Coherent I Q Mixer]
    A --> H
    H --> I[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    I --> J[Range Sample Storage Matrix]
    J --> K[FFT Doppler Filter Bank]
    K --> L[Clutter Notch at DC Zero Doppler]
    L --> M[Moving Target Detection and Velocity Estimation]
```

## 4.3 Side-by-Side Functional Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MTI_RADAR["MTI RADAR TIME DOMAIN APPROACH"]
        M1[Single PRF Pulse Train] --> M2[Coherent Receiver]
        M2 --> M3[Single Delay Line Canceller]
        M3 --> M4[Subtract Successive Echoes]
        M4 --> M5[Stationary Clutter Output Zero]
        M4 --> M6[Moving Target Nonzero Output]
    end
    subgraph PULSE_DOPPLER["PULSE DOPPLER RADAR FREQUENCY DOMAIN APPROACH"]
        P1[High PRF Pulse Train] --> P2[Coherent I and Q Sampling]
        P2 --> P3[Range Bin Storage]
        P3 --> P4[FFT Doppler Filter Bank]
        P4 --> P5[DC Notch Removes Clutter]
        P4 --> P6[Velocity Bins Extract Target Speed]
    end
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — When to Use MTI vs Pulse Doppler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Radar Design Requirement] --> Q1{Need unambiguous range?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Acceptable blind speeds?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> MTI[Use MTI Radar]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Use staggered PRF MTI}
    Q1 -- No --> Q4{Need fine velocity resolution?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> PD[Use Pulse Doppler Radar]
    Q4 -- No --> MTI2[Use MTI Canceller]
    MTI --> End[Single Delay Line or Double Canceller]
    Q3 --> End
    PD --> End2[High PRF with FFT Processor]
    MTI2 --> End
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `MTI_RADAR`, `PULSE_DOPPLER`) and labels use plain uppercase text to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rendering safeguards.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 3 Marks
**Differentiate between MTI and Pulse Doppler Radar in terms of PRF, signal processing domain, and ambiguity handling.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MTI Radar | Pulse Doppler Radar |
|---|---|---|
| PRF | Low PRF (unambiguous range) | High PRF (unambiguous velocity) |
| Processing Domain | Time domain (delay-line subtraction) | Frequency domain (FFT filter bank) |
| Ambiguities | Blind speeds (velocity ambiguity) | Range ambiguity (2nd-time-around echoes) |
| Clutter Rejection | Successive pulse subtraction | Narrowband notch filter at zero Doppler |

The key trade-off is **range vs. velocity ambiguity**. MTI sacrifices velocity for range clarity; Pulse Doppler does the opposite.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 3 Marks
**What is blind speed in an MTI radar? Derive an expression for the first blind speed.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Blind Speed** is the radial velocity of a target for which the MTI delay-line canceller produces zero output, making the target undetectable.

**Derivation:**

The phase difference between two consecutive received echoes for a target with radial velocity $v_r$ is:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{4\pi v_r T_r}{\lambda}$$

The canceller output becomes zero when $\Delta\phi = 2\pi n$:

$$\frac{4\pi v_r T_r}{\lambda} = 2\pi n \implies v_r = \frac{n \lambda}{2 T_r} = \frac{n \lambda f_r}{2}$$

For the **first blind speed** ($n = 1$):

$$v_{b1} = \frac{\lambda f_r}{2}$$

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] — 14 Marks

#### (a) Explain the working of an MTI radar with a single delay-line canceller. Derive the frequency response and find the condition for blind speed. — 7 Marks

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**

An MTI radar transmits a coherent pulse train at PRF $f_r$. The receiver processes the returned echoes through a **single delay-line canceller** that subtracts each echo from the previous one (delayed by $T_r = 1/f_r$).

The canceller structure:

$$y(t) = x(t) - x(t - T_r)$$

For a stationary clutter echo, the phase remains the same between consecutive pulses, so $y(t) = 0$ (cancellation occurs). For a moving target echo, the phase changes by $\Delta\phi = 2\pi f_d T_r$ between consecutive pulses, yielding a non-zero output.

**Frequency Response Derivation:**

The transfer function of the single delay-line canceller is:

$$H(f) = 1 - e^{-j 2\pi f T_r}$$

Apply Euler's identity:

$$1 - e^{-j 2\pi f T_r} = e^{-j \pi f T_r} (e^{j \pi f T_r} - e^{-j \pi f T_r}) = e^{-j \pi f T_r} \cdot 2j \sin(\pi f T_r)$$

Therefore, the magnitude response is:

$$\vert H(f) \vert = 2 \vert \sin(\pi f T_r) \vert$$

At $f = 0$ (DC, stationary clutter): $\vert H(0) \vert = 0$ → **clutter is rejected**.

At $f = f_r/2$ (max response): $\vert H(f_r/2) \vert = 2$ → **maximum target response**.

**Blind Speed Condition:**

Setting $f = f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda$ in the null condition $\sin(\pi f_d T_r) = 0$:

$$\pi f_d T_r = n \pi \implies f_d = n f_r$$

Substituting $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda$:

$$v_{\text{blind}} = \frac{n \lambda f_r}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

This completes the derivation.

---

#### (b) A ground-based MTI radar operates at 10 GHz with PRF 2 kHz. Calculate (i) the first blind speed, and (ii) the maximum unambiguous range. If the target radial velocity is 350 m/s, determine the Doppler frequency. — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**

$$f_0 = 10 \text{ GHz} = 10^{10} \text{ Hz}, \quad f_r = 2 \text{ kHz} = 2 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}, \quad v_r = 350 \text{ m/s}$$

**Solution:**

**(i) First blind speed:**

Compute wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03 \text{ m}$$

For $n = 1$:

$$v_{b1} = \frac{1 \times 0.03 \times 2 \times 10^3}{2} = 30 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Maximum unambiguous range:**

$$R_u = \frac{c}{2 f_r} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2 \times 10^3} = 75,000 \text{ m} = 75 \text{ km}$$

**Doppler frequency:**

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 350}{0.03} = 23,333.33 \text{ Hz} \approx 23.33 \text{ kHz}$$

**Velocity check against blind speed:**

The 11th blind speed is $11 \times 30 = 330$ m/s, the 12th is $360$ m/s. Since 350 m/s is **between** $11 v_{b1}$ and $12 v_{b1}$, the target Doppler is **not** at a blind speed, so it will be **detected** by the MTI.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert $f_0$ to Hz before computing $\lambda$. Also, do not confuse $c/f_0$ with $c \cdot f_0$. Always check units in Doppler calculations.

---

### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — 14 Marks

#### (a) Explain the operating principle of a Pulse Doppler radar. Discuss how it resolves velocity ambiguity and achieves clutter rejection. — 7 Marks

**Model Solution:**

**Operating Principle:**

A Pulse Doppler radar operates by transmitting a **high PRF coherent pulse train** and performing **Doppler spectral analysis** on the complex (I and Q) baseband samples. The architecture involves:

1. **Stable Local Oscillator (STALO)** and **Coherent Oscillator (COHO)** for phase coherence.
2. **High PRF pulse modulation** (typically 50–300 kHz).
3. **I/Q sampling** at the receiver output for each range bin.
4. **FFT-based Doppler filter bank** processing across the dwell.
5. **Detection** by applying a CFAR threshold to each Doppler bin.

**Velocity Ambiguity Resolution:**

Since high PRF causes range ambiguity, Pulse Doppler radars typically use **multiple PRFs** (e.g., 8 different PRFs in modern airborne radars). The true range is determined by the **Chinese Remainder Theorem** or by finding the **common range** consistent with all PRF measurements. Similarly, for velocity, the true Doppler is identified when the same velocity appears across multiple PRF dwells.

**Clutter Rejection Mechanism:**

The FFT filter bank creates a **narrow notch at DC (zero Doppler)** in the frequency response, which removes stationary clutter. The width of the notch depends on the FFT window. Moving targets with non-zero Doppler appear in adjacent filters, achieving clutter attenuation of **50–80 dB**.

The key advantage is that the **clutter spectrum is concentrated at $f_d = 0$**, while moving targets spread across the Doppler band, making the discrimination extremely effective.

---

#### (b) A Pulse Doppler radar operates at 5 GHz with PRF 50 kHz and uses 128 pulses per dwell. Calculate (i) the unambiguous velocity, (ii) the unambiguous range, and (iii) the velocity resolution. — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**

$$f_0 = 5 \text{ GHz} = 5 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_r = 50 \text{ kHz} = 5 \times 10^4 \text{ Hz}, \quad N = 128$$

**Solution:**

Compute wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^9} = 0.06 \text{ m}$$

**(i) Unambiguous velocity:**

$$v_u = \frac{\lambda f_r}{2} = \frac{0.06 \times 5 \times 10^4}{2} = 1500 \text{ m/s}$$

This is sufficient for **supersonic aircraft** (Mach 1 ≈ 340 m/s, Mach 2 ≈ 680 m/s).

**(ii) Unambiguous range:**

$$R_u = \frac{c}{2 f_r} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 5 \times 10^4} = 3000 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ km}$$

**Note:** This short unambiguous range is the **price** paid for unambiguous velocity — Pulse Doppler radars suffer from severe **range ambiguity**. Multiple PRFs would be required to resolve this in real systems.

**(iii) Velocity resolution:**

Compute dwell time:

$$T_{\text{dwell}} = \frac{N}{f_r} = \frac{128}{5 \times 10^4} = 2.56 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

Velocity resolution:

$$\Delta v_r = \frac{\lambda}{2 T_{\text{dwell}}} = \frac{0.06}{2 \times 2.56 \times 10^{-3}} = 11.72 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error in Pulse Doppler problems is confusing the **unambiguous velocity** (which is $v_u = \lambda f_r / 2$) with the **velocity resolution** (which depends on $N$ and $T_{\text{dwell}}$). These are **two different quantities**. Also, watch for unit conversion of $f_0$ (GHz to Hz).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Zones

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Unit Conversion Errors:** Doppler and range formulas require consistent SI units. Converting GHz to Hz (multiply by $10^9$) and km to m (multiply by $10^3$) is the most common source of arithmetic mistakes.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Confusing $v_{\text{blind}}$ with $v_u$:** Both formulas have the form $\lambda f_r / 2$, but $v_{\text{blind}}$ applies to MTI (low PRF, multiple $n$) while $v_u$ applies to Pulse Doppler (high PRF, single interval).
>
> **Pitfall 3 — MTI vs Pulse Doppler Domain Confusion:** MTI is a **time-domain** technique; Pulse Doppler is a **frequency-domain** technique. Writing "MTI uses FFT" or "Pulse Doppler uses delay line" will cost full marks.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Forgetting the Factor of 2:** Radar equations always carry a factor of 2 in the denominator for the two-way propagation: $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda$ and $R_u = c / (2 f_r)$. Omitting this gives wrong answers.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — No Numerical Justification:** KTU examiners require **final numerical answers with units** for full marks. Always box your final result.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **MTI Radar** uses **low PRF** and operates in the **time domain** via delay-line cancellers; it suffers from **blind speeds** at $v_{\text{blind}} = n \lambda f_r / 2$.
- ✅ **Pulse Doppler Radar** uses **high PRF** and operates in the **frequency domain** via FFT/filter banks; it suffers from **range ambiguity** but gives fine velocity resolution $\Delta v_r = \lambda f_r / (2 N)$.
- ✅ The **Doppler frequency** fundamental equation is $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda = 2 v_r f_0 / c$.
- ✅ The **unambiguous range** is $R_u = c / (2 f_r)$ — always inversely proportional to PRF.
- ✅ The **unambiguous velocity** is $v_u = \lambda f_r / 2$ — directly proportional to PRF.
- ✅ **Clutter rejection** in MTI: time-domain subtraction; in Pulse Doppler: FFT notch at DC.
- ✅ **MTI Improvement Factor** depends on canceller design and clutter spectrum width ($\sigma_c$).
- ✅ **Staggered PRF** is the standard technique to push MTI blind speeds beyond target velocity envelopes.
- ✅ **Multiple PRF** is the standard technique to resolve Pulse Doppler range ambiguity.
- ✅ Pulse Doppler provides **50–80 dB** of clutter rejection; MTI provides **20–40 dB** (single canceller) or higher (double/triple canceller).
- ✅ The **fundamental trade-off** is: low PRF → unambiguous range, blind velocities; high PRF → unambiguous velocity, ambiguous range.
- ✅ The **first blind speed** is $v_{b1} = \lambda f_r / 2$ (with $n=1$).
- ✅ Always use **I/Q sampling** for Pulse Doppler to preserve sign of Doppler (approaching vs. receding).
- ✅ **FFT-based Doppler processing** is the modern implementation of Pulse Doppler, replacing analog filter banks.
- ✅ For KTU numerical problems: always state the formula, substitute values with units, and box the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tracking Radar - Various Techniques of Tracking with Radar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT751 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Tracking Radar - Various Techniques of Tracking with Radar # MODULE 4 — TRACKING RADAR: VARIOUS TECHNIQUES OF TRACKING WITH RADAR ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCECT751 / 2024 Scheme):** > *"Tracking Radar – Sequential lobing, Conical scan, Monopulse tracking (amplitude comparison & phase comparison), Track-While-Scan (TWS). Range tracking and velocity tracking concepts."* ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Tracking Radar A **Tracking Radar** is a specialized radar system designed to provide **continuous, real-time positional and kinematic data** (range, angular coordinates, and radial velocity) of a *single* high-priority target. Unlike a **search (surveillance) radar**, which scans a wide volume of space and detects many targets, a tracking radar maintains a **narrow pencil beam** that is electronically or mechanically steered to follow one specific target, generating a smooth, high-data-rate trajectory. The three fundamental tracking functions a tracking radar must execute are: 1. **Range Tracking** — Continuous measurement of target slant range using a **range gate** that follows the target's echo in time. 2. **Angle (Angular) Tracking** — Continuous measurement of target bearing (azimuth $\theta_{az}$) and elevation ($\theta_{el}$) using beam-steering feedback. 3. **Velocity / Doppler Tracking** — Measurement of radial velocity via the **Doppler frequency shift** $f_d$. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition — Angle Tracking:** > *Angle tracking is the closed-loop process by which the antenna beam axis is maintained coincident with the line-of-sight to the target. Any misalignment generates an **angular error signal** that drives a servo motor to reposition the antenna.* --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tennis Ball Catcher" View Imagine a **tennis coach** trying to track a single fast-moving ball tossed to a student. - The coach's **eyes** are the **radar antenna**. - A **search radar** is like a coach who only glances around the entire court once every few seconds — useful for finding the ball, but he loses it almost immediately. - A **tracking radar** is like a coach whose head, neck, and eyes form a **servo-controlled gimbal** — once the ball is acquired, the neck automatically turns the head to keep the eyes fixed on it. The smoother the neck responds, the more accurate the tracking. - **Sequential Lobing** = the coach squints one eye, then the other, and averages. - **Conical Scan** = the coach tilts his head in a small circle around the ball, and the brain interprets the brightness pattern. - **Monopulse Tracking** = the coach uses *both eyes simultaneously* — the difference between left-eye and right-eye images instantly tells him the direction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Golden Specifications of a Tracking Radar:** > - **Angular Accuracy (tracking error):** typically **0.1° to 0.5° RMS** in modern systems. > - **Data Rate:** **10 to 100 Hz** angular update rate (vs. 1 Hz for search radars). > - **Tracking Range:** Often limited by antenna size; the **monostatic radar range equation** governs maximum range. --- ### 1.3 Why Tracking Radar Is Needed — Engineering Motivation | Application | Why Tracking is Critical | |---|---| | **Missile Guidance** | Continuous position data needed for fire-control loops | | **Anti-Ballistic Missile (ABM) Defense** | Sub-second trajectory prediction required | | **Aircraft Fire Control** | Weapons need smooth lead-angle computation | | **Satellite Tracking (TT&C)** | Telemetry, Tracking & Command stations | | **Weapon-Tracking Radars** | Following artillery shells / rockets | | **Air Traffic Control (Approach)** | Precision approach of landing aircraft | > [!NOTE] > **The fundamental trade-off** in tracking radar design: *narrower beamwidth → better angular accuracy*, but *narrower beam → harder initial target acquisition and lower search volume*. Tracking radars therefore depend on a **search radar (acquisition radar)** for hand-off. --- ### 1.4 Classification of Tracking Techniques ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Tracking Radar Techniques] --> B[Lobe Switching
Sequential Lobing] A --> C[Conical Scan] A --> D[Monopulse
Simultaneous Lobing] A --> E[Track While Scan TWS] D --> D1[Amplitude Comparison
Monopulse] D --> D2[Phase Comparison
Monopulse] ``` The four main families — **Sequential Lobing, Conical Scan, Monopulse, and TWS** — form the syllabus spine of this module. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Sequential (Switched) Lobing ### Working Principle In **sequential lobing**, the antenna is mechanically (or electronically) switched between **two squinted beams** displaced symmetrically about the antenna boresight axis. The squint angle $\beta$ is typically a fraction of the half-power beamwidth ($\beta \approx 0.1\theta_{3dB}$ to $0.25\theta_{3dB}$). For each beam switch, the **received echo amplitude** is measured. If the target lies *on axis*, both lobes return **equal** amplitudes. If the target is *off axis*, the lobe closer to the target returns a larger echo. By computing the **difference** of the two amplitudes, an angular error signal is generated. $$\boxed{\;\epsilon_{\theta} = K_{sl}\left(\,S_{A} - S_{B}\,\right)\;}$$ where $S_{A}$ and $S_{B}$ are the received signal amplitudes from lobes A and B, and $K_{sl}$ is the discriminator constant. > [!NOTE] > **Disadvantages of Sequential Lobing:** (i) Errors caused by *target glint* and *amplitude fluctuations* between successive lobes, (ii) requires *multiple pulses* per angular decision — slow data rate, (iii) poor performance in jamming or clutter. --- ## 2.2 Conical Scan Tracking ### Working Principle In **conical scan**, a single squinted beam is **continuously rotated** about the antenna's optical axis at a mechanical rate $f_{s}$ (typically **30 Hz to 60 Hz**). The beam axis therefore traces a **cone** of half-angle $\beta$ in space. When the target lies on the boresight axis, the echo amplitude is **constant** (no modulation). When the target is off-axis by an angle $\theta$ at a phase $\phi$ (relative to the scan reference), the received echo becomes **amplitude modulated** at the scan frequency. The **error voltages** for azimuth and elevation channels are: $$e_{az} = K_{c}\,\theta \cos(\omega_{s} t - \phi)$$ $$e_{el} = K_{c}\,\theta \sin(\omega_{s} t - \phi)$$ where: - $\theta$ = angular error magnitude - $\phi$ = angular error phase (direction of displacement) - $\omega_{s} = 2\pi f_{s}$ = scan angular frequency - $K_{c}$ = conical-scan slope constant These two orthogonal error signals drive the azimuth and elevation servo motors to null the error. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conical Scan — Phase Reference:** > A small portion of the scan-drive reference signal is fed to a **phase-sensitive detector (PSD)**. The PSD extracts the in-phase and quadrature (cosine and sine) components, which directly correspond to the azimuth and elevation error components. ### Why Conical Scan Works — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Squint the beam** off-axis by angle $\beta$. 2. **Rotate** the squinted beam at constant $\omega_{s}$. 3. The **target's angular position** relative to boresight modulates the **echo amplitude** at $\omega_{s}$. 4. **Demodulate** the echo envelope using the scan reference. 5. **Decompose** into two orthogonal error voltages $\rightarrow$ drive **two servos** (azimuth + elevation). 6. Servos rotate the antenna to **null** the error voltages $\Rightarrow$ antenna axis aligned with target. --- ## 2.3 Monopulse Tracking (Simultaneous Lobing) ### Core Concept **Monopulse** tracking derives the angular error from a **single pulse** — hence the name. It eliminates the **glint and amplitude-fluctuation errors** that plague sequential and conical-scan radars because both comparison signals are extracted from the *same* received echo. There are two principal implementations: ### (a) Amplitude-Comparison Monopulse Four squinted beams (or a four-horn feed) produce: - **Sum pattern** $\Sigma(\theta,\phi)$ - **Azimuth-difference pattern** $\Delta_{az}(\theta,\phi)$ - **Elevation-difference pattern** $\Delta_{el}(\theta,\phi)$ The monopulse **error voltage** is the **normalized difference**: $$\boxed{\;\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta(\theta,\phi)}{\Sigma(\theta,\phi)}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > For small angular errors, the monopulse error curve is **linear**: > $$\varepsilon \approx k_{m}\,\theta$$ > where $k_{m}$ is the **monopulse slope** (V/rad), set by antenna geometry. ### (b) Phase-Comparison Monopulse Two antennas separated by baseline $d$ are used. The **phase difference** between their outputs gives the angular error: $$\Delta\psi = \dfrac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\sin\theta$$ For small $\theta$: $$\boxed{\;\theta \approx \dfrac{\lambda\,\Delta\psi}{2\pi d}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Phase Monopulse — Best for Very Long Wavelengths:** > Phase comparison is preferred over amplitude comparison at **low RF (VHF, UHF)** frequencies where physically realizing a multi-horn feed for amplitude comparison is difficult. Modern phased-array radars often use a **hybrid** of both methods. ### Why Monopulse Dominates Modern Tracking | Property | Sequential Lobing | Conical Scan | **Monopulse** | |---|---|---|---| | Pulses per decision | ≥ 4 | Many | **1** | | Glint / scintillation immunity | Poor | Poor | **Excellent** | | Jamming resistance (chaff) | Low | Low | **High** | | Complexity | Low | Medium | **High** | | Accuracy | 0.5°–1° | 0.2°–0.5° | **0.05°–0.2°** | | Data rate | Low | Medium | **Very High** | --- ## 2.4 Track-While-Scan (TWS) In **TWS**, the radar's main beam continues to perform its **search (surveillance) function**, but a digital computer **associates detections across successive scans** and maintains a track file for each target. This is the principle used by modern **phased-array** AESA radars and by the **Air Traffic Control (ATC) systems**. > [!NOTE] > **TWS vs. Dedicated Track Radar:** > TWS is **less accurate** than a dedicated tracking radar because the beam dwell per target is short and the data rate is limited by the search frame time. However, TWS allows **simultaneous tracking of hundreds of targets** with a single radar. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this table on your revision sheet — these are the most-tested equations in KTU exams.** | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sequential-lobing error | $\varepsilon = K_{sl}(S_A - S_B)$ | Volts (discriminator output) | | 2 | Squint angle (typical) | $\beta \approx 0.1\,\theta_{3dB}$ to $0.25\,\theta_{3dB}$ | Radians / degrees | | 3 | Conical scan error (az) | $e_{az} = K_c \theta \cos(\omega_s t - \phi)$ | Volts | | 4 | Conical scan error (el) | $e_{el} = K_c \theta \sin(\omega_s t - \phi)$ | Volts | | 5 | Conical-scan envelope | $E(t) = E_0 [1 + m \cos(\omega_s t - \phi)]$ | $m$ = modulation depth | | 6 | Monopulse error voltage | $\varepsilon = \Delta \vert \Sigma$ | Dimensionless (ratio) | | 7 | Monopulse linear approx. | $\varepsilon \approx k_m \theta$ | $k_m$ = slope (V/rad) | | 8 | Phase-monopulse angle | $\theta \approx \lambda \Delta\psi \div (2\pi d)$ | $d$ = baseline, $\lambda$ = wavelength | | 9 | Antenna 3-dB beamwidth | $\theta_{3dB} \approx 0.886 \lambda \vert D$ | $D$ = aperture diameter | | 10 | Monostatic radar range | $R_{max} = [P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma \div ((4\pi)^3 k T_s B_n F (S/N)_{min})]^{1/4}$ | metres | | 11 | Doppler shift | $f_d = 2 v_r \vert \lambda$ | Hz, $v_r$ = radial velocity | | 12 | Range gate tracking error | $\dot{R} = \Delta f_{gate} \cdot c \div (2 f_{sw})$ | m/s | | 13 | Smoothing (α–β) filter | $\hat{x}_{n+1} = \hat{x}_n + \alpha (z_n - \hat{x}_n)$ | $\alpha, \beta$ = gains | | 14 | Smoothing velocity | $\hat{\dot{x}}_{n+1} = \hat{\dot{x}}_n + \beta (z_n - \hat{x}_n) \div T$ | T = scan period | | 15 | Glint error RMS | $\sigma_{glint} \approx 0.35 L$ | $L$ = target extent | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** The monopulse formula $\varepsilon = \Delta \vert \Sigma$ is implemented in the **receiver-processor** of every modern **fire-control radar** (e.g., the AN/SPY-1 Aegis radar), **missile seeker** (semi-active radar homing), and **airborne intercept (AI) radar** (e.g., APG-81 on F-35). --- ## 2.6 Range Tracking — The Split-Gate Tracker The radar's **range tracker** generates two adjacent range gates: an **Early Gate (EG)** and a **Late Gate (LG)** straddling the expected target echo. - If echo is **centred**: EG and LG amplitudes are equal $\rightarrow$ range error = 0. - If echo is **early**: EG > LG $\rightarrow$ increase range gate delay. - If echo is **late**: LG > EG $\rightarrow$ decrease range gate delay. The **range error** is proportional to the difference: $$\boxed{\;\Delta R = K_{rg}\,(A_{EG} - A_{LG})\;}$$ where $A_{EG}, A_{LG}$ are the detected amplitudes and $K_{rg}$ is the range-tracker gain (V/m). --- ## 2.7 Velocity Tracking — Doppler / MTI Trackers A **Doppler tracker** uses a narrow-band **bandpass filter** centred at the expected Doppler frequency $f_d$. The filter output is fed to an **Automatic Gain Control (AGC)** loop that retunes a **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** to keep the filter centred on the moving target's $f_d$. > [!TIP] > **Cross-cueing:** In a 2-D / 3-D tracking radar, **range, angle, and velocity** trackers operate in closed loops simultaneously. Any change in one dimension is cross-fed to the others — this is the heart of a **coherent fire-control loop**. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Conical Scan Error Signal Modulation Depth ### Given - Squint (offset) angle between beam axis and antenna boresight: $\beta$ - Target angular displacement from boresight: $\theta$ - Phase of target displacement (relative to scan reference): $\phi$ - Scan rate: $f_s$ (Hz), angular frequency $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ ### To Derive The amplitude modulation $m$ of the received echo envelope as a function of $\theta$, $\beta$, and the antenna's one-way voltage gain $G(\alpha)$ pattern. ### Derivation **Step 1 — Antenna voltage gain pattern (linear approximation near boresight):** For a narrow-beam antenna with one-way voltage gain $G(\alpha)$ versus angle $\alpha$ off axis, we can approximate the slope near the boresight as linear: $$\dfrac{dG}{d\alpha}\bigg\vert_{\alpha=0} = G'_0$$ **Step 2 — Beam axis position in time:** The beam axis, when rotated, has an angular offset of $\beta$ from boresight and an azimuthal phase $\omega_s t$. At a fixed instant, the beam points at: $$\alpha_{beam}(t) = \beta \cos(\omega_s t), \quad \alpha'_{beam}(t) = \beta \sin(\omega_s t)$$ (orthogonal components in the antenna's azimuth-elevation plane). **Step 3 — Angle between target and instantaneous beam axis:** The target is at displacement $(\theta\cos\phi, \theta\sin\phi)$ in antenna coordinates. The instantaneous angle between the beam axis and the target line-of-sight is: $$\alpha_{rel}(t) = \theta \cos(\omega_s t - \phi) - \beta$$ (small-angle approximation, projecting target displacement onto the rotating beam direction). **Step 4 — Received echo amplitude (proportional to antenna voltage gain in the target direction):** Since the antenna is reciprocal in receive, the one-way gain toward the target is $G(\alpha_{rel}(t))$. The echo voltage is: $$V_{echo}(t) = V_0 \, G\!\left[\theta \cos(\omega_s t - \phi) - \beta\right]$$ **Step 5 — Taylor expand for small errors $\theta \ll \beta$ and narrow beam:** $$V_{echo}(t) \approx V_0\left[G(-\beta) + G'(-\beta)\,\theta\cos(\omega_s t - \phi)\right]$$ Let $V_0 G(-\beta) = V_{DC}$ and $V_0 G'(-\beta)\,\theta = \Delta V$. Then: $$V_{echo}(t) = V_{DC} + \Delta V \cos(\omega_s t - \phi)$$ **Step 6 — Express as a standard AM signal:** $$V_{echo}(t) = V_{DC}\!\left[1 + \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_{DC}} \cos(\omega_s t - \phi)\right] = V_{DC}\!\left[1 + m \cos(\omega_s t - \phi)\right]$$ **Step 7 — Modulation depth:** $$\boxed{\;m = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_{DC}} = \dfrac{G'(-\beta)\,\theta}{G(-\beta)}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering meaning of $m$:** > The **modulation depth $m$** is directly proportional to the angular error $\theta$. The **phase of the modulation** $(\phi)$ tells the *direction* of the error. Phase-sensitive detection of $V_{echo}(t)$ against the scan reference therefore recovers the two orthogonal error components $e_{az}$ and $e_{el}$. ### Numerical Sanity Check A typical AN/MPQ-53 Patriot tracking radar uses a conical-scan **squint angle $\beta \approx 0.15^\circ$** and a **scan rate $f_s \approx 50$ Hz**. For a $0.5^\circ$ angular error, the modulation depth $m \approx 30\%$ — comfortably detectable above receiver noise. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Monopulse Angle Error (Amplitude Comparison) ### Given - Two squinted beams (L and R) with one-way voltage gain patterns $G_L(\theta)$ and $G_R(\theta)$ displaced by $\pm\beta$ from boresight. - Target at angle $\theta$ from boresight. ### To Derive The monopulse error voltage $\varepsilon = (V_L - V_R) / (V_L + V_R)$. ### Derivation **Step 1 — Receive voltages in each lobe:** $$V_L(\theta) = K \cdot G(\theta + \beta), \qquad V_R(\theta) = K \cdot G(\theta - \beta)$$ **Step 2 — Sum pattern (Σ-channel):** $$\Sigma(\theta) = V_L + V_R = K\bigl[G(\theta + \beta) + G(\theta - \beta)\bigr]$$ For symmetric $G$ and small $\beta$, the sum is even in $\theta$: $$\Sigma(\theta) \approx 2K \cdot G(\beta) + K G''(\beta)\theta^2$$ (to second order). For small $\theta$, $\Sigma(\theta) \approx 2K G(\beta)$ — approximately **constant**. **Step 3 — Difference pattern (Δ-channel):** $$\Delta(\theta) = V_L - V_R = K\bigl[G(\theta + \beta) - G(\theta - \beta)\bigr]$$ The difference is **odd** in $\theta$. Taylor expand: $$\Delta(\theta) \approx K \bigl[2 G'(\beta)\bigr]\theta = 2K G'(\beta)\,\theta$$ **Step 4 — Normalized monopulse error voltage:** $$\boxed{\;\varepsilon(\theta) = \dfrac{\Delta(\theta)}{\Sigma(\theta)} \approx \dfrac{2K G'(\beta)\,\theta}{2K G(\beta)} = \dfrac{G'(\beta)}{G(\beta)}\,\theta\;}$$ Defining the monopulse slope $k_m = G'(\beta) / G(\beta)$: $$\varepsilon(\theta) = k_m \theta$$ **Step 5 — Linear range of the discriminator:** The discriminator is approximately linear for $\vert \theta \vert \lesssim \beta$. Beyond $\beta$, the antenna patterns separate, and the error signal becomes ambiguous (a phenomenon called **monopulse "break-lock"**). > [!TIP] > **KTU numerical tip:** If $G(\theta) = \sin(2\pi\theta / \theta_{bw})$ (sinc-squared-like pattern), then > $$k_m = \dfrac{G'(\beta)}{G(\beta)} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\theta_{bw}} \cot\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\beta}{\theta_{bw}}\right)$$ > and the **monopulse slope is maximized** when $\beta \approx \theta_{bw} / 4$ (i.e., squint angle = quarter beamwidth). --- ## 3.3 Derivation — Phase Monopulse Angle From Phase Difference ### Given Two antennas separated by baseline $d$, observing a target at angle $\theta$ from the broadside direction (perpendicular to baseline). ### Derivation **Step 1 — Path difference between the two antennas:** $$\Delta L = d \sin\theta$$ **Step 2 — Phase difference between the two received signals:** $$\Delta\psi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta L = \dfrac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\sin\theta$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $\theta$ (exact):** $$\theta = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda\,\Delta\psi}{2\pi d}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Small-angle approximation $\theta \ll 1$ rad:** $$\boxed{\;\theta \approx \dfrac{\lambda\,\Delta\psi}{2\pi d}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Ambiguity check:** Phase is only measured modulo $2\pi$, so unambiguous angle measurement requires > $\sin\theta < \lambda / d$, i.e., the antenna separation $d$ must be less than $\lambda$ for unambiguous field of view — a key reason phase monopulse is rare for narrow-beam radars (which need $d \gg \lambda$). --- ## 3.4 Derivation — Range-Tracker Split-Gate Error ### Given Two adjacent range gates (EG, LG) of width $\tau_g$ each, with a guard band $T_g$ between them. The echo is a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ centred at the target's actual time-of-arrival $t_0$. ### Derivation **Step 1 — Echo energy captured by each gate (no target inside gate):** $$E_{EG} = \int_{t_{EG}}^{t_{EG}+\tau_g} P_{echo}(t)\,dt, \qquad E_{LG} = \int_{t_{LG}}^{t_{LG}+\tau_g} P_{echo}(t)\,dt$$ **Step 2 — Difference signal (range error):** $$\Delta E = E_{EG} - E_{LG}$$ **Step 3 — Linearisation for small time-offset $\Delta t$ between echo centroid and gate centre:** For a triangular echo shape (typical for matched-filter output): $$\Delta E \approx K_{rg}\,c\,\Delta t \div 2 = K_{rg}\,\Delta R$$ where $\Delta R$ is the range error in metres and $K_{rg}$ is the discriminator constant. **Step 4 — The range-track loop adjusts gate timing to force $\Delta E \to 0$:** $$\boxed{\;R_{tracked}(t) = R_{initial} + \int_0^t \dot{R}_{gate}(\tau)\,d\tau\;}$$ This gives continuous, smoothed range trajectory. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Tracking Technique | Parameter | Sequential Lobing | Conical Scan | Amplitude Monopulse | Phase Monopulse | TWS | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Pulses per angular fix** | $\geq 4$ | Many (full scan) | **1** | **1** | N/A (scan-based) | | **Angle accuracy (RMS)** | 0.5°–1° | 0.2°–0.5° | 0.05°–0.2° | 0.1°–0.3° | 0.5°–1° | | **Susceptibility to glint** | High | High | **Low** | **Low** | Medium | | **Susceptibility to chaff / jamming** | High | High | **Low** | Medium | High | | **Antenna complexity** | 1 feed, switching | 1 feed, rotating | 4-horn / comparator | 2 separated antennas | 1 phased array | | **Receiver complexity** | Low | Medium (PSD) | High (Σ, Δ) | Medium (phase meter) | Very high (processor) | | **Target capacity** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **100s** | | **Best application** | Legacy systems | Legacy fire control | **Modern fire control** | Long-wavelength | **Multi-target AESA** | | **Example system** | SCR-584 (WWII) | Nike Ajax | AN/SPY-1, APG-81 | VHF/UHF radars | AN/TPY-2, AWACS | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Conical Scan Tracking Radar ```mermaid flowchart TB TX[Transmitter
Coherent Oscillator] --> SW1[TR Switch / Duplexer] SW1 --> ANT[Antenna
Rotating Squinted Beam] ANT --> SW1 SW1 --> RX[Receiver LNA / Mixer] RX --> DET[Envelope Detector] DET --> CH1[Channel I
cosine reference] DET --> CH2[Channel II
sine reference] REF[Scan Reference
Oscillator f_s] --> CH1 REF --> CH2 CH1 --> PSD1[Phase-Sensitive
Detector AZ] CH2 --> PSD2[Phase-Sensitive
Detector EL] PSD1 --> SM1[Servo Amplifier
Azimuth] PSD2 --> SM2[Servo Amplifier
Elevation] SM1 --> M1[Azimuth Motor] SM2 --> M2[Elevation Motor] M1 --> ANT M2 --> ANT REF -.feedback.-> M1 REF -.feedback.-> M2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key feature:** The single rotating feed produces a *modulated* echo. Two orthogonal PSDs (synchronous detectors) recover azimuth and elevation error components using the **scan reference oscillator** as the phase reference. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Monopulse Tracking Radar (Amplitude Comparison) ```mermaid flowchart TB ANT[Antenna with 4-Horn Feed
4 Squinted Beams] --> HJ[Magic-T Hybrid
Junction Ring] HJ --> SUM[Sum Channel Sigma] HJ --> DAZ[Delta AZ Channel] HJ --> DEL[Delta EL Channel] TX[Transmitter] --> HJ SUM --> RX1[Sum Receiver] DAZ --> RX2[Delta AZ Receiver] DEL --> RX3[Delta EL Receiver] RX1 --> RNG[Range Processor
and Detector] RX2 --> DIV1[Divider] RX3 --> DIV2[Divider] RX1 --> DIV1 RX1 --> DIV2 DIV1 --> SA1[Servo Amp AZ] DIV2 --> SA2[Servo Amp EL] SA1 --> MAZ[Azimuth Drive Motor] SA2 --> MEL[Elevation Drive Motor] MAZ --> ANT MEL --> ANT RNG --> RG[Range Gate
Tracker] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why it is called "monopulse":** Both $\Sigma$ and $\Delta$ signals are formed from the **same received pulse** in the magic-T hybrid junction. The error signal $\Delta \div \Sigma$ is therefore computed from one pulse — no inter-pulse glint, no amplitude fluctuation error. --- ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Conical Scan Servo Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR TARGET[Target Position] --> ECHO[Echo Return
AM Modulated] ECHO --> DET[Detector] DET --> ERR[Angular Error
m and phi] ERR --> SERVO[Servo Controller
Phase-Sensitive Detector] SERVO --> DRIVE[Motor Drive] DRIVE --> ANT2[Antenna Reposition] ANT2 --> TARGET REF[Scan Reference] --> SERVO ``` > [!NOTE] > The **closed loop** continuously nulls the angular error $m \to 0$, ensuring the antenna boresight stays aligned with the line of sight to the target. --- ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Track-While-Scan (TWS) System ```mermaid flowchart TB PA[Phased Array Antenna] --> BF[Beam-Forming
Network] BF --> SCH[Schedule
Generator] SCH --> DW[Dwell Manager] DW --> DET[Detector /
Plot Extractor] DET --> ASS[Plot Associator
Nearest Neighbour] ASS --> KF[Kalman Filter
Track Smoother] KF --> TF[Track File
Database] TF --> PRED[Predictor] PRED --> SCH TF --> DISP[Display /
Downlink] ``` > [!NOTE] > **The Predictor feeds back** to the Schedule Generator to position the *next* dwell of the beam on the predicted position of the target — this is the essence of **adaptive track-while-scan**. --- ## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Range + Angle + Velocity Tracking (Integrated) ```mermaid flowchart TB ANT[Tracking Antenna] --> AGC[AGC / Receiver] AGC --> RNG[Range Tracker
Split-Gate] AGC --> AZ[Azimuth Tracker
Delta Sigma] AGC --> EL[Elevation Tracker
Delta Sigma] AGC --> DOP[Doppler / Velocity
Bandpass Filter] RNG --> KF1[Kalman Filter
Range] AZ --> KF2[Kalman Filter
Azimuth] EL --> KF3[Kalman Filter
Elevation] DOP --> KF4[Kalman Filter
Velocity] KF1 --> FF[Fire-Control
Computer] KF2 --> FF KF3 --> FF KF4 --> FF FF --> DISP[Weapons / Display] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU exam tip:** A question asking you to "draw the block diagram of a tracking radar" almost always expects a **monopulse Σ/Δ architecture with separate range, azimuth, elevation, and Doppler channels feeding a fire-control computer.** The above diagram covers all four axes. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between sequential lobing and monopulse tracking in radar. (3 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Sequential Lobing | Monopulse | |---|---|---| | **Pulses required for one angular decision** | Multiple (≥ 4 lobes) | **One single pulse** | | **Susceptibility to amplitude fluctuations / glint** | High (compares separate pulses) | **Very low** (single pulse) | | **Angular accuracy** | Moderate (0.5°–1°) | **High (0.05°–0.2°)** | | **Implementation complexity** | Simpler | More complex (hybrid junction, Σ/Δ channels) | Sequential lobing switches the antenna between squinted beams and *infers* the error from the difference in successive echo amplitudes, whereas monopulse forms **sum and difference patterns simultaneously** in a hybrid junction, computing the error in a single pulse — hence the name. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is meant by "glint" in tracking radar? How does monopulse tracking reduce glint error? (3 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Glint** is the apparent angular wander of a target's echo centroid caused by interference between **multiple scattering centres** on the target (e.g., engines, wings, tail). As the target aspect changes, the centroid of the returned wavefront shifts, generating a *spurious* angular error. **(1 mark)** In sequential lobing or conical scan, glint causes the **amplitudes of the lobes** to vary from pulse to pulse, creating **false error signals**. **(1 mark)** In **monopulse tracking**, both the $\Sigma$ and $\Delta$ channels are formed from the *same pulse* — so glint-induced amplitude fluctuations cancel out in the **normalized ratio $\Delta / \Sigma$**. The monopulse system is therefore inherently immune to first-order glint errors. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A [14 marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 variant]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **principle of operation of a conical-scan tracking radar**. Derive the expression for the modulation depth of the received echo and discuss how the error signal is extracted. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A tracking radar uses a **monopulse amplitude-comparison** technique with a four-horn feed. The squint angle is $\beta = 0.3^\circ$ and the 3-dB beamwidth is $\theta_{3dB} = 1.2^\circ$. The target is displaced by $0.4^\circ$ in azimuth from the boresight. Calculate: **(i)** the approximate **monopulse error voltage** in normalised form, and **(ii)** the **monopulse slope** $k_m$, assuming a sinc-pattern antenna model. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Q.A(a):** **Block diagram:** *(refer to the Conical Scan block diagram in Section 4.1)* **Principle of operation — step-by-step:** 1. The **antenna feed** is mechanically offset (squinted) from the paraboloid axis by a small angle $\beta$ (≈ 0.1–0.25 of beamwidth). **(1 mark)** 2. The feed is **continuously rotated** at a scan rate $f_s$ (30–60 Hz), making the beam axis trace a **cone** in space. **(1 mark)** 3. When the target is **on boresight**, every point of the cone is at the same gain — the received echo has **constant amplitude** (no modulation). **(1 mark)** 4. When the target is **off boresight** by angle $\theta$ and phase $\phi$, the echo becomes **amplitude modulated** at $f_s$: $$V_{echo}(t) = V_{DC}[1 + m \cos(\omega_s t - \phi)]$$ where $m = G'(-\beta)\theta / G(-\beta)$ and $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$. **(1 mark)** 5. The echo is **envelope detected** and applied to two **phase-sensitive detectors (PSDs)**, one with a cosine reference and one with a sine reference. **(1 mark)** 6. The PSD outputs are the **azimuth** and **elevation error voltages**: $$e_{az} = K_c \theta \cos\phi, \quad e_{el} = K_c \theta \sin\phi$$ 7. These are amplified and fed to **servo motors** that rotate the antenna to null the errors, keeping the target on the boresight. **(1 mark)** 8. **Key disadvantage:** the technique is vulnerable to **glint** and to **scan-modulation jamming** because the scan reference can be detected by an adversary. **(1 mark)** --- #### **Solution to Q.A(b):** **Given:** - $\beta = 0.3^\circ$, $\theta_{3dB} = 1.2^\circ$, $\theta = 0.4^\circ$ (azimuth error). **Step 1 — Use the sinc-pattern model:** $$G(\alpha) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi\alpha \vert \theta_{3dB})}{\pi\alpha \vert \theta_{3dB}}$$ with first derivative: $$G'(\alpha) = \dfrac{\pi}{\theta_{3dB}}\!\left[\dfrac{\cos(\pi\alpha \vert \theta_{3dB}) \cdot (\pi\alpha \vert \theta_{3dB}) - \sin(\pi\alpha \vert \theta_{3dB})}{(\pi\alpha \vert \theta_{3dB})^2}\right]$$ **Step 2 — Compute $G(\beta)$:** $$\dfrac{\pi\beta}{\theta_{3dB}} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.3}{1.2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.7854 \text{ rad}$$ $$G(0.3^\circ) = \dfrac{\sin(0.7854)}{0.7854} = \dfrac{0.7071}{0.7854} \approx 0.9003$$ **Step 3 — Compute $G'(\beta)$:** Using $G'(\alpha) \approx (\pi/\theta_{3dB}) \cos(\pi\alpha / \theta_{3dB})$ for small $\alpha$: $$G'(0.3^\circ) \approx \dfrac{\pi}{1.2^\circ} \cos(0.7854) = 2.618 \text{ rad}^{-1} \cdot 0.7071 = 1.8515 \text{ per deg}$$ (Strictly convert: $\pi/1.2^\circ = 150°$ per radian inverse… for **per-degree** we have $G'(0.3) = 1.8515$ /degree.) **Step 4 — Monopulse slope $k_m$:** $$k_m = \dfrac{G'(\beta)}{G(\beta)} = \dfrac{1.8515}{0.9003} \approx 2.057 \text{ per deg}$$ **Step 5 — Monopulse error voltage (normalised):** $$\varepsilon = k_m \cdot \theta = 2.057 \times 0.4 \approx 0.823 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Validity check:** The error $\theta = 0.4^\circ$ exceeds the squint angle $\beta = 0.3^\circ$! The linear approximation $\varepsilon = k_m \theta$ is **not strictly valid** here. In an exam, state this caveat and indicate that the **true error voltage would be lower** (the discriminator curve saturates beyond $\beta$). Full credit is given if you note the limitation and provide the linearised answer. **Final answers:** - **(i)** Normalised monopulse error voltage: $\varepsilon \approx 0.82$ (with caveat that the linearised model breaks down at $\theta > \beta$). - **(ii)** Monopulse slope: $k_m \approx 2.06$ per degree. **(7 marks total: 3 + 2 + 2 for the three calculation parts)** --- ### **Question B [14 marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 variant]** **(a)** Explain the working of a **monopulse tracking radar** using **amplitude comparison**. With a block diagram, describe the formation of sum ($\Sigma$) and difference ($\Delta$) patterns using a **magic-T hybrid junction**. Why is this technique preferred over conical scan in modern fire-control radars? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **conical-scan tracking radar** operates at $f = 10$ GHz ($\lambda = 3$ cm) with a 1.5 m diameter parabolic dish (efficiency $\eta = 0.55$). The squint angle is $\beta = 0.2^\circ$ and the scan rate is $f_s = 45$ Hz. The target returns an echo with measured modulation depth $m = 0.25$ at a phase $\phi = 60^\circ$. - **(i)** Compute the **antenna 3-dB beamwidth** in degrees. **(2 marks)** - **(ii)** Estimate the **angular error magnitude** $\theta$ of the target from the boresight, and decompose it into **azimuth and elevation** error components. **(3 marks)** - **(iii)** If the target is at range $R = 50$ km, what is the **lateral displacement** of the target from the antenna boresight in metres? **(2 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Q.B(a):** **Monopulse amplitude-comparison — principle:** 1. The antenna feed is divided into **four horns** (or a four-port comparator), each pointing at a slightly different direction in the azimuth-elevation plane. **(1 mark)** 2. The four outputs are combined in a **magic-T hybrid junction** to form three channels: - **Sum ($\Sigma$) channel:** all four horns added in phase $\rightarrow$ a single narrow beam on boresight. - **Azimuth-difference ($\Delta_{az}$):** $(L_1 + L_2) - (R_1 + R_2)$ $\rightarrow$ a pattern with a *null* on boresight and lobes left/right. - **Elevation-difference ($\Delta_{el}$):** $(L_1 + R_1) - (L_2 + R_2)$ $\rightarrow$ a pattern with a *null* on boresight and lobes up/down. **(2 marks)** 3. From a *single* received pulse, the receiver computes: $$\varepsilon_{az} = \dfrac{\Delta_{az}}{\Sigma}, \quad \varepsilon_{el} = \dfrac{\Delta_{el}}{\Sigma}$$ These are the **normalised error voltages** in azimuth and elevation. **(1 mark)** 4. The error voltages drive **servo amplifiers** and **motors** to steer the antenna to null the errors, keeping the target on boresight. **(1 mark)** 5. **Why preferred over conical scan:** Monopulse derives the angle error from a **single pulse**, completely eliminating errors due to *target glint* and *amplitude scintillation* between pulses, and providing **jam resistance** because the scan reference is not transmitted. Monopulse radars also achieve **3-5× better angular accuracy** and much higher data rates. **(2 marks)** > [!NOTE] > **Block diagram:** Refer to the **Monopulse Block Diagram** in **Section 4.2** of these notes. **(Full marks awarded for diagram.)** --- #### **Solution to Q.B(b):** **Given:** - $f = 10$ GHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / 10^{10} = 0.03$ m = 3 cm. - $D = 1.5$ m, $\eta = 0.55$, $\beta = 0.2^\circ$, $f_s = 45$ Hz, $m = 0.25$, $\phi = 60^\circ$, $R = 50$ km. **(i) 3-dB beamwidth:** $$\theta_{3dB} = \dfrac{0.886 \lambda}{\eta D} = \dfrac{0.886 \times 0.03}{0.55 \times 1.5}$$ $$\theta_{3dB} = \dfrac{0.02658}{0.825} = 0.03222 \text{ rad} = 0.03222 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 1.846^\circ$$ $$\boxed{\;\theta_{3dB} \approx 1.85^\circ\;} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ **(ii) Angular error magnitude:** For a sinc-pattern antenna near boresight: $$m \approx \dfrac{2\pi \beta \theta}{\theta_{3dB}^2} \quad \text{(valid for small squint and small error)}$$ Solving for $\theta$: $$\theta \approx \dfrac{m \cdot \theta_{3dB}^2}{2\pi \beta}$$ Plug in (using consistent units — convert everything to radians): - $\beta = 0.2^\circ = 0.003491$ rad - $\theta_{3dB} = 1.85^\circ = 0.03222$ rad - $m = 0.25$ $$\theta = \dfrac{0.25 \times (0.03222)^2}{2\pi \times 0.003491} = \dfrac{0.25 \times 1.038 \times 10^{-3}}{0.02193} = \dfrac{2.595 \times 10^{-4}}{0.02193}$$ $$\boxed{\;\theta \approx 0.01183 \text{ rad} \approx 0.678^\circ\;}$$ **Azimuth and elevation decomposition:** $$\theta_{az} = \theta \cos\phi = 0.01183 \times \cos(60^\circ) = 0.01183 \times 0.5 = 5.92 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} = 0.339^\circ$$ $$\theta_{el} = \theta \sin\phi = 0.01183 \times \sin(60^\circ) = 0.01183 \times 0.866 = 1.024 \times 10^{-2} \text{ rad} = 0.587^\circ$$ $$\boxed{\;\theta_{az} \approx 0.34^\circ, \quad \theta_{el} \approx 0.59^\circ\;} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$ **(iii) Lateral displacement at $R = 50$ km:** For small angles, lateral displacement $\delta = R \cdot \theta$: $$\delta = 50{,}000 \text{ m} \times 0.01183 = 591.5 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{\;\delta \approx 592 \text{ metres}\;} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** $0.678^\circ$ at 50 km is roughly $0.0118 \times 50{,}000 \approx 590$ m — consistent with the calculation. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Tracking-Radar Questions (Module 4):** > > 1. **Confusing "amplitude-comparison monopulse" with "phase-comparison monopulse"** in 14-mark questions. Always state which hybrid junction you are using (magic-T vs. Rat-Race) and which feed configuration. > > 2. **Forgetting the "monopulse" property** — students often describe conical-scan or sequential-lobing in a monopulse question. Always emphasise *single-pulse error extraction*. > > 3. **Skipping the block diagram** in 7-mark parts. A neat block diagram carries **2 to 3 marks by itself** in KTU valuation. > > 4. **Unit errors in numerical problems** — beamwidth must be in **radians** in the formula $k_m = G'(\beta)/G(\beta)$, or in **degrees** consistently throughout. Mixing units is the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers. > > 5. **Not addressing glint / jamming immunity** when asked "why monopulse is preferred." A one-line mention of *glint* and *amplitude scintillation cancellation* earns the full design-reasoning mark. > > 6. **In TWS questions, forgetting the predictor feedback** to the schedule generator. The adaptive beam-scheduling is the heart of TWS — without it, the system is just a search radar with a tracker bolted on. > > 7. **Conical-scan students often forget the "two orthogonal PSDs"** — one for azimuth, one for elevation. Always draw both phase-sensitive detectors with the sine and cosine reference inputs. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Tracking Radar Techniques (Module 4)** - ✅ **Tracking radar** continuously tracks *one* target with a narrow pencil beam; outputs **range, angle, and velocity** at high data rate. - ✅ The three classical techniques are: **Sequential (Switched) Lobing, Conical Scan, and Monopulse (Simultaneous Lobing)**. TWS is the modern multi-target variant. - ✅ **Sequential lobing** switches the beam between two squinted positions; the *difference* in echo amplitudes gives the angular error. **Vulnerable to glint.** - ✅ **Conical scan** rotates a squinted beam at $f_s$ (30–60 Hz). The echo becomes **amplitude modulated**; two orthogonal PSDs extract azimuth and elevation errors. Vulnerable to scan-modulation jamming. - ✅ **Monopulse tracking** uses a **four-horn feed** and a **magic-T hybrid** to form **$\Sigma$, $\Delta_{az}$, $\Delta_{el}$** channels from a **single pulse**. The normalised error voltage $\varepsilon = \Delta / \Sigma \approx k_m \theta$ drives the servos. - ✅ **Phase-comparison monopulse** uses two antennas separated by $d$ and measures the **phase difference** $\Delta\psi = (2\pi d / \lambda) \sin\theta$ — preferred for long-wavelength (VHF/UHF) radars. - ✅ **Track-While-Scan (TWS)** uses a **phased-array beam** and a **computer tracker** (Kalman filter) to follow many targets simultaneously while still performing search. - ✅ **Range tracking** uses a **split-gate (early-late gate)** tracker; the **range error** is proportional to $\Delta E = E_{EG} - E_{LG}$. - ✅ **Velocity / Doppler tracking** uses a **narrow bandpass filter** centred at the expected $f_d = 2v_r/\lambda$, retuned by a **VCO + AGC loop**. - ✅ **Modern fire-control radars** (Aegis, Patriot, APG-81) universally use **monopulse amplitude comparison** because of glint immunity and sub-tenth-degree accuracy. - ✅ **Squint angle** $\beta$ is typically $0.1$ to $0.25$ of the 3-dB beamwidth. The optimal monopulse slope is at $\beta \approx \theta_{3dB} / 4$. - ✅ **Antenna 3-dB beamwidth:** $\theta_{3dB} \approx 0.886 \lambda / (\eta D)$ — this formula appears in nearly every KTU numerical. - ✅ **Modulation depth $m$** in conical scan is **directly proportional** to angular error $\theta$ — this is the principle used in conical-scan angle discrimination. - ✅ **KTU exam key phrases** to memorise: "**single-pulse error extraction**," "**magic-T hybrid junction**," "**sum-and-difference patterns**," "**split-gate tracker**," "**phase-sensitive detector**," "**Kalman filter for smoothing**." > [!NOTE] > **Module 4 Take-away Sentence (for your last-night revision):** > *"Tracking radar extracts the angular error by either sequentially switching lobes (slow, glint-prone), continuously rotating a squinted beam (conical scan), or by forming sum-and-difference patterns in a single pulse (monopulse) — with monopulse being the dominant modern technique for high-accuracy, glint-immune, single-pulse angle tracking."*
